The GUI plugin is minimally invasive (10 tools added to existing runtimes). - Efficient: Total compute cost is not reported. At a median of 76 tool calls per task × 114 tasks × multiple backbones, frontier model API costs are substantial. The trajectory-aware judge adds a multi-turn GPT-5.5 subprocess per rollout, potentially 10–30 turns each. - Effective: The benchmark demonstrably exposes a gap (best 41.2% PassRate vs. >78% on OSWorld-Verified) and the cheat detection removes real inflation (20.2pp for GPT-5.5). The OSWorld CLI re-evaluation provides a compelling existence proof that existing benchmarks mismeasure GUI capability. - Scalable: 114 tasks is limited; per-domain reliability at 10–18 tasks per domain is low. Coverage is restricted to English-language Linux desktop tasks; generalization to Windows, macOS, or non-English domains is not evaluated. The paper delivers benchmark artifacts (tasks, environment snapshots, evaluation code), not trained models. Strengths: - Channel non-substitutability is formalized and empirically validated: the 19-atom taxonomy gives auditable per-task justification for P1, and the ablation operationalizes the claim at population level with a +31.6pp gap—much larger than in any prior hybrid benchmark. - The OSWorld CLI re-evaluation (Appendix E) is the paper's sharpest contribution: showing CLI agents match vision agents at 79.1% vs. 77.3% on an ostensibly GUI-native benchmark directly undermines the standard interpretation of GUI benchmark leaderboards. - The trajectory-aware judge with empirically-derived shortcut detection is principled and quantified: 20.2pp inflation removal for GPT-5.5 changes the interpretation of frontier model capabilities and provides a methodology other benchmarks can adopt. - Real-world task sourcing with traceable provenance and evaluation inside deployed agent runtimes (not custom simulators) increases the ecological validity of the results relative to prior work. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The paper explicitly limits scope to English-language tasks on Linux desktops and acknowledges that extending to other languages, operating systems, a larger task pool, and broader backbone × harness coverage is future work. The authors do not frame the 114-task scale, single-judge-backbone design, or absence of human baselines as limitations. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The trajectory-aware judge uses a single fixed GPT-5.5 backbone with no inter-rater reliability or judge accuracy evaluation. A benchmark claiming to measure agent performance needs systematic validation that the judge itself is accurate; co-author spot-checking is not a substitute. - The sharp cross-harness asymmetry (Claude Opus 4.7: 35.1% on OpenClaw → 13.2% on Codex CLI; GPT-5.5: 33.3% on OpenClaw → 14.9% on Claude Code) is reported but not analyzed mechanistically—which specific tool schema differences, system prompt conventions, or action-loop design elements cause these 20pp drops is left unexplained. - 114 tasks produces unreliable per-domain PassRates at 10–18 tasks per domain; differences of 5–10pp between domains (e.g., WEB 66.7% vs. DAV 15.4% for Opus 4.7) could reflect noise as much as genuine domain difficulty, yet no confidence intervals are reported. - The OSWorld CLI re-evaluation uses a custom judge that credits "substantive intent by any path"—a more permissive criterion than OSWorld's state-matching verifier, which may explain part of the 79.1%/77.3% result beyond the channel comparison. - No human performance baseline exists, making it impossible to determine whether 41.2% represents a ceiling imposed by task design, by the runtime scaffolding, or by fundamental model limitations. Bottom Line: Any researcher building or evaluating computer-use agents should read this paper—it provides quantified evidence that existing benchmarks overestimate hybrid-interface capability and that outcome-only evaluation inflates scores by 10–20pp, both findings directly actionable for the field.
Recommended 2606.09426 PDF
#12 — Highly Innovative (76)

A Masked Mixture Model for Compact and Accurate Matrix Factorization

Y Park, SJ Lee, J Lee, U Kang · yongchanpark.github.io · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Masked Mixture Factorization (MMF), a matrix factorization variant that approximates a target matrix as a sum of K masked bilinear components, where per-instance learnable shift scalars gate which latent dimensions each row or column emphasizes. - Why it's important: Classical MF forces every instance to use the same global latent basis uniformly, which wastes capacity on heterogeneous matrices (e.g., users with disjoint preference profiles) and requires large rank increases to compensate — MMF breaks this bottleneck within the MF parameter budget. - How it does it: Each of K components shares the same base factor matrices U and V but applies element-wise instance-specific masks f_k(r; s) driven by a single learnable shift scalar s per instance per component, lifting effective rank from R to KR while adding only (I+J)K parameters. - The good: MMF achieves best RMSE on ML-1M (0.827) and ML-10M (0.769) without side information, trains 5–25x faster than graph-based competitors, and the expressivity and identifiability results are formally proved. - The missing: Top-N ranking is evaluated on ML-1M only; recent strong non-graph MF-style baselines (EASE, RecVAE) are absent from the ranking comparison, and the mask function family must be chosen by dataset-specific search with no principled guidance. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers in matrix completion, collaborative filtering, or low-rank modeling who want a theoretically grounded, drop-in improvement over standard MF at negligible computational cost.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (80) and Non-Obviousness (78) drive the score: peer-reviewed at KDD 2026, two theorems with complete proofs, released code, completion results averaged over five runs with SDs ≤0.001, and no Goodhart concerns. Novelty (75) is a genuine mechanism — instance-wise latent-dimension gating via shift-parameterized smooth masks achieving KR effective rank at (I+J)(K+R) parameters, not a trivial combination. Completeness (75) and Resilience (75) are strong; the identifiability experiment uses only 2 seeds and the ranking comparison omits EASE/RecVAE, which caps them. Practical Impact (65): released drop-in code, but untested beyond ML-10M. A theoretically grounded, rigorously executed contribution.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 80 Non-Obv 78 Completeness 75 Resilience 75 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Standard matrix factorization represents a matrix X as UV⊤, forcing every row and column to interact through the same R latent dimensions with equal weighting. In heterogeneous matrices — user-item rating data where user subpopulations care about disjoint item attributes, face image matrices with instance-specific illumination effects, or document-term matrices covering disjoint topics — this global sharing is a binding representational constraint. Increasing rank is the standard remedy but scales parameters as (I+J)R. Prior adaptive approaches either maintain multiple independent local factorizations (LLORMA: complexity without within-model dimension gating) or introduce heavy neural architectures requiring side information and graph construction (GC-MC, LightGCN). The gap is a lightweight, inductive-bias-driven mechanism that provides per-instance dimension selectivity within the bilinear factorization structure, without external data or deep networks. Key Idea The single technical trick is to replace the global product UV⊤ with a sum of K masked products ∑_k (U⊙M^U_k)(V⊙M^V_k)⊤, where each mask M^U_k(i,r) = f_k(r; s^U_{k,i}) is a smooth function of latent dimension index r evaluated at a single learnable scalar shift s per instance per component. The shift determines where along the latent dimension axis each instance concentrates its capacity; different components use differently scaled versions of the same base function, producing coarse-to-fine coverage without additional parameters. Because all K components share U and V, the model attains effective rank KR with only (I+J)(K+R) parameters — far fewer than the (I+J)KR needed for naive rank-KR MF. Methodology MMF parameterizes X̂ = ∑_{k=1}^{K} (U⊙M^U_k)(V⊙M^V_k)⊤ with U ∈ R^{I×R}, V ∈ R^{J×R} (shared base factors), and per-component masks M^U_k(i,r) = f_k(r; s^U_{k,i}) where s^U_{k,i} ∈ R is a learnable scalar. The default multi-scale instantiation is f_k(r;s) = (1/K)f(k/K·(r−s)), where f is a smooth base function (Gaussian or sinusoidal); the 1/K normalization keeps mixture scale stable and k/K diversifies effective widths. Objectives are standard squared loss for reconstruction, MSE for completion, and BPR for Top-N ranking. Training is end-to-end SGD on U, V, and all shift parameters. Three critical design decisions: (1) Shared-basis masking rather than K independent factor sets — reduces parameter count by K-fold while preserving interpretability; justified by the rank-amplification theorem. (2) Shift-scalar parameterization rather than free mask matrices — reduces mask parameters from O(K(I+J)R) to O((I+J)K); well-justified and validated. (3) Multi-scale f_k design — achieves component diversity without extra parameters; empirically validated in ablations showing smooth localized masks outperform monotone or non-smooth ones. Two theorems anchor the method. Theorem 1 shows that under mild analytic and mask linear-independence conditions, MMF achieves rank min{I, J, KR} for Lebesgue-almost-every parameter setting. Theorem 2 shows that MMF generically eliminates the continuous rotational symmetry of standard MF (only coordinate-wise scaling remains), improving identifiability. Key Results Matrix reconstruction (relative Frobenius error vs. SVD): MMF reduces error by 7.3%–20.2% on Yale-B (2016×2414 face images) and 7.3%–40.3% on Reuters (2000×3000 document-term matrix) at matched parameter budgets up to B=120; improvement grows with budget. On synthetic block-diagonal matrices H2–H6 (up to 6 heterogeneous communities), MMF outperforms SVD and all MF variants with widening advantage as block count increases, consistent with the theoretical rank-amplification analysis. Matrix completion (RMSE on 5 benchmarks): MMF achieves best results on ML-1M (0.827, vs. next-best 0.829 for IMC-GAE) and ML-10M (0.769, vs. 0.770 for STAR-GCN); second-best on ML-100K (0.884) and Douban (0.726). Methods marked with side information (STAR-GCN, IDCF-GC, GHRS, MoRGH) are competitive or better on Flixster (extremely sparse at density 0.003), where graph propagation with auxiliary data is particularly advantageous. IGMC and IDCF-GC are out-of-memory on ML-10M; MMF scales without issue. Top-N recommendation (ML-1M, BPR loss): Recall@10=0.1807 (best, vs. SGL's 0.1804), NDCG@10=0.2530 (2nd, SGL=0.2535), Recall@20=0.2742 (best), NDCG@20=0.2647 (best). LightGCN and SGL use graph propagation and self-supervised contrastive learning; MMF uses a straightforward BPR objective. Training speed (wall-clock): MMF trains in 15.2s on ML-100K and 67.3s on ML-1M, vs. 43.6–984s and 384–7468s for graph-based competitors. Ablation shows smooth localized masks (Gaussian, sine) consistently outperform monotone (sigmoid, tanh) and non-smooth (triangle) families; optimal K/B ratio is ~0.5 for random dense data and lower (~0.3) for structured heterogeneous data. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The genuine novelty is instance-wise latent dimension gating within a single bilinear factorization via shift-parameterized smooth masks, backed by formal expressivity and identifiability analysis. LLORMA uses multiple local factorizations but not within-model dimension selection; mixture-of-experts methods use independent expert networks rather than shared-basis masking. Applying smooth shift-parameterized masks to achieve KR effective rank with (I+J)(K+R) parameters is not a straightforward combination of existing ideas. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations cover mask function family (5 options), K/B ratio, and qualitative expressivity/identifiability verification — these are well-targeted. Matrix completion results are averaged over five runs with standard deviations ≤0.001. The identifiability experiment (Figure 7) uses only 2 random seeds; a stronger validation would use 5+ seeds and report aggregate diagonal alignment statistics. Proofs for both theorems are complete and appear in appendices (Appendices A and B), using standard real-analytic geometry. Code is publicly released at https://github.com/snudatalab/MMF. No methodological Goodhart's-Law concerns: evaluation datasets are independent of the training protocol, and no metric is used that was jointly optimized. The paper is peer-reviewed (KDD 2026). Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Runs on standard hardware (pure matrix operations, no graph construction, no message passing); the shift-scalar parameterization requires no specialized infrastructure beyond a standard deep learning framework. - Efficient: Per-epoch runtime is 1.1–1.3ms vs. 0.5–0.9ms for plain MF on synthetic matrices (modest overhead); end-to-end training on ML-1M takes 67s vs. 384–7468s for graph-based alternatives. - Effective: Achieves best RMSE on ML-1M and ML-10M and best/near-best Recall@20/NDCG@20 on ML-1M ranking without side information; the gains on Reuters reconstruction reach 40% over SVD at matched budgets. - Scalable: Tested up to ML-10M (70K users, 10K items, 10M ratings); no test at social-media or e-commerce scale (100M+ interactions), and the paper makes no explicit claim of performance at that scale. Software artifact: code released at https://github.com/snudatalab/MMF. Strengths - The expressivity theorem (Theorem 1) is tight and clean: it shows rank KR is achieved for Lebesgue-almost-every parameter setting under mild conditions, directly justifying the observed empirical gains without hand-waving. - The identifiability result (Theorem 2) is practically valuable, not just theoretically decorative: it predicts and empirically confirms (Figure 7) that MMF factors are reproducible across random seeds, unlike standard MF which produces rotationally ambiguous solutions. - The parameter-efficiency argument is quantified and honest: (I+J)(K+R) versus (I+J)KR for naive rank-KR MF, with experiments explicitly holding budgets equal across all baselines. - Coverage of three distinct evaluation regimes (controlled reconstruction, real-world completion, ranking) on both synthetic and real data provides a credible multi-faceted validation of the central hypothesis. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors note that Flixster results are not top-tier (0.898 vs. best 0.872), attributing this to extreme sparsity (density 0.003) where graph-based methods with side information hold an inherent advantage. They note that optimal mask function family and K/B ratio are data-dependent, requiring per-dataset tuning. They identify cold-start/inductive generalization as a gap, flagging it as future work. The authors engage with these limitations directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Top-N ranking evaluation covers only ML-1M; standard recommendation benchmarks such as Amazon product data, Yelp, or Gowalla are absent, making generalization claims about ranking performance narrow. - The ranking comparison omits strong non-graph MF-style baselines (EASE, RecVAE, SimpleX) that are competitive on implicit-feedback benchmarks without graph machinery — the comparison field is weaker than it could be. - The identifiability experiment (Figure 7) uses exactly 2 seeds; this demonstrates the concept but does not statistically establish reproducibility across a distribution of initializations. - No principled rule is given for selecting the mask function family (Gaussian vs. sine) — the ablation shows which is better post-hoc per dataset, but a practitioner must run the search themselves. - Scalability beyond ML-10M is untested; the computational advantage over graph methods may narrow if batched graph methods use sparse neighbor sampling at scale. Bottom Line Any practitioner using standard MF or low-rank models for collaborative filtering or matrix completion should read this — the method is a genuine theoretical improvement, the implementation overhead is negligible, and the empirical gains over MF baselines are consistent and credibly controlled.
Search Results
#13 — Highly Innovative (75)

UniSVQ: 2-bit Unified Scalar-Vector Quantization

H Wang, H Zhao, X Yu, Z Yao, X Han, Z Liu, M Sun · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces UniSVQ, a 2-bit post-training quantization framework for LLMs that parameterizes vector-quantization codewords as an affine transformation of integer lattices, bridging scalar and vector quantization in a single unified representation. - Why it's important: At 2-bit, scalar quantization methods lose 30–50%+ accuracy due to outlier sensitivity, while vector quantization methods require large codebooks that exceed GPU L1 cache and tank inference throughput; practitioners need a method that delivers VQ-quality accuracy with SQ-level inference efficiency. - How it does it: By constraining the VQ codebook to an affine image of integer coordinate vectors (codewords = A·w̄ + B, w̄ ∈ ℤ^d), the affine transform commutes with matrix multiplication, so existing optimized integer GEMM kernels can be reused while the codebook shrinks ~64× versus unconstrained VQ. - The good: On Qwen-3-32B, UniSVQ retains 98% of FP16 accuracy (76.15% avg. 0-shot vs. 78.01% FP16) while delivering 1.68× throughput and 75% memory reduction versus FP16, with only 20 auxiliary floating-point parameters per weight matrix. - The missing: The strongest VQ baseline (Qtip, 71.08% avg. on Qwen-3-8B vs. UniSVQ's 67.95%) is relegated to an appendix on one model; throughput is measured only at batch size 1; no code or weights are released. - Read it or not: Worth reading for LLM deployment engineers who need 2-bit weight quantization compatible with optimized integer kernels and without large codebook overhead; less urgent for those already using QuIP# or Qtip at full accuracy.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (82) and Novelty (80) drive the score: parameterizing VQ codewords as an affine image of an integer lattice — so the affine transform commutes with matmul, reusing integer kernels while shrinking the codebook ~64× — is a clean, principled insight that unifies SQ and VQ rather than an obvious recombination. Rigor (75) is supported by peer review (ICML 2026) and thorough weight-level (SNR/MSE) and task-level ablations, but no seeds/error bars and no released code. Completeness (75) is fair, with honest scope limits but the strongest VQ baseline (Qtip) relegated to a one-model appendix. Resilience (72) is dented by batch-size-1-only throughput and the unaddressed ~3-point Qtip gap. Impact (62) — custom kernels but no released artifact. Weighted: 0.20*80+0.25*75+0.15*82+0.15*75+0.15*72+0.10*62 = 75.
empirical Novelty 80 Rigor 75 Non-Obv 82 Completeness 75 Resilience 72 Impact 62
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation At 2-bit post-training quantization, two established paradigms face a fundamental accuracy-vs-efficiency trade-off. Scalar quantization (SQ) maps each weight independently to 4 discrete values per group — cheap to decode and kernel-compatible, but extremely sensitive to weight outliers whose clipping error dominates at 2 bits; best-in-class SQ methods (SpinQuant, OSTQuant) still lose 12–37% relative accuracy on zero-shot tasks, with smaller models degrading more severely. Vector quantization (VQ) jointly quantizes groups of weights against an unconstrained floating-point codebook, achieving far lower degradation, but the codebook (up to R^{d × 2^{bd}} entries) must fit in GPU L1 cache or suffer high-bandwidth codebook transfer costs that cut throughput; all existing VQ methods address this via multi-level codebooks or complex trellis codes that require decompression at inference time. No prior method simultaneously achieves VQ-level accuracy, SQ-level kernel compatibility, and compact codebook storage. Key Idea The unstructured codebook — not vector quantization per se — is the root cause of VQ's inference overhead. If the codebook is constrained so every codeword is an affine image of an integer vector (C_i = A·w̄_i + B, w̄_i ∈ ℤ^d), then the affine operation commutes with linear-layer matrix multiplication: the matmul runs on integer weights using standard SQ kernels (with activations absorbing the affine transform), and the codebook reduces from O(d·2^{bd}) floats to d² + d parameters (20 floats for d=4 at 2-bit), a ~64× storage reduction. After a Randomized Hadamard Transform renders weights approximately Gaussian and incoherent, this structured codebook approximates the optimal unconstrained codebook with only modest accuracy loss. Methodology The pipeline has three stages. (1) Preprocessing: A bilateral Randomized Hadamard Transform R(W) = U·S_U·W·S_V·V (U,V Hadamard matrices, S_U, S_V random ±1 diagonal matrices) rotates weights to suppress outliers and render them approximately Gaussian with the incoherence property max(W_RHT) ≤ μ‖W‖_F/√mn. The RHT is fully reversible via Fast Walsh–Hadamard Transform in O(n log n). (2) Quantization: Codewords are initialized as C_i = s·G·w̄_i − s·G·b·1 where G is a random orthogonal matrix, s = √(12/(2^{2b}−1)), and b = (2^b−1)/2. A random orthogonal G is chosen over the mathematically optimal D4 generator specifically because it preserves a diagonal covariance matrix for the integer codebook, keeping the quantization grid isotropic and matching the Gaussian assumption — the D4 generator introduces non-diagonal covariance over integer inputs. Quantization uses the LDLQ method (LDL decomposition of the calibration Hessian H = LDL^T), sequentially adjusting unquantized columns to compensate for committed quantization error. (3) Block-wise fine-tuning: The affine parameters A and B are optimized per Transformer block by minimizing MSE between the quantized block's output and the FP16 reference on the same input. Training uses 1,024 RedPajama sequences (7:1 split), batch size 16, lr 5e-5, up to 5 epochs with early stopping at patience 3; ~6 hours on A100 for an 8B model. Key design decisions: (a) d=4 is justified over d=8 since fine-tuning at d=4 matches or exceeds d=8 without fine-tuning, while d=8 adds O(8Nn) overhead; (b) random orthogonal initialization over D4 is well-justified both theoretically (covariance argument in Appendix A.1) and empirically (6-point avg. accuracy gap). Key Results On Qwen-3-32B, UniSVQ achieves 76.15% avg. 0-shot accuracy (98% of FP16's 78.01%), vs. 66.65% (SpinQuant) and 68.29% (OSTQuant) among SQ baselines, and 76.30% (QuIP#) and 75.37% (AQLM) among VQ baselines — matching or slightly exceeding VQ. On Qwen-3-8B, UniSVQ achieves 67.95% avg. (92% of FP16), surpassing QuIP# (67.55%) and AQLM (64.94%). On Llama-3-8B-Instruct, UniSVQ scores 67.62% avg. (91% of FP16), outperforming all SQ methods (best: 46.71%, OSTQuant) and AQLM (59.84%), but trailing QuIP# (69.72%). Weight-level isolation on Qwen-3-4B: UniSVQ achieves SNR 8.48 dB vs. −3.45 dB (GPTQ) and −3.06 dB (SpinQuant), an 11.9 dB gap. Throughput on Llama-3-8B (single A100, batch=1): UniSVQ 101.65 tok/s vs. FP16 60.38, AQLM 70.97, QuIP# 79.60, GPTQ 130.70; peak memory 3.87 GB vs. 15.72 GB FP16. GPTQ's higher throughput is moot given near-random accuracy at 2-bit. The appendix comparison with Qtip (trellis-coded VQ, 71.08% avg. on Qwen-3-8B) reveals UniSVQ trails by ~3.1 points — a nontrivial gap. Baselines span 2023–2025 and are current and strong; calibration data is identical across methods. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The novel contribution is the linear-constrained quantization grid — parameterizing VQ codewords as an affine image of an integer lattice — which is a non-obvious structural insight that unifies SQ and VQ under a single framework. All components (RHT from QuIP/QuIP#, LDLQ from QuIP#, block-wise MSE fine-tuning from OmniQuant/FlatQuant lineage) are individually known; the genuine novelty is recognizing that an affine constraint on the codebook simultaneously preserves integer kernel compatibility and captures most of VQ's distributional flexibility. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are thorough and well-targeted: they isolate fine-tuning (+0.96 avg. points), orthogonal initialization vs. D4 (+6.95 points), vector dimension d=4 vs. d=8 (marginal), and RHT (catastrophic without it — 67.95 → 39.06%). Table 4 performs quantizer-level isolation at weight-level (MSE/SNR), which is a strong design. No error bars or multiple random seeds are reported for accuracy experiments; throughput is averaged over 3 runs. No code or model weights are released, limiting reproducibility. The paper is accepted at ICML 2026 (peer-reviewed), which raises confidence. One methodological concern: the fine-tuning uses the same 1,024 calibration sequences as Hessian computation for LDLQ; standard in the field but not evaluated for calibration-distribution overfitting via held-out perplexity beyond WikiText-2/C4. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL (method and systems paper evaluated by accuracy and throughput measurements). - Practical: Runs on a single NVIDIA A100; quantization + fine-tuning of an 8B model takes ~6 hours, which is commercially feasible. Off-the-shelf SciPy for orthogonal matrix generation; custom CUDA kernels for autoregressive generation (based on Dao-AILab open-source code). - Efficient: Reduces peak GPU memory from 15.72 GB to 3.87 GB (75.4%) and increases throughput from 60.38 to 101.65 tok/s (1.68×) on Llama-3-8B vs. FP16. Codebook auxiliary parameters: 20 floats per weight matrix, with O(N·n·d) additional activation-side compute per forward pass (d=4, negligible vs. O(N·n·m) matmul). Memory advantage over VQ (codebook 40 bytes vs. 2048 bytes for d=4, 2-bit) is the key efficiency driver. - Effective: On the largest tested model (Qwen-3-32B), UniSVQ retains 98% of FP16 zero-shot accuracy across 6 benchmarks, which credibly solves the stated problem. On smaller models (4B–8B) the retention drops to 87–92%. Results are weaker relative to QuIP# on Llama-3-8B than on Qwen-3, suggesting model-family dependence. - Scalable: Tested up to 32B parameters on Qwen-3. No experiments at 70B+ scale. Throughput is measured at batch size 1 (single-sequence autoregressive); batched inference behavior at higher concurrency is unaddressed. Activation and KV-cache quantization are out of scope, leaving those bottlenecks unaddressed at inference scale. The work delivers a method and custom CUDA kernels but no released code, dataset, or trained model weights. Strengths - The core insight — that constraining codewords to an affine image of an integer lattice simultaneously satisfies kernel compatibility and distributional fit — is clean, principled, and derives directly from a formal geometric analysis of quantization grids. It is not an obvious combination of prior work. - The 1/64 codebook storage reduction (40 vs. 2,048 bytes for d=4, 2-bit) is quantitatively compelling and mechanistically explains the observed throughput advantage over AQLM (70.97 tok/s) and QuIP# (79.60 tok/s). - The counter-intuitive finding that a random orthogonal initialization beats the mathematically optimal D4 lattice generator is well-explained by the covariance argument (D4 introduces non-diagonal covariance over integer inputs, breaking isotropy), and the ablation quantifies the 6-point cost of ignoring this. - The ablation design separates component contributions at both weight level (MSE/SNR in Table 4) and task level (0-shot accuracy), providing meaningful credit assignment rather than end-to-end black-box comparisons. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state two limitations: (1) weight-only quantization — activation and KV-cache quantization are not addressed, leaving significant inference efficiency gains on the table for end-to-end deployment; (2) interaction of the linear-constrained grid with highly optimized GEMM kernels (e.g., production-grade fused kernels beyond the custom Dao-AILab-based implementation) "remains to be explored." The authors confront these directly rather than minimizing them, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The strongest VQ baseline, Qtip (71.08% avg. on Qwen-3-8B vs. UniSVQ's 67.95%), appears only in an appendix and covers only one model and no throughput comparison. A ~3-point gap relative to Qtip on a single model size is enough to matter in practice and warrants main-body treatment with throughput-vs-accuracy trade-off analysis. - All throughput measurements use batch size 1, single-sequence autoregressive generation. In LLM serving, continuous batching at higher concurrency is standard; the L1-cache codebook advantage of UniSVQ may behave differently at batch sizes ≥8. - No code, model weights, or quantization scripts are released; the method description is complete enough that reimplementation is feasible, but correctness verification requires significant engineering effort. - The fine-tuning and Hessian computation share the same 1,024 calibration sequences. While standard in the PTQ community, this creates potential for calibration overfitting; the reported WikiText-2/C4 perplexity serves as a partial check but a held-out downstream benchmark not seen during calibration would strengthen the claim. - Results on Llama-3-8B are notably weaker relative to QuIP# (67.62 vs. 69.72%) than on Qwen-3 variants, suggesting the method's efficacy may be sensitive to model architecture or training paradigm, but no hypothesis is offered and no additional model families (e.g., Mistral, Gemma) are evaluated. Bottom Line A practitioner targeting 2-bit weight-only LLM deployment on single-GPU hardware will find UniSVQ directly useful: it is the first method in this class that matches VQ accuracy while remaining compatible with optimized integer kernels, and the core affine-lattice insight is principled enough to generalize beyond the specific implementation — though missing code release and the unaddressed gap to Qtip mean it is not yet a definitive solution.

#14 — Highly Innovative (75)

SpenseGPT: Practical One-shot Pruning Enabling Sparse and Dense GEMMs for LLM Inference

J Lee, S Hwang, S Rajbhandari · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Spense, a hybrid sparse-dense weight format that splits each LLM weight matrix into a 2:4 sparse region and a contiguous dense region, along with two one-shot post-training pruning algorithms (SpenseGPT and SpenseGPT+) that populate this format without requiring custom compilers or activation expansion. - Why it's important: Semi-structured 2:4 sparsity is natively accelerated on NVIDIA B200, AMD, and Meta MTIA hardware, but its strict 50% constraint degrades accuracy on challenging reasoning tasks without retraining — blocking practical deployment of sparse inference for large reasoning models. - How it does it: By exploiting the permutation-invariance of GLU-style MLP intermediate indices, Spense rearranges selected "dense" indices into a contiguous block, enabling execution via unmodified cuSPARSELt and cuBLASLt kernels, while SpenseGPT+ scores index importance using a Wanda-style activation-weighted magnitude estimator aggregated across all three MLP projections. - The good: SpenseGPT+ at 25% MLP sparsity on Seed-OSS-36B reaches average score 73.12 vs. dense baseline 73.86 across AIME/GPQA/IFEval/LCB, while achieving up to 1.2× end-to-end VLLM decoding speedup on B200 with FP8 — the first demonstrated one-shot pruning with real serving-level speedup on B200 while preserving model quality. - The missing: Evaluation is limited to two model families on a single GPU generation (B200) with a single serving stack (VLLM/FP8); attention layers are left unpruned, capping the maximum achievable sparsity benefit, and no analysis of calibration-distribution sensitivity is quantified. - Read it or not: Worth reading for ML systems engineers and LLM inference practitioners targeting NVIDIA Blackwell or AMD hardware who need a practical, library-compatible path to sparse LLM deployment without retraining.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (82) and Novelty (78) lead: the GLU permutation-invariance insight enabling a contiguous dense block that maps onto unmodified cuSPARSELt/cuBLASLt kernels is genuinely new, and the ablation showing dense-index placement direction causes a ~70-point AIME swing is a sharp, mechanistically illuminating finding. Rigor (75) — three seeds, scoring and placement ablations, current baselines, honest ProxSparse-OOM report; docked for non-standard EVALSCOPE harness and no released code. Completeness (78) and Resilience (72) are solid but the decode-phase (batch=1, M=1) regime where Fig. 1 shows sparse GEMM underperforming is not reported, undercutting the headline speedup. Impact (60) — real B200 serving speedup demonstrated but no software artifact released. Weighted: 0.20*78+0.25*75+0.15*82+0.15*78+0.15*72+0.10*60 = 75.
empirical Novelty 78 Rigor 75 Non-Obv 82 Completeness 78 Resilience 72 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Large-scale LLM inference on modern accelerators is memory-bandwidth-bound, and hardware vendors have built sparse tensor core support (2:4 semi-structured sparsity) into NVIDIA B200, AMD, and Meta MTIA chips that can halve effective memory bandwidth and deliver up to 2× GEMM speedup. The barrier to practical adoption is the strict 50%-per-group constraint: post-training pruning to 2:4 without retraining causes significant accuracy degradation on demanding reasoning benchmarks (SparseGPT on Seed-OSS-36B drops avg from 73.86 to 65.18; Wanda and HyperPrune collapse to ~10 avg score). Existing relaxed-sparsity approaches that soften this constraint are either GPU-generation-specific (PATCH requires Triton for Ampere only) or introduce input-activation expansion overhead that eliminates the latency benefit (SlideSparse achieves only 1.04× end-to-end speedup at 25% sparsity on B200). Key Idea: The core insight is that the intermediate dimension of GLU-style MLPs is permutation-invariant — any reordering of (gate, up, down) projection index triplets leaves the MLP output unchanged. This means "important" indices (those that would cause large reconstruction error if compressed) can be identified, gathered into a contiguous block, left dense, and the remaining indices pruned to 2:4 — all while the resulting layout maps cleanly onto two sequential standard GEMM calls (one sparse via cuSPARSELt, one dense via cuBLASLt) with zero runtime overhead beyond the two GEMMs already required. Methodology: The pipeline has three stages. First, the Spense format definition: a weight matrix W is split into a contiguous p% dense region and a (100−p)% region compressed to 2:4 sparsity. Two variants handle output-split (for Wgate, Wup) and input-split with accumulation (for Wdown). Second, dense-index selection: SpenseGPT trivially selects the last p% of indices; SpenseGPT+ assigns each intermediate index an importance score by combining normalized Wanda-style scores (|W_ij| * sqrt(H_jj), averaged per row/column) from all three projections and selecting top-pN indices. Third, SparseGPT (OBS-based second-order reconstruction) is applied to the sparse regions, with dense indices placed on the right/bottom to serve as a compensation buffer during left-to-right column processing. Critical design decisions: (a) using Wanda-style per-index scores that assume independence across indices rather than SparseGPT-like scores that assume inter-weight dependencies — ablation shows SparseGPT-based selection achieves avg 27.79 vs. 70.76 for SpenseGPT+, validating this choice; (b) placing dense indices at the back rather than front or in-place — ablation shows front/in-place placement yields avg ~29, back yields 70.76; (c) pruning only MLP layers, leaving attention untouched. Key Results: On Qwen3-32B and Seed-OSS-36B (32B–36B parameter reasoning models), evaluated on AIME2024, GPQA Diamond, IFEval, and LiveCodeBench v6. At 25% MLP sparsity, SpenseGPT+ reaches average scores of 70.76 (Qwen3-32B, dense: 71.68) and 73.12 (Seed-OSS-36B, dense: 73.86), near-lossless compression. SparseGPT at 50% reaches only 60.27 and 65.18. End-to-end VLLM decoding speedup on B200 with FP8: Spense achieves up to 1.2× vs. dense baseline; SlideSparse achieves only ~1.04× at the same sparsity target. Pruning time: SpenseGPT takes 1.33–1.52h (same as SparseGPT); SpenseGPT+ takes 1.87–2.15h on a single B200 GPU. ProxSparse OOMs at this scale. The dramatic sensitivity of AIME score to dense-index placement (76.67 vs. 8.89 with different strategies at the same sparsity) is a notable and counter-intuitive finding. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION. The Spense format is a new weight storage and execution format; SpenseGPT+ introduces a new importance-scoring heuristic for hybrid sparse-dense index selection. The permutation-invariance observation that enables contiguous dense regions is the genuinely novel element; the underlying pruning engine (SparseGPT + OBS) and the importance-scoring style (Wanda) are existing techniques applied within this new framework. Rigor & Reproducibility: Experiments are run three times with different random seeds and averages are reported, which is appropriate for stochastic reasoning benchmarks. Ablations cover both the scoring strategy and the placement strategy, with clean quantitative comparisons. The baseline set (SparseGPT, Wanda, HyperPrune) is current and relevant; ProxSparse is included but OOMs, which is an honest result. No code or model weights are released (not stated in the paper). The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2606.10445v1, June 2026) and has not undergone peer review. Evaluation uses EVALSCOPE rather than the standard lm-evaluation-harness, with a footnote acknowledging that harness results for Qwen3-32B were anomalously low — this is a mild reproducibility concern since comparisons against prior work that used different harnesses are not directly validated. Deployability (EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS): - Practical: Runs on a single B200 GPU using standard VLLM with cuSPARSELt and cuBLASLt — both shipping libraries on commercially available NVIDIA hardware; AMD support via hipSPARSELt/hipBLASLt is claimed but not experimentally validated. - Efficient: SpenseGPT+ pruning takes under 2.2h on a single B200 for 32–36B models; runtime inference reduces GEMM cost while adding negligible non-GEMM overhead, in contrast to SlideSparse's activation-expansion overhead that erodes gains at large batch sizes. - Effective: At 25% MLP sparsity, the method preserves average benchmark scores within 1% of the dense baseline on two distinct model families across reasoning, code, and instruction-following tasks; the 1.2× end-to-end speedup is measured in a realistic serving setup (batch=32, seqlen=4096, VLLM). - Scalable: Tested only on 32–36B models; no experiments on smaller or larger models, and no analysis of how accuracy-sparsity tradeoffs change with model scale. Attention layers are excluded from pruning, so the method's benefit is bounded by MLP fraction of total compute. The paper stops at paper form with no released software artifact. Strengths: - The permutation-invariance insight for GLU MLPs is clean and enables a format that requires zero changes to inference kernels — a genuine practical contribution over prior hybrid-sparsity work. - The ablation revealing that placement direction (back vs. front vs. in-place) causes a 70-point AIME swing is a sharp empirical finding that illuminates a subtle interaction between SparseGPT's left-to-right column processing and dense-index placement, providing mechanistic understanding rather than just empirical tuning. - Demonstrated real end-to-end speedup on B200 — a currently deployed, commercially available GPU — closing the gap between theoretical sparse-tensor-core potential and actual serving latency. - Pruning cost (≤2.15h on one GPU) is practical for production model owners. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly note that (1) speedup depends on the underlying GEMM library efficiency and will vary with batch size, sequence length, hardware backend, and serving configuration; (2) SpenseGPT+ is sensitive to calibration distribution mismatch; and (3) evaluation on non-B200 accelerators (AMD, Meta MTIA) is absent. These are confronted honestly and briefly in a dedicated Limitations section. Analyst-Identified Gaps: Attention layers are excluded from pruning without ablation or justification — for models where attention GEMMs are a significant fraction of total compute, this caps the method's practical benefit and should be quantified. The 1.2× speedup figure is measured at batch=32, seqlen=4096; Figure 1 shows sparse GEMM underperforms dense at small M values, so the speedup under decode-phase autoregressive conditions (batch=1, M=1) would likely be much smaller or negative, yet this is not reported. The Wanda-style independence assumption for SpenseGPT+ scores is acknowledged but not quantified — it is unclear how much accuracy is left on the table compared to an oracle dense-index selection. No cross-model generalization study (e.g., Llama, Mistral families) is presented, limiting confidence in the method's breadth. Bottom Line: A practitioner deploying 30–40B reasoning LLMs on NVIDIA B200 hardware gains a concrete, library-compatible path to ~1.2× serving speedup with near-zero accuracy loss in under 2.2 GPU-hours — making this paper directly actionable for LLM inference optimization work targeting Blackwell-generation hardware.
Search Results 2606.10445 PDF
#15 — Highly Innovative (75)

From Layers to Submodules: Rethinking Granularity in Replacement-Based LLM Compression

E Cunegatti, M Vukojevic, E Nielsen, G Iacca · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SUBFIT, a post-training LLM compression method that replaces Attention and FFN submodules independently and non-contiguously with analytically fitted low-rank residual bypasses, operating below the full-layer granularity assumed by all prior replacement-based methods. - Why it's important: Deployers who need real inference speedup without sparse kernels or retraining are currently forced to accept layer-level removal, which wastes the fact that redundancy distributes unevenly across submodule types and depths; SUBFIT directly addresses this inefficiency. - How it does it: Sequential scoring selects low-impact Attention submodules via a cosine-magnitude impact score, then FFN submodules on the partially compressed model; each removed submodule is replaced by an affine-plus-low-rank surrogate whose parameters are recovered in closed form from calibration statistics, with a single input basis shared across all selected FFN layers. - The good: At 25% sparsity, SUBFIT achieves 2.42× aggregate PPL degradation and 84.6% downstream accuracy retention versus 4.34× and 81.6% for the strongest baseline (ReplaceMe Cosine), with the PPL margin growing from 0.11× at 12.5% to 5.69× at 37.5% sparsity. - The missing: All experiments are confined to 3B–8B-parameter models; whether non-contiguous submodule selection remains advantageous at 70B+ scale — where attention redundancy patterns may differ — is entirely unevaluated. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners working on deployable LLM compression at the 3B–8B scale; the closed-form fitting, real A100 speedup measurements, and thorough ablations make this immediately applicable, though the 70B+ gap limits confident extrapolation.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (78) drives the score: code is released, calibration is disjoint from evaluation, and ablations are thorough and well-targeted (residual components, shared vs. per-layer basis, rank sensitivity, scoring criterion) across 10 models / 5 sparsity levels / 13 tasks — unusually broad for this literature; docked for no seeds and preprint status. Completeness (80) is strong with quantitatively detailed, directly confronted limitations. Novelty (72) and Non-Obviousness (75) reflect the genuinely new sub-layer non-contiguous granularity with shared FFN basis and the actionable masked-vs-post-replacement disagreement finding, built from standard components. Resilience (72) is capped by the untested 70B+ scale and absent quantization comparison. Impact (70) — released code plus promised checkpoints. Weighted: 0.20*72+0.25*78+0.15*75+0.15*80+0.15*72+0.10*70 = 75.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 78 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 80 Resilience 72 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Deploying LLMs under memory and latency constraints requires parameter reduction that translates to real inference speedup. Quantization and unstructured pruning require custom hardware or sparse kernels to realize speedup. Structured removal of full transformer components achieves direct speedup, but naively deleting layers collapses the residual stream with sharp quality loss. A recent line of replacement-based methods addresses this by fitting lightweight modules at removed locations — but every such method treats the full transformer layer as the atomic removal unit and restricts selection to contiguous depth ranges. Both constraints are empirically unwarranted: Attention submodules are more redundant than FFN submodules and their removable instances are scattered across depth; contiguous layer blocks conflate two different redundancy profiles. The result is unnecessary quality loss at any given compression budget, particularly at aggressive sparsity levels. Key Idea The core insight is that Attention and FFN submodules have fundamentally different residual contribution structures — Attention outputs can be approximated by a low-rank (rank-256) linear map because they contribute small, near-identity rotations, while FFN outputs require a higher-rank (rank-4096) map but share a common input subspace across selected layers that can be factored into a single shared basis — so treating each submodule type as a distinct, independently selectable and independently approximated removal target, without requiring spatial contiguity, captures the actual distribution of redundancy better than any layer-level method. Methodology SUBFIT operates post-training in two sequential stages. Given a pretrained decoder-only transformer with L layers and target sparsity s, it selects round(Ls) Attention and round(Ls) FFN submodules independently. Stage 1 (Attention selection and replacement): Each Attention submodule is scored by I(h, δ) = (1 − cos(h, h+δ)) × (‖δ‖₂/(‖h‖₂ + ε)), which multiplies a cosine-rotation factor (small when the submodule acts near-identity) by a relative magnitude factor (small when the contribution is negligible). The round(Ls) lowest-scoring submodules are selected non-contiguously. Each is replaced by a surrogate F̃_ℓ(x) = g_ℓ ⊙ x + b_ℓ + (x − μ_ℓ)V_ℓ^T W_ℓ, where g_ℓ ∈ ℝ^d is a per-feature affine gate, b_ℓ is a bias, μ_ℓ is input centering, and V_ℓ, W_ℓ ∈ ℝ^{r×d} form a rank-r factorized interaction term. All parameters are recovered analytically: μ_ℓ and b_ℓ from empirical means (decoupling affine and low-rank components via Frisch-Waugh-Lovell), g_ℓ by per-feature ridge-regularized univariate regression, V_ℓ from the top-r eigenvectors of the centered input covariance, and W_ℓ by ridge least-squares on the projected residual. Stage 2 (FFN selection and replacement): FFN submodules are scored on the post-Attention-replacement activations to account for residual-stream shift. Base models use a replacement-aware score combining masking effect and surrogate residual error; instruction-tuned models use cosine-only scoring (norm-based factors are noisy post-SFT). The FFN surrogate uses the same structure but with a single input basis V ∈ ℝ^{r×d} shared across all selected FFN layers — estimated from joint calibration statistics — while W_ℓ, g_ℓ, b_ℓ, μ_ℓ remain layer-specific. This reduces basis storage by a factor of |S_fn| and reduces per-layer FFN surrogate parameters by roughly 10× relative to the original FFN block. Three critical design decisions: (1) Sequential Attention-first ordering is justified by the empirical observation that attention is more redundant and that FFN scoring on post-replacement activations accounts for residual-stream shift; this choice is not directly ablated against reversed or joint ordering. (2) Shared FFN input basis is justified and directly ablated in Table 6, showing negligible quality difference versus per-layer basis. (3) Asymmetric ranks (256 for Attention, 4096 for FFN) are justified by the rank sensitivity ablation in Figure 3 showing clear saturation points. Key Results SUBFIT is compared against four calibration-only replacement baselines — Streamline (FFN) and Streamline (Layer) (Chen et al., ICLR 2025) and ReplaceMe (LS) and ReplaceMe (Cosine) (Shopkhoev et al., NeurIPS 2025) — on five base models (Llama-3.2-3B, Qwen3-4B, Llama-3.1-8B, Qwen3-8B, DeepSeek-7B) and five instruction-tuned variants across five sparsity levels. Perplexity (base models): SUBFIT achieves the lowest PPL degradation in 9 of 10 model–sparsity settings at 12.5% and 25%. The advantage is most pronounced on the Qwen family: at 25% sparsity, SUBFIT reaches 2.54× on Qwen3-4B and 3.18× on Qwen3-8B versus 5.37× and 6.80× for the strongest baseline. Aggregate PPL factor at 25%: SUBFIT 2.42× (±0.40) versus ReplaceMe Cosine 4.34× (±1.40). At 37.5%, SUBFIT is 5.76× (±1.37) versus ReplaceMe Cosine 22.28× (±130.68). Exceptions: on Qwen3-8B at 30% and DeepSeek-7B at some sparsities, SUBFIT and ReplaceMe Cosine are competitive. Downstream accuracy (instruction-tuned, 25%): SUBFIT achieves best aggregate on 4 of 5 models; only exception is Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct where ReplaceMe Cosine is marginally higher (+0.11%). Average accuracy retention: SUBFIT 84.6% versus 81.6% for best baseline at 25%; 73.9% versus 70.9% at 37.5%. Inference efficiency on a single NVIDIA A100 80GB at 25% sparsity: TTFT speedup 1.18×–1.40×, decode speedup 1.12×–1.17×, KV-cache reduction proportional to sparsity (25% Attention submodules removed). No hardware-specific optimization applied. The 13-task evaluation suite (HellaSwag, PIQA, Winogrande, OpenBookQA, SocialIQA, BoolQ, TruthfulQA, MMLU, ARC-C, ARC-E, MathQA, SciQ, GSM8K) uses LM Evaluation Harness zero/few-shot. Baselines are current and strong. Scale is moderate (3B–8B only). Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. SUBFIT is the first replacement-based compression method to operate at sub-layer (Attention/FFN submodule) granularity with non-contiguous selection and type-specific fitted bypasses using a shared FFN input basis. The individual components — low-rank approximation, ridge regression, cosine-based importance scoring — are standard, but their composition at sub-layer granularity with a closed-form fitting procedure and cross-layer FFN basis sharing has not appeared in this form. The empirical finding that masked-degradation scoring and post-replacement quality disagree in 5 of 8 tested cases is also a novel and actionable empirical observation. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are thorough and targeted: residual compensation components (Table 5 — isolates which compensation type drives quality recovery), shared vs. per-layer FFN basis (Table 6), rank sensitivity by submodule type (Figure 3), replacement-aware vs. masked scoring (Table 7), calibration set size (Figure A1). No error bars or multiple seeds are reported; the paper states a fixed random seed but does not quantify variance across runs. GSM8K is correctly isolated from the main aggregate with explicit justification (instability under compression). Code is available at https://github.com/eliacunegatti/SubFit; compressed checkpoints announced as post-review release. The calibration data (SlimPajama, SlimOrca, 8k sequences at 1024 tokens) does not overlap with any evaluation benchmark. This is an arXiv preprint (arXiv:2606.02559v1, June 2026); results have not been peer reviewed. The key methodological concern is that the FFN scoring criterion differs between base and instruction-tuned models, with the instruction-tuned choice (cosine-only) motivated by a single citation rather than an ablation — the paper does not directly show that cosine-only beats replacement-aware scoring for instruction-tuned FFN selection. Deployability (EMPIRICAL) - Practical: Runs on a single NVIDIA A100 80GB GPU with no sparse kernels or custom hardware; compression cost is ~2,000–2,600 seconds once offline, after which the compressed model runs on standard inference stacks. - Efficient: Adds ~10% of removed parameters and ~15% of removed MACs at 25% sparsity as permanent inference overhead — more than ReplaceMe (which adds zero deployed parameters by folding into existing weights), but substantially less than Streamline variants. - Effective: Direct evidence — perplexity on Lambada/C4/WikiText-2, 13-task downstream accuracy, and TTFT/decode speedup measured on real hardware — consistently shows SUBFIT solves the stated problem better than baselines, with the margin widening at aggressive sparsity. - Scalable: Tested only at 3B–8B; the closed-form eigen-decomposition scales as O(d²) in hidden dimension, which may be costly for models with d ≥ 8192. No experiments at 13B–70B; scale generalization is claimed but not tested. Delivers: code repository; compressed model checkpoints (post-review). Strengths - The asymmetric approximation insight — Attention contributions are low-rank-approximable at rank 256 while FFN contributions share a common input subspace requiring rank 4096 — is non-obvious a priori and is directly validated by the rank sensitivity ablation (Figure 3) and residual compensation decomposition (Table 5). - Closed-form fitting with no gradient-based optimization is a genuine practical advantage: no learning-rate tuning, no GPU memory for backward passes, and compression is fully reproducible from calibration statistics alone. - The empirical demonstration that masked-degradation scoring and post-replacement quality disagree in 5 of 8 cases (Table 7) challenges an implicit assumption widespread in structured pruning literature and motivates the replacement-aware scoring design. - Evaluation breadth — 10 models across 3 families, 5 sparsity levels, 13 downstream tasks, and real-hardware inference benchmarking — substantially exceeds the typical two-model, two-sparsity evaluation in this literature. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state that SUBFIT adds ~10% of removed parameters and ~15% of removed MACs as deployed bypass overhead (Tables A9/A10), unlike ReplaceMe which absorbs its transformation into existing weights at zero overhead. Offline compression takes 2,000–2,600 seconds, slower than Streamline. These limitations are confronted directly and with quantitative detail, which is credible. The authors do not acknowledge the scale limitation (3B–8B only) or the missing comparison against quantization baselines. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The evaluation is confined to 3B–8B-parameter models. At 70B+ scale, attention redundancy patterns and inter-layer transition linearity may differ; the key claim that non-contiguous selection outperforms contiguous has not been demonstrated at those scales, which is where deployment pressure is often greatest. - No comparison against quantization-based methods (e.g., AWQ, GPTQ) at equivalent deployed memory footprint. A practitioner choosing between 25% structural compression and 4-bit quantization needs this comparison; its absence leaves the most practically relevant question unanswered. - The Attention-first sequential ordering is motivated by citing prior work on attention redundancy but is never directly ablated against FFN-first or joint ordering; the choice may not be optimal for all model families. - For instruction-tuned models, FFN scoring uses a cosine-only criterion motivated by a cited observation about SFT magnitude calibration, but no ablation directly shows cosine-only outperforms replacement-aware scoring in this setting. - No evaluation on generation quality metrics (e.g., MT-Bench, AlpacaEval) that better reflect instruction-following quality for the instruction-tuned variants; classification-benchmark accuracy may not capture degradation in fluency or instruction adherence. Bottom Line SUBFIT delivers a clear and well-evidenced improvement over current replacement-based LLM compression baselines at the 3B–8B scale, with clean ablations and immediately usable code — the outstanding question for practitioners targeting larger deployments is whether the advantage survives at 70B+, which the paper does not address.
Recommended 2606.02559 PDF
#16 — Highly Innovative (75)

OpenSkill: Open-World Self-Evolution for LLM Agents

Z Yan, D Song, H Zhang, W Liang, Y Zhang, Y Dai… · arxiv.org · automated generated environments for agent testing - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces OpenSkill, a three-stage framework that enables LLM agents to build both reusable task skills and their own verification signals from scratch using only a task prompt and publicly accessible resources (documentation, repositories, the web), with no target-task supervision during learning. - Why it's important: Real deployed agents rarely have access to curated skill libraries, successful execution traces, or reward oracles, yet all prior self-evolving agent systems assume at least one of these; OpenSkill is the first to close that gap under a formally enforced no-supervision constraint. - How it does it: A deep-research agent retrieves grounded knowledge and independently verifiable anchors from the open web; a separate LLM verifier synthesizes deterministic pytest assertions from those anchors (never from GT tests) to form a proxy test suite; the agent refines its skill files against that proxy in an iterative sandbox loop before zero-shot deployment. - The good: On SkillsBench (84 tasks, 11 domains), OpenSkill achieves 43.6% / 42.1% pass rate on Opus 4.6 / GPT 5.2 versus the strongest baselines' 34.7% / 33.3%, approaching the human ceiling of 44.5% / 44.8%, while skills generated by one model transfer to four weaker models with 5.5–14.8 pp gains and zero retraining. - The missing: The Manufacturing domain collapses to 0% for every automated method including OpenSkill without explanation; verifier precision is only 56.9% (60.7% overall agreement with GT outcomes), and the key ablations are performed on a single benchmark-agent pair (SocialMaze, Opus 4.6). - Read it or not: Researchers building agents that must adapt to new domains at deployment should read this—the supervision-free formulation is principled, the leakage audit is unusually thorough, and the cross-model transfer result is directly practical—but the ~\$1,800-per-benchmark compute cost and unexplained Manufacturing failure warrant caution before adoption.
Why this rank: Empirical profile (built pipeline scored by agentic pass-rate). Novelty (85) is the top contributor — a genuine NEW PROBLEM formulation (supervision-free open-world self-evolution with a formally enforced leakage constraint) plus a new integrated method, the strongest novelty in this cluster. Non-Obviousness (80) is high: the insight that the open web supplies independently verifiable anchors to ground a proxy verifier without touching ground truth is non-trivial. Rigor (70) is good — code released, four-layer leakage audit, two model families, three benchmarks, seven baselines, Fisher's exact test — but tempered by ablations confined to a single SocialMaze/Opus pair, no error bars, a backbone version confound, and benchmark circularity on a 2026 preprint SkillsBench. Completeness (72) and Resilience (70) are solid but the undiagnosed Manufacturing 0% collapse and 56.9% verifier precision are real soft spots. Practical Impact (75) reflects the released Dockerized artifact and the directly useful cross-model transfer (5.5-14.8 pp, zero retraining). This matches the user's positive calibration (principled formulation, thorough auditing). Composite: 0.20*85+0.25*70+0.15*80+0.15*72+0.15*70+0.10*75 = 75.
empirical Novelty 85 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 72 Resilience 70 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation LLM agents deployed to solve open-ended tasks (software engineering, scientific analysis, cybersecurity) require procedural knowledge that changes across environments and task types. Existing self-evolving systems—Voyager, Reflexion, AutoSkill, SkillRL, Skill Creator—assume access to at least one of: human-curated skills, interaction traces from successful episodes, or a reward/verifier signal during the learning phase. In real deployments none of these may be available; the agent receives only a natural-language task instruction. A harder structural gap also exists: prior self-improvement loops that do use external feedback risk leaking target-task supervision if that feedback is derived from ground-truth tests or solutions. No prior work simultaneously handles cold-start skill acquisition from the open web and builds a supervision-free verification signal, making iterative self-improvement without a grader impossible outside curated benchmarks. Key Idea The core technical insight is that the public internet contains not only the domain knowledge needed to construct skills but also independently verifiable facts—known dataset statistics, documented API output formats, reference values for standard computations—that can anchor a proxy test suite without ever touching hidden ground-truth answers. By retrieving these two orthogonal knowledge types (skill content and verification anchors) in separate passes, OpenSkill builds a self-contained learning loop: the verification anchors ground deterministic pytest assertions that serve as a reliable proxy verifier, and the same open-world resources that teach the skill also supply the criteria for judging it, closing the loop with no target-task supervision. Methodology The pipeline runs in three stages. Stage 1 (Open-World Knowledge Acquisition): An LLM Agent Planner submits a sanitized query (benchmark name filtered out) to a commercial deep-research agent (Gemini deep-research-pro-preview-12-2025, polled asynchronously up to 3600 s) to retrieve background knowledge k_i; a second, lighter pass (gemini-3.1-flash-lite, temperature 0.3) retrieves verification knowledge k_v_i consisting of four anchor classes: hand-computable reference values, dataset statistical invariants (row counts, value ranges, sum-to-one constraints), cross-validation procedures, and known input–output pairs from published benchmarks. A Skill Planner then decomposes k_i into 1–4 SKILL.md documents. Stage 2 (Leakage-Free Skill Evolution): An Independent Verifier—a separate LLM session sharing the Docker container but not the skill creator's conversation—generates a deterministic pytest suite from k_v_i without seeing the GT test directory (tests/ and solution/ are never mounted). The skill creator iterates up to J=3 rounds: when the virtual pass rate r̃ < 1, a Gemini-based LLM classifier diagnoses each failure as SELF-FIXABLE (implementation bug) or NEEDS-DR (knowledge gap), triggering targeted web retrieval in the latter case (capped at 3 searches per task). Stage 3 (Zero-Shot Evaluation): The frozen skill set is copied to the target agent's workspace and evaluated under 5 independent runs against the hidden GT test suite. Three critical design decisions: (1) the leakage barrier is enforced at four independent layers (code API, container filesystem, GT oracle bypass, prompt instructions), which is unusually rigorous and well-justified; (2) deterministic equality assertions anchored to independently verifiable facts are a principled choice but restrict coverage to what the open web can confirm, explaining the 11.1% uncovered GT intents; (3) the J=3 refinement cap is empirically validated via ablation showing performance degrades at J=5 and J=10, suggesting proxy overfitting. Key Results On SkillsBench (primary benchmark, 84 tasks, 11 domains): Opus 4.6—OpenSkill 43.6% vs. Skill-Creator 34.7% (+8.9 pp), Self-Gen 23.9%, AutoSkill 24.7%, Memento 30.1%; human ceiling 44.5%. GPT 5.2—OpenSkill 42.1% vs. CoT 33.3% (+8.8 pp), Skill-Creator 29.2%, AutoSkill 11.2%, Memento 15.6%; human ceiling 44.8%. OpenSkill is best or tied-best in 8/11 domains on Opus 4.6, 7/11 on GPT 5.2. Largest domain gains—Opus: Health 69.6% (vs. 31.2% next best), Software 59.9%; GPT: Energy 80.0% (above human at 53.3%), Cybersecurity 52.5%. Manufacturing collapses to 0% for all automated methods on both agents. On SocialMaze: 82.7% (Opus) and 70.7% (GPT), +0.9 to +2.2 pp over best baselines. On ScienceWorld: 90.0% (Opus) and 85.3% (GPT). Cross-model transfer (Opus 4.6 skills deployed without adaptation on Haiku 4.5, Qwen 3 Coder, DeepSeek V3, Mistral Large 3): improvements over no-skill of 5.5–14.8 pp; AutoSkill skills transfer worse than no-skill on the same models. Virtual verifier quality: 88.9% coverage of GT test intents (120/135) on 15 sampled tasks; 56.9% precision, 80.5% recall, 60.7% agreement with GT on N=84 tasks; Fisher's exact test OR=2.97, p=0.035. Ablation on SocialMaze: open-world query alone +6.1 pp, virtual verifier alone +6.3 pp, both combined +8.2 pp over parametric-only baseline. Novelty Classification NEW PROBLEM + NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The open-world self-evolution problem formulation—building both skills and verification signal from public resources with no target-task supervision—is a genuinely new setting with a formal definition and an enforced leakage constraint. The specific coupling of deep-web retrieval → skill synthesis → verification-anchor-grounded proxy tests is new as a complete system; individual components (RAG, iterative skill refinement, self-generated test suites) each exist in prior work, but their integration to realize a supervision-free learning loop is the genuine contribution. Code and pipeline are publicly released. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations cover iteration count (J ∈ {1,3,5,10}) and component contribution (no components, +VV, +DR, +both), but exclusively on SocialMaze with Opus 4.6—a single benchmark-agent pair—raising questions about generality of the J=3 optimum. No error bars or significance tests are reported on the main SkillsBench and ScienceWorld tables; the 5 independent evaluation runs per task reduce variance but no confidence intervals are given. The virtual verifier alignment study uses Fisher's exact test (p=0.035), providing appropriate statistical grounding for the proxy signal claim. Code is released at https://github.com/OpenLAIR/OpenSkill. A methodological concern: third-party baselines (Skill Creator, AutoSkill, Memento) use claude-opus-4-7 as their backbone, while OpenSkill uses claude-opus-4-6; the version difference is documented but not ablated, and could introduce minor confounds. The primary benchmark (SkillsBench) is itself a 2026 preprint specifically designed so skill quality is the bottleneck, which may inflate OpenSkill's relative advantage on it. This is a preprint; results and conclusions have not undergone peer review. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS (a built pipeline evaluated by pass rate on agentic benchmarks). - Practical: Requires access to Gemini deep-research-pro (commercial API), Claude Opus 4.6 or GPT 5.2, and Docker; per-task cost is ~1.14M tokens and ~131 minutes of wall-clock time, totaling ~\$1,800 at list pricing for the 84-task SkillsBench run—prohibitive for most academic labs but potentially acceptable as a one-time skill-creation cost. - Efficient: Skill creation dominates token budget (749K of 1.14M tokens) but skill reuse across models incurs zero creation cost; evaluation-only median run time (368.2 s) is comparable to the no-skill baseline (347.6 s), making deployed skill use cost-neutral at inference time. - Effective: Achieves 43.6%/42.1% vs. human ceiling 44.5%/44.8% on SkillsBench, with consistent gains across two model families and three benchmarks; the complete Manufacturing failure (0%) indicates a hard and unresolved failure mode for at least one task type. - Scalable: Not tested beyond 84 tasks; the one-time creation + reuse argument is plausible but unvalidated at scale; the deep-research API imposes a 3600 s hard timeout per task, which limits throughput. Delivers a software artifact: GitHub repository with Docker-containerized pipeline and SKILL.md skill files as portable deployment artifacts. Strengths - The leakage-free constraint is formally defined and audited at four independent layers (code API, container filesystem, GT oracle bypass, prompt-level instructions), making the no-supervision claim unusually credible compared to prior self-evolving agent work. - The virtual verifier's reliance on independently verifiable anchors (reference values, statistical invariants) rather than LLM-generated guesses is a principled mechanism that achieves 88.9% GT intent coverage without accessing GT tests—a concrete empirical validation of the proxy signal's quality. - Cross-model transferability (same SKILL.md files improve four weaker models by 5.5–14.8 pp with zero retraining) validates the model-agnostic artifact design and provides an immediately practical deployment path. - The breadth of evaluation—two architecturally distinct LLM families, three benchmarks spanning agentic coding, social reasoning, and interactive science, seven automated baselines with identical evaluation harnesses—sets a high bar for comparative rigor in this space. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly flag three limitations: (1) open-world sources may be noisy, outdated, or contradictory, requiring provenance tracking; (2) virtual tasks may overestimate skill quality if too easy, or reintroduce supervision if inadvertently derived from hidden answers; (3) open-world retrieval increases cost and latency over closed-world methods. These are stated directly in a dedicated section, which is a credibility positive. The authors do not acknowledge the Manufacturing domain failure, the verifier's 56.9% precision, or the narrow scope of ablations as limitations. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The Manufacturing domain collapse to 0% for every automated method is never diagnosed; no ablation, case study, or hypothesis is offered, leaving a visible failure mode entirely unexplained. - Virtual verifier precision of 56.9% (29.76% of all tasks are false positives: proxy pass, GT fail) is not treated as a limitation; roughly one-third of skills that pass virtual tests fail GT evaluation, and it is unclear whether the J=3 refinement cap adequately contains the resulting proxy overfitting. - Component ablations are performed only on SocialMaze (Opus 4.6); whether the optimal J=3 and the +8.2 pp combined gain replicate on SkillsBench or with GPT 5.2 is not shown. - The backbone version discrepancy (baselines: claude-opus-4-7; OpenSkill: claude-opus-4-6) is documented but unablated; a version-matched baseline would strengthen causal claims. - SkillsBench is specifically engineered so skill quality is the bottleneck, creating a potential circularity: the primary benchmark is designed to reward exactly what OpenSkill optimizes, and it is a 2026 preprint rather than an established community standard. - No wall-clock or token breakdown is reported for baselines, making it impossible to evaluate whether the +8.9 pp gain justifies the roughly 10–100× increase in compute cost over closed-world methods. Bottom Line A practitioner building agents that must adapt to new task domains at deployment time will gain genuine, actionable insight from OpenSkill's supervision-free formulation, leakage audit methodology, and cross-model transfer results—but should weigh the substantial compute cost, the unresolved Manufacturing failure, and the noisy proxy signal against the performance gains before committing to the approach.
Search Results 2606.06741 PDF
#17 — Highly Innovative (75)

AugMask: Training Diffusion Models on Incomplete Tabular Data via Stochastic Augmentation and Masking

J Kim, T Park, K Lee · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces AugMask, a plug-and-play training framework that adapts missing-unaware score-based diffusion models to incomplete tabular data by filling missing entries with stochastic conditional samples (augmentation) while restricting the denoising loss to originally observed coordinates (masking). - Why it's important: Real-world tabular datasets ubiquitously contain missing values, yet every major diffusion-based tabular generator (TabDDPM, TabDiff, CDTD, TabSyn) requires fully specified inputs, blocking their direct application to practical settings. - How it does it: Per-feature LightGBM models estimate conditional mean and heteroskedastic variance for each missing entry; samples from these distributions serve as input context but are excluded from the denoising loss, with the training objective connected theoretically to a Rao-Blackwellized marginalization that yields a variance-weighted sensitivity penalty. - The good: On 90 MCAR scenarios (6 datasets × 5 missing ratios × 3 seeds), AugMask+TabDiff achieves average Summary Rank 3.2 and AugMask+CDTD achieves 3.8, both statistically superior (Wilcoxon p<0.05) to all four specialized missing-aware baselines including ForestDiff and MIWAE; at pmiss=0.9, CDTD+AugMask achieves IoU=0.929 on UMAP density overlap vs. 0.157 for the NoAug baseline. - The missing: MNAR (Missing Not At Random) is entirely absent from evaluation, and the method is explicitly restricted to feature-space diffusion — latent diffusion architectures (e.g., TabSyn) can use AugFull but not AugMask. - Read it or not: Essential reading for practitioners working on tabular data synthesis under incomplete data, and worth reading for researchers interested in how stochastic augmentation connects to Rao-Blackwellization and implicit sensitivity regularization.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (82) and Novelty (75) drive the score: a clean conditioning/supervision separation principle backed by a Rao-Blackwellized variance-weighted sensitivity analysis (Proposition 3.3 with full proof), validated across 180 scenarios with Wilcoxon testing, well-structured ablations isolating masking vs. stochasticity, a misspecification sweep, and released code. The result statistically surpasses all specialized missing-aware baselines. Non-Obviousness (75) high — stochasticity beyond imputation accuracy is a concrete mechanistic prediction confirmed empirically. Completeness (75) and Resilience (72) strong but bounded by the entire absence of MNAR evaluation, the unanalyzed News-dataset β-Recall anomaly, and no privacy audit. Impact (65) from a plug-and-play recipe (no library/weights). Resembles the positive set's theory+rigor profile.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 82 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 75 Resilience 72 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Deep generative models for tabular data are routinely applied to settings with missing values, but all standard neural network-based architectures assume fully observed inputs and cannot process NaN entries. Existing workarounds fall into two failure modes: constant placeholder filling (zeros, column means, special tokens) forces the denoiser to condition on a deterministic signal correlated with missingness, creating artificial input patterns and sparsity bias; EM-style self-imputation (e.g., DiffPuter) updates missing entries during training using the model's own evolving predictions, but because diffusion objectives seek diversity rather than accuracy, the completions are overly dispersed and create a moving training target with noisy gradients. Neither approach cleanly separates the role of missing entries as conditioning context (what the model receives as input) from their role as training targets (what the model is supervised to reproduce). The field of tabular data generation — servicing data scarcity and privacy in healthcare, finance, and social science — therefore lacks a principled approach to training from incomplete observations. Key Idea Missing entries should provide uncertain conditioning context for the denoiser, not serve as supervised targets. The key technical insight is that sampling plausible completions from a conditional distribution T(·|x_obs, m) and masking the loss to observed coordinates decouples these two roles. The stochasticity of the augmentation is not incidental: a second-order Taylor expansion of the marginalized per-sample Rao-Blackwellized objective reveals that the conditional variance σ²_j of the augmenter induces a term σ²_j ||φ̄'_θ(μ_j)||² — a variance-weighted sensitivity penalty that penalizes the denoiser for relying too strongly on uncertain completions. Deterministic imputation (zero variance) omits this penalty entirely; stochastic augmentation activates it, adapting the denoiser's reliance on context to its conditional uncertainty. Methodology AugMask is a two-step training strategy applied to any score-based diffusion backbone operating in feature space. Step 1 — Stochastic Augmentation (Algorithm 1): For each feature j missing in a training sample i, draw z_ij from a factorized conditional T_F. For continuous features, fit a LightGBM regressor for conditional mean μ̂_ij and a second regressor on log-squared residuals for heteroskedastic variance σ̂²_ij (Harvey 1976), then sample z_ij ~ N(μ̂_ij, σ̂²_ij). For categorical features, fit a LightGBM classifier for conditional probabilities p̂_ij, apply label smoothing (α=0.05), and sample categorically. The augmented dataset D^A is constructed once offline with default LightGBM hyperparameters (no tuning) and kept fixed during all diffusion training epochs. Conditioning includes the observation mask pattern m_{i,−j} directly. Step 2 — Observed-Only Loss: Optimize L^AugMask = E[m ⊙ ||D_θ(x^{A,t}, t) − x^{A,0}||²], where m is the binary observation mask. The augmented values enter through the noisy input x^{A,t} but are excluded from the loss. For comparison, AugFull uses the same augmented inputs but applies the loss to all coordinates; NoAug uses constant mean/mode filling with no masking. Theoretical analysis: Proposition 3.3 proves (under local smoothness and finite third moment conditions) that the Rao-Blackwellized objective decomposes as l^RB ≈ ||e_θ(μ_j)||² + σ²_j ||φ̄'_θ(μ_j)||², where the second term is the variance-weighted sensitivity penalty. The multivariate extension (Eq. 15–16) decomposes into a diagonal term (captured by factorized augmentation) and a cross-coordinate correction term Δ bounded by co-missingness rate × denoiser coupling × conditional correlation; the factorized approximation is most accurate when co-missing features are weakly correlated or coupled. Key design decisions: (i) LightGBM with defaults — well-justified as gradient-boosted trees natively handle mixed-type tabular data and accept the missingness mask as context; (ii) fix augmentation at the start rather than cycling — empirically shown to preserve recall (Table 5), with theoretical consistency argued from the variance estimation sufficiency; (iii) factorized per-feature augmentation — theoretically principled for the diagonal sensitivity terms, with bounded error for the cross-coordinate terms.
