Without this comparison, the claim that per-GQA-group block selection is superior to per-head token-level selection (DSA) or large-block GQA selection (MoBA) is an architectural argument, not an empirical result. - The 8-point EgoSchema gain and 12.8-point VisualWebBench gain for MSA-PT over Full-Attention at equal training budget are extraordinary for a method that reduces attended context and warrant a dedicated analysis; the paper offers no explanation. Random variance in a single training run cannot be ruled out. - Decoding analysis is thin: the 7.6× speedup is stated but the kernel design section focuses almost entirely on prefill (KV-outer, pre-scheduled chunking). Decoding with incremental KV cache has different access patterns and its implementation details are not described. - The block-size ablation (Table 4) shows RULER-32K degrading from 66.1 (B_k=32) to 64.6 (B_k=128), a -1.5 point drop the paper dismisses as minor; at longer contexts (128K–1M) where retrieval precision matters more, this coarseness trade-off could be more damaging and is not evaluated there. - Cross-architecture validation is absent: whether the training recipe (specific k, B_k, warmup length, KL coefficient λ) transfers to models with different GQA ratios, head dimensions, or non-MoE feedforward structure is an open question. Bottom Line Engineers building or retrofitting GQA-based long-context LLMs for 100K–1M token inference should read this paper — it delivers a production-validated, open-sourced algorithm-kernel co-design with unusually honest ablations, but should verify the training recipe on their own architecture before adoption, since all quality evidence comes from MiniMax's proprietary 109B MoE stack with no peer-method comparisons.
Recommended 2606.13392 PDF
#23 — Innovative (73)

G-HEMP: FAST MULTI-GPU PRIVATE INFERENCE FOR LARGE-SCALE GCNS WITH HOMOMORPHIC ENCRYPTION

R Ran, Z Gong, Z Li, X Lu, J Li, W Wen · openreview.net · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces G-HEMP, a multi-GPU framework for privacy-preserving GCN inference under CKKS homomorphic encryption, combining a block-diagonal parallel packing scheme (BDPP) with a graph-aware ciphertext partitioning policy (GP-X) that together reduce memory overhead and enable near-linear multi-GPU scaling. - Why it's important: Outsourcing GCN inference to the cloud over sensitive graph data (medical records, financial networks) demands end-to-end encryption, but existing HE approaches either run out of GPU memory on graphs with ~20K nodes or, when naively distributed across GPUs, actually increase latency by >50% due to key-switching data dependencies. - How it does it: BDPP packs feature ciphertexts along block-diagonals of f×f sub-matrices so the adjacency matrix A requires zero f-fold replication (reducing its memory footprint from O(nf) to O(n)), while GP-X partitions both A and X across GPUs at whole-ciphertext granularity, eliminating the inter-GPU synchronization bottleneck that makes limb-level partitioning counterproductive. - The good: On 4 NVIDIA A100 GPUs, GP-X achieves 3.63–3.88× speedup over single-GPU latency across all three benchmarks, while the prior state-of-the-art multi-GPU method (Cinnamon's limb-level partition) degrades to 0.3× of single-GPU performance under identical conditions. - The missing: All evaluations use graphs with at most 19,717 nodes; real-world graphs in recommendation and biomedical domains routinely reach millions of nodes, and the claimed scalability to 16 GPUs is not experimentally validated. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers and engineers building privacy-preserving ML inference pipelines on GPU clusters; the BDPP insight (matching rotation complexity to Penguin while eliminating adjacency matrix replication) is clean and the multi-GPU partitioning analysis is rigorous, though practical impact is currently limited to small graphs.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Practical Impact (66) reflects a real multi-GPU framework on Liberate-FHE delivering near-linear scaling, though absolute latency is minutes-scale and capped at ~20K-node graphs. Novelty (80) and Non-Obviousness (78) are high: BDPP is a genuine Pareto advance — O(n) adjacency memory at O(n+2f) rotation complexity simultaneously, where prior work (Penguin) traded one against the other, and the whole-ciphertext-granularity insight for GP-X is a clean root-cause fix for why limb-level partitioning fails. Rigor (75) is strong with analytic+empirical complexity verification, current strong baselines (Penguin NeurIPS'23, Cinnamon ASPLOS'25), and MLSys 2026 peer review, though the 'dup' Penguin variant is partly apples-to-oranges and code is unreleased. Completeness (70) and Resilience (72) are good but bounded by the small-graph, link-prediction-only scope. Weighted: 0.15*80+0.15*75+0.10*78+0.15*70+0.15*72+0.30*66 = 73.
systems Novelty 80 Rigor 75 Non-Obv 78 Completeness 70 Resilience 72 Impact 66
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Cloud-based GCN inference on sensitive graph data — social networks, medical records, financial transactions — requires that both node features and graph topology remain private from the server. Homomorphic encryption (HE) enables the server to compute on ciphertexts without decrypting, but the dominant bottleneck is the encrypted AXW computation (adjacency–feature–weight matrix chain). Prior CPU-focused HE-GCN methods (Penguin, NeurIPS'23) achieve asymptotically efficient packing but require duplicating the encrypted adjacency matrix f times (once per feature), causing a 19,717-node graph's encrypted A to consume 60 GB — more than a single H100/A100 GPU's memory capacity. Naive multi-GPU extensions using limb-level partitioning (Cinnamon, ASPLOS'25) fragment each ciphertext's RNS limbs across devices, triggering expensive key-switching communication at every CMult and Rotation, empirically increasing total latency by over 50% compared to a single GPU. Key Idea: The core insight is that the f-fold adjacency matrix replication required by prior packing (to match the feature packing layout of X) is avoidable if X is instead packed along block-diagonals of f×f sub-matrices. In this BDPP layout, each f×f block's elements come from different nodes and features, making the blocks independent; the adjacency matrix can therefore be packed at the same granularity without replication, reducing its memory cost from O(nf) to O(n) while preserving O(n+2f) rotation complexity. For multi-GPU distribution, the second key insight is that the minimum correct partition granularity is one complete ciphertext (not a limb), so that key-switching operations execute locally within each GPU without triggering cross-device data transfers. Methodology: G-HEMP operates in two phases. First, BDPP packs input features X into a 3D ciphertext tensor (n'×f'×f): for each collection of n f×f sub-matrices along the node dimension, it extracts the k-th diagonal from each block, concatenates them, and encrypts the result. The adjacency matrix A is packed identically. This eliminates the f-fold duplication that Penguin requires. During inference, step 1 computes X'=XW via PMult and Add (no CMult needed since W is plaintext); step 2 rotates X' ciphertexts n-1 times with offset f, multiplies with corresponding A ciphertexts, and sums; step 3 applies two rotations (left and right) to produce the output. Lazy relinearization — deferring key-switching by accumulating unrelinearized products — reduces constant-factor overhead below Penguin's even at identical asymptotic complexity. Second, the GP-X partitioning policy distributes both encrypted A and encrypted X across K GPUs in independent block-diagonal groups, duplicating only plaintext W (0.00026 GB vs 59 GB for A). Each GPU handles a self-contained subproblem (Ai, Xi), communicates only aggregated partial-result ciphertexts (~0.0015 GiB each), and overlaps this minimal PCIe transfer with local computation. Critical design decisions: (i) ciphertext granularity as partition unit — well-justified by profiling showing limb-level splits cause SM stalls; (ii) no bootstrapping — justified by depth control within a 7-level CKKS circuit; (iii) polynomial approximation (x²) for ReLU — a standard CKKS workaround with accuracy recovery via retraining, though tested only on link prediction AUC. Key Results: Evaluated on three link prediction benchmarks — Amazon-Photo (7,650 nodes, 745 features), Amazon-Computers (13,381 nodes, 767 features), PubMed (19,717 nodes, 500 features) — using 2-layer GAE models. Single-GPU BDPP vs feature-wise packing: 4.41× speedup on Amazon-Photo (193.79s vs 854.20s), 4.28× on Amazon-Computers (750.14s vs 3210.23s); PubMed causes OOM on all single-GPU methods including BDPP. Multi-GPU GP-X on 4 NVIDIA A100-SXM4-80GB: 3.63× speedup on Amazon-Photo (32.03s), 3.79× on Amazon-Computers (118.80s), 3.88× on PubMed (254.97s). Limb-level partition (Cinnamon baseline) with 4 GPUs: 0.3× of single-GPU performance on all datasets. Rotation count reduction: BDPP reduces from 4,064 to 189 on the (128,64,32) micro-benchmark (>20× fewer). Memory: BDPP uses 1.69 GB vs Penguin's 7.55 GB on (128,64,32). CPU vs GPU: BDPP GPU is 10.5–11.1× faster than Penguin CPU. PubMed with 2 GPUs (GP-X): 832.9s, demonstrating that the largest tested graph becomes feasible only with multi-GPU BDPP. Baselines are current and strong: Penguin (NeurIPS'23) and Cinnamon (ASPLOS'25) are the two most recent directly competing systems. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The genuinely novel contribution is BDPP: achieving O(n) adjacency matrix memory cost at O(n+2f) rotation complexity simultaneously — prior methods trade one off against the other. The GP-X partitioning policy is also novel in the HE-GCN context, though the idea of partitioning at whole-ciphertext granularity is a logical deduction from the known structure of KSO. The application of diagonal-wise encoding to block-diagonal layouts adapts Halevi-Shoup (2014) and Penguin's prior work; the paper extends rather than replaces these foundations. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present in Table 2 comparing FWP, Penguin, and BDPP across four micro-benchmark scales, and Table 5 systematically varies packing method × partition policy. No error bars or multiple-seed results are reported — HE inference is deterministic so this is acceptable. Code is not publicly released (not mentioned). The implementation uses Liberate-FHE v0.9.0 (open-source). The analytical complexity claim in Table 1 is verified both formally and empirically. One concern: Penguin comparison in end-to-end benchmarks uses a modified "dup" variant because the original Penguin runs OOM, making the latency comparison (Penguin+dup is 3,253s vs BDPP's 193s on Amazon-Photo) partially apples-to-oranges — the paper acknowledges this but the modification makes Penguin's numbers worse than an idealized Penguin would be. The paper is peer-reviewed at MLSys 2026. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS — a built framework evaluated by deployment on real hardware. - Practical: Runs on commercially available NVIDIA A100 and RTX A6000 GPUs using the open-source Liberate-FHE library; no custom hardware required. - Efficient: On 4 A100s, PubMed link prediction completes in 254.97s; compared to >1,989s for LLP and infeasible single-GPU alternatives, but absolute latency is still minutes-scale for a ~20K node graph, which may be prohibitive for interactive applications. - Effective: Achieves the stated goal of fitting and accelerating HE-GCN inference on graphs that previously caused OOM; link prediction AUC is maintained at baseline levels via retraining with x² activation. - Scalable: 4-GPU near-linear scaling demonstrated; 16-GPU scaling for Amazon-Photo is claimed based on input granularity analysis but not experimentally validated; behavior on million-node graphs (where A would be terabytes encrypted) is unaddressed. The system delivers a framework implementation (using Liberate-FHE), not a standalone library or trained model artifact. Strengths: - The BDPP complexity-memory pareto improvement is analytically clean: matching Penguin's O(n+2f) rotation complexity while reducing memory from O(nf) to O(n) is a genuine advance, not just engineering. - The profiling-driven motivation (Figure 1d showing naive 2-GPU partition incurs >50% latency increase) correctly identifies the root cause and directly informs the GP-X design. - The GP-X communication volume analysis — showing that aggregation cost is constant under fixed input size and that PCIe transfer of 0.0015 GiB ciphertexts can be hidden behind computation — is rigorous and implementationally validated. - Lazy relinearization enabled by BDPP's block-independence property is a practically important constant-factor improvement that the paper correctly attributes to structure rather than complexity class. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors note that bootstrapping is excluded (justified by depth control), that ReLU is replaced with x² (with retraining), and that scalability is constrained by input granularity (explicitly limiting Amazon-Photo to 16 GPUs). They do not present this as a limitation per se but acknowledge it in the scalability analysis. The paper does not explicitly acknowledge the restriction to small-scale graphs or the link-prediction-only evaluation scope. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The three evaluation datasets (max 19,717 nodes) are small by industry standards; graphs in recommendation systems (Amazon product graphs, social networks) routinely contain millions to billions of nodes. Extrapolating the framework's performance to those scales is speculative — ciphertext counts and communication volumes will grow quadratically with graph size, and the claim of practical applicability to recommendation systems is unsupported at evaluated scales. - Only link prediction via GAE is evaluated; node classification and graph classification tasks have different A-sparsity patterns and multi-hop aggregation depths that could affect BDPP's rotation savings. - The x² activation approximation is used throughout; no evaluation of accuracy degradation on harder tasks or deeper models is provided. - Only 2-layer GCNs are tested; deeper models (which increase the number of encrypted AXW computations chained) are not evaluated. - The 16-GPU scalability claim for Amazon-Photo is analysis-only, not experimentally verified. - No discussion of the client-side encryption and key-generation cost, which dominates total system latency for interactive use cases where encryption happens at inference time. Bottom Line: A practitioner building privacy-preserving GCN inference on GPU clusters will find the BDPP+GP-X combination concretely useful and the paper's analysis of why limb-level parallelism fails in the HE-GCN setting is directly actionable, but the evaluation's restriction to ~20K-node graphs leaves the real-world viability for production-scale graphs undemonstrated.
Citations
#24 — Innovative (72)

On the Geometry of On-Policy Distillation (58 ▲ )

Zhennan Shen, Yanshu Li, Qingyu Yin, Chak Tou Leong, Zhilin Wang, Yanxu Chen, Rongduo Han, Sunbowen Lee, Yi R. Fung · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 9 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper characterizes the parameter-space update geometry of on-policy distillation (OPD) for LLM post-training, establishing that OPD occupies a distinct "relaxed off-principal regime" between SFT and RLVR and exhibits "subspace locking" — an early-emerging, low-dimensional update channel that is both persistent and functionally sufficient for training. - Why it's important: OPD is increasingly used to improve LLM reasoning, yet practitioners have no principled basis for its design choices (token selection, rollout policy, objective composition); understanding the update geometry provides a mechanistic foundation for those choices. - How it does it: Four parameter-space diagnostics (bf16-aware update sparsity, principal-angle rotation, normalized spectral shift, update-mask overlap) characterize OPD's static position; cumulative update trajectories (stable rank, Frobenius norm, Hill tail estimates, subspace similarity) characterize its dynamics; and three controlled perturbation experiments identify objective composition — not token density or rollout policy — as the sensitive control axis. - The good: A rank-16 subspace projection experiment at 20% training progress preserves OPD's AIME 2024 performance while substantially degrading SFT under the identical bottleneck, demonstrating the locked subspace is not merely descriptive but functionally sufficient. - The missing: All experiments use Qwen3-family models on math-domain data; whether the identified geometric properties generalize to other model architectures, larger scales (70B+), or non-math task distributions is untested and the single-seed trajectory results carry no uncertainty estimates. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers designing LLM post-training pipelines who want mechanistic justification for OPD recipe choices, particularly around objective composition and parameter-efficient training.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Novelty (80) and Non-Obviousness (82) anchor the score — the subspace-locking finding (early-emerging, persistent, functionally sufficient low-rank update channel unique to OPD) is a NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING not derivable from prior SFT/RLVR geometry, and the result that objective composition — not token selection — is the geometry-sensitive lever is genuinely counter-intuitive. Rigor (70) and Resilience (72) are solid: the rank-16 functional-sufficiency test is a clean falsifiable causal experiment (OPD preserved, SFT degraded under identical bottleneck), bf16-aware sparsity accounting is careful, and the ordering is robust across seven OPD variants. Completeness (72) is good with honest scope limits, though single-seed trajectories and no K-ablation leave the functional cliff uncharacterized. Practical Impact (45) is low — no software artifact, dataset, or model is released; it is a diagnostic study whose recommendations require practitioners to implement geometry-aware training themselves. Weighted: 0.20*80+0.25*70+0.15*82+0.15*72+0.15*72+0.10*45 = 72.
empirical Novelty 80 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 82 Completeness 72 Resilience 72 Impact 45
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Post-training for LLM reasoning operates under three paradigms — SFT, RLVR, and OPD — whose empirical properties differ substantially. Prior geometric analyses have shown SFT induces dense, principal-aligned weight updates that distort pretrained spectral structure, while RLVR produces sparse, off-principal updates that preserve it. OPD combines features of both: dense token-level KL distillation resembles SFT, while on-policy rollout generation and policy-gradient optimization connect it to RLVR. This hybrid character makes OPD's parameter trajectory difficult to infer from either paradigm, leaving practitioners without principled guidance for decisions about token supervision density, rollout policy, teacher scale, and objective composition. The paper addresses this gap by asking where OPD sits in parameter space, how its updates evolve during training, and which component drives that evolution. Key Idea The core insight is "subspace locking": OPD's cumulative parameter updates (ΔWt = Wt − W₀) rapidly converge into a narrow low-dimensional channel — stable rank ~18–27 — before 20% of training steps, and this channel is directionally stable (high similarity to the final update subspace from the first checkpoint) and functionally sufficient (rank-16 projection of gradients to the early-emerged subspace preserves downstream performance). This is distinct from SFT, which progressively expands its update subspace over training, and RLVR, which contracts late. The mechanistic explanation is that OPD uses heterogeneous per-token coefficients in the gradient (token-level teacher–student KL disagreements), whereas RLVR uses a shared scalar advantage; heterogeneous coefficients broaden the accessible directional support within the same geometry-steered, KL-anchored update family, yielding a regime that is less selective than RLVR but more geometry-preserving than SFT. Methodology The paper analyzes Qwen3-family checkpoints on math-domain data (dapo-math-17k). The main comparison centers on Qwen3-8B with a shared SFT anchor (iter_0005375, trained on Dolci-Think SFT for 5k steps from base), from which both OPD (Qwen3-32B teacher, 300 steps, GRPO advantage, lr=1e-6) and RLVR (verifiable math reward via DeepScaler, 1k steps, GRPO, lr=1e-6) are initialized. All runs use H200 80GB GPUs in bf16. Section 3 applies four diagnostics at training endpoint: (1) bf16-aware sparsity — fraction of entries unchanged at bfloat16 precision with a relative tolerance of η=10⁻³; (2) principal-angle rotation between top-512 singular subspaces of W₀ and W₊; (3) normalized spectral shift NSS = ||σ(W₊)−σ(W₀)||₂/||σ(W₀)||₂; (4) overlap of the update mask with a principal-entry mask (top-α of rank-64 SVD reconstruction) and a low-magnitude mask (bottom-α by |W₀|), using α=0.5. Section 4 tracks cumulative update ΔWt across checkpoints via stable rank (||ΔW||²_F/||ΔW||²_op), Frobenius norm, and Hill tail estimates. Subspace stability is measured as SimK(t, tend) = (1/K)||V_K(t)ᵀV_K(tend)||²_F. Functional sufficiency is tested by extracting the top-16 right singular vectors of ΔWt at ~20% progress and constraining subsequent gradients g ← gV₁₆V₁₆ᵀ; the effect on AIME 2024 avg@8 (and four additional benchmarks in the appendix) is compared between OPD and SFT under the same projection. Section 5 tests three controlled perturbations: token sparsification (top-KL and random retention at 25/50%); off-policy rollouts (teacher-generated trajectories with clipping threshold ε=1.0); and objective mixing A^(α) = αA_OPD + (1−α)A_RLVR for α ∈ {0.75, 0.50, 0.25, 0.05, 0.01}. Each changes exactly one factor while holding others fixed. Critical design decisions: (1) Shared SFT anchor for OPD and RLVR cleanly attributes geometric differences to paradigm rather than initialization — well-justified. (2) bf16-aware sparsity prevents rounding artifacts from inflating or deflating apparent update counts — methodologically careful. (3) K=16 as projection rank is chosen near but slightly below the observed effective stable rank at 20% progress; no ablation over K is presented, making the exact threshold somewhat arbitrary. Key Results Update sparsity (Qwen3-8B controlled): SFT leaves 8.1% of weights unchanged, OPD leaves 51.6%, RLVR leaves 77.2%. OPD sparsity is stable across teacher scale (4B–32B teachers: 50.3%–51.7%), student scale (14B: 56.6%), code domain (57.1%), and MoE teacher (48.6%). Published reference GRPO checkpoints (Klear, GT-Qwen3, Polaris) fall within 69.9%–79.9%, consistent with the ordering. Subspace rotation and spectral drift: SFT principal angles exceed 10° in a representative layer; OPD ~1°; RLVR <0.5°. Spectral drift NSS: SFT at 10⁻³, OPD at 10⁻⁴, RLVR at 10⁻⁵. Update localization: Global update density SFT→OPD→RLVR = 92.73%→46.28%→27.76%. Low-magnitude overlap: 31.88%→53.59%→73.48%, versus the 50% random baseline, indicating OPD and RLVR shift updates toward low-magnitude regions while SFT concentrates on principal weights. Subspace locking trajectory: OPD stable rank stays within ~18–27 throughout training. SFT expands monotonically; RLVR contracts late. OPD Frobenius norm substantially exceeds RLVR while ending with comparable stable rank, ruling out a small-update explanation. Subspace similarity shows OPD aligns with its final V₁₆ from the first checkpoint; SFT and RLVR converge more gradually. Functional sufficiency: Rank-16 projection from the 20% checkpoint leaves OPD AIME 2024 avg@32 approximately intact (~62–65% range maintained) while degrading SFT avg@32 from ~55% to ~35%. This qualitative pattern holds across AMC 2023, AIME 2025, Minerva, and Beyond AIME. Controls: Token sparsification at 25–50% density (both top-KL and random) and off-policy rollouts leave the stable rank trajectory within the OPD band. Objective mixing at α≤0.25 (OPD component below 25%) produces a distinct spectral trajectory departing from the OPD-like regime. Novelty Classification NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW FORMULATION. The identification of subspace locking — early-emerging, persistent, functionally sufficient low-dimensional update channel specific to OPD — is genuinely novel and not derivable from prior analyses of SFT or RLVR geometry. The relaxed Three-Gate formalization extending Zhu et al.'s RLVR account to OPD via heterogeneous token coefficients in the gradient covariance is a new formal account. The paper applies existing diagnostic tools (principal angles, spectral drift, stable rank) from recent literature to a new setting; the tools are not novel, but the findings they reveal are. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and systematic: Table 9 specifies exactly which factor each of the three perturbation experiments changes, holding all others fixed. The robustness of the sparsity ordering across seven OPD variants (teacher scale 8B–32B, student scale 4B–14B, code domain, MoE teacher) strengthens generalizability. Functional sufficiency is validated across five benchmarks, not only the primary AIME 2024. Weaknesses: No error bars or multi-seed averages on trajectory plots (stable rank, subspace similarity curves), making within-condition variance unassessable. The "multi-seed" variant appears only in Table 6 for sparsity robustness, not for trajectory results. No code or model weights are stated to be publicly released; no repository URL is provided. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2606.07082v2, not peer-reviewed). The authors acknowledge their Three-Gate account is "mechanistic explanation consistent with the evidence, rather than complete causal or formal theory" — an honest qualification. Evaluation metric (AIME 2024 avg@8 or avg@32) is a reasonable but narrow downstream signal; no inference-efficiency or generalization benchmarks are reported. Deployability (EMPIRICAL) - Practical: Experiments run on H200 80GB GPUs; the diagnostic analysis is offline post-hoc SVD on checkpoints and requires no special hardware beyond standard LLM training infrastructure. - Efficient: The rank-16 constrained training experiment adds no overhead beyond standard OPD training; the projection is applied at gradient computation time. Diagnostic analysis (SVD on saved checkpoints) is a one-time offline cost. - Effective: The functional sufficiency test directly shows that a rank-16 bottleneck preserves OPD task performance on five math reasoning benchmarks — the evidence is task-level, not a proxy — but the paper makes no performance improvement claim; it characterizes existing dynamics. - Scalable: Tested only at 4B, 8B, and 14B student scales; behavior at 70B+ is unstated and untested. The claim that subspace locking is a general OPD property has not been stress-tested at large scale or across architectures. The work delivers no software artifact, trained model, dataset, or implementation — it is a diagnostic analysis paper whose practical recommendations (monitor the locked channel, use objective composition as geometry control lever) are actionable in principle but require practitioners to implement geometry-aware training themselves. Strengths - The functional sufficiency experiment is a clean, falsifiable causal test: identical rank-16 bottleneck applied to OPD and SFT produces opposite outcomes (OPD preserved, SFT degraded), directly distinguishing a descriptive low-rank observation from a functionally significant one. - The three-factor ablation separates token supervision density, rollout policy, and objective composition as independent axes, revealing that the first two are inert with respect to subspace locking while the third is sensitive — a surprising result that contradicts the intuition that token selection is the primary design lever. - bf16-aware sparsity accounting is methodologically careful in a way most prior work is not; the tolerance-based comparison prevents rounding artifacts from dominating the sparsity measurement and makes results hardware-comparable. - Robustness of the sparsity ordering across diverse OPD configurations (seven variants spanning teacher scale, student scale, data domain, and teacher architecture) establishes the finding as a property of the training paradigm rather than a configuration artifact. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state two limitations: (1) analysis focuses on controlled Qwen3-family reasoning settings, and the observed geometry may vary across other model families, modalities, and task distributions; (2) the Three-Gate account and covariance analysis are mechanistic explanations consistent with evidence, not complete causal or formal theories of OPD optimization. These are confronted directly and honestly rather than minimized. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The rank-16 projection is tested at a single K chosen near (but arbitrarily below) the observed effective stable rank; no ablation over K is presented, leaving the functional cliff uncharacterized — it is unclear whether K=8 would collapse performance or K=32 would make SFT robust too. - The paper provides no downstream performance comparison between OPD and RLVR at matched compute budget, so whether OPD's geometrically distinct trajectory translates to better or worse sample efficiency on reasoning tasks cannot be inferred. - All trajectory experiments show single-run curves without multi-seed variation; given the sensitivity to objective composition even at small alpha values, noise in the diagnostic trajectories could affect interpretation of the boundary between OPD-like and RLVR-like regimes. - The Three-Gate mechanism implies specific testable predictions (e.g., that the locked subspace should align with directions of maximal teacher–student divergence), but no such mechanistic validation experiment is performed — the account remains consistent with the data but underdetermined. - RLVR uses binary math-accuracy reward (DeepScaler); whether RLVR with dense reward shaping or a different reward signal would change the geometric ordering and thereby affect the OPD-RLVR comparison is not tested. Bottom Line This paper delivers a credible and informative mechanistic account of OPD's parameter-space dynamics — particularly the subspace locking phenomenon and the finding that objective composition, not token selection, is the geometry-sensitive control axis — but scope is restricted to Qwen3 math settings and the practical algorithm it motivates is left for future work.
Recommended 2606.07082 PDF
#25 — Innovative (72)

EVIL Baseline: LLM -Discovered Heuristics can be strong Baselines for Scientific Inference

D Berghaus · openreview.net · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper demonstrates that LLM-guided evolutionary search (EVIL) can automatically discover compact, interpretable Python/NumPy heuristics that serve as zero-shot baselines for scientific inference — specifically temporal point process prediction, Markov jump process rate estimation, and time series imputation — matching or outperforming specialized deep learning models on most benchmarks. - Why it's important: Practitioners routinely invest significant compute and model complexity in dynamical-systems inference without knowing whether a well-structured heuristic could do most of the work; EVIL provides a <$1, <15-minute calibration tool that answers this question before model development begins. - How it does it: OpenEvolve (an open-source AlphaEvolve implementation) iteratively mutates a trivial seed program via LLM-proposed diffs, scores candidates on a held-out subsplit, and stores promising solutions in a MAP-Elites archive, with NumPy-only constraints that impose an Occam's-razor bias toward generalizable solutions. - The good: EVIL achieves 87.8% accuracy on MIMIC-II (75 mark types, never seen during evolution), outperforming all neural baselines trained specifically on that dataset, and consistently beats both NeuralMJP and FIM-MJP on Hellinger distance across 3 of 4 real-world MJP datasets while running ~200× faster on a single CPU core. - The missing: The paper does not compare against hand-tuned classical statistical estimators (e.g., EM-based MJP rate estimation) designed by domain experts, leaving unclear whether EVIL finds anything beyond what a careful statistician would produce manually. - Read it or not: Researchers developing foundation models or deep learning systems for dynamical-systems inference should read it — the empirical calibration message and the MJP result in particular are genuinely surprising and practically useful.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (75) and Practical Impact (75) drive the score: a <$1, zero-parameter NumPy heuristic discovered via LLM-guided evolution outperforming a foundation model (FIM-MJP, pretrained on 45k synthetic processes) on Hellinger distance across 3 of 4 real MJP datasets is a genuine empirical surprise, and the released open-source pipeline plus the calibration-tool framing make it directly actionable. Rigor (70) and Resilience (70) are solid — seed-variance and long-run ablations, two-variant decomposition, code available — though baseline error bars are imported from prior work and no significance tests are run, leaving some dataset-level wins uncertain. Completeness (75) is high: the paper constructively identifies windowed imputation as a failure case and uses it to validate its own thesis. Novelty (68) is moderate since the evolutionary machinery (OpenEvolve/MAP-Elites) is prior art; the contribution is the demonstration and framing. Weighted: 0.20*68+0.25*70+0.15*75+0.15*75+0.15*70+0.10*75 = 72. Strongly resembles the user's positive set (rigorous, surprising, controlled empirical findings).
empirical Novelty 68 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 75 Resilience 70 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Researchers building deep learning models for scientific inference tasks — including temporal point processes (event sequence forecasting), Markov jump processes (rate matrix estimation from noisy discrete observations), and time series imputation — lack a principled way to establish strong baselines before investing in model development. Ad-hoc baselines (last-value imputation, majority-class prediction) are too simple to be informative; domain-expert heuristics require considerable design effort and may not cover the full heuristic space. The central gap is quantifying how much learned representations actually add over the statistical structure already accessible from the raw data. Foundation inference models (FIM-MJP, FIM-PP, FIM-ℓ) address this with synthetic pretraining but are expensive to develop, require GPUs, and are opaque. This paper asks whether an automatically discovered, interpretable heuristic can serve as the calibration tool. Key Idea The core insight is that constraining evolutionary search to short NumPy-only programs forces a short-description-length bias (analogous to MDL/Occam's razor), while the LLM proposer contributes prior knowledge about statistical methods — transition statistics, recency weighting, motif retrieval — in combinations that human designers might not explore. The result is a single general-purpose function per task that computes exact statistics on in-context data at inference time, rather than approximating through learned parameters, which gives it natural adaptability to arbitrary mark counts and dataset sizes without retraining. Methodology The pipeline uses OpenEvolve (open-source AlphaEvolve) with a weighted LLM ensemble: GPT-5-mini (80%, weight 0.8) and GPT-5 (20%, weight 0.2) at temperature 0.7 with max 16,000 output tokens. Each iteration proposes code modifications in diff format; the modified program is evaluated on a held-out subsplit carved from the existing training split (so EVIL has access to strictly less data than baselines trained on the full training set). Promising solutions are stored in a MAP-Elites archive (3 islands, population 50, archive size 20, elite selection ratio 0.2). All runs use 100 iterations and are fixed with identical system prompts and initial programs throughout the study to avoid implicit benchmark overfitting. Two variants are reported: EVIL (evolved on target-dataset subsplits) and EVIL (synthetic prior) (evolved exclusively on synthetic data, the strictest zero-shot condition). Critical design decisions: (1) NumPy-only constraint — well-justified, enforces interpretability and compactness; (2) separate held-out subsplit for evolution scoring rather than test set — well-justified to prevent overfitting; (3) fixed system prompts/initial programs across all benchmarks — well-justified to avoid implicit search-space tuning toward reported metrics. For temporal point processes, the scoring function is accuracy − RMSE_Δt; for MJPs, −CE − RMSE_Q (cross-entropy on initial distribution plus RMSE on off-diagonal rate entries); for imputation, −mean MAE across datasets. Key Results Temporal point processes (N=5 long-horizon): EVIL achieves OTD=4.05 on TAXI, matching FIM-PP finetuned (4.08) and beating CDiff (5.97) and FIM-PP zero-shot (6.77). On RETWEET, EVIL OTD=15.56, best across all methods. On AMAZON sMAPE, EVIL (synthetic prior) achieves 57.9, far below CDiff (81.3) and FIM-PP finetuned (78.7). On MIMIC-II (75 marks, zero-shot transfer), EVIL achieves 87.8% accuracy vs. best trained baselines at 85.51% (DTPP) — notably, neural TPP models cannot natively handle mark-count mismatches whereas EVIL computes transition statistics at inference time. Runtime: FIM-PP zero-shot requires 964s on A100 40GB GPU; EVIL requires 5s on 1 CPU core (~192× speedup). Markov jump processes (Hellinger distance ×10^−2, lower is better): EVIL (synthetic prior) achieves 0.16 (DFR), 0.30 (IonCh), 0.93 (ADP), and 0.015 (PFold), consistently better than both NeuralMJP (0.30/0.48/1.38/0.015) and FIM-MJP (0.27/0.41/1.39/0.014) on DFR, IonCh, and ADP. On the entropy production stress test (DFR), EVIL tracks the ground truth more closely than FIM-MJP across the full voltage range, including at high voltages where rare transitions differ by four orders of magnitude. Time series imputation (50% point-wise, MAE): EVIL achieves best-in-class on 6 of 8 datasets including 4 zero-shot holdout sets (Solar: 0.413 vs. FIM-ℓ 0.595; Electricity: 0.078 vs. 0.083). On windowed Motion Capture, EVIL (2.98±2.04) is clearly worse than FIM-ℓ (1.77±0.63) and LatentODE (1.66±0.99), with large variance across all methods. Ablations: An 800-iteration run finds 21 new validation-set best programs but yields negligible test-set improvement, suggesting the 100-iteration budget captures most generalizable gains. Five independent seeds show moderate variability (TAOBAO sMAPE: 131.8±17.7) but a broadly stable performance profile. Novelty Classification NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The evolutionary framework (MAP-Elites, LLM-proposed diffs, OpenEvolve/AlphaEvolve) is prior work; FunSearch and LLM-SR are closely related predecessors. The genuine novelty is (1) demonstrating that this paradigm extends from single-instance combinatorial optimization to discovering general-purpose scientific inference functions that generalize zero-shot across datasets, and (2) the calibration-tool framing: EVIL as a cheap diagnostic to determine where deep learning adds value over heuristics. The discovered algorithms themselves — second-order transition hierarchies, recency-weighted mixing, motif retrieval with level-shift correction — combine known statistical building blocks in configurations the authors report they could not find in prior literature. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and informative: dataset-specific evolution (Table 12), seed variance (Table 13, 5 seeds), and a long run (Table 14, 800 iterations). Error bars for EVIL show 0.000 standard deviation in most tables because the final evolved program is deterministic — variance is only reported across independent evolution runs in Table 13. Neural baseline error bars are drawn from prior published work rather than re-runs in a unified codebase, introducing potential preprocessing discrepancies. Code is available at an anonymous repository (https://anonymous.4open.science/r/evil_algorithms-374D/). No statistical significance tests are reported; given the moderate seed variance on some datasets (TAOBAO sMAPE 131.8±17.7 vs. CDiff 124.3), some claimed wins may not be statistically robust. The paper correctly notes that system prompts and scoring functions were held fixed throughout to avoid implicit benchmark overfitting — a credibility-positive methodological commitment. This is a preprint under review at ICML; results are not yet peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Yes — runs on a single CPU core with standard NumPy; no GPU required for inference. - Efficient: Inference is ~200× faster than FIM-PP on A100 GPU (5s vs. 964s per evaluation); discovery costs <$1 and <37 minutes wall-clock even for the hardest task (imputation), versus months of engineering for a foundation model. - Effective: Clearly effective for point-wise missing patterns and most temporal point process tasks; clearly ineffective for contiguous-window imputation (40% worse MAE than LatentODE on Motion Capture), which the authors acknowledge as a case where learned dynamical priors are necessary. - Scalable: Not stress-tested beyond the benchmark datasets; the MIMIC-II result (75 marks vs. 22 during training) is promising but represents a single distribution shift; performance on datasets with fundamentally different statistical properties remains an open question. The work delivers a software artifact (Python programs, discoverable via the open-source OpenEvolve pipeline) and a reusable methodology. Strengths - The MJP result is the most striking finding: a <$1 evolved NumPy function with zero neural parameters outperforms FIM-MJP (pretrained on 45,000 synthetic processes with 300 paths each) on Hellinger distance across 3 of 4 real-world datasets, including real biophysical recordings (IonCh) and molecular dynamics (ADP). This is a genuine empirical surprise that should prompt re-examination of what MJP inference requires. - The MIMIC-II zero-shot generalization (87.8% accuracy on 75-mark dataset, surpassing all task-specific trained neural baselines) demonstrates that the evolved algorithm captures a structurally general principle — context-derived transition statistics — rather than overfitting to the evolution training distribution. - The two-variant design (EVIL vs. EVIL (synthetic prior)) provides a clean decomposition of how much gain comes from target-data exposure versus synthetic pretraining, giving practitioners actionable guidance on when target-domain data during evolution is needed. - The paper is intellectually honest about failure modes: it explicitly identifies windowed imputation as a case where heuristics fall short, frames this as evidence that deep learning is warranted there, and uses the failure constructively to validate the calibration-tool thesis. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge: (1) weaker performance on tasks with strong dataset-specific dynamics (windowed imputation); (2) deterministic outputs with no uncertainty quantification; (3) difficulty assessing novelty of discovered algorithms since they recombine known statistical primitives; (4) unknown generalization to fundamentally different data distributions. These are stated directly and credibly, adding to the work's trustworthiness. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The scoring functions used during evolution (accuracy − RMSE_Δt for TPP; −CE − RMSE_Q for MJP) directly optimize the same metrics reported in the main evaluation tables. While the authors fix scoring functions before seeing benchmark results, the evaluation metrics are not fully held out from the search objective, creating a partial alignment that weakens claims of purely general-purpose generalization. - No comparison against hand-designed classical statistical estimators specifically tuned for each task (e.g., EM for MJP rate estimation, Hawkes process MLE for point processes) is included as a baseline, making it impossible to determine whether EVIL finds algorithms beyond what a careful statistician would produce with a day's effort. - For EVIL (synthetic prior) in the MJP setting, the synthetic training data is drawn from the same family as FIM-MJP's pretraining corpus (Berghaus et al. 2024) — the comparison between EVIL (synthetic prior) and FIM-MJP is therefore between two methods exposed to the same data distribution, differing only in how they use it, which is not made fully explicit. - The seed-variance ablation (Table 13) shows TAOBAO sMAPE at 131.8±17.7, which is comparable in magnitude to differences between methods; no significance tests are run, so some claimed dataset-level wins are statistically uncertain. - The motif retrieval strategy independently rediscovered by EVIL for imputation is acknowledged as convergent with Zhang & Gilpin (2026)'s context parroting, but the paper does not quantitatively compare the two methods side by side. Bottom Line Any practitioner committing to a deep learning pipeline for dynamical-systems inference should first run EVIL — the cost is trivial, the heuristics are inspectable, and the empirical evidence presented here makes a credible case that competitive performance often requires less model complexity than the field currently assumes.
