Alan Poldrack, Jiachen Li, Zhenglu Li, Molei Tao, Jing Huang, Wenqi Shi, Costas Spanos, Lichao Sun, Chenguang Wang, Orson Xu, Zhen Dong, Hector Gomez, Aylin Caliskan, Ali Emami, Haimin Hu, Zhi Li, Lihui Liu, Murphy Niu, Yi Shao, Jianxin Sun, Mikko Tolonen, Ting Wang, Sanjiv Das, Yanjun Gao, Wenbo Guo, Erika J Schneider, Zhiyong Lu, Yian Ma, Mark Mueller, Radha Poovendran, Somayeh Sojoudi, Yinglun Zhu, Dawn Song · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 9 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Agents' Last Exam (ALE), a benchmark of 960 expert-authored task workflows (1,490 runnable instances) spanning 55 SOC/O*NET-grounded digital industries, evaluated via deterministic automated verification rather than human judges or open-ended LLM scoring. - Why it's important: Existing agentic benchmarks saturate quickly and cover only a handful of domains, leaving no reliable instrument to measure whether frontier AI agents can perform the long-horizon, economically valuable professional work that would translate to GDP-scale impact. - How it does it: Domain experts contribute past real professional projects (tasks that took them days to weeks), which are screened through a five-gate QC pipeline, provisioned into remote VM environments, and scored through structured deliverable-based or milestone-based checks against expert-provided references and rubrics. - The good: The hardest tier (Last-Exam, 38 tasks) achieves a 0% full-pass rate across all tested frontier agents including Codex+GPT-5.5 and Claude Code+Fable 5, demonstrating that the benchmark has substantial headroom even against the strongest current systems. - The missing: Only 150 of 1,490 task instances are publicly released; the private pool is opaque, reproducibility of leaderboard numbers depends on the authors' infrastructure, and no longitudinal analysis yet shows whether the benchmark resists training-on-benchmark contamination over time. - Read it or not: Researchers and engineers building or evaluating generalist computer-use agents should read this; the taxonomy, evaluation pipeline, and baseline numbers are the best currently available reference for measuring real professional-workflow capability, though practitioners who only need QA-style benchmarks can skip it.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile (operational benchmark infrastructure, portal + VM + leaderboard). Novelty (78) reflects a genuinely novel combination — exhaustive SOC/O*NET occupational grounding, expert-sourced authentic shipped workflows, and deterministic automated verification at scale — that no prior benchmark achieves together; the 0% Last-Exam pass rate confirms real headroom. Impact (72), the dominant systems weight, is constrained because the infrastructure is not self-hostable and reproducibility depends on the authors' continued operation. Rigor (68) is the main limiter: only a subset has 3-run std devs, 90% of tasks are a private opaque pool, code/infra is unreleased, the anti-contamination rotation is intent not implementation, and it is a preprint. Resilience (72) and Completeness (72) are reasonable given honest limitation disclosure, but tier assignment appears post-hoc and the failure taxonomy derives from a single agent. Weighted: 0.15*78 + 0.15*68 + 0.10*70 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*72 + 0.30*72 = 72.
systems Novelty 78 Rigor 68 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 72 Resilience 72 Impact 72
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: AI systems have cleared celebrated narrow benchmarks (olympiad mathematics, competitive programming, board games) yet have not produced commensurate economic transformation. The root problem is an evaluation gap: available benchmarks either measure static knowledge (MMLU, GPQA, HLE), cover only a few software-centric domains (SWE-bench covers 5/55 ALE subdomains; OSWorld covers 5/55), require expensive human grading (GDPval, Remote Labor Index), or use curator-invented rather than real professional workflows. No existing benchmark simultaneously achieves wide industry coverage, authentic task sourcing, long-horizon complexity, and automated verification. The result is that the AI field lacks a signal that would indicate readiness for professional deployment. Key Idea: The core insight is that grounding benchmark design in the U.S. federal occupational taxonomy (SOC 2018 / O*NET) provides a principled, exhaustive, and defensible criterion for which professional domains to cover, while sourcing tasks from work experts have already shipped — not synthetically constructed scenarios — guarantees authenticity and complexity without requiring the benchmark creators to have deep domain expertise themselves. Automated verification is unlocked by engineering every task's output into a checkable artifact (file, numeric field, geometric surface, behavioral world state), rejecting any proposed task whose only scoring path is holistic LLM judgment. Methodology: The pipeline has two major phases: benchmark construction and evaluation. Construction uses a five-gate staged protocol: (1) advisory committees map each domain's workflow landscape; (2) experts submit via a web portal — a natural-language description, input files, target software, expected deliverable, and evaluation specification; (3) first-pass conference-style review (major/minor revision, borderline accept, accept, strong accept); (4) engineering implementation converts accepted specs into runnable VM assets and codified evaluation logic with engineer dry-runs; (5) final QC peer review by expert committees. The evaluation pipeline decouples three components: a task specification (main.py exposing load(), start(), evaluate()), an agent (harness + foundation model), and a remote VM environment with a four-directory layout (input/, software/, output/, reference/). Agents interact via an action loop using screenshots, shell, mouse/keyboard, file edits, and API calls for up to five hours. Scoring uses a small palette of artifact modes (exact/hashed, numeric/tabular with tolerances, geometric distances, vision-LLM probes for cases requiring visual judgment, behavioral world state) combined via gate-and-score patterns. Critical design decisions: (a) 90% of tasks held private to limit contamination — well-justified given the benchmark's long-term validity goal; (b) LLM-as-judge rejected wherever a deterministic alternative exists — justified and enforced, not merely aspirational; (c) three difficulty tiers (Near-Term, Full-Spectrum, Last-Exam) enabling cost-matched evaluation at different budgets — practically motivated but the tier assignment criteria are not formally specified. Key Results: Across the 160-task public set (67 Near-Term, 55 Full-Spectrum, 38 Last-Exam), the best overall pass rate is 24.0% achieved by Codex+GPT-5.5 (38.1% Near-Term, 22.7% Full-Spectrum, 0.0% Last-Exam at $570 API cost and 390 wall-clock hours across tiers). Claude Code+Fable 5 reaches 34.3%/20.9%/0.0% with overall 22.0%. On the CLI-only subset (ALE-CLI, 105 tasks), Codex+GPT-5.5 scores 23.3% overall — compared to 82% this same configuration achieves on Terminal-Bench, demonstrating ALE-CLI is substantially harder. Model choice accounts for roughly 3× the performance spread that harness choice does among well-engineered systems. Domain-level analysis shows computational mathematics and agriculture/environment score highest (~55–85%) and education lowest (below 25%) for both frontier models. Failure taxonomy (analyzed on Claude Code+Opus 4.7 failed runs) attributes ~31% to Understanding failures, ~47% to Approach failures (domain knowledge, wrong strategy, hallucination), and ~22% to Execution failures — indicating domain knowledge gaps, not execution mechanics, are the dominant bottleneck. GUI usage remains systematically below task demand: 34% of tasks require graphical software, but agents substitute Bash/CLI alternatives, often failing as a result. Novelty Classification: NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK, NEW SYSTEM/TOOL, NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The genuine novelty is the combination of exhaustive SOC/O*NET grounding, expert-sourced authentic workflows, and deterministic automated verification at scale — no prior benchmark achieves all three simultaneously. The five-layer agent capability taxonomy (Brain/Eyes/Body/Hands/Feet) and the Generalist CUA framing are conceptually useful but not formally novel contributions. Rigor & Reproducibility: The paper reports score standard deviations from three independent runs for a subset of configurations (± values in Table 1), acknowledging compute budget constraints prevented full repetition. The public/private split (10%/90%) is empirically validated for representativeness (Appendix D.1). The failure taxonomy is systematic and methodologically credible. Code and infrastructure are not released publicly — evaluation requires the authors' VM infrastructure, which limits independent reproducibility. The benchmark itself is a preprint (arXiv 2606.05405v2, June 2026); peer-reviewed status not stated. A key concern is potential Goodhart's Law vulnerability: with a leaderboard and a public submission portal, task-specific optimization could occur even with private tasks, and the mitigation (rolling rotation of tasks) is stated as a design intention but not yet evaluated empirically. Deployability: Profile: SYSTEMS — the paper builds and evaluates an operational benchmark infrastructure. - Practical: Evaluation requires access to cloud VMs running Linux or Windows with pre-installed domain software; the infrastructure is available through the authors' leaderboard portal but not releasable for self-hosting, making independent evaluation dependent on authors' continued operation. - Efficient: A single frontier agent run on one task costs $3–10 on average and takes minutes to hours; full public-set evaluation per agent configuration costs hundreds to thousands of dollars and tens to hundreds of wall-clock hours (Table 1 shows totals ranging from $38 for lightweight configs to $2.7K for expensive multi-tier Claude Code runs). - Effective: The benchmark credibly differentiates frontier agents on professionally grounded tasks, reveals capability gaps invisible to narrower benchmarks, and the 0% Last-Exam pass rate confirms headroom for years of AI progress — it measures what it claims to measure. - Scalable: The task pool is designed as a living benchmark with continuous expert contributions and rolling public/private rotation; this design addresses long-term validity but depends on sustained organizational commitment from the UC Berkeley team and advisory network. No stress-test of the evaluation infrastructure at larger scale is reported. The paper delivers an operational evaluation system (web portal, VM infrastructure, leaderboard) plus the dataset; it stops short of releasing the infrastructure for self-hosting. Strengths: - The SOC/O*NET grounding is a principled choice that makes the taxonomy defensible, exhaustive, and comparable across benchmarks, and the LLM-assisted cross-benchmark mapping onto 55 subdomains is a reusable methodological contribution. - Expert-sourced real project workflows — not synthetic constructs — give authenticity that curator-authored benchmarks cannot match; the multi-gate QC process (submission review, engineer dry-runs, committee peer review) is more rigorous than most benchmark construction pipelines. - The deliberate rejection of open-ended LLM-as-judge scoring in favor of engineered deterministic checks is a methodologically sound decision that substantially increases reproducibility and reduces judge bias. - The five-layer GCUA taxonomy (Brain/Eyes/Body/Hands/Feet) cleanly exposes the mismatch between existing agent families and ALE's task demands, and the empirical finding that model choice dominates harness choice 3:1 is a concrete, actionable insight for practitioners. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors note that 150/1,490 tasks are public and that the public subset's representativeness is empirically validated but the private pool is opaque to the community. They acknowledge that benchmark contamination through pre-training data overlap remains a risk and describe rolling rotation as a mitigation. The paper notes compute budget constraints prevented full three-run repetition across all configurations. The authors acknowledge that tasks not representable as checkable artifacts are excluded, which may systematically under-represent certain professional domains. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The tier assignment process (what makes a task Near-Term vs. Full-Spectrum vs. Last-Exam) is not formally specified and appears to be post-hoc based on observed agent performance rather than a priori task characteristics — this makes tier boundaries moving targets as model capability improves. The failure taxonomy (31% Understanding, 47% Approach, 22% Execution) is derived exclusively from Claude Code+Opus 4.7 failures; it is not clear whether this distribution generalizes to other agents. The GUI-underutilization finding (agents substituting CLI for GUI tasks) is observed but not causally analyzed — it is unclear whether this reflects harness design, agent training, or task ambiguity in the natural-language description. The paper does not report inter-rater agreement among QC reviewers, leaving uncertainty about consistency of the "authenticity" and "complexity" judgments across 55 domains and 300+ contributors. The rolling task rotation anti-contamination strategy is described but not yet implemented or evaluated. Bottom Line: ALE is the most ambitious and methodologically credible benchmark for long-horizon professional AI agent capability currently available; practitioners building or evaluating generalist computer-use agents should treat its results as the new reference baseline, while being aware that full replication requires author-provided infrastructure and that the benchmark's long-term anti-contamination properties remain untested.
Recommended 2606.05405 PDF
#27 — Innovative (72)

Towards Budget-Aware Agents : Do LLM Agents Know What They Will Spend?

Y Lin, Z Wang, M Liu, Y Shan, L Bai, J Zhang, X Jin… · openreview.net · generating environments for agent testing in enterprises - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a framework for evaluating and training "budget awareness" in LLM agents — the ability to estimate mid-execution how much remaining resource (tokens, money, time) a task will require, expressed as a calibrated interval or an explicit impossibility declaration. - Why it's important: LLM agents deployed with token, cost, or time budgets currently waste substantial compute on hopeless trajectories because they cannot self-detect infeasibility; a budget-aware agent could save 28–64% of tokens on failed runs at the cost of only 1.6–4.2 percentage points in overall success rate. - How it does it: A rollout-replay protocol records unconstrained agent trajectories, then re-queries the same model on every prefix to predict a remaining-budget interval or declare the task impossible, decomposing the capability into three scored sub-capabilities: feasibility prediction, early failure detection, and interval calibration. - The good: The finding that binary feasibility is a calibration problem (SFT alone raises Qwen-7B accuracy from 25.5% to ~90%) while precise interval estimation is a reasoning problem (SFT+RL caps coverage at 47% with 28% median midpoint error) is a crisp, actionable distinction. - The missing: The rollout-replay protocol decouples estimation from execution by design, but this means the paper does not evaluate online budget estimation — where budget prediction itself consumes tokens and interacts with task execution — leaving the deployment-critical regime unstudied. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on agent efficiency, resource-constrained deployment, or LLM metacognition; the empirical findings about universal optimistic bias and late failure recognition are practically significant and not obvious.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Novelty (80) is the strongest dimension — NEW PROBLEM plus a genuinely non-obvious empirical finding (feasibility = calibration, interval = reasoning) that the SFT experiment validates cleanly, and it resembles the user's positive calibration set in methodology and rigor. Non-Obviousness (80) is high: universal optimistic bias and late-firing failure alarms were not derivable from prior work. Rigor (70) is solid but preprint-capped — thorough SFT/RL ablations, but no error bars across frontier models, no baseline heuristic estimator, '(?)' citation placeholders, and no released code/weights. Completeness (70) and Resilience (70) are good with transparent hypothesis testing, but the offline rollout-replay design leaves the deployment-critical online regime unstudied (author-acknowledged). Practical Impact (55) reflects that no artifact ships — only a reproducible protocol. Weighted: 0.20*80+0.25*70+0.15*80+0.15*70+0.15*70+0.10*55 = 72.
empirical Novelty 80 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 70 Resilience 70 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM agents deployed in production consume resources (tokens, API costs, money, warehouse capacity) that grow rapidly with task horizon, yet existing benchmarks only measure these resources post-hoc. Operators need agents that can estimate mid-execution whether they will finish within budget, when to abort, and when to request additional resources. Prior work either studies how to act under a known budget constraint or collects single-point estimates at task start — neither captures the confidence-aware, progressive nature of real resource monitoring. The gap is especially acute for multi-resource external budgets where constraints interact (e.g., holding more inventory depletes warehouse space while improving revenue potential). Key Idea: The core insight is a separation between the agent's task-execution ability and its metacognitive budget-estimation ability, formalized by replaying logged trajectories and querying the same model as an estimator on each prefix without letting estimation interfere with execution. This rollout-replay construction cleanly isolates budget awareness as a distinct measurable capability and enables scoring three sub-capabilities (feasibility prediction, early failure detection, interval calibration) independently. Methodology: The protocol has two phases. First, the agent completes a task with no budget constraint, logging the full trajectory and per-turn costs. Second, for each non-terminal prefix, the model is re-queried to predict either a remaining-budget interval [L, H] or declare "impossible". Three sub-capability metrics are defined: (1) Macro-F1 over feasible/impossible binary labels (all-turn and first-turn variants); (2) Fail-F1, precision-recall F1 restricted to trajectories that ultimately fail; (3) a coverage-times-tightness reward Sk = 1[Rk ∈ [L,H]] · max(0, 1 − (H−L)/Rk) restricted to successful trajectories where the model correctly predicts feasibility. Experiments span four environments: Sokoban (8×8 grid, 2,500-token cap), Search-R1 (multi-hop QA, 3,500-token cap), SWE-bench (code agents, 160-turn cap), and Warehouse (11-turn supply-chain simulation with three coupled budget dimensions — cost, time, warehouse occupancy — sourced from real enterprise data). Five frontier models are evaluated (GPT-5.2, Claude Opus 4.7, Claude Sonnet 4.6, Gemini 3.1 Pro, Qwen3-235B), all run with low-thinking configurations. Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct is fine-tuned with SFT and GRPO-based RL on Sokoban to test trainability. Critical design decisions: (a) separating rollout from estimation to avoid token contamination — well-justified but leaves online estimation as future work; (b) 50/50 feasible/infeasible balance for Warehouse via challenge-conditioned probes — justified to prevent trivial-prediction bias; (c) SFT as mandatory warm-start for RL — empirically validated by collapse without it. Key Results: Across 20 model-environment pairs, task success correlates weakly with feasibility prediction (r ≈ 0.35): on Search-R1, Opus 4.7 achieves 75.8% task success but only 23.1% interval hit rate, while Sonnet 4.6 achieves 71.1% success with 36.5% hit rate. On SWE-bench, Opus leads task success (71.9%), Gemini leads feasibility F1 (58.2%), and GPT-5.2 leads interval hit rate (44.3%) — three different leaders across three sub-capabilities. All 20 model-environment pairs exhibit optimistic bias (underestimating remaining budget), with weaker models more optimistic; on failed trajectories, models predict feasibility above 70% even after consuming 60% of budget, with the alarm firing only in the final 20% of execution. Early stopping on impossible predictions saves 28–64% of tokens on failed trajectories at 1.6–4.2 percentage points success cost. For Qwen-7B training: SFT alone raises feasibility accuracy from 25.5% to ~90%; SFT+RL caps interval coverage at 47% with 28% median midpoint relative error; RL without SFT warm-start collapses to all-impossible or invalid outputs. These experiments used 128 rollouts per model per environment (64 for SWE-bench), yielding 2,000–3,000 estimation samples per model-task pair — moderate scale, not stress-scale. Novelty Classification: NEW PROBLEM + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The formalization of budget awareness as progressive interval estimation is the primary contribution — no prior work studies this as a standalone agent capability with calibrated intervals and infeasibility declarations across both internal and external budget modalities simultaneously. The rollout-replay evaluation protocol is a novel methodological contribution. The empirical finding that feasibility prediction is a calibration problem while interval estimation is a reasoning problem is a genuine and non-obvious result that was not derivable from prior literature. Rigor & Reproducibility: The paper is a preprint under ICML review — results carry preprint-level confidence. Ablations over SFT width, epoch, and RL initialization are thorough and informative. No error bars or significance tests are reported across the frontier model evaluations; 128 rollouts per cell means some estimates could have meaningful variance. The Warehouse environment's feasibility probe sampling procedure (50/50 balance via random budget assignment with seed 42) is a defensible but artificial construction that may not reflect real deployment distributions. The rollout-replay separation is methodologically clean but creates a known limitation: it measures off-policy estimation ability, not on-policy. No code or model weights are released as of the preprint. References appear as "(?)" placeholders throughout, indicating a draft in preparation — citation-level verification is not possible from this version. The SFT/RL training is described in sufficient detail to reproduce (FSDP, batch size 16, lr 5×10⁻⁶, GRPO with 16 rollouts per prompt). Deployability (4-property check): EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: The frontier model evaluations run entirely via API (OpenAI, Anthropic, OpenRouter); the SFT/RL training runs on 8 GPUs with Qwen2.5-7B, commercially feasible. - Efficient: The rollout-replay protocol doubles inference cost (one pass for execution, one per prefix for estimation); the paper does not report estimation latency or cost relative to task cost, which is a gap. - Effective: The early-stopping policy derived from impossible predictions demonstrably reduces token waste (28–64% savings on failed trajectories), but the paper evaluates only binary stopping — not budget reallocation or resource requests. - Scalable: The largest environment (SWE-bench) uses up to 160-turn trajectories, which is moderate scale; no test at longer horizons or multi-agent settings is reported. Warehouse's 11-turn horizon is relatively short for supply-chain deployment. The paper does not claim or test scalability beyond these benchmarks. The work delivers no software artifact; it provides an evaluation protocol and empirical benchmark that a practitioner could implement from the paper. Strengths: - The rollout-replay construction cleanly separates task ability from estimation ability, enabling a controlled evaluation that prior work's post-hoc or single-point designs could not achieve. - The feasibility-is-calibration vs. interval-is-reasoning distinction is empirically validated by the SFT experiment and is the paper's sharpest and most actionable finding. - The Warehouse environment is the most realistic benchmark in the paper: grounded in real enterprise demand data with genuinely coupled multi-resource constraints, going well beyond token-counting tasks. - The hypothesis-testing structure (four explicit hypotheses, mixed support) provides an unusually transparent account of what the experiments do and do not show. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The Conclusion explicitly names interval estimation as the "central open problem" and notes that online estimation (interleaved with execution) is left to future work. The 50/50 Warehouse balance is acknowledged as an evaluation design choice, not a claim about deployment prevalence. The paper does not acknowledge the optimistic-bias finding as a potential artifact of low-thinking model configurations (which may suppress cautious reasoning), nor does it discuss whether the rollout-replay setting — where the agent is asked to estimate budget as a separate meta-task rather than as part of task execution — may produce different behavior than production deployment. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The most significant gap is the absence of online estimation results: the paper explicitly defers this, but it is the setting that matters for deployment, and the decoupled rollout-replay approach may systematically overestimate on-policy capability (the estimator sees a clean prefix without the cognitive overhead of deciding next actions). Second, all frontier models are evaluated with constrained low-thinking configurations; it is plausible that full extended thinking would improve interval calibration substantially, since the paper itself argues that interval estimation is a reasoning bottleneck — this confound is unaddressed. Third, the training experiments are confined to Sokoban, a puzzle with bounded, predictable token cost; generalization of the SFT+RL findings to Search-R1, SWE-bench, or Warehouse is claimed but untested. Fourth, no baseline estimator is compared (e.g., a simple heuristic that extrapolates from mean per-turn cost) — it is unclear how much of the models' performance exceeds a trivial extrapolation. Fifth, the paper evaluates only whether the budget estimator is a useful signal; it does not evaluate whether budget-aware agents trained end-to-end (using budget awareness as a planning input) outperform budget-unaware ones on task performance. Bottom Line: Practitioners building long-horizon LLM agents under resource constraints will find the early-stopping result directly actionable and the calibration-vs-reasoning distinction a useful frame for prioritizing future work, but the gap between offline rollout-replay and the on-policy deployment setting limits how far these findings transfer without further validation.
Search Results
#28 — Innovative (72)

Geometry-Aware Tabular Diffusion

DT Zagardo · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces pairwise geometric features — arctan angles and log-lengths between all column pairs — used simultaneously as inputs and as auxiliary supervised prediction targets for tabular diffusion denoisers, achieving new state-of-the-art results on 10 benchmark datasets with a compact residual MLP. - Why it's important: Tabular synthesis underpins privacy-preserving data sharing and augmentation, and current diffusion models leave inter-column dependency modeling entirely to implicit attention mechanisms; explicit relational supervision fills this gap and transfers across architecturally distinct backbones without retuning. - How it does it: Geometric features (θ_ij = arctan(v_j − v_i) and ℓ_ij = ½ log(1 + (v_j − v_i)²)) are computed from all O(d²) column pairs, concatenated to the denoiser input, and forced to be reconstructed via auxiliary losses whose combined weight (λ_θ=15, λ_ℓ=15, λ_c=8) accounts for ~95% of total training loss. - The good: An architecture-matched ablation — InputsOnly (same architecture, zero geometric supervision) vs Full+GEOM — achieves Cohen's d = −0.08 vs d = 0.81, cleanly isolating supervision as the operative variable rather than added capacity. - The missing: The headline tables compare GATD at 1000 sampling steps against TabDiff at 50 steps; at equal 50-step budgets GATD loses Shape, and the population-level MLE advantage is not significant for GNN and Transformer backbones. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners building tabular generative models — the supervision-versus-capacity ablation design and multi-seed protocol make this a methodologically above-average entry; less relevant for those focused on NLP, vision, or theoretical ML.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (80) is the standout: peer-reviewed at ICML 2026, and the architecture-matched InputsOnly ablation is an unusually careful capacity-vs-supervision control that correctly isolates supervision as the operative variable, backed by a 3×20 multi-seed protocol with seed-stability reporting and a cross-architecture sign-test at p=5.2e-9. Novelty (70) and Non-Obviousness (72) are real — portable pairwise geometric auxiliary supervision transferring across MLP/GNN/Transformer. Completeness (75) strong with directly-confronted limitations, but Resilience (68) is dented by the headline comparing GATD at 1000 steps vs TabDiff at 50 (GATD loses Shape at equal budget) and a weak loss-weight sweep. Impact (58): no released code. Methodologically above-average; aligns with the positive set.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 80 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 75 Resilience 68 Impact 58
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Synthesizing realistic tabular data for privacy-preserving sharing and limited-data augmentation requires faithfully reproducing inter-column dependencies — a harder problem than image or text generation because tabular data mixes heterogeneous column types, exhibits non-Gaussian marginals, and encodes arbitrary correlation structures with no spatial or sequential inductive bias. Current tabular diffusion methods (TabDDPM, STaSy, TabSyn, TabDiff) address column relationships either by ignoring them (MLP backbones) or by learning them implicitly through self-attention (transformer backbones). The specific gap is that no prior tabular generator provides explicit pairwise relational supervision: inter-column structure must emerge from the denoising objective alone, placing the full burden of relational modeling on the backbone architecture. The paper asks whether explicit geometric structure can be provided as a portable auxiliary signal rather than requiring a specific backbone to discover it. Key Idea The central insight is that the directional relationship between any two normalized column values can be encoded as an angle (θ_ij = arctan(v_j − v_i)) and a magnitude (ℓ_ij = ½ log(1 + (v_j − v_i)²)), and that supervising the network to predict these quantities — rather than merely supplying them as inputs — forces it to internalize inter-column structure in a way that transfers to improved denoising. The crucial finding is that geometric inputs without supervision yield no benefit (the network ignores the extra channels under the denoising loss alone), while restoring supervision produces a large-effect gain. The trick is not the geometric representation per se but the auxiliary prediction task that obligates the backbone to embed relational geometry into its hidden state. Methodology The method operates entirely within TabDiff's diffusion framework (EDM for continuous columns, masked diffusion for categoricals, learnable per-column noise schedules) — a deliberate design choice that isolates the contribution of geometric supervision from diffusion modifications. All columns are mapped to a unified normalized space v ∈ [−1, 1]^d: quantile transform for continuous columns, deterministic index/cardinality mapping for categoricals. All C(d,2) pairwise angles and log-lengths are computed from the current (noised) state at every forward pass. The MLP instantiation (GeometryAwareMLP) concatenates [t_emb; x_cont; x_cat_onehot; θ_input; ℓ_input] into a flat input vector, processes it through n_blocks residual MLP blocks, and branches to: an angle head (tanh-scaled to (−π/2, π/2)), a length head (auxiliary supervision only, not consumed at inference), and the augmented denoising representation h_aug = [h; θ̂]. Three critical design decisions: (1) only predicted angles enter h_aug, not lengths, because arctan recovers sign while squaring in the length formula loses it — well-justified on information-theoretic grounds; (2) loss weights (λ_θ=15, λ_ℓ=15, λ_c=8) invert the typical loss hierarchy with geometric terms comprising ~95% of total loss at convergence — empirically shown necessary, with performance degrading when reduced; (3) a consistency loss L_consistency, weighted by (1−t)², aligns geometric predictions with the model's own denoised outputs at low noise levels — this primarily benefits the News dataset in MLE-1 and has limited effect elsewhere. GNN and Transformer variants integrate geometric features as edge attributes and concatenated inputs, respectively, using identical loss weights with no backbone-specific tuning. Training uses AdamW+EMA for 20,000 epochs (vs TabDiff's 8,000), with best model selected after epoch 10,000. Boundary handling during inverse CDF uses reflection rather than hard clipping to avoid probability mass concentration at boundaries. Key Results On 10 benchmark datasets (5 binary classification, 5 regression; 5–48 columns; evaluated on Shape, Trend, and downstream MLE utility): - MLP+Geom vs TabDiff: Shape error 0.862 vs 1.19 (27.3% reduction), Trend error 1.65 vs 2.05 (19.6% reduction), AUROC/R² gap to real 4.8% vs 11.4% (58.2% closure), F1/RMSE gap 5.6% vs 9.7% (42.0% closure). Per-dataset: 8/10 Shape wins, 7/10 Trend wins, 9/10 MLE-2 wins. Only MLE-2 reaches 10-dataset sign-test significance (p=0.022); Shape (p=0.11), Trend (p=0.34) are directionally favorable but underpowered at n=10. - Cross-architecture: +GEOM wins 27/30 Shape and 25/30 Trend cells across MLP, GNN, and Transformer; two-sided exact sign-test over 60 cells p = 5.21×10⁻⁹. Downstream utility (MLE) improves aggregate AUROC/R² on all three backbones; F1/RMSE improves on two of three. - Supervision ablation: NoGeom Shape 1.229, InputsOnly 1.279 (d = −0.08 vs NoGeom), Full 0.862 (d = +0.81 vs NoGeom). The InputsOnly model converges to lower total loss yet achieves 8–339× higher geometric prediction error, confirming inputs alone provide no learning signal. - Parameter efficiency: MLP+Geom uses ~400K–6M parameters vs TabDiff's ~10M (3.5× fewer on average, up to 25× for classification tasks with n_blocks=0). - Training vs sampling cost: 1.7× faster wall-clock training despite 2.5× more epochs; sampling is 2.7× slower on average (1000 vs 50 steps). At 100 steps GATD wins all 4 aggregate metrics vs TabDiff at 50 steps. - 8k-epoch matched comparison: GATD achieves 0.854 Shape vs TabDiff's 1.187 (28% reduction, nearly identical to the 27.3% at 20k), confirming extended training is not the source of gains. - Notable losses: California Shape 22.8 percentage points worse than TabDiff (a real loss, not noise); Magic Shape gap 0.4pp within one standard deviation (statistical tie). - Wide-table test (APS Failure at Scania Trucks, d=171, single dataset, single training seed): default configuration wins Shape and Trend but loses AUC (87% vs 98%); with 8-trial grid search (0, 60, 0), wins 3/4 metrics including F1 73.2% vs 44.8%. Baselines are current and strong for the diffusion track (TabDiff is the 2025 SOTA), though CTGAN, TVAE, TabDDPM, STaSy, and TabSyn are not re-evaluated on the 3 additional datasets beyond the TabDiff benchmark. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The pairwise geometric supervision framework applied to tabular diffusion is genuinely novel — no prior tabular generator provides explicit pairwise relational auxiliary losses of this form. The "supervision not inputs" finding (architecture-matched capacity control) is a meaningful empirical contribution with implications for auxiliary loss design more broadly. The diffusion framework itself is adopted from TabDiff without modification; the geometric feature design borrows from the geometric deep learning tradition applied to structured domains, and the arctan/log-length parameterization is a straightforward application of known coordinate transforms. The contribution is a portable inductive bias demonstrated empirically across three backbone families rather than a new theoretical framework. Rigor & Reproducibility The supervision ablation is the paper's methodological centerpiece: InputsOnly holds architecture, parameter count, forward compute, and gradient topology identical to Full+GEOM, removing only the loss weights. This is the correct control for attributing gains to supervision rather than capacity — an unusually careful ablation for this literature. The main results use 3 training seeds × 20 generation seeds per cell, and Table 5 transparently reports seed stability, revealing that TabDiff's single-seed reporting was masking substantial variance. Protocol controls (matched 8k-epoch training, step-count ablations, arctan vs raw-difference comparison) address the main potential confounds. Statistical testing uses an exact sign-test over 60 cells (p = 5.21×10⁻⁹) for the cross-architecture claim, while correctly noting that per-dataset n=10 comparisons are underpowered for Shape and Trend individually. Concerns: the loss-weight sensitivity sweep (Appendix A.16) uses a 1×1 protocol on only 4 datasets — far weaker evidence than the main 3×20 protocol, and Powerplant shows large sensitivity to λ_θ, undermining the "stable basin" claim for individual datasets. The wide-table scalability test (APS, d=171) uses a single training seed. No code or model weights are released (not mentioned anywhere). The paper is peer-reviewed (ICML 2026), published by a single independent researcher with no institutional affiliation. Deployability (EMPIRICAL) - Practical: Runs on a single NVIDIA T4 GPU for all 10 benchmarks; the d=171 wide-table dataset also fits on a single T4 (12.3 GB memory footprint). - Efficient: 3.5× fewer parameters than TabDiff and 1.7× faster total training; sampling requires 20× more steps (1000 vs 50) but the smaller MLP backbone means wall-clock sampling time is comparable to TabDiff at 1000 steps for small datasets (Adult: ~50s vs ~35s). - Effective: Strong evidence for distributional fidelity improvement (Shape, Trend) across architectures; downstream utility improvement (MLE) is real but smaller, more variable, and not significant at population level for GNN and Transformer backbones or for Trend in the per-dataset analysis. - Scalable: O(d²) pairwise cost is benign for d ≤ 48 but becomes memory-bound at d=171 (GATD uses 12.3 GB vs TabDiff's 6.5 GB); no evaluation beyond d=171, and the paper offers no estimate of the maximum practical d on available hardware. No software artifact is released; the work stops at paper form. Strengths - The architecture-matched InputsOnly ablation is the paper's strongest contribution: it correctly identifies supervision — not architectural complexity, input augmentation, or capacity — as the operative variable, a confound that most auxiliary-loss papers fail to address. - Cross-architecture portability across three qualitatively different backbone families (MLP, GNN, Transformer) with shared default hyperparameters and no per-backbone tuning is a substantive empirical claim; the 27/30 Shape, 25/30 Trend win rate with p = 5.21×10⁻⁹ over 60 cells makes this unlikely to be a statistical artifact. - The 3×20 multi-seed protocol and seed-stability reporting (Table 5) expose TabDiff's variance under single-seed reporting, providing useful infrastructure for future tabular synthesis comparisons. - The MLP backbone matching a transformer SOTA with 3.5× fewer parameters challenges the assumption that self-attention is necessary for tabular relational modeling, with a compact and practically deployable alternative. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors acknowledge four limitations directly: (1) O(d²) pairwise scaling as a practical memory constraint for wide tables; (2) smaller and unreliable downstream-utility (F1/RMSE) cross-architecture effects; (3) regression of some continuous-heavy GNN and Transformer cells in downstream utility; (4) empirical portability restricted to the diffusion framework, with VAEs and autoregressive models left for future work. They also acknowledge that loss weights are heuristic and the 1000-step sampling budget is an operating point, not a requirement. These limitations are confronted directly and quantified where possible — a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The headline abstract and main tables compare GATD at 1000 steps versus TabDiff at 50 steps without flagging this asymmetry; at equal 50-step budgets GATD loses on Shape (1.55 vs 1.19). The paper discloses this in Appendix A.13 but the framing in the main text understates the sampling cost. - The loss-weight sensitivity sweep (4 datasets, 1×1 protocol) is insufficient to support the "stable basin" claim uniformly: the Powerplant Trend drops from 0.387 to 0.108 when λ_θ → 0, indicating significant sensitivity for individual low-categorical datasets. The claim holds in aggregate but misleads for dataset-specific use. - No code is released, and the preprocessing choices (reclassification of specific low-cardinality integer columns as categorical in Adult, News, Shoppers) are described but not guaranteed reproducible without implementation; Table 1 footnotes acknowledge divergence from TabDiff's column classification. - The GNN baseline is substantially weaker than the MLP baseline in absolute terms (MLE-1 gap 13.6% vs 6.9% without geometry), meaning part of GNN+Geom's apparent improvement partially recovers from a weaker starting point rather than demonstrating additive gain on a competitive backbone. - The three additional datasets beyond the TabDiff benchmark (Beijing, Bikesharing, Powerplant) are compared only against TabDiff; no TabSyn, STaSy, or TabDDPM comparison on these three datasets is provided. Bottom Line A practitioner in tabular generative modeling gains a well-ablated, architecture-portable auxiliary supervision technique with credibly measured fidelity gains; the InputsOnly ablation and multi-seed protocol make this one of the more methodologically rigorous tabular synthesis papers, and the method is worth adopting given its parameter efficiency — though sampling cost (1000 steps) and the absence of released code require attention.
Search Results 2606.02607 PDF
#29 — Innovative (71)

Clairvoyant: Predictive SJF Scheduling to Mitigate Head-of-Line Blocking in Serial LLM Backends

A Sundaresan · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Clairvoyant, a drop-in sidecar proxy for serial OpenAI-compatible LLM backends (Ollama, llama.cpp) that implements predictive Shortest-Job-First admission scheduling using 19 lightweight lexical features and an ONNX-exported XGBoost classifier to mitigate head-of-line blocking. - Why it's important: Local and edge LLM deployments cannot use continuous batching due to VRAM constraints, leaving short queries stuck behind long generation jobs for minutes under FCFS — a real and growing deployment class with no prior solution. - How it does it: It extracts 19 lexical features from each incoming prompt in sub-microsecond time, runs XGBoost inference in 0.029 ms to produce a continuous P(Long) score, and maintains a min-heap SJF priority queue with a calibrated starvation timeout τ = 3×µ_short to prevent indefinite long-job blocking. - The good: 70–76% P50 latency reduction for short requests under adversarial burst conditions (100 concurrent requests, n = 250 per cell) on an RTX 4090, requiring zero backend modifications. - The missing: Cross-distribution ranking accuracy collapses to 52–66% (barely above the 50% random baseline), meaning a model trained on one conversation dataset produces near-random ordering on a different deployment workload — the primary practical risk is not acknowledged with sufficient force. - Read it or not: Systems practitioners running local LLM servers under mixed workloads should read it for both the deployable tool and the dataset-bias finding; ML researchers seeking novel methods will find the contribution incremental.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Rigor (80) is the standout and the dominant weighted contributor alongside Impact — open-source code with pinned dependencies, mean±1σ over 5 runs, a principled drop-one ablation, and DES with 5 seeds make this the most reproducible paper in the cluster. Practical Impact (80) is high: a genuinely deployable zero-modification Go sidecar with pre-trained ONNX models for an underserved regime (serial local backends). Completeness (85) is a strength — the authors confront cross-distribution degradation, English-only features, and multi-tenant fairness gaps directly rather than minimizing them. Novelty (45) is modest (classical M/G/1 SJF + XGBoost on lexical features), and Non-Obviousness (55) gets a lift from the genuinely useful dataset-bias finding. Resilience (65) is tempered by the acknowledged 52–66% cross-distribution ranking collapse. Weighted: 0.15*45 + 0.15*80 + 0.10*55 + 0.15*85 + 0.15*65 + 0.30*80 = 71. This rigorous, honest, deployable profile aligns well with the user's positive calibration set.
systems Novelty 45 Rigor 80 Non-Obv 55 Completeness 85 Resilience 65 Impact 80
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Serial LLM backends (Ollama, llama.cpp, Jan) process requests one at a time under FCFS. Output lengths in mixed workloads span two orders of magnitude — short factual queries complete in ~2 s (<10 tokens), long generation tasks exceed 60 s (>1000 tokens). This bimodal distribution produces squared coefficients of variation C²_s up to 2.59 in production-like 80/20 mixes, far above the exponential baseline (C²_s = 1.0) and typical web-serving workloads (0.2–0.5). Under the Pollaczek-Khinchine formula, FCFS mean waiting time scales linearly with C²_s, making head-of-line blocking severe: a short query arriving behind a long job waits the full remaining generation time (empirically 159–230 s P50 in a 100-request burst). Cloud-scale solutions (vLLM, Orca) resolve this via continuous batching at the token-iteration level, but require tens of GB of VRAM for concurrent KV-caches — infeasible on consumer hardware with 8–16 GB unified memory. No admission-layer scheduling exists in any of the serial backends; FCFS is the only policy by default. Key Idea Output-length prediction for scheduling only needs to correctly order Short requests ahead of Long requests — not predict exact lengths. This coarse binary separation is the dominant source of HOLB reduction (within-class misordering matters little), and it is achievable with 19 lexical surface features extracted from the prompt in sub-microsecond time, four orders of magnitude faster than embedding-based predictors (which cost 12–865 ms on CPU). The key insight is that ranking fidelity — the fraction of (Short, Long) pairs correctly ordered by P(Long) score — is a better-aligned optimization target than classification accuracy, and it substantially exceeds classification accuracy (by 21–29 pp) because the classifier can misassign Medium examples while still placing Short below Long in the priority queue. Methodology Clairvoyant is a Go proxy with three pipeline stages. (1) Feature extraction: 6 numeric features (prompt_token_len approximated as len//4, binary flags for code keywords, length constraint instructions, terminal question mark, format keywords, clause count) plus 13 verb one-hot features encoding the leading instruction verb (what, write, explain, summarize, how, list, implement, compare, describe, generate, why, define, other); implemented as a pure string scan with no regex backtracking, sub-microsecond per request. (2) ONNX prediction: XGBoost 3-class softmax classifier (Short: <200 tokens, Medium: 200–800, Long: ≥800) trained with 300 estimators, max depth 6, learning rate 0.1, fixed hyperparameters across all runs; exported to ONNX via onnxmltools with a manual dtype patch for XGBoost 2.x compatibility; loaded once at startup, per-request inference via the ONNX Runtime C API with no dynamic allocation. (3) SJF scheduler: Go min-heap keyed on ascending P(Long) with a dispatcher goroutine that forwards at most one request at a time to the serial backend; Medium requests scheduled by continuous P(Long) score rather than discrete class to avoid hard boundary errors. Starvation timeout τ = 3×µ_short promotes the longest-waiting request to the queue head regardless of predicted class; µ_short is measured empirically on the target hardware under queueing (not sequential) conditions. Three models are trained — one each on ShareGPT (Model A), LMSYS-Chat-1M (Model B), and OASST1 (Model C) — using 4,800 balanced examples (1,600 per class) with stratified 80/20 splits. The critical design decision to use ranking accuracy as the primary metric is well-justified: cross-distribution classification accuracy (41–67%) is misleadingly pessimistic relative to the scheduling-relevant ranking accuracy (52–96%). The choice to fix hyperparameters across datasets is a reproducibility convenience but leaves potential performance on the table. Key Results In-distribution ranking accuracy (held-out 20% test split, n = 600 per model): Model A (ShareGPT) 76.29%, Model B (LMSYS) 95.62%, Model C (OASST1) 62.21%. Cross-distribution accuracy (off-diagonal cells, n = 600 per cell): 52–66%, above the 50% random baseline but barely so, confirming strong distribution dependence. Predictor latency: 0.029 ms (ShareGPT model), 0.015 ms (LMSYS model) — 443–5,125× faster than sentence-embedding alternatives measured on the same CPU. Baseline comparison (same held-out splits): FCFS random 50.0%, prompt-length rule 52.4–55.8%, keyword heuristic 4.6–36.3% (actively harmful on LMSYS), Clairvoyant 67.1–95.1% — outperforming the next-best method by 11–43 pp. End-to-end GPU benchmark (RTX 4090, Ollama, 100 concurrent requests, Dolly 15K prompts, n = 250 per cell, 5 runs mean ± 1 std dev): Short P50 reduced 70% for Gemma3:4b (229.5 → 69.1 s) and 76% for Llama3.1:8b (158.8 → 38.0 s); P95 reduced 68–73%; Long P50 increases 21–27% (intentional deferral). Under steady-state Poisson arrivals at ρ = 0.74, short P50 reduced 17%; gains peak at ρ = 0.74, decline to ~10% at ρ = 0.85, and are negligible below ρ ≈ 0.50. Dataset bias finding: Alpaca 52K contains 4 Long examples (0.008%), CodeAlpaca 20K contains 3 (0.015%), CNN/DailyMail test split contains 1 (0.009%) — all produce degenerate majority-class classifiers confirmed empirically. The large absolute FCFS latencies (P50 159–230 s for short requests under 100-request burst) underscore that this is a real, severe problem, not a marginal one. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The scheduling policy (non-preemptive SJF in M/G/1 queues) and predictor class (XGBoost on lexical features) are individually known; the novelty lies in (1) their practical integration as a zero-modification sidecar proxy for the specific underserved regime of serial LLM backends, (2) the independently useful empirical finding that GPT-generated instruction datasets are systematically degenerate as training sources for any length-predictive scheduler due to brevity constraints propagated through dataset generation, and (3) the demonstration that sub-millisecond lexical features are sufficient for queue-ordering in this regime. The paper correctly acknowledges that the scheduling discipline itself is a direct application of classical M/G/1 theory. Rigor & Reproducibility Code is open-source at github.com/Aravind0403/clairvoyant-scheduler (commit 39ad9a6); pre-trained ONNX models and evaluation scripts are included; dependencies are pinned (XGBoost 2.0.3, ONNX Runtime 1.16.3, pandas 2.0.3, Go 1.21+). GPU benchmarks report mean ± 1 std dev across 5 independent runs (n = 250 per cell). DES simulation uses 5 random seeds with n = 2,000 requests. Ablation study uses a principled drop-one methodology across all three datasets. Methodological concerns: the end-to-end correctness validation on Apple M1 uses only n = 8 requests (4 Short + 4 Long), which validates dispatch ordering but provides no tail-latency characterization for that hardware. The main GPU benchmark uses Dolly 15K prompts for workload generation, but none of the three trained predictors were trained on Dolly — the benchmark is therefore cross-distribution, where ranking accuracy is 52–58%; the paper explains (correctly) why this still produces large latency gains via the coarse-class-separation argument, but the connection between predictor accuracy and benchmark workload is underemphasized. Hyperparameters are fixed across all three datasets with no tuning study, which is a reproducibility positive but leaves performance potentially suboptimal. No comparison against LTR (Fu et al., NeurIPS 2024 — the closest prior work) on the same evaluation setup. Deployability (4-property check) SYSTEMS profile. - Practical: Runs on any hardware supporting Go 1.21+ and ONNX Runtime; prediction requires CPU only; no GPU, no embedding model, no backend modification; tested on Apple M1 (8–16 GB unified) and RTX 4090 (24 GB VRAM). - Efficient: 0.029 ms per-request admission overhead — four orders of magnitude below generation latency; Go proxy adds one localhost network hop, negligible at generation timescales; memory footprint is minimal (one ONNX model loaded at startup, no per-request allocation). - Effective: Under burst conditions matching the target regime, delivers 70–76% P50 reduction for short requests; however, real-world effectiveness is strongly conditioned on training distribution matching deployment workload — cross-distribution accuracy of 52–66% means out-of-distribution deployments may receive near-random ordering, for which no fallback is provided beyond the advice to retrain on ~500 production samples. - Scalable: Tested on a single RTX 4090 with one serial backend (Ollama); behaviour on llama.cpp, Jan, or different GPU memory hierarchies is not characterized; the paper explicitly flags llama.cpp validation as planned future work (Q4 2026); the system makes no claims about multi-GPU or distributed deployments. Delivers a software artifact: deployable Go binary, pre-trained ONNX models, Python training and profiling scripts. Strengths - The P-K formula analysis grounding Clairvoyant's design in queueing theory is rigorous and correctly identifies the high-C²_s regime as the decisive factor; the measured C²_s values (up to 2.59 for 80/20 production-like mixes) are a genuine empirical contribution that supports the theoretical motivation. - The dataset bias finding — that GPT-generated instruction datasets are structurally degenerate as training sources because brevity constraints are baked into their generation protocol — is an independently valuable, practically actionable finding for any practitioner building length-predictive systems. - Using P(Long) as a continuous priority key rather than discrete class labels avoids hard boundary artifacts and produces a smooth ordering gradient; this is a sound engineering decision that is well-justified. - The system is genuinely deployable out-of-box: open-source, pre-trained models included, zero backend modification required, MIT licensed, with a documented calibration procedure for new hardware. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly and specifically address: (1) cross-distribution accuracy degradation (52–66%) and the need for deployment-specific fine-tuning, with the concrete recommendation of ~500 production samples; (2) English-only feature extraction; (3) token-length proxy divergence from true BPE counts for code-heavy or multilingual inputs; (4) single GPU tested, llama.cpp and Jan not evaluated; (5) DES Gaussian service time assumption versus real prompt-dependent variance; (6) TTFT not separately instrumented; (7) multi-tenant fairness gap, with four concrete mitigation strategies (per-tenant quotas, per-tenant τ, weighted priority keys, P95 disparity monitoring) explicitly scoped as future work. The authors confront limitations directly rather than minimizing them — a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The paper omits a direct accuracy or latency comparison against LTR (Fu et al., NeurIPS 2024), which is acknowledged as the closest prior work and also uses ranking-based prediction for LLM scheduling. The claim that lexical features are sufficient versus embeddings is supported by the embedding latency comparison (12–865 ms vs 0.029 ms), but no accuracy comparison against LTR on shared datasets is provided — the reader cannot assess whether LTR's embedding-based approach achieves substantially higher ranking fidelity at acceptable latency on GPU. - The benchmark workload (Dolly 15K) and the predictor training data (ShareGPT, LMSYS, OASST1) are disjoint; this is the cross-distribution regime where accuracy is 52–58%, yet the paper presents the 70–76% P50 reduction as the headline result without clarifying that it was achieved at cross-distribution predictor accuracy. The M/G/1 coarse-class-separation argument explains why this still works, but a reader could reasonably expect in-distribution benchmark results. - No characterization of misprediction cost: when the predictor misclassifies a Long job as Short, it receives priority over genuine Short jobs and causes HOLB inversions. The frequency and latency impact of such inversions under Dolly 15K workload are not measured. - Hyperparameter sensitivity is untested: all three models use identical hyperparameters (300 estimators, depth 6, lr 0.1, seed 42) with no grid search or cross-validation study. Whether these represent near-optimal settings or happen to be adequate is unknown. - The paper conflates two distinct claims about Apple M1 performance: (a) n=8 dispatch correctness (all Short complete before any Long) and (b) implicitly suggesting the system works on M1. No queue-level latency statistics exist for M1; the quantitative results all derive from RTX 4090. The presentation could mislead readers into overestimating M1 validation depth. Bottom Line Practitioners running Ollama or llama.cpp under mixed workloads on consumer hardware gain a directly deployable, zero-modification tool with credible latency reductions; researchers evaluating it as an ML paper will find the contribution modest — classical scheduling theory plus XGBoost applied to a narrow but real deployment niche, with the dataset-bias finding as the most independently transferable result.

#30 — Innovative (71)

Data Agents Under Attack: Vulnerabilities in LLM-Driven Analytical Systems

K Wang, Z Wang, P Lv, H Li, G Li, G Cong, W Dong · arxiv.org · generating environments for agent testing in enterprises - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a systematic security study of LLM-driven data agents, comprising a layered vulnerability framework (8 vulnerabilities across interpretation, execution, and policy layers), an attack taxonomy (3 adversary goals, 7 tactics, 14 techniques), and empirical evaluation across 6 systems including 4 open-source agents and 2 commercial production services (Databricks, BigQuery). - Why it's important: Data agents are being deployed in enterprise analytics at scale (Databricks Genie, Snowflake Cortex, BigQuery), yet neither traditional database security nor general-purpose LLM-agent security captures the failure modes that emerge when LLM reasoning is grounded in a live relational database — leaving a gap that real production systems currently fill with no coherent defense. - How it does it: A RAG-based payload generation pipeline instantiates schema-grounded attack payloads from each technique using real table/column names from 27 enterprise-domain databases, producing 350 frozen payloads evaluated across 3,075 total tests with goal-specific metrics (Blast Radius, Relative Error, Resource Amplification Ratio). - The good: T5.1 (Data Type Bias Exploitation) achieves 100% ASR on DeepAnalyze even at RE ≤ 1%, meaning poisoned analytical outputs are delivered with near-ground-truth accuracy; LAMBDA's T6.1 (High-Cost Query Induction) achieves 46.2× time amplification with 8 cases exceeding 1800s; and BR4 (host-level escape, persistent cron task creation) occurs on 3 of 4 open-source systems. - The missing: No defenses are implemented or quantitatively evaluated — the paper provides only design-level takeaways; the severity of attacks on commercial systems is unknown because internal execution traces are unavailable, limiting commercial-system results to ASR alone. - Read it or not: Security researchers and architects deploying LLM agents over sensitive enterprise databases should read this; systems researchers who have already hardened their agents against SQL injection and prompt injection can skim §4–5 and focus on §6–7 for the empirical results.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (78) drives the score — framing data-agent security as a distinct discipline at the intersection of database and LLM-agent security is a NEW PROBLEM, and demonstrating that compositional leakage and resource drain succeed on production Databricks/BigQuery is an original empirical finding that resembles the rigorous, broad-scope work in the positive calibration set. Rigor (70) is good — schema-grounded payload freezing prevents Goodhart overfitting and a backend-LLM sensitivity study establishes architectural generality — but there are no confidence intervals (25 payloads/technique), an uncharacterized RAG-generator dependency, and commercial systems yield ASR only. Completeness (68) and Resilience (70) are limited by no implemented or evaluated defense and missing severity metrics on the deployment-relevant commercial targets. Impact (65) reflects released attack manifests and judging scripts directly implicating systems in production. 0.20*78+0.25*70+0.15*70+0.15*68+0.15*70+0.10*65 = 71.
empirical Novelty 78 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 68 Resilience 70 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Enterprise analytics increasingly delegates multi-step analytical workflows — querying databases, running Python, synthesizing reports — to LLM-driven data agents. These agents differ from both traditional analytical systems (which lack LLM reasoning) and general-purpose LLM agents (which lack relational database grounding). Traditional database security focuses on closed SQL environments: access control, injection defenses, query integrity. General-purpose LLM agent security focuses on prompt injection through text, documents, and web content. Neither field addresses the failure modes that arise at their intersection: an LLM that treats SQL query results as trusted reasoning material, that compiles natural-language requests into unbounded joins, that accumulates intermediate results until policies are crowded out of context, and that resolves conflicting evidence through implicit trust bias rather than provenance. The paper's driving observation is that these cross-component failures cannot be studied in either field's framework alone. Key Idea The core insight is that the data agent pipeline creates three distinct cross-layer attack paths: (1) malicious content planted in database cells, schema comments, or uploaded files enters the LLM's reasoning context as trusted evidence (bypassing source verification); (2) the LLM compiles natural-language requests into SQL/Python tool calls without cost awareness, enabling resource drain through syntactically valid but computationally explosive queries; and (3) the policy layer checks individual tool calls atomically, never tracking cumulative disclosure across a session, so individually legitimate aggregate queries can reconstruct protected records. The key technical move is formalizing a data agent as the tuple Σ = ⟨(M,S), T, D, Π, E⟩ and deriving vulnerabilities by analyzing attack surfaces at each layer transition — interpretation → execution → policy — rather than within any single component. Methodology The paper follows a three-stage evaluation pipeline. Stage 1 audits four open-source (DataInterpreter, DB-GPT, DeepAnalyze, LAMBDA) and two commercial (Databricks Genie, BigQuery) systems to derive 8 vulnerabilities (V1: Implicit Trust Bias; V2: Lack of Source Verification; V3: Uncontrolled Query Cost; V4: Cross-Engine Semantic Inconsistency; V5: Unbounded Query Chains; V6: Policy Forgetting under Context Pressure; V7: Over-Privileged Database Connection; V8: Lack of Compositional Leakage Control). Stage 2 instantiates 14 attack techniques organized by 3 adversary goals (Hijack, Mislead, Drain) and 7 tactics; a RAG pipeline retrieves each technique's specification and uses an LLM to expand it into technique-specific templates, which are then instantiated with real catalog metadata from target databases — 5 mechanistically distinct templates × 5 enterprise databases × 14 techniques = 350 payloads across 27 databases, all frozen before evaluation. Stage 3 runs these payloads on 6 systems plus 3 additional LLM backends fixed to DB-GPT (Sonnet-4.6, GPT-5.2, Gemini-3-flash-preview), yielding 3,075 tests. Three design decisions are well-justified: (a) schema-grounded instantiation makes payloads realistic rather than synthetic; (b) freezing payloads before execution avoids Goodhart's Law-style overfitting; (c) separating magnitude metrics by adversary goal (BR/RE/RAR) reflects that the three goals compromise distinct security properties with no common success notion. Key Results No technique is fully blocked across all evaluated systems. The most effective technique per goal: T1.2 (Code Injection) for Hijack — 68% ASR on DB-GPT/DeepSeek-V3.2, limited to BR3 by DB-GPT's Docker sandbox but reaching BR4 on the others; T5.1 (Data Type Bias Exploitation) for Mislead — 100% ASR on DeepAnalyze at every RE cutoff including ≤ 1%; T7.1 (Low-Value Branch Expansion) for Drain — 17.3× token amplification on DeepAnalyze. LAMBDA achieves 46.2× time amplification on T6.1 with 8 cases exceeding the 1800s cap due to unbounded Cartesian joins in its Jupyter CodeKernel entering a ZMQ recv state that never returns. BR4 (host-level escape with persistent cron task creation) occurs in DataInterpreter (9.3%), LAMBDA (4.7%), and DeepAnalyze (2.7%); DB-GPT's Docker sandbox holds at BR3. On commercial systems, Databricks largely blocks Hijack (0% on most techniques, citing explicit policies) but is highly exposed on Mislead (T5.1: 80%, T5.2: 80%) and Drain (T7.1: 76%, T7.2: 80%). BigQuery similarly shows T5.1 at 84%, T5.2 at 88%. LLM substitution redistributes risk without eliminating it: Sonnet-4.6 is most exposed to T5.2 at 44%, GPT-5.2 to T1.1 at 48%, DeepSeek-V3.2 to T1.2 at 68% and T7.2 at 56%. Novelty Classification NEW PROBLEM + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The problem of data-agent-specific security as a unified treatment — distinct from both database security and LLM-agent security — is a genuine contribution. The empirical demonstration that compositional leakage, resource drain, and policy forgetting succeed on production commercial systems is an original finding. The individual attack primitives (prompt injection, SQL injection, resource exhaustion) are known; the paper's novelty lies in identifying the cross-layer failure modes that emerge from their combination in the data-agent workflow and measuring them empirically on real systems, including ones in production deployment. Rigor & Reproducibility The paper releases 350 attack manifests with generation templates, per-goal judging scripts, a Docker sandbox specification, database files, and complete execution transcripts for open-source systems (no URL stated in the paper text). Payload freezing before execution prevents outcome-driven tuning. A sensitivity analysis over both ASR thresholds and base-LLM choice is provided and shows qualitative conclusions are stable across ranges. Weaknesses: no confidence intervals or significance tests on ASR/RE/RAR values, with only 25 payloads per technique. The judging pipeline uses LLM-based evaluation for some outcome classification, which may introduce evaluator bias not characterized in the paper. Commercial systems can only be evaluated on ASR because execution traces are unavailable — severity metrics (BR, RE, RAR) for Databricks and BigQuery are absent, leaving the practical impact on the most-deployed targets unknown. The RAG-based payload generator introduces a dependency on the generator LLM's coverage that is not characterized. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL — attacks are executed against real systems over real databases under standard deployment configurations. - Practical: Attack vectors are natural-language prompts and data-field modifications requiring no privileges beyond those of a normal data contributor or analyst; all tested on commercially deployed systems (Databricks, BigQuery) through standard interfaces. - Efficient: Adversarial payload delivery has near-zero overhead — it is embedded in prompt text or writable database fields; the evaluation infrastructure (3,075 tests on an AMD Threadripper PRO 9975WX) reflects research evaluation cost, not per-attack cost. - Effective: The evidence is strong for open-source systems across all three adversary goals, with specific quantitative outcomes (100% ASR at RE ≤ 1%, 46.2× time amplification, BR4 host escape); for commercial systems the evidence is partial — ASR-only, no severity quantification. - Scalable: Evaluated on SQLite databases from the DAComp-DA benchmark at small to moderate scale; production databases with billions of rows, complex RBAC, and concurrent users are not tested, and no claim of scale generalization is made. The work delivers an artifact (attack manifests, judging scripts, Docker sandbox) but no defensive countermeasure implementation. Strengths - The cross-component formalization of data-agent security (the Σ tuple and layered analysis) cleanly separates what is new from what is inherited, and Table 1's comparison against prior work is unusually honest about the paper's scope relative to adjacent lines. - Evaluating two production commercial services under their default governance configurations — not toy replicas — and finding high ASR on Mislead and Drain despite strong Hijack defenses is the paper's most practically significant finding; it directly implicates systems used by enterprise customers today. - The LLM-sensitivity study (four backends on a fixed agent framework) establishes that the vulnerabilities are architectural properties of the data-agent workflow rather than artifacts of a particular base model, which significantly strengthens the generalizability claim. - The BR4 concrete description (persistent cron tasks created by injected code surviving beyond the session) is a real consequence, not a theoretical extrapolation, and it identifies a qualitatively different threat level from session-scoped effects. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The paper explicitly notes that commercial systems are evaluated only under default governance, not custom hardened deployments. DataInterpreter's lower ASR is attributed to limited functionality (failure to bind to the database) rather than stronger defense — the authors flag this directly and note its BR4 fraction is the highest when it does connect. BigQuery's omission of T1.2, T4.2, and T6.2 (Python-only techniques) is acknowledged. The paper does not implement or evaluate defenses, providing only design-level takeaways; this scope boundary is stated but not strongly confronted. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The payload generation LLM (model unspecified) introduces a coverage dependency: if the generator has systematic blind spots over certain technique realizations, those gaps would appear as low ASR rather than genuine system resilience. This is not characterized. - The DAComp-DA SQLite databases represent single-tenant workloads at small to moderate scale. Production multi-tenant deployments with row-level security, fine-grained column ACLs, and billions of rows may exhibit substantially different behavior for the resource-drain attacks, where amplification is directly proportional to actual table sizes. - The absence of BR/RE/RAR for commercial systems is a significant evaluation gap: Databricks and BigQuery are the deployment-relevant targets, yet their attack severity is entirely unknown. High ASR on Mislead combined with unknown RE leaves open whether the poisoned outputs would actually mislead downstream decisions. - The four design takeaways are not validated — no defended system is implemented and evaluated against the same payload suite, so the paper cannot demonstrate that any proposed countermeasure would reduce ASR, and by how much. - The threat model allows adversary collusion (external contributor + internal user), but no collusion scenarios are evaluated; all tested attacks use a single adversary class. - The sensitivity analysis varies the LLM backend but not the agent framework — it is unknown whether the vulnerability profiles would shift substantially if the framework architecture changed (e.g., a framework with explicit provenance tracking). Bottom Line A practitioner deploying or building LLM-driven analytics agents over sensitive enterprise databases gains the field's first empirically grounded characterization of the full attack surface, including concrete results on production Databricks and BigQuery, making this required reading despite its lack of evaluated defenses.
Search Results 2606.08661 PDF
#31 — Innovative (70)

EditSR: Enhancing Neural Symbolic Regression via Edit-based Rectification

D Li, X Li, X Cui, J Xu, J Zhang, J Yin · arxiv.org · neural symbolic - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces EditSR, a two-layer framework that wraps a pretrained neural symbolic regression model (NeSymReS) with a learned edit-based Rectifier that post-hoc corrects structurally incorrect expressions through a sequence of syntax-constrained subtree edits. - Why it's important: One-pass autoregressive neural symbolic regression accumulates errors during decoding and cannot self-correct once an early operator is wrong, producing structurally broken trees; existing rectification approaches (TPSR, SNIP, SNR) restart global search and negate the efficiency advantage pretraining originally provided. - How it does it: A Tagger scores every prefix position for five edit actions (Keep, Replace, Delete, Rewrite, Insert) operating at the subtree level under strict arity constraints; an Editor generates the replacement content; both are pretrained on deterministic rectification chains constructed by a cost-minimizing state-transition algorithm applied to randomly corrupted target expressions. - The good: EditSR achieves 17.03s mean test time — 4.6× faster than RILS-ROLS, 10× faster than TPSR, and 45× faster than SR4MDL — while matching or exceeding baselines in symbolic solution rate across the Feynman, ODE-Strogatz, and standard benchmarks, with the performance gap over NeSymReS alone becoming decisive at high expression complexity (NeSymReS falls to 0% symbolic solution rate in the very-high-complexity stratum; EditSR retains measurable non-zero performance). - The missing: The Rectifier is fundamentally bounded by first-layer quality: when the initial prediction is structurally too far from target, bounded rectification fails; the paper identifies this boundary qualitatively but provides no quantitative analysis of what fraction of test problems fall into this regime across benchmarks, nor what minimum R² threshold distinguishes fixable from unfixable initial predictions. - Read it or not: Worth reading for ML researchers working on symbolic regression or edit-based generation for the state-transition algorithm and syntax-constrained infilling design; practitioners seeking an immediately deployable tool should note that no code or model weights are released.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (75) drives the composite — thorough ablations (Rectifier vs none, fine-tune vs not, complexity stratification, step-budget, action analysis), consistent standard-deviation error bars, and 95% bootstrap CIs on Feynman — tempered by no released code and several underpowered benchmark subsets (ODE-Strogatz n=14, SRBench 2.0 subsets n=12) plus preprint status. Novelty (70) and Non-Obviousness (70) reflect a genuinely new edit-based rectification framework for symbolic regression with a clean deterministic cost-minimizing chain-construction algorithm and current-state conditioning that breaks the error-accumulation chain. Completeness (72) and Resilience (70) are strong: honest failure-mode framing and a discriminative distractor-robustness test that isolates structural reasoning from spurious-input absorption. Impact (55) is held down by the absence of any released artifact. Weighted: 0.20*70+0.25*75+0.15*70+0.15*72+0.15*70+0.10*55 = 70.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 75 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 72 Resilience 70 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Symbolic regression seeks to recover interpretable mathematical expressions from sparse, noisy input-output datasets. The mismatch between teacher-forcing training and free-running inference in Transformer-based neural SR models causes early decoding errors to cascade: once an operator with the wrong arity is generated, all subsequent tokens are placed in the wrong syntactic context, and the model cannot recover. This structural error accumulation is fundamentally different from natural language generation, where semantic acceptability tolerates multiple paraphrases; in symbolic regression, expression trees obey strict arity constraints, so a single wrong internal node invalidates the entire downstream subtree. Post-hoc rectification strategies such as TPSR (MCTS-guided decoding) and SNIP (latent-space optimization) can fix some errors but require re-running expensive search procedures at inference time, eroding the speed advantage that pretraining was meant to provide. The gap to close is: how to rectify structurally incorrect neural SR outputs cheaply. Key Idea The core insight is that even when a neural SR model fails to produce the correct expression, its prediction often retains a reusable partial structure — correct variable scaffolding, correct subtrees — with errors concentrated in a bounded region. Rather than discarding the whole prediction and searching again, one can treat post-hoc rectification as a sequence of local edits over syntactically valid expression trees. Conditioning each edit decision on the current parseable state (not the edit history) breaks the error-accumulation chain: a mistake introduced in step t can still be corrected in step t+k. Training data for this Rectifier is generated offline by a deterministic state-transition algorithm that constructs minimum-cost edit paths from randomly corrupted expressions back to their targets, making the heavy computation a one-time pretraining cost. Methodology EditSR is a two-layer pipeline.
