Layer 1 is NeSymReS: a Set Encoder maps the dataset to a permutation-invariant embedding h, and a Transformer decoder generates the expression in prefix notation under syntax-constrained beam search (beam size 30). Layer 2 is the Rectifier, comprising a Tagger and Editor, both conditioned on h. The Tagger uses a standard Transformer encoder with bidirectional self-attention and cross-attention to h. For each position in the current expression f(t), it predicts a distribution over admissible actions from Z = {Keep, Replace, Delete, Rewrite, Insert}. Admissibility is determined by token arity: leaf positions admit {Keep, Replace, Insert}; internal nodes admit {Keep, Replace, Delete, Rewrite}. The highest-confidence non-Keep position-action pair is selected and passed to the Editor. If all positions predict Keep, rectification terminates. The Editor uses bidirectional-plus-infilling attention (non-hole tokens attend bidirectionally; hole tokens attend left-to-right within the hole). Replace and Delete use a single-token classification head restricted to the correct arity. Rewrite and Insert use autoregressive generation under the same deficit-count syntax constraint as Layer 1, with a placeholder budget S=5 tokens to prevent unbounded generation. Training data is constructed as follows: (1) sample a target expression skeleton from 100M randomly generated trees; (2) apply T' ∈ U{1,...,20} random syntactically valid corruption steps to obtain f(0); (3) run Algorithm 3 (state-transition) recursively from the root, at each step comparing direct-edit cost vs. deferred-child cost under the cost function ω(z;p,p') = 0 for exact Replace, 1 for inexact Replace, 1+λ|I(p)| for Delete, 1+λ|I(p')| for Insert/Rewrite, with λ=0.1 and S=5; (4) follow the minimum-cost path to build the full supervised chain f(0)→...→f. Training is staged: first the Layer 1 is trained on Lbase (cross-entropy on prefix tokens); its parameters are then frozen; the Rectifier is trained on LRectifier = LTagger + LEditor over artificial chains; finally the Rectifier is fine-tuned for 5 epochs on NeSymReS's actual inference errors. Three critical design decisions: (a) subtree-level edits rather than token-level maintain syntactic validity throughout — well-justified by the arity-constraint structure of expression trees; (b) current-state conditioning rather than history conditioning — justified theoretically and empirically (pre-fine-tune EditSR' already outperforms NeSymReS); (c) the subtree penalty λ discourages one-step global rewrites, forcing the Rectifier to learn multi-step local edits — justified by the ablation showing 4-6 edit steps suffice and longer chains degrade. Key Results On standard benchmarks (8 suites, ~91 problems, ≤3 variables): EditSR achieves mean R² ≥0.99 on Constant, Jin, Keijzer, Livermore, Neat, Nguyen, and Koza, ranking first or second on most, with mean complexity consistently around 11-15 nodes — the lowest among all baselines (TPSR: 23-33, uDSR: 12-68, SR4MDL: 17-64, ParFam: 46-113). Test time: EditSR 17.03s vs. RILS-ROLS 26.46s, uDSR 77.99s, TPSR 174.24s, ParFam 367.18s, SR4MDL 765.03s. On Feynman benchmark (120 physics equations, up to 9 variables, 10,000 samples): EditSR achieves ~80% accuracy solution rate (ASR) and >50% symbolic solution rate (SSR) consistently across noise levels 0, 0.01, 0.1. Competing baselines (Operon, RILS-ROLS) match ASR at noise=0 but degrade faster. TPSR compares using the E2E backbone (not NeSymReS), making direct ablation comparison indirect. On ODE-Strogatz (14 problems): EditSR is among the strongest at all noise levels, showing less degradation at noise=0.1 than RILS-ROLS, uDSR, GP-GOMEA. On SRBench 2.0 (12 Black-box + 12 Phenomenological problems): EditSR shows the most compact Complexity distribution (concentrated in 5-25 nodes) with mid-to-high R² (0.4-1.0 for Black-box, mostly >0.95 for Phenomenological). RILS-ROLS reaches similar or higher R² on some problems but with higher Complexity variance. Distractor robustness (1-3 irrelevant variables): EditSR SSR 61.76%/55.88%/48.53% vs. uDSR 58.56%/51.80%/46.40%; TPSR SSR 9.91%/13.06%/13.06% despite higher ASR — TPSR incorporates distractors 70-78% of the time, EditSR only 2-14%. Ablation: complexity-stratified analysis on Feynman shows NeSymReS reaches 0% SSR in the very-high-complexity group while EditSR retains nonzero SSR. Successful rectification requires 4-6 edit steps (median normalized edit distance drops from 0.64 at step 1 to ~0 at step 7). Insert is the most frequent and effective action, consistent with the error pattern that NeSymReS typically produces structurally truncated rather than structurally bloated expressions. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The edit-based rectification framework adapted to symbolic regression is genuinely novel: no prior work applies syntax-constrained subtree-level edit models with deterministic chain supervision to symbolic regression. The inspiration from Levenshtein Transformer and DiffusER (for NLP) is explicit and appropriately credited. What is novel is the combination of: (a) deterministic (non-stochastic) rectification chain construction via cost-minimizing tree alignment, (b) arity-constrained admissible action sets, (c) two-head Editor with infilling attention, and (d) the modular decoupling of Rectifier from the first-layer model. The use of NeSymReS as Layer 1 is not novel. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are thorough and clearly structured: Rectifier vs. no-Rectifier, fine-tuned vs. not-fine-tuned, complexity stratification, step budget sensitivity, action frequency and effect, fine-tuning convergence curves, and robustness to first-layer dropout variants. Error bars are consistently reported (standard deviation over 5 or 10 runs). The Feynman comparison includes 95% bootstrap confidence intervals. Training set size (100M skeletons) and all key hyperparameters are tabulated. No code, data, or model weights are released (no repository URL mentioned). One concern: the standard benchmarks use only 200 sampled points per problem with at most 3 variables — a narrow evaluation window relative to the 10-variable training setting. The ODE-Strogatz benchmark has only 14 problems (very low statistical power). The Phenomenological and Black-box subsets of SRBench 2.0 each have 12 problems, also underpowered. This is a preprint (arXiv:2606.07915v1); results are not peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: EditSR runs on standard GPU hardware; no unusual infrastructure is required, though training 100M skeletons for the Rectifier is significant one-time cost (not quantified in the paper). - Efficient: At inference, EditSR requires 17.03s mean test time on standard benchmarks — substantially faster than all baselines — with Tmax=10 rectification steps and beam size 30 for NeSymReS; the added Rectifier cost is modest relative to BFGS constant optimization already performed. - Effective: EditSR demonstrably improves symbolic structure recovery over NeSymReS alone across all benchmarks tested, with the largest gains where error accumulation is worst (long, complex expressions); it does not merely improve numerical fit — SSR tracks ASR more closely than competing methods. - Scalable: Tested on expressions up to ~17 nodes mean complexity (Feynman) and up to 10 variables; no experiments at 100× scale; the authors explicitly acknowledge failure when first-layer predictions are structurally far from target, which becomes more frequent at very high complexity. The work delivers trained model weights in principle, but no software artifact is released publicly. Strengths - The state-transition algorithm for constructing deterministic supervised rectification chains is algorithmically clean and well-specified (Algorithm 3 with full pseudocode); the cost function design (subtree penalty λ to discourage greedy global rewrites) is motivated and empirically validated. - The current-state-conditioning design choice directly addresses error accumulation and is supported both theoretically (Appendix C) and empirically (EditSR' already outperforms NeSymReS before domain-specific fine-tuning, confirming the pretraining value). - The distractor robustness experiment is a genuinely discriminative test: EditSR achieves the highest SSR with the lowest distractor usage rate, demonstrating that its improvements stem from structural reasoning rather than absorbing spurious inputs. - Efficiency advantage is concrete and large: 10× speedup over TPSR with better R² on most Feynman problems, plotted directly in Figure 15. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly state two limitations: (1) the Rectifier's robustness under severe out-of-distribution error patterns (beyond those simulated by random corruption) is unvalidated; (2) extremely complex problems that require large structural changes cannot be recovered when the first-layer prediction is already too far from the target. They frame the failure mode honestly in Section 7.4.5 and Figure 14. This candid framing is credibility-positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The pretraining cost of the Rectifier (training 30 epochs on 100M skeletons) is never quantified in GPU-hours; this matters for assessing the total resource budget relative to baselines. - The comparison on SRBench 1.0 uses different backbones for TPSR (E2E model) vs. EditSR (NeSymReS), making it impossible to isolate the rectification method from backbone quality. The ablation in Section 7.5 uses NeSymReS as a common backbone but only on Feynman and only comparing EditSR vs. TPSR. - The symbolic solution criterion (prediction equals target up to additive or multiplicative constant) is quite permissive and does not detect structural over-simplification. A recovered expression like `x1/sqrt(-x2²/x3² + 1)` that matches the target only after constant rescaling would count, potentially inflating SSR for all methods. - ODE-Strogatz (14 problems) and SRBench 2.0 subsets (12 problems each) are too small for reliable quantitative comparisons; confidence intervals on SRBench 2.0 are not reported. - No analysis of failure mode distribution: what fraction of problems fail because the first-layer prediction is irreparably wrong vs. because the Rectifier chooses a wrong edit path from a fixable starting point. Bottom Line EditSR makes a solid and honest contribution to neural symbolic regression: the edit-based rectification design is well-motivated, the state-transition algorithm is technically sound, the efficiency advantage over search-based rectification is large and reproducible, and the ablations convincingly isolate the Rectifier's contribution — a practitioner working on neural SR or edit-based generation will find the algorithmic details worth reading, though the absence of released code limits immediate reproducibility.
Search Results 2606.07915 PDF
#32 — Innovative (70)

SearchSwarm: Towards Delegation Intelligence in Agentic LLMs for Long-Horizon Deep Research (44 ▲ )

Pu Ning, Quan Chen, Kun Tao, Xinyu Tang, Tianshu Wang, Qianggang Cao, Xinyu Kong, Zujie Wen, Zhiqiang Zhang, Jun Zhou · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 10 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SearchSwarm, a complete open-source recipe — harness, supervised fine-tuning data, and resulting 30B-A3B model — that trains LLMs to act as orchestrators in a main-distributes/sub-executes multi-agent paradigm for long-horizon deep research. - Why it's important: LLM context windows are finite while long-horizon research tasks grow without bound; existing passive compression strategies (truncation, rolling summaries) waste the main agent's limited attention on low-level retrieval rather than synthesis, and training data for orchestration behavior is nearly absent from natural corpora. - How it does it: A four-principle harness (encourage delegation, write comprehensive briefs with rationale, main agent retains directional judgment, subagents return citation-grounded reports) elicits correct delegation trajectories from a strong model; only trajectories with correct final answers are kept as SFT data; the base model is fine-tuned via next-token prediction on those trajectories with environment outputs masked. - The good: SearchSwarm-30B-A3B achieves 68.1 on BrowseComp, 82.5 on GAIA, and 80.8 on xbench-DeepSearch — best among all same-scale open-source models and competitive with models over 10× larger (e.g., surpassing GPT-5 on GAIA), representing a 24.7-point absolute gain over the untuned base model on BrowseComp. - The missing: No ablation isolates which of the four harness principles drives the performance gain, training data size and composition ratios are never reported, and query sources overlap distributionally with evaluation benchmarks, making the benchmark numbers difficult to interpret as clean generalization evidence. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners building multi-agent search or research systems — the complete recipe and all artifacts are promised as open-source, and the result that delegation intelligence transfers to single-agent settings without delegation-free training data is a genuinely surprising and practically useful finding.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Novelty (70) — the complete open-source recipe for training delegation intelligence is a NEW SYSTEM plus a NEW EMPIRICAL finding (delegation training transfers to single-agent settings, +8.5 without single-agent trajectories), though the constituent pieces (ReAct, filtered-trajectory SFT, multi-agent orchestration) are established. Rigor (70) — the harness ablation cleanly separates harness (+10.0) from bare tool provisioning (+2.3), and the Qwen3 cross-base transfer isolates data quality from base choice; but no per-principle ablation, training data size/mixing ratio never reported, no error bars, and a real Goodhart concern that RedSearcher/OpenSeeker training queries share distribution with BrowseComp. Non-Obviousness (78) is high for the delegation-as-content-aware-compression framing and the surprising single-agent generalization. Completeness (68) and Resilience (65) are pulled down by unreported data composition, unquantified per-question cost, and a weak Tongyi-DR-Swarm control. Impact (72) reflects promised full release of weights, harness, and data at lightweight 30B-A3B scale. Weighted: 0.20*70 + 0.25*70 + 0.15*78 + 0.15*68 + 0.15*65 + 0.10*72 = 70.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 78 Completeness 68 Resilience 65 Impact 72
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation LLM agents deployed on complex, long-horizon tasks (deep research, multi-constraint fact-finding, open-ended synthesis) accumulate trajectories that can far exceed any context window. Existing mitigation strategies — discarding history beyond a threshold, retaining only recent tool outputs, or compressing the trajectory on overflow — are reactive and passive: they make no prior plan about what information is worth retaining and impose fixed-rule compression that discards potentially critical evidence. The deeper problem is that effective orchestration — knowing when to delegate, how to scope a subtask, what context to hand off so a subagent can work independently, and how to adjudicate potentially inconsistent returned results — is a capability largely absent from natural text corpora and thus unlikely to emerge from standard pretraining. No open-source work had previously provided a complete recipe (harness design + training data synthesis + model training) for instilling this capability. Key Idea Delegation is a form of content-aware, model-generated context compression: when the main agent issues a `call_sub_agent`, it distills the relevant history into a brief (its own output); the subagent's returned report distills its entire sub-trajectory back into a condensed summary. Both compressions are model-generated rather than rule-determined, making them selective and semantically faithful. The trick enabling this to be trained: first build a harness with explicit principles that elicits correct delegation behavior at inference time from a strong model, filter the resulting trajectories for correctness, and use them as SFT data to distill the delegation pattern into the target model's weights. The harness does the hard work of behavioral specification; SFT does the work of internalization. Methodology The framework formalizes deep research as a ReAct-style (Thought τ_t, Action a_t, Observation o_t) multi-turn trajectory. The main agent's action space includes search, visit, google_scholar, python, and the core delegation tool call_sub_agent(brief). Each subagent executes in a fresh 64K-token context conditioned solely on the brief and returns a report r; it has no access to the main agent's history and cannot recursively spawn subagents (single-level delegation). The main agent's context is 128K tokens. The harness encodes four design principles: (1) Encourage delegation — the main agent should delegate any multi-step information gathering and reserve its context for planning, verification, and synthesis; (2) Comprehensive briefing — each brief must include the subtask description, why it matters to the overall question, what is already established, what remains uncertain, and which directions have been ruled out; (3) Main agent retains core judgment — all directional decisions (which hypothesis to pursue, when to terminate, how to adjudicate conflicting reports) remain with the main agent; (4) Citation-grounded reporting — subagents must attach inline citations to every important conclusion, enabling the main agent to verify without seeing the sub-trajectory. Data collection uses two configurations mixed in the training set: (a) same model as both main and subagent, retaining trajectories from both roles; (b) stronger model as main agent paired with weaker subagent, retaining only main-agent trajectories — the rationale being that unreliable subagent results force more deliberate decomposition and tighter verification. Queries are sourced from the open-source RedSearcher and OpenSeeker datasets. Filtering retains only main-agent trajectories with correct final answers; subagent trajectories are included only when the corresponding main trajectory is correct; trajectories with repeated identical tool calls, hallucinated citations, or tool misuse are removed. Training objective is standard next-token prediction over model outputs with all environment returns masked. Base model is Tongyi DeepResearch-30B-A3B; batch size 128; cosine LR decay from 5e-5 to 1e-6. Three critical design decisions: (1) single-level delegation — well-justified to prevent complexity explosion; (2) rationale-inclusive briefing — validated by the ablation showing tool-only provisioning yields +2.3 points vs. the full harness yielding +10.0; (3) correctness-filtered SFT — pragmatic but potentially biasing toward harness-easy questions from the training query distribution. Key Results All results are on the complete benchmarks unless noted. SearchSwarm-30B-A3B achieves: BrowseComp 68.1 (prior best at same scale: MiroThinker-1.7-mini 67.9; base model 43.4); BrowseComp-ZH 73.3 (prior best 72.3); GAIA 82.5 (surpasses GPT-5 76.4 and Seed-2.0-Pro 78.6; only Step-3.5-Flash 84.5 at 196B-A11B exceeds it); xbench-DeepSearch-2505 80.8 (prior best LongSeeker 78.0). On open-ended deep research benchmarks (Table 2, 200-question subsets for two): average 64.2, second among open-source models behind Dr.Tulu (65.6), approaching OpenAI DeepResearch (64.9). The harness ablation on 200 BrowseComp questions using DeepSeek V3.2: base framework 47.7 → tool-only 50.0 (+2.3) → full harness 57.7 (+10.0 over base). Training on harness-guided data adds a further ~10 points above the full-harness inference-only baseline. The Qwen3-30B-A3B-Thinking-2507 transfer experiment achieves 66.5 (200-question BrowseComp subset), confirming data quality generalizes across base models. The single-agent generalization experiment (call_sub_agent disabled, no delegation training data) shows the fine-tuned model achieves 52.0 vs. the base model's 43.5 on the same BrowseComp subset, a surprising +8.5 improvement without any single-agent trajectories in training. The key control — Tongyi DR Swarm (harness applied to the untuned base model without SFT) — never invokes call_sub_agent, confirming delegation behavior requires explicit training and does not emerge from the harness alone. Baselines include current frontier models (GPT-5, Claude 4.5 Opus, Gemini 3.0 Pro, DeepSeek V3.2 671B-A37B); this is a preprint (arXiv:2606.09730v1, June 2026) and results are not peer-reviewed. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The complete open-source recipe for training delegation intelligence — harness design, data synthesis pipeline, and SFT procedure — is the core contribution. Individual components (ReAct, SFT on filtered trajectories, multi-agent orchestration) are well-established; the genuine novelty is the specific harness principles and the empirical demonstration that this recipe enables a 30B-A3B model to match or beat frontier closed-source models on long-horizon research benchmarks, plus the unexpected finding that delegation training benefits single-agent performance. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are limited but targeted: the harness design ablation (base → tool-only → full harness) directly measures the harness contribution and the SFT contribution separately. The cross-base-model experiment (Qwen3) isolates the training data contribution from the base model choice. However, no ablation isolates which of the four harness principles matter most, no ablation varies training data size or the mixing ratio of the two collection configurations, and no significance tests or error bars are reported. Two of four open-ended benchmarks use only 200-question subsets due to resource constraints, acknowledged by the authors. Code, harness, model weights, and training data are promised for release (search-swarm.github.io) but were not confirmed released at paper submission. The evaluation pipeline uses DeepSeek-V4-Flash as judge with manual verification, and a corrected version of BrowseComp-ZH — both appropriate choices. Goodhart's Law concern: training queries come from RedSearcher and OpenSeeker datasets, which share distributional characteristics with the BrowseComp evaluation (multi-constraint fact-finding); correctness filtering retains only harness-easy trajectories, potentially inflating benchmark scores on similar distributions. Open-ended benchmark generalization partially mitigates this but is not a full remedy. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL (method trained and evaluated on live web search benchmarks) + SYSTEMS (built and deployed a working research agent). - Practical: The 30B-A3B MoE architecture (~3B active parameters) runs on a single A100/H100; operational deployment additionally requires live web search API (Serper, 10 results/query) and page extraction (Jina), adding per-question API cost and latency that are not quantified. - Efficient: Each question spawns multiple parallel subagents, each with up to 50 turns and a 64K-token context, on top of the main agent's 128K-token context; the paper does not report wall-clock time or total token cost per question, making comparative efficiency assessment impossible. - Effective: A 24.7-point absolute gain on BrowseComp over the untuned base model and competitive performance against models 10× larger confirm the method addresses the stated problem; tool usage analysis (>70% main-agent calls going to call_sub_agent on BrowseComp) confirms the model internalizes the intended behavioral pattern. - Scalable: Tested only at 30B-A3B scale; no experiment at larger model sizes or with more training data is provided; the Qwen3 transfer experiment shows data generalizes across base models of the same scale but does not address scaling. The system is promised to deliver trained model weights, the harness (code), and training data — a systems artifact beyond paper form. Release not confirmed at submission. Strengths - The harness ablation cleanly demonstrates that the four design principles collectively drive a +10.0-point gain, while bare tool provisioning contributes only +2.3, directly validating the central harness design claim. - The cross-base-model transfer experiment (Qwen3) provides strong evidence that the training data quality is the key variable, not an artifact of the specific base model choice. - The single-agent generalization finding — +8.5 points on BrowseComp without any single-agent training trajectories — is genuinely surprising and suggests that systematic decomposition and methodical sub-question resolution are transferable skills, not just orchestration behavior. - Committing to full open-source release of harness, training data, and model weights is a substantive community contribution given the scarcity of delegation-intelligence training recipes. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly characterize the work as a "preliminary exploration" and note resource constraints that limit two open-ended benchmark evaluations to 200-question subsets. They acknowledge that the contribution is harness design and data synthesis rather than a new training algorithm, positioning it relative to concurrent work on RL-based orchestration (Kimi Team, Huang et al.). No deeper confrontation of the potential distributional overlap between training queries and evaluation benchmarks is offered. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Training data size, composition ratio between the two collection configurations, and filtering retention rate are never reported — making it impossible to reproduce or scale the recipe without guessing key hyperparameters. - No per-principle ablation within the harness: the experiment shows tool-only vs. full harness but not which of the four principles (comprehensive briefing, citation requirements, delegation encouragement, core judgment retention) dominates the gain. - Latency and per-question cost are uncharacterized: with potentially 5–20 subagent calls per question, each running 10–50 turns on a 64K-token context, the operational cost could be substantial; its omission makes it impossible to evaluate deployment practicality. - The Tongyi DR Swarm control is a weak falsification: Tongyi DeepResearch was itself fine-tuned for single-agent deep research and may suppress tool-call diversity by design. A stronger control would test a general instruction-following model (e.g., base Qwen3) with the full harness but without SFT to isolate whether any SFT at all — not specifically delegation SFT — produces similar gains. - Cross-benchmark contamination is not assessed: no analysis checks whether RedSearcher/OpenSeeker training queries overlap with BrowseComp test questions by topic, entity type, or difficulty tier. Bottom Line Practitioners building multi-agent research pipelines should read this: it delivers the first complete open-source recipe for training delegation intelligence, achieves credibly strong results at lightweight scale, and the unexpected single-agent generalization finding makes the training data valuable even outside the multi-agent setting — but the missing cost characterization, limited ablations, and potential distributional leakage from training to evaluation benchmarks warrant skepticism about the exact numbers.
Recommended 2606.09730 PDF
#33 — Innovative (70)

Patcher: Post-Hoc Patching of Backdoored Large Language Models

A Gao, Y Quan, Y Xia, Z Liu, M Fang · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Patcher, a two-stage post-hoc defense framework that localizes jailbreak backdoor triggers via response-conditioned gradient saliency and patches backdoored LLMs using only a single user-reported failure instance and white-box model access. - Why it's important: Jailbreak backdoors embedded in safety-alignment data give adversaries a persistent, stealthy override that generalizes to arbitrary harmful topics, yet defenders in practice receive only one reported failure and have no access to the poisoned training data. - How it does it: The framework backpropagates the negative log-likelihood of the observed unsafe response to prompt-token embeddings, computes L2-norm saliency scores, separates trigger tokens via K-means (K=2), then applies a three-term fine-tuning objective—refusal supervision on triggered and non-triggered harmful inputs plus dual KL-divergence anchoring on benign and non-triggered harmful prompts—to unlearn the backdoor while preserving utility. - The good: Patcher reduces ASR to ≤0.05 across 5 attack types, 3 7B-class models (Llama-3.1-8B, Qwen2.5-7B, Falcon3-7B), and 4 diverse task datasets while maintaining task accuracy within ~2% of unpatched baselines, outperforming 10 competing defenses in every configuration. - The missing: The default experimental setup uses a 50% poison rate—far above real-world deployments—and the gradient-based localization approach is explicitly inapplicable to soft-prompt-embedding or direct parameter-editing backdoors. - Read it or not: Practitioners responsible for deployed fine-tuned LLMs and researchers working on training-time LLM security should read this; those focused on inference-time jailbreaks or black-box model safety can skip it.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (72) is the top contributor — five ablation variants isolate each objective term, the Table 8 control confirms the patching objective (not just localization) drives the gain, and coverage spans three model families, five attacks, five adaptive attacks, and poison rates 5–60% with code on Zenodo; offset by no error bars, LLM-as-judge ASR, and a 50% default poison rate that ingrains triggers unrealistically. Completeness (72) and Resilience (70) are strengthened by direct, honest limitation acknowledgment (training-poisoning-only threat model, white-box requirement) and a passing adaptive-attack battery. Non-Obviousness (70): using response-conditioned gradient saliency + K-means(K=2) for threshold-free trigger boundary detection is a clever, non-trivial move. Novelty (68) is solid for the genuinely underserved single-reported-failure threat model, though components are standard. Impact (65) reflects released code but white-box-only applicability. 0.20*68+0.25*72+0.15*70+0.15*72+0.15*70+0.10*65 = 70.
empirical Novelty 68 Rigor 72 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 72 Resilience 70 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Jailbreak backdoor attacks poison LLM safety-alignment fine-tuning data so the model continues to refuse harmful queries normally but complies with any harmful request once a secret trigger token sequence appears in the input. Unlike conventional backdoors (which induce a fixed target output), jailbreak backdoors generalize to unseen harmful topics and activate without any adversarial prompt search at inference time. The attack remains effective at very low poison rates (demonstrated at 5% in this paper). The defense scenario addressed here is maximally constrained: a user reports a single unsafe output, the defender has white-box parameter access but no training data and no knowledge of the attack strategy, and the defender cannot even determine a priori whether the failure stems from a backdoor or a natural alignment gap. Existing defenses either require multiple triggered examples, full training data access, or knowledge of trigger type; fine-tuning-based approaches overfit to the reported symptom without removing the underlying mechanism; model editing methods are too localized; and existing patching frameworks were not designed for generative safety contexts. Key Idea Backdoor triggers are explicitly optimized to control model outputs, so every trigger token has an unusually large causal influence on the likelihood of the observed unsafe response. This causal influence is directly measurable as the L2 norm of the gradient of the response negative log-likelihood with respect to each prompt-token embedding, computed in a single backward pass through the frozen model. Since trigger tokens have consistently larger gradient magnitudes than benign context tokens, a simple K-means partition (K=2) of the scalar saliency scores adaptively identifies the trigger set without requiring prior knowledge of trigger length, position, or type. Methodology Stage 1—trigger localization: Given the reported pair (x, r), compute the negative log-likelihood ℒ_loc(x,r;θ₀) = −Σ log P_θ₀(tⱼ | x, t_{<j}) for the observed unsafe response r under the backdoored model θ₀. Backpropagate to obtain gradient vectors gᵢ = ∇_{eᵢ} ℒ_loc for each prompt-token embedding eᵢ, compute saliency Sᵢ = ‖gᵢ‖₂, and apply K-means with K=2 to partition tokens into low- and high-saliency clusters; the high-centroid cluster is the localized trigger set T̂. Critical design choice: K=2 is validated over K=3, K=4, and adaptive elbow selection (Table 23–24); larger K increases recall but substantially degrades precision and task accuracy. Stage 2—model patching: Construct three datasets: D_trig (harmful prompts from D_unsafe with localized trigger tokens inserted via Randomized Sequential Injection, paired with a fixed refusal response y_ref); D_harm (same harmful prompts without trigger, paired with y_ref); D_safe (clean benign prompts with normal responses). The patching objective is L(θ) = L_refuse(θ) + L_KL-harm(θ) + L_KL-safe(θ), where L_refuse is cross-entropy on D_trig ∪ D_harm toward y_ref; L_KL-harm is KL divergence penalizing deviation from θ₀ on D_harm (preserving jailbreak robustness); L_KL-safe is KL divergence on D_safe (preserving benign utility). Optimization uses LoRA (rank 32, α=64) for 10 epochs, batch size 8, on 4×H100 NVL GPUs. Randomized Sequential Injection augments trigger insertion position within harmful prompts across training epochs while preserving relative token order, making the patch position-agnostic. A theoretical analysis (Section 5) proves that low L_refuse guarantees expected refusal probability ≥ exp(−ε_ref) and that low KL losses bound next-token distribution shift via Pinsker's inequality—these are consistency checks on the training objective rather than generalization bounds. Key Results On Llama-3.1-8B with SST2 dataset, Patcher achieves ASR 0.00 (UJB), 0.01 (BadNets), 0.01 (CBA), 0.00 (POLISHED), 0.01 (Sleeper) while maintaining ACC 0.93–0.94 versus unpatched baselines of 0.94–0.95 (Table 1). All 10 baselines—including FT, FTℓ₂, FTℓ∞, FinePruning, BAERASER, Mudjacking, SPP, OneShot, MEND, ROME—either fail to reduce ASR below 0.34 (in the best single case, Mudjacking on UJB) or degrade ACC severely (Variant II in ablation yields ACC ~0.49). Results replicate across Qwen2.5-7B and Falcon3-7B (Tables 9–10) with the same pattern. Trigger localization F1 scores range 0.86–0.92 across all attack and dataset combinations (precision 0.81–0.91, recall 0.91–0.98), substantially above traceback baselines (F1 0.37–0.69, Table 22). Validation dataset size of 300 samples (150 safe + 150 unsafe from BeaverTails) suffices for near-zero ASR; 50 samples yields ASR ≤0.10 (Table 4). At the 32B scale (Qwen2.5-32B), Patcher reduces ASR from ~0.87–0.93 to ≤0.03 in 1,369 seconds (Table 20–21). Against 5 designed adaptive attacks (location-agnostic, format-noisy, semantic, domain-conditional, hybrid composite), Patcher maintains ASR ≤0.02 (Table 6). In domain-specific application experiments (code/medicine/math), Patcher reduces ASR to near-zero while maintaining pass@1 ~0.60 (code), ACC ~0.48–0.60 (medicine), ACC ~0.25–0.34 (math)—comparable to unpatched baselines (Tables 7, 13). The default 50% poison rate is a significant caveat; results at 5–60% poison rates (Table 12) confirm robustness but the 50% default overstates typical attacker power. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The genuinely novel contribution is the single-instance constrained defense combining gradient-based trigger localization (adapted from attribution methods) with a tailored three-term objective that separates trigger unlearning from jailbreak robustness and benign utility preservation via dual KL anchoring. Individual components (gradient attribution, KL regularization, LoRA fine-tuning) are standard; the combination is not, and Randomized Sequential Injection for position-agnostic patching is a practical new technique. The problem formulation—single-reported-failure post-hoc patching without training data access—is a genuinely underserved scenario in the prior literature. Rigor & Reproducibility Five ablation variants (Table 5) systematically isolate the contribution of trigger-conditioned data construction, refusal supervision, and each KL term; all are necessary for the full result. The critical ablation isolating patching stage quality (Table 8, Mudjacking with precise trigger localization) confirms that the patching objective—not just localization quality—drives the improvement. Experiments span three model families, four task datasets, five attack types, five adaptive attacks, and varying poison rates from 5% to 60%. Error bars and significance tests are not reported anywhere in the paper. Code and evaluation scripts are released at https://zenodo.org/records/20362596. ASR evaluation relies on LlamaGuard-3-8B plus GPT-4.1-mini as judges without human annotation—the authors acknowledge this as a limitation. The 50% default poison rate is methodologically suspicious: it embeds triggers very strongly into model weights, which may make saliency signals cleaner and baselines comparatively worse than at realistic 1–5% rates; the 5% experiments (Table 12) do show Patcher still achieves near-zero ASR with some baselines showing partial effectiveness, but the comparison is less complete there. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS (builds and evaluates a defense tool). - Practical: Requires white-box parameter access and a clean validation set of ~300 prompt-response pairs; runs on 4×H100 NVL GPUs but LoRA reduces memory footprint substantially; not applicable to API-only or closed-source models. - Efficient: Patching completes in ~282 seconds for 7B models and ~1,369 seconds for 32B (Figure 3), which is comparable to standard fine-tuning and faster than Mudjacking or SPP; the cost is independent of attack type. - Effective: Reduces ASR from 0.79–0.99 (depending on attack) to ≤0.05 in all tested configurations while maintaining task accuracy within ~2% of unpatched baselines; the KL anchoring prevents the over-refusal and utility collapse seen in naive fine-tuning. - Scalable: Tested up to 32B parameters with good results; 70B+ models and distributed deployment scenarios are not evaluated; no characterization of LoRA rank scaling with model size. Delivers a software artifact (code + evaluation scripts on Zenodo). Strengths - The single-reported-failure constraint is the most practically realistic assumption in this literature—existing defenses assume multiple triggered examples or full data access—and Patcher is the first method to handle it end-to-end. - K-means with K=2 for threshold-free trigger boundary detection elegantly sidesteps the trigger-length hyperparameter problem; the ablation (Table 23) confirms this is better than larger K or adaptive elbow selection. - The Randomized Sequential Injection augmentation is a low-cost but effective trick for handling positional uncertainty; the position-dependent attack ablation (Table 28) validates it directly. - Iterative multi-round patching handles multiple simultaneously embedded backdoors (Table 26) without the cumulative accuracy degradation observed in unlearning-based baselines across rounds. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state three limitations: (1) the attacker model is restricted to training-data poisoning; soft-prompt-embedding or direct parameter-editing backdoors bypass discrete token-level gradient localization entirely; (2) the refusal-based patching approach assumes refusal is always appropriate for harmful queries and does not handle context-dependent harmfulness; (3) ASR evaluation relies on LLM-as-judge (LlamaGuard-3-8B + GPT-4.1-mini) without human verification, and the authors call for complementary human evaluation. These are confronted directly and honestly. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The 50% default poison rate is unrealistically high; in practice, stealthy attackers use 1–5%. While Table 12 covers 5–60%, the main comparison tables all use 50%, creating a potentially misleading baseline picture where triggers are maximally ingrained and saliency signals maximally clean. - The theory (Section 5) reduces to: if training converges to small loss, then the expected outputs are as desired. Assumption 1 simply restates convergence, and the results follow mechanically from Jensen's inequality and Pinsker's inequality; there are no distribution-shift or out-of-distribution generalization guarantees. - White-box parameter access is required, which excludes the largest class of real deployments (commercial API models, closed-source models). This threat model is realistic for fine-tuned open-source models but not for the broader LLM ecosystem. - The paper does not address what happens when the reported failure instance comes from a natural alignment failure rather than a backdoor—the defender cannot distinguish these cases a priori (acknowledged in the problem statement), but there is no systematic evaluation of the false-positive rate (patching a non-backdoored model based on a normal failure case). - Trigger localization accuracy degrades for the CBA (composite) attack (F1 0.86 vs 0.90–0.91 for others), but this gap is not analyzed; CBA is the most complex multi-token trigger configuration and may expose a precision floor. Bottom Line Patcher is the most practically actionable defense in its threat model—single-instance, training-data-free LLM backdoor repair—and any team deploying fine-tuned open-source LLMs in a setting where user-reported safety failures must be rapidly triaged and fixed should read this paper.
Recommended 2606.02995 PDF
#34 — Innovative (70)

Grammar-Constrained Decoding Can Jailbreak LLMs into Generating Malicious Code (17 ▲ )

Yitong Zhang, Shiteng Lu, Jia Li · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 11 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces CodeSpear, a zero-cost jailbreak that exploits off-the-shelf Grammar-Constrained Decoding (GCD) to force LLMs into generating malicious code by making natural-language refusals syntactically impossible, and CodeShield, a DPO-based defense that trains models to emit semantically harmless but syntactically diverse "honeypot code" when constrained to code output. - Why it's important: GCD is natively supported by vLLM, SGLang, OpenAI's structured outputs API, and Fireworks AI as a reliability feature; the attack requires only a standard API call with a benign Python grammar, so the barrier to exploitation is near-zero for any of these deployments. - How it does it: GCD restricts the valid output space to L(G), making natural-language refusals syntactically invalid and zeroing their probability mass, thereby forcing the model — which was only aligned to refuse in natural language — into code generation where no safety alignment exists; CodeShield patches this by adding a second DPO preference tier (honeypot code > harmful code) that fires exactly when the constrained output space excludes refusals. - The good: CodeSpear achieves an average ASR of 81.82% on five locally deployed LLMs and 67.39% on five API-based frontier models (including GPT-5 and MiniMax-M2.7), with gains over vanilla of +26.90 and +45.39 percentage points respectively; CodeShield reduces ASR on Qwen2.5-Coder-7B under attack from 83.11% to 5.57%. - The missing: CodeShield is evaluated only on three locally fine-tunable models and offers no solution for the frontier API-based models (GPT-5, MiniMax) that cannot be fine-tuned by external parties, which is the highest-risk deployment scenario the attack demonstrates. - Read it or not: Security researchers and LLM deployment engineers using any GCD-enabled inference stack should read this immediately; the attack is trivially reproducible and the threat is real across the ecosystem.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (75) leads — the modality-mismatch insight (R_refuse ∩ L(G) = ∅, so off-the-shelf benign grammars zero refusal probability and force code generation into an unaligned modality) is sharp and counterintuitive, and the honeypot-code defense is its elegant dual. Novelty (72): prior constrained-decoding attacks required adversarial grammars; showing benign grammars suffice is genuinely new, and CodeShield's dual-regime DPO is a real method. Rigor (68) is good for the breadth — 10 LLMs including frontier APIs, three repeated runs with std, a real 10-round adaptive-attack robustness check, full CodeShield code released — but every metric depends on an LLM judge (87%/85% spot-checked) and it is an unreviewed preprint. Resilience (68) and Completeness (68) are aided by honest limitations but the threat model omits provider output-moderation layers and offers no inference-time defense for the highest-risk black-box API targets. Impact (65): the attack is trivially reproducible across the GCD ecosystem; the defense needs weight access. 0.20*72+0.25*68+0.15*75+0.15*68+0.15*68+0.10*65 = 70.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 68 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 68 Resilience 68 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation LLM safety alignment is almost universally grounded in the natural-language modality: RLHF, DPO, and SFT-based alignment approaches train models to respond to malicious prompts with natural-language refusals. This implicitly assumes natural language remains available in the output space at inference time. GCD — now standard in production inference frameworks (vLLM, SGLang) and commercial structured-output APIs (OpenAI, Fireworks AI) — removes this assumption. When a code grammar is enforced, standard natural-language refusals such as "I cannot assist with that" become syntactically invalid and receive zero probability under the constrained distribution. The model is then forced to generate code, for which it has received no safety alignment. Prior work on constrained-decoding attacks required carefully crafted adversarial grammars, limiting practicality. The gap this paper closes is showing that entirely benign, off-the-shelf language grammars are sufficient to exploit the misalignment. Key Idea The core insight is a modality-mismatch: GCD eliminates refusals not by persuading the model to comply, but by making refusals grammatically impossible — R_refuse ∩ L(G) = ∅, so the model's learned refusal behavior receives zero probability mass. The complementary defense insight is that safe behavior in the code modality must be structurally diverse, not anchored to a fixed pattern (e.g., always outputting `pass`), because an attacker can exclude any fixed syntactic pattern with a single grammar rule. Training the model to prefer randomly sampled, semantically harmless code snippets (honeypot code) over harmful code gives the model many safe code-space exits that cannot simultaneously be excluded without also removing syntax needed by malicious programs. Methodology CodeSpear is trivially simple: given a malicious prompt p and a standard code grammar G (Python, C++, or Java), invoke the model through a standard GCD interface — y ~ P^G_M(·|p). At each decoding step t, the valid token set V_G(y<t) = {v ∈ V | ∃ y>t s.t. (y<t, v, y>t) ∈ L(G)} is computed; all tokens outside this set are zeroed; sampling proceeds from the renormalized distribution. No adversarial grammar, no gradient access, no prompt engineering, no model fine-tuning. CodeShield uses DPO with a three-tier preference hierarchy over three response types: natural-language refusal y_refuse (from existing safety datasets), honeypot code y_honeypot (K=5 snippets per prompt randomly sampled from OpenCodeInstruct — semantically unrelated to the malicious request), and harmful code y_harmful (collected by querying the target model under GCD before training). Two preference pairs per honeypot sample are constructed: (p, y_refuse, y_honeypot) encodes that NL refusals dominate code in unconstrained decoding; (p, y_honeypot, y_harmful) encodes that honeypot dominates harmful code in constrained decoding. DPO is minimized over this dataset (lr=1e-5, 1 epoch) with 40k general SFT samples from OpenCodeInstruct added for utility preservation. Training data: 744 PKU-RLHF seed prompts filtered by Qwen3-32B, augmented to 2,000 by DeepSeek-V4-Pro. Critical design decisions: (1) Honeypot diversity via random corpus sampling rather than fixed-template responses — well-justified by an adaptive attack analysis showing fixed patterns are trivially excluded. (2) Dual-preference structure spanning both modalities in one DPO objective — well-justified by the Safe-DPO ablation. (3) Collecting harmful training responses by running GCD against the pre-alignment model — reasonable but ties training data quality to initial model vulnerability. Key Results On locally deployed LLMs (Table I, 5 models × 2 benchmarks), CodeSpear achieves average ASR 81.82% and MR 54.58%, vs. the strongest baseline CodeJailbreaker at 71.25% / 48.45% and Vanilla at 54.92% / 36.61%. On Qwen2.5-Coder-7B/MalwareBench specifically, ASR rises from 29.79% to 83.44%. On API-based frontier LLMs (Table II, 5 models), CodeSpear achieves average ASR 67.39% / MR 36.62% with a +45.39 / +25.59 pp gain over Vanilla; on MiniMax-M2.7/RMCBench, ASR rises from 20.33% to 85.53%. GPT-5 and GPT-5-mini show lower ASR (~50%) because they default to `pass` statements — but a tightened grammar excluding `pass` raises GPT-5 ASR from 55.49% to 70.30% (Table III), confirming fixed-pattern safe behavior is fragile. CodeShield (Table IV, 3 models): On Qwen2.5-Coder-7B under CodeSpear, average ASR/MR fall from 83.11%/54.12% to 5.57%/2.78%. The Safe-DPO ablation (NL-only DPO) still yields 77.39%/45.03% under attack, confirming that honeypot code is the essential component. Without attack, CodeShield reduces ASR from 28.36% to 2.08% on the same model. Utility (Table V): On Qwen2.5-Coder-7B, pass@1 HumanEval drops 3.45pp (70.93% → 67.48%) and pass@3 MBPP drops 1pp. On LLaMA3-8B, pass@1 HumanEval drops 6.3pp (53.66% → 47.36%). Adaptive attack (Table VI, N=10 grammar-tightening rounds with DeepSeek-V4-Pro as proxy): ASR/MR remain comparable or lower than standard CodeSpear (e.g., Qwen2.5-Coder-7B RMC: 6.04%/0.16% vs. CodeSpear 7.69%/4.40%). LLM judge (DeepSeek-V4-Flash) agreement with 100-sample human annotation: 87% on ASR, 85% on MR. Novelty Classification NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW METHOD. The finding that benign, off-the-shelf code grammars — not adversarially crafted ones — bypass safety alignment by eliminating the refusal output space is genuinely novel and counterintuitive; prior constrained-decoding attacks (APT, prefix-tree) required adversarially constructed grammars. CodeShield's dual-regime DPO preference construction (separate preferences for constrained vs. unconstrained decoding regimes within one training objective) is a new method within the established DPO framework. The honeypot-code concept as a code-modality analogue of NL refusal is new. What is not novel: DPO itself, grammar-constrained decoding, and the general observation that safety alignment is fragile. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and informative: Safe-DPO isolates the honeypot contribution; Figure 4 sweeps K ∈ {1,3,5,7,10}; Figure 3 sweeps grammar languages. Three repeated runs with standard deviations are reported. LLM judge reliability is spot-checked via 100 human-labeled examples (87%/85% agreement). Code for CodeShield is fully released at https://github.com/TsinghuaISE/CodeSpear-CodeShield; CodeSpear code is restricted to researchers. This is a preprint (arXiv:2606.11817v1, 10 Jun 2026) and has not undergone peer review. A methodological concern: all quantitative results depend on the LLM judge (DeepSeek-V4-Flash), and the judge prompt is in supplementary materials absent from this PDF. A second concern: malicious training prompts are synthesized by DeepSeek-V4-Pro from PKU-RLHF; distribution overlap with RMCBench and MalwareBench test sets is not quantified. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL — the paper runs attack and defense experiments on real LLMs through real local and API deployments. - Practical: CodeSpear requires only a standard GCD API call, runs on any hardware capable of querying an LLM endpoint, and is immediately usable against any vLLM/SGLang/OpenAI structured-output deployment. CodeShield fine-tuning requires model weight access and GPU resources; compute cost is not specified in the main text. - Efficient: CodeSpear adds zero overhead beyond standard GCD. CodeShield uses 1 epoch over 2,000 preference pairs plus 40k SFT samples — modest by fine-tuning standards, but requires a GPU and full weight access. - Effective: CodeSpear demonstrably jailbreaks 10 LLMs including frontier API models. CodeShield demonstrably restores safety on 3 models with minor utility cost. However, CodeShield cannot be applied to black-box API models by external parties — the most frontier targets remain undefended. - Scalable: CodeSpear scales trivially to any GCD-enabled endpoint and any programming-language grammar. CodeShield does not scale to black-box API deployments; the paper offers no inference-time defense alternative for that scenario. The paper delivers a DPO training recipe and full source code; trained model weights are not stated as released. Strengths - The mathematical formalization of why GCD eliminates refusals (R_refuse ∩ L(G) = ∅) is sharp and immediately gives intuition for both the attack and the defense; it is the kind of insight that will transfer to other constrained-output modalities (JSON schemas, regex constraints). - The attack's zero-cost, zero-adversarial-component design is its most practically important property: it lowers the exploitation bar to anyone with API access, distinguishing it cleanly from prior constrained-decoding attacks that required per-request adversarial grammar construction. - The adaptive attack evaluation (10-round grammar tightening by a capable adversary model) is a genuine robustness check; most jailbreak papers do not include this, and the result here supports the structural-diversity argument substantively. - The dual-regime DPO preference construction elegantly teaches two distinct safe behaviors (NL refusal vs. honeypot code) under two distinct output-space regimes using a single standard DPO loss, without modifying the algorithm. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors acknowledge: (1) GCD implementation variation across inference engines may affect absolute ASR numbers, so results should be read as evidence of a general risk class rather than precise universal figures. (2) The two benchmarks (RMCBench at 10 scenario types, MalwareBench at 6 malware types) may not cover all malicious code generation scenarios. (3) CodeSpear source code is restricted to controlled research release. (4) CodeShield requires fine-tuning access, unavailable for black-box API deployments. These are confronted directly, which adds credibility. Analyst-Identified Gaps - CodeShield is evaluated on only three models, all locally fine-tunable. The five API-based frontier models tested in the attack evaluation (GPT-5, GPT-5-mini, MiniMax-M2.5/M2.7, GPT-OSS-120B) cannot be fine-tuned by external parties, yet the paper provides no inference-time defense for this scenario — the highest-risk deployment case remains open. - The LLaMA3-8B utility degradation under CodeShield is 6.3pp on HumanEval pass@1 (53.66% → 47.36%), meaningfully larger than the Qwen models. The paper does not flag this as a concern and instead highlights a positive Qwen2.5-7B MBPP result — a selective framing that understates the utility cost for weaker base models. - Honeypot code is randomly sampled from OpenCodeInstruct with no domain or content filtering. Code snippets involving network sockets, file I/O, or system calls could be coincidentally relevant to certain malicious requests. The claim that all honeypot code is "semantically harmless" is asserted but not empirically verified. - The threat model excludes inference-time output filters (content moderation layers), which many API providers (including OpenAI) apply on top of model outputs. The paper does not model or test whether such filters would catch CodeSpear-generated malicious code, potentially overestimating practical ASR against hardened API deployments. - Training data (2,000 malicious prompts) was synthesized by DeepSeek-V4-Pro from PKU-RLHF, a general safety dataset not tailored to code. Quantification of overlap with test benchmarks is absent. Bottom Line Any practitioner deploying LLMs with GCD-enabled APIs or inference frameworks should treat this as an immediate operational concern and evaluate CodeShield as a plug-in alignment step; the attack is real, requires no sophistication to execute, and the defense is practical for organizations with model weight access.
Recommended 2606.11817 PDF
#35 — Innovative (70)

KG-SoftMAP: Soft Knowledge-Graph Priors for Bayesian Network Structure Learning from Sparse Discrete Data

G Xu, JE Corter · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces KG-SoftMAP, a MAP method that encodes a weighted, directed knowledge graph as a soft, data-overridable edge prior (logit-linear in KG confidence weights) combined with the BDeu score, enabling Bayesian network structure learning from discrete data matrices with observation rates as low as 5%. - Why it's important: Standard BN structure learners — both classical and modern differentiable — recover near-zero directed structure when most variable pairs lack joint observations, which is the norm in educational assessments, clinical registries, and sensor networks; prior approaches either hard-constrain edges (brittle under uncertain knowledge) or outsource the graph entirely to an LLM (discarding data). - How it does it: Per-edge inclusion log-odds are set to β₀ + β₁·w_uv (a linear function of the KG confidence weight), creating a sparsity penalty for non-KG edges and a tunable bonus for KG-supported edges, then the MAP objective is maximized by a two-phase greedy hill-climbing search (forward add/swap, backward delete) on complete-case BDeu scores. - The good: On six synthetic bnlearn benchmarks with ground-truth DAGs, KG-SoftMAP achieves Directed-F1 0.46–0.96 at ρ≥0.2, while every data-only or continuous-SEM baseline (GES, MMHC, PC, GOLEM, DAGMA, SDCD, GRaSP) stays at or below DF1 0.44; a zero-signal random_disjoint KG yields exactly DF1 0.00, confirming that gains come from KG signal, not the MAP search alone. - The missing: The soft prior's margin over near-hard constraints is modest under a shared informative KG (Appendix I.2: near-hard is comparable or slightly above at ρ≥0.2), meaning softness pays off mainly under uncertain KG fidelity — a regime the real-data experiments cannot verify because no ground-truth DAG exists there. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners building interpretable diagnostic models from sparse discrete data (education, medicine, sensors) who have imperfect domain KGs; the honest two-track evaluation and corruption controls are a methodological model, though the novelty delta over hard-constraint methods is narrower than the framing implies.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. This is the strongest paper in the cluster. Rigor (80) drives the score: thorough ablations (same-KG search ablation isolating prior from search engine, corruption sweep across four error types, a zero-signal random_disjoint control yielding DF1 0.00, near-hard-prior comparison), 3-5 seeds, and error bars throughout — though no public code and preprint status cap it. Completeness (85) is exemplary — the two-track design (structural claims confined to synthetic ground-truth DAGs, deployment claims to real data) explicitly prevents the common overclaim, and limitations are confronted with specific future work. Resilience (80) is high: falsifiable boundary conditions are stated, including where the prior actively hurts. Novelty (60) and Non-Obviousness (60) are moderate — a principled but natural point in the flat-to-hard prior spectrum, with the paper honestly noting the soft-prior margin over near-hard constraints is small under informative KGs. Impact (45) reflects an algorithm and protocol but no released library. Weighted: 0.20*60 + 0.25*80 + 0.15*60 + 0.15*85 + 0.15*80 + 0.10*45 = 70. Resembles the user's positive set in controlled methodology and honest scope.
empirical Novelty 60 Rigor 80 Non-Obv 60 Completeness 85 Resilience 80 Impact 45
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: In domains such as educational assessment, clinical registries, and sensor networks, data matrices are extremely sparse: each instance records only a small fraction of variables, so most variable pairs share too few joint observations for reliable conditional-independence testing or BDeu scoring. At observation rates ρ < 5%, classical score-based methods (GES, MMHC), constraint-based methods (PC), and modern differentiable learners (NOTEARS family) all recover near-zero directed structure. Domain knowledge often exists in the form of weighted directed graphs — prerequisite hierarchies, disease-symptom relationships, process orderings — but existing injection mechanisms sit at two poles: hard constraints (brittle under uncertainty) or LLM-generated graphs (ignores data entirely). A general framework for treating an imperfect, confidence-weighted KG as a soft, data-overridable structure prior for discrete BN learning has received little attention. Key Idea: The core insight is that an edge-factored Bernoulli structure prior, parameterized so that each edge's inclusion log-odds is a linear function of its KG confidence weight, adds a constant bonus (or penalty) to the greedy BDeu score delta for every candidate edge operation. This integrates cleanly into standard hill-climbing: KG-supported edges get a positive logit bonus that biases inclusion, while non-KG edges get a negative logit penalty that biases exclusion, and data evidence shifts the score by exactly as much as the BDeu local score change — so the prior is overridable by strong data in either direction with no separate reconciliation step. Methodology: Stage 1 (optional) uses a structured LLM call (gpt-4o-mini, temperature 0) per reference document to extract a weighted directed graph K = {(u,v,w_uv)} via a schema-constrained prompt; a second call per instance extracts concept-level deviations used only for data preprocessing. Stage 2 maximizes the MAP objective log P(D|G) + log P(G|K) by greedy coordinate ascent. The data term is the sum of per-node BDeu scores under complete-case scoring (rows where all relevant variables are observed); an optional EM extension replaces complete-case counts with model-based expectations for MAR settings. The KG prior factorizes over edges as log P(G|K) = Σ_{(u,v)∈E} logit(θ_uv) + C, where logit(θ_uv) = β₀ + β₁·w_uv with β₀ = logit(θ₀) and β₁ = logit(θ_max) − β₀; defaults are θ₀ = 0.01, θ_max = 0.8. The greedy search has forward (add/swap) and backward (delete) phases; acyclicity and max in-degree ≤ 4 are enforced at each step; a score cache avoids redundant BDeu recomputation. Termination is finite because the MAP objective strictly increases at each accepted move in the finite DAG space. Three design decisions: (1) complete-case scoring rather than imputation — well-justified for MCAR and avoids imputation bias; (2) logit-linear prior rather than a flat or hard prior — justified by the robustness-vs.-recovery tradeoff analysis, though the margin over near-hard constraints under an informative KG is small; (3) edge-factored prior that ignores Markov equivalence — acknowledged as a bias toward an arbitrary representative, with an equivalence-aware variant compared in Appendix L showing |ΔDF1| < 0.03. Key Results: On six standard bnlearn discrete benchmarks (cancer 5/4, asia 8/8, sachs 11/17, child 20/25, insurance 27/52, alarm 37/46) with n = 1,000 instances masked to various observation rates: at ρ = 0.05, KG-SoftMAP achieves DF1 0.14–0.29 versus near-zero for all baselines; at ρ = 0.2–0.4, DF1 = 0.46–0.96 across asia/child/alarm while the strongest data-only baseline (GRaSP-BDeu) reaches 0.12–0.44 and all continuous baselines (GOLEM, DAGMA, SDCD) reach at most 0.25 after preprocessing rescue. A corruption sweep on three networks at ρ = 0.4 shows graceful degradation: at 70% reversed-edge corruption DF1 falls to 0.25–0.30; a zero-signal random_disjoint KG gives DF1 0.00 and SHD rises above the no-prior floor (28.6 vs. 16.9), confirming the prior can hurt when completely wrong. The same-KG ablation (Table 2) shows MMHC+Prior reaches mean DF1 0.92 vs. KG-SoftMAP 0.96, while the identical KG given to BNLEARN's Castelo–Siebes cs prior yields mean DF1 0.62–0.63 across four networks — confirming the prior is the dominant lever, not the specific search engine, and that KG-SoftMAP's sparse-aware complete-case scoring contributes the remaining margin, which grows with network size. On real educational data (SAF-eval, 191 concepts, N = 1,392, ρ ≈ 4.4%): KG-SoftMAP+VE achieves F1_FAIL 0.751 vs. logistic regression 0.780 (−0.03), with ECE 0.019 vs. 0.015 (well-calibrated). On ASSISTments (no expert KG), BN F1_FAIL = 0.14 vs. LR 0.58, confirming the prior adds nothing without meaningful signal. On Eedi (expert ontology), within-topic edge fraction increases from 0.045 to 0.123 without prediction improvement. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD. The logit-linear edge-factored KG prior combined with sparse complete-case BDeu scoring is a natural and principled combination, but the specific formulation — where each KG confidence weight linearly controls the log-odds of edge inclusion, integrating directly into greedy hill-climbing deltas — appears to be an underexplored point in the spectrum between flat and hard priors. The LLM-based KG extraction pipeline is a straightforward application of structured prompting. The primary novelty is the systematic empirical demonstration that this simple prior form suffices to recover substantial directed structure in a regime where all data-only methods fail, and the controlled KG quality analysis that quantifies the boundary conditions. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are thorough: a same-KG search ablation isolates the prior from the search, a corruption sweep across four error types at three rates quantifies degradation, a zero-signal control confirms gains require signal, a near-hard-prior comparison (Appendix I.2) checks the softness claim, and an EM extension is tested on a controlled MAR mechanism. Three to five random seeds are used throughout. Error bars (±SD) are reported for all synthetic results. However, no code or data repository is mentioned in the paper; reproducibility depends entirely on the described protocol. The synthetic KG is derived from the ground-truth DAG (true edges get high-confidence weights, 10% noise edges get low weights), which is disclosed clearly — this is a controlled experimental design rather than a realistic KG scenario. The real-data SAF KG is LLM-extracted from reference text and may reflect the LLM's parametric knowledge; the paper explicitly flags this and calls for a wrong-reference ablation as future work. The two SAF artifacts (SAF-full and SAF-eval) are run independently and their numbers are deliberately not compared cell-by-cell, which is methodologically careful. The paper is a preprint; these results have not been peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: The greedy search requires only a sparse discrete matrix and a weighted directed graph; it runs in under 14 seconds on a 191-node problem (SAF-eval, single run on standard hardware) and under 1 second on the largest bnlearn benchmark (37 nodes), with no GPU requirement. - Efficient: Search time scales roughly quadratically in the number of variables under the max in-degree constraint; at p = 200 with ρ ≈ 5%, wall-clock is ~5 seconds; KG construction adds one LLM call per reference document (gpt-4o-mini at temperature 0), which is cheap but requires external API access. - Effective: On synthetic data with known ground truth the method recovers directed structure substantially above all baselines; on real data it produces a well-calibrated joint model that trails logistic regression by 0.03 F1 on one dataset, fails to improve prediction where no meaningful KG exists, and demonstrates only relatedness (not prerequisite) consistency with a third taxonomy. - Scalable: Tested up to p ≈ 200 variables; scaling beyond this is explicitly flagged as untested and would likely require candidate-parent pruning or decomposition. The complete-case BDeu scoring requires joint observations, which become exponentially sparse as max in-degree and ρ decrease together — a regime the paper does not analyze beyond p = 200. The work delivers an algorithm and evaluation protocol; no software library or trained model is released. Strengths: - The two-track evaluation design — structural claims confined to synthetic data with ground-truth DAGs, deployment claims confined to real data — prevents the common overclaim of treating predictive accuracy as evidence of correct structure, and is explicitly stated as such. - The KG corruption and zero-signal controls (particularly the random_disjoint result of DF1 = 0.00 and SHD above the no-prior floor) provide a sharp, falsifiable boundary on when the method helps and when it can actively hurt. - The logit-linear prior form integrates with zero implementation complexity into standard greedy hill-climbing, with finite-improvement guarantees, and the default hyperparameters (θ₀ = 0.01, θ_max = 0.8) sit on a plateau across the tested grid, reducing sensitivity to tuning. - The same-KG ablation with BNLEARN's Castelo–Siebes cs prior (Table 3) honestly demonstrates that the KG prior is the dominant factor — any soft-prior learner benefits substantially — while identifying sparse-aware complete-case scoring as the source of the remaining margin. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: Structural recovery claims are explicitly limited to synthetic data; the authors state this prevents overclaiming. The SAF KG is LLM-extracted and may reflect parametric LLM knowledge beyond the reference text; a wrong-reference control is identified as necessary future work. The MAR EM extension is validated only on a synthetic controlled mechanism, not real MAR data. The search provides finite monotone improvement for the implemented two-phase procedure, with no global or joint local-optimality guarantee. Scaling beyond a few hundred variables is untested. The authors confront these limitations directly with specific future work proposals, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The near-hard prior comparison (Appendix I.2, sachs at 10% noise) shows the soft prior's margin over a near-hard prior (θ_max = 0.9999) is zero at ρ = 0.05 and modest at ρ ≥ 0.2 — the paper's central claim of "softness as the mechanism" is less distinct from hard constraints than the framing implies, especially under informative KGs. The real-data evaluation covers only educational datasets; the claimed applicability to clinical registries and sensor networks is asserted but not demonstrated, and the educational domain has peculiar structure (answer-level, no persistent student IDs) that may not transfer. The LLM KG construction pipeline is evaluated only on SAF and only with gpt-4o-mini; no ablation tests what happens with smaller or worse LLMs, which is the practical concern for many deployment scenarios. At the stated motivating sparsity level (ρ = 0.05), the method recovers only partial structure (DF1 0.14–0.29) — an improvement over near-zero baselines, but likely insufficient for many applied inference tasks; the paper does not characterize which graph properties are preserved in this partial-recovery regime. No public code release is mentioned, limiting reproducibility of the multi-step pipeline. Bottom Line: A practitioner building a diagnostic BN from sparse discrete data with an available domain KG — educational, clinical, or process-control — gains a concrete, low-overhead method with honest scope characterization; the work should be read and the KG quality controls adapted, though those working under tight KG-quality certainty may find a simple near-hard constraint achieves comparable results with less implementation overhead.
Search Results 2606.10358 PDF
#36 — Innovative (70)

Fast Organic Crystal Structure Prediction with Unit Cell Flow Matching

A Lo, L Mucko, AH Cheng, A Cai, AJA Price, W Matusik… · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Clari, a flow-matching generative model that predicts organic crystal unit cells directly from 2D molecular graphs, replacing bulk-crop representations and triangle-update layers with a unit-cell formulation and pair-bias attention. - Why it's important: Traditional crystal structure prediction requires several CPU-years per molecule, and the prior generative alternative (OXtal) still takes minutes per molecule — both are prohibitive for virtual screening of large compound libraries relevant to pharmaceuticals, agrochemicals, and organic semiconductors. - How it does it: Clari encodes lattice vectors as three virtual atom tokens and runs a Diffusion Transformer (DiT) with additive pair-bias attention and a data-fitted lattice source prior, trained under a flow-matching objective augmented with volume and pairwise-distance auxiliary losses and self-conditioning. - The good: At equal sampling budget (ns=k=30), Clari-M achieves a Sol@30 of 0.697 on the OXtal Rigid test set versus OXtal's 0.300, while generating 150 crystal structures per molecule in 2.2 seconds on an H100 — a 15–30× wall-clock speedup. - The missing: Clari ignores stereochemistry (input is a 2D graph agnostic to chirality), which is a critical gap for the pharmaceutical applications the paper foregrounds; no chiral-tag or 3D-conformer conditioning is attempted. - Read it or not: Practitioners working on generative materials discovery, pharmaceutical polymorph screening, or ML for periodic systems should read this; it delivers a concrete, well-ablated architecture that credibly surpasses prior generative CSP while being fast enough for library-scale use.
Why this rank: Empirical profile (systems-leaning, but scored empirical given the model-evaluation framing). Practical Impact (75) and Rigor (75) carry the score: code is released, the 15–30× speedup with simultaneous quality gain over OXtal is a genuine throughput breakthrough, and the ablation table decomposes each design choice incrementally with bootstrap confidence intervals — plus the authors honestly report the sobering strict 15/15 metric alongside the lenient headline. Novelty (65) is a strong method combination (unit-cell flow matching + virtual lattice tokens + pair-bias DiT) but adopts flow-matching/DiT/OT-coupling from prior work; the lenient Sol metric, single-seed training, OXtal timing conversion by a constant 2.5× factor, and missing stereochemistry temper Resilience and keep the composite below the top band. Composite: 0.20*65 + 0.25*75 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*65 + 0.10*75 = 70.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 75 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 72 Resilience 65 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Organic crystal structure prediction (CSP) — determining the 3D packing of a molecule in a solid — is a prerequisite for modeling solid-state properties relevant to pharmaceuticals (polymorphism affects solubility and bioavailability), organic semiconductors, pigments, and energetic materials. Traditional physics-based CSP pipelines search enormous configurational spaces via simulated annealing or evolutionary algorithms followed by DFT relaxation, costing several CPU-years per molecule and restricting use to targeted campaigns. The first generative alternative, OXtal, collapsed this cost by learning to sample experimental crystal structures end-to-end, but inherited AlphaFold3's computationally heavy design: it models large bulk crops of symmetry-related molecule copies and uses triangle-update layers with cubic complexity in atom count. This imposes minutes per molecule and prevents screening libraries of millions of compounds. Key Idea: The central insight is that a crystal's unit cell is the minimal redundancy-free representation — all symmetry-related copies can be reconstructed by tiling — so training directly on unit cells avoids the quadratic-to-cubic cost of processing bulk crops. Coupled with replacing triangle-update layers (which explicitly propagate pairwise geometry through multiplicative path-connected operations) with additive pair-bias attention (which encodes pairwise information through attention logit offsets at linear rather than cubic cost per layer), these two changes together reduce the computational footprint dramatically without sacrificing — and in fact improving — prediction quality. Methodology: Clari represents a crystal unit cell as (L, C, F, E): lattice matrix L ∈ ℝ^{3×3}, Cartesian atom coordinates C ∈ ℝ^{N×3}, atom features F, and bond adjacency E. The lattice is treated as three virtual atom tokens; lattice and atom rows are concatenated into a unified matrix x = (½L; C)/σ and jointly predicted under a flow-matching objective with linear interpolant x_t = (1−t)x₀ + tx₁ and MSE velocity regression. Three critical design decisions: (1) The source distribution p₀ is a data-fitted Gaussian decomposed over atom density, cell angles, and volume-normalized lengths rather than a standard normal, which avoids degenerate lattices. (2) The full objective L_Clari = L^L_FM + L^C_FM + L_vol + L_pair adds a volume relative-error loss and a pairwise periodic-distance loss (with steric clash penalty) computed on the one-step endpoint estimate. (3) Self-conditioning recycles the previous-timestep endpoint estimate x̂₁ as additional input, costing ~20% extra training time for a consistent quality gain. The architecture is a DiT with AdaLN-Zero modulation, gated attention, QKNorm, and SwiGLU feedforwards; pair features (bonds, topological distance, Cartesian/periodic distances) enter via additive attention logit bias. No triangular operations appear anywhere. Training uses the Muon optimizer for Transformer weight matrices and Adam for all others, with approximate optimal-transport coupling (Hungarian algorithm + Kabsch alignment) to straighten flow trajectories. Two model scales were trained: Clari-M (88M parameters, 4× H100, 14 h) and Clari-L (173M parameters, 8× H100, 17 h). At inference, a Heun ODE solver runs 20 steps; best-of-N candidates are ranked by UMA (Universal Model for Atoms) energy without geometry relaxation. Key Results: On OXtal's benchmark test sets (50 rigid, 50 flexible, 6/5/8 entries from CSP Blind Tests 5/6/7), Clari-M at ns=k=30 achieves Sol@30 of 0.697/0.241/0.554/0.311/0.210 versus OXtal's 0.300/0.220/0.167/0.200/0.125 — a consistent 2–3× improvement in solve rate. Scaling to Clari-L and adding inference-time scaling (ns=400, k=200) pushes CSP5 to 0.975 and CSP7 to 0.566, both exceeding the average DFT blind-test participant (0.544 and 0.421 respectively). At the practical operating point ns=150, k=30, Clari-L generates 150 structures in 2.2 s and selects 30 by UMA energy in 6.0 s total.
