Wall-clock timing measurements are on a single H100, with OXtal timings converted from L40S by a factor of 2.5 (a potential source of error). The CSD Teaching Subset (773 crystals spanning organometallics, fullerenes, boranes) shows Clari-L Sol@1000=0.763 at the exhaustive setting. Ablations quantify each component: self-conditioning alone drops clash rate from 20.33% to 9.79% and raises PoseBusters from 80.11% to 85.23%; the data-fitted lattice prior versus standard Gaussian degrades volume error from 1.55% to 2.88%. Under the strict 15/15 molecule-matching criterion (Table 7), solve rates are substantially lower — e.g., Flexible subset Clari-L 0.460 at ns=k=1000 — reflecting that the headline Sol metric (8/15 match) is lenient. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK. The genuine novelty is the combination of unit-cell flow matching with virtual lattice tokens and pair-bias DiT replacing triangle layers for CSP; this is not a straightforward application of existing ideas since it requires rethinking data representation, source prior, and auxiliary losses specific to periodic crystals. The CSD Teaching Subset constitutes a new benchmark contribution. The flow-matching framework and DiT architecture are adopted from prior work in biomolecular generation, and the optimal-transport coupling and self-conditioning are existing techniques. Rigor & Reproducibility: Code is released at https://github.com/aspuru-guzik-group/clari. Ablations are systematic and decompose each design choice incrementally (Table 1: base DiT → lattice tokens → auxiliary losses → self-conditioning → source prior; Table 2: timestep distribution × discretization grid search). Bootstrap confidence intervals over 5000 resamples are reported throughout, providing honest uncertainty quantification on the small CSP Blind Test sets (n=5–8 entries). No significance tests between models are reported. The primary Sol@k metric requires only 8/15 molecule matches (loose COMPACK tolerances: 50% distance, 75° angle), which the authors acknowledge limits scope. The strict 15/15 results (Table 7) are included and tell a more sobering story, particularly for CSP7 where Clari-L at ns=k=1000 achieves Sol@1000=0.875 (8/15) but only 0.125 (15/15). OXtal timing conversion from a different GPU by a constant factor of 2.5 is an approximation. No multi-seed training runs are reported. This is a preprint (arXiv:2606.03199v1, June 2026); results have not undergone peer review. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL/SYSTEMS — Clari is a trained model evaluated on real crystal datasets. - Practical: Clari-L trains on 8× H100 GPUs over 17 hours and runs inference on a single H100; this is within reach of academic labs with GPU cluster access but not commodity hardware. - Efficient: Generating 150 crystal structures takes 2.2 seconds on an H100, versus minutes for OXtal; the pair-bias attention avoids triangle operations' cubic cost, enabling handling of unit cells up to 512 atoms at 173M parameters. - Effective: Sol@30 of 0.772 on the Rigid OXtal test set and 0.975 on CSP5 (at ns=400, k=200) demonstrate the method solves the stated problem at levels exceeding prior generative and competitive with traditional DFT pipelines; the flexible molecule subset (Clari-L Sol@30=0.346) remains more challenging. - Scalable: At ns=k=1000, Clari-L achieves Sol@1000=0.875 on CSP7 (8/15 metric); the paper demonstrates scaling ns improves results sublinearly but with diminishing returns beyond ns=150 for Sol@30, indicating ranking noise as a bottleneck at scale. No evaluation is reported beyond 512 atoms per unit cell. Clari delivers a trained model (weights not explicitly mentioned as released, but code is public), making it a reusable software artifact. Strengths: - The unit-cell formulation with virtual lattice tokens is an architectural insight that directly translates to 15–30× inference speedup and simultaneous quality improvement over OXtal, rather than trading off one for the other. - The data-fitted lattice source prior is a well-motivated and empirically validated departure from the standard Gaussian: the visualization (Figure 3b) makes the mismatch of the normal prior concrete, and the ablation (model E) shows a measurable reconstruction penalty. - Systematic ablation table (Table 1) provides clean decomposition of each design choice's contribution, with bootstrap uncertainty throughout — rare and valuable in this application domain. - Removing the RDKit sanitization requirement and modelling explicit hydrogens are practical engineering choices that meaningfully expand the chemistry accessible to the model (fullerenes, metal complexes), demonstrated qualitatively in Figures 2 and 9. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly state: (1) no stereochemistry — the 2D molecular graph input is chiral-tag-agnostic, limiting pharmaceutical applicability; (2) Z (number of molecules per unit cell) must be provided at inference time, though sweeping Z=1,2,4 is feasible; (3) Clari occasionally generates structures with steric clashes or unphysical voids; (4) the Sol metric's 8/15 match requirement is approximate and limits result scope; (5) benchmark splitting for multi-component crystals without RDKit sanitizability is imperfect; (6) unit-cell representations may be ill-suited for amorphous systems. The authors confront these directly, which is credibility-positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The timing comparison relies on converting OXtal L40S times to H100-equivalent by dividing by 2.5 — a rough scalar factor that may not hold uniformly across molecule sizes, making the 15–30× speedup claim imprecisely bounded. The strict 15/15 Sol results in Table 7 show that CSP7 Clari-L at ns=k=1000 achieves only 0.125 under the strict criterion versus 0.875 under the lenient one — an 87.5-percentage-point gap that suggests the model often produces approximate rather than exact crystal structures; this discrepancy merits more discussion. No comparison against FastCSP or MolCrystalFlow (cited as concurrent work) is provided in quantitative terms, limiting the paper's positioning in the immediate peer landscape. The UMA energy ranking step introduces a dependency on an external MLIP (uma-s-1p2); its accuracy on the specific crystal families tested (organometallics, fullerenes) is not validated. The CSD Teaching Subset — while introduced as a new benchmark — overlaps with the training set chemistry, and the leakage filtering (refcode families + RDKit-sanitizable component matching) may not catch all leakage for non-sanitizable entries. Bottom Line: Any researcher or practitioner in computational materials discovery, pharmaceutical polymorphism, or generative modelling for periodic systems should read Clari — it is a well-executed, carefully ablated paper that delivers a genuine throughput breakthrough while matching or exceeding state-of-the-art solve rates, and the open-source release makes the contribution immediately actionable.
Citations 2606.03199 PDF
#37 — Innovative (70)

AI-enhanced business process simulation for decision making support

F Meneghello · iris.uniroma1.it · business process mining or process mining - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This thesis introduces a family of AI-enhanced hybrid business process simulation (BPS) frameworks—RIMS, WISDOM, DRLHSM, and a proactive scheduling method—that integrate deep learning, generative AI, reinforcement learning, and constraint programming into discrete event simulators to improve simulation fidelity, what-if scenario generation, and process optimization. - Why it's important: Traditional BPS models are systematically oversimplified, treating human resources as machines and ignoring trace history in routing decisions, which causes decision-support analyses to mislead practitioners operating real processes with thousands of interacting instances. - How it does it: The core trick is runtime integration: instead of fitting statistical distributions to log data and simulating with them, RIMS calls trained LSTM and Decision Tree models during simulation at each event step, so intercase features (queue length, resource occupation, work-in-progress) are accurate at prediction time rather than stale. - The good: RIMS and its variants form the Pareto-optimal frontier across control-flow, time, congestion, and resource metrics on nine event logs spanning both real and synthetic settings, outperforming or tying with Simod, DeepGenerator, and Dsim in 22 of 24 DRLHSM evaluation scenarios with resource constraints, and achieving 5–14% Makespan reduction in a real hospital scheduling deployment. - The missing: Nearly all methods are evaluated using the same Petri-net model that was discovered from the same log being evaluated against, which limits confidence that improvements hold under independently discovered models; and the RL policy evaluation (Chapter 8) is restricted to a single loan application process type. - Read it or not: Researchers working on hybrid simulation, prescriptive process monitoring, or process-aware RL will find this a thorough, well-structured reference covering the full stack from simulator design to optimization; practitioners looking for a plug-and-play tool should note that RIMS targets expert users requiring Python integration.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Strongest paper in the cluster. Rigor (72) is the standout — explicit ablations (RIMS vs RIMS+ vs RimsDT isolate queue features and DT routing), Student's t-tests at p≤0.05, 60/20/20 temporal splits, 100 simulation replications, and publicly released code + logs on GitHub; the thesis (non-peer-reviewed) status and the shared-Petri-net evaluation design pull it back from higher. Novelty (70) is strong — runtime integration of prediction inside the DES loop is a clean architectural insight, plus a NEW PROBLEM (formal BPSP with resource calendars and Proposition 1 extending Beck-Wilson bounds). Non-Obviousness (70) high — demonstrating that queue order diverges between post- and runtime-integration is a non-obvious, well-motivated finding. Completeness (70) and Resilience (65) reflect honestly confronted limitations and the acknowledged generalization caveat. Practical Impact (70) is high — RIMSTool is a real released Python library and the approach delivers 5-14% makespan reduction in a real hospital deployment. Weighted: 0.20*70+0.25*72+0.15*70+0.15*70+0.15*65+0.10*70 = 70. This resembles the user's positive set (rigorous, mechanism-led, artifact-releasing systems work).
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 72 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 70 Resilience 65 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Organizations use business process simulation to evaluate what-if scenarios before real deployment—changing resource allocations, resequencing activities, or stress-testing under higher load. The central failure mode of existing BPS tools (SIMOD, Prosimos, BIMP) is that they approximate complex human-driven dynamics with hand-fitted statistical distributions: activity duration is a fixed exponential regardless of which resource performs it, and routing at XOR gateways uses fixed branching probabilities regardless of case history. Deep learning models for predictive process monitoring (PPM) learn these dependencies accurately but generate traces independently, assigning resources without awareness of who is currently busy with other cases—producing infeasible resource overlaps. Neither paradigm alone provides both accurate individual trace behavior and correct multi-instance congestion dynamics. Key Idea: The central insight is that accuracy gains from predictive models are lost when those predictions are applied after simulation rather than during it. Post-integration (as in Dsim) predicts all times for a trace at once, then patches them in, so the queue order that determines which trace gets a resource next is computed from stale inter-case features. Runtime integration queries the model at each discrete event step, when the simulation clock is accurate and resource occupation is current, so the queue naturally self-organizes to match reality. This single architectural change—synchronizing prediction with simulation state—underlies all contributions. Methodology: The thesis is organized around five research tasks instantiated as distinct systems. RIMS (Chapter 4): Implements DES via SimPy with pluggable LSTM modules for trace arrival time (Prophet time-series), processing time (PT_m), and waiting time (W_m), plus an optional Decision Tree (DT_m) per XOR gateway. The simulator calls these models at each event step and passes current intercase features (work-in-progress, resource occupancy, queue length). RIMS+ adds queue length as a feature to W_m after a replay pass that extracts ground-truth queue values from the training log. RimsDT adds decision-tree classifiers at gateways, trained with prefix encodings aligned to the Petri-net model via conformance checking. Evaluation uses 9 logs (4 real, 5 synthetic), split 60/20/20 by time, comparing against Simod, DeepGenerator, and Dsim on 10 metrics spanning control-flow (CLFD, NGD, CONF, L_traces), time (AED, CED, RED, CAR), congestion (CTD), and resources (CWD). WISDOM (Chapter 5): Labels traces in an event log by what-if scenario class y, then trains either a Conditional Variational Autoencoder (CVAE with LSTM encoder/decoder) or Counterfactual Generation (DiCE genetic algorithm) as a trace generator G_θ to produce new traces conforming to y=+. These generated traces are replayed alongside original traces through the RIMS simulator, which resolves inter-trace resource contention and corrects waiting times without needing explicit simulation parameters per scenario. Evaluated on Sepsis, BPIC2012, BPIC2017_W logs with sampling factor Υ controlling how many positive traces are withheld as test set. DRLHSM (Chapter 6): Adapts RIMS to support pausing/resuming for RL interaction. A Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) agent observes state (resource availability, activity queue lengths, time-of-day) and selects resource-to-activity assignments to minimize average cycle time. Evaluated on 6 logs under 4 scenario types (with/without calendars, with/without restricted resources), comparing against FIFO_Trace, FIFO_Act, SPT, and Random heuristics. Proactive BPSP (Chapter 7): Formalizes the Business Process Scheduling Problem (BPSP) as a variant of RCMPSP, proves that the Beck & Wilson [8] lower-bound guarantee extends to BPSP with resource calendars (Proposition 1), transforms stochastic durations to deterministic using q coefficient from Monte Carlo simulation of the critical path, solves with Google OR-Tools CP solver to obtain candidate schedules, then selects the best via RIMS Monte Carlo evaluation at the 95th percentile of makespan. Evaluated on JSP benchmarks and a real DayHospital dataset. Policy Evaluation (Chapter 8): Extends RIMS to support multi-actor simulation by deriving two DFGs—G_α (agent-feasible transitions) and G_M (global simulation including environment gateways)—then evaluates MDP-based and CQL offline RL policies learned from historical loan-application logs. The simulator runs traces where the bank agent follows policy ρ while customer actions follow discovered branching probabilities or Decision Trees. Key Results: On 9 event logs, RIMS and its variants form the Pareto-optimal frontier for time and control-flow across all baselines in both queue-present and queue-absent settings. On BPI12W, RimsintraDET achieves CLFD of 0.2031 vs. 0.2576 for Simod/Dsim/RIMS and 0.2307 for LSTM. On the DayHospital scheduling problem (50–1,000 patients/day, up to 1,600 activities), the CP + RIMS proactive approach achieves 5%–14% Makespan reduction on medium and large days (up to ~87 minutes median improvement). DRLHSM outperforms the best heuristic in 14 of 24 scenario evaluations and ties in 8 more; the largest reported improvement is 307% over SPT on the Production log with restricted calendars (904,604s vs. 3,767,052s). In WISDOM evaluation, WisdomCVAE achieves CLFD of 0.0657 and NGD of 0.0546 on BPI2017_W vs. 0.3131/0.3609 for SIMOD. In synthetic RL experiments (L2000), the best policies achieve 1,497 accepted traces vs. 501 for ρcustomary out of 2,000 simulated traces. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD (RIMS runtime integration, WISDOM GenAI+BPS pipeline, DRLHSM), NEW PROBLEM (formal BPSP definition with resource calendars and the proposition extending Beck-Wilson bounds to it), NEW SYSTEM/TOOL (RIMSTool Python library), NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING (runtime vs. post-integration comparison demonstrating queue-order correctness; GenAI vs. BPS trade-off between control-flow conformance and resource overlap). The core novelty is architectural—plugging prediction inside the DES loop—rather than algorithmic; the component models (LSTM, Decision Tree, CVAE, PPO) are standard. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present: RIMS vs. RIMS+ vs. RimsDT variants isolate the contribution of queue features and decision-tree routing. Student's t-tests with p≤0.05 determine statistical significance in DRLHSM comparisons. RIMS evaluation uses 60/20/20 temporal splits; DRLHSM uses 100 simulation replications (25 for BPI17W). Code and logs are publicly released on GitHub (https://github.com/francescameneghello/RIMS and related repos). A methodological concern: all compared methods in Chapters 4–6 share the same Petri-net process model discovered from the same log, meaning process model quality is not varied and improvements cannot be attributed to runtime integration alone versus model choice. The DRLHSM resource-restriction scenarios (RQ3) are constructed ad hoc per dataset, making cross-log comparisons rough. Scheduling results for DayHospital are self-evaluated without comparison to prior scheduling methods (authors note this is intentional due to incompatible assumptions). Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL and SYSTEMS (both apply—the thesis both runs experiments and delivers a tool). - Practical: RIMSTool runs in Python on standard hardware; the DayHospital CP+simulation experiment completes in under 20 minutes on an M2 MacBook with 16 GB RAM. DRLHSM training at 10^7 decision steps is more expensive (25 replications on BPI17W are noted as costly). - Efficient: RIMS introduces overhead relative to Simod because it calls external models at each event step; exact overhead is not quantified. DRLHSM training time is not reported. BPSP evaluation takes on average 5 minutes per scheduling instance. - Effective: For process fidelity (RIMS), evidence is strong across 9 diverse logs. For optimization (DRLHSM), effectiveness holds primarily when resources have heterogeneous performance or constrained calendars; synthetic logs with uniform resource performance show little advantage. Hospital scheduling shows meaningful real-world Makespan reduction. - Scalable: BPI17W (30,276 traces) is the largest dataset tested, and DRLHSM training is already expensive there. Object-centric or large-scale enterprise processes with hundreds of activity types are not tested. The BPSP approach is evaluated up to 50-trace scheduling horizons per day; scalability to weeks-long horizons is not assessed. The thesis delivers RIMSTool as a software artifact (Python library with SimPy, GitHub), plus experimental code for each chapter. Strengths: - The runtime integration insight is crisp and directly addressable: demonstrating empirically that queue order diverges between post-integration and runtime-integration on the same running example is convincing motivation. - Breadth is genuine: the same hybrid simulator is shown to work in five distinct modes (accurate simulation, what-if generation, RL training environment, scheduling evaluator, policy A/B tester), providing conceptual unification. - The BPSP Proposition 1 extending Beck-Wilson guarantees to processes with resource calendars is a formal result not previously established, filling a gap between scheduling theory and process mining practice. - Evaluation on both real and synthetic logs of varying sizes and queue levels, with statistical significance testing, reflects appropriate rigor for the simulation-quality claims. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly note: RIMS is evaluated on a single discovered simulation model (design choices may influence results); predictive models are inherently black-box and limit what-if scenario definition; DRLHSM performance depends on simulator quality and assumes resource substitution affects only processing time, not activity sequence; WISDOM requires what-if behavior to be present in the training log; the RL policy evaluation (Chapter 8) is restricted to loan application processes; BPSP discovery assumes complete, non-preemptive logs with fixed resource capacities; scheduling focuses solely on Makespan minimization without fairness or cost objectives. These are confronted directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The shared-model evaluation design in Chapters 4–6 (all methods use the identical Petri net discovered by Simod) means improvements could partially reflect that the RIMS simulator is simply a better implementation of the same model rather than purely the runtime integration principle—an ablation varying the discovery algorithm would clarify this. The DRLHSM reward function is per-completed-trace and does not capture trace-level history, which the authors acknowledge but do not empirically quantify the impact of. For WISDOM, the baseline SIMOD requires generating 4–30× more traces to obtain the target count of positive-class traces (because it cannot condition generation), making the comparison somewhat unequal in practice—WISDOM's advantage partly reflects that the BPS baseline is poorly suited for conditional generation rather than a general simulation quality claim. The policy evaluation in Chapter 8 tests the sensitivity of rankings to simulation model choice (Mtip vs. Mspag) but not to the discovered process model's quality for the real process, leaving open whether the approach generalizes beyond loan application processes. Bottom Line: A practitioner or researcher in process mining, prescriptive monitoring, or simulation-based optimization will find this thesis the most comprehensive treatment of hybrid BPS currently available, delivering both a deployable Python tool and a set of evaluated methods; the limitation to watch is that the generalization claims rest on a relatively controlled evaluation setup where all methods share the same process model.
Search Results
#38 — Innovative (70)

Lip Forcing: Few-Step Autoregressive Diffusion for Real-time Lip Synchronization (29 ▲ )

Paul Hyunbin Cho, Jinhyuk Jang, SeokYoung Lee, Joungbin Lee, Siyoon Jin, Heeseong Shin, Jung Yi, Yunjin Park, Chulmin Park, Seungryong Kim · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 10 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Lip Forcing, an analysis-driven distillation framework that converts a 50-step, 14B-parameter bidirectional video diffusion teacher into a 2-step causal autoregressive student for real-time video-to-video lip synchronization, achieving up to 31.58 FPS with sub-millisecond time-to-first-frame. - Why it's important: Diffusion-based lip-sync models produce the highest visual fidelity and audio-visual alignment but run at under 4 FPS due to full-sequence bidirectional attention and many denoising steps, making them unusable for live translation, virtual avatars, and interactive agents. - How it does it: By empirically probing the teacher's denoising trajectory to identify a CFG fidelity–sync tradeoff and a sync-favoring mid-trajectory band (ODE steps 20–40), then instantiating this finding as a windowed DMD training schedule, an analysis-derived 2-step inference landing at ODE step 30, and a SyncNet-based reward that closes the residual sync gap. - The good: The 14B student achieves the lowest FVD (107.88) across all baselines on HDTF and tops the user study on visual quality, identity preservation, and naturalness while running 39.8× faster than its teacher and 4.7× faster than LatentSync. - The missing: Trajectory analysis is conducted on n=10 clips from a single teacher lineage, out-of-domain benchmarks (Hallo3, TalkVid) reveal a meaningful sync gap vs. LatentSync not prominently discussed, and no 2-step Self Forcing ablation isolates the recipe's contribution from the step-count reduction alone. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners working on diffusion distillation or real-time video generation — the trajectory-analysis methodology is the transferable insight, not just the specific cutoffs.
Why this rank: Systems profile. This is the strongest-executed paper in the cluster on its axis. Practical Impact (72) reflects a real-time streaming system (31.58 FPS, sub-ms TTFF, 39.8× over the teacher) with planned code/weights. Non-Obviousness (75) and Rigor (68) are anchored by the analysis-driven recipe: the teacher-trajectory probe identifying a CFG fidelity–sync band and parameterizing the distillation schedule from it is a diagnostic that precedes and determines design rather than post-hoc rationalization, and the MOS study correcting the Sync-C metric artifact is methodologically valuable. Completeness (70) is high — four limitations confronted directly. Resilience (68) is tempered by the n=10 trajectory analysis, the OOD sync gap relegated to the appendix, and the missing 2-step Self-Forcing ablation that would cleanly isolate the recipe from step-count reduction. Novelty (65) is moderate — known techniques applied to a new domain, with the methodology as the genuine addition. 0.15*65+0.15*68+0.10*75+0.15*70+0.15*68+0.30*72 = 70.
systems Novelty 65 Rigor 68 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 70 Resilience 68 Impact 72
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Audio-driven video-to-video (V2V) lip synchronization edits an existing speaker video to match target audio while preserving identity, head pose, and background. Recent diffusion-based methods substantially improved visual fidelity and audio-visual alignment over earlier GAN-based pipelines, but their full-sequence bidirectional transformer attention scales quadratically with clip length, and achieving high synthesis quality requires 20–50 denoising steps. These factors compound to inference speeds well below real-time for all existing diffusion lip-sync methods (0.38–15.47 FPS before this paper), blocking deployment in latency-sensitive applications. Autoregressive diffusion reduces this burden through chunk-wise causal generation but had not been applied to lip synchronization; likewise, few-step distillation via Distribution Matching Distillation (DMD) is underexplored here because naïve adaptation introduces artifacts from the complex entanglement of audio-visual signals. Key Idea: The core insight is that a bidirectional lip-sync diffusion teacher's response to classifier-free guidance (CFG) is not uniform across the denoising trajectory: CFG improves audio-visual synchronization specifically within a mid-trajectory band (roughly ODE steps 20–40 on a 50-step shifted schedule), while degrading reference fidelity when applied at early or late steps. A no-CFG → CFG mixed 2-step schedule placing the second denoising step at ODE step 30 achieves a reference-sync operating point that no fixed CFG scale can reach. Transferring this finding into the student's distillation training schedule (windowed CFG during DMD) and its inference schedule (2 steps at j ∈ {0, 30}, no CFG at inference) allows a few-step causal student to recover most of the 50-step teacher's quality without the computational cost. Methodology: The framework finetunes OmniAvatar (a Wan 2.1 video diffusion transformer, available at 1.3B and 14B) on a V2V lip-sync inpainting task (OmniAvatar-LS) to produce the teacher, then distills it into causal students at both scales. The input pipeline concatenates five tensors (noisy target video, lip-region mask, reference frame, reference masked video, reference frame sequence) totaling 65 channels per latent voxel at 21×64×64 resolution (4× temporal, 8× spatial VAE compression of 81 frames at 512×512). Audio is injected via the OmniAvatar Audio Pack (Wav2Vec 2.0 encoded, added directly to latents). Training has two stages. Stage 1: Diffusion Forcing pretraining — the causal student is trained on real data with independent per-chunk noising and a rectified flow matching loss, giving it a clean initialization. Stage 2: Self Forcing DMD distillation with three recipe modifications: (i) Sync-Window DMD (SW-DMD) — teacher CFG is gated to 4.5 inside the sync-favoring band j ∈ [20, 40] and dropped to 1.0 outside; (ii) 2-step inference schedule at JLF = (0, 30), analysis-derived to balance fidelity and sync; (iii) SyncNet reward — a SyncNet confidence score R modulates the DMD generator gradient as w(x̂₀) = exp(β·R(D(x̂₀), a)) with β=2, decoded via the lightweight TAE rather than the Wan VAE, and detached from the backward pass. At inference, a rolling KV cache (1-frame sink, 6-frame window) with dynamic (cache-slot-indexed) RoPE enables stable long-horizon streaming rollout independent of sequence length. Critical design decisions: (1) Windowed vs. fixed CFG in training — well-justified empirically; ablation (Tab. 3) confirms windowed outperforms both all-CFG and no-CFG endpoints on FVD. (2) Landing step j=30 — grounded in a 2×2 Euler-step factorial probe; ablation (Tab. 5) sweeps j1 ∈ {13, 25, 30, 37} and shows j=30 as a balanced operating point. (3) Using TAE rather than Wan VAE for reward — engineering choice for efficiency, not ablated. Key Results: Primary benchmark: HDTF test set (33 clips, 81 frames, 512×512). Baselines include Wav2Lip, VideoReTalking, MuseTalk (1-step, 23.07 FPS), Diff2Lip (25-step), LatentSync (20-step, 3.23 FPS), X-Dub (30-step, 0.91 FPS), OmniAvatar-LS at both scales, and Self Forcing 1.3B (4-step, 27.48 FPS). All measurements on a single NVIDIA H100. Lip Forcing (1.3B): 31.58 FPS, TTFF 0.32 ms, FVD 118.86, FID 6.76, CSIM 0.943, Sync-C 6.88. This is 17.6× faster than OmniAvatar-LS (1.3B, 1.79 FPS) and the fastest diffusion method above the 25 FPS real-time threshold. Against Self Forcing 1.3B (4 steps), Lip Forcing (2 steps) improves FVD by 5.9 points while running faster. Lip Forcing (14B): 15.11 FPS, TTFF 0.54 ms, FVD 107.88 (best in table), FID 7.01, CSIM 0.949, Sync-C 7.59. 39.8× faster than the 14B teacher (0.38 FPS) and 4.7× faster than LatentSync (3.23 FPS) with better FVD (107.88 vs. 117.91). Memory footprint: 8.78/9.39 GB (1.3B, allocated/reserved) and 40.63/41.38 GB (14B) on H100. MOS user study (30 clips from HDTF and TalkVid, n raters on 10 pages each, 4 Likert axes): Lip Forcing (14B) scores 4.38 Sync, 4.33 Qual, 4.46 ID, 4.32 Nat — top on three axes, tied with X-Dub on Sync. This resolves a counter-intuitive result in automated metrics: Wav2Lip and VideoReTalking exceed ground-truth Sync-C (7.95), indicating SyncNet over-optimization at the cost of perceptual quality, while Lip Forcing's lower Sync-C (7.59) does not translate to a perceived synchronization gap. Additional benchmarks: Hallo3 (30 held-out clips, out-of-domain): Lip Forcing (14B) FVD 87.85 (best), but Sync-C 6.58 trails LatentSync's 8.38, indicating a sync gap on out-of-domain data. TalkVid (30 held-out clips): Lip Forcing (14B) FVD 111.98 (best). Long-video (HDTFlong, up to 6 min): temporally stable, 14B achieves FVD 118.97 and Sync-C 8.68. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD. Lip Forcing applies an existing family of techniques (DMD, Self Forcing, SyncNet reward, rectified flow) to a new domain (V2V lip synchronization streaming) but the central contribution is the analysis-driven recipe derivation: a teacher trajectory probe that identifies a task-specific tradeoff and parameterizes the distillation schedule from it, rather than applying generic distillation settings. The paper also reports the first autoregressive diffusion model for V2V lip synchronization. The individual components (DMD, SyncNet reward weighting, dynamic RoPE, TAE decoder) are existing or contemporaneous work; the synthesis and the trajectory analysis methodology are the genuine additions. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and well-structured across four tables: component isolation (Tab. 2), CFG schedule shape (Tab. 3), step count (Tab. 4), and second-step landing sweep (Tab. 5). The trajectory analysis reports ±1 SE across n=10 clips (small but visually decisive given the effect size). Main comparison tables report point estimates without error bars or significance tests. One paired t-test is reported (Sec. C.4: j1=25 vs. j1=30, p=0.13, n=10), which is actually reassuring since a non-significant result is what validates the plateau claim. Cross-check under audio-only CFG drop mode (App. C.3) confirms the trajectory findings are not artifacts of the drop mode. Code/weights are planned for release alongside a model card (per the broader-impact section) but are not confirmed available at preprint time; the project page is listed. Training was conducted on NVIDIA H200 GPUs (~3,800 H200-hours total including unreported runs). Total compute is transparently reported. Methodological concern: the primary test set (HDTF, 33 clips) overlaps with the training distribution; the out-of-domain benchmarks are relegated to the appendix. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS — a built streaming inference system evaluated by throughput and quality metrics. - Practical: Runs on a single NVIDIA H100 at 31.58 FPS (1.3B, 8.78 GB VRAM) or 15.11 FPS (14B, 40.63 GB VRAM); the 1.3B variant is accessible to a wide range of commercial deployments, while 14B requires high-end single-GPU hardware. - Efficient: 17.6–39.8× speedup over same-scale bidirectional models; sub-millisecond TTFF at both scales; KV cache fixed at 7 latent frames regardless of rollout length, bounding memory under long-horizon generation. - Effective: FVD, CSIM, SSIM, and MOS Qual/ID/Nat results confirm the 14B model solves the stated problem of real-time high-fidelity lip synchronization; the sync gap in automated Sync-C is shown to be a metric artifact rather than a perceptual failure by the MOS study. - Scalable: Dynamic RoPE and rolling KV cache enable stable rollout to 6 minutes (180× the training chunk) without quality or identity degradation; scaling to higher resolutions or non-face-aligned settings is not evaluated. The paper stops at preprint form with a planned code/weights release; no confirmed public artifact is available at submission time. Strengths: - Analysis-driven recipe: The teacher trajectory probe (CFG fidelity–sync tradeoff, Euler-step 2×2 factorial, plateau identification with SE bands) provides an unusually grounded basis for each distillation component — the methodology is not post-hoc rationalization but a diagnostic that precedes and determines the design. - Speedup with best-in-class fidelity: Achieving 39.8× speedup over the 14B teacher while posting the lowest FVD in the full baseline table — including 50-step bidirectional diffusion methods — demonstrates the recipe is not merely trading quality for speed. - MOS study corrects automated-metric blind spots: Identifying that Wav2Lip and VideoReTalking exceed ground-truth Sync-C via SyncNet gaming, and confirming Lip Forcing's perceptual sync is on par with the best baselines despite trailing on automated Sync-C, is a methodologically valuable finding. - Multi-scale validation with compounding recipe gains: Distilling at 1.3B and 14B, and observing that the sync gap closes at 14B (Sync-C 7.59 vs. 6.88), confirms that the recipe generalizes across model size rather than being tuned to a single capacity. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly confront four limitations: (1) the trajectory-analysis recipe is tied to the specific 14B OmniAvatar teacher lineage and requires re-running the diagnostic for other architectures; (2) the 1.3B student trails on fidelity and is appropriate only for streaming-constrained deployments; (3) the SyncNet reward is a proxy metric susceptible to over-optimization, though the capped β=2 appears to stay below the reward-hacking regime; and (4) dual-use (deepfake) risk is acknowledged, with non-binding mitigations (watermarking, detection research). The authors engage limitations directly, a credibility-positive sign in this framework. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The trajectory analysis uses n=10 clips; the reported plateau significance test (p=0.13, n=10) is borderline, and no other core comparison is tested for statistical significance. - Out-of-domain performance (Hallo3 Sync-C 6.58 vs. LatentSync 8.38; TalkVid Sync-C 7.24 vs. LatentSync 8.78) reveals a meaningful sync gap that is understated relative to the primary-benchmark framing; these tables appear only in the appendix. - The most informative ablation would be Self Forcing at 2 steps (matching Lip Forcing's step count) rather than the reported 4-step Self Forcing baseline, which conflates recipe contribution with step-count reduction. - The SyncNet reward strength β=2 is inherited from Re-DMD without tuning or sensitivity analysis; there is no ablation on β. - The 14B student's 40+ GB VRAM requirement is a real deployment barrier for most production environments outside large cloud providers, but this constraint receives only a passing mention rather than a dedicated discussion. Bottom Line: Lip Forcing is the strongest published result on real-time diffusion-based V2V lip synchronization, and its teacher trajectory analysis methodology — applicable to any conditional diffusion model with a fidelity–conditioning tradeoff — is directly actionable for practitioners adapting other bidirectional generators to streaming deployment.
Recommended 2606.11180 PDF
#39 — Innovative (70)

End-to-End Context Compression at Scale (17 ▲ )

Ang Li, Sean McLeish, Haozhe Chen, Nimit Kalra, Zaiqian Chen, Artem Gazizov, Venkata Anoop Suhas Kumar Morisetty, Bhavya Kailkhura, Harshitha Menon, Zhuang Liu, Brian R. Bartoldson, Tom Goldstein, Sanae Lotfi, Micah Goldblum, Pavel Izmailov · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 9 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Latent Context Language Models (LCLMs), a family of encoder-decoder context compressors that map long input token sequences to shorter sequences of continuous latent embeddings, trained end-to-end on over 350B tokens at compression ratios of 4×, 8×, and 16× using a 0.6B encoder and 4B decoder. - Why it's important: Long-context LLM inference is bottlenecked by KV cache memory growth, and existing KV cache compression methods either degrade quality substantially at high compression ratios, require full-context prefill before compression, or are incompatible with standard inference engines like vLLM and SGLang. - How it does it: The encoder maps fixed-size windows of N input tokens to a single latent vector via mean pooling over causal hidden states, projects them through an MLP adapter, and feeds the compressed sequence to a frozen-then-fine-tuned decoder; a four-stage training curriculum (adapter warmup → encoder training → end-to-end continual pre-training → SFT) with interleaved compressed/uncompressed spans and auxiliary reconstruction data prevents catastrophic forgetting. - The good: At 16× compression, LCLM achieves 81.05% on GSM8K — a short, information-dense benchmark where all KV cache compression methods collapse to near 0% — while being 8.8× faster to first token than the best KV baseline on RULER@4k. - The missing: At 4× and 8× compression ratios, KVzip matches or outperforms LCLM on RULER and LongBench while LCLMs are faster; the paper does not evaluate on multi-turn or cross-turn cache reuse scenarios where query-agnostic compression is the claimed advantage. - Read it or not: Read it if you work on long-context LLM inference, agentic systems, or efficient serving — it is a credible systems paper with open models, rigorous architecture search, and genuine wall-clock efficiency gains; skip it if you only care about maximum accuracy at low (4×) compression ratios.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (75) drives the composite: a cleanroom architecture search with random initialization isolates each design choice and is validated at full scale, and models/code/recipe are open-sourced — though no error bars or seeds are reported and the synthetic SFT data overlapping LongBench is not ablated. Novelty (68) and Non-Obviousness (68) are solid but not top-tier: the contribution is scaling and a multi-stage curriculum over individually-prior components (mean pooling, causal masking, MLP adapter), with the interleaved-span format and causal-encoder finding the genuinely fresh insights. Completeness (70) and Resilience (65) are good — limitations (mixed scaling, KVzip competitive at 4×–8×) are confronted honestly — but the headline query-agnostic advantage is untested on multi-turn cache reuse. Practical Impact (75) is high: open models, vLLM/SGLang-compatible standard KV format, and measured wall-clock TTFT/memory wins including 1M-token operation where baselines OOM. Weighted: 0.20*68 + 0.25*75 + 0.15*68 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*65 + 0.10*75 = 70.
empirical Novelty 68 Rigor 75 Non-Obv 68 Completeness 70 Resilience 65 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Long-context LLM inference in production systems faces a fundamental memory and latency bottleneck: the KV cache grows linearly with sequence length, making million-token contexts prohibitively expensive. KV cache compression methods — which evict or compress cached attention keys and values — suffer from several practical limitations: many require the full prompt to be prefilled before eviction, query-dependent variants produce caches that cannot be reused across turns, and methods that apply non-uniform eviction across attention heads and layers are incompatible with paged-attention engines that assume a shared sequence length. Prior soft-token encoder-decoder compressors exist but are trained on domain-specific data at small scale, are not evaluated on information-dense long-context benchmarks like RULER, and do not convincingly preserve the base LLM's general capabilities. Key Idea The core insight is that encoder-decoder soft-token compression can match or exceed KV cache compression on the accuracy-efficiency Pareto frontier if trained at sufficient scale (350B tokens) with a carefully staged curriculum that prevents catastrophic forgetting of the decoder's base capabilities. Crucially, the encoder window size (W=1024 tokens) is set large enough that local context is richly shared before pooling, and training uses interleaved compressed and uncompressed spans throughout the sequence — rather than the standard first-half-compressed split — so the decoder learns to condition on latent context at arbitrary positions. Methodology The LCLM architecture consists of three components: (1) an encoder (Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B) that processes non-overlapping windows of W=1024 tokens with causal attention and pools each block of N tokens to a single latent vector via mean pooling; (2) an MLP adapter (two-layer FFN with RMSNorm and GELU) that projects encoder hidden states to the decoder's embedding dimension; and (3) a decoder (Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507) that consumes the latent sequence in place of the original tokens. Training proceeds in four stages with progressively unfrozen components: Stage 0 trains only the adapter with both encoder and decoder frozen; Stage 1 unfreezes the encoder; Stage 2 unfreezes the decoder at a small learning rate (1e-5) for end-to-end continual pre-training; Stage 3 applies SFT on a curated mixture. The pre-training data mixes Common Crawl text, code, reasoning, and long-context documents, with each sequence partitioned into alternating compressed/uncompressed spans delimited by `<|memory_start|>` and `<|memory_end|>` tokens; loss is computed only on uncompressed tokens. An auxiliary reconstruction objective — asking the decoder to reproduce the original text from the compressed latents — is included in both pre-training and SFT to preserve fine-grained retrieval fidelity. Total data: 283B pre-training tokens (encoder side), 350B+ combined. The architecture is selected via a controlled from-scratch pre-training sweep (38B tokens, Qwen3-0.6B for both encoder and decoder) across pooling operator (mean, concat, token-based), encoder window size (W ∈ {16, 256, 1024}), attention mask (causal vs. bidirectional), and adapter design (MLP vs. attention-based MLP). Mean pooling and causal masking are confirmed superior at scale. Critical design decision: causal masking in the encoder outperforms bidirectional despite the encoder only processing prompts — the paper attributes this to alignment between training and the pre-trained Qwen3-Embedding model's original causal structure, but the mechanism is not fully analyzed. Key Results All evaluations use Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507 as the base decoder on a single H200 GPU. At 16× compression, LCLM achieves: RULER@4k 75.06%, RULER@8k 70.23%, RULER@16k 65.91%, LongBench EN-16 macro 39.08%, GSM8K 81.05%. The uncompressed baseline scores 94.41% / 93.58% / 93.74% / 45.21% / 93.25% respectively, so degradation at 16× is substantial for RULER but acceptable on dense tasks. At 4× compression, LCLM scores 91.76% / 91.03% / 89.96% on RULER, approaching the uncompressed baseline. KVzip at 4× achieves 94.43% / 93.28% / 93.93% on RULER — better than LCLM — but near 0% on GSM8K because it cannot handle short, uniformly important prompts. LCLM is 8.8× faster to first token than the fastest KV baseline on RULER@4k and 5.2× faster on LongBench@64k. Peak memory at 512k–1M tokens: LCLM is the only method that does not OOM at 1M tokens. The agentic EXPAND harness raises RULER NIAH average from 72.51% to 92.46% at 8k context and from 70.18% to 89.91% at 16k context at 16× compression, recovering most of the gap to uncompressed. Models and code are open-sourced (huggingface.co/latent-context, github.com/LeonLixyz/LCLM). Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. The genuine novelty lies in training an encoder-decoder soft-token compressor at scale (350B tokens, full parameter updates to both encoder and decoder) with a carefully designed multi-stage recipe and interleaved data format that preserves general LLM capabilities — this closes a gap prior small-scale or domain-specific soft-token methods could not close. The architecture (encoder → pooling → adapter → decoder) and the individual components (mean pooling, causal masking, MLP adapter) are each drawn from prior work; the contribution is their systematic combination at scale with the multi-stage training recipe and the agentic EXPAND application. Rigor & Reproducibility The architecture search is a genuine strength: it is conducted in a cleanroom setting with randomly initialized weights, isolating the effect of each design choice, and the small-scale findings are validated at full scale (Figures 8–10 confirm the predictive validity). The staged training ablation is implicit (the paper notes direct end-to-end training underperforms without providing numbers), though the decision to use full-parameter training over LoRA is empirically motivated. Error bars and multiple seeds are not reported; all efficiency measurements are mean over 30 runs with 20 warmup steps, which is adequate for TTFT but no significance tests accompany accuracy comparisons. Code and models are open-sourced. One potential concern: the SFT data includes synthetic long-context tasks (repo summarization, document summarization, repo code generation) generated by Qwen3 models that are related to the evaluation setting, which could inflate LongBench and code task numbers — the paper does not ablate the impact of this synthetic data on downstream benchmarks. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL (a trained model family evaluated by benchmark performance and wall-clock measurements). - Practical: The 0.6B encoder + 4B decoder runs on a single H200 (141GB); peak memory plateaus near 30–50 GB at 128k–512k context tokens, making it feasible on high-end but commercially available hardware. - Efficient: At 16× compression, TTFT is 8.8× faster than the best KV cache baseline at 4k context and 5.2× faster at 64k; the encoder batch processes 131k tokens per forward pass, yielding favorable GPU utilization; the decoder KV cache is in standard format, compatible with vLLM and SGLang for further acceleration. - Effective: At 16× compression, the method solves the original GSM8K failure mode of KV cache methods and achieves strong long-context QA accuracy; at 4× and 8× compression, KVzip is competitive or superior on RULER/LongBench, so the method is most decisively effective at the highest compression ratios where alternatives collapse. - Scalable: The paper demonstrates operation up to 1M input tokens without OOM, where all KV baselines fail; compression time and memory scaling curves are reported up to 1M tokens. Accuracy at contexts beyond 16k is not evaluated, so quality scaling to million-token contexts is a claim, not a measurement. The scaling experiments (larger encoder or decoder) yield mixed and somewhat confounding results, suggesting the training recipe is not yet optimized for larger model families. The work delivers open-source models, inference code, and a training recipe — a fully reusable software artifact. Strengths - The architecture search is cleanly executed: random initialization isolates design choices from pre-trained model biases, and small-scale findings are validated at full scale, lending unusual credibility to the design choices. - LCLM is the first soft-token compressor to be evaluated on RULER — a synthetic benchmark specifically designed to stress fine-grained long-context retrieval — and achieves competitive results, closing a gap that prior encoder-decoder methods left open. - The systems-realizable efficiency advantage is genuine: unlike many KV cache methods that mask entries without reducing actual memory or requiring specialized kernels, LCLM reduces the sequence length before decoder prefill, producing measurable wall-clock TTFT and memory reductions on standard hardware with standard inference engines. - The agentic EXPAND mechanism is a creative and practically well-motivated use of the LCLM's compression structure, enabling coarse-to-fine retrieval without requiring architectural modifications to the decoder. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors note that scaling results are mixed: the 8B decoder achieves lower pre-training loss but worse downstream performance, attributed to a mismatch between the training mixture and the 8B model's hybrid-thinking architecture. They also note that the 4B encoder does not uniformly outperform the 0.6B encoder despite lower loss, suggesting the training recipe is tuned around the 0.6B-4B configuration. Future directions — adaptive compression by information density, extending compression to generated state and CoT — are discussed as limitations of the current static approach. These limitations are confronted directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - At 4× and 8× compression, KVzip and AM-Fast/Slow are competitive with or superior to LCLM on RULER@4k–8k (KVzip: 90.27% vs. LCLM: 85.42% at 8×); the Pareto frontier claim holds primarily on the time dimension and at the highest (16×) compression ratios. The accuracy-efficiency trade-off story should be qualified by compression ratio. - The evaluation does not include multi-turn or cross-turn cache reuse scenarios, which are the primary practical motivation for query-agnostic compression. Without such evaluation, the claim that LCLMs solve the multi-turn limitation of query-dependent methods is untested. - The SFT data includes synthetically generated long-context tasks (repository summarization, document summarization, code generation) produced by Qwen3 models; these overlap thematically with the LongBench subtasks. The potential for data leakage or distribution inflation is not ablated. - The agentic EXPAND results are evaluated only on RULER NIAH (needle-in-haystack retrieval), the easiest possible testbed for this paradigm; how the agent performs on tasks requiring synthesis across multiple retrieved segments is not tested. - GSM8K involves prompts of a few hundred tokens — well within the 16× compression block. The strong performance here is interesting but does not constitute evidence of general short-context compression quality; a broader dense-prompt benchmark (e.g., MMLU in few-shot mode) would better validate this claim. Bottom Line For practitioners building long-context inference pipelines where high compression ratios (8×–16×) and compatibility with standard inference engines matter, LCLM is a meaningfully better choice than existing KV cache compression methods and represents a credible open-source artifact; at lower compression ratios (4×), KVzip is still competitive in accuracy and should be compared before adopting the higher complexity of an encoder-decoder system.
Recommended 2606.09659 PDF
#40 — Innovative (70)

Flow-DPPO: Divergence Proximal Policy Optimization for Flow Matching Models (32 ▲ )

Bowen Ping, Xiangxin Zhou, Penghui Qi, Minnan Luo, Liefeng Bo, Tianyu Pang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 10 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Flow-DPPO, an RL fine-tuning algorithm for flow matching image/video generation models that replaces PPO-style ratio clipping with an exact KL divergence-based asymmetric mask as the trust-region constraint. - Why it's important: PPO ratio clipping is structurally biased in the high-dimensional continuous Gaussian action space of flow models—clipping decisions are dominated by sampling noise rather than true policy divergence, causing spurious over-constraining on some trajectory steps while under-constraining others, which degrades image quality and enables reward hacking. - How it does it: The Gaussian structure of per-step policies in flow matching models admits exact, closed-form KL divergence computation (||μ_old − μ||² / 2σ²) from two forward passes already required during training, enabling a deterministic, noise-free trust-region mask at zero additional cost. - The good: Flow-DPPO achieves a 23.5 percentage point improvement in GenEval2 compositional accuracy over Flow-GRPO on FLUX2-9B under multi-reward training (57.7 vs. 46.8) while maintaining 4.5× lower KL divergence from the pretrained model (0.17×10⁻³ vs. 0.77×10⁻³), and enables stable multi-epoch sample reuse where ratio-clipping methods plateau or degrade. - The missing: The paper's main motivating application for multi-epoch efficiency—long-video generation—is never experimentally tested; all evaluations are on text-to-image generation at 512×512 resolution. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone working on RL alignment of diffusion or flow matching models; the theoretical analysis of ratio clipping's Gaussian-space bias is rigorous and the code is released, making the method immediately actionable.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (78) and Novelty (70) lead: the formal variance analysis showing std[log r] = sqrt(2·KL) — proving ratio clipping in continuous Gaussian action spaces is dominated by sampling noise rather than true divergence — is a genuinely non-obvious diagnosis, and exploiting the Gaussian per-step policy to compute exact KL at zero extra cost is a real structural insight rather than a routine TRPO adaptation. Rigor (68) is held back by single-run training curves with no error bars or seeds and the unablated MixGRPO confound shared with baselines, though code is released, baselines are current (2025–26), and results are consistent across three production-scale base models, two samplers, and single/multi-reward settings. Completeness (65) suffers because the headline motivation — multi-epoch efficiency for long-video generation — is asserted but never experimentally tested. Resilience (70) is supported by large, convincing FLUX2-9B margins (57.7 vs 46.8) and the 4.5× lower KL, but smaller SD3.5 margins under single-run noise weaken some pairwise claims. Practical Impact (70) reflects released code on production 9B models. Weighted: 0.20*70 + 0.25*68 + 0.15*78 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*70 + 0.10*70 = 70.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 68 Non-Obv 78 Completeness 65 Resilience 70 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Practitioners fine-tuning flow matching image generators (FLUX, SD3.5) with reward signals—compositional accuracy, human preference scores—use online RL methods that cast the denoising trajectory as an MDP. Prior methods (Flow-GRPO, Flow-CPS) apply PPO-style ratio clipping as a trust-region surrogate. The fundamental problem is that in continuous, high-dimensional Gaussian action spaces, the probability ratio r = p_new/p_old is a single-sample Monte Carlo estimate of the Total Variation divergence whose log has variance exactly equal to 2×KL. When KL is moderate (e.g., 0.5), individual log-ratio samples fluctuate by ±1.0 standard deviation around a mean of −0.5, corresponding to a roughly 3× multiplicative spread in the ratio itself. The narrow clipping range [0.8, 1.2] (log range ≈ [−0.22, 0.18]) therefore clips updates based on noise rather than true divergence, and the ratio distribution exhibits a systematic left-shift below 1 (mean < 1) that makes clipping effectively asymmetric—over-constraining negative-advantage samples while under-constraining positive-advantage ones. Key Idea For flow matching models with Gaussian per-step policies (both Flow-SDE and CPS samplers), the KL divergence between old and new policies reduces to a deterministic closed form: D_KL(π_θold || π_θ) = ||μ_θold − μ_θ||² / (2σ²). This quantity requires no sampling, is exact (not a Monte Carlo estimate), and is computed from the difference between two velocity-network forward passes already performed during training—making a true divergence-based trust region strictly cheaper than ratio clipping, not more expensive. Methodology The paper first derives a Policy Improvement Bound (Theorem 2) for finite-horizon undiscounted flow model MDPs with K−1 denoising steps: J(π_θ) − J(π_θold) ≥ L'_θold(π_θ) − 2ξ(K−1)(K−2)·(D^max_TV)², where ξ is the maximum absolute reward and D^max_TV is the maximum per-step TV divergence. This bound justifies constraining per-step divergence to guarantee monotonic policy improvement, and directly parallels the TRPO theory. The paper also shows that for Gaussian policies, constraining KL is equivalent to constraining TV (both are monotone functions of ||μ_old − μ||/σ), so the KL threshold directly enforces the bound. The Flow-DPPO objective is L^Flow-DPPO(θ) = E[1/G Σ_i 1/T Σ_t (M^i_t · r^i_t(θ) · Â^i − β·D_KL(π_θ || π_ref))], where the divergence mask M^i_t = 0 if and only if D_t > δ AND sgn(Â^i · (r^i_t − 1)) > 0 (i.e., both the KL exceeds the threshold and the update would push the policy further from π_θold). Otherwise M^i_t = 1. Three critical design decisions: (1) Exact KL vs. ratio clipping—justified by formal variance analysis. (2) Asymmetric masking—ablated empirically; symmetric masking causes training collapse by blocking all updates when divergence is large, preventing corrective moves. (3) Divergence threshold δ—ablated at 10⁻⁵, 10⁻⁶, 10⁻⁷; tighter thresholds yield more stable training at the cost of slower early learning. KL formulas are derived for both Flow-SDE and CPS samplers with sampler-specific variance terms. The appendix develops a predictive mask variant that predicts post-update divergence analytically but leaves empirical validation to future work. Key Results On FLUX2-9B under multi-reward RL (GenEval2 + CLIP + PickScore + HPSv2): Flow-DPPO 57.7 GenEval2 vs. Flow-GRPO 46.8, Flow-CPS 47.1, GRPO-Guard 49.0, Diffusion-NFT 47.3; Flow-DPPO+CPS achieves 55.2. On SD3.5 multi-reward: Flow-DPPO+CPS 51.6 vs. GRPO-Guard 47.8 (best baseline). KL divergence from pretrained model on FLUX2-9B single-reward: Flow-DPPO 0.17×10⁻³ vs. Flow-GRPO 0.77×10⁻³ (4.5× lower). Out-of-domain PickScore on FLUX2-9B multi-reward: Flow-DPPO+CPS 22.97 vs. Flow-GRPO 22.62. Multi-epoch training (G64-I2, samples reused twice): Flow-DPPO improves consistently while Flow-GRPO plateaus or degrades. Results are consistent across three base models (SD3.5-medium, FLUX2-9B, FLUX.1-dev) and both single- and multi-reward configurations. Baselines are current (all 2025–2026 preprints/publications). Total compute: ~140K GPU hours on NVIDIA H20 96GB GPUs. This is a preprint; results are not yet peer-reviewed. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The core novelty is the application of exact divergence-based trust regions to flow matching RL fine-tuning, exploiting the Gaussian policy structure that makes this tractable at zero cost. The policy improvement bound is a straightforward adaptation of TRPO/DPPO theory to the finite-horizon undiscounted setting with terminal rewards. The asymmetric mask design extends the DPPO concept (Qi et al., 2026) from LLMs—where exact divergence requires approximate vocabulary reductions—to flow models where the Gaussian structure provides it for free. The variance analysis proving ratio clipping's fundamental inadequacy in continuous action spaces is the key intellectual contribution distinguishing this from a routine application. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and informative: asymmetric masking (critical—symmetric variant collapses training, Figure 3), divergence threshold sensitivity (three values tested, Figure 3), reference KL regularization strength β (three values, Figures 11/13), CFG scale (Figure 10), multi-epoch configurations G32-I2 and G64-I2 (Figure 5). No error bars, confidence intervals, or multi-seed results are reported; all training curves reflect single runs, and the noise visible in figures makes single-run comparisons uncertain at marginal differences. Code is released at https://github.com/Tencent-Hunyuan/UniRL/tree/main/FlowDPPO. A methodological concern: all methods—including baselines—use the MixGRPO trick (mixing ODE and SDE sampling from Li et al., 2025), which may shift absolute performance; the contribution of this technique to Flow-DPPO's margin over baselines is not isolated. This is a preprint; vendor-affiliated authors (Tencent Hunyuan) evaluate on public benchmarks (GenEval2, PickScore), which reduces but does not eliminate self-report concerns. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Runs on NVIDIA H20 96GB GPUs with LoRA (r=32–64); accessible hardware for industrial practitioners, though ~90K GPU hours for main results is substantial. - Efficient: The divergence mask adds zero FLOPs over baseline (reuses already-computed velocity means); multi-epoch training with G32-I2 achieves equivalent or better performance at half the rollout compute, a concrete efficiency gain. - Effective: Consistently outperforms all four baselines on GenEval2 (in-domain) and largely on OOD metrics (PickScore, CLIP, HPSv2) across three base models, two samplers, and single/multi-reward settings; lower KL divergence from pretrained model confirms trust-region enforcement works as designed. - Scalable: Tested on production-scale 9B-parameter models (FLUX2-9B, FLUX.1-dev); the claimed advantage for long-video generation—the primary motivation for multi-epoch sample reuse—is asserted but never experimentally demonstrated. Delivers a code artifact (GitHub repository with the training algorithm). Strengths - The variance analysis (Appendix D) is rigorous and non-obvious: it formally shows that std[log r^i_t] = √(2·D_KL), so at even moderate KL=0.5 the log-ratio's standard deviation (1.0) exceeds the log-clip range width (≈0.4), proving that a substantial fraction of clipping decisions are noise-driven rather than divergence-driven. - The Gaussian policy structure of flow models yields a genuine structural advantage over the LLM setting: exact KL computation at zero extra cost, whereas DPPO for LLMs requires approximate Binary or Top-K vocabulary reductions over vocabularies of 100K+ tokens. - Empirical consistency across architecturally distinct base models (transformer-based SD3.5 and FLUX variants), two distinct samplers (Flow-SDE and CPS), and multiple reward configurations substantially reduces the risk of cherry-picking. - The multi-epoch training result (Figure 5) demonstrates a practically consequential capability—stable sample reuse that reduces rollout compute by up to 50%—that ratio-clipping methods cannot achieve, addressing a real bottleneck in expensive generative model RL training. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The predictive mask analysis in Appendix E treats the policy mean μ as a free parameter, whereas in practice it is a neural network output with shared weights across all inputs; the analysis is a local approximation that becomes less accurate for large learning rates or when the network Jacobian changes significantly across one step. The authors acknowledge this and explicitly leave empirical validation of the predictive and first-order masks to future work. They also flag one setting where Flow-DPPO+CPS shows higher KL than the Flow-CPS baseline (FLUX2-9B w/o CFG under CPS schedule after epoch 500). Overall the authors confront limitations with reasonable candor. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The paper's headline motivation—multi-epoch efficiency for long-video generation—is never validated experimentally; all results are on 512×512 text-to-image generation, leaving the primary use case as an unverified claim. - No multi-seed experiments or statistical significance tests are reported; given the visible training-curve noise, several pairwise comparisons with small margins (e.g., Flow-DPPO 57.7 vs. GRPO-Guard 49.0 on FLUX2-9B multi-reward is large and convincing, but SD3.5 multi-reward margins are smaller) may not be statistically reliable. - The MixGRPO trick (Li et al., 2025) is applied uniformly but not ablated; it is unclear how much of Flow-DPPO's improvement is attributable to the divergence mask vs. the ODE-SDE mixing strategy shared with baselines. - The threshold δ (set to 10⁻⁷ or 10⁻⁶ depending on sampler) requires careful per-model tuning; sensitivity across model scales and samplers is incompletely explored. - All fine-tuning uses LoRA; whether Flow-DPPO's advantage holds under full fine-tuning—which changes the optimization landscape—is not evaluated. Bottom Line A practitioner fine-tuning flow matching models with RL gains a theoretically grounded, computationally free improvement over PPO clipping that is empirically consistent across three production-scale models and released as code—this is a paper worth implementing.
Recommended 2606.11025 PDF
#41 — Innovative (69)

Redesign Mixture-of-Experts Routers with Manifold Power Iteration (74 ▲ )

Songhao Wu, Ang Lv, Ruobing Xie, Yankai Lin · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 11 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper proposes Manifold Power Iteration (MPI), a drop-in router redesign for Mixture-of-Experts LLMs that aligns each router row with the principal singular direction of its associated expert's weight matrix via a "Power-then-Retract" update applied once per training step. - Why it's important: Standard MoE routers optimize token–router dot products without any constraint enforcing that router rows actually encode expert features, producing a structural mismatch that impairs routing quality, slows convergence, and degrades downstream performance regardless of optimizer choice. - How it does it: One step of power iteration per forward pass nudges each router row toward the principal singular vector of the corresponding expert gate weight matrix, followed by L2 normalization (retraction) scaled as C'/√N to prevent norm explosion and decouple logit scale from expert count. - The good: MPI yields ~1.04× faster 11B pretraining convergence, +1.2–1.4 accuracy points averaged across 25 benchmarks at 1B scale (consistent across four optimizers), and +9.71 points on GSM8K at 11B — with only 0.2% training throughput overhead and zero inference overhead. - The missing: The paper compares MPI only against vanilla MoE trained in-house; no alternative principled router designs (expert-choice routing, hash routing, stronger regularization baselines) are evaluated, leaving the marginal contribution over the broader router literature uncertain. - Read it or not: MoE LLM practitioners should read this — the method is a near-zero-cost engineering add-on with consistent empirical gains, and the theoretical grounding is tight enough to build on; researchers studying MoE routing more broadly should note the missing baseline comparisons before treating the gains as definitive.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (78) and Novelty (75) are the strengths: aligning router rows with the principal singular direction of their expert via one online power-iteration step, with a proof connecting it to adaptive-step steepest ascent on the Rayleigh quotient under a spherical constraint, is principled and well-grounded. The composite is pulled down by Rigor (65) and Resilience (65): the only baseline is the authors' in-house vanilla MoE — no alternative router designs (expert-choice, hash, entropy-regularized) are compared — plus no seeds/error bars, no released code, and testing tops out at 11B. Impact (60) reflects pseudocode only with no artifact. Weighted: 0.20*75+0.25*65+0.15*78+0.15*68+0.15*65+0.10*60 = 69.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 78 Completeness 68 Resilience 65 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation In sparse MoE Transformers, the router is a linear matrix R ∈ ℝ^{N×D} whose rows act as proxy feature vectors for N experts. Token routing is determined by dot-product similarity between input tokens and these rows. The fundamental problem is that no training objective or architectural constraint forces R[i] to encode the intrinsic features of expert W_i: the router rows learn whatever direction minimizes next-token prediction loss, which may diverge substantially from the direction most representative of their expert. This structural gap means token–router affinity is a noisy proxy for token–expert compatibility, producing suboptimal routing decisions that manifest as slower training convergence and weaker downstream performance. The problem is optimizer-agnostic — it persists under AdamW, Muon, and their Hyperball variants. Key Idea The principal singular direction of a matrix is its maximally informative one-dimensional linear projection (Eckart–Young theorem, 1936). Aligning router row R[i] with the left principal singular vector of expert weight W_g^i maximizes the Rayleigh quotient ‖R[i] W_g^i‖₂² / ‖R[i]‖₂², ensuring the router encodes the expert's dominant representational direction. Since computing exact SVD at every training step is prohibitive, a single step of power iteration — R̂[i] = R[i] W_g^i (W_g^i)^T — cheaply approximates and progressively tracks convergence to this direction over training, requiring only two standard matrix–vector products per expert per step. Methodology MPI introduces two operations applied to the router matrix R at each forward pass: 1. Power Iteration: R̂[i] = R[i] W_g^i (W_g^i)^T — one step of the classical power method applied to the outer product of the expert gate weight, nudging R[i] toward the principal singular direction of W_g^i. 2. Retraction: R'[i] = C · R̂[i] / ‖R̂[i]‖₂, where C = C'/√N. This L2 normalization (a) prevents norm divergence from cumulative power iterations, (b) rectifies expert-bias from disparate router norm scales, and (c) enforces O(1) routing logit norms independent of N (justified by the bound ‖xR'^T‖_∞ ≤ O(C√N), requiring C ∝ 1/√N). The paper proves (Section 3.3, Appendix A) that this update is equivalent to steepest ascent on the Rayleigh quotient objective under a spherical manifold constraint with an adaptive step size that naturally decreases as R[i] approaches the principal direction. Three critical design decisions are well-justified: (1) single iteration rather than multiple — empirically, 10 iterations reduce throughput by 5% with 0.002–0.003 worse loss and −1.39 pp downstream; (2) retraction is mandatory — without it, loss spikes occur under AdamW/Muon; (3) C' is searched over {1, 2, 4, 8} on small models and transfers to 11B (C'=4 optimal). Only W_g (the gate weight of the SiLU-gated FFN expert) is used for power iteration; W_p and W_o show no significant difference. Experiments span 1B (100B tokens), 3B and 11B (350B tokens + 100B mid-training on OLMo-3 data) models on FineWeb-Edu, implemented in TorchTitan with MegaBlocks MoE kernels and FSDP. Key Results At 1B scale across four optimizers, MPI improves average accuracy over 25 benchmarks by +1.30 pp (AdamW: 42.26→43.56), +1.34 pp (AdamH: 42.59→43.93), +0.54 pp (Muon: 43.01→43.55), +1.20 pp (MuonH: 42.78→43.98). At 11B scale, MPI achieves ~1.04× faster convergence (reaching the same pretraining loss ~4% sooner in token count) and improves the 7-task challenge average from 40.92 to 42.76, with the largest gain on GSM8K (17.89→27.60, 8-shot CoT). Perplexity improves on math held-out data from 1.852→1.581 at 11B. Load balancing MaxVio_Batch drops from 1.133→1.024 and MaxVio_Global from 0.964→0.711 at 3B. Router–expert alignment metric λ (normalized projection onto expert singular direction) roughly doubles: 0.22–0.37 for vanilla MoE vs. 0.62–0.70 for MPI across all 12 layers. Training overhead: 0.2%; inference overhead: 0% (router precomputed at load time). Baseline is vanilla MoE trained with identical configuration — no external published MoE baselines appear. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The core novelty is the principled alignment of MoE router rows with the principal singular directions of their corresponding experts via online power iteration, along with the theoretical proof connecting this update to spherical manifold optimization of the Rayleigh quotient. While power iteration, SVD, and Rayleigh quotients are classical linear algebra, their application as a per-step router update rule in MoE training, and the resulting convergence theory in that context, has not appeared in prior work. The retraction mechanism and the 1/√N scaling principle are derived results, not ad hoc choices. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and informative: power iteration alone vs. retraction alone vs. full MPI properly isolates each component, confirming that row-wise normalization without power iteration matches vanilla MoE on accuracy (while improving balance loss). Sensitivity to C' is tested over four values. Number of power iteration steps (1 vs. 10) is ablated. Expert weight matrix choice (W_g vs. W_p vs. W_o) is explored. Four optimizers at 1B scale provide partial robustness coverage substituting for multi-seed experiments. No explicit error bars, standard deviations, or significance tests are reported for any result. No code or model weights are released (no repository URL provided). The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2606.12397v1) and has not undergone peer review. A methodological concern: the only baseline is vanilla MoE trained in the authors' own pipeline — no externally validated MoE checkpoints appear, creating the possibility that pipeline-specific advantages inflate reported gains. The primary evaluation suite (25 multiple-choice tasks via OLMES) is well-established, mitigating benchmark-gaming risk. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL - Practical: The method adds ~5 lines of PyTorch to the MoE forward pass (Figure 1), requires no architectural changes, and is compatible with FSDP and MegaBlocks — deployable on any standard GPU cluster running existing MoE training stacks. - Efficient: Training overhead is 0.2% (measured at 11B scale, 34.97B tokens/day baseline), equivalent to N extra tokens of compute per batch step; inference overhead is zero because the power-iterated router is precomputed once during model loading. - Effective: Gains are consistent across scales (1B, 3B, 11B), optimizers (4 variants), and evaluation protocols (perplexity, 25-task accuracy, challenge benchmarks), and the ablation confirms the mechanism is causal rather than an artifact of normalization alone. - Scalable: Tested up to 11B parameters and 350B tokens; the computational cost scales as O(N × D × d_ffn) per MoE layer per step, which is dominated by the existing expert computation, making the theoretical scalability argument strong — but no experiments beyond 11B validate this claim empirically. The work delivers no software artifact — it stops at paper form with pseudocode. Strengths - The theoretical analysis (Section 3.3 and Appendix A) is complete and transparent: MPI is proven to implement adaptive-stepsize steepest ascent on the Rayleigh quotient under a spherical norm constraint, giving the method a rigorous geometric interpretation rather than relying on empirical motivation alone. - Optimizer agnosticism is demonstrated rather than assumed: consistent gains across AdamW, AdamH, Muon, and MuonH at 1B scale rule out the explanation that improvements stem from interaction with any specific optimizer's update structure. - The C = C'/√N scaling principle is derived from a concrete bounded-logit assumption, making the hyperparameter principled and transferable — the small-scale sweep at C' ∈ {1,2,4,8} successfully transfers to 11B with no collapse, validating the theory. - The post-hoc λ analysis (Table 5) directly verifies the mechanism: MPI produces ~2× higher router–expert alignment scores across all layers, linking the design principle to the observed performance gains. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors acknowledge that improved load balancing is tentative and attribute it speculatively to retraction, deferring deeper investigation (Section 4.2). They do not investigate combinations of expert weight matrices (W_g, W_p, W_o) for power iteration. Exploration of Sigmoid gating (vs. Softmax) is explicitly deferred. Whether row-wise normalization alone can improve load balancing as a side effect is flagged as an open question. The authors express hope — but do not claim — that 11B findings extrapolate to larger scales. These are direct and honest acknowledgments that increase confidence in the reported results. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The paper compares MPI exclusively against the authors' in-house vanilla MoE. Alternative principled router designs — expert-choice routing, hash routing, differentiable top-k, or router regularization via entropy penalties — are not compared. The "orthogonality" argument justifies composability, not superiority over alternatives. - The causal chain from higher λ (alignment) to downstream accuracy improvement is demonstrated by correlation, not intervention: an experiment zeroing out the alignment effect while keeping other MPI components would strengthen the mechanistic claim. - Testing tops out at 11B parameters and 350B tokens; by 2026 production MoE standards (DeepSeek-V4, Kimi K2, cited in the paper), this is small. Whether gains persist at 100B+ parameter scale or with trillion-token training remains open. - The GSM8K gain at 11B (+9.71 pp: 17.89→27.60) is disproportionately large compared to other benchmarks and relative to the 3B gain (+4.7 pp: 16.22→20.92).
