No analysis is provided for why math reasoning benefits so much more than other capabilities — this surprising finding deserves scrutiny. - All training data is English-language (FineWeb-Edu, OLMo-3); whether MPI advantages hold in multilingual or domain-specific MoE settings is untested. - No convergence rate bound is provided for the power iteration within the training dynamics — the theoretical guarantee is asymptotic (eventual convergence) without sample complexity or mixing time analysis. Bottom Line A practitioner building or fine-tuning MoE LLMs gains a concrete, near-zero-cost improvement from this work — MPI is a principled, well-analyzed, and empirically consistent upgrade to the MoE router — but publication-level confidence requires validation at production scale and honest head-to-head comparison with other router design choices.
Recommended 2606.12397 PDF
#42 — Innovative (69)

Reinforcement Learning Elicits Contextual Learning of Unseen Language Translation

H Hu, Z Šnajdr, P Chen, J Vamvas, R Sennrich · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper proposes training LLMs with GRPO-based reinforcement learning using chrF as the reward signal to acquire a transferable meta-skill of exploiting in-context linguistic resources (dictionaries, parallel sentences, grammar passages) when translating entirely unseen languages. - Why it's important: Standard fine-tuning on low-resource languages catastrophically suppresses a model's ability to leverage in-context linguistic evidence for unseen languages, leaving the hundreds of endangered languages with no practical NLP support even when grammar documentation exists. - How it does it: By training the model on seen low-resource languages with rich linguistic context in the prompt and rewarding only surface-level translation quality (chrF), RL forces the policy to learn how to use context rather than memorize language-specific mappings—a meta-skill that transfers across language families. - The good: RL-trained Qwen3-4B averages chrF 0.27 on 5 completely unseen languages versus 0.09 for SFT and 0.18 for the untuned base model, with RL outperforming SFT on every individual unseen language while SFT falls below the base model—a sharp causal result. - The missing: All five unseen test languages receive dictionary and parallel sentence context at test time, so the true grammar-only zero-resource scenario is never tested; additionally, only 100 test sentences per unseen language with no significance testing makes cross-language averages noisy. - Read it or not: Worth reading for MT and low-resource NLP researchers—the meta-skill framing and the finding that SFT actively degrades context utilization are novel and directly actionable, and the ablation design is unusually clean.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (80) and Novelty (78) drive the score: the meta-skill framing (RLVR teaches context-utilization, not language content) is genuinely new, and the counter-intuitive finding that SFT actively suppresses context utilization below the base model is crisp and well-evidenced by a disciplined matched train/test ablation design. Rigor (62) is the main limiter — no error bars on 100-sentence test sets, no comparison to published MTOB baselines, and synthetic GPT-5-mini dictionaries (the most impactful component) are unvalidated by native speakers; code is released but no weights. Completeness (68) is dented because the motivating grammar-only zero-resource scenario is never actually tested. Strongly aligned with the user's positive RL-phenomenology taste. Weighted: 0.20*78 + 0.25*62 + 0.15*80 + 0.15*68 + 0.15*62 + 0.10*65 = 69.
empirical Novelty 78 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 68 Resilience 62 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Translating extremely low-resource or entirely unseen languages is a persistent failure mode for LLMs despite their general capability. Two dominant paradigms—post-training on additional parallel data and in-context learning via grammar books or parallel examples—both fall short in the same way: they overfit to specific languages seen during training or prompting and show limited zero-shot transfer to new language families. Linguists produce grammar books and bilingual dictionaries for endangered languages, but current LLMs cannot reliably exploit this heterogeneous in-context evidence in a transferable way. The specific gap being addressed is the absence of a training method that teaches models the skill of leveraging linguistic context rather than the content of any particular language. Key Idea The core insight is that translating a new language from its grammar book is formally analogous to RLVR reasoning tasks like math: the answer is verifiable (chrF against a reference translation), and the context (dictionary, parallel sentences, grammar) is a structured support set that must be actively reasoned over. By optimizing only the translation outcome reward while keeping linguistic context in the prompt during training, the RL objective has no path to improvement except learning to extract and apply the contextual evidence—there is no language-specific knowledge to memorize because training and evaluation use different language families. This reframes extreme low-resource translation as meta-learning via outcome-based RL rather than supervised language acquisition. Methodology Data spans 14 languages from 10 families: 8 grammar-book languages (Choguita Rarámuri, Gyeli, Japhug, Kagayanen, Kalamang, Tuatschin, Ulwa, Vamale) with parallel sentences extracted from LaTeX sources of Language Science Press grammars, and 6 Romansh varieties with existing parallel corpora. Only the 4 seen Romansh varieties plus 7 grammar-book languages (22 directions total) are used for training; 2 Romansh varieties and 5 languages from unrelated families are held out as test-only. Bilingual dictionaries for 7 non-Kalamang languages are synthesized via GPT-5 mini using two prompt designs (v1: token-specific demonstrations; v2: format-only), with the better-performing variant selected per language via downstream chrF. Each prompt contains: linguistic introduction, translation instruction, up to 2 LCS-retrieved dictionary entries per source token (~20–34 total), 3 or 5 LCS-retrieved parallel sentence pairs, and 2 grammar excerpts (~2.8k tokens average). LCS outperformed embedding-based retrieval in preliminary experiments. The closing instruction requests step-by-step meta-linguistic reasoning with the final translation in a \boxed{} environment; only the boxed output is scored. Two backbones are compared: Qwen3-4B-Base and Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct, each trained with both SFT (cross-entropy on gold references) and RL. RL uses GRPO: 8 rollouts per prompt, group-relative advantage normalization (no separate value model), PPO-style clipped objective, KL penalty (1e−4) against a frozen reference policy, chrF/100 as the reward. Full-parameter fine-tuning with DeepSpeed ZeRO-2, FSDP, and SGLang for rollout (TP=4). Training runs 1 epoch; batch size 64 for RL, 128 for SFT; learning rate 1e−6 for RL, 1e−5 for SFT. Three critical design decisions: (1) LCS-based retrieval—justified by preliminary comparison; (2) matched train/test context in ablations so policies are never evaluated on components absent at training; (3) evaluating no-context performance at test time to separate context-dependent from internalized knowledge—well-motivated and cleanly executed. Key Results On seen Romansh→German (4 varieties averaged), Qwen3-4B SFT reaches chrF 0.60 versus RL's 0.52; Llama SFT 0.56 versus RL 0.48. SFT's advantage persists on the held-out Similar Romansh varieties (Qwen SFT 0.55 vs. RL 0.48). On 5 unseen languages (Kalamang, Dinka, Wolof, Guarani, Kachin) the ranking reverses: Qwen RL averages 0.27 versus SFT 0.09 and base model 0.18; Llama RL averages 0.24 versus SFT 0.09 and base 0.14. SFT falls below the untuned base model on every unseen language, indicating that supervised training on Romansh actively suppresses context-leveraging ability for unrelated families. Context ablation (RL, Qwen3-4B-Base): dictionary removal costs 8.4 chrF on seen Romansh and 8.4 on En→Kalamang; parallel sentence removal costs 1.0 on seen Romansh but 7.3 on En→Kalamang (where parallel examples provide a complete in-distribution analogy); grammar removal costs only 0.8–1.5 across all settings. Train–test context mismatch analysis shows test-time context dominates: no/full (trained without, evaluated with) outperforms full/no (trained with, evaluated without) on all sets (En→Kal: 0.28 vs. 0.17), but full/full exceeds no/full by 7 chrF on En→Kal (0.35 vs. 0.28), confirming training with context teaches more systematic exploitation. Training reward trajectories confirm full-context run reaches 0.68 training chrF versus 0.62 without dictionary and 0.29 for task-only. Baselines used (Qwen3-4B-Base and Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct untuned) are current and relevant but no comparison to published MTOB state-of-the-art figures from prior work (e.g., Tanzer et al. 2024) is provided, making absolute performance context hard to assess. Test scale: 100 sentences per unseen language, 1,462 for seen Romansh. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The use of RLVR to teach a language-independent meta-skill of in-context linguistic reasoning for translation is genuinely novel—prior RL-for-MT work optimizes translation quality from source-target pairs without exploiting in-context linguistic resources, and prior grammar-book translation work uses ICL or SFT without RL. The empirical finding that SFT actively degrades context utilization below the base model for unseen families is non-obvious and well-supported by the controlled experimental design. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present, carefully matched (train and test always use the same context configuration), and address the most important questions (component importance, training-vs-test-time context, reward trajectory). Two backbone models produce consistent patterns. No error bars, confidence intervals, or significance tests are reported despite small test sets (100 sentences per unseen language). Code is released at https://github.com/hanxuhu/rl-new-language. Synthetic dictionary data generated by GPT-5 mini is not validated by native speakers of the target languages; since dictionaries are the most impactful retrieval component, quality issues here could systematically bias results. This is an arXiv preprint (v1, June 2026), not peer-reviewed. The 3 vs. 5 parallel sentence condition is mentioned as an experimental variable but results are not broken out by this condition in the main tables. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Inference uses 3–4B parameter models on standard GPU hardware; full-parameter RL training requires multi-GPU setup (DeepSpeed ZeRO-2, FSDP, SGLang TP=4), which is available on standard research clusters. - Efficient: Prompts average ~2.8k tokens; GRPO with 8 rollouts multiplies inference compute by 8× during training; one epoch over 23k pairs is manageable, but the retrieval pipeline (LCS over dictionary and parallel sentence pools) adds preprocessing overhead not quantified in the paper. - Effective: RL consistently and substantially outperforms SFT on unseen languages across both models and all 5 language directions tested; however, absolute chrF 0.27 on unseen languages remains low relative to seen-language performance (0.52), and adequacy is not confirmed by human evaluation. - Scalable: Tested only at 3–4B scale; no ablation on model size or training language diversity is provided; whether the meta-skill generalizes further with larger models or more typologically diverse training languages is an open claim. The work delivers code and model-training recipes; no trained model weights are released. Strengths - The meta-skill framing cleanly unifies ICL and post-training under one falsifiable objective and generates sharp predictions (RL should generalize; SFT should memorize), which the data confirms. - The controlled ablation design—matched train/test context, train-test mismatch 2×2 analysis—is methodologically disciplined and isolates the causal contributions of training signal versus test-time context. - The empirical finding that SFT actively suppresses context utilization below the base model on unseen languages is a crisp, counter-intuitive result that has direct practical implications for practitioners choosing between SFT and RL for cross-lingual transfer. - The resource diversity (10 language families, 14 languages, bidirectional translation) is unusually broad for a paper of this scale and strengthens generalization claims. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge two limitations: (1) no human evaluation—chrF numbers are treated as relative improvement signals only, with human evaluation deferred to future work; (2) absolute performance on unseen languages still lags behind seen languages, pointing to headroom from richer context and stronger context-utilization signals. Both are stated directly without minimization, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No comparison to published MTOB benchmark baselines (Tanzer et al. 2024, Hus & Anastasopoulos 2024 prior results) is included; the paper uses those test sets but reports only its own trained baselines, making it impossible to assess absolute progress relative to the field. - All five unseen test languages still receive dictionary and parallel sentence context at test time; the paper never tests the grammar-only scenario that motivates the framing (a truly zero-resource language where only a grammar book exists). - Synthetic dictionary quality for 7 of 8 grammar-book languages is unvalidated by native speakers; given dictionaries are the most impactful component (−8.4 chrF when removed), corrupt entries could systematically inflate or deflate results in ways not detectable from chrF alone. - Training data is strongly dominated by Romansh↔German (21,890 of 23,587 training pairs); the 7 non-Romansh training languages contribute only 9,695 pairs across 14 directions, raising the question of whether the meta-skill is learned from those low-resource directions or primarily from the Romansh data and then transferred. - The 3 vs. 5 parallel sentence ablation is set up as an experimental condition (Section 3.1.5) but results broken out by this variable never appear in the paper. Bottom Line A practitioner working on endangered language NLP or on extending RLVR beyond reasoning tasks will find this paper directly useful: the meta-skill framing is sharp, the SFT-suppresses-context finding is non-obvious and well-evidenced, and the ablation results give concrete guidance on which context components to invest in—but should verify claims against published MTOB baselines and treat absolute chrF numbers on 100-sentence test sets with appropriate uncertainty.
Citations 2606.06428 PDF
#43 — Innovative (69)

Assessing Automated Prompt Injection Attacks in Agentic Environments

D Hofer, E Debenedetti, F Tramèr · arxiv.org · automated generated environments for agent testing - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a systematic empirical evaluation of automated prompt injection attacks against tool-calling LLM agents, adapting GCG (gradient-based white-box) and TAP (tree-search black-box) from jailbreaking to indirect prompt injection in the AgentDojo framework across 80 task pairs and three target models. - Why it's important: LLM agents that autonomously retrieve untrusted external data are exposed to indirect prompt injection, yet no prior work had systematically compared automated attack optimization paradigms in realistic stateful, multi-step agentic environments where success requires executing specific tool calls with correct arguments. - How it does it: GCG optimizes 30 adversarial tokens (prefix+suffix) in tool outputs toward affirmative natural-language confirmation targets; TAP runs a breadth-first tree search using GPT-5 as attacker and GPT-5-mini as judge, with structured per-iteration feedback; both are evaluated against AgentDojo's deterministic environment-state check functions as ground truth. - The good: A random-signal ablation shows that gradient guidance in GCG provides no measurable benefit over random token search on Qwen3-4B (~33% vs. ~25% ASR), and attacker model capability produces a 4.2–11.8× ASR improvement for universal TAP — findings with broad implications for attack methodology assumptions. - The missing: All undefended agent baselines are tested; given that recent defenses (CausalArmor, AgentSentry) report near-zero ASR on AgentDojo, the paper does not establish whether any of these attacks are practically relevant against deployed defended systems. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone working on LLM agent security or adversarial robustness of instruction-following systems, particularly for the coercive/exploitative attack taxonomy and the counterintuitive findings about gradient utility and universal optimization.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (78) is the lead contributor — the random-signal ablation, prefix+suffix and target-formulation ablations, 95% bootstrap CIs, and stated code release on a public framework (AgentDojo) are solid, though the 80-of-900 task selection is unjustified and GPT-5 serves as both attacker and target. Non-Obviousness (75) is high: the finding that gradient guidance gives no benefit over random search, and that black-box TAP beats white-box GCG, is genuinely counterintuitive. Novelty (60) is moderate — the GCG/TAP adaptation is methodologically incremental over Pandya et al./Dreadnode; the contribution is the systematic comparison. Completeness (70) and Resilience (68) are pulled down by the absence of any defended-agent baseline, leaving practical threat unquantified. Impact (55) reflects a framework extension only, no attack library or model artifact. 0.20*60+0.25*78+0.15*75+0.15*70+0.15*68+0.10*55 = 69.
empirical Novelty 60 Rigor 78 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 70 Resilience 68 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation LLM agents process trusted system/user instructions and untrusted tool outputs (emails, file contents, web pages) through a single shared attention context delimited only by special tokens — not architectural separation. An adversary with write access to any external data source the agent retrieves can embed malicious instructions that the model follows as if they were legitimate commands, causing goal hijacking, data exfiltration, or unauthorized financial transactions. While automated adversarial methods (GCG, PAIR, TAP) have achieved high success rates in static single-turn jailbreaking, their effectiveness in the qualitatively harder agentic setting — where the attacker must coerce specific multi-step tool invocations with correct arguments against a stateful environment — was not characterized. Prior prompt injection work relied on manual attacks or evaluated only text-generation settings (e.g., summarization), not tool-executing agents. Key Idea The core technical insight is that prompt injection in agentic settings is a semantic exploitation problem, not a token-optimization problem. The necessary context for a functional exploit (tool schemas, task descriptions, calling conventions) already exists in the agent's input; an effective injection needs only to exploit the model's instruction-following bias via authority framing or contextual prerequisite framing — not to encode system-specific details into adversarial tokens. This explains why black-box TAP (semantic search) consistently outperforms white-box GCG (token-level gradient descent) despite lacking model access, and why the gradient itself provides little benefit over random search in GCG. Methodology The paper formalizes single-task injection as argmax over adversarial token sequence xadv to maximize P(y* | C(xfixed, xadv)), and task-universal injection as the sum of this objective across a corpus of k task pairs. For GCG: prefix+suffix structure with 15 tokens each (30 total), 800 optimization steps, batch size 256, targeting affirmative natural-language confirmations (not JSON) to avoid tokenization fragility — a decode-reencode filter rejects candidates with unstable round-trip tokenization. For TAP: branching factor 3, width 8, depth 5, 3 root nodes; the attacker model (GPT-5) receives structured feedback (score, full agent response, triggered tool calls) and maintains per-branch conversation history; on-topic pruning is omitted as ineffective in the injection setting; refusal mitigation uses defensive-security framing and evaluator penalties for refusals. The framework is extended with a custom TransformersLLM class providing gradient access and multi-GPU parallelism. Evaluation: n=4 independent optimization runs × m=6 evaluation runs, reporting ASR, S@N (N=4), and utility. Success is verified via AgentDojo's deterministic check functions (not LLM-as-judge). Critical design decisions: affirmative target formulation (well-justified: 13% vs. 5% ASR over JSON target); prefix+suffix over single-sided (well-justified: 14% vs. 8% ASR over prefix-only); omission of on-topic pruning (stated but not rigorously quantified). Key Results On Qwen3-4B (undefended): Single-Task TAP 44.6% ASR, Universal TAP 45.2%, Single-Task GCG 23.0%, Universal GCG 24.1%, Direct Instruction ~15–25% (domain-dependent), Random Prefix-Suffix comparable to GCG. Slack suite is most vulnerable (~67% ASR, Single-Task TAP); Workspace most robust. GPT-5 substantially more robust: best attack (Universal TAP) achieves 4.7% ASR but 30.0% S@N — meaning at least one of four attempts succeeds on 30% of task pairs. GCG transfer from Qwen3-4B within family: Qwen3-32B 24.7–36.0% ASR; cross-family to GPT-5, GPT-5-mini, Claude Sonnet 4.5: all below 2%; Gemini 2.5 Flash partial exception at 7.7% universal GCG. Attacker model capability for universal TAP: GPT-5 vs. GPT-5-mini yields 4.2× improvement on Qwen3-4B and 11.8× on GPT-5. Random-signal GCG achieves ~33% ASR vs. ~25% for gradient-guided GCG on Qwen3-4B. LLM judge (GPT-5-mini): 100% recall but 52.3% precision on Qwen3-4B; 85.9% accuracy on GPT-5 (dominated by true negatives). Experiments run on individual NVIDIA H200 GPUs (144GB); single-task GCG ~1.5h/run, universal GCG ~21.9h/run, single-task TAP ~0.6h/run. Baselines are current (Qwen3, Gemma3, GPT-5 at experiment time); open-weights baselines are 4B-parameter instruction-tuned models with function calling, which is small relative to frontier deployment. Novelty Classification NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The empirical findings — that black-box semantic search outperforms white-box gradient optimization, that gradient guidance in GCG provides no benefit over random search for agent injection, that universal attacks match single-task performance, and the coercive/exploitative attack strategy taxonomy — are genuinely novel. The AgentDojo extension (TransformersLLM class, gradient support, universal optimization infrastructure) is a tool contribution. The adaptation of GCG and TAP to prompt injection is methodologically incremental (prior work by Pandya et al. and Dreadnode existed for each separately); the contribution is the systematic comparative evaluation in a realistic agentic framework. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations cover: injection structure (prefix+suffix vs. single-sided), optimization signal (gradient vs. random), target formulation (affirmative vs. JSON), attacker model capability (GPT-5 vs. GPT-5-mini), and evaluator accuracy. Error bars (95% bootstrap CIs) are reported on key figures. n=4 seeds × m=6 evaluation runs provide reasonable but not extensive coverage of stochasticity. Code release is stated in the ethical considerations section. AgentDojo is publicly available. Methodological concerns: (1) The 80-task-pair evaluation set is manually selected from AgentDojo's 900+ pairs — selection criteria are not fully specified, and representativeness is asserted rather than demonstrated. (2) GPT-5 is used as both attacker model and frontier target, creating a potential self-knowledge advantage that the paper does not discuss. (3) LLM judge precision of 52.3% on Qwen3-4B means TAP's optimization signal is badly miscalibrated, likely suppressing measured ASR below what a better judge would achieve. (4) Gemma3-4B is excluded from TAP evaluation due to infrastructure limitations, creating an asymmetric comparison across methods. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL - Practical: GCG requires a single H200 GPU (144GB) for white-box optimization; TAP requires only API access to an attacker LLM — both are commercially available. - Efficient: TAP at ~0.6h per single-task run is substantially cheaper than GCG at ~1.5h; universal GCG at ~21.9h per run makes large-scale automated red-teaming impractical without significant compute. - Effective: TAP achieves 44.6% ASR on undefended Qwen3-4B but only 4.7% ASR (30% S@N) on GPT-5 with medium reasoning; effectiveness is strongly model-dependent and drops sharply against frontier targets. - Scalable: Tested only on 4B-parameter open-weights models for white-box evaluation; cross-family GCG transfer below 2% ASR against frontier models indicates the attack paradigm does not scale across model families — a fundamental barrier for model-agnostic exploitation. The paper delivers an extended AgentDojo codebase and planned public release, but does not include trained models, datasets, or a reusable attack library beyond the framework extension. Strengths - The random-signal ablation (GCG with random token proposals matching gradient-guided GCG) is the most intellectually important result: it challenges the field's assumption that gradient access is the key advantage of white-box attacks, and generalizes the RAILS finding to the agentic injection setting. - The attacker model capability analysis for universal TAP (4.2–11.8× ASR improvement from GPT-5-mini to GPT-5) identifies a critical evaluation variable that prior work conflated with the method itself, improving reproducibility standards for future black-box attack assessments. - The coercive/exploitative attack strategy taxonomy (Section 6.4) provides a mechanistic, qualitative explanation for the GPT-5 robustness gap: authority-override injections fail because GPT-5 ignores them, while domain-native document framing and contextual prerequisite framing exploit the same instruction-following capability that makes the agent useful — a finding with direct implications for detection-based defenses. - Deterministic success verification via AgentDojo's environment-state check functions eliminates LLM-as-judge noise from ground truth, enabling principled comparison across methods. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state: (1) 80-task-pair subset may not capture full AgentDojo diversity; (2) LLM judge unreliability introduces optimization bias toward false positives on Qwen3-4B; (3) single source model (Qwen3-4B) for GCG transfer understates potential cross-family rates achievable with multi-source optimization. These are confronted directly, which is a credibility positive. Missing from their self-critique: the GPT-5 attacker/target circularity and the absence of defended agent baselines. Analyst-Identified Gaps - GPT-5 serves as both the primary attacker model in TAP and the frontier target for robustness evaluation; any implicit self-knowledge GPT-5 has about its own refusal patterns could inflate or deflate measured ASR in ways the paper does not acknowledge or bound. - No defended agent baselines are evaluated: the paper cites CausalArmor and AgentSentry as achieving near-zero ASR on AgentDojo with high utility preservation, but does not test whether the optimized injections bypass these defenses — leaving the practical threat level entirely unquantified for production deployments. - Gemma3-4B's lower vulnerability is attributed to a capability-robustness tradeoff (lower instruction-following ability → fewer successful injections), but this is not ablated; the paper does not test whether Gemma3 with the same baseline utility as Qwen3-4B would show similar attack susceptibility. - Utility under attack is tracked but not broken down by domain, attack method, or task type, which would reveal whether successful injections are detectable via utility degradation — a key practical concern for deployment monitoring. - The paper evaluates undefended agents in 2026, when a body of published defenses exists; the framing as establishing attack effectiveness without engaging with defenses limits the actionability of the findings for practitioners. Bottom Line Practitioners building or securing LLM agents should read this for the TAP > GCG finding and the transferability analysis, but should treat the reported ASR numbers as upper bounds on threat in defended settings rather than evidence that current production deployments are compromised at these rates.
Search Results 2606.10525 PDF
#44 — Innovative (69)

GlucoFM-Bench: Benchmarking Time-Series Foundation Models for Blood Glucose Forecasting

B Lu, Z Liang, R Pontius, S Tang, T Prioleau · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces GlucoFM-Bench, a standardized benchmark evaluating eight time-series foundation models and task-specific deep learning baselines for continuous blood glucose forecasting across 15 publicly available CGM datasets spanning 1,117 individuals. - Why it's important: Automated insulin delivery systems require accurate short-horizon glucose predictions, yet no prior work had systematically benchmarked general-purpose TSFMs on this clinically consequential task across diverse diabetes populations and data-availability regimes. - How it does it: The benchmark applies zero-shot, few-shot (5% of training data), and full-shot evaluation protocols to four pre-trained TSFMs, two LLM-adapted models, and two supervised baselines, with stratified analyses by glycemic range and diabetes type, augmented by Clarke and Surveillance Error Grid clinical risk metrics. - The good: Zero-shot TimesFM2.5 achieves an overall 30-minute RMSE of 18.75 mg/dL — only 4.3% worse than the best full-shot model (LSTM at 17.97 mg/dL) — demonstrating compelling out-of-domain transfer without any glucose-specific training; pre-trained TSFMs also outperform supervised models specifically in the hypoglycemic range, the most safety-critical regime. - The missing: The benchmark is univariate-only, omitting insulin delivery and carbohydrate intake covariates that are essential for real closed-loop AID systems and that would likely change the model rankings. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners building glucose forecasting pipelines or evaluating TSFMs in healthcare domains; the public code and standardized dataset splits are immediately reusable, though those focused on general TSFM benchmarking will find the findings largely recapitulate known TSFM behavior transposed to a new domain.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (78) and Completeness (80) are the top contributors: 15 datasets / 1,117 individuals / ~2.6M test records, per-participant means and SDs, Wilcoxon tests with BH-FDR correction, released code and HF dataset, plus clinically grounded CEG/SEG metrics; honest scope limitations. Novelty (58) is moderate because several findings recapitulate known TSFM behavior, though the hypoglycemia advantage and fine-tuning calibration collapse are non-obvious (Non-Obv 58). Resilience (72) solid. Impact (68) from reusable public assets. Held just below the 75 band by the largely-confirmatory findings, univariate-only scope, and absent compute reporting. Resembles the positive set in rigor.
empirical Novelty 58 Rigor 78 Non-Obv 58 Completeness 80 Resilience 72 Impact 68
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Diabetes management increasingly relies on CGM data and automated insulin delivery systems that require short-to-medium-horizon blood glucose forecasting (15–90 minutes ahead). Existing supervised deep learning models — RNNs, LSTMs, Transformers — are typically trained and evaluated on individual datasets or narrow cohorts, limiting cross-dataset and cross-population generalizability. Time-series foundation models offer potential zero-shot and few-shot transfer by pre-training on large heterogeneous corpora, but established TSFM benchmarks cover energy, finance, weather, and traffic — not glucose. A small number of glucose-specific foundation models (GluFormer, sensor-pretrained transformers) exist, but how general-purpose TSFMs compare to each other and to task-specific baselines across diverse diabetes populations (T1D, T2D, prediabetes, no diabetes), glycemic ranges, and data regimes was completely unknown before this work. Key Idea The core insight is that general-purpose TSFMs, despite pre-training entirely on non-glucose time series, transfer meaningfully to glucose forecasting in zero- and few-shot settings because glucose dynamics share temporal structure (local trend, periodic patterns, variability) with signals encountered during pre-training — but under full data availability, the inductive biases of a domain-specific LSTM outweigh the generalization advantages of large pre-trained models. A secondary insight is that the hypoglycemic range — rare in training data (2.4% of test records) — is precisely where TSFMs' generalizable representations provide the most advantage over supervised methods that overfit to frequent in-range and hyperglycemic patterns. Methodology The benchmark frames glucose prediction as a sliding-window regression task over CGM time series at 15, 30, 60, and 90-minute horizons with input context lengths of 1–24 hours (12 hours as default). Fifteen CGM datasets are harmonized to 5-minute resolution via upsampling/downsampling and linear interpolation for gaps under one hour; gaps longer than one hour become sequence boundaries. Per-participant chronological 80/20 splits are applied to the 12 open-access datasets; three controlled-access datasets (OhioT1DM, DiaTrend, T1DEXI) serve as external test sets. Eight models are evaluated: (1) a persistence baseline; (2) supervised DL — LSTM (Martinsson et al.) and GPFormer (Zhu et al., Transformer-based); (3) pre-trained TSFMs — Chronos-2 (encoder-decoder), Moirai 2.0 (decoder-only), Timer (decoder-only), TimesFM 2.5 (decoder-only); (4) LLM-adapted — TimeLLM (GPT-2 with textual prompt reprogramming) and CALF (GPT-2 cross-modal fine-tuning). Full-shot uses 1-hour stride; few-shot uses 20-hour stride to yield ~5% of windows. Metrics include RMSE, MAE, Clarke Error Grid Zone A ratio, and Surveillance Error Grid no-risk ratio. Pairwise Wilcoxon tests with Benjamini-Hochberg FDR correction are reported. Three critical design decisions: (1) per-participant chronological splits are well-justified to prevent leakage and respect individual glycemic trajectories; (2) univariate-only forecasting is a pragmatic necessity given covariate inconsistency across public datasets but limits clinical realism; (3) fixed single-fraction few-shot definition (deterministic 5% stride) is reproducible but precludes analysis of sample-efficiency curves. Key Results At the primary 30-minute horizon with a 12-hour context window: zero-shot TimesFM2.5 achieves the best RMSE of 18.75 mg/dL (SEG no-risk 88.59%), surpassing the persistence baseline (22.25 mg/dL) by 16%; Chronos-2 follows at 19.16 mg/dL. Timer fails to improve over the persistence baseline (22.55 mg/dL), indicating substantial architecture-dependence in zero-shot transfer. In the few-shot regime, Chronos-2 leads overall at 18.95 mg/dL; the supervised LSTM is second at 19.24 mg/dL; TimeLLM is worst at 26.34 mg/dL. Under full-shot training, LSTM dominates at 17.97 mg/dL; the closest TSFM is Chronos-2 at 18.79 mg/dL (difference of 0.82 mg/dL, or 4–21% depending on dataset). Strikingly, TimesFM and Moirai degrade under full-shot fine-tuning (TimesFM: 18.75→19.47 mg/dL; Moirai: 19.48→20.30 mg/dL), consistent with pre-training representations being disrupted by full gradient updates. In the hypoglycemic range, pre-trained TSFMs outperform supervised DL across all protocols, with Chronos-2 achieving a 30-min hypoglycemic RMSE of 21.09 mg/dL (zero-shot) vs. LSTM's 28.87 mg/dL (few-shot). Probabilistic calibration degrades severely for TimesFM under fine-tuning: PI80 coverage drops from 79.9% (zero-shot) to 36.2% (full-shot), while Chronos-2 maintains ~76-77% throughout. All pairwise comparisons are statistically significant (q < 10⁻³) except LSTM vs. TimesFM few-shot (q=0.235) and Timer vs. GPFormer few-shot (q=0.663). T1D cohorts show persistently higher errors than T2D/PreD/ND cohorts across all models and protocols. Novelty Classification NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. GlucoFM-Bench is the first systematic cross-model evaluation of general-purpose TSFMs on glucose forecasting. The benchmark design, dataset curation, and standardized splits are the primary contributions; all component models are prior work. The empirical findings — that TSFMs match nearly full-shot performance in zero-shot while fine-tuning degrades their probabilistic calibration, and that TSFMs specifically dominate in hypoglycemia — are non-obvious and advance the community's understanding of TSFM transfer to physiological time series. Rigor & Reproducibility Code is publicly released at GitHub (https://github.com/Augmented-Health-Lab/GlucoseML_benchmark) and the benchmark dataset on HuggingFace (https://huggingface.co/datasets/glucofmbench/GlucoFM-Bench). Per-participant RMSE means and standard deviations are reported throughout. Pairwise Wilcoxon tests with BH-FDR correction are provided for all full-shot comparisons. However, the few-shot protocol is run at a single fixed fraction (5%) with a single deterministic stride; no sensitivity analysis over data fraction or sampling variability is reported, making sample-efficiency curves unavailable. Training wall-clock time and inference latency are not reported, preventing computational cost comparisons. This is a preprint; results have not undergone peer review. One methodological concern: the LSTM baseline (Martinsson et al., 2019) is a single fixed architecture with model-specific hyperparameters from the original paper — a more comprehensive supervised baseline sweep would strengthen the claim that LSTM is the full-shot upper bound. Deployability (EMPIRICAL) - Practical: All models run on commercially available GPUs (A100, RTX 4080); the LSTM (0.03M parameters) is trivially deployable on any hardware. - Efficient: Models span 0.03M (LSTM) to 1.2B (Chronos-2) parameters; inference cost and training time are not reported, making direct efficiency comparison impossible despite this being directly relevant to AID deployment. - Effective: In zero/few-shot settings TSFMs reduce RMSE 4–21% over persistence; in full-shot LSTM establishes the best overall accuracy at 17.97 mg/dL 30-minute RMSE, representing clinically meaningful but imperfect performance (SEG no-risk ~88%). - Scalable: The benchmark covers up to ~11M CGM measurements and 1,117 individuals; it does not test streaming inference, real-time adaptation, or personalization at deployment scale relevant to AID systems. The paper delivers reusable code, standardized dataset splits, and trained model checkpoints are available via original model repositories. Strengths - Scale and clinical diversity: 15 datasets, 1,117 individuals across four diabetes types, five countries, and ~2.6M test records substantially exceed any prior glucose forecasting benchmark. - Clinically grounded evaluation: CEG and SEG error grids complement aggregate RMSE with risk-stratified clinical relevance, which is necessary for healthcare deployment claims. - The stratified finding that pre-trained TSFMs outperform supervised DL specifically in hypoglycemia — the most life-threatening and data-sparse glycemic range — is actionable and counterintuitive, providing a concrete argument for TSFM use in safety-critical edge cases. - The probabilistic calibration collapse in TimesFM and Moirai after fine-tuning (PI80 drops from ~80% to 36–41% full-shot) is a strong practical warning that is not widely known and has direct implications for uncertainty-aware AID control. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly acknowledge three limitations: (1) univariate-only forecasting, with multivariate extension deferred because covariates are inconsistently available and only two TSFMs support multivariate inputs; (2) incomplete TSFM coverage since the field evolves rapidly; (3) the benchmark is retrospective and not a clinical decision system, with deployment requiring prospective validation and regulatory review. These are confronted honestly and the rationale for scope decisions is credible. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The few-shot protocol is fixed at a single data fraction (5%) with deterministic stride — no ablation over data fractions or sampling seeds makes it impossible to assess sample-efficiency curves or estimate how quickly supervised models catch up to TSFMs as data grows. - Inference latency and training time per model are entirely absent, which is critical information for real-time AID deployment where prediction must complete within seconds. - The LSTM "upper bound" relies on one specific architecture from 2019 with original hyperparameters; a grid-searched or larger LSTM, or other competitive supervised models (TCN, N-BEATS, PatchTST), might close the gap further or reveal the LSTM's dominance is architecture-specific. - The PI80 calibration collapse in TimesFM (36.2% full-shot) is reported but not analyzed mechanistically — given that uncertainty quantification is arguably more important than point accuracy in AID contexts, this finding deserves dedicated investigation rather than a one-sentence attribution to prior work. - Inter-individual variance in per-participant RMSE often exceeds the mean gap between models (e.g., T1D RMSE STDs of 4–10 mg/dL vs. model gaps of 1–2 mg/dL), yet the paper does not analyze whether model rankings are consistent across individuals or whether certain participant profiles favor TSFMs over LSTM — an important consideration for personalized deployment. Bottom Line A solid, reproducible benchmark that fills a genuine gap and delivers actionable findings — particularly the zero-shot competitiveness of Chronos-2 and TimesFM and TSFM superiority in hypoglycemia — backed by public code and datasets, but the absence of covariate modeling and computational cost reporting limits its direct applicability to real AID system design.
Search Results 2606.06881 PDF
#45 — Innovative (69)

Synthetic pre-training of graph-network models for predicting solid-state NMR parameters

CB Mahmoud, C Bornes, CJ Heard, L Grajciar… · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a synthetic pre-training and fine-tuning protocol for equivariant graph neural network models (TensorMACE) that predict full magnetic shielding tensors for solid-state NMR, achieving 3–10× reduction in required first-principles quantum-mechanical training data. - Why it's important: GIPAW-based first-principles NMR calculations for periodic solid-state systems are computationally expensive, creating a bottleneck that limits both direct spectral simulation and training data collection for ML models of complex materials. - How it does it: An existing approximate ML NMR model generates synthetic tensorial labels for 5000 structures at negligible cost; pre-training TensorMACE on these labels initializes weights in a physically meaningful region of parameter space before fine-tuning on a small set of true GIPAW calculations; a parity-consistent angular-momentum coupling construction (Clebsch-Gordan products of equivariant outputs) resolves a native MACE parity mismatch for the antisymmetric σ(1) tensor component. - The good: In-domain, TensorMACE-5000-250(L) matches the accuracy of a direct model trained on 800 GIPAW structures (3× QM data reduction), and in the extreme low-data regime, 10 fine-tuning structures with pre-training match the ση accuracy of 250 fine-tuning structures without it (25× reduction for that metric). - The missing: The paper evaluates cross-domain transfer only on Si–O–Al–H zeolites and never validates predicted tensors against a simulated or experimental powder NMR spectrum, leaving the ultimate practical utility undemonstrated. - Read it or not: Worth reading for ML practitioners working on equivariant property prediction and computational materials scientists needing cost-efficient solid-state NMR workflows; can be skipped by those outside atomistic ML or solid-state NMR.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (75) and Completeness (78) drive the score: systematic ablations independently varying pre-training size, label quality, capacity, and chemical domain give clean mechanistic insight, and all data, synthetic labels, weights, and scripts are publicly released — above-average reproducibility for the field. Novelty (60) is a solid new-method combination rather than a new formulation, and the unaddressed confound (synthetic labels drawn from the fine-tuning distribution) plus preprint status and absence of any spectral validation hold Resilience (68) and the composite below the highly-innovative band. Composite: 0.20*60 + 0.25*75 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*78 + 0.15*68 + 0.10*65 = 69.
empirical Novelty 60 Rigor 75 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 78 Resilience 68 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Solid-state NMR spectroscopy depends on accurate prediction of the magnetic shielding (MS) and electric field gradient (EFG) tensors, which govern powder line shapes and quadrupolar broadening and cannot be recovered from isotropic shifts alone. Computing these quantities via GIPAW DFT is expensive and restricted to well-behaved (non-metallic) configurations, so the supply of ground-truth training data for ML models is severely limited, particularly for chemically heterogeneous materials. Existing ML approaches for tensorial NMR either require large QM datasets or are restricted to specific chemical classes. The gap is a workflow that achieves high accuracy on MS tensors with a small GIPAW budget, without sacrificing the equivariance needed to correctly represent rank-2 tensorial outputs. Key Idea The core insight is that an existing approximate ML NMR model—even one trained on only ~200 structures and carrying ~1–2 ppm error—can generate structurally informative synthetic tensorial labels for thousands of configurations at negligible cost, and pre-training an equivariant GNN on these labels encodes the geometry–tensor correlation prior to any first-principles supervision. This biases model initialization into a physically meaningful region, so subsequent fine-tuning on a small GIPAW dataset converges to higher accuracy than training from random initialization. A secondary technical contribution is a general parity-consistent coupling formula (Eq. 2) that allows the MACE architecture to represent the antisymmetric σ(1) irrep, which the standard alternating-parity channel layout cannot directly express. Methodology TensorMACE is built on the MACE E(3)-equivariant GNN: atomic environments are encoded in radial and angular basis functions and refined through 3 message-passing layers with a 6.0 Å cutoff, 160-dimensional channels, and 4096 tensor products. The MS tensor σ is decomposed into irreducible spherical representations σ(0), σ(1), σ(2); σ(1) is predicted via parity-consistent Clebsch-Gordan coupling of same-parity equivariant outputs (Eq. 2). Two architectures are compared: large (max feature rank L=3) and small (max feature rank L=1). The training objective is MSE on spherical components plus a learnable per-element mean offset. AdamW (LR=0.003) with adaptive LR scheduling (patience 10) and early stopping (100 epochs) is used; models converge in ~200 epochs. Four random seeds are run per configuration and errors are averaged. Pre-training uses 5000 a-SiO2 structures labeled by a NequIP-based ML model; fine-tuning uses 10–250 GIPAW/PBE structures from a 450-structure a-SiO2 dataset or GIPAW/PBEsol structures from a 450-structure Si–O–Al–H zeolite dataset. The %RMSE metric (RMSE normalized by the standard deviation of QM values) is used for cross-quantity comparison. Critical design decisions: (1) per-element offset absorbing functional-dependent PBE vs PBEsol shifts—well-justified; (2) rank-3 vs rank-1 expressivity isolated by identical hyperparameters otherwise—well-designed ablation; (3) use of ML synthetic labels rather than DFT from a cheaper functional—motivated by computational cost but not compared against the DFT-level alternative. Key Results In-domain (a-SiO2): TensorMACE-5000-250(L) achieves RMSE of 0.6/1.58 ppm for Si/O σ(0) versus 1.1/2.8 ppm for direct TensorMACE-0-250(L), and 1.3/1.9 ppm for σ(2) versus 3.2/3.3 ppm. This matches the accuracy of a directly trained model on 800 structures (the full prior-work training set), a 3× QM data reduction. In the extreme low-data regime (10 fine-tuning structures), TensorMACE-5000-10(L) reaches ση accuracy comparable to the direct model on 250 structures (~25× QM reduction for that parameter). Lower-quality synthetic labels (NequIP trained on 50 structures, RMSE ~2.0/3.8 ppm for Si/O) still provide a 5× QM data reduction, showing robustness to label quality. Pre-training set size exhibits diminishing returns beyond 1000 synthetic structures. Small architecture (L=1): ση %RMSE for Si is 29.3% (fine-tuned large) vs 70.0% (fine-tuned small) vs 45.2%/110.2% for the corresponding direct models; synthetic pre-training narrows but does not close the capacity gap. Cross-domain (a-SiO2 → zeolite, 250 fine-tuning structures): RMSE for Si/O/Al/H σ(0) is 5.1/8.9/4.8/1.3 ppm (fine-tuned) vs 6.6/12.3/5.7/1.5 ppm (direct); improvement is largest for Si and O (seen during pre-training) and smallest for Al and H (unseen). H–O–H environments, chemically most distant from the pre-training domain, show only 10–25% %RMSE improvement. All baselines are the same architecture trained directly; no comparison to non-MACE equivariant models or DFT-level pre-training is included. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The specific contribution—synthetic pre-training of equivariant GNNs for solid-state tensorial NMR using ML-generated labels, combined with the parity-consistent coupling construction for σ(1) in MACE—is not present in prior literature. Pre-training and fine-tuning for MLIPs is established (MACE-MP, Ref. 33), and equivariant NMR models for molecular solids exist (Ref. 19), but the combination of ML synthetic supervision with solid-state tensorial NMR in an E(3)-equivariant framework is genuinely new. The TensorMACE architecture is an incremental extension of the authors' own prior work (Ref. 18). Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are systematic and informative, varying pre-training set size (10, 100, 1000, 5000), label quality, model capacity, and chemical domain independently. Four random seeds with error bars are reported; standard deviation bars are visible in learning curves and appear reasonable. No formal statistical significance tests. Code, data (train/test sets, synthetic labels), graph-pes input files, and trained model weights are publicly released at https://github.com/cbenmahm/synthetic-nmr and https://github.com/vldgroup/graph-pes—above-average reproducibility for the field. One methodological concern: the synthetic labels are generated by an ML model trained on data drawn from the same distribution as the fine-tuning set (a-SiO2 protocol of Ref. 18/41); this is internally consistent but means pre-training and fine-tuning data are correlated, potentially inflating apparent benefit versus a truly independent pre-training distribution. The authors do not fully disentangle structural coverage from correlated label noise as the driver of improvement. This is a preprint (arXiv:2606.11038v1, dated June 2026); results have not been peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check) EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: Yes; TensorMACE runs on standard GPU hardware via PyTorch through the publicly released graph-pes package, and fine-tuning datasets of 10–250 structures are well within reach for any group running GIPAW calculations. - Efficient: Synthetic pre-training is essentially free (ML inference, not DFT); actual GPU training times and memory costs are not reported, making direct comparison to GIPAW data generation cost infeasible from this paper. - Effective: The evidence shows consistent, substantial improvement in tensorial MS prediction accuracy across all components and both chemical systems in the low-data regime; gains are real and reproducible across seeds. - Scalable: Tested only up to 250 fine-tuning structures and two chemically related oxide systems; cross-domain benefit degrades for elements not seen in pre-training, and no experiment tests significantly different chemistry (e.g., phosphates, metal oxides) or larger structural diversity. The work delivers a software artifact (graph-pes library, trained model weights) and a reusable training protocol. Strengths - The ablation design—independently varying pre-training set size, label quality, model capacity, and chemical domain—provides unusually clean mechanistic insight into what synthetic pre-training actually encodes (geometry–tensor correlations, not element-specific electronic effects). - Tensor orientation spherical maps (Fig. 5) diagnose directional accuracy of the predicted rank-2 tensor beyond scalar RMSE, directly connecting to the physical NMR observable (orientation-dependent resonance frequency); this analysis reveals that eigenvalue accuracy is well-preserved while residual errors arise from principal-axis misalignment. - The parity-consistent coupling formula (Eq. 2) for σ(1) is a concrete, reusable architectural fix for a known limitation of MACE's alternating-parity channel layout, with public implementation in graph-pes. - Above-average reproducibility: all data, synthetic labels, model weights, and training scripts released. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state that cross-domain transferability is "inherently partial": benefit is strongest for chemical species seen during pre-training and for locally similar environments, while element-specific electronic effects require first-principles fine-tuning. They frame the work as "proof-of-concept" rather than a deployable broad model, and acknowledge that no comprehensive ML synthetic NMR label generator for inorganic systems (beyond silica and molecular crystals) currently exists, which is the key bottleneck for extending the approach. They also note that pre-training guides but does not substitute for architectural expressivity. These limitations are confronted directly and honestly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No NMR spectrum simulation: the paper reports tensor RMSE but never demonstrates that predicted tensors reproduce a powder lineshape or quadrupolar spectrum, either simulated or experimental. This is the practical endpoint that practitioners care about, and the tensor errors alone are insufficient to assess whether the improvement is spectroscopically meaningful. - No comparison to DFT-level pre-training (e.g., using a cheaper functional or a smaller basis set as the label source): the claim that ML synthetic supervision is a viable alternative is not tested against this natural baseline, making it unclear whether the benefit comes from structural coverage or from the quality/type of the supervisory signal. - Cross-domain evaluation is restricted to one chemically proximate system (Si–O–Al–H zeolites); no experiment tests genuinely distant chemistry (transition-metal oxides, phosphates, halides), so the boundary of transferability is unknown. - The fine-tuning learning curves (Figs. 2 and 6) are not shown beyond 250 structures; the claim that "asymptotic accuracy is unchanged" by pre-training is asserted but not demonstrated at the data-rich limit. - Wall-clock costs of pre-training and fine-tuning are not reported; without this, the practical time savings of the protocol (vs simply computing more GIPAW data) cannot be evaluated. Bottom Line A practitioner needing ML NMR models for solid-state Si/O systems with limited GIPAW budget gains a concrete, reproducible, and well-ablated workflow with demonstrated 3–10× QM data efficiency gains, but the narrow chemical scope, absence of spectral validation, and preprint status mean this is a methodological stepping stone rather than a production-ready solution.
Search Results 2606.11038 PDF
#46 — Innovative (69)

InterleaveThinker: Reinforcing Agentic Interleaved Generation (73 ▲ )

Dian Zheng, Harry Lee, Manyuan Zhang, Kaituo Feng, Zoey Guo, Ray Zhang, Hongsheng Li · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 12 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces InterleaveThinker, a multi-agent framework (Planner + Critic + frozen image generator) that endows any off-the-shelf image generator with the ability to produce coherent interleaved text-image sequences. - Why it's important: Existing high-quality image generators are architecturally restricted to single-image output, and Unified Multimodal Models that natively support sequential generation suffer from visual over-reliance and step-wise error accumulation that degrade long-horizon outputs. - How it does it: A Planner agent generates the entire step-by-step instruction sequence upfront (blocking intermediate visual feedback), while a separately trained Critic agent evaluates each generated image and refines prompts for regeneration; a dual-reward GRPO strategy trains the Critic via single-step RL to avoid optimizing prohibitively long trajectories end-to-end. - The good: On RISE (reasoning-based image editing), InterleaveThinker lifts FLUX.2-klein-9B from 13.3 to 28.9 — matching proprietary GPT-Image-1 — without any task-specific training, demonstrating unexpected transfer from sequential planning to single-image reasoning. - The missing: The step-wise reward (Eq. 5) is computed by Gemini 2.5 Pro, the same model used to generate training data, creating a circular evaluation loop that could inflate Critic quality and limit reproducibility for third parties without proprietary API access. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners building sequential visual generation pipelines or studying agentic RL for multimodal tasks; those primarily interested in single-image generation quality can skip it.
Why this rank: Systems profile. Practical Impact (75) is anchored by released code and model weights on GitHub/HuggingFace plus a full Planner-Gen-Critic pipeline. Non-Obviousness (75) is high: decoupling global planning from local visual critique to break UMM visual over-reliance is a genuine and well-supported design insight, and the unexpected transfer to single-image reasoning (RISE 13.3→28.9, matching GPT-Image-1) is a surprising empirical finding. Novelty (72) reflects a new system plus new datasets, though the components (GRPO, VLM-as-judge, iterative refinement) are adapted. Rigor (62) is reduced by the Gemini-2.5-Pro circularity (it generates training data, filters, and computes the RL reward), absence of error bars, missing inference-cost analysis, and preprint status. Resilience (60) and Completeness (65) are pulled down by narrow two-generator evaluation and no long-sequence stress test. 0.15*72+0.15*62+0.10*75+0.15*65+0.15*60+0.30*75 = 69.
systems Novelty 72 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 65 Resilience 60 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Real-world visual applications — step-by-step tutorials, embodied robot manipulation, visual storytelling — require coherent multi-step image sequences where each image builds on the prior context. State-of-the-art image generators (FLUX, Stable Diffusion, Qwen-Image) are architecturally restricted to single-image output. Unified Multimodal Models (UMMs) like Janus-Pro, Emu3.5, and BAGEL natively support interleaved generation but exhibit two failure modes in long-horizon tasks: visual over-reliance (a UMM conditioned heavily on the preceding image halts at a visually plausible but semantically incomplete intermediate state) and step-wise error accumulation (early-step quality degradation compounds through the sequence). No prior open-source system matches proprietary interleaved generation models like Nano Banana Pro. Key Idea The core insight is that planning and visual evaluation must be separated into distinct agents to prevent myopic conditioning on intermediate visual states. A Planner agent pre-commits to a global N-step instruction sequence before any images are generated, completely eliminating the intermediate visual feedback that causes over-reliance. A Critic agent then operates locally within each step — comparing the generated image against the pre-committed instruction — without updating or influencing the global plan, enabling targeted prompt refinement without trajectory-level credit assignment. Methodology The pipeline has three modules: (1) Planner receives the user's text-image input sequence S and outputs {(u_i, p_i, a_i)}_{i=1}^N — per-step instruction, T2I prompt, and auxiliary text — via a single forward pass. (2) Generator (frozen, e.g., FLUX.2-klein-9B) produces image I_i^t from refined prompt r_i^t and previous image I_{i-1}. (3) Critic evaluates (I_{i-1}, I_i^t, p_i, r_i^t) and outputs binary judgment j_i^t, refined prompt r_i^{t+1}, and reasoning, iterating up to T_max=5 times per step. Training proceeds in two stages. First, SFT cold-start: Planner and Critic both initialized from Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct, trained on Interleave-Planner-SFT-80k and Interleave-Critic-SFT-112k respectively (2 epochs, lr=2e-5, batch=32). The Planner receives no RL due to sparse reward signals over 25+ generator calls. Second, Critic RL via GRPO: trained on Interleave-Critic-RL-13k (1 epoch, lr=2e-6, batch=16, rollout N=8, KL coeff=1e-3) with a dual-reward: Accuracy Reward R_acc penalizes deviation of the Critic's binary judgment from ground-truth label; Step-wise Reward R_step is the Gemini 2.5 Pro score difference between I_i^{t+1} and I_i^t. Final reward is R = 0.5R_format + 0.5(0.2R_acc + 0.8R_step). Three critical design decisions: (a) upfront global planning to break the feedback loop — well-justified by the ablation showing single-agent integration degrades avg from 66.3 to 54.5; (b) single-step RL rather than trajectory-level RL — justified by computational intractability (25+ generator calls per trajectory) but relies on the assumption that independent per-step success guarantees global success; (c) SFT-RL data split by score variance (high variance → RL, low variance → SFT) — a principled heuristic without formal justification. Dataset construction uses Gemini 2.5 Pro and Nano Banana Pro to generate ~40k text prompts across 8 categories and 75 sub-categories, then synthesizes agentic trajectories. Filtering discards steps with non-monotone score progressions. The Planner dataset (80k) augments synthesized trajectories with existing open-source interleaved data; the Critic datasets (112k SFT, 13k RL) are fully synthesized. Training takes 50 hours on 8× H800 GPUs. Key Results On UEval (8-task interleaved generation benchmark, preprint): InterleaveThinker+FLUX.2-klein-9B achieves 66.3 avg vs. best open-source UMM Emu3.5 at 49.1 (+17.2); comparable to proprietary Nano Banana (66.0) and GPT-5-Thinking (66.4), below Nano Banana Pro (76.1). +Qwen-Image-Edit reaches 67.2. Task scores are heterogeneous: Textbook (92.0 vs. 57.4 for Emu3.5) and Diagram (82.1 vs. 41.1) show large gains; Tech (36.6) and Exercise (43.8) remain relatively weak. On CoMM Tasks 3/4 (interleaved and pure-text inputs): InterleaveThinker+FLUX.2-klein-9B scores 9.3/9.6 (style), 9.2/9.6 (entity), 9.1/9.5 (trend), 9.1/9.6 (completeness), 9.7/9.8 (image quality), and 5.2/8.2 (IRS) vs. prior best open-source DuoGen (pure-text only) at –/9.2 across first four dimensions. Notably, IRS (text-image alignment) remains substantially lower (5.2) on interleaved input vs. pure-text (8.2), indicating interleaved context degrades text adherence. On WISE (world-knowledge T2I reasoning): FLUX.2-klein-9B baseline 0.47 → 0.73 (+0.26); Qwen-Image-Edit-2511 0.60 → 0.72 (+0.12). Best open-source baseline BAGEL scores 0.72; proprietary Nano Banana Pro 0.87. On RISE-Bench (reasoning image editing): FLUX.2-klein-9B 13.3 → 28.9 (+15.6), matching GPT-Image-1 (28.9); Qwen-Image-Edit 19.4 → 30.0. Nano Banana Pro leads at 47.2. Ablations on UEval confirm: dual reward is additive (+1.1 over no step reward); multi-agent vs. one-agent (+11.8 avg); T_max=5 vs. T_max=1 (+6.1); filtered vs. unfiltered data (+3.5). Oracle baselines (Gemini-2.5-Pro or GPT-4.1 as planner+critic) reach 77.4 and 67.5, showing the current gap is primarily from Planner/Critic model capacity, not the architecture. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM + NEW DATASET. The decoupled Planner-Gen-Critic architecture for interleaved generation is genuinely novel as a system design; the datasets (Interleave-Planner-SFT-80k, Critic-SFT-112k, Critic-RL-13k) are new artifacts. The dual-reward single-step RL approach to approximate trajectory-level optimization is an engineering contribution, though the individual components (GRPO, VLM-as-judge rewards, iterative refinement) are all adapted from prior work. The primary novelty is the system design insight that decoupling planning from visual evaluation resolves UMM's fundamental sequential failure modes. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and informative, isolating contributions of the multi-agent structure, dual reward signals, data filtering, and Tmax. No error bars or significance tests are reported across any benchmark table. Code and model weights are publicly released at github.com/zhengdian1/InterleaveThinker and huggingface.co/InterleaveThinker, which is a strong positive for reproducibility. However, a critical circularity exists: Gemini 2.5 Pro is used to generate training trajectories, score filtering criteria, and the step-wise RL reward (Eq. 5) — third parties without Gemini API access cannot replicate the RL training. Benchmark scores on UEval, CoMM, and RISE rely heavily on LLM-as-judge scoring (CoMM uses GPT-family judges for 0-10 scales), introducing variability that is not quantified. The paper is a preprint; results have not been peer-reviewed. The RISE metric methodology is itself an arXiv preprint, and "Nano Banana" is not formally identified (only cited as "Google. Nano banana. 2025."), making proprietary comparisons unverifiable. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS — the paper builds and evaluates a full pipeline. - Practical: Inference requires running two 8B VLMs (Planner, Critic) plus a frozen image generator; with T_max=5 and 25+ steps per trajectory, one interleaved generation requires up to 125 Critic calls plus 125+ generator forward passes, which demands multiple consumer or datacenter GPUs and significant wall-clock time that is not measured in the paper. - Efficient: The paper claims that single-step RL "drastically reduces computational costs" compared to full-trajectory RL, which is accurate relative to end-to-end RL; however, inference latency per trajectory is not reported, and the per-image cost is substantially higher than a single UMM forward pass. - Effective: On UEval and CoMM the pipeline demonstrably solves the interleaved generation task, matching proprietary models on those benchmarks; the IRS gap on interleaved inputs (5.2 vs. 8.2 for pure-text in Table 2) suggests text-image alignment is weaker in the harder input regime. - Scalable: The framework is tested with two image generators (FLUX.2-klein-9B and Qwen-Image-Edit-2511) at 1024×1024 resolution; no experiments explore longer sequences (>25 steps) or higher-resolution generators, so the scaling claim to "any generator" is stated but only partially validated. The work delivers a software artifact (code + trained model weights on HuggingFace). Strengths - The diagnosis of visual over-reliance and step-wise error accumulation as distinct, separable failure modes in UMMs is precise and well-supported by the one-agent ablation, which quantifies the cost of entangled planning and evaluation at -11.8 avg points. - The dual-reward formulation that proxies trajectory-level RL through single-step optimization is practically important: it reduces training compute from intractable (25+ generator calls per RL rollout) to tractable, and the ablation confirms both reward components contribute independently. - The dataset construction pipeline — using score-variance to split SFT vs. RL data and balancing binary judgment distribution to prevent Critic collapse — reflects genuine engineering care; the ablation on unfiltered data (-3.5 avg) validates that filtering matters. - Unexplained generalization to reasoning benchmarks (WISE, RISE) without any reasoning-specific training is a notable empirical finding that suggests the planning mechanism improves structured reasoning broadly, not only sequential generation. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors state one limitation directly: the framework's generative capacity is bounded by the base generator's training distribution, so concepts absent from the frozen generator's corpus cannot be synthesized or corrected by the Critic. Figure 8 illustrates this with a color-shift artifact on an out-of-distribution clothing-folding task. The authors do not acknowledge the Gemini circularity in the reward pipeline, inference latency costs, the IRS gap on interleaved inputs, or the narrow evaluation across only two generator backbones. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Circular reward signal: Gemini 2.5 Pro generates training data, scores quality for filtering, and computes the step-wise RL reward. If Gemini has systematic biases or blind spots, the Critic is trained to satisfy those biases rather than true visual quality. No human evaluation is provided to validate alignment between Gemini scores and human preference. - Missing inference cost analysis: A single interleaved task can invoke up to 25×T_max = 125 model calls. The paper provides no latency, throughput, or cost-per-output numbers, making it impossible to assess whether the system is practical relative to a single UMM forward pass. - Two-generator evaluation is narrow: The "universal applicability" claim is supported only by FLUX.2-klein-9B and Qwen-Image-Edit-2511. Generators with different editing mechanisms (e.g., inpainting-based, video generators) are not tested. - No long-sequence stress test: All reported examples have 4–5 steps. The failure modes (error accumulation, visual over-reliance) the paper targets are most severe at longer horizons; performance at 15+ or 25+ steps is not evaluated. - IRS gap is unexplained: Text-image alignment on interleaved inputs (5.2 in Table 2) is dramatically lower than on pure-text inputs (8.2). The paper does not analyze or acknowledge this systematic gap. - Proprietary model identities obscured: "Nano Banana" and "Nano Banana Pro" appear to be Google Gemini image generation variants but are not formally identified, making those comparison points unverifiable. Bottom Line Practitioners building multi-step visual generation pipelines will find the Planner-Gen-Critic decomposition and dual-reward RL scheme genuinely useful, but the heavy reliance on Gemini 2.5 Pro as training oracle, reward signal, and implicit evaluation judge substantially limits reproducibility and makes the published benchmark numbers difficult to independently verify.
Recommended 2606.13679 PDF
#47 — Innovative (69)

HYDRA-X: Native Unified Multimodal Models with Holistic Visual Tokenizers (24 ▲ )

Guozhen Zhang, Xuerui Qiu, Yutao Cui, Tianhui Song, Changlin Li, Junzhe Li, Tao Huang, Xiao Zhang, Yang Li, Jianbing Wu, Miles Yang, Zhao Zhong, Liefeng Bo, Limin Wang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 12 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces HYDRA-X, a unified multimodal model built on HYDRA-XTOK, a single Vision Transformer that jointly tokenizes images and videos into one shared representation space for five tasks: image/video understanding, image/video generation, and instruction-guided image editing. - Why it's important: Prior unified multimodal models either use frame-wise tokenizers that discard temporal dynamics or cascaded VAE+encoder designs where the VAE is trained without semantic constraints, creating representational gaps that limit video understanding and generation quality. - How it does it: The key mechanism is a three-part tokenizer design — tubelet causal attention (attending only to the immediately preceding frame), hierarchical 2×2 temporal patchify (instead of a single 4× step), and a lightweight Decompressor that upsamples temporally compressed features back to native frame rate so both image and video teacher distillation can be applied at full resolution. - The good: On image editing, HYDRA-X scores 4.34 overall on ImgEdit-Bench, outperforming BAGEL-14B (3.20) at 7B parameters, with the ablation showing a 7 dB PSNR gain in source reconstruction from the tokenizer-stage source-target interaction alone — a zero-parameter-cost design change that isolates the locus of the editing failure to the tokenizer, not the LLM. - The missing: The video generation evaluation is limited to 17-frame, 640×384 clips; long video generation and video editing are explicitly not addressed, leaving open the core question of whether the holistic ViT scales to realistic video lengths. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on unified multimodal architectures or visual tokenizer design — the ablation study on attention scope and temporal patchify schedule provides actionable, counter-intuitive findings that challenge prevailing design choices.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (68) and Non-Obviousness (78) drive the score: the controlled ablations are the paper's strongest asset, and the finding that full spatiotemporal attention actively degrades reconstruction (reversing prevailing video-VAE assumptions) plus the zero-parameter source-target interaction yielding a 7 dB PSNR gain are sharp, mechanistically-grounded results. Novelty (72) reflects three genuine tokenizer-design contributions on top of an otherwise derivative UMM architecture. Completeness (68) is solid with directly-confronted limitations, but Resilience (65) is reduced by the unquantified ImgEdit train/test overlap (Goodhart risk on headline editing numbers), the absence of error bars, and unreleased code/weights. Practical Impact (55) is held back because no artifact is released and video generation is limited to 17 frames with weak Dynamic Degree. 0.20*72+0.25*68+0.15*78+0.15*68+0.15*65+0.10*55 = 69.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 68 Non-Obv 78 Completeness 68 Resilience 65 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Building a single model that handles visual understanding and generation jointly requires a visual tokenizer that simultaneously preserves pixel-level fidelity (for generation), semantic richness (for understanding), and temporal structure (for video). Existing approaches fall into two failure modes: frame-wise tokenizers (e.g., TransNext) process each video frame independently and leave the LLM with disjoint per-frame features that carry no cross-frame dynamics; cascaded designs (e.g., Show-o2, TUNA) prepend a 3D causal VAE before a semantic encoder, but the VAE is trained without semantic constraints and may discard understanding-relevant information. A third gap specific to image editing is that prior unified models encode source and target images independently, forcing the LLM to reconstruct cross-image alignment from already-compressed semantic streams — a structurally lossy pipeline for identity-faithful edits. Key Idea: The core insight is that full spatiotemporal attention and aggressive single-step temporal compression — the two standard choices in ViT-based video tokenizers — are both actively harmful. Replacing them with (1) causal attention restricted to a 2-frame tubelet window and (2) two sequential 2× temporal patchify stages preserves image pretraining priors and improves reconstruction fidelity. A second insight is that the temporal compression ratio mismatch between the tokenizer latent (1+T/4 tokens) and available video semantic teachers (operating at full frame rate) can be bridged by a lightweight Decompressor — a small ViT that upsamples the compressed latent back to native temporal length at training time only — enabling direct distillation from pretrained video encoders. The third insight repurposes the video tubelet causal path for image editing: treating a source-target pair as a length-2 clip allows the Sem-ViT to inject structural source information into the target representation before the LLM ever processes either. Methodology: HYDRA-XTOK is initialized from SigLIP 2 and trained in three stages. In Stage 1 (foundation), the tokenizer is trained on ImageNet at 256×256, then at mixed resolutions up to 2048px, for 300k AdamW iterations (lr=2×10⁻⁴). The reconstruction loss combines L1, LPIPS perceptual loss, adversarial GAN loss, and a KL divergence term regularizing the latent posterior. Semantic distillation combines an image-teacher term (SigLIP-SO400M cosine distance at the Sem-ViT output) and a video-teacher term (InternVideo-Next-L cosine distance at the Decompressor output). Stage 2 freezes the encoder and refines the decoder with adversarial training. Stage 3 freezes Gen-ViT and the decoder, normalizes the latent channel-wise, and updates only Sem-ViT to harmonize representations. The UMM is trained in three progressive stages totaling 270K steps on 512 GPUs: first aligning visual-text representations with a frozen LLM (50K steps, 256 GPUs), then full-parameter joint training on 30M understanding + 30M generation + 2M editing + 10M video samples (200K steps), then high-quality SFT (20K steps). The UMM loss is a uniform sum of next-token prediction (NTP) and rectified flow matching (FM) objectives. The bottleneck dimension is C=64; the Decompressor is a 4× temporal upsampler using two (conv → reshape → transformer) stages. Three critical design decisions: (1) Tubelet-only attention — well-justified by controlled ablation showing full attention degrades ImageNet PSNR from 31.73 to 31.10 and DAVIS rFVD from 11.19 to 16.20; (2) Hierarchical patchify — similarly ablated, with 2×2 schedule outperforming single 4× on all metrics; (3) Decompressor with video-teacher distillation — ablated across four configurations, showing removal of the Decompressor collapses MVBench from 45.4 to 42.1 and video-only distillation at the Decompressor output outperforms image-only distillation there. Key Results: Image understanding (7B scale, unified models): HYDRA-X scores AI2D 86.5, MME 2350.0, OCRBench 84.5, ChartQA 86.5, matching or exceeding Show-o2-7B (78.6, 1620.5, 32.4, 52.3) and the predecessor HYDRA-7B (85.1, 2068.6, 57.7) on nearly every metric. Video understanding: MVBench 59.1, Video-MME 60.0, LongVideoBench 59.5, LVBench 30.0 — improving over Show-o2-7B (55.8, 57.4, 55.5) across all reported metrics; remains below GPT-4o (71.9 Video-MME) and Gemini-1.5-Pro (75.0). Image generation: GenEval Overall 0.88, WISE Overall 0.56, competitive with BAGEL-14B (0.88, 0.52) at half the parameters. Video generation (VBench, 17 frames, 640×384): Total 83.49, QS 83.97, SS 81.57 — leading all unified models, +2.15 Total over Show-o2-1.5B.
