Image editing: ImgEdit-Bench Overall 4.34 (vs. BAGEL-14B 3.20, OmniGen2-4B 3.44), GEdit G-Overall 7.17; trails Qwen-Image-20B by 0.39 on GEdit G-Overall. Reconstruction: DAVIS rFVD 11.61 vs. AToken's 29.19; UCF rFVD 3.11 vs. AToken's 7.77. The paper is an arXiv preprint; no peer review has occurred. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The genuinely novel contributions are: (1) the empirical finding that tubelet-2 causal attention outperforms full spatiotemporal attention for ViT-based video reconstruction, reversing conventional assumption; (2) the Decompressor mechanism for bridging the temporal resolution mismatch in distillation; (3) the tokenizer-stage source-target interaction that repurposes the video temporal path for image editing at zero parameter cost. The overall UMM architecture (autoregressive LLM + flow matching head + NTP + FM loss) is a direct extension of prior work (HYDRA, Show-o2, TUNA) rather than new. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are the paper's strongest rigor signal. Table 1 isolates attention mask vs. patchify schedule on a single controlled setup; Table 2 ablates distillation configurations with end-task scores across five benchmarks; Table 3 isolates the STI design with matched parameter count and training. Error bars and significance tests are absent throughout. Seeds and training variance are not reported. The 7 dB Recon-PSNR gain from STI is a direct per-sample measurement on ImgEdit rather than a downstream proxy, which is credible. No code, weights, or data are released. Benchmarks used (MVBench, VideoMME, GenEval, VBench, ImgEdit-Bench, GEdit-Bench) are public and standard. A potential concern: the editing evaluation uses ImgEdit-Bench and GEdit-Bench, on which the model was fine-tuned using ImgEdit data (Stage 2/3 includes image editing data); the extent of overlap between training and benchmark images is not stated. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (systems). - Practical: The 7B model is trained on 512 A100-class GPUs and inferences over images and short video clips; deployment on commercially available multi-GPU nodes is feasible, though 512-GPU training is not accessible outside industry. - Efficient: Tubelet attention reduces per-forward-pass latency on a 17×512×512 clip from 0.49s (full attention) to 0.17s (tubelet) and 0.25s for the hierarchical patchify variant; the Decompressor is discarded at inference, adding no inference cost. At 16× spatial and 4× temporal compression, the token budget is comparable to AToken-So/C. - Effective: The model demonstrates consistent gains over prior unified baselines on all five tasks; the editing improvement is specifically validated by the Recon-PSNR diagnostic and qualitative examples showing identity-faithful reconstruction under independent encoding fails. - Scalable: Video generation is evaluated only at 17 frames × 640×384; the paper explicitly states that long video generation was not explored due to resource constraints, so scalability to realistic video lengths is undemonstrated. The work delivers a trained model and tokenizer but does not release weights or code; the artifact exists in paper form only. Strengths: - The ablation that full spatiotemporal attention degrades reconstruction is a sharp empirical finding that directly contradicts the prevailing assumption in video VAE design; the mechanism (disruption of locality priors from image pretraining) is plausible and the result is cleanly supported by Table 1. - The Decompressor is an unusually simple solution to a genuine structural mismatch (teacher operates at native frame rate, latent is temporally compressed); the key insight — train it, discard it at inference — avoids runtime overhead while enabling full-rate semantic supervision. - Tokenizer-stage source-target interaction achieves a 7 dB PSNR improvement and 0.4 ImgEdit score gain at strictly zero parameter cost, with qualitative evidence (Figure 5) making the failure mode of independent encoding viscerally clear. - Reconstruction results (Table 9, Appendix B) show HYDRA-XTOK trained only on ImageNet-1.2M still exceeds FLUX.1 on ImageNet PSNR (32.96 vs. 32.86) and rFID (0.154 vs. 0.176) at twice the compression ratio, providing a strong architectural baseline claim that isolates design from data-scale effects. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly state three limitations: (1) training data scale and model parameter count remain bottlenecks for high-dimensional video distributions; (2) long video generation and video editing were not explored due to resource constraints; (3) only a 7B dense LLM was used, and pairing with MoE or Mixture-of-Transformers backbones is identified as a clear extension. These are confronted directly in Appendix E rather than minimized. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - Video generation is evaluated only at 17 frames × 640×384, which is well below the clips produced by dedicated video generation systems (Hunyuan Video: 13B, longer clips); the VBench Dynamic Degree score of 35.42 (vs. Show-o2's 40.83 and CogVideoX's 70.97) reveals that HYDRA-X produces significantly less motion, a critical quality dimension not prominently discussed. - The editing training data (ImgEdit dataset) is drawn from the same source as one of the two editing benchmarks (ImgEdit-Bench), and the degree of train/test overlap is not analyzed; this creates a Goodhart's Law risk on the headline editing numbers. - All baselines in the video understanding comparison that include the full set of benchmarks are either smaller (1.5B) or older; the strongest current video-understanding UMM comparison is thin, and the gap to proprietary video models (GPT-4o, Gemini-1.5-Pro) is not closed. - The source-target interaction experiment (Table 3) does not ablate whether the benefit is specifically due to causal direction (target sees source but not vice versa) or simply to joint encoding; a symmetric attention variant would clarify the mechanism. - Code and weights are not released, limiting independent reproducibility. Bottom Line: A practitioner working on unified multimodal architectures gains real value from this paper — specifically the counter-intuitive ablation results on attention scope, the Decompressor trick for resolving teacher-resolution mismatch, and the tokenizer-stage STI insight for editing — but the missing code/weights release, unaddressed train-test overlap on editing benchmarks, and very limited video generation scale (17 frames, low Dynamic Degree) mean the system-level claims should be treated as promising preprint findings rather than validated production results.
Recommended 2606.13289 PDF
#48 — Innovative (69)

Ekka: Automated Diagnosis of Silent Errors in LLM Inference

Y Gu, Z Zhang, S Zhu, X Fu, J Wu, Y Wang, B Kasikci · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces EKKA, an automated system that diagnoses silent errors in LLM serving frameworks (vLLM, SGLang) by using LLM agents to align intermediate activation tensors across a buggy framework and a reference implementation, then applying change-point analysis on a precision-normalized error ratio to identify the root-cause module. - Why it's important: Silent errors in LLM serving stacks cause output quality degradation without any runtime error signals, forcing developers to spend months on manual differential debugging—a problem that worsens as serving frameworks incorporate increasingly complex optimizations like paged attention and custom CUDA kernels. - How it does it: EKKA builds compressed Model Trees of both frameworks, uses an LLM agent to iteratively map semantically equivalent modules despite structural disparities (e.g., fused QKV vs. separate projections), generates executable Python alignment code to normalize tensor shapes across frameworks, and finds the earliest module where the normalized error ratio permanently elevates. - The good: 80% pass@1 and 88% pass@5 on a 17-bug real-world benchmark, outperforming Mini-SWE-Agent and OpenCode by 24–34%, with 4 newly diagnosed bugs in vLLM and SGLang confirmed by developers. - The missing: The benchmark covers only 17 model-stack bugs selected for reproducibility; 36 additional candidate bugs were excluded due to hardware constraints or large model sizes, leaving the system's accuracy on the harder, more common failure modes untested. - Read it or not: Essential reading for ML systems researchers and LLM serving framework developers; the cross-framework differential debugging pipeline and precision-normalized error metric are directly applicable to production debugging workflows.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Practical Impact (70) and Non-Obviousness (75) lead: the precision-normalized error ratio is a genuinely non-obvious, empirically-separated bug signal (non-buggy p99 ≤ 1.41 vs. buggy ≥ 3.13), and framing activation alignment as agent-generated code handles fused/unfused heterogeneity that defeats rule-based matchers. Rigor (68) benefits from ICML 2026 peer review and unusually careful stage-isolating ablations, but the 17-bug benchmark is small with no CIs, no public repo URL, and a mild Goodhart risk since 12 bugs and the 1.5 threshold come from the same motivating study. Novelty (72) reflects a strong domain-specific integration rather than a new primitive. Completeness (65) and Resilience (65) are pulled by the exclusion of 36 of 53 candidate bugs (hardware/large-model) and untested coverage of MoE/VLM families, though 4 developer-confirmed new diagnoses are credible out-of-distribution validation. Weighted: 0.15*72 + 0.15*68 + 0.10*75 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*65 + 0.30*70 = 69.
systems Novelty 72 Rigor 68 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 65 Resilience 65 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation LLM serving frameworks like vLLM and SGLang incorporate aggressive optimizations—paged attention, radix attention, tensor parallelism, custom CUDA kernels—that make them susceptible to silent errors: bugs that produce plausible but incorrect outputs without triggering any runtime warnings. One real case: a sliding window attention misuse in vLLM caused Gemma 3's Hellaswag accuracy to drop ~30% while the framework appeared fully operational, leading developers to spend months misdiagnosing it. A study of 90 GitHub issues across vLLM and SGLang finds that accuracy regression accounts for 43.8% of symptoms, with root causes spanning framework logic (30.6%), model implementation (25.5%), kernel backends (24.5%), and numerical precision (19.4%). Existing fault localization methods require explicit pass/fail signals absent here; DL testing tools are black-box or limited to API-level comparisons; general-purpose SWE agents lack the domain-specific scaffolding to navigate optimized serving engine internals. The core difficulty is a semantic gap: a high-level benchmark regression traces to a tensor computation error somewhere in a 48-layer transformer stack with heterogeneous internal layouts across frameworks. Key Idea The key insight is that virtually every production LLM serving framework has a semantically correct (slower) reference implementation—HuggingFace Transformers—and the diagnosis problem can be recast as: automatically align the intermediate activation tensors of the buggy framework against those of the reference at module granularity, compute a precision-normalized error ratio that separates implementation bugs from floating-point accumulation noise, and find the earliest module where the ratio permanently elevates. The non-trivial part is the alignment step: optimized frameworks fuse, reshape, and reorder tensors in ways that make naive tensor comparison impossible, so EKKA delegates alignment to an LLM agent that reads the forward method source code and generates the necessary transformation. Methodology EKKA runs in two stages. Stage 1 (Diagnosis Information Collection) statically parses both codebases into a Code Index (class definitions, inheritance chains) and transforms the PyTorch module hierarchy into a compressed Model Tree that abstracts repetitive layers using index variables (e.g., layers[0..47]). PyTorch forward hooks then capture per-module output tensors and call sequences during a single forward pass on both frameworks. Stage 2 (Agent-based Bug Diagnosis) has three LLM-agent-driven steps: 1. Component Mapping: An agent iteratively produces one-to-one or one-to-many module correspondences between the two Model Trees. A `get_class_definition` tool resolves ambiguities where names match but semantics differ (e.g., HuggingFace RotaryEmbedding computes cos/sin embeddings; vLLM RotaryEmbedding applies them). A Mapping Validator checks each mapping against the Model Tree and forces the agent to either map or explicitly reason about every component. Ablation shows validation contributes 21.7% mapping accuracy gain; the codebase tool adds 7.6%. 2. Activation Alignment: For each mapped component pair, the agent generates executable Python postprocessing code that transforms reference activations to match the target's shape and layout (e.g., squeezing a batch dimension and concatenating separate Q/K/V tensors into vLLM's fused QKV format). A Code Validator enforces shape compatibility. Helper tools (`get_tensor_sum`, `infer_tensor_match`) assist matching. A knowledge base of verified alignment code examples from known-good model/framework pairs provides in-context examples; ablation shows tools and knowledge base account for the largest single accuracy gain (pass@1 drops from 0.84 to 0.39 when removed). 3. Error Analysis: The precision-normalized error ratio R = ||X_T − Y_R||₂ / (||X_R − Y_R||₂ + ε) is computed per module, where X_T is the target output in low precision (BF16/FP8), X_R is the reference in low precision, and Y_R is the reference in full precision (FP32). The denominator normalizes by the reference framework's own precision-induced error, making the ratio close to 1 for numerically correct components regardless of dtype. An LLM agent then applies a sustained elevation heuristic: it seeks the earliest module where the error ratio permanently shifts above the threshold (empirically set to 1.5), distinguishing real bugs from transient alignment noise. The threshold of 1.5 is supported by data: non-buggy component pairs across BF16/FP8 settings show p99 ≤ 1.41; ground-truth buggy components show ratios from 3.13 to 1093.75. Key Results On a 17-bug benchmark spanning vLLM and SGLang with models including Llama, Gemma, Qwen, DeepSeek, ERNIE, Mamba, and Whisper: EKKA achieves 80% pass@1 and 88% pass@5. Baselines under identical conditions (Claude Sonnet 4.5, same codebase and reproduction scripts): Mini-SWE-Agent 60%/66%, OpenCode 48%/66%. EKKA improves pass@1 by 28–34% and pass@5 by 24–30%. Average API cost: ~$29.60 per case (4.03M input tokens at $12 + 304K output tokens at $17.60), ranging up to $30 in the worst case without prompt caching. Under Claude Haiku 4.5 (weaker backbone), EKKA degrades to 67%/77% while baselines fall to 31%/66% and 27%/55%, preserving a substantial gap. Threshold sensitivity tests show pass@5 is stable at 0.88 across thresholds 1.5–2.5; pass@1 varies by at most 0.04. Four new bugs in vLLM and SGLang diagnosed by EKKA and confirmed by developers demonstrate generalization beyond the benchmark. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. The individual primitives—change-point detection, LLM agents for code generation, differential testing across framework implementations—are established. What is genuinely novel is their integration into a domain-specific pipeline that explicitly solves the structural heterogeneity problem (fused vs. unfused ops, different tensor layouts, different module naming conventions) that makes naive differential comparison infeasible for production LLM serving stacks, and the precision-normalized error ratio that provides a stable, dtype-agnostic bug signal without requiring a priori error bounds. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are well-designed: Component Mapping and Activation Alignment are ablated independently by holding the other fixed at ground truth, measuring both intermediate metrics (mapping coverage, alignment accuracy) and end-to-end pass@k. Five trials per case are run, appropriate for stochastic LLM agents. No confidence intervals or significance tests are reported; with 17 bugs and binary pass/fail, the benchmark is small enough that a few cases dominate the headline accuracy numbers. No public repository URL is provided in the paper, though Docker containers for bug reproduction are described. The paper appears in ICML 2026 proceedings (PMLR 306), making it peer-reviewed. One concern: 12 of 17 benchmark bugs were drawn from the same 70-bug study that motivated the design and threshold selection—mild Goodhart's Law risk that the threshold of 1.5 and knowledge base construction are tuned to this population. The 5 post-study bugs and 4 newly diagnosed bugs partially mitigate this. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS — EKKA is a built agentic pipeline evaluated by deployment on real bugs from production frameworks. - Practical: Runs on standard CUDA hardware; requires running both the buggy target and the HuggingFace reference simultaneously for a single forward pass, which is feasible for models tested (up to ~8B parameters on available GPUs) but excluded 9 benchmark cases requiring large models or Blackwell/ROCm-specific hardware. - Efficient: Average diagnosis consumes 4.03M input tokens + 304K output tokens at ~$30/case; diagnosis latency (wall-clock time per case across multi-stage agentic calls) is not reported, which is a relevant omission for CI/CD integration. - Effective: Achieves 80% pass@1 on a curated benchmark of 17 reproducible model-stack bugs, with developer-confirmed diagnoses of 4 new real bugs; the 20% failure rate and explicit exclusion of orchestration, concurrency, and hardware bugs limit the coverage claim. - Scalable: Not tested at scale; large-model bugs (9 cases requiring hardware unavailable to the authors) and distributed multi-node bugs are excluded; the paper does not evaluate how diagnosis cost or accuracy changes with model size. EKKA delivers a software tool (LangGraph-based agentic pipeline with a Docker benchmark), not a trained model or released library. Strengths - Framing activation alignment as code generation is practically powerful: the LLM agent reads forward method source code and infers necessary tensor transformations (squeezes, concatenations, index selections), handling the one-to-many fused-op case that would defeat any rule-based matcher. - The precision-normalized error ratio provides a theoretically grounded and empirically validated dtype-agnostic signal—non-buggy components remain below 1.41 at p99 across BF16 and FP8, while the minimum buggy component ratio is 3.13, giving a clean separation. - Ablations are unusually careful in isolating each pipeline stage at both intermediate and end-to-end metrics, revealing that activation alignment tools and knowledge base (not component mapping) are the single largest contributor to final accuracy. - Four developer-confirmed new bug diagnoses constitute out-of-distribution real-world validation, rare for systems papers of this kind. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly state that EKKA is less suitable for: (1) engine orchestration or concurrency bugs where no stable divergence trace exists; (2) hardware corruption; (3) performance bugs. They acknowledge module-level localization granularity (not function or line level) as a limitation and describe finer-grained localization as future work requiring per-variable capture inside forward functions. They note the prototype is PyTorch-based but argue the core pipeline is framework-agnostic. These are confronted directly rather than minimized, which is credible. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The benchmark selection process excluded 36 of 53 candidate model-stack bugs due to hardware constraints (5 Blackwell-only, 2 ROCm-only), large model size (9 cases), framework features outside EKKA's scope (4 cases), and reproducibility difficulties (4 cases). The reported 80% pass@1 applies only to the tractable minority; accuracy on harder bugs is unknown. - The error threshold of 1.5 is calibrated on the same models (Llama-3.1-8B, gemma-2-2b, Qwen2.5-7B) used in the benchmark; threshold robustness to model families outside this set (e.g., MoE models, vision-language models) is untested despite those appearing in the bug list. - The knowledge base construction requires running alignment on known-good model/framework pairs as a bootstrapping step; the cost of extending coverage to new models or frameworks is unquantified and not discussed. - All evaluation uses HuggingFace as the reference; bugs that manifest in HuggingFace itself, or scenarios where no trusted reference exists (novel architectures), are not addressed. - Wall-clock diagnosis time per case is unreported, which matters for practical CI/CD integration where a 2-hour debugging session that costs $30 is acceptable but a 12-hour session is not. Bottom Line A practitioner maintaining LLM serving frameworks will find EKKA directly deployable and the differential debugging framing—combined with agent-generated alignment code and the precision-normalized error ratio—a genuine step forward over both manual debugging and general-purpose SWE agents, with developer-confirmed new bug discoveries as the most credible evidence of real-world value.
Search Results 2606.04594 PDF
#49 — Innovative (68)

Multi-Segment Attention: Enabling Efficient KV-Cache Management for Faster Large Language Model Serving

C Shi, Y Chen, Y Chen, X Miao, B Cui · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces AsymCache, an LLM serving system that aligns KV-cache eviction decisions with GPU attention-kernel latency by jointly optimizing cache hit rate and position-aware recomputation cost, supported by a novel Multi-Segment Attention (MSA) CUDA kernel that processes non-contiguous KV cache segments in a single kernel call. - Why it's important: KV-cache memory pressure is the primary GPU bottleneck in long-context, multi-turn, and agentic LLM workloads, yet existing eviction policies (LRU, LFU, suffix-only) ignore how a block's sequence position determines its recomputation cost, leaving large latency reductions on the table. - How it does it: The core trick is to score each cache block by the product of its estimated future-access frequency (a piecewise exponential function admitting O(log n) balanced-tree maintenance) and its positional recomputation cost (derived from a profiled linear latency model), then always evict the block with the lowest expected latency impact. - The good: Under high-dispersion multi-session workloads on Llama 3.1-70B, AsymCache reduces TTFT by up to 2.03× and TPOT by up to 1.71× over vLLM-LRU, with bitwise-identical outputs; the integration with Continuum (an agent serving system) further cuts average job latency by up to 18.1%. - The missing: Evaluation uses only two long-context QA datasets (LongBench, LooGLE) converted into synthetic multi-turn workloads with 300 requests each; there is no evaluation on production-traffic traces, short-context workloads, or models beyond Llama 3.1-8B/70B, leaving generalizability across workload types and model families unconfirmed. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners building or extending LLM serving infrastructure who care about lossless KV-cache management; the O(log n) eviction algorithm and MSA kernel are directly implementable and the ablations are honest.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. The standout dimension is Non-Obviousness (75): the proof via Cauchy's functional equation that O(log n) balanced-tree eviction forces an exponential frequency function is an elegant, non-decorative theoretical result that directly shapes the algorithm. Rigor (62) is above cluster norm because this is one of the few papers with explicit per-component ablations (§6.3), an R²>0.999 cost-model fit, and hyperparameter sensitivity — though no error bars/seeds, no public code URL, synthetic QA-derived workloads, and a reimplemented Pensieve baseline hold it back. Practical Impact (70) reflects a bitwise-lossless vLLM extension with the MSA kernel and a Continuum integration, discounted by the absent code release. Completeness (65) and Resilience (62) are decent, with the main caveat being the 0%-hit-rate regime where the 'cache management' framing arguably overstates benefit. Weighted composite: 0.15*72 + 0.15*62 + 0.10*75 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*62 + 0.30*70 ≈ 68.
systems Novelty 72 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 65 Resilience 62 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: During LLM inference, KV-caches grow proportionally to context length and the number of concurrent sessions; for a 70B model with 8,192 hidden size, a single 32K-token session occupies over 40 GB in half-precision, exceeding one A100. This forces serving systems to evict KV-cache blocks from GPU memory and recompute them on demand. The dominant eviction policies—LRU (vLLM, SGLang) and LFU variants—maximize cache hit rate without accounting for the asymmetric recomputation cost of tokens at different sequence positions: attention's O(n²) complexity means recomputing a token near position 30,000 is far more expensive than one near position 100. Pensieve partially addresses this for single-user dialogues by caching suffixes, but cannot handle cross-user prefix sharing and incurs O(n) eviction overhead. No existing lossless system jointly optimizes hit rate, recomputation asymmetry, and eviction efficiency. Key Idea: The central insight is that a KV-cache block's value to retain is the product of two independent quantities: (1) its probability of being reused (estimated from historical access frequency), and (2) the additional prefill latency incurred if it is evicted (determined by its positional index in the sequence, because attention cost scales quadratically). Evicting the block with the minimum expected latency cost—rather than the least-recently-used or lowest-frequency block—is shown to yield significant latency reductions. The trick enabling this at O(log n) cost is recognizing that the order-preserving rule required for balanced-tree eviction forces the frequency function to be exponential; the paper proves this formally and uses a piecewise exponential that also fits observed reuse-time distributions. Methodology: AsymCache consists of three integrated components. (1) Multi-Segment Attention (MSA): a single CUDA kernel (built on CUTLASS/FlashAttention, ~2K LoC in C++/CUDA) that dispatches each request to a CTA group and retrieves per-block effective sequence lengths from a precomputed array, enabling non-contiguous (prefix + suffix) KV context in one kernel call rather than two sequential calls. (2) Computational-Aware Block Evictor: scores blocks by f_B(t) × ΔT_B, where ΔT_B = 2k₅·(l₁+q₁) + (k₂-k₃+k₅) is derived from a profiled linear latency model (R² > 0.999 across 1,100 profiling instances), and f_B(t) = min(exp(-τ/α), exp(-(τ-τ₀)/β)) is a piecewise exponential fitted to observed cache-reuse time distributions. The piecewise exponential is stored in two balanced trees (one per segment), achieving O(log n) insert/remove/min operations; the paper proves via Cauchy's functional equation that exponential functions are the unique family satisfying the required order-preserving property. (3) Chunking Scheduler: adapts prefill chunk size downward when decode-request count exceeds a threshold, reducing TPOT under load while enforcing a lower bound to avoid GPU underutilization. An online coefficient λ shifts the piecewise exponential's turning point in response to measured workload lifespan, with zero overhead to the balanced tree. The system is implemented as ~6K LoC Python + 2K LoC C++/CUDA extending vLLM. Key Results: Experiments run on an AMD EPYC 9K84 server with 4× NVIDIA H20 (96 GB each), using Llama 3.1-8B and 3.1-70B on LongBench (avg. 34.8K input tokens) and LooGLE (avg. 24.4K input tokens), with synthetic multi-turn workloads at QPS 0.01–0.04 under low-dispersion (5:1 inter:intra session ratio) and high-dispersion (10:1) conditions, 300 requests per dataset. Baselines: vLLM-LRU, a reimplemented Max-Score policy (O(n) reuse-probability eviction), and Pensieve+MSA (reimplemented with MSA kernel extension). Under low-dispersion, AsymCache reduces TTFT by 1.21–2.03× and TPOT by 1.06–1.40× over vLLM-LRU across all model/dataset combinations. Under high-dispersion, TTFT reductions reach 1.66× and TPOT reductions reach 1.71× (70B, LooGLE). Under queuing conditions (LongBench, QPS=0.04), adding the chunking scheduler amplifies TTFT gains to 1.90–2.03× and TPOT gains to 1.62–1.71× over baselines. Ablation confirms the O(log n) algorithm contributes 4.7% additional TTFT reduction beyond the O(n) variant. The MSA single-kernel implementation eliminates ~4.61ms overhead over two-kernel suffix caching for 1K-token sequences, where the dual-kernel overhead otherwise makes suffix caching unattractive. Agentic integration with Continuum on BFCLv4 reduces average job latency by 4.4–18.1% and P90 tail latency by 6.8–16.6% over Continuum alone. Hyper-parameter sensitivity study shows stable performance across wide ranges of lifespan, reuse probability, and slope change ratio. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. The genuinely novel contributions are: (a) the formal identification that O(log n) eviction requires an exponential frequency function (proved via Cauchy's equation), leading to the piecewise exponential design; (b) the position-aware, expected-latency-minimizing eviction criterion combining hit-rate and recomputation asymmetry; and (c) the MSA CUDA kernel supporting arbitrary non-contiguous KV segments in a single dispatch. The broader concept of eviction-cost awareness is not new (Pensieve does it for suffixes), but the combination of mathematical rigor, algorithmic efficiency, and general multi-segment support is a meaningful advance over prior art. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations isolate the contributions of MSA, the O(log n) algorithm, and the chunking scheduler (§6.3). The linear cost model achieves R² > 0.999 across 1,100 profiling instances, a strong fit. Hyper-parameter sensitivity is demonstrated across three dimensions. Error bars are absent from all figures; no confidence intervals, multiple seeds, or significance tests are reported. The 300-request cap per dataset is justified as representative but not verified against a held-out full-dataset run. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2606.02964v1); no peer review confirmation. Code is promised as an artifact (the proof of the order-preserving theorem references "our artifact") but no URL is provided in the paper, so reproducibility is unconfirmed. Pensieve is reimplemented rather than used from source (it is not open-source), introducing potential fidelity risk for that baseline. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS (a built serving system, evaluated by deployment on real hardware). - Practical: Runs on H20 GPUs using standard CUDA 12.8 and CUTLASS; requires vLLM as the base and approximately 8K LoC of modification, which is non-trivial to maintain but not exotic. - Efficient: Eviction control-plane overhead is ~2 ms per request (0.1% of prefill time) at 8K cache blocks, matching LRU; MSA kernel overhead over prefix caching is negligible for long sequences and eliminated for short sequences compared to two-kernel alternatives. - Effective: Evidence shows real latency reductions (TTFT, TPOT) on two benchmark-derived workloads; outputs are bitwise-equivalent to standard inference by design. However, the workloads are synthetic traces derived from QA benchmarks, not production traffic. - Scalable: The O(log n) eviction algorithm is argued to scale to >100K cache blocks (CPU offload scenario); no experiment at that scale is reported. Multi-GPU tensor parallelism is tested (4× H20 for 70B), but distributed KV-cache scenarios (disaggregated prefill/decode) are not evaluated. The work delivers a software system (vLLM extension), but no public repository URL is provided. Strengths: - The mathematical proof that O(log n) eviction necessitates an exponential frequency function (via Cauchy's functional equation) is a clean theoretical contribution that directly shapes the algorithm design—not decoration. - The expected-latency criterion E(B,t) = f_B(t) × ΔT_B is elegant and unifies prefix-reuse and suffix-cost objectives that prior systems treat as exclusive choices; real workload analysis (Figure 3) motivates the bimodal hit-position distribution empirically before proposing the solution. - The MSA kernel's CTA-group dispatch with per-block effective sequence length lookup is a practical engineering contribution that eliminates the dual-kernel overhead that would otherwise make non-contiguous caching impractical for short sequences. - The integration experiment with Continuum (§6.5) demonstrates composability at a different abstraction level (block vs. request granularity), providing evidence of real-world applicability beyond the core benchmark. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors acknowledge that AsymCache currently stores KV cache solely in GPU memory and does not support multi-level hierarchies (DRAM, disk, remote); they note that eviction cost models would differ across tiers and flag this as future work. They also note that tighter co-design between block-level eviction and program-level agent scheduling policies remains future work. These are stated directly and credibly. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The synthetic multi-turn workload construction (converting LongBench/LooGLE QA pairs into dialogues, 300 requests, Gamma inter-arrival) may not capture real production traffic patterns; there is no evaluation on actual deployment traces or a public production benchmark. - No evaluation on models other than Llama 3.1-8B/70B; it is unclear whether the cost model coefficients (fitted per-GPU) generalize across architectures with different attention variants (e.g., MLA in DeepSeek, sliding-window attention). - The agentic workload gain over Continuum (4.4–18.1%) uses GPT-5.1 pre-generated outputs for reproducibility, meaning the experiment does not test AsymCache under live LLM generation where output length is stochastic. - Hit-rate improvements under high-dispersion (Figure 12, LooGLE) for vLLM-LRU and Max-Score show 0% block-level hit rates, meaning AsymCache's TPOT gains in that regime come primarily from preferring cheaper-to-recompute blocks at 0% hit rate—the "cache management" framing may overstate benefit compared to simply claiming "position-aware recomputation scheduling." - No measurement of GPU memory fragmentation or overhead from maintaining two balanced trees at runtime for large-scale deployments. Bottom Line: A practitioner building or maintaining an LLM serving stack on top of vLLM who handles long-context, multi-turn, or agentic workloads will find both the O(log n) eviction algorithm and the MSA kernel directly useful, and the latency gains are large enough to warrant adoption if the vLLM integration cost can be absorbed.
Search Results 2606.02964 PDF
#50 — Innovative (68)

Rethinking the Divergence Regularization in LLM RL (26 ▲ )

Jiarui Yao, Xiangxin Zhou, Penghui Qi, Wee Sun Lee, Liefeng Bo, Tianyu Pang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 10 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces DRPO (Divergence Regularized Policy Optimization), a policy optimization objective for LLM RL that replaces DPPO's hard binary mask with a smooth advantage-weighted quadratic regularizer over the Binary-TV probability shift, yielding bounded, continuous per-token gradient weights with corrective signals outside the trust region. - Why it's important: Off-policy LLM RL (from training-inference numerical mismatches and mini-batch staleness) is endemic in production pipelines; existing methods either use ratio-based trust regions that are poorly behaved on long-tailed vocabularies, or divergence-based hard masks that discard gradients rather than correcting them, leading to training instability especially under low-precision (FP8) settings. - How it does it: Multiplying SPO's quadratic penalty by the behavior probability µ(y_t|s_t) converts the implicit χ²-type penalty (ratio-based, unbounded variance) into an ℓ²₂-type penalty (absolute probability shift, bounded in [0,1]), yielding the same Binary-TV trust-region boundary as DPPO but enforced through a continuous gradient weight that attenuates diverging updates and reverses sign outside the boundary. - The good: DRPO consistently matches or exceeds the best baseline across all six experimental settings (Qwen3-4B, Qwen3-30B MoE, Qwen3.5-35B MoE, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-1.5B, under BF16, FP8-rollout, and FP8-end-to-end precision), with the gradient-weight derivation providing a principled explanation for why KL, TV, and ratio-based alternatives fail. - The missing: All experiments use a single math reasoning task (AIME 2024/2025 via ~13K DAPO problems); no evaluation on RLHF with learned reward models, coding tasks, or instruction-following benchmarks, leaving generalizability outside math RL undemonstrated. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners implementing LLM RL pipelines, especially with off-policy training or FP8 precision, as the method is a drop-in replacement for GRPO/DPPO and the gradient-weight analysis offers actionable design principles; those outside the LLM RL optimization space can skip.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (72), Completeness (70), and Resilience (70) carry the score: thorough component-isolating ablations (advantage weighting, divergence type, mask vs. global), an analytical gradient-geometry argument (Appendix C) explaining why KL/K3/TV reintroduce bad geometry, public code, and FP8-E2E stress tests across four models up to 35B MoE. Novelty (62) is modest — a clean but incremental synthesis of DPPO's boundary and SPO's smooth quadratic via a single µ-multiplication. The composite is held below 75 by single-domain (AIME math only) evaluation, curve-only results with no tabulated peak accuracy or error bars on a ~30-problem benchmark, and no empirical comparison to TROLL or mirror-descent alternatives.
empirical Novelty 62 Rigor 72 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 70 Resilience 70 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation LLM post-training via RL is inherently off-policy: rollouts are generated by optimized inference engines (e.g., vLLM with FP16/FP8) that have numerical behavior differing from the training engine, and collected trajectories are split across multiple mini-batches causing policy staleness. This mismatch makes trust-region control essential. The dominant recipe—PPO/GRPO ratio clipping—uses the per-token importance ratio r_t = π(y_t|s_t)/µ(y_t|s_t) as a proxy for distributional shift. But in large, long-tailed vocabularies, a low-probability token (µ = 1e-5) can exhibit a ratio of 100× from a tiny absolute probability change, while a high-probability token (µ = 0.9) can shift by 0.1 in probability mass with a modest ratio. Ratio-based trust regions systematically over-constrain exploratory rare tokens and under-constrain high-probability ones. DPPO (Qi et al., 2026) addressed this by switching to a Binary-TV absolute-probability-shift mask, but its binary on/off gradient zeroing still discards rather than corrects out-of-boundary tokens and introduces abrupt gradient changes near the threshold. Key Idea The key insight is that DPPO's Binary-TV constraint |π(y_t|s_t) − µ(y_t|s_t)| ≤ δ is equivalent to a token-adaptive ratio constraint |r_t − 1| ≤ δ/µ(y_t|s_t). Applying SPO's smooth-regularizer principle (replace hard clip with an advantage-weighted concave quadratic in r_t) to this token-adaptive ratio constraint—and simplifying using the identity µ(y_t|s_t)(r_t − 1) = π(y_t|s_t) − µ(y_t|s_t)—yields a quadratic regularizer in absolute probability space rather than ratio space. The resulting per-token gradient weight w_t = 1 − sign(Â_t(r_t−1)) · |π(y_t|s_t) − µ(y_t|s_t)|/δ is bounded in [1−1/δ, 1+1/δ] for all tokens, smoothly attenuates diverging updates, and reverses sign (becomes corrective) outside the trust-region boundary. Methodology DRPO optimizes L_DRPO = E_{y~µ}[Σ_t r_t Â_t − (|Â_t|/2δ) · µ(y_t|s_t)(r_t−1)²]. The pipeline: (1) collect rollouts from a behavior policy µ (inference engine), (2) compute group-relative advantages Â_t = R(x,y_i) − mean_j[R(x,y_j)] (GRPO-style, no critic), (3) for each token compute Binary-TV shift D^{Bin-TV}_t = |π(y_t|s_t) − µ(y_t|s_t)| and gradient weight w_t = 1 − sign(Â_t(r_t−1)) · D^{Bin-TV}_t/δ, (4) update via w_t · r_t · Â_t · ∇ log π(y_t|s_t). Critical design decisions: (a) multiplying the regularizer by |Â_t| to decouple the trust-region boundary from reward scale—ablations confirm this is essential; (b) using Binary-TV rather than full TV or KL divergence, with Appendix C proving analytically that KL, K3, and sampled-TV alternatives all reintroduce ratio-based or binary gradient geometry; (c) applying the regularizer globally (not masked), with ablation showing that the Mask-DRPO variant (penalty only outside DPPO boundary) achieves comparable performance, confirming the primary gain is from out-of-boundary correction. The framework is implemented on VeRL + Megatron + vLLM with 4×8 NVIDIA H20 GPUs. Key Results Evaluated on AIME 2024 and AIME 2025 (MAA competition math, rule-based verification), averaging 16 sampled responses per problem. Six settings: Qwen3-4B-Base (800 steps), Qwen3-30B-A3B-Base (300 steps, BF16, FP8-rollout, FP8-E2E), Qwen3.5-35B-A3B-Base (110 steps), DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-1.5B (3000 steps). Baselines: unregularized surrogate, GRPO (clip-higher ε_low=0.2, ε_high=0.28), SPO (δ=12.5), DPPO (δ=0.15). Results are presented as learning curves rather than single final-accuracy numbers, making exact comparisons difficult to extract; the paper claims DRPO "matches or exceeds" best baseline in all six settings. Key qualitative findings: ratio-based methods (GRPO, SPO) collapse in FP8 settings while DRPO trains stably; DPPO trains stably but converges more slowly and to lower accuracy than DRPO in most settings; unregularized surrogate fails in 3/6 settings including a 0.25→0.17 accuracy drop on Qwen3-4B. Hyperparameter sensitivity: DRPO at δ=2.5 vs δ=12.5 shows only minor degradation; DPPO requires tighter tuning with different optimal δ per setting. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The core novelty is the observation that multiplying SPO's quadratic penalty by µ(y_t|s_t)—a single scalar factor—changes the trust-region geometry from ratio-based (χ²-type) to absolute-probability-shift-based (ℓ²₂-type), closing the gap between DPPO's divergence-motivated mask and SPO's smooth enforcement. This is a well-motivated but incremental algorithmic contribution: the method is a clean synthesis of DPPO (divergence-based boundary) and SPO (smooth quadratic), not a fundamental conceptual departure. The analytical framework for comparing regularizers via induced gradient geometry (Appendix C) is a genuine contribution. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are thorough and well-targeted: advantage weighting (|Â_t| vs. without), alternative divergences (KL, K3, TV vs. Binary-TV), mask ablation (penalty everywhere vs. outside-only), hyperparameter sweeps for DRPO, DPPO, and KL baselines. No error bars or significance tests are reported on the AIME accuracy curves; given AIME has at most ~30 problems per year, the evaluation noise floor may be substantial (16 samples × ~30 problems = ~480 evaluations per checkpoint). Results are presented as learning curves, which is appropriate for stability analysis but makes peak-accuracy comparisons imprecise. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/Tencent-Hunyuan/UniRL/tree/main/DRPO. This is a preprint (arXiv:2606.09821v1, June 8, 2026); not peer-reviewed. Only one benchmark domain (math competition problems) is tested, which is both a methodological concern and an acknowledged scope limitation. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL — the paper runs actual RL training experiments on multiple models. - Practical: Runs on 4×8 NVIDIA H20 GPUs, a commercially available configuration, using standard VeRL + vLLM stack; no specialized hardware required beyond what comparable LLM RL work demands. - Efficient: DRPO adds a per-token scalar multiplication (computing |π(y_t|s_t) − µ(y_t|s_t)|) relative to GRPO, which is negligible overhead; the method uses the same GRPO-style critic-free advantages, so it does not add a value model. - Effective: Evidence shows DRPO solves the specific problem (training stability under off-policy settings and FP8 precision) on math reasoning; no evidence it generalizes to reward-model-based RLHF, coding, or instruction-following. - Scalable: Tested up to 35B parameters (MoE); the largest dense model tested is 1.5B (R1D). No experiments at 70B+ or with full-precision dense models at scale. Scalability is claimed but not empirically verified beyond the tested range. The work delivers code and a drop-in objective function; no new datasets, benchmarks, or trained models are released. Strengths - The gradient-weight lens for analyzing trust-region regularizers (Appendix C) is genuinely useful: it shows that KL, K3, and sampled-TV penalties all reintroduce ratio-based or binary geometry, providing a principled criterion ("does the gradient weight depend on absolute probability shift or on the ratio?") for evaluating future proposals. - The Binary-TV variance analysis (variance of |r_t−1| contains a χ² term that diverges on low-probability tokens, while |π_t−µ_t| is bounded in [0,1] with variance ≤ 1/4) provides a formal justification for the empirical instability of ratio-based methods in FP8 settings. - Ablations are structured to isolate each design component (advantage weighting, divergence type, inside vs. outside boundary application), making the contribution claims individually testable. - FP8-E2E experiments are a practically relevant stress test: this is a regime where ratio-based methods collapse and the advantage of divergence-based approaches is most pronounced. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors do not include a formal limitations section. Implicitly acknowledged scope boundaries: evaluation restricted to math reasoning with rule-based verifiers (no learned reward models); Qwen3.5 training was truncated (110 steps) due to Megatron's limited support for that architecture at experiment time. The reliance on a single AIME benchmark for all accuracy measurements is not flagged as a limitation by the authors. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Single-domain evaluation: all experiments use math competition problems (AIME, DAPO dataset). The method's benefit over ratio-based approaches may be specific to math RL, where response lengths are long and token distributions are highly non-uniform; generalization to RLHF, coding, or alignment tasks is undemonstrated. - Peak-accuracy numbers are not tabulated: the paper presents only learning curves, making it impossible to extract final accuracy values or verify claims of "matching or exceeding" baselines without manual reading of figures. - AIME is a small evaluation set (~30 problems per year); with 16 samples per problem and no reported variance, the accuracy measurements are noisy, and performance differences visible in learning curves may not be statistically significant. - No comparison to TROLL (Becker et al., 2025) or Kimi-style mirror descent (Team et al., 2025a/2026) in the main experiments; these are mentioned in related work but not empirically evaluated. - The hyperparameter δ=12.5 is used for both SPO and DRPO, but these parameters govern qualitatively different constraints (ratio vs. absolute probability shift), so the choice may not place them on a fair comparative footing. Bottom Line A practitioner implementing off-policy LLM RL—particularly with FP8 precision or MoE architectures—gains a theoretically grounded and empirically validated drop-in improvement over GRPO and DPPO, but the single-domain (math) evaluation and curve-only result presentation require the reader to verify generalizability before adopting DRPO for production RLHF pipelines.
Recommended 2606.09821 PDF
#51 — Innovative (68)

LEAP: Supercharging LLMs for Formal Mathematics with Agentic Frameworks

PN Kung, L Song, D Hwang, J Yoon, CL Li, S Severini… · arxiv.org · Quoc V. Le - new articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces LEAP (LLM-in-Lean Environment Agentic Prover), an agentic framework that enables general-purpose LLMs, without any domain-specific fine-tuning, to achieve state-of-the-art formal theorem proving in Lean via hierarchical AND-OR DAG decomposition and iterative compiler feedback. - Why it's important: Specialized prover models (AlphaProof, DeepSeek Prover V2, Goedel-Prover-V2) require expensive fine-tuning on formal corpora; if general-purpose LLMs can match them through scaffolding alone, the barrier to building formal verification systems drops dramatically. - How it does it: LEAP decomposes each proof goal into subgoals via informal natural-language blueprints, translates blueprints into Lean proof sketches, enforces acyclicity in a shared AND-OR DAG to enable lemma reuse across branches, and uses an LLM reviewer to filter formally-valid but mathematically-vacuous decompositions before committing them to the DAG. - The good: LEAP solves all 12 Putnam 2025 problems (100%) while direct-formalization baselines solve 0%, and achieves 70% overall on the new Lean-IMO-Bench, surpassing Aristotle (a specialized IMO Gold-medal system) at 48%. - The missing: LEAP requires up to 3,000 LLM calls per problem (with rollout=2), making it far more expensive than the Pass@128 baselines it is compared against; no compute-normalized comparison is provided, and only one backbone LLM (Gemini-3.1-pro) is evaluated. - Read it or not: Researchers in formal verification, automated theorem proving, or agentic LLM systems should read this — it makes a mechanically-verified empirical case that overturns the prevailing assumption that specialized fine-tuning is indispensable for competitive formal proving.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (72) and Non-Obviousness (72) lead: the AND-OR DAG with hierarchical lemma memoization is a principled fix for exponential blowup in recursive decomposition, and the mechanically-verified result that an un-fine-tuned general LLM matches specialized provers (12/12 Putnam 2025, 70% Lean-IMO-Bench vs Aristotle's 48%) overturns a prevailing field assumption. Rigor (68) benefits enormously from Lean-compiler ground truth and public proof/benchmark artifacts, but is pulled down by the uncorrected compute asymmetry (rollout=2 with up to 3,000 calls vs Pass@128 baselines), single-run results, and a single-problem reviewer ablation. Resilience (62) is the weakest dimension — the headline comparison is not budget-normalized and only one backbone (Gemini-3.1-pro) is tested, so the 'general LLMs' claim is unverified. Weighted: 0.20*72+0.25*68+0.15*72+0.15*65+0.15*62+0.10*70 = 68.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 68 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 65 Resilience 62 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Despite LLMs' impressive informal mathematical reasoning, they fail to generate machine-verifiable proofs in formal languages like Lean. Human verification of complex proofs is prohibitively expensive (the Kepler conjecture required four years of peer review and a decade of formal effort), motivating automated formal proving. Prior ATP systems predominantly fine-tune specialized models on large formal corpora, which is resource-intensive and produces brittle specialists. Agentic approaches like Hilbert combine general LLMs for informal reasoning with specialized models for Lean steps, but still depend on fine-tuned backends. Crucially, existing benchmarks (MiniF2F, PutnamBench) are increasingly saturated and fail to stress-test multi-step, non-routine reasoning. The gap between general LLMs' strong informal ability and near-zero one-shot formal proving performance is the central problem. Key Idea The core insight is that the one-shot Lean generation bottleneck for general LLMs reflects the difficulty of producing long, syntactically valid, type-correct proofs in a single attempt — not a deficit in mathematical understanding. An agentic loop that decomposes goals into an AND-OR DAG, bridges informal reasoning to Lean via natural-language blueprints, reuses proven lemmas across branches (hierarchical memoization), and filters useless decompositions via an LLM reviewer unlocks general LLMs for formal proving without any fine-tuning. Methodology LEAP operates as a DFS-with-backtracking agent over an AND-OR DAG. Each open goal is first attempted via direct formalization: generate an informal proof, translate it to Lean, submit to the compiler, and iterate up to 20 revision steps using compiler error messages and LeanSearch retrieval. If direct formalization fails, LEAP enters blueprint decomposition: generate an informal blueprint proposing supporting lemmas, translate it to a Lean proof sketch that is sorry-free in the main body but allows sorry placeholders only for newly proposed lemmas, submit to Lean, and have an LLM reviewer assess whether the proposed subgoals meaningfully simplify the parent goal before committing as an AND node. Three design decisions are critical: (1) requiring the main theorem body to be sorry-free preserves AND-OR soundness — once all children are proved, the parent is proved; (2) an acyclicity check before committing sketches prevents circular dependencies; (3) anticipatory lemma planning allows the DAG to accumulate auxiliary lemmas that may prove useful downstream even if not immediately needed. The backbone is Gemini-3.1-pro throughout. No training or fine-tuning is performed. Key Results On Putnam 2025 (12 problems): LEAP solves 12/12 (100%) with rollout=2. Direct formalization baselines Gemini-3.1-pro and Goedel-Prover-V2-32B each solve 0/12 at Pass@128. Hilbert solves 4/12 (33.3%) but requires up to 7-day time limits per rollout due to O((n·b)^d) search complexity. Aristotle (closed-source) solves 9/12 (75%) at rollout=2. Computational cost for LEAP ranges from 46 LLM calls (Problem B2) to 3,000 (Problem A5), with proof lengths from 300 to 2,000 lines. On Lean-IMO-Bench (60 problems, Basic and Advanced sets): LEAP achieves 83.3%/56.7% (Basic/Advanced) vs. Gemini-3.1-pro 20%/3.3%, Goedel-V2-32B 10%/0%, Hilbert 36.6%/6.6%, Aristotle 76.7%/20%. LEAP maintains 100% on Algebra and Number Theory regardless of difficulty tier but achieves only 16.7% and 12.5% on Geometry. Ablation: removing DAG memoization (naive tree) drops performance from 83.3%/56.7% to 73.3%/40.0%. Removing the LLM reviewer causes failure on Putnam A5 even after 8 rollouts, due to the agent repeatedly producing formally-valid but cyclically-restating decompositions. Iterative formalization raises Gemini-3.1-pro's Basic solve rate from 20.0% (Pass@128 one-shot) to 36.6% (Pass@1 iterative with 20 revision steps); Goedel-Prover-V2-32B does not benefit from iteration (10.0% → 6.6%), indicating it lacks the compiler-error interpretation capability needed for feedback loops. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK. The AND-OR DAG with hierarchical memoization for agentic formal theorem proving is novel as an architectural design, distinguishing LEAP from prior recursive decomposition systems (Hilbert) that suffer exponential blowup. The informal-to-formal bridge via iteratively-refined blueprints extends the "draft, sketch, and prove" paradigm (Jiang et al., 2023) with recursive decomposition and shared proof state. Lean-IMO-Bench fills a genuine gap between saturated undergraduate benchmarks and research-level problems. The finding that general LLMs without fine-tuning can match specialized provers is an empirical finding that challenges a prevailing assumption in the field. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and informative: DAG vs. tree, with/without LLM reviewer, one-shot vs. iterative formalization. However, the LLM reviewer ablation is conducted on a single problem (Putnam A5), not the full benchmark; its contribution across all problems is not quantified. No error bars or significance tests are reported; all results are single-run at rollout=2. Lean proofs are publicly available at https://github.com/google-deepmind/superhuman/tree/main/leap, and Lean-IMO-Bench at https://imobench.github.io — these are genuine reproducibility positives since the Lean compiler provides ground-truth verification. Key concern: the evaluation is asymmetric in compute — LEAP uses rollout=2 with up to 3,000 LLM calls per rollout, while direct baselines use Pass@128 (128 parallel single-pass attempts). A single LEAP rollout on Problem A5 consumes vastly more inference compute than 128 one-shot attempts; no FLOPs- or wall-clock-normalized comparison is provided. This is an arXiv preprint (v2, June 2026); peer-reviewed confidence level is not established. The principal competitor Aristotle is closed-source, making it scientifically unverifiable (the authors acknowledge this). Deployability (4-property check) EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: Requires only a Lean 4 installation and API access to a frontier LLM; no GPU training infrastructure needed, making deployment accessible relative to fine-tuned prover systems. - Efficient: Costs are high in inference compute — up to 3,000 LLM calls per problem (roughly hours of wall time and substantial API cost), with the hardest problems requiring two full rollouts; geometry problems remain intractable even at this budget. - Effective: All claimed results are mechanically verified by the Lean compiler, providing the strongest possible correctness guarantee — evidence quality is higher than self-reported informal math results. - Scalable: Proof search space grows rapidly with decomposition depth; the paper identifies this as the primary open challenge and provides no experiments at problem scales harder than Putnam/IMO-Advanced, nor any study of how cost scales with problem difficulty beyond the reported per-problem LLM call counts. The work delivers verified Lean proof artifacts and a new benchmark dataset; no software library or packaged CLI is described. Strengths - The AND-OR DAG with lemma memoization is a principled solution to the exponential blowup in naive recursive decomposition, directly validated by comparing against Hilbert (which hits O((n·b)^d) complexity and requires 7-day limits per rollout). - All reported successes are machine-verified by the Lean compiler — a correctness guarantee absent in informal math reasoning benchmarks, making the empirical claims unusually reliable. - The empirical finding that a general LLM without fine-tuning matches or exceeds specialized provers on Putnam 2025 and Lean-IMO-Bench is non-obvious and significant; it is supported by multiple ablations showing the contribution of each design component. - Lean-IMO-Bench is a technically well-motivated benchmark contribution: expert-formalized statements, balanced across four mathematical domains, with a difficulty gradient specifically designed to test long multi-step reasoning where existing benchmarks are saturating. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge that geometry performance is near zero across all methods due to Lean's lack of domain-specific geometric frameworks, and retain it in the benchmark only to evaluate general-purpose reasoning under formalization constraints. They acknowledge the scalability challenge: decomposition grows the search space rapidly, and branch prioritization remains an open problem. The hybrid architecture (general LLM for high-level reasoning combined with a specialized model for local formal steps) is identified as a promising direction explicitly out of scope for this paper. The closed-source status of Aristotle is noted as a limitation on direct comparison. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Compute asymmetry is not addressed: LEAP at rollout=2 with thousands of LLM calls per problem is compared to baselines at Pass@128. A budget-matched comparison (e.g., 128 independent LEAP rollouts vs. Gemini-3.1-pro at Pass@128, or total-tokens-matched) is absent and would substantially change the narrative. - Only one backbone LLM (Gemini-3.1-pro) is evaluated. The claim that this approach works for "general LLMs" is unverified for GPT-4o, Claude, Llama, or smaller open-source models; it is plausible that the approach is highly sensitive to model quality. - The LLM reviewer ablation covers one problem only; the reviewer's contribution to the full Lean-IMO-Bench performance is unknown, making it unclear whether this component is essential or marginal across the broader benchmark. - No analysis of failure mode distribution: it is unknown what fraction of unsolved problems fail due to budget exhaustion versus fundamental inability to decompose the proof. This matters for assessing where more compute or better search would help. - Geometry (16.7%/12.5%) is included in aggregate scores that flatter LEAP relative to geometry-free subsets; since all systems fail geometry, aggregate score differences primarily reflect non-geometry domains. Bottom Line Practitioners in formal verification and AI for mathematics should read this paper — it provides mechanically-verified evidence that agentic scaffolding with general-purpose LLMs can match specialized fine-tuned provers on competition-level benchmarks, a result that challenges a widely-held assumption, though the compute cost and single-model evaluation scope leave important questions open.
Recommended 2606.03303 PDF
#52 — Innovative (68)

Toward Generalist Autonomous Research via Hypothesis-Tree Refinement (68 ▲ )

Jiajie Jin, Yuyang Hu, Kai Qiu, Qi Dai, Chong Luo, Guanting Dong, Xiaoxi Li, Tong Zhao, Xiaolong Ma, Gongrui Zhang, Zhirong Wu, Bei Liu, Zhengyuan Yang, Linjie Li, Lijuan Wang, Hongjin Qian, Yutao Zhu, Zhicheng Dou · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 11 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Arbor, an autonomous research framework that organizes long-horizon iterative artifact improvement through a persistent Hypothesis Tree (HTR) in which each node binds a hypothesis, implementation branch, experimental evidence, and distilled insight, managed by a long-lived coordinator dispatching short-lived executors to isolated worktrees. - Why it's important: Current coding agents treat each trial as an independent local attempt, discarding evidence from failures and successes alike; this prevents cumulative research progress over long horizons and causes overfitting to development feedback rather than producing transferable artifact improvements. - How it does it: A coordinator maintains a rooted tree where depth encodes hypothesis granularity, propagates insights upward after each executor returns, and admits candidates to the current best artifact only when they pass a held-out test evaluator in a fresh worktree — separating exploratory search from verified progress. - The good: Arbor achieves the best held-out result on all six real-research tasks versus Codex (GPT-5.5) and Claude Code (Opus 4.6) under identical resource budgets, attaining more than 2.5× the average relative held-out gain, with BrowseComp improving from 45.33% to 67.67% accuracy versus baselines stuck at 50–53%. - The missing: The six AO tasks are all short-horizon, single-scalar-metric, AI-infrastructure tasks constructed by the authors; no independent evaluation on tasks outside this self-constructed suite exists, and the baselines (Codex, Claude Code in /goal mode) are not the strongest available specialized ML-engineering agents. - Read it or not: Read it if you work on autonomous agents, AI-for-ML, or multi-agent orchestration — the HTR design is concrete and the ablations credibly isolate the mechanism; skip it if you need evidence of generalization beyond AI infrastructure tasks or domains with richer scientific objectives.
Why this rank: Systems profile (open-sourced autonomous-research framework evaluated by deployment). The top of this cluster: the held-out merge gate, hypothesis-bound executors, and the non-obvious ablation finding that insight propagation matters more than tree structure give high Novelty (72) and Non-Obviousness (72). Completeness (75) is a standout — four limitations confronted substantively plus unusually full prompt/hyperparameter/task disclosure. Rigor (62) is helped by three-run averaging with std and the independent MLE-Bench Lite result (86.36% Any Medal), but the six headline tasks are author-constructed with non-strongest baselines, which also bounds Resilience (62). Practical Impact (68) reflects a real extensible artifact tempered by 20–43M-token, 48-hour runs. Weighted composite ≈ 68.
systems Novelty 72 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 75 Resilience 62 Impact 68
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Long-horizon autonomous research requires an agent to maintain competing hypotheses, run experiments, interpret both successes and failures, and carry those lessons forward to reshape later exploration. Prior coding agents (Codex, Claude Code, OpenHands) extend single-trajectory execution but have no mechanism to prevent each experiment from being treated as an independent local attempt — failed trials are discarded rather than converted into constraints, and dev-set feedback becomes an oracle that can be overfit. Scientific agent systems (AI Scientist, AIDE, R&D-Agent) move closer to research automation but generally follow predefined workflows or revise a single line of work at a time. The gap is a general framework that makes multi-trial experimentation cumulative: evidence from all attempts — including failures — should narrow the search space and guide future hypothesis formation, while artifact-level progress should be admitted only when it generalizes beyond the feedback used during exploration. Key Idea: The core trick is externalizing the research state into a persistent hypothesis tree rather than relying on the agent's context window or a flat experiment log. Each tree node binds a hypothesis, the artifact version implementing it, a development score, a factual result, and a distilled insight — so local experimental outcomes can be propagated upward as direction-level lessons and eventually as global priors on the root. Because the tree persists to disk after every mutation and the coordinator re-grounds itself by reading the tree (not conversational memory) at each cycle, the research state survives context compression and agent restarts. Promotion to the current best artifact passes through a held-out merge gate that runs Etest in a separate detached worktree, enforcing the distinction between exploratory improvement and verified progress. Methodology: Arbor formalizes autonomous research as Autonomous Optimization (AO): given an initial artifact M₀, a natural-language objective O, a development evaluator Edev, and a held-out evaluator Etest, produce M* = argmax Stest(M') over candidates found without using Etest during search. The HTR algorithm runs a six-step coordinator loop: (1) Observe — read the tree including frontier, pruned lessons, and validated findings; (2) Ideate — select a parent node and attach k pending child hypotheses using accumulated evidence as priors and pruned nodes as negative constraints, gated by skill-loaded idea-drafting and fatal-flaw-scan procedures; (3) Select — choose pending leaves balancing expected impact, evidence from ancestors and siblings, and exploration diversity; (4) Dispatch — send selected leaves to parallel short-lived executors, each in an isolated git worktree branched from trunk HEAD, injected with the hypothesis, ancestor insights, and evaluation commands via template variables; (5) Backpropagate — write returned (score, result, insight, branch-ref) back to leaf nodes and synthesize upward: each ancestor's insight is rewritten to abstract over its children's insights; (6) Decide — the best dev-scoring node triggers the merge gate, which runs Etest in a fresh detached worktree and promotes the artifact only on strict improvement; pruned subtrees based on falsified insights narrow future search. Critical design decisions: (a) executors are hypothesis-bound and cannot change the assigned direction, ensuring evidence is attributable to the node tested; (b) Etest is accessible only through GitMergeBranch, not to executors, preventing the held-out evaluator from leaking into search; (c) context compression is handled by persisting the tree to JSON so the coordinator can re-ground itself from disk rather than conversational memory. The implementation runs both coordinator and executors on Claude Opus 4.6 by default, with a 48-hour wall-clock limit, 20 coordinator cycles max, and max tree depth 2. Key Results: On six self-constructed AO tasks, Arbor achieves the best held-out result on all six: Optimizer Design (3237.5 steps vs. 3287.5 Claude Code, 3325 Codex; +2.63% relative); Architecture Design (loss 1.028 vs. 1.033 Claude Code; +6.38%); Terminal-Bench 2.0 (77.36% pass vs. 71.70% Claude Code, 73.59% Codex); BrowseComp (67.67% acc. vs. 53.33% Claude Code, 50.00% Codex; +22.34pp absolute); Search-Agent Data Synthesis (+13 gap vs. +7 Claude Code); Math-Reasoning Data Synthesis (+19.79 gap vs. +7.29 Claude Code). Average relative held-out gain is reported as >2.5× that of Codex and Claude Code. On MLE-Bench Lite with GPT-5.5, Arbor achieves 86.36% Any Medal and 77.27% Gold — the highest reported Any Medal and Gold rates in the comparison table of 14 systems. Token consumption for Arbor runs 20.12M–43.19M tokens, comparable to single-trajectory baselines (not substantially larger). Ablations on MLE-Bench Lite (Claude Opus 4.6 backbone): full Arbor 81.82% Any Medal; w/o tree 63.64%; w/o insight feedback 54.54% — notably, removing insight propagation while keeping the tree causes a larger drop than removing the tree entirely. Cross-task transfer: a BrowseComp-optimized harness, frozen and evaluated on HLE and DeepSearchQA without further tuning, improves HLE from 25.50% to 31.50% and DeepSearchQA from 61.00% to 69.00%. This is a preprint (arXiv:2606.11926v1, June 2026); the authors explicitly label it a living technical report. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The hypothesis tree with upward insight propagation and hypothesis-bound executors is a genuinely new architectural design for autonomous ML engineering. The formalization of Autonomous Optimization (AO) as a distinct problem class with a dev/test protocol is a useful new framing. The finding that insight propagation matters more than tree structure alone (removing propagation hurts more than removing hierarchy) is a non-obvious empirical result. The individual components — tree search, coordinator/executor separation, held-out merge gates — each appear in prior work (AIDE uses tree search; AI Scientist-v2 uses agentic tree search; RE-Bench uses held-out evaluation), but their specific integration with persistent evidence accumulation and hypothesis-bound executors that cannot redirect their own hypotheses is novel. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and credibly isolate the two key components (tree structure, insight propagation) on a standard benchmark (MLE-Bench Lite). Three-run averaging with standard deviation is reported for stochastic methods. The dev/test split discipline is a genuine methodological strength — the paper explicitly enforces immutable test evaluators and demonstrates that dev performance can diverge from test (Terminal-Bench: Claude Code dev 75.00 → test 71.70; Arbor dev 72.22 → test 77.36). Code is open-sourced at https://github.com/RUC-NLPIR/Arbor. However, the six AO tasks were constructed by the authors specifically for this paper, creating a conflict of interest: the task design, baselines, and evaluation harnesses are all author-controlled. MLE-Bench Lite is an independent third-party benchmark and is therefore the strongest evidence. The baselines are run through their official /goal autonomous mode, which is a reasonable but potentially non-optimal configuration (e.g., neither Codex nor Claude Code is specialized for long-horizon ML engineering). No significance tests or error bars are reported for the six AO tasks (only Avg@3 across full runs, which are expensive). The three-run average is reported for cross-run reliability but confidence intervals are not presented. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS — Arbor is a built tool evaluated by deployment on real tasks. - Practical: Arbor runs on commercially available hardware; the six AO tasks use four NVIDIA A100-80GB GPUs for training evaluations, and MLE-Bench Lite uses one A100, which is standard research infrastructure. - Efficient: Token consumption of 20–43M tokens per run at Claude Opus 4.6 pricing is expensive for routine use; each run spans up to 48 hours wall-clock; this is appropriate for research automation but prohibitive for frequent deployment without significant cost reduction. - Effective: Evidence that it solves the stated problem (cumulative hypothesis refinement outperforming flat coding agents) is credible on MLE-Bench Lite (independent benchmark) and plausible on the six AO tasks, but the AO tasks are author-constructed and the baselines are not the strongest specialized ML-engineering agents available. - Scalable: The paper does not test beyond depth-2 trees or 20 coordinator cycles; whether deeper trees, longer horizons, or tasks requiring genuine domain knowledge (biology, math proofs) are handled is stated as future work, not demonstrated. The system delivers an open-source software artifact (GitHub repository) with a defined coordinator/executor interface, YAML plugins, and skill system for extensibility. Strengths: - The held-out merge gate is a principled and well-executed design choice that directly addresses a known failure mode in autonomous search (dev-set overfitting), and the Terminal-Bench example makes the benefit concrete and measurable. - The ablation showing that insight propagation matters more than tree structure alone is a non-obvious finding that advances understanding of what makes the architecture work, not just that it works. - Cross-task transfer (BrowseComp-trained harness improving HLE and DeepSearchQA without retraining) provides evidence that discoveries are mechanistically general, not benchmark-specific. - The paper's engineering detail is unusually complete: coordinator and executor prompts, tool schemas, hyperparameter tables, and the full AO task specifications are all published, making replication feasible. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly and substantively acknowledge four limitations: (1) evaluation scope is restricted to AI infrastructure tasks (model training, harness engineering, data synthesis) and does not cover biology, mathematics, or physics; (2) the AO interface optimizes a single scalar objective, whereas real research involves multi-objective trade-offs; (3) idea generation remains far below expert human researcher quality — agents can make local refinements but struggle to identify new high-level mechanisms or reason from first principles; (4) cost and infrastructure challenges (prompt caching, evaluator scheduling, isolated worktree startup) make long runs expensive and potentially unreliable. These are confronted directly rather than minimized, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The six primary AO tasks are entirely author-constructed, meaning the authors controlled task design, baseline configuration, and evaluation harnesses — a conflict that independent benchmarks like MLE-Bench Lite avoid. The comparison on MLE-Bench Lite is the only fully independent evaluation, and it is run against systems using different backbone models (e.g., MARS uses Gemini-3-Pro), making head-to-head attribution to the Arbor framework versus backbone model unclear. - The baselines (Codex and Claude Code in /goal mode) are general coding agents, not specialized ML-engineering agents; stronger comparators would be AIDE, ML-Master 2.0, or MARS on the same six AO tasks — none of which is evaluated on Arbor's task suite. - The tree depth is capped at 2, and the max coordinator cycles at 20; it is unknown whether the gains hold or erode at greater depth or longer horizons, and there is no scaling study. - The cost analysis (Figure 4) shows token ranges but does not report dollar costs or wall-clock time per task, making practical cost comparison with baselines difficult; the claim that Arbor uses "comparable" tokens to baselines is visually suggestive in Figure 4 but some Arbor points appear at higher token counts than baseline points achieving lower gains. - The insight propagation step relies on an LLM call to synthesize children's insights into a parent summary; there is no evaluation of whether these summaries are faithful or introduce hallucinated constraints that misdirect later search. Bottom Line: Arbor makes a credible and practically grounded contribution to autonomous ML engineering — the held-out gate, hypothesis-bound executors, and insight propagation are principled improvements over flat coding agents — but practitioners should weight the MLE-Bench Lite results (independent benchmark, 86.36% Any Medal) more heavily than the six author-constructed AO task results before adopting it.
Recommended 2606.11926 PDF
#53 — Innovative (68)

ADR: AN AGENTIC DETECTION SYSTEMFORENTERPRISE AGENTIC AI SECURITY

C Li, P Hu, J Xu, B Ozbas, O Liu, C Van, M Li, W Zhou… · openreview.net · generating environments for agent testing in enterprises - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces ADR, an end-to-end enterprise security framework for detecting and responding to attacks against MCP-driven AI agents, comprising a lightweight telemetry sensor, a two-tier online detector (fast triage + deep reasoning with enterprise context), an offline evolutionary red-teaming engine, and a companion benchmark (ADR-Bench). - Why it's important: Enterprise AI agents operating through MCP autonomously execute file I/O, code generation, and API calls, but existing EDR tools and static guardrails are blind to the semantic causal chain — the user prompt, agent reasoning, and tool invocation sequence — that distinguishes malicious from benign agent behavior at scale. - How it does it: A sensor reconstructs full agent sessions from SQLite/JSONL local caches of agentic tools; a two-tier detector routes all events through cheap LLM triage (GPT-4o) and escalates suspicious ones to deep semantic reasoning (Claude Sonnet 4) augmented with three MCP-accessible enterprise context providers (source code, threat intel, policies);
