Methodology TVI-CoT builds on a standard MLLM (vision encoder + tokenizer + LLM) augmented with a grounding head and three special vocabulary tokens. The pipeline is: (1) the vision encoder produces visual features v ∈ ℝ^{N×d}; (2) the LLM autoregressively generates a sequence of ⟨Think⟩/⟨Look⟩/⟨Answer⟩ interleaved steps; (3) at each ⟨Look⟩ step, the grounding head computes attention weights αt = softmax(ht Wq (v Wk)^T / √d) where ht is the current hidden state encoding all prior reasoning; (4) the top-k=32 visual tokens are selected as the grounding set Gt, and the attended representation v^att_t = Σ αt,i · vi · Wv is linearly projected and concatenated into the LLM context for the next ⟨Think⟩ step. Training combines a standard autoregressive SFT loss with an auxiliary grounding loss (Lground) that maximizes attention on ground-truth bounding-box regions; for samples lacking explicit annotations, weak supervision from object detection or a pretrained grounding model is used. The combined loss L = LSFT + λ·Lground uses λ=0.1 validated on held-out performance. Training data (~150K samples) is constructed by: (a) 55K synthetic chains from LLaVA-178k using Gemini3-Pro to generate interleaved annotations and Qwen3-VL to validate; (b) 52K samples from Visual-CoT with augmented bounding boxes; (c) 43K from Zebra-CoT. Three critical design decisions: top-k hard selection (k=32 ablated, k=64 slightly degrades due to visual redundancy — well-justified); λ=0.1 grounding weight (validated, contributes +1.9%/+3.0% on MMMU/MathVerse); adaptive vs. fixed step count (adaptive substantially outperforms all fixed-step configurations — well-justified by Table 6). Key Results On 8 benchmarks evaluated against open-source 7–8B models and MLLM-based CoT methods, TVI-CoT (initialized from Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct) achieves: MMMU 64.3% (+6.1% over backbone), MMBench 84.7% (+1.3%), MathVerse 60.2% (+3.8%), MathVista 76.6% (+3.4%), ScienceQA 95.2% (+3.4%), MMStar 69.8% (+2.7%), AI2D 85.6% (+2.0%), MMT-Bench 65.4% (+2.1%). MathVista 76.6% exceeds Claude-Opus-4.1 (74.5%) at a fraction of the model scale. Geometry subcategories show the largest improvements: plane geometry +6.3% and solid geometry +6.5% on MathVerse; figure question answering +7.0% and geometry problem solving +5.7% on MathVista. At 32B scale (LoRA only): +1.7% average over Qwen3-VL-32B. Efficiency: 1.12× inference latency and 1.10× memory overhead over the backbone, versus Visual-CoT's 1.16× latency and 1.25× memory. The comparison to other CoT methods (VL-Rethinker-7B, Vision-R1-7B, VAPO-Thinker-7B) is partly confounded by different base models, but the consistent +2–6% over the same backbone is the cleanest signal. Proprietary model comparisons (GPT-5-high, Gemini-2.5-Pro) are included as reference points only; TVI-CoT does not match GPT-5-high (84.4% MMMU) or Gemini-2.5-Pro. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The genuine novelty is the reasoning-conditioned, adaptive re-attention over frozen visual tokens integrated into autoregressive CoT generation via learned control tokens trained end-to-end. This is architecturally distinct from Visual-CoT (which requires explicit bounding-box prediction and region cropping), Mint-CoT (which generates new interleaved visual tokens), and VAPO-Thinker (which performs a single verification step). The cross-attention mechanism itself is standard; the novel element is its placement inside the reasoning loop with adaptive invocation learned from data. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and informative: removal of interleaving drops MMMU from 64.3% to 56.6% (text→visual order) or 58.9% (visual→text), removal of grounding loss drops to 62.4%, and random grounding degrades to 51.2% — a dramatic -13.1% that confirms grounding quality is not incidental. Fixed-step ablations (Table 6) cleanly motivate adaptive allocation. Error bars and significance tests are not reported; no multiple-seed runs are disclosed. Code is claimed available at https://github.com/hulianyuyy/TVI-CoT. No pretrained model weights are stated as released. Two methodological concerns: (1) training data is derived from LLaVA-178k via Gemini3-Pro, and LLaVA-178k contains instruction-tuning data that may include ScienceQA samples — the paper does not audit for benchmark contamination, leaving the 95.2% ScienceQA figure potentially inflated; (2) the oracle grounding experiment (replacing predicted regions with ground-truth annotations) is conducted on only 100 samples, too small to support strong conclusions about the grounding ceiling. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Training requires 8×A100 80GB GPUs (36 hours); inference can run on any hardware supporting the 8B base model, which is commercially available. - Efficient: 1.12× inference latency and 1.10× memory over the backbone — modest overhead attributable to the grounding head computation and cached visual feature reuse without rerunning the encoder. - Effective: Consistent +2–6% gains across all 8 benchmarks over the same backbone with ablations confirming each component contributes; the strongest gains (geometry, +6–7%) align mechanistically with the iterative visual consultation hypothesis. - Scalable: Tested at 8B (full fine-tuning) and 32B (LoRA only); attention over N visual tokens scales linearly with sequence length but no experiments at higher resolution (larger N) or on multi-image inputs are reported, and no claims about scaling are tested beyond 32B. The paper delivers training code; trained model weights and training dataset are not explicitly confirmed as released. Strengths - The control-token integration adds minimal architectural complexity (one grounding head, three vocabulary tokens) to a standard MLLM, making the approach conceptually portable to other backbones. - Table 6 demonstrating that the adaptive mechanism (64.3% MMMU) substantially outperforms all fixed-step configurations — including the optimal fixed configuration (62.3% at step=1) — is a concrete and non-obvious result showing that task heterogeneity requires per-sample step allocation. - Figure 2 grounding pattern analysis provides empirical evidence that the model learns task-appropriate visual consultation: geometry problems average 2.83 ⟨Look⟩ operations versus 1.34 for math word problems, aligned with the accuracy gains in exactly those subcategories. - The efficiency comparison in Table 8 is rigorous: TVI-CoT achieves +17.0% accuracy over Visual-CoT with lower latency and memory, because soft attention over cached features is cheaper than bounding-box prediction plus region cropping. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors note in the Impact Statement that training data derived from public VQA datasets and Gemini-generated chains "may encode source biases that future work should audit," and that deployment in sensitive domains requires human oversight. No formal limitations section exists; the main text does not discuss benchmark contamination, the LoRA-constrained 32B result, or the absence of RL-trained comparisons on the same backbone — all material omissions. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Benchmark contamination: LLaVA-178k, the primary training source, is known to include ScienceQA instruction-tuning data; the paper does not check whether any ScienceQA test samples appear in the Gemini3-Pro-annotated training set, which could inflate the 95.2% result. - Missing RL baseline on the same backbone: VL-Rethinker and VAPO-Thinker use different base models. Applying GRPO-based reinforcement learning to Qwen3-VL-8B directly is a natural and strong baseline that is absent; without it, the contribution cannot be separated from the effect of the stronger backbone. - Oracle experiment undersized: The grounding ceiling experiment on 100 samples yields +3.0%, but the small sample and the absence of error bars make this figure unreliable. - No high-resolution or multi-image evaluation: The method's behavior when N (number of visual tokens) is large — as in document understanding or multi-panel figure benchmarks — is untested; the top-k=32 selection may become a bottleneck in these settings. - No analysis of failure modes: When the adaptive mechanism over-triggers or under-triggers ⟨Look⟩ operations, the effect on accuracy and reasoning coherence is not analyzed. Bottom Line TVI-CoT is a well-executed ICML 2026 paper with a clean mechanism, informative ablations, and reproducible geometry-task gains; practitioners working on multimodal reasoning should read it, but should verify the ScienceQA result against contamination and demand a comparison to RL-trained methods on the same backbone before treating the numbers as definitive.
Search Results 2606.08464 PDF
#59 — Innovative (67)

Time Series as Language: A Universal Tokenizer for General-Purpose Time Series Foundation Models

Y Zhang, R Qi, J Zheng, J Zhang, L Pan, J Yan · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces UniTok, a causally structured vector-quantized autoencoder that converts time series into discrete tokens, and UniTok-FM, a 129M-parameter foundation model pretrained via next-token prediction on those tokens, supporting zero-shot forecasting, prompt-boosted forecasting, few-shot generation, and few-shot classification through training-free in-context inference. - Why it's important: Existing time series foundation models are narrowly tailored to forecasting and require task-specific fine-tuning or separate models for generation and classification; a single architecture covering all three without task-specific heads would substantially reduce engineering overhead. - How it does it: UniTok enforces an incremental tokenization property—prefix tokens are independent of future observations—via prefix normalization (statistics computed from a fixed-length prefix), a progressive-resolution causal autoencoder (finer token resolution at early positions with limited causal context), and a structure-preserving reconstruction loss combining L1, adversarial, surrogate perceptual, and high-frequency wavelet terms; UniTok-FM then performs NTP over context windows containing multiple similar-pattern series, enabling in-context inference at test time. - The good: Using only 5 in-context prompt examples, a single unified model matches Chronos-Bolt on GIFT-Eval forecasting (MASE 0.824 vs 0.808), matches Diffusion-TS trained on 1,000 samples on generation predictive score (0.601 vs 0.582), and matches MOMENT on UCR-FewShot classification (0.755 vs 0.778) — all without task-specific fine-tuning. - The missing: The model is restricted to univariate series (multivariate uses channel independence only), and no error bars, confidence intervals, or significance tests are reported across any of the three task evaluations. - Read it or not: Read it if you work on time series foundation models or multi-task TS learning — the incremental tokenization property and multi-series in-context pretraining are principled ideas with immediate architectural implications; skip it if you only need a forecasting-optimized model, where Chronos-2 and TiRex remain substantially better.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (78) and Non-Obviousness (75) drive the score: the formally-defined incremental tokenization property, prefix normalization, and multi-series in-context NTP are principled new adaptations that unify forecasting/generation/classification under one model. Rigor (62) is limited — component ablations and multi-backbone tests are present, but no error bars/seeds/significance anywhere, reimplemented generation metrics, and no code release. Completeness (65) and Resilience (62) are moderate (univariate-only, asymmetric few-shot classification comparison). Practical Impact (58) reflects strong benchmarks but no released artifact and a real forecasting gap to Chronos-2/TiRex. Methodologically the kind of principled-idea paper the positive set favors.
empirical Novelty 78 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 65 Resilience 62 Impact 58
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Time series foundation model pretraining is fragmented: different models use different objectives (next-patch prediction, mask-and-reconstruction, fixed-horizon regression), different backbones (Transformers with TS-specific positional embeddings, xLSTMs), and almost all support only zero-shot forecasting. The root obstacle to applying NTP — which unified LLM pretraining — is that time series are continuous and unbounded, making tokenization non-trivial. Chronos, the closest prior work, applies point-wise uniform binning after mean-scale normalization, which loses temporal structure and restricts the model to forecasting. Practitioners building TS pipelines must currently maintain separate specialized models for forecasting, generation, and classification, and no prior TSFM supports generation or classification via in-context inference without fine-tuning. Key Idea The central insight is that a tokenizer satisfying the incremental tokenization property — where encoding any prefix yields exactly the prefix of the full token sequence — is the prerequisite for applying NTP to variable-length time series. Once this property holds, context windows can be formed by concatenating multiple same-pattern series, and the trained model inherits the ability to do in-context inference across diverse tasks (forecasting, generation, classification) simply by arranging prompt and query tokens differently at inference time, with no architectural changes. Methodology UniTok is a VQ-VAE using Finite Scalar Quantization (FSQ) with quantization levels [8, 8, 6, 5] (codebook size 1,940). The 113M-parameter encoder has S=4 blocks of causal convolutions (kernel size 9) and causal self-attention. Three design decisions distinguish it from image tokenizers: 1. Prefix normalization: scale statistics (β, γ) are extracted from a fixed prefix of length P1=8 or P2=128 depending on series length, rather than the full series, so normalization is not contaminated by future values. Scale statistics are encoded losslessly as 8 hexadecimal tokens each (bit-level Float32 representation), preserving absolute scale for classification tasks without learned embeddings. 2. Progressive-resolution causal autoencoder: uniform downsampling bottlenecks early tokens (which have small causal receptive fields) into a single quantized vector. The fix concatenates the first 2^(s-1) full-resolution vectors with the downsampled remainder at each block s, giving higher token density to early, information-poor positions. This is well-justified by the causal asymmetry analysis and supported by ablation. 3. Structure-preserving reconstruction loss (Eq. 8): L1 + adversarial + surrogate perceptual (discriminator latent space, avoiding the need for a separate pretrained feature extractor) + high-frequency wavelet loss (DWT at 2 finest scales, thresholded via median absolute deviation). Adaptive weight balancing follows the gradient-norm method of Esser et al. (VQGAN). The wavelet component is justified because L1 alone produces over-smooth reconstructions. UniTok-FM is a Qwen3 transformer (129M parameters, 8 layers, 16 heads, hidden size 1,024) trained from scratch via NTP on context windows of up to 800 tokens formed from 5–8 non-overlapping segments extracted from the same source series. The tokenizer is frozen during LM training. Pretraining data: union of GIFT-Pretrain (230B time points, 88 sub-datasets) and Chronos-Dataset (67 subsets), with test splits strictly excluded. Training: 100K steps, AdamW, global batch size 384, 4× A100 GPUs. At inference, all four task modes (zero-shot forecasting, prompt-boosted forecasting, few-shot generation, few-shot classification) are implemented by varying the prompt context and using either autoregressive generation or conditional likelihood evaluation — no weight updates required. Key Results Forecasting (GIFT-Eval, 97 tasks, aggregated MASE): UniTok-FM(ZeroShot) 0.851, UniTok-FM(Prompt) 0.824, vs. Chronos-Bolt 0.808, Moirai 0.901, Chronos 0.876, PatchTST (supervised) 0.849. Best models are Chronos-2 (0.698) and TiRex (0.716). On CRPS: UniTok-FM(Prompt) 0.573 vs Chronos-Bolt 0.574 — effectively tied. Generation (Stocks, ETTh, Energy, fMRI; 5 in-context prompts): predictive score 0.601 vs Diffusion-TS at 1,000 training samples (0.582); discriminative score 0.420, best among all models at 5-sample budget. Baselines trained at 1,000 samples achieve 0.096 discriminative score (SDFormer), indicating UniTok-FM with 5 prompts is weaker on realism but competitive on dynamics capture. Classification (UCR-FewShot, 53 datasets, average accuracy): UniTok-FM 0.755, MOMENT 0.778 (requires downstream classifier, pretrained on UCR), Mantis 0.840 (idem), UniTS 0.697. UniTok-FM wins on 10 of 53 datasets. Scaling: training loss decreases consistently from Small (14M) to Medium (26M) to Base (129M). Forecasting MASE improves from 0.873 → 0.858 → 0.851 (zero-shot). Classification accuracy improves from 0.698 → 0.741 → 0.755. Inference efficiency vs. Chronos (Base) on Jena Weather: UniTok-FM reduces inference time and memory at all trajectory counts (10–200) due to shorter token sequences, while also achieving lower MASE. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The incremental tokenization property as a formal design constraint, prefix normalization, and progressive-resolution causal quantization are genuinely new adaptations of image VQ-VAE to unbounded variable-length time series. The multi-series in-context NTP pretraining enabling training-free classification via conditional likelihood is new for TSFMs. The underlying components — VQ-VAE, FSQ, causal attention, wavelet losses, NTP — are individually well-established; the contribution is their integration under the incremental property constraint and the resulting multi-task generalization. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations (Table 5) isolate each of the three UniTok components across all three downstream tasks, demonstrating each contributes and identifying the trade-off (structure-preserving loss hurts forecasting marginally but is essential for generation). Architecture generality is tested across four LLM backbones (GPT2, Llama2, Gemma2, Qwen3). Scaling is tested across three backbone sizes. No error bars, confidence intervals, or significance tests are reported anywhere. Code and model weights are not mentioned as released, limiting direct reproducibility. The generation evaluation reimplements the predictive/discriminative metrics in PyTorch 2 with updated optimizers and 100K training steps rather than the original TensorFlow 1 implementation; this change in experimental protocol makes numeric comparisons to published baselines uncertain in both directions — the authors flag instability in the original implementation as motivation, which is fair, but comparisons should be interpreted cautiously. Test-set exclusion from pretraining is carefully documented. Deployability (EMPIRICAL) - Practical: Trains on 4× A100 GPUs; inference runs on a single A100. Standard commercially available hardware. - Efficient: Total model size ~242M parameters (113M tokenizer + 129M LM). Shorter token sequences than Chronos yield lower inference latency and memory at equivalent trajectory counts. Two-stage inference (encode → generate → decode) adds latency vs single-pass models. - Effective: Strong evidence across three tasks that the model captures underlying dynamics; it matches task-specific models in generation and classification without fine-tuning, and is within 2% MASE of Chronos-Bolt on forecasting with prompt boosting. Evidence is on established benchmarks with held-out test sets. - Scalable: Scaling tested only up to 129M parameters; no experiments at 500M or beyond. Maximum series length is 2,048 points; behavior on longer series (e.g., the 100K-point Electricity/15T histories in GIFT-Eval) requires truncation. The paper does not test this boundary condition explicitly. No code/model release is mentioned; the artifact currently exists at paper form. Strengths - The incremental tokenization property is formally defined (Eq. 2) and enforced throughout the architecture, providing a principled and verifiable design constraint rather than an informal heuristic. - Hexadecimal encoding of Float32 scale statistics is lossless, zero-parameter, and effectively unlimited in dynamic range — a simple but non-obvious solution to the unbounded-scale problem that avoids embedding table failures on novel domains. - Training-free few-shot classification via Bayesian likelihood comparison under class-specific prompts requires no task-specific training data, no fine-tuning, and no separate classifier head — this is a materially new capability for TSFMs. - The progressive-resolution design is well-motivated by a specific information-asymmetry argument (early causal tokens have limited receptive fields) and is validated by ablation showing degraded reconstruction at early sequence positions without it. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly note: (1) a performance gap remains versus task-specific SOTA models such as Chronos-2 and TiRex; (2) the model is univariate only, with multivariate handled via channel independence that ignores cross-channel dependencies. Both are stated directly in the conclusion. These are genuine limitations confronted honestly. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The few-shot classification comparison is structurally asymmetric: all competing TSFMs (UniTS, MOMENT, Mantis) were pretrained on corpora that include UCR training splits, while UniTok-FM explicitly excludes UCR. The paper notes this, but does not control for it — a version of UniTok-FM pretrained with UCR data would be a fairer head-to-head. - Prompt retrieval in prompt-boosted forecasting draws from the target series' own historical data or from the training split of other entities in the same dataset. The retrieval mechanism is described minimally; whether this constitutes implicit look-ahead contamination in some evaluation setups is not addressed. - The generation evaluation re-implements metrics (predictive/discriminative score) with different optimizer, training length, and framework than the original baselines. The claim of beating Diffusion-TS at 1,000 samples rests on this re-implementation; the numeric gap is modest and may not be robust. - No uncertainty quantification (error bars, seeds, significance tests) is reported for any metric across forecasting (97 tasks), generation (4 datasets), or classification (53 datasets). Aggregated scores could mask high per-task variance. - The model is never tested on series longer than 2,048 points despite GIFT-Eval containing histories exceeding 100K points; how truncation affects performance is not studied. - Code and model weights are not released (not stated), preventing external reproduction. Bottom Line UniTok-FM is the first time series foundation model to credibly unify forecasting, generation, and classification under a single NTP-pretrained model with training-free in-context inference; the architectural blueprint is directly reusable and the incremental tokenization framework is a principled contribution, but practitioners who need top forecasting accuracy should currently prefer Chronos-2 or TiRex, which outperform it by a substantial margin on GIFT-Eval.
Search Results 2606.09861 PDF
#60 — Innovative (67)

SCAIL-2: Unifying Controlled Character Animation with End-to-end In-Context Conditioning (32 ▲ )

Wenhao Yan, Fengjia Guo, Zhuoyi Yang, Jie Tang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 10 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SCAIL-2, an end-to-end video diffusion framework that unifies character image animation, character replacement, and multi-character animation into a single model by directly conditioning on driving video frames instead of extracted pose skeletons. - Why it's important: Intermediate representations like skeleton maps discard visual information (occlusions, object interactions, non-human body shapes) and fail entirely when characters are animals or when multiple characters overlap — limitations that prevent current systems from handling production-grade animation scenarios. - How it does it: The core move is reverse driving: a synthetic video (generated from a real video via a pose-driven model) serves as the driving input, while the original real video serves as the denoising target — bypassing synthetic artifacts in supervision. Task unification uses per-mode RoPE positional embeddings and in-context mask channels (environment switch + character binding slots) layered on top of the visual context. - The good: On Studio-Bench human evaluation, SCAIL-2 beats the strongest open-source competitor (Wan-Animate) by 65%/78.3%/68.3% win rates in Motion Consistency / Physical Plausibility / Identity Consistency for single-character animation, and achieves 93.3% win rate over MultiAnimate in multi-character Motion Consistency. - The missing: Human evaluation comparisons against the closest end-to-end competitor (DreamActor-M2, cited but described as closed-source) are absent; all key results rest on win-rate human studies with small sample sizes (60 pairwise comparisons per metric), and no inter-annotator agreement is reported. - Read it or not: Read if you work on video generation, character animation, or synthetic data pipelines; it is a practical, well-ablated system paper with genuine insights on reverse driving and regional DPO, but practitioners should note the preprint status and the absence of peer review.
Why this rank: Systems profile. Practical Impact (68) reflects promised weights plus a synthetic dataset subset and direct relevance to VFX/avatar production. Non-Obviousness (78) is high: reverse driving — using a pose-driven synthetic video as the driver while supervising against the real video to escape artifact contamination — is a genuinely non-obvious design that solves the root cause rather than patching symptoms. Novelty (72) spans a new system, dataset, and the Bias-Aware regional DPO. Rigor (60) is reduced by small human-eval samples (60 pairwise comparisons, no IAA or confidence intervals), the absence of any DreamActor-M2 comparison (the closest end-to-end competitor), and preprint status. Resilience (60) is bounded because capability is gated by the synthetic generator G's quality. 0.15*72+0.15*60+0.10*78+0.15*65+0.15*60+0.30*68 = 67.
systems Novelty 72 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 78 Completeness 65 Resilience 60 Impact 68
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Controlled character animation — transferring motion from a driving video to a reference character — currently depends on extracting intermediate representations (skeleton keypoints, segmentation masks, background crops) before feeding a video diffusion model. These intermediates create two failure modes: (1) skeleton maps are ambiguous under occlusion, depth ordering of interacting bodies, and non-human characters such as animals; (2) background masking for character replacement discards environment cues and constrains body-shape changes. The gap is acute for multi-character scenarios and for tasks that require composing animation with replacement, since no existing open-source model unifies these sub-tasks. The problem owners are researchers and practitioners in film VFX, entertainment, and avatar generation. Key Idea: The central insight is reverse driving: instead of using a pose-driven synthetic video as the denoising target (which bakes in generation artifacts), use it as the driving input and supervise the model against the original real video. This decouples the information source for motion (the synthetic driver, which conveys pose) from the quality target (the real video, which preserves realistic textures and interactions). Combined with direct VAE-space concatenation of driving frames, the model receives full visual information without any pose extraction at inference time, while its training signal remains artifact-free. Methodology: The framework fine-tunes Wan2.1-14B-I2V, a 14B-parameter image-to-video diffusion transformer using flow matching. The model receives three concatenated token streams: reference image zref, noisy video zt, and driving video zdriv, with zdriv spatially offset by ΔW on the width axis. Task unification proceeds in two complementary mechanisms. First, Mode-Specific Shifted RoPE: Animation Mode assigns zref temporal index 0 and zt indices [1,Tv] (temporal separation), while Replacement Mode assigns zref a vertical spatial shift ΔH_ref (spatial separation), signaling which background to adopt without any textual cue. Second, In-Context Mask Conditioning: 4(K+1) extra channels are concatenated to the context, comprising one environment-switch channel (complement of character mask union) and K=6 binding-slot channels (SAM3-derived character masks indicating motion-to-character routing); critically, masks are derived from inputs only, never from ground-truth frames, preserving end-to-end behavior. Training uses a three-objective decomposition: O1 motion binding (which driving character maps to which reference character), O2 environment weaving (read background from EI or Ey), O3 universal transfer (pose disentangled from identity). The MotionPair-60K dataset (59,376 pairs) is synthesized via an agentic loop combining SCAIL and Wan-Animate as animation generators, MoCha for replacement, VLM-based Quality Checking (Gemini), and SAM3 segmentation. Pretraining runs 3,500 steps at batch 128, lr 1e-5, on 64 H100 GPUs for ~1 week using FSDP-2. Post-training applies Bias-Aware DPO: preference pairs are constructed by propagating extra pose-estimation errors through the pipeline (using the weaker ViTPose as degraded estimator) to produce negative samples that share global motion but have worse finger articulation than positives; DPO is applied with a regional mask covering hand bounding boxes only, with LoRA rank 128 adapters (backbone frozen), 400 steps at lr 1e-4, λ=0.01 SFT anchor, β=5000. Key Results: On Studio-Bench cross-identity single-character animation, SCAIL-2 wins vs. SCAIL by 68.3%/68.3%/46.7% (Motion Consistency / Physical Plausibility / Identity Consistency) and vs. Wan-Animate by 65%/78.3%/68.3%. Against proprietary Kling 3.0 Motion Control, win rates are 28.3%/25%/53.3%, suggesting competitive but not dominant performance versus closed commercial systems. Multi-character animation: 93.3% win rate over MultiAnimate in Motion Consistency, 86.7% in Identity Isolation. Character replacement: 67.9% win in Environment Integration over both MoCha and Wan-Animate. On pose-driven automatic metrics (Studio-Bench, self-driven): SSIM 0.6453 / PSNR 19.09 / FVD 287.11, best among all open-source baselines (SCAIL: 0.6407/19.08/309.63, SteadyDancer: 0.6386/18.40/332.20, Wan-Animate: 0.6340/18.62/305.31). On X-Dance Video-Bench: Imaging Quality 4.43 / Appearance Consistency 4.38, highest among compared methods. Qualitative ablations show end-to-end driving substantially outperforms pose-driven driving for complex multi-character interactions. Notably, the model generalizes zero-shot to 3D body mesh inputs (SAM3D-Body) that were never seen in training, achieving the best FVD among all conditions. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW METHOD. The reverse driving paradigm (using synthetic video as driver rather than target) is a genuinely non-obvious design choice that solves the artifact-contamination problem in end-to-end training without requiring real paired data. In-context mask conditioning and mode-specific RoPE are incremental given prior in-context animation designs (SCAIL), but their joint formulation for task unification is novel. Bias-Aware DPO is a concrete and useful adaptation of diffusion-DPO to synthesize-and-degrade preference pairs targeting a specific anatomical region. MotionPair-60K addresses a real data gap. Standard components: Wan2.1 backbone, FSDP-2 training, SAM3 segmentation, DPO optimization. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and substantive: driving mode comparison (end-to-end vs. pose-driven for interactions), network module ablations (environment switch, mode-specific RoPE, binding slots, binding slot inference), data composition ablations (animation-only vs. replacement-only vs. combined), and post-training ablations (base vs. SFT vs. DPO). Quantitative ablations in Appendix D cover multi-character metrics. No error bars or inter-annotator agreement statistics are reported for human evaluation (each comparison involves 60 pairwise ratings, which is borderline for statistical reliability). Code and model weights are promised at the project page (https://teal024.github.io/SCAIL-2/), along with a large subset of synthetic data — not yet confirmed available as of the preprint date. The paper is an arXiv preprint (v2, June 2026), not peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS (a trained model with an evaluation campaign). - Practical: The model runs on standard cloud GPU infrastructure (64 H100s for training); inference on a single H100 is not stated but the 14B parameter count suggests multi-GPU or quantization may be required for consumer deployment. - Efficient: Training takes ~1 week on 64 H100s; dataset synthesis required running SCAIL and Wan-Animate over ~60K video pairs via VLM-in-the-loop quality checking, which is expensive to replicate. Inference cost relative to alternatives is not reported. - Effective: Human evaluation demonstrates it solves the stated problem of end-to-end motion transfer under complex interactions where pose-driven baselines fail — the ablation comparing driving modes confirms the gain is attributable to the end-to-end paradigm rather than architectural differences. - Scalable: No scaling experiment is reported; the method's reliance on the quality of the synthetic generator G means capability is bounded by the best available pose-driven model — improvement is positional, not fundamental, at this axis. The paper delivers trained model weights and a dataset subset (pending release). Strengths: - The reverse driving idea is conceptually clean and directly addresses the root cause (synthetic artifact contamination) rather than adding noise-correction postprocessing. - Task unification via paired RoPE/mask signals actually produces measurable synergistic gains (replacement data helps animation and vice versa per ablation Fig. 9d/e), not just theoretical elegance. - Bias-Aware DPO's bootstrapped error propagation is an efficient way to construct preference data for fine-grained motion without human labeling, and the regional masking focuses signal exactly where the prior is wrong. - Zero-shot generalization to unseen driving representations (3D body mesh, animal characters) with no degradation demonstrates genuine capability rather than benchmark overfitting. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly state that end-to-end training has strict dependence on large-scale, high-quality paired training data, and that data fidelity is bounded by the generator G's capability. They acknowledge that reliable positive samples for fine-grained regions (fingers) remain hard to obtain. Facial animation and lip-syncing are called out as future directions not covered. These are confronted honestly rather than minimized. Analyst-Identified Gaps: Human evaluation sample sizes (60 pairwise comparisons per metric) are small for claimed percentage differences — a 3% difference in win rate is within noise at this sample size, yet no confidence intervals are reported. DreamActor-M2 is the most relevant prior end-to-end work but is described as closed-source; no comparison is attempted even for qualitative outputs, leaving the most important baseline gap unaddressed. The VLM-based quality checker (Gemini) that filters synthetic data has a 30% discard rate, but the effect of data quality threshold on final model performance is not ablated. The preference DPO dataset contains only ~1K pairs, which is very small relative to pretraining; whether DPO generalizes beyond the Wan-Animate distribution used to generate it is not tested. Human evaluation is pairwise win-rate (three annotators implied per figure legend formatting), but annotator count, annotation protocol, and IAA are not disclosed. Bottom Line: SCAIL-2 is a well-executed system paper with a genuinely useful insight (reverse driving) and strong ablation discipline; practitioners working on video character animation will find the end-to-end formulation, synthetic data pipeline, and regional DPO directly applicable, making it worth reading despite its preprint status and the absence of a DreamActor-M2 comparison.
Recommended 2606.10804 PDF
#61 — Innovative (67)

CoVEBench: Can Video Editing Models Handle Complex Instructions? (44 ▲ )

Jiangtao Wu, Jiaming Wang, Yiwen He, Yuanxing Zhang, Shihao Li, Dunyuan Liu, Xuedong Zhao, Jialu Chen, Zekun Moore Wang, Jiaheng Liu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 9 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces CoVEBench, a compositional video editing benchmark comprising 416 source videos, 626 multi-point editing instructions (averaging ~3 atomic operations each), and 9,990 fine-grained MLLM-judged checklist items spanning 7 editing dimensions and 19 categories. - Why it's important: Existing benchmarks evaluate isolated single-point edits with average prompt lengths under 9 words and coarse global metrics (CLIP score), which cannot diagnose whether each operation in a multi-step instruction succeeds or fails, nor whether unrelated content is correctly preserved. - How it does it: Complex instructions are decomposed into per-operation, per-element verifiable questions in four formats (AB-MCQ, Single-TF, Dual-TF, Score-MCQ 1–10), evaluated by Qwen3.5-122B-A10B under controlled source-video visibility, explicitly separating edit execution, physical plausibility, and preservation into orthogonal signals. - The good: The MLLM judge achieves >93% agreement with human annotations (Cohen's κ = 0.84) on objective questions, and UAS-derived system-level rankings exactly match human rankings — the benchmark measures what it claims to measure. - The missing: Only 2 closed-source models are evaluated, the benchmark covers text-only instructions (no reference images, bounding boxes, or audio), and no training dataset or improved method is provided. - Read it or not: Essential reading for researchers building or evaluating video editing models; the diagnostic granularity and failure-mode characterization are substantially more informative than any prior benchmark in this space.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (68) and Completeness (70) are the strengths: human-alignment validation is thorough for a benchmark (κ=0.84 on objective questions, UAS rankings exactly matching human rankings, <0.5-point repeat stability), limitations are stated honestly, and the falsifiable demonstration that Text-Video CLIP compresses 8 models into a 1.16-point range while UAS spans 47 points is a concrete argument for necessity. Practical Impact (70) reflects public release on GitHub/HuggingFace and immediate diagnostic utility. Novelty (65) lies in the three-tier UAS/IFS/VRS decomposition and the joint-vs-sequential editing finding rather than the LLM-as-judge mechanism. Resilience (65) is tempered by only 2 closed-source models, uncharacterized judge bias, and no IAA for the manual refinement. A clean, useful contribution.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 68 Non-Obv 62 Completeness 70 Resilience 65 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Instruction-guided video editing in real-world use involves compositional requests: a single prompt might simultaneously ask for subject replacement, camera motion change, object addition, and background modification while requiring all unspecified elements to remain intact. Prior benchmarks (EditBoard, VE-Bench, FiVE, VEditBench, IVEBench) use average prompt lengths of 7–9 words covering isolated single-point edits, and rely on global similarity metrics such as CLIP score. As shown in Table 6, Text-Video CLIP produces scores ranging from only 22.15 to 23.31 across 8 models — near-constant variance that contradicts human judgments and masks model-specific failures entirely. No benchmark simultaneously covers compositional editing, camera and motion editing, visual effects, and fine-grained per-operation diagnostic metrics. The gap between evaluation capability and real-user workflow demands motivates CoVEBench. Key Idea The core technical insight is that compositional instruction evaluation requires explicit decomposition into per-operation, per-element binary or scored questions, evaluated under controlled visibility conditions — some questions access only the edited video (for checking edit execution and physical plausibility without contamination from source-video memory), while others access both videos (for comparing preservation). This decomposition enables strict Union Accuracy (UAS), which scores 1 only if both the execution and realism sub-questions for a given edit point are correct, cleanly separating instruction following from visual quality in a way that a global similarity metric cannot. Methodology Data construction proceeds in three stages. (1) Video collection: 416 videos (≥480p, 3–21s) sourced from Pexels, Mixkit, Vript, UltraVideo, and LMArena, filtered by visual quality, deduplication, and human review. (2) Instruction generation: a 7-dimension taxonomy (Subject, Background, Camera, Style, Motion, Position, Special Effects) with 83 manually defined category combinations is used; MLLMs (GPT-5, Gemini-3.1-Pro, Qwen3-VL-plus, Doubao-Seed-1.8) are prompted with 5 dynamically rotated combinations and few-shot examples to select the best fit and generate tailored instructions; 67.2% of outputs are retained after manual filtering. Prompt diversity is quantified: TF-IDF cosine similarity across 195,625 pairs has mean 0.0168, median 0.0106. (3) Checklist generation: Gemini-3-Flash, GPT-5, and DeepSeek-V4-Pro decompose instructions into questions tagged as Execution Accuracy, Physical Logic, or Semantic Preservation using four question formats with strict visibility rules. Evaluation uses 11 metrics across 3 dimensions: Instruction Compliance (UAS, IFS, VRS, all MLLM-judged), Video Quality (VQR via VisualQuality-R1, AES via Aesthetic Predictor v2.5, MSM via optical-flow field consistency, TQ via DOVER++), and Video Fidelity (SEM via instruction-aware MLLM checklist, SSIM, MF via CoTracker with Hungarian matching, SRC via GroundingDINO+SAM2+DINOv2 cosine similarity). Key design decisions: SSIM excludes background-change cases to avoid penalizing intended modifications; SRC excludes camera-motion and global stylization cases; SEM is instruction-aware so that style-transfer-induced color shifts are not penalized as preservation failures. Key Results Ten models are evaluated (2 closed-source: Wan2.7, HappyHorse1.0; 8 open-source). Best UAS: Wan2.7 at 56.89, HappyHorse1.0 at 55.18; best open-source UAS: OmniWeaving at 30.14, Kiwi at 29.03 — roughly half the closed-source scores. The gap between IFS and UAS is diagnostic: Wan2.7 achieves IFS=82.02 but UAS=56.89, indicating that passing the visual-quality bar (VRS) — not raw instruction execution — is the primary bottleneck. VACE scores only UAS=9.69 despite IFS=22.92. Text-Video CLIP scores (Table 6) span 22.15–23.31 with rankings contradicting human judgment, concretely validating the inadequacy of global metrics. Joint editing outperforms sequential decomposition by a substantial margin: UAS 30.63% vs 23.70%, IFS 56.56% vs 48.68%, VRS 56.48% vs 51.62%. Performance degrades monotonically with more edit points and longer instructions across all models. Camera control, motion editing, and subject replacement are the hardest categories; style transfer and background modification are easiest. The MLLM judge (Qwen3.5-122B-A10B) achieves >93% human agreement with Cohen's κ=0.84; all 11 metrics achieve >85% pairwise alignment with human preferences (Table 4). Inference efficiency varies 20×: Kiwi at 0.651 s/frame (31.5 GB VRAM) vs VACE at 12.827 s/frame (113 GB VRAM). Novelty Classification NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK; NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The three-tier metric structure separating execution, quality, and preservation within a compositional editing setting is genuinely novel relative to prior benchmarks. The specific empirical finding that joint multi-operation editing outperforms sequential single-point decomposition is new and practically significant. The MLLM-as-judge approach for video editing evaluation is an engineering innovation on top of known LLM-as-judge techniques, not a fundamental methodological contribution. Rigor & Reproducibility Metric stability is validated: repeated evaluation on OmniWeaving and Kiwi shows score variation <0.5 points. Human alignment is validated on 100 cases from 2 models (Wan and Kiwi) for IFS, and on 60 pairwise samples for all other metrics. System-level rankings from UAS exactly match human rankings. No error bars or significance tests are reported for main benchmark scores. Only 2 closed-source models are evaluated, making the closed-vs-open-source comparison statistically thin. The MLLM judge's own biases (e.g., category-specific over/under-scoring) are not characterized. Dataset and evaluation code are publicly released on GitHub and HuggingFace. The paper is a preprint; results are not peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check) EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: Model evaluation requires a single NVIDIA H200 GPU (commercially available but expensive); the MLLM judge (Qwen3.5-122B-A10B at 122B parameters) requires substantial memory and is not lightweight to self-host. - Efficient: Evaluation cost is high — 9,990 MLLM-judged checklist items per benchmark run plus 11 automated metrics including CoTracker (point tracking), SAM2+GroundingDINO (masking), and DINOv2 (feature extraction); not a routine eval loop. - Effective: The benchmark diagnoses failures at individual edit-operation granularity, validated against human judgment with Cohen's κ=0.84; it measures what it claims to measure across three separable dimensions. - Scalable: Dataset tested only at current scale (416 videos, 626 instructions, 3–21s clips); no analysis of evaluation cost or benchmark validity at 10× scale; checklist generation and MLLM judging costs grow linearly with dataset size. The contribution is benchmark + evaluation code; no trained model, training dataset, or improved editing method is released. Strengths - The three-metric decomposition (UAS/IFS/VRS) cleanly separates edit execution from visual quality, revealing that model failures differ in kind (omission vs. artifact introduction), a diagnostic capability unavailable in any prior benchmark. - The instruction-aware SEM metric for preservation is conceptually sounder than global SSIM — it identifies which elements should remain unchanged and penalizes only unintended modifications, distinguishing intentional style changes from collateral damage. - The empirical validation against human judgment is thorough for a dataset paper: κ=0.84 on objective questions, <0.5-point score stability on repeated evaluation, and pairwise ranking alignment validated across all 11 metrics. - The concrete demonstration (Table 6) that Text-Video CLIP collapses 8 models into a 1.16-point range while UAS spans 47 points provides a falsifiable argument for why the new benchmark is necessary. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly note: (1) scope is restricted to text-guided instructions, excluding multi-modal control signals such as reference images, bounding boxes, or audio; (2) no solution model or LLM-driven agent is proposed; (3) no paired large-scale training dataset is provided for fine-tuning against the benchmark tasks. These are stated directly and honestly. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The claim that "closed-source models tend to show stronger instruction compliance" rests on exactly 2 closed-source models — insufficient for a reliable generalization. - The 32.8% checklist rejection rate during manual filtering is reported, but the distribution of rejected question types (execution vs. realism vs. preservation) is not analyzed; systematic gaps in coverage are possible. - Human alignment is validated on 100 cases from only 2 models (Wan and Kiwi) for IFS, and 60 pairwise samples for other metrics — small samples that may not generalize across the 10-model evaluation set or across different editing categories. - Temporal and complexity analyses (Figure 4) cover only 3 models (InsV2V, Kiwi, Lucy); whether the monotonic degradation with edit points holds for closed-source models is untested. - No inter-annotator agreement statistic is reported for the 6-annotator manual refinement process, leaving checklist consistency uncharacterized. - The MLLM judge (Qwen3.5-122B-A10B) may have been trained on video editing content from the same sources used to construct the benchmark; potential judge bias by editing category is not analyzed. - All source videos are 3–21 seconds; generalization to professional long-form editing scenarios is not evaluated. Bottom Line Practitioners building or evaluating video editing models gain clear, immediate value from CoVEBench — it is the first benchmark with diagnostic granularity matching real compositional workflows, its validation against human judgment is credible, and its failure-mode analysis exposes specific bottlenecks (camera control, subject replacement, physical plausibility) that aggregate metrics hide entirely.

#62 — Innovative (67)

Direct 3D-Aware Object Insertion via Decomposed Visual Proxies (22 ▲ )

Jingbo Gong, Yikai Wang, Yushi Lan, Yuhao Wan, Ziheng Ouyang, Rui Zhao, Ming-Ming Cheng, Qibin Hou, Chen Change Loy · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 8 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces DIRECT, a diffusion-based framework that inserts a reference object into a background image at a user-specified 6-DoF pose by decomposing conditioning into geometry (RGB-rendered coarse 3D proxy), appearance (original reference image), and scene context (background), injected through separate LoRA pathways within a fine-tuned FLUX.1-Fill-dev backbone. - Why it's important: All existing high-fidelity object insertion methods operate in the 2D image plane and cannot place objects at precise, user-controlled 3D orientations — a critical gap for AR, virtual staging, and e-commerce photography where spatial alignment is non-negotiable. - How it does it: A feed-forward image-to-3D model (TRELLIS) lifts the reference into a 3D Gaussian proxy that the user rotates interactively; the rendered RGB proxy provides dense, semantically unambiguous geometric guidance, while modality-specific LoRA adapters with separate RoPE embeddings prevent the proxy's degraded texture from overwriting the high-fidelity reference appearance. - The good: On the authors' 200-sample benchmark, DIRECT (FLUX) achieves PSNR 23.09 / SSIM 0.871 / LPIPS 0.147 / CLIP-I 0.959 / DINO 0.936 / Matching Error 17.8 px, beating every baseline on every metric simultaneously, including appearance-focused and geometry-focused alternatives. - The missing: The evaluation benchmark is constructed from the same two datasets (SA-1B, MVImgNet) used for training with the same automated pipeline, and no independent third-party benchmark or human perceptual study is provided. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers in controllable generation, reference-based editing, or 3D-aware image synthesis — the decomposed injection design and Real-Target/Synthetic-Source data strategy are transferable ideas with clear ablation support.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Unlike most of this cluster, this is peer-reviewed (ICML 2026), which lifts Rigor (65) despite no released code/weights and single-run numbers. Non-Obviousness (72) and Novelty (70) are anchored by the RGB-proxy-as-geometry insight (resolving the 180° depth/normal ambiguity for symmetric objects) and the decomposed triplet injection (separate LoRA adapters + RoPE) that verifiably solves condition entanglement, plus the Real-Target/Synthetic-Source data pipeline. Completeness (68) is strong — honest failure cases (Fig. 11) and direct limitation acknowledgment. Resilience (62) is reduced by the closed evaluation loop (benchmark from the same two training datasets) and the Matching-Error metric circularity (it rewards following the TRELLIS proxy, as ZeroComp's ME=5.2 with poor LPIPS exposes). Impact (62) reflects an interactive tool with no public release and single-object scope.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 68 Resilience 62 Impact 62
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Object insertion — placing a reference object seamlessly into a target scene — is a core capability for virtual staging, e-commerce, AR, and creative design. Diffusion-based methods (AnyDoor, InsertAnything, IMPRINT, SEELE) achieve strong identity preservation and scene harmonization but are confined to 2D: they cannot control where in 3D space an object is oriented. Text-guided models hallucinate geometrically plausible but incorrect poses from spatially ambiguous descriptions. Parametric 3D-aware models (Object3DIT) attempt rotation control via scalar angles but lack dense spatial correspondence, failing to translate low-dimensional parameters into correct pixel-level deformations on complex geometries. The specific gap is: no existing system accepts a single reference image, an arbitrary background, and a user-specified 6-DoF pose and produces a composite with simultaneously correct 3D orientation and preserved fine-grained appearance. Key Idea The single core insight is to use an RGB-rendered coarse 3D proxy as the geometry conditioning signal rather than depth maps, normal maps, or scalar pose parameters. Depth and normals are invariant to 180° rotations for symmetric objects (a road sign upright vs. inverted looks identical in both), so they cannot convey semantic orientation. The RGB proxy retains textural cues that disambiguate pose. Crucially, because the 3D reconstruction degrades texture fidelity (blurring, artifacts), naively conditioning on the proxy corrupts output quality. The key move is to then treat appearance and geometry as orthogonal sources and route them through separate, non-interfering adaptation pathways — modality-specific LoRA adapters and distinct RoPE positional embeddings — so the model can extract structure from the proxy without inheriting its degraded appearance. Methodology The pipeline has three main stages. (1) 3D Proxy Lifting: The reference object is segmented (background removed), then fed to TRELLIS to produce a 3D Gaussian representation P. In a Viser-based interactive viewer, the user rotates and translates P over the background canvas, finalizing a 6-DoF pose ξ and insertion mask M. P is rendered at ξ to yield the geometry image Igeo (RGB, not depth). (2) Triplet Condition Assembly: Three signals are formed — Appearance (original Iref), Geometry (rendered Igeo), and Context (full background Ibg encoded globally by a frozen SIGLIP encoder into cglobal tokens). Locally, Igeo is pasted into Ibg within a dilated mask to form Ilocal, which is cropped around the insertion region for the inpainting backbone. (3) Decomposed Injection into FLUX.1-Fill-dev: The model processes a unified token sequence Z = [cglobal, zt, zref, zgeo] through DiT blocks. Separate LoRA adapters (rank 128) handle each condition in the self-attention layers; separate RoPE embeddings spatially isolate appearance and geometry tokens. The objective is rectified flow matching with reference dropout (p=0.1) for classifier-free guidance on appearance. Two critical training tricks: (a) Shape-Decomposed Mask Augmentation replaces the precise GT mask with a randomly sampled external real-object mask, preventing "shape leakage" where the model bypasses the geometry condition and simply fills the GT mask boundary; (b) Progressive resolution training: 200k steps at 512² on 4 A100s, then 40k steps at ~1024² on 8 A100s. Training data construction uses a "Real-Target, Synthetic-Source" strategy: for SA-1B images, a Qwen3-VL + SAM-3 agent filters complete, unoccluded objects, and Qwen-Image-Edit synthesizes a novel-view reference at a random angle while the real image anchors the GT target — avoiding reliance on synthetic 3D datasets with simplified backgrounds. This yields 65k pairs from SA-1B combined with 93k quality-filtered MVImgNet samples (160k total). For training, geometric alignment of Igeo to each GT is computed offline: TRELLIS reconstructs P, VGGT estimates relative pose from 6 canonical renders + Igt, a coarse Sim(3) alignment maps the camera pose to the proxy frame, then differentiable Gaussian Splatting refinement minimizes an L1 silhouette loss to yield the precise rendering pose. Key Results On the authors' 200-sample benchmark (100 MVImgNet + 100 SA-1B, no training overlap, SA-1B subset manually verified): DIRECT (FLUX) achieves PSNR 23.09 / SSIM 0.871 / LPIPS 0.147 / CLIP-I 0.959 / DINO 0.936 / Matching Error 17.8 px vs. the strongest composite baseline TRELLIS+InsertAnything at 22.00 / 0.843 / 0.217 / 0.935 / 0.902 / 19.6. Object3DIT+InsertAnything has ME=98.9 px, confirming near-zero pose control. The SD-based variant similarly dominates both SD baselines. Pose-change stratification (0–45°, 45–90°, 90–135°, 135–180°) shows stable SSIM (0.864–0.881), CLIP-I (0.956–0.967), and ME (15.7–19.4) across bins — no degradation with larger rotations. Ablation (Table 3): Hybrid data provides the largest identity/pose gain (CLIP-I 0.904→0.943, ME 26.9→22.7); Shape-Decomposed Mask Augmentation provides the largest perceptual quality gain (LPIPS 0.190→0.155, ME 20.7→19.0). The ZeroComp intrinsic-guided baseline achieves ME=5.2 (best) but PSNR=20.66 / LPIPS=0.297 / CLIP-I=0.892 — confirming the geometry-appearance trade-off that DIRECT resolves. End-to-end inference: 9.54s, 10.05 GB GPU memory. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The decomposed triplet injection (separate LoRA adapters + separate RoPE per condition stream within FLUX.1) is the primary novel architectural contribution — it addresses a concrete, documented failure mode (condition entanglement) that naive LoRA concatenation does not solve. Using RGB-rendered proxy rather than depth/normals for geometry guidance is a specific, well-motivated design choice backed by ablation. The Real-Target/Synthetic-Source data pipeline is a novel data engineering contribution for this task. The overall task is not new (Object3DIT addressed a version), but the formulation via dense visual proxies and decomposed injection is a substantive methodological advance over sparse scalar control. The FLUX backbone, LoRA, TRELLIS, SIGLIP, and RoPE are all existing components; their combination is non-trivial but not purely additive. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are incremental and clearly structured (Table 3), attributing contribution to each component. No error bars or multiple-seed experiments are reported. No code, model weights, or dataset are released at paper time (peer-reviewed at ICML 2026). The evaluation benchmark is derived from the same datasets (SA-1B, MVImgNet) and the same automated pipeline used during training; the SA-1B test subset is manually verified but is structurally similar to training pairs by construction — this creates a mild Goodhart's Law concern. The Matching Error metric is introduced by the authors (no prior standard), uses TRELLIS for training and the same TRELLIS proxy as the geometric target for ME computation — a potential circular dependency: any method that closely follows Igeo from TRELLIS will trivially minimize ME regardless of perceptual quality, as evidenced by ZeroComp's ME=5.2 with poor LPIPS. Baselines are all cascaded rather than end-to-end trained for this task, which may structurally disadvantage them. Deployability (EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS) Profile: EMPIRICAL/SYSTEMS — the paper builds and evaluates an interactive inference system. - Practical: Runs on a single A100 at ~10 GB peak memory; 9.54s end-to-end; accessible to studio and developer environments but not consumer hardware. - Efficient: The 3D proxy stage (5.05s, TRELLIS) runs before user interaction, so perceived 2D generation latency is only 4.21s — faster than baselines' 6.03s diffusion stage; total overhead vs. cascade is modest. - Effective: Achieves the best numbers on all six metrics on the test split and produces visually convincing results including text preservation, plausible shadows, and reflections; effectiveness is hard-bounded by upstream 3D reconstruction quality. - Scalable: Handles single-object insertion only; behavior at batch scale, in video, or with multiple simultaneous objects is not tested or claimed. The system delivers an interactive web-based 3D viewer but no public code or model release. Strengths - The decomposed injection (separate LoRA adapters + RoPE) directly and verifiably solves condition entanglement: the ablation comparison against vanilla LoRA concatenation (Fig. 9, Table 3) shows the failure mode clearly and the fix works. - RGB-based geometry proxy elegantly resolves the semantic ambiguity of depth/normal maps for symmetric objects (Fig. 8) — a practical insight that generalizes to any dense-proxy-based editing system. - The Real-Target/Synthetic-Source data pipeline sidesteps the need for artist-created multi-view captures by synthesizing reference views from in-the-wild single images, enabling diverse training without 3D asset creation at scale. - Shape-Decomposed Mask Augmentation addresses a non-obvious training artifact (shape leakage) and delivers the largest single-component LPIPS improvement (0.190→0.155) in the ablation. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state: (1) Geometric accuracy is bounded by upstream image-to-3D quality — severe topology errors (e.g., a rectangular plaque reconstructed as a square) propagate to the output, and Fig. 11 illustrates this failure honestly. (2) Physical effects (lighting, reflections, occlusion) are handled via learned context guidance rather than explicit physical simulation, so results are "visually plausible" rather than physically correct. (3) The training dataset inherits category imbalance and collection bias from SA-1B and MVImgNet; residual construction errors from imperfect masks or synthesis artifacts may remain. The authors confront limitations directly with concrete failure cases rather than minimizing them. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Closed evaluation loop: The 200-sample test benchmark is constructed by the same automated pipeline and drawn from the same two source datasets as training. Even with manual verification, there is no evaluation on a completely disjoint dataset (CO3D, OmniObject3D, or real user-captured scenes) — generalization beyond the training distribution is unverified. - No user study: For an interactive creative tool, perceptual quality as judged by human raters (graphic designers, photographers) is more decision-relevant than PSNR/SSIM. Its absence leaves the practical value claim unsupported. - Baseline asymmetry: All baselines are cascaded systems not end-to-end trained for pose-controllable insertion. An end-to-end ablation that disables the decomposed injection but retains the same training data and backbone would better isolate the architectural contribution from the training data advantage. - Matching Error metric circularity: ME measures how closely the output matches Igeo (rendered by TRELLIS) via MASt3R dense correspondences. Methods that more aggressively follow the TRELLIS proxy will score lower ME regardless of appearance fidelity — ZeroComp's ME=5.2 vs. DIRECT's 17.8 is an artifact of this: ZeroComp is geometrically compliant but visually poor. The metric does not distinguish between "correct pose" and "copied proxy texture." - Single-object scope: Multi-object insertion and composition, common in the stated application domains (staging, e-commerce), are not addressed. Bottom Line A practitioner in controllable generation or 3D-aware image editing gains real, reusable ideas from this paper — particularly the decomposed injection design and the data construction strategy — but the evaluation's reliance on an in-house benchmark and lack of human studies warrants caution before adopting the quantitative claims as definitive.
Recommended 2606.06601 PDF
#63 — Innovative (67)

UniSHARP: Universal Sharp Monocular View Synthesis (13 ▲ )

Meixi Song, Dizhe Zhang, Hao Ren, Ruiyang Zhang, Bo Du, Ming-Hsuan Yang, Lu Qi · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 8 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces UniSHARP, a feedforward 3D Gaussian Splatting framework that performs monocular novel view synthesis from a single image across perspective, wide-FoV, fisheye, and panoramic cameras in one unified model. - Why it's important: Existing monocular 3DGS methods (SHARP, Flash3D) are hard-coded to the pinhole camera model and fail entirely on the wide-FoV and omnidirectional cameras that dominate AR/VR headsets, action cameras, and embodied AI platforms. - How it does it: It replaces image-plane Gaussian coordinates with a ray-distance space (per-pixel unit rays × radial depth) that is agnostic to projection type, then trains one shared network on a mixture of all camera types simultaneously. - The good: On panoramic NVS, UniSHARP achieves 29.244 PSNR on HM3D versus Matrix3D's 23.398 — a 5.8 dB gain — while running 12.5× faster at inference time. - The missing: Most evaluation sets (WildRGB-D, DL3DV, RealEstate10K, HM3D, OmniRooms) overlap with the training distribution; genuinely out-of-domain tests are limited to Tanks and Temples (perspective) and Replica (panoramic), making true generalization harder to assess. - Read it or not: Researchers building NVS pipelines for omnidirectional or action-camera imagery should read this; those focused on standard perspective NVS can treat it as a useful reference that their methods now need to outperform.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (74) and Non-Obviousness (74) lead: the ray-distance Gaussian parameterization is the right abstraction — camera-model-agnostic by construction rather than by engineering workaround — enabling a single model across perspective, wide-FoV, fisheye, and panoramic cameras, and the panoramic gains (+5.8 dB on HM3D, +4.8 dB on Replica) are large enough to be practically meaningful. Rigor (64) reflects clean component ablations (target-view rendered depth identified as the single largest loss contributor) but single-run numbers with no error bars and, critically, most evaluation sets being in-distribution — only Tanks-and-Temples and Replica are genuinely held out, and OmniRooms-Wide is a projection of training data. Resilience (62) is bounded by this generalization concern and the missing mixed-vs-camera-specific training ablation. Completeness (65) and Impact (62) reflect honest scope statements and a released dataset/model but no inference code.
empirical Novelty 74 Rigor 64 Non-Obv 74 Completeness 65 Resilience 62 Impact 62
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Methods like SHARP and Flash3D learn to regress 3D Gaussian primitives from a single perspective image, but they parameterize Gaussians in normalized image-plane coordinates derived from a pinhole camera model. This makes them geometrically incompatible with wide-FoV, fisheye, and equirectangular panoramic cameras, which have fundamentally different projection functions. The workaround of decomposing a panorama into cubemap faces and running a perspective model on each face introduces visible seams at face boundaries because the resulting Gaussian fields are not globally consistent. The practical problem is that cameras in embodied AI, VR, and action-camera applications almost universally use non-pinhole optics, so the entire class of monocular 3DGS methods is inapplicable to these settings. Key Idea: The core insight is to decouple camera projection from scene representation by predicting a per-pixel unit ray field and a radial (metric) depth, placing every Gaussian in a shared 3D ray-distance space rather than in projection-specific image coordinates. Because a pixel displacement in this space always corresponds to a consistent angular/metric displacement regardless of the camera model, the same Gaussian predictor can be supervised jointly with data from heterogeneous cameras without any camera-specific branches. At inference time, even if camera intrinsics are unknown, the ray field can be estimated and used directly. Methodology: The pipeline has four stages. (1) Ray-based universal representation: a UniK3D ViT-L encoder predicts a per-pixel unit ray r_p ∈ S² and radial distance d_p > 0, yielding 3D points x_p = d_p r_p regardless of camera type. (2) Geometry Anchored Gaussians: two-layer Gaussians are initialized on the native ray grid — the first layer at the estimated visible surface, the second with an independent depth head for disocclusions — each carrying ray direction, inverse distance, base scale, identity quaternion, source color, and opacity. (3) Feature Conditioned Gaussian Residuals: a Gaussian decoder fuses 2D semantic image embeddings with 3D ray-based geometric features to predict a residual tensor ∆ ∈ R^{B×14×L×Hg×Wg} covering tangent-plane offsets, scale, quaternion, color, and opacity corrections; anchors and residuals are composed to yield the final Gaussians. (4) Training objective: L = L_app + L_dep + L_geo, where L_app is an L1 color term plus binary-cross-entropy opacity mask plus a perceptual (Gram/deep-feature) term on target views; L_dep supervises both source inverse-radial depth and the rendered target distance; L_geo adds second-layer total variation, floater suppression near first-layer depth discontinuities, and multi-scale Sobel depth-edge alignment. Panoramic equirectangular distortion is handled by latitude-dependent probabilistic dropout of the second Gaussian layer during training (dropout probability = 1 − cos(latitude)/max cos(latitude)), shifting adaptation from architectural branches to training-time regularization. A pose-free extension recovers the camera model from the predicted ray field by fitting pinhole or fisheye parameters, enabling calibration-free inference without a separate network branch. Training runs for 10^6 iterations on 8 H20 GPUs with Adam and cosine decay. The three key design decisions are: ray-distance parameterization (well-justified as the only projection-agnostic option that preserves metric consistency), the second Gaussian layer (ablated: −0.93 PSNR on WildRGB-D without it), and target-view rendered depth supervision (ablated: −1.14 PSNR on WildRGB-D, −2.12 on HM3D without it — the single largest loss component). Key Results: On the authors' FoV-stratified benchmark: perspective (in-distribution) — WildRGB-D 21.556/0.674/0.143 PSNR/SSIM/LPIPS vs. SHARP's 18.300/0.568/0.212; DL3DV 19.468 vs. 17.368; RealEstate10K 24.495 vs. 24.215. Zero-shot perspective generalization on Tanks and Temples (out-of-distribution): 16.315 PSNR vs. Flash3D's 15.985 and SHARP's 15.964. Panoramic (HM3D, in-distribution): 29.244/0.895/0.065 vs. Matrix3D 23.398/0.793/0.114 and PanoDreamer 21.856/0.708/0.152. Panoramic out-of-distribution (Replica): 30.182/0.933/0.038 vs. Matrix3D 25.379/0.887/0.069. Wide-FoV (OmniRooms-Wide, in-distribution): 25.243/0.854/0.076 vs. 18.881/0.795/0.173 (Matrix3D). Fisheye (ScanNet++ Fisheye, out-of-distribution for perspective baselines, in-distribution for UniSHARP): 20.660/0.771/0.184 vs. 16.382/0.690/0.371 (Matrix3D). Inference time: 3.1s vs. 8.6s (PanoDreamer, 2.8×) and 38.8s (Matrix3D, 12.5×). Pose-free setting: 20.850 PSNR vs. calibrated 21.556 on WildRGB-D. The panoramic margin is the most striking result; the perspective margin over the parent SHARP model is also notable given that universal training adds no per-camera overhead. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK. The ray-distance Gaussian prediction space is the genuine technical novelty — prior monocular 3DGS methods are uniformly restricted to image-plane Gaussian placement under pinhole assumptions. The FoV-stratified benchmark (OmniRooms simulated panoramas, OmniRooms-Wide projected fisheye, and the stratified evaluation protocol) fills a real infrastructure gap. The component techniques — UniK3D ray prediction, ViT-L backbone, 3DGS rasterization, perceptual loss — are all established; the contribution is their purposeful integration into a camera-unified framework. Rigor & Reproducibility: Architectural ablations (Table 7) and training-loss ablations (Table 8) are present and cover the main design choices; each removes one component cleanly. No error bars, confidence intervals, or multiple-seed runs are reported — all numbers are single-run. Code and model weights are not released at preprint time; no public repository URL is mentioned. A methodological concern: the majority of evaluation sets (WildRGB-D, DL3DV, RealEstate10K, HM3D, OmniRooms, OmniRooms-Wide) are subsets or derivatives of the training distribution; the only genuinely out-of-domain tests are Tanks and Temples (perspective) and Replica (panoramic). OmniRooms-Wide is explicitly derived by projecting the same OmniRooms panoramas used in training, so the wide-FoV numbers do not test cross-domain generalization. The pose-free evaluation is limited to WildRGB-D (perspective only), leaving open whether ray-field-based camera recovery degrades on fisheye or panoramic inputs. This is a preprint; results have not been peer reviewed. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS — the paper builds and evaluates a real feedforward inference pipeline. - Practical: Training requires 8 H20 GPUs; inference at 3.1s per scene runs on a single GPU with commercially available hardware. - Efficient: 2.8× to 12.5× faster than panoramic baselines at inference; the feedforward design avoids per-scene optimization or diffusion sampling, making it competitive for real-time-adjacent applications. - Effective: Evidence across six datasets and four camera categories consistently shows large improvements over all relevant baselines on both in-distribution and the limited out-of-distribution tests; the method solves the stated cross-camera NVS problem credibly. - Scalable: Tested at standard NVS evaluation scale (source-target pairs within 0.5m, up to 1024×2048 panoramas); behavior at 10× scene complexity or 4K+ resolution is not evaluated or claimed. The work delivers a trained model and a benchmark dataset (OmniRooms under CC BY-NC 4.0) but stops at preprint form with no released inference code. Strengths: - The ray-distance parameterization is the right abstraction: it is camera-model-agnostic by construction, not by engineering workaround, and it directly enables the mixed-camera training without any camera-specific branch. - The panoramic gains (+5.8 dB PSNR on HM3D, +4.8 dB on Replica over the nearest baseline) are large enough to be practically meaningful, not marginal. - Target-view rendered depth supervision is identified as the single most important loss component through controlled ablation, a non-obvious finding that informs future monocular 3DGS work. - The FoV-stratified benchmark consolidates perspective, wide-FoV, fisheye, and panoramic evaluation under one protocol, which the field was missing. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors state clearly that UniSHARP is a feedforward geometry-inference model rather than a generative completion method and that when target views expose large regions entirely outside the source image, the model has insufficient evidence and produces holes or weakly supported structures near outer boundaries. They frame long-range extrapolation as future work. This is an honest and direct acknowledgment of the fundamental scope boundary of feedforward NVS. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - Most evaluation sets are in-distribution: WildRGB-D, DL3DV, RealEstate10K, HM3D, and OmniRooms are all used in training; OmniRooms-Wide is a projection of OmniRooms. Only Tanks and Temples (perspective) and Replica (panoramic) are genuinely held-out, making the generalization claim harder to verify. - No comparison against an optimization-based NeRF or 3DGS baseline initialized from a single view (e.g., ZeroNVS, WonderWorld) — the baselines are exclusively feedforward methods, which biases the runtime comparison favorably. - The pose-free setting is evaluated only on a perspective dataset (WildRGB-D); camera-type recovery accuracy for fisheye and panoramic inputs is not reported. - No ablation of mixed-camera training versus camera-type-specific training: it is unclear whether universal training hurts perspective quality compared to a perspective-only model of the same capacity. - The wide-FoV evaluation relies entirely on simulated projected data from the authors' own dataset, with no real wide-FoV capture dataset as an independent test. Bottom Line: For anyone building NVS for omnidirectional or wide-FoV cameras, UniSHARP is currently the only feedforward method that addresses the problem systematically with credible numbers, and it is worth reading; the perspective-only NVS community can treat it as a useful data point but the main baselines there (SHARP, Flash3D) are more directly relevant.
Recommended 2606.07514 PDF
#64 — Innovative (67)

OpenRFM: Dissecting Relational In-Context Learning

Z Chen, J Yin, J Gu, S Xiong, X Liu, R Zhang, K Zhou… · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces OpenRFM, a relational foundation model that diagnoses two structural failure modes of the Relational Transformer (RT) backbone — sparse label coverage and a lazy pre-training regime — and fixes them via dual-stage in-context learning and homophily-aware pre-training, surpassing the commercial KumoRFMv1 on a 24-task benchmark at mean rank 4.92 vs. 7.73. - Why it's important: Commercial RFMs outperform all open parametric alternatives, blocking practitioners without proprietary systems from using foundation-model-style prediction over relational databases; the origin of this gap was unknown, preventing principled design choices. - How it does it: RT's BFS-walk context is reinterpreted as implicit kernel regression over labeled task-table rows, which fails when those rows are sparse; the fix adds a batch-level cross-query attention layer from a pre-trained tabular model (TabICL/TabPFN) plus a synthetic pre-training prior with an explicitly sampled homophily latent that is support-identifiable, pushing RT from a lazy frozen-kernel regime into genuine feature learning. - The good: OpenRFM (TabPFN head) achieves AUC 0.926 on t-fraud (KumoRFMv1: 0.841), R² 0.533 on i-sales (KumoRFMv1: −0.101), and best mean rank across all 24 tasks; the corruption-probe experiments cleanly confirm the kernel-regression diagnosis across four pre-training regimes. - The missing: Main ranking results (Tables 11–12) report no error bars or significance tests, making statistical claims about rank differences unverifiable; OpenRFM still trails JUICE×8 ensemble on several regression tasks (e.g., st-adv R² 0.317 vs. 0.501) without adequate discussion. - Read it or not: Researchers and engineers working on relational or tabular foundation models should read this — the diagnostic framework (kernel regression view, corruption probes, formal theory) is reusable and the open model outperforms a commercial system, but practitioners only needing inference tooling should wait for a code release confirmation.
Why this rank: Theoretical/empirical hybrid scored on empirical weights.
