Critical design decisions: (a) paragraph boundaries as step delimiters — well-justified as a natural segmentation matching the reasoner's generation rhythm; (b) the two-component action (strategy + free-form phrase) — well-justified as separating semantic intent from surface form; (c) asymmetric reward shaping — well-justified by identifying the perverse incentive of naive over-budget penalties (early termination on unsolved problems). Key Results Evaluated on MATH-500, AIME24, AMC (2022+2023), OlympiadBench (math), and GPQA Diamond (science QA) with three reasoners. Headline results at matched budget scales: - DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B: ACTS achieves 85.2% on MATH-500 (vs. 92.6% Vanilla) with −57.0% tokens; 60.0% on AIME24 (vs. 51.3% Vanilla) with −37.1% tokens — improving absolute accuracy while cutting cost. BudgetGuidance reaches only 82.8%/50.7% at 47–10% savings; DEER reaches 77.8%/50.0% at 53–28% savings. - Qwen3-8B: ACTS achieves 95.2% on MATH-500 (vs. 97.2% Vanilla) at −37.0% tokens; gains are narrower as the stronger reasoner already produces efficient traces. - GPQA Diamond (out-of-domain): ACTS (7B) achieves 46.8% vs. 38.9% Vanilla, −47.7% tokens — a +7.9pp gain on a benchmark never seen during training. - Throughput: ACTS matches Vanilla within 1% on Qwen3-8B and within 11% on 7B; DEER achieves only 57% of Vanilla throughput due to iterative probe-and-resume overhead. - Token savings decomposition at matched accuracy budget: on 7B reasoner, 28% of savings come from Rescue (Vanilla wrong → ACTS correct with fewer tokens), 64% from Early-term (both wrong, ACTS shorter), <5% from Regress. On Qwen3-8B, 42% from Shorten (both correct, ACTS trims post-solution detours). The budget sweep (Figure 4) shows smooth, largely monotone accuracy-token curves that dominate the line connecting Vanilla and NoThink endpoints — a Pareto improvement across the full budget range on nearly all cells. Surprising finding: for the 1.5B reasoner, ACTS surpasses Vanilla accuracy on hard benchmarks (AIME, GPQA) at lower token cost, suggesting the controller compensates for the weak reasoner's tendency to spiral into confused traces. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The combination of MDP-formulated step-level strategy control with a decoupled controller emitting both a categorical strategy and a free-form steering phrase, trained via SFT on LLM-annotated synthetic trajectories plus GRPO with asymmetric budget-conditioned reward shaping, is novel as an integrated framework. Individual components (strategy taxonomies from prior cognitive science literature, GRPO, budget penalties) are existing ideas; the genuine novelty lies in their synthesis and the decoupled inference architecture. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablation between ACTSπSFT (SFT only) and ACTS (SFT+RL) is reported across all benchmarks and reasoners, consistently showing RL improvement. Seeded repeats are used for small benchmarks (5× AIME24/AMC, 3× GPQA). Error bars are not reported in Table 1 — for larger benchmarks (MATH-500, OlympiadBench), statistical significance of accuracy differences is unclear. Code is released at https://github.com/Andree-9/ACTS. This is a preprint (arXiv:2606.03965v1); results are not peer-reviewed. Methodological concern: the RL stage trains the controller paired with the 7B reasoner, but evaluation pairs the same controller with 1.5B and Qwen3-8B without re-optimization — an unstated distribution mismatch. Strategy label annotation is performed by Qwen3-32B with no human validation, leaving open the question of whether the strategy curriculum reflects genuine reasoning structure or annotator artifacts. Deployability (4-property check) EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: Requires 8×A100-80GB (split 4+4 between controller and reasoner); feasible at enterprise scale but not consumer hardware. Controller (4B) adds moderate overhead. - Efficient: Asynchronous two-server serving amortizes controller-reasoner round trips to within 1–11% throughput overhead vs. Vanilla; total token cost (counting both controller and reasoner tokens) is reduced 20–64% depending on benchmark and reasoner. - Effective: On math benchmarks, evidence is strong and multi-benchmark: ACTS matches or beats Vanilla accuracy at substantially lower token cost, with the mechanism confirmed by per-trial outcome decomposition. GPQA generalization is suggestive but single-domain. - Scalable: Only tested on ≤8B reasoners; the paper explicitly states that 70B-scale open-weight and frontier proprietary reasoners are beyond their compute budget and leaves direct validation for future work — no experimental evidence of scalability beyond 8B. Delivers a working software artifact (code on GitHub) and a trained controller checkpoint. Strengths - The MDP formulation cleanly separates strategy selection from linguistic surface form, producing a genuinely reasoner-agnostic controller that transfers across model families without retraining the reasoner — a principled and practically valuable design. - The budget-conditioned reward shaping explicitly identifies and corrects the perverse incentive created by naive over-budget penalties (which reward early termination on unsolved problems), resulting in a more stable and effective RL objective. - The token savings decomposition into Rescue/Shorten/Regress/Early-term provides mechanistic insight into how accuracy and efficiency interact, going beyond aggregate numbers to explain why the method works. - The asynchronous SGLang inference pipeline achieves near-Vanilla throughput despite added controller calls, solving the latency problem that would otherwise make the approach impractical in serving contexts. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state two limitations: (1) evaluation is limited to reasoners up to 8B parameters from two model families, and 70B-scale or frontier proprietary reasoners are not evaluated; (2) ACTS requires an externally supplied thinking-token budget at inference time and does not autonomously infer a difficulty-appropriate budget. Both limitations are confronted directly rather than minimized, and future work directions (difficulty estimator, learned early-confidence signal) are proposed — a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The strategy annotation pipeline uses Qwen3-32B as an LLM annotator with no human validation of label quality; the strategy distribution in Figure 2 may reflect annotator biases rather than genuine reasoning structure, and this could silently corrupt the SFT curriculum. - RL training uses the 7B reasoner as the fixed partner; for Qwen3-8B, the RL improvement over SFT is substantially smaller than for 7B, suggesting the RL stage does not transfer as cleanly as the SFT stage — but this asymmetry is not discussed. - Error bars are absent from Table 1 for the larger benchmarks; given that ACTS sometimes shows <2pp accuracy differences from Vanilla on MATH-500, the significance of these differences is not established. - The throughput comparison in Section 5.5 splits 8 GPUs as 4+4 (controller vs. reasoner), giving the reasoner half the parallelism it has under Vanilla. The near-parity result is impressive, but the comparison is not GPU-cost-neutral — a more rigorous comparison would equalize reasoner compute. - Generalization is tested on one out-of-domain benchmark (GPQA); code generation, formal reasoning, or multi-step QA are untested, leaving open whether the domain-agnostic strategy vocabulary actually generalizes beyond STEM question answering. Bottom Line ACTS is a well-engineered, reproducible method that achieves the unusual combination of reducing token cost while matching or exceeding accuracy on math reasoning benchmarks, with a principled MDP framework, honest analysis of savings mechanisms, and practical asynchronous inference — practitioners working on inference-time efficiency should read this paper.
Recommended 2606.03965 PDF
#70 — Innovative (66)

Claw-SWE-Bench: A Benchmark for Evaluating OpenClaw-style Agent Harnesses on Coding Tasks (55 ▲ )

Mengyu Zheng, Kai Han, Boxun Li, Haiyang Xu, Yuchuan Tian, Wei He, Hang Zhou, Jianyuan Guo, Hailin Hu, Lin Ma, Chao Xu, Guohao Dai, Lixue Xia, Yunchao Wei, Yunhe Wang, Yu Wang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 11 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Claw-SWE-Bench, a 350-instance multilingual SWE-bench-style benchmark and adapter protocol that makes heterogeneous agent harnesses ("claws") directly comparable under a fixed prompt, runtime budget, workspace contract, patch extraction procedure, and evaluator. - Why it's important: Existing SWE-bench-style leaderboards bundle the model, harness, and task set into a single reported number, making it impossible to attribute performance differences to any individual factor; the paper demonstrates that harness choice alone produces performance swings as large as 27.4 pp — comparable to moving between model capability tiers. - How it does it: A thin adapter interface (five lifecycle methods: create_agent, send_task, backup_session, delete_agent, get_docker_args) converts each harness's native execution style into SWE-bench's patch-prediction contract, while the shared orchestrator fixes everything outside that interface; a cost-aware, rank-aware local-search procedure selects an 80-instance Lite subset that preserves the full benchmark's accuracy and cost structure at ~23% of cost. - The good: The quantified harness effect is the paper's strongest contribution — under a fixed Qwen 3.6-flash model, the five-claw spread reaches 27.4 pp (38.6%–66.0%), and the bare vs. full adapter comparison shows that patch-extraction strategy alone explains a 54.3 pp gap (19.1% → 73.4%) with the same GLM 5.1 backbone. - The missing: All main results are single-run aggregates with no variance estimates, so differences of a few percentage points cannot be interpreted as stable system superiority; the adapter's 54.3 pp gap is reported as a holistic comparison, not a component ablation, and individual adapter contributions remain unmeasured. - Read it or not: Essential for anyone designing or interpreting SWE-bench-style evaluations of coding agents; if your work centers on model training rather than evaluation methodology, the methodological contributions are informative but not actionable.
Why this rank: Empirical profile (measurement-infrastructure paper). Non-Obviousness (72) and Novelty (70) are strong: treating the agent harness as a controlled variable and quantifying a previously invisible confound — harness choice alone produces a 27.4 pp swing under a fixed model, comparable to crossing model tiers — is a substantively novel, practically important finding, alongside the future-commit leakage discovery and a principled cost-aware Lite-80 construction. Rigor (60) is honestly limited by single-run aggregates with no error bars (acknowledged), but partially offset by released code/data, documented 1,148-hour compute, and the well-executed future-commit ablation. Completeness (68) and Resilience (64) are solid — limitations are confronted directly, the bare-vs-full adapter gap is framed as a bundle effect rather than overclaimed, and the Pareto cost analysis is candid. Impact (68) reflects reusable infrastructure that plugs in new claws/models unchanged. Composite ≈ 0.20·70 + 0.25·60 + 0.15·72 + 0.15·68 + 0.15·64 + 0.10·68 = 66. This is among the most careful papers in the cluster and aligns with the user's preference for rigorous, honestly-bounded empirical work.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 68 Resilience 64 Impact 68
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation SWE-bench has become the de facto standard for evaluating repository-level coding agents, but published resolved-rate numbers conflate three causally distinct factors: the LLM being evaluated, the agent harness (scaffold, tool interface, prompt template, stopping policy) that turns the LLM into an autonomous coding system, and the task instances being solved. General-purpose agents like OpenClaw cannot directly enter SWE-bench scoring because they lack the patch-prediction contract the evaluator requires, and because their native outputs (conversation text, JSON, logs) are not the `model_patch` field the evaluator reads. Even when harness-specific systems are compared, their prompts, budgets, and termination policies differ, making cross-system attribution impossible. Beyond accuracy, cost is invisible: the same Pass@1 can correspond to API costs that differ by two orders of magnitude, a difference that determines whether academic groups can afford to participate in benchmarking. Key Idea The core insight is that the agent harness should be treated as a controlled experimental variable rather than bundled with the model and task set. The paper achieves this through a two-layer decomposition: a thin adapter interface standardizes the boundary between each harness and the benchmark lifecycle (the harness plugs in through five abstract methods; everything else — container management, prompt instantiation, patch collection, evaluation — is owned by the benchmark layer), while a shared orchestrator fixes all confounds outside that boundary. Patches are collected from repository state via git diff rather than parsed from agent output, making the output contract independent of whether the harness produces JSON, plain text, or nothing structured. This cleanly separates what the harness does internally from what the evaluator sees. Methodology The benchmark is built from 300 instances drawn from SWE-bench-Multilingual (Java, Go, Rust, JS/TS, C/C++, Ruby, PHP) and 50 from SWE-bench-Verified-Mini (Python), for 350 total across 43 repositories. Each instance runs in its upstream SWE-bench Docker image, repository reset to base_commit, with the testbed mounted at /testbed. A critical fix: the paper discovered that non-Python SWE-bench-Multilingual container images exposed git commits after base_commit, allowing agents to inspect future fixes via `git log`; the runner removes reachable future history before each run. All harnesses share a 3600-second wall-clock timeout, one run per instance, and 3-thread concurrency. The five evaluated claws are OpenClaw (Node.js, stateful per-instance agent isolation), Hermes-Agent (Python, stateless CLI), ZeroClaw (Rust binary, stateless), NanoBot (Python, stateless with workspace-pollution scrub), and GenericAgent (Python, file-based input/output protocol). Two experimental grids are run: OpenClaw × 9 models (GPT 5.5, Claude Opus 4.7, GLM 5.1, DeepSeek-V4 Pro, DeepSeek-V4 Flash, Kimi 2.6, Qwen 3.6-flash, MiniMax M2.7, Seed 2.0-mini), and 5 claws × 2 models (GLM 5.1 and Qwen 3.6-flash). The Lite-80 subset is selected via binary optimization with three loss terms: L1 resolve-rate parity over a 17×8 calibration grid, a pairwise ranking hinge that penalizes ordering reversals between columns differing by >3 pp on full-350, and log-space cost parity; per-language 200-restart within-quartile 1-swap local search enforces hard constraints of 10 instances per language with a fixed 2/3/3/2 difficulty-quartile allocation. The primary metric is Pass@1 (fraction of instances whose patch is marked RESOLVED by the official SWE-bench evaluator); secondary metrics are total API cost in USD, mean wall-clock duration, input/output token counts, average turns, and cache hit rate. Key Results Adapter necessity: with GLM 5.1 on full-350, the bare adapter (model writes unified diff in final response) achieves 19.1% Pass@1 with 69.1% apply-failure rate; the full adapter (model edits files, runner exports patch from git state) achieves 73.4% with <1.5% apply failures — a 54.3 pp gap using the same backbone. Model axis (OpenClaw fixed): GPT 5.5 leads at 78.0% ($1399.1 full-run cost); Claude Opus 4.7 is 0.9 pp lower at $1082.0; DeepSeek-V4 Flash achieves 70.3% at $8.2; DeepSeek-V4 Pro achieves 71.7% at $81.3; Seed 2.0-mini is lowest at 48.6%. Total model spread: 29.4 pp. Claw axis (models fixed): on GLM 5.1, the five-claw spread is 12.5 pp (60.9%–73.4%); on Qwen 3.6-flash, the spread is 27.4 pp (38.6%–66.0%), with the generic baseline collapsing hardest. Per-language, Go is the hardest language for all 9 models in the model sweep (range 33.3%–61.9%), sitting 11–22 pp below each model's overall mean. Future-commit cleanup reduces Claude Opus 4.7's rate by 8.0 pp (84.7% → 76.7%) but affects GPT 5.5, MiniMax M2.7, and Seed 2.0-mini by ≤1 pp, indicating model-specific sensitivity to the leakage. Lite-80 reproduces full-350 Pass@1 within 0.4 pp on average across 17 calibration columns, with maximum cross-claw gap of 3.68 pp (nanobot × Qwen 3.6-flash), and runs at 22.9% of full-350 cost. Novelty Classification NEW BENCHMARK + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The adapter protocol and cost-aware Lite subset construction are genuine engineering contributions; the 17-column cost-aware rank-aware selection with explicit K-sweep validation is more principled than standard random or stratified sampling. The empirical finding that harness choice produces a 27.4 pp spread under a fixed model — comparable to moving between frontier and flash model tiers — is substantively novel and practically important. What is not novel: the SWE-bench evaluation formulation itself (inherited wholesale from upstream), the constituent harnesses (all pre-existing systems), and the benchmark instances (drawn entirely from upstream sources). The paper's contribution is measurement infrastructure and controlled comparison, not a new algorithm or model. Rigor & Reproducibility All main results are single-run aggregates with no multi-seed replication; the paper acknowledges this and notes that small differences of a few pp should not be interpreted as stable superiority. No error bars, confidence intervals, or significance tests are reported. The adapter diagnostic (bare vs. full) tests the necessity of the complete adapter as a unit, not individual components; no ablation attributes the 54.3 pp gap to specific adapter features (workspace preparation vs. patch extraction vs. future-commit cleanup vs. artifact scrubbing). The future-commit cleanup comparison is the closest thing to a controlled ablation and is well-executed. Code, dataset instance IDs, Lite subset construction scripts, and figure-generation scripts are released at https://github.com/opensquilla/claw-swe-bench and https://huggingface.co/datasets/TokenRhythm/Claw-SWE-Bench; the five harness adapter packages are also released. API keys are explicitly withheld. Total compute is documented: approximately 1,148 hours of end-to-end wall-clock across 17 (claw, model) columns on a 16-core/61 GiB server with no local GPU. This is a preprint (arXiv:2606.12344v1); results have not undergone peer review. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL and SYSTEMS — the paper builds and evaluates a benchmark infrastructure system. - Practical: The full evaluation runs on a 16-core CPU server with 61 GiB RAM and no GPU; all model inference routes through external APIs, so local compute requirements are modest. - Efficient: A full 350-instance run at 3-thread concurrency requires ~1,148 total wall-clock hours across 17 columns; Lite-80 reduces per-column cost to ~23%, which the paper validates is a practical entry point for iterative development. - Effective: The benchmark demonstrably detects harness differences of 12.5–27.4 pp and model differences of 29.4 pp under controlled conditions; the Lite subset preserves cross-system rankings with mean absolute gap of 1.88 pp, which is sufficient for screening but not for final reporting. - Scalable: The benchmark scales linearly in instances and harnesses; no stress-test at larger instance counts is conducted, and the single-run protocol means variance is unknown and reliability at fine-grained comparisons is unvalidated. The paper delivers a software artifact (adapter framework, Lite construction toolkit, evaluation scripts) and a dataset (instance ID lists). The benchmark infrastructure is reusable for new claws and models without changes to the evaluation layer. Strengths - The adapter interface design is clean: by collecting patches from repository state rather than parsing agent output, the output contract is genuinely output-format-agnostic, which is the right engineering choice and removes a major source of harness-specific advantage. - The future-commit leakage discovery and fix is a concrete contribution to evaluation hygiene; the model-specific impact (0.6–8.0 pp depending on model) shows this is not a minor technicality but a meaningful confound in upstream benchmarks. - The cost-aware Lite construction is more principled than the common approach of random sampling; the three-term objective (resolve parity, ranking hinge, cost parity) and explicit K-sweep with sensitivity analysis across perturbation scenarios demonstrate genuine care about the subset's fitness for its intended purpose. - The Pareto cost–accuracy analysis makes explicit a trade-off that leaderboards routinely obscure: DeepSeek-V4 Flash achieves 70.3% at $8.2 while GPT 5.5 achieves 78.0% at $1399.1, and these cannot be compared on accuracy alone by any practitioner with a real budget. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors acknowledge three main limitations directly: (1) single-run aggregates mean small differences should not be over-interpreted as stable superiority, and multi-seed replication is needed; (2) the five-claw × two-model sweep is insufficient to fully decompose harness × model interactions, and a wider model axis would be needed to distinguish general harness mechanisms from backbone-specific effects; (3) cost analysis depends on provider-side pricing and cache accounting policies, which vary and change. The authors propose retaining raw token traces in future releases for re-pricing audits. These limitations are confronted directly and honestly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The bare vs. full adapter comparison is framed as testing adapter necessity, but since the two conditions differ on at least four dimensions simultaneously (workspace preparation, future-commit cleanup, prompt phasing, git-based patch extraction vs. direct diff output), the 54.3 pp gap cannot be attributed to any individual design decision. A component ablation is absent and would substantially strengthen the paper's design guidance. - The five claws differ not just in agent loop but also in tool surface, turn limit (nanobot caps at 30, zeroclaw at 50, hermes at 300), memory strategy, and stopping rule. The paper is careful to call these "harness" differences, but readers should note this is an aggregate bundle effect, not a clean isolation of any single harness design variable. - The paper reports only one held-out system (OpenSquilla) for Lite validation, which is thin evidence that Lite generalizes to future systems not in the calibration pool. - Go is consistently the hardest language across all models and most claws, but the paper does not investigate why — whether this reflects repository characteristics, language-specific tool behavior, or training data representation. This is a notable omission given that Go constitutes 42/350 instances. - All experiments use a single provider routing layer (OpenRouter, DashScope, etc.); provider-specific caching policies affect cost accounting, and the paper notes this but does not fully control for it — cache hit rate varies from 63.9% to 98.5% across systems, which materially affects cost comparisons. Bottom Line A practitioner building or comparing SWE-bench-style coding-agent pipelines gains directly actionable infrastructure and a quantified, previously invisible confound (harness effect ≥27 pp); the single-run limitation means the paper's specific numbers should be treated as point estimates, but the methodological framework and the direction of the findings are credible and non-obvious.
Recommended 2606.12344 PDF
#71 — Innovative (66)

SWE-Explore: Benchmarking How Coding Agents Explore Repositories (95 ▲ )

Shaoqiu Zhang, Yuhang Wang, Jialiang Liang, Yuling Shi, Wenhao Zeng, Maoquan Wang, Shilin He, Ningyuan Xu, Siyu Ye, Kai Cai, Xiaodong Gu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 9 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SWE-Explore, a benchmark that isolates and evaluates the repository exploration sub-capability of coding agents as a ranked, line-level context-selection task across 848 issues spanning 10 programming languages and 203 open-source repositories. - Why it's important: Existing coding benchmarks like SWE-bench reduce agent performance to a binary pass/fail signal that conflates exploration, bug localization, and patch synthesis, hiding whether failure stems from missing the relevant code or from an inadequate fix — a distinction that matters for targeting agent improvements. - How it does it: Ground-truth line regions are derived algorithmically from the intersection of read-actions across ≥2 independent successful agent trajectories, refined with LLM-assisted promotion of load-bearing optional reads, and audited manually, then scored against explorer outputs using a line-budget-adapted nDCG and a restricted-context downstream repair protocol. - The good: Context efficiency achieves Pearson r=0.950 correlation with downstream repair rate, empirically validating that the benchmark's upstream exploration metrics genuinely predict what matters for patch generation. - The missing: The benchmark is restricted to instances already solved by at least two agents in the pool, systematically excluding hard unsolved cases and likely overrepresenting well-structured Python repositories (64.5% of instances), so conclusions about the exploration bottleneck may not generalize to genuinely novel or difficult issues. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building or evaluating coding agents, particularly those working on context retrieval, bug localization, or agent scaffolds — the metric design and trajectory-grounded annotation methodology are the durable contributions.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (75) and Non-Obviousness (76) lead: the trajectory-intersection annotation methodology is genuinely new for code-agent evaluation, and the finding that file-level localization is near-saturated while line-level recall is the binding constraint — with context efficiency predicting downstream repair (Pearson r=0.950) better than any coverage metric — is a non-obvious, causally validated insight. Rigor (58) is constrained by a solved-instances-only construction bias, 64.5% Python skew (C++ has 7 instances), no inter-annotator agreement, and no quantitative ablation of the LLM-refinement step, though the restricted-context downstream protocol is a real causal control. Completeness (64) and Resilience (62) benefit from directly confronted limitations but are capped by the systematic exclusion of hard unsolved cases. Impact (65) reflects released benchmark + validated metric library. Composite ≈ 0.20·75 + 0.25·58 + 0.15·76 + 0.15·64 + 0.15·62 + 0.10·65 = 66.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 58 Non-Obv 76 Completeness 64 Resilience 62 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Repository-level coding agents fail for two distinct reasons: they either never find the relevant code, or they find it but generate a wrong patch. Existing benchmarks like SWE-bench collapse both failure modes into a single resolve/fail bit, making it impossible to diagnose which stage caused the failure. The localization sub-problem has attracted prior work (LocAgent, OrcaLoca, ContextBench, CoSIL), but these lack a common, precisely specified evaluation target: they operate at file or function granularity, use human-annotated rather than behavior-grounded labels, or do not pair exploration scores with a controlled downstream repair check. Without line-level ground truth derived from actual solution behavior, there is no way to tell whether an agent that reaches the right file also surfaces the specific spans that support a correct fix. Key Idea: The benchmark derives ground truth not from manual annotation or static code analysis but from the intersection of file-read actions extracted from ≥2 independent successful agent trajectories — editor view calls, shell reads (cat/head/tail/sed -n), and grep -n hits — normalized to repository-relative (path, line-start, line-end) tuples. Regions consistently surfaced across different successful solution paths are treated as behaviorally necessary evidence. This trajectory-intersection approach produces line-level targets without human labelers needing to reason about which spans are relevant. Methodology: The pipeline has three stages. First, benchmark construction: successful trajectories from strong LLMs (GPT-5.4, Gemini-3-Pro, Sonnet-4.6, GLM-5.1, Kimi-K2.6) are collected and read-actions parsed into normalized (p, s, e) regions; raw core context is their file-wise line-level intersection; an LLM refinement step promotes load-bearing optional regions (those adjacent to core evidence, frequently revisited, or near patch sites); every promoted region is manually audited. Second, evaluation: a new explorer outputs K=5 ranked regions; these are scored against the refined core using precision/recall/F1 at the line level, file-level and region-level hit rates, a line-budget-adapted nDCG@B (gain = newly covered core lines, budget B=500 lines), first useful hit (FUH = 1 - i*/|P|), and context efficiency (fraction of predicted lines inside core ∪ optional). Third, downstream validation (one-time, not standard): each explorer's K=5 regions are materialized as the only visible repository context for a fixed Mini-SWE-Agent patcher (GPT-5.4), and the patched diff is judged by the original SWE-bench harness. The design decision to fix K=5 is justified by the per-instance average of 4.7 core regions. The line-budget nDCG formulation (penalizing verbose regions that exhaust budget without proportional gain) is a careful design choice that avoids rewarding whole-file emission. Key Results: Across 14 explorers on 848 instances: sparse retrievers (BM25 HitFile=0.079, TF-IDF HitFile=0.140) and the dense baseline (Potion HitFile=0.088) perform near Random (HitFile=0.004). All agentic explorers substantially outperform them. General-purpose coding agents (Claude Code, Codex, OpenHands, Mini-SWE-Agent, AweAgent, all powered by GPT-5.4) cluster tightly: HitFile 0.645–0.682, nDCG@500 0.867–0.954, but Recℓ only 0.140–0.194. CoSIL achieves the highest non-oracle Recℓ (0.788) and F1 (0.602) via iterative code-graph search but at a noise rate of 0.471. AutoCodeRover reaches the highest precision (0.680) but very low HitReg (0.272) and Recℓ (0.233). Oracle: HitFile=0.923, Recℓ=0.953, CtxEff=1.000. Downstream resolve rates: Oracle 59.7%, CoSIL 59.3%, Mini-SWE-Agent 50.0%, Claude Code 48.0%, sparse retrievers 12.7–26.0%, Random 4.7%. The controlled context-degradation experiment (sweeping α ∈ {0,25,50,75,100}% of oracle core regions, with and without noise padding) shows a threshold effect: resolve rate on the easier subset jumps sharply between α=50 and α=75, and redundant non-core context degrades performance by 7–9 pp only when core evidence is absent. The LLM-comparison ablation (same Mini-SWE-Agent scaffold, six different LLMs) shows GPT-5.4/GPT-5.4-mini at the top tier and reveals that no LLM substantially closes the gap between HitFile (~0.5–0.65) and Recℓ (~0.05–0.19), confirming the bottleneck is in exploration strategy rather than model capability alone. Novelty Classification: NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The trajectory-intersection annotation methodology is genuinely novel for code-agent evaluation. The empirical finding that file-level localization is near-saturated for modern agents while line-level recall remains the binding constraint, and that context efficiency predicts downstream repair better than any coverage metric, is a non-obvious, quantified insight. The combination of these two — a benchmark + a metric family validated against a causal downstream protocol — is the paper's unique contribution. Standard IR metrics (nDCG, precision/recall) applied to code retrieval are known; the extension to line-budget nDCG is a small but principled adaptation. Rigor & Reproducibility: The downstream correlation study uses n=150 shared instances — small but sufficient to compute stable Pearson/Spearman correlations across 14 explorers. No per-instance error bars or significance tests are reported; the small number of explorers (14) means correlation estimates have wide uncertainty. The three-variant ground-truth analysis (pure intersection, refined core, full union) is described but results deferred to Appendix B without quantitative comparison in the main text. The human audit procedure is described qualitatively, with no inter-annotator agreement reported. No ablation quantifies how much the LLM refinement step changes the core target relative to pure intersection. Code, benchmark records, metric scripts, and the evaluator are released publicly at https://github.com/Qiushao-E/SWE-Explore-Bench and https://huggingface.co/datasets/SWE-Explore-Bench/SWE-Explore-Bench. This is a preprint; results have not been peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS (a built benchmark infrastructure with evaluation pipeline). - Practical: The benchmark and metric evaluator run on CPU for retrieval baselines; agentic explorer runs require LLM API access and the source SWE-bench harness infrastructure, which is publicly available. - Efficient: Upstream scoring (precision/recall/nDCG) is computationally trivial once explorer output is produced; the expensive component is running the agentic explorers themselves (14 systems × 848 instances × LLM calls), which the paper acknowledges in Appendix G without reporting wall-clock totals. - Effective: The downstream protocol directly demonstrates that higher exploration scores translate to higher repair rates (Oracle 59.7% vs. Random 4.7%), establishing that the benchmark measures a causally relevant capability. - Scalable: The benchmark is fixed at 848 instances from existing sources; it does not grow automatically. Scaling to new repositories or unsolved issues requires new trajectory collection, which depends on having strong-enough agents to produce successful runs — a circular dependency for hard instances. The paper delivers a software artifact (benchmark records, evaluation scripts, metric library). Strengths: - The trajectory-intersection annotation methodology replaces expensive human labeling with behavioral signal from successful solutions, and the subsequent LLM refinement + human audit step recovers load-bearing evidence missed by pure intersection — a credible pipeline with explicit quality controls. - The downstream restricted-context protocol cleanly separates exploration quality from patch quality by giving a fixed patcher only the explorer's output and measuring SWE-bench harness resolve rate, providing causal validation that the metrics measure something real. - The finding that general-purpose agents converge to nearly identical exploration profiles regardless of harness complexity (Claude Code, Codex, OpenHands, Mini-SWE-Agent, AweAgent are tightly clustered) is a surprising and actionable empirical result: it implies the exploration bottleneck is architectural, not a scaffold-engineering problem. - The controlled context-degradation experiment (GT fraction vs. noise injection) establishes that missing core evidence is the dominant failure mode and reveals a non-obvious threshold effect (performance jumps between 50% and 75% core coverage), with direct implications for where to invest in agent improvement. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly note in Appendix G that SWE-Explore covers only instances solved by at least two agents in their pool, so it does not represent the full distribution of unsolved repository-level issues. They also acknowledge that trajectory-derived ground truth is an empirical approximation of useful context — valid solution paths using different evidence are not captured, so the scoring target is not provably complete. They note the restricted-context protocol should be read as metric validation rather than an absolute measure of patch-generation ability. They confront these limitations directly and do not minimize them. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The 64.5% Python skew (driven by SWE-bench Verified's Python-centricity) means multilingual findings rest on small per-language samples (e.g., C++ has only 7 instances) — cross-language conclusions should be treated with caution. The inter-annotator agreement for the human audit step is not reported, making it impossible to assess the reliability of ground-truth labels. The LLM refinement step's impact on core target size and composition is not ablated quantitatively in the main paper. All agentic explorers in Table 6 use GPT-5.4 as the underlying model, so the comparison conflates scaffold effects with model effects; the model-ablation in Table 5 uses only one scaffold (Mini-SWE-Agent). The trajectory filter (requiring ≥2 successful runs from the pool) creates a dataset systematically biased toward easier instances and toward repositories well-represented in frontier LLM training data, which may inflate all scores. The paper does not compare against the human-annotated ContextBench ground truth to validate whether trajectory-intersection captures the same evidence human experts consider necessary. Bottom Line: A practitioner building or benchmarking a coding agent's context-retrieval component gains a concrete evaluation target and a validated metric family — the line-level bottleneck finding and the trajectory-grounded annotation methodology are immediately actionable — though the Python-heavy, solved-instances-only scope limits how broadly the findings generalize.
Recommended 2606.07297 PDF
#72 — Innovative (66)

SentGuard: Sentence-Level Streaming Guardrails for Large Language Models

J Yu, X Wang, Y Wang, J Li, Y Teng, X Ma, Y Wang · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SentGuard, a sentence-level streaming guardrail that detects unsafe LLM outputs in real time by buffering streamed tokens into sentence chunks, evaluating each prefix asynchronously in parallel with the next chunk's decoding, and releasing only verified sentences to the user. - Why it's important: As LLMs increasingly stream long reasoning-intensive responses, existing guardrails force a binary choice between response-level methods (too slow to intervene early) and token-level methods (unstable decisions on fragmented semantics); neither is fit for production streaming deployment. - How it does it: A lightweight waiting buffer introduces a one-sentence offset so the guard evaluates the current hidden prefix while the target LLM decodes the subsequent chunk, paired with a coarse-to-fine two-stage SFT objective that trains the model on both full conversations and densely-sampled sentence prefixes near unsafe transition points. - The good: On WildGuardTest, SentGuard improves Det@2 from 71.48% (best baseline) to 88.73% and cuts MFDS from 8.22 to 2.58, while maintaining a 4.49% SFPR — a large early-detection gain without inflating false positives. - The missing: All evaluation is conducted on the authors' own StreamSafe benchmark plus four pre-existing static benchmarks; there is no test against live jailbreak attacks crafted to exploit sentence-boundary timing, which is the primary adversarial surface introduced by the new paradigm. - Read it or not: Read it if you build or deploy streaming LLM safety systems — the sentence-shifted async buffer is a deployable engineering contribution and the StreamSafe dataset fills a genuine annotation gap; skip it if your safety concern is input-side or prompt-level, where SentGuard adds nothing.
Why this rank: Systems profile. Practical Impact (75) leads — a deployable single-A800 streaming guardrail with a clean async sentence-shifted buffer hiding guard cost behind decoding, plus a released-on-publication dataset (StreamSafe, 62K annotated prefixes). Rigor (65) is moderate: ablations cleanly isolate the sentence-supervision contribution and the 3B–30B backbone-generalization check is strong, but all numbers are single-seed with no variance, and latency is from replayed simulation rather than a live serving stack. Resilience (58) is the weakest dimension — no evaluation against timing-aware adversaries that front-load benign sentences, which is precisely the new surface the paradigm introduces, and no stress test on long reasoning traces. Novelty (60) is incremental (engineering design + standard SFT recombined). 0.15*60+0.15*65+0.10*60+0.15*65+0.15*58+0.30*75 = 66.
systems Novelty 60 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 60 Completeness 65 Resilience 58 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Production LLM deployments increasingly deliver responses token-by-token in real time, including long chain-of-thought traces where harmful content can emerge gradually rather than at the start. Safety teams operating these systems need guardrails that can intervene before harmful content reaches users without blocking benign generation. Response-level guardrails (LlamaGuard, WildGuard, ShieldGemma) classify only after the full response is generated, exposing users to all harmful tokens and wasting decoding compute on continuations that should have been stopped. Token-level streaming guardrails (ShieldHead, SCM, Kelp, Qwen3Guard-Stream) fire on each token but operate on fragments with incomplete semantics, yielding high false-positive rates and excessive invocation counts — particularly problematic for reasoning-heavy outputs where unsafe intent accumulates across multiple sentences before becoming unambiguous. Key Idea: Sentence boundaries are the natural unit for streaming safety moderation: they are semantically complete enough for reliable classification yet granular enough for timely intervention. The core trick is a one-sentence asynchronous offset: while the target LLM decodes sentence S_{i+1} into a hidden buffer, the guard evaluates the prefix up through S_i. Guard inference cost is hidden behind normal decoding time, so user-facing latency matches post-hoc moderation in the common (safe) case while still enabling sentence-level early stopping in the unsafe case. Methodology: SentGuard has three tightly coupled components. First, StreamSafe dataset construction: full conversations are collected from two sources — simulated harmful-query/response pairs and the public Aegis2.0 dataset — then each assistant response is segmented into sentence chunks via punctuation-based boundary detection. For safe responses, two prefix positions are sampled (one early, one full). For unsafe responses, the earliest unsafe-evidence sentence is identified by annotation, and prefixes are sampled densely in a window immediately after that transition point (window d=3), with safe-labeled prefixes drawn from the pre-trigger region. Each instance receives structured annotations: final safety state (safe/uncertain/unsafe), response mode, risk level, and violated category from an 8-class taxonomy. Total: 62K full and partial instances. Second, two-stage SFT training on Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507 using LoRA (rank 16, α=32): Stage 1 trains on full conversations with standard cross-entropy loss L_full to establish output format and coarse safety judgment; Stage 2 adds a sentence-prefix term L_sent weighted by λ=4, where prefixes are sampled according to a distribution π_i that concentrates around early unsafe-trigger regions for unsafe trajectories and is uniform for safe ones. The combined objective is L_ctf = L_full + λ·L_sent. Third, sentence-shifted inference: a waiting buffer groups streamed tokens into sentence chunks; SentGuard evaluates the hidden prefix (x, y_{≤S_i}) while the LLM decodes S_{i+1}; the front-end displays only previously verified chunks. Intervention (block/halt) fires when the guard outputs uncertain or unsafe under the strict mapping. Design decisions: (1) using punctuation-based sentence segmentation rather than a learned splitter — practical but brittle for code, mathematical notation, and abbreviations, not deeply justified; (2) the strict mapping (uncertain→unsafe) for streaming, loose mapping for comparison — reasonable given the asymmetric cost of exposure vs. over-refusal, well-motivated; (3) dense sampling near the unsafe transition point — directly justified by the goal of calibrating the model to fire early rather than wait for full context. Key Results: On streaming detection across 5 benchmarks against 6 baselines (LlamaGuard3-8B, LlamaGuard4-12B, WildGuard-7B, Qwen3Guard-8B-Gen, Qwen3Guard-8B-Stream) and 2 closed-source frontier models (Gemini-3.5-Flash, GPT-5.5): SentGuard achieves the best Det@2 on all 5 benchmarks, averaging 90.5% detection within 2 sentences and MFDS of 1.72. Highlights: WildGuardTest Det@2 = 88.73% vs. best baseline 71.48% (+17.25 pp); StreamSafe Det@2 = 89.75% vs. best baseline 61.54% (+28.21 pp). Full-response F1 averages 88.7%, ranking first, versus 88.5% for Qwen3Guard-8B-Gen (second). SFPR averages 7.41%, lower than WildGuard-7B (23.76%) and GPT-5.5 (24.55%), competitive with LlamaGuard4-12B (6.37%). Latency analysis (simulated traces, 160-token average response, 16 chunks, 36 ms per guard call): at 20–160 tokens/s decoding speeds, async overhead = 36 ms, matching post-hoc moderation and 16× lower than blocking sentence-level moderation (576 ms); at 320 tokens/s overhead rises to 102.5 ms. Decoding token savings on unsafe responses: SentGuard uses only 4.67%–26.69% of response tokens before detecting unsafe content, saving 64–451 tokens per unsafe response depending on benchmark. Backbone generalization on StreamSafe across Qwen2.5-3B through Qwen3-30B-A3B shows Det@2 within 89.56%–89.75% and Full F1 within 98.64%–98.94%, indicating the gains are data/supervision-driven rather than scale-driven. Baselines are current and representative; closed-source frontier models are evaluated in zero-shot mode rather than fine-tuned, which is a realistic but asymmetric comparison. Evaluation is on standard safety benchmarks (BeaverTails, Safe-RLHF, XSTest, WildGuardTest) plus authors' own StreamSafe. Scale tested: model size 3B–30B parameters, response length ~160 tokens — not stress-tested at longer reasoning traces (thousands of tokens). Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK. The sentence-level streaming paradigm positioned between token-level and response-level moderation is a genuine problem formulation contribution. The asynchronous waiting-buffer architecture is a new engineering design rather than a novel algorithm per se. StreamSafe (62K annotated instances with per-sentence prefix supervision) fills a gap not covered by existing safety benchmarks. The coarse-to-fine SFT objective is a combination of standard SFT with prefix-progressive sampling — not architecturally novel, but applied here in a new way to the streaming safety domain. The formal invocation-schedule framing (Section 3.1) provides useful vocabulary but is descriptive rather than a theoretical result. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and informative: full-response-only training vs. full+partial training (Table 3) cleanly isolates the contribution of sentence supervision, showing +22–29 pp gains in Det@1/Det@2. Backbone generalization across four model sizes (Table 4) is a meaningful robustness check. Error bars and significance tests are absent throughout — all results are from a single training run (explicitly stated). Latency numbers are derived from replayed simulated traces rather than measured end-to-end in a live serving environment, which limits their external validity. StreamSafe is partly evaluated on its own test set using a model trained on its training set — the benchmark was constructed to match the training distribution, which is a mild Goodhart concern for StreamSafe-specific numbers (though cross-benchmark numbers mitigate this). Code and model weights are promised for release upon publication but not yet available. The evaluation framework maps all models to a unified binary decision space, which is methodologically sound but masks category-level performance differences. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS — SentGuard is a built guardrail system evaluated by streaming deployment simulation. - Practical: Trains and runs on a single NVIDIA A800 GPU (80GB), with inference at 36 ms per call on a 4B-parameter LoRA fine-tuned model — commercially viable hardware. - Efficient: Guard inference cost is hidden behind LLM decoding at typical serving speeds (20–160 tokens/s), adding only 36 ms user-facing overhead vs. 576 ms for synchronous sentence-level blocking; at 320 tokens/s the model becomes the bottleneck and overhead rises to 102.5 ms. - Effective: Evidence shows it detects unsafe intent substantially earlier than baselines across 5 benchmarks, with the streaming-specific StreamSafe benchmark showing particular advantage; full-response F1 is maintained at 88.7%, so streaming capability does not degrade standard classification. - Scalable: Tested on responses averaging 160 tokens / 16 sentences; performance at 1000+ token reasoning traces (common in DeepSeek-R1-class models) is not evaluated, and the chunking overhead at very high decoding speeds (>320 tokens/s) begins to surface — the paper acknowledges this but does not test it. The software artifact (model + buffer framework) is promised for open release; currently exists only in paper form. Strengths: - The asynchronous buffer architecture is a clean, deployment-ready engineering insight: by tolerating a one-sentence display lag it converts a sequential bottleneck into a parallel overlap, achieving early-detection benefits at near-zero latency cost in the common safe-response case. - StreamSafe's annotation schema (safe/uncertain/unsafe per prefix with structured evidence) captures the temporal evolution of safety risk in a way no prior public dataset does, and the dense-sampling strategy around unsafe transition points is a principled training design. - The backbone generalization experiment (Table 4) is a strong robustness check: gains from 3B to 30B parameters are nearly identical, confirming the contribution is in the data and supervision design rather than model scale. - Maintaining competitive full-response F1 (avg. 88.7%, first overall) while achieving large streaming gains demonstrates that the method does not trade one capability for the other — a common failure mode in streaming safety work. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly limit scope to text-only responses and acknowledge that extending to multimodal (audio, image, video) is a future direction, particularly noting that audio lacks explicit sentence boundaries, requiring new chunking strategies. The authors handle this directly, which is a credibility positive. They also explicitly discuss dual-use risks of StreamSafe (could be misused for jailbreak optimization) and recommend controlled access. No other limitations are acknowledged. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The most significant unaddressed gap is adversarial robustness to attacks that exploit the sentence-boundary timing: an adversary aware of SentGuard could craft prompts that front-load benign sentences and embed harmful content only in later chunks — the paper includes no evaluation against such targeted evasion. The latency evaluation uses replayed simulation traces rather than a live serving stack; system-level bottlenecks (tokenizer overhead, network, batch scheduling) that would affect real deployment are not characterized. The safe/unsafe label in StreamSafe's training set is derived in part from simulated harmful queries generated by the authors' own red-teaming framework, creating a potential closed-loop alignment between the training distribution and the evaluation distribution on StreamSafe specifically — the cross-benchmark results partially mitigate this but the concern stands. The uncertain-as-unsafe strict mapping is used in all reported streaming numbers without ablating the threshold η or the loose mapping's streaming performance, so the FP/recall trade-off curve is not characterized. Training on a single seed with no reported variance means reproducibility confidence is limited. Evaluation on very long-context reasoning traces (where the guard must remain stable over many sentences of safe chain-of-thought before a harmful conclusion) is not tested despite this being the primary motivating deployment scenario. Bottom Line: SentGuard delivers a practically deployable, well-validated solution to a real production problem — streaming LLM safety — with a clean architecture, strong empirical gains over current baselines, and a useful accompanying dataset; practitioners building streaming LLM serving infrastructure should read it, though adversarial robustness to timing-aware attacks remains an open and important question before production deployment in high-stakes contexts.
Recommended 2606.02041 PDF
#73 — Innovative (66)

SpatialClaw: Rethinking Action Interface for Agentic Spatial Reasoning (80 ▲ )

Seokju Cho, Ryo Hachiuma, Abhishek Badki, Hang Su, Byung-Kwan Lee, Chan Hee Song, Sifei Liu, Subhashree Radhakrishnan, Seungryong Kim, Yu-Chiang Frank Wang, Min-Hung Chen · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 12 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SpatialClaw, a training-free agentic framework for 3D/4D spatial reasoning that replaces single-pass code execution and structured tool-call interfaces with a persistent, stateful Python kernel in which a VLM writes one executable code cell per step, conditioned on all prior outputs. - Why it's important: Spatial reasoning—metric distances, camera motion, multi-view geometry, temporal correspondence—remains a fundamental weakness in VLMs, and the bottleneck for tool-augmented agents is not tool availability but the rigidity of the action interface through which those tools are invoked. - How it does it: A 5-stage loop (plan → generate code cell → execute in persistent IPython kernel → assemble feedback of stdout + variable summaries + rendered images → submit answer) allows the agent to inspect intermediate perception outputs (masks, depth maps, point clouds) and revise its geometric analysis across steps using NumPy, SciPy, and Matplotlib in addition to Depth Anything 3 and SAM3 primitives. - The good: +11.2 percentage points average accuracy over the next-best spatial agent (SpaceTools) across 20 benchmarks and 6 VLM backbones ranging from 26B to 397B parameters, with zero benchmark- or model-specific adaptation, and largest gains (+18.3pp on DSI-Bench, +14.3pp on MindCube) precisely on tasks requiring chained geometric computation. - The missing: The paper reports no latency or cost-per-query figures; a system running up to 30 LLM steps plus repeated Depth Anything 3 and SAM3 GPU calls per question could be prohibitively slow for real-world deployment, and this is entirely uncharacterized. - Read it or not: Researchers building spatial reasoning agents or studying agentic VLM architectures should read it; the 3-way controlled action interface ablation (Table 2) with identical toolsets is the paper's most reusable finding and cleanly motivates the design principle.
Why this rank: Systems profile (training-free framework, code released). Practical Impact (65) and the systems weighting reward a reusable, model-agnostic artifact validated across 6 backbones (26B-397B) and 20 benchmarks with zero per-model adaptation — unusually strong generalization evidence. The standout is the 3-way controlled action-interface ablation holding toolset/prompts/backbone constant (Non-Obviousness 70). The method is an acknowledged CodeAct adaptation, capping Novelty (62). The complete absence of latency/cost reporting for a system running up to 30 LLM steps plus repeated GPU perception calls materially limits Completeness (65) and deployment assessment. Weighted (systems): 0.15*62+0.15*68+0.10*70+0.15*65+0.15*65+0.30*65 = 66.
systems Novelty 62 Rigor 68 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 65 Resilience 65 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation VLMs fail at tasks requiring structured 3D/4D reasoning—metric distance, camera pose, object motion, multi-view geometry, temporal correspondence—because these require composing perceptual signals into explicit geometric computations that VLMs cannot perform reliably from pixels alone. Tool-augmented agents provide specialist perception modules (depth estimators, segmenters) but are bounded by their action interface. Single-pass code execution requires committing to a complete analysis strategy before observing any intermediate mask or depth map; a wrong segmentation or a depth distribution that does not match the intended object cannot trigger a revised computation. Structured tool-call interfaces (JSON/XML dispatch) prevent the agent from composing operations in ways that emerge only at test time—e.g., chaining a KD-tree nearest-neighbor search over SAM3 point clouds to answer a closest-point distance question. The gap is largest for dynamic 4D tasks (camera motion, temporal sequences, multi-view reasoning) where no pre-specified API can anticipate all needed geometric compositions. Key Idea The key insight is to treat code not as a one-shot program or a dispatch layer for pre-registered tools, but as an orchestration space in a persistent kernel: perception outputs (masks, depth maps, point clouds, camera extrinsics) become ordinary Python variables that survive across steps, and each step's stdout, variable summaries, and rendered images condition the next code cell. This collapses the distinction between "invoking a tool" and "composing tool outputs with numerical primitives"—a nearest-neighbor distance becomes `scipy.spatial.KDTree(pts_door).query(pts_heater, k=1)` generated in response to observing that the centroid-based distance was wrong. The approach is CodeAct (Wang et al., 2024) applied to spatial reasoning with domain-specific design choices the paper is explicit about borrowing from. Methodology The framework runs two independent GPU-serving roles: a VLM backend (served via vLLM) and a perception backend (Depth Anything 3 DA3Nested-Giant-Large + SAM3, behind an HTTP service). For each question, a stateful IPython kernel is initialized with: InputImages (up to 32 frames at 768px long-edge), Metadata (fps, frame indices), tools.Reconstruct (DA3 → per-frame depth, intrinsics, extrinsics, dense point maps in world coordinates), tools.SAM3 (text/point/box-prompted segmentation, image and video modes), utility namespaces (tools.Geometry, tools.Mask, tools.Time, tools.Graph, tools.Draw), NumPy, SciPy, Matplotlib, show() for rendering into context, vlm.locate() and vlm.ask_with_thinking() for isolated VLM sub-queries, and ReturnAnswer(). The 5-stage outer loop: (I) A planner LLM session (no images, tool docs only) produces an analysis plan prepended to the main agent context—this separation prevents the planner from pre-concluding from question text. (II) Main agent generates Purpose/Reasoning/NextGoal/Code fields in markdown per step. (III) Code cell undergoes AST + regex security checking (blocks file I/O, network, exec/eval, GPU-backend imports) before execution in the persistent kernel; rejections are fed back as error messages. (IV) Feedback assembly: stdout, truncated tracebacks with post-fault code stripped, variable summaries (type/shape), and show()-rendered images appended to context. A per-frame type contract enforces frame-index alignment when composing PerFrameMask with Reconstruction point maps, surfacing spatial misalignment errors immediately rather than propagating silently. (V) ReturnAnswer() terminates; if Nmax=30 steps exceeded, forced chain-of-thought fallback. No training objective—inference-only. The unified system prompt encodes general spatial reasoning principles (median over mean, metric computation over pixel impressions, cross-validate across two independent evidence sources) without benchmark-specific templates. Key Results SpatialClaw achieves 59.9% average accuracy across 20 benchmarks (Gemma4-31B backbone) versus 53.4% for the no-tool baseline and 48.7% for SpaceTools (next-best spatial agent, same backbone, official implementation). The 20 benchmarks span single-image (ERQA, Omni3D, OmniSpatial, SPBench), multi-view (MindCube, MMSI, SPAR-Bench), video/4D (MMSI-Video, OSI-Bench, PAI-Bench, VSI-Bench-U, VSTI-Bench, DSI-Bench), general spatial (BLINK, SpatialTree, ViewSpatial), and general video (CV-Bench, PerceptComp, Video-MME, Video-MME-v2). Largest gains vs. SpaceTools: DSI-Bench +19.9pp, MindCube +19.9pp (Gemma4-31B), MMSI +18.2pp. Gains are consistent across all 6 backbones (Qwen3.5-122B through 397B, Qwen3.6-27B and 35B, Gemma4-26B and 31B) without modification. Controlled action interface ablation with identical toolsets (Table 2): No-tool 53.4%, Single-Pass Code 55.2%, Structured Tool-Call 56.7%, SpatialClaw 59.9%—SpatialClaw wins on 19/20 benchmarks over Single-Pass Code and on 18/20 over Structured Tool-Call. Tool ablation (Table 4, Gemma4-26B-A4B, 500 samples/benchmark): removing utility wrappers drops only 0.5pp (56.9→56.4%), confirming open-ended code largely subsumes pre-defined helpers; removing perception tools drops 5.5pp (56.9→51.4%), isolating interface contribution at +2.7pp above no-tool. LLM-judge attribution of SpatialClaw wins over structured tool-call: 52.2% code composition, 19.5% control flow, 28.3% interface-neutral. Failure modes (1,000 incorrect samples): geometric computation error 21%, tool-selection error 18%, tool-unsupported visual judgment 14%, VLM hallucination 14%. Benchmarks exceeding 1,000 samples are capped at 1,000 with fixed random seed; baselines use official implementations. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The persistent-kernel code-as-action-interface is an acknowledged adaptation of CodeAct (Wang et al., 2024) to the spatial reasoning domain; the paper is transparent about this lineage. What is genuinely novel: (1) domain-specific engineering (per-frame type contract enforcing frame-index alignment, planner/executor separation, AST+regex security sandbox, integration of DA3 and SAM3 as first-class kernel callables); (2) the systematic 3-way controlled action interface comparison across 20 benchmarks and 6 backbone models is the most comprehensive such comparison in the spatial reasoning literature; (3) the empirical finding that open-ended code composition provides systematic +3.2pp over structured tool-calls on the same toolset is non-obvious and has implications beyond spatial reasoning. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and well-structured: (1) 3-way interface comparison with identical toolsets (Table 2), (2) tool composition ablation removing utility wrappers and perception tools separately (Table 4), (3) LLM-as-judge attribution of wins (Figure 6), (4) failure mode taxonomy via LLM-judge (Figure 7), (5) per-metacategory win/loss breakdown across 13 categories (Figure 4). No error bars, multiple runs, or significance tests are reported; all numbers are single-run evaluations, though benchmark subsets use fixed seeds. Code and a project page are linked in the paper header. The paper is an arXiv preprint (v1, June 11, 2026); not peer-reviewed. The LLM-judge analyses use Gemini-3.1-Pro (proprietary), with no inter-annotator reliability check. The SpaceTools comparison is somewhat attenuated by the authors' own note that SpaceTools is designed for RL fine-tuning and may underperform zero-shot; this is disclosed but not resolved. SpatialClaw regresses vs. the no-tool baseline on a minority of benchmarks (e.g., BLINK −2.3pp with Gemma4-31B, SpatialTree −5.1pp with Qwen3.5-397B), and these regressions are not analyzed. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL (SYSTEMS). - Practical: Requires two independent GPU serving roles—a large VLM (27B–397B, served via vLLM) and a perception backend (DA3Nested-Giant-Large + SAM3); deployable on GPU clusters but not on consumer hardware. - Efficient: Per-query cost is high and uncharacterized—up to 30 LLM steps with multi-role VLM calls (planner, main agent, vlm.locate, vlm.ask_with_thinking) plus repeated DA3 and SAM3 GPU inference; no wall-clock time or token count is reported, making cost-benefit assessment impossible. - Effective: Achieves consistent improvements over baselines on 20 diverse spatial reasoning QA benchmarks; evidence is QA accuracy rather than downstream deployment outcomes (e.g., robot navigation success), but the benchmark diversity is broad. - Scalable: Tested across 6 backbone models (26B–397B) without adaptation, which is a positive scalability signal; however, video input is hard-capped at 32 frames and 768px long-edge, and neither cap is ablated—performance on longer or higher-resolution video is unknown. Delivers a software framework (code linked); perception models (DA3, SAM3) are third-party. Strengths - The 3-way controlled action interface ablation (Table 2) is the paper's strongest contribution: it holds toolset, prompts, and backbone constant and varies only the interface format, cleanly attributing the +3.2pp gain over structured tool-calls to the interface design itself. - Generalization across 6 VLM backbones (two model families, 26B–397B) and 20 benchmarks without any per-model or per-benchmark adaptation constitutes unusually strong evidence that the design principle—not a lucky hyperparameter—drives the gains. - The per-frame type contract (§E.5) that enforces frame-index alignment at composition time is a non-obvious engineering contribution that catches a class of silent semantic errors (applying mask from frame A to point cloud from frame B) before they corrupt downstream reasoning. - The negative result from ablation I—removing pre-defined utility wrappers barely changes accuracy—is informative for the tool-augmented agent literature: it implies that scientific libraries plus open-ended code largely subsume curated spatial helpers, reducing the engineering burden of tool design. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state in §H.1 that the primary bottleneck is now perceptual quality—both VLM hallucinations and DA3/SAM3 errors—rather than the action interface, and that further interface design improvements have diminishing returns at the current evaluation scale. They identify reinforcement learning for tool selection, geometric code quality, and error recovery as the principal future direction. The failure mode analysis (§C) transparently quantifies perception errors (28% of failures) alongside geometric reasoning errors (21%), which is a credibility-positive acknowledgment that the system's ceiling is constrained by underlying model quality. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The complete absence of latency and cost reporting is the most significant practical omission: a practitioner cannot evaluate whether 59.9% accuracy at unknown cost-per-query is preferable to 53.4% at near-zero marginal cost (no-tool baseline). A single-digit-second-per-query system is viable for research; a multi-minute system is not. - The LLM-judge attribution (Figures 6, 7) uses Gemini-3.1-Pro without human validation or inter-annotator agreement, so the 52.2% composition attribution is soft evidence. A model that produced code-based reasoning traces may be judged more favorably by a model family that also uses code-based reasoning. - Regressions vs. the no-tool baseline on several benchmarks (e.g., BLINK −2.3pp, SpatialTree −5.1pp for some backbones) are unreported in the analysis sections; understanding when iterative code execution actively hurts would clarify deployment conditions. - The 32-frame cap and 768px resolution are engineering constraints, not evaluated—whether accuracy degrades as clip length approaches the cap (truncation artifacts for long videos) is not examined. - SpaceTools is noted to be designed for RL fine-tuning and may underperform zero-shot; comparing the fine-tuned SpaceTools configuration against zero-shot SpatialClaw would determine whether the gap closes with equal compute investment. Bottom Line Practitioners building spatial reasoning agents gain a clear, actionable design principle and compelling empirical support across an unusually broad evaluation: persistent iterative code execution outperforms both single-pass code and structured tool-calls on the same toolset, and the gains are largest exactly where theory predicts—multi-step geometric composition across frames and viewpoints.
Recommended 2606.13673 PDF
#74 — Innovative (66)

Predicting student grades via adaptive multi-level learning models

AR Ibrahimzada, K Kosif, AS Gulsen, YS Yaglica… · nature.com · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a model-agnostic pipeline that partitions student-course training data into homogeneous course-based clusters via adaptive K-Means, selects a specialist regression model per cluster through season-matched temporal validation, and predicts letter grades before enrollment via a soft-ensemble weighted by centroid proximity. - Why it's important: Pre-course grade prediction from institutional records alone — without surveys, LMS logs, or external content — can support at-risk intervention and advising at institutions that lack rich behavioral data, and most prior work applies a single global model that cannot handle the heterogeneity of a real university course catalog. - How it does it: The framework runs K-Means for k=2 through k=⌊n/τ⌋, keeps the top-3 configurations by silhouette score, selects the best of 9 candidate models per cluster using the most recent same-season historical semester as validation, then at test time soft-assigns each instance to all clusters by inverse-Euclidean distance and combines cluster predictions as a weighted sum. - The good: On 46,827 instances (SEHIR dataset) the framework achieves RMSE 0.57 vs. RMSE 1.02 for the best prior method (Cakmak 2017), with statistical significance at p ≈ 9×10⁻¹⁵⁵ (Mann-Whitney U on squared errors), and it outperforms Zhang et al.'s knowledge-graph collaborative filtering on the external ANHUI dataset (W-RMSE 0.84 vs. 1.02) without using any course content. - The missing: The primary SEHIR dataset is proprietary and non-reproducible, and no comparison to any post-2022 deep learning EDM method is included; the improvement over the global best baseline is only 0.03 RMSE absolute (0.60→0.57), whose practical advising significance is never analyzed at the per-student level. - Read it or not: Worth reading for EDM practitioners building pre-course grade prediction systems under institutional data constraints; ML researchers will find the algorithmic contribution modest — a careful pipeline engineering of well-known components rather than a methodological advance.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. This is the strongest paper in the cluster and resembles the user's positive set in methodological discipline. Rigor (78) drives the score — released code, multiple ablations (student- vs course-based clustering, τ-sweep, 9-model selection), non-parametric significance testing with bootstrap, and explicit data-leakage formalization; reduced by the proprietary SEHIR primary dataset, in-dataset τ tuning, and preprint status. Novelty (50) is moderate — the contribution is a careful integration of well-known components (adaptive K-Means, season-matched validation, soft-ensemble) rather than a methodological invention. Non-Obviousness (60) is lifted by the genuine, quantified finding that course-based clustering dominates student-based clustering. Completeness (72) is helped by an honest four-point limitations section but dinged by the misleading '18.6%' headline that compares against a weak CF baseline rather than the 0.03-RMSE gain over the strong global model. Resilience (65) and Impact (65, open-source repo) round it out. Weighted: 0.20*50 + 0.25*78 + 0.15*60 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*65 + 0.10*65 ≈ 66.
empirical Novelty 50 Rigor 78 Non-Obv 60 Completeness 72 Resilience 65 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Universities need to predict exact letter grades for individual students in specific courses before those courses begin, to enable academic advising, at-risk intervention, and resource planning. The dominant limitation of prior work is threefold: most methods predict binary pass/fail outcomes during a course rather than ordinal grades before it; approaches that do target grades typically depend on data unavailable at many institutions (SAT scores, LMS clickstreams, course syllabi, knowledge graphs); and nearly all methods apply a single global model to the entire student population, ignoring the fundamental heterogeneity between course types (introductory vs. senior-level, grading-curve-heavy vs. mastery-based). Collaborative filtering methods require dense student–course interaction matrices and struggle with cold-start and sparsity. The specific gap the paper addresses is exact pre-course grade regression using only course enrollment and historical grade records, across institutionally diverse settings. Key Idea The core insight is that student-course grade distributions form a mixture of course-archetype-specific distributions — not a single global distribution — and that segmenting by course characteristics rather than student characteristics produces clusters that are far more internally homogeneous (silhouette 0.20 vs. 0.12 for student-based partitioning). Within each homogeneous course cluster, a simple variance-reduction ensemble (Bagging) can operate near its theoretical optimum because the feature space is no longer contaminated by conflicting grading curves from incompatible course types. Season-matched temporal validation then ensures the model selection step uses only historically consistent data, avoiding the cross-season distributional noise that a random holdout would introduce. Methodology The framework operates on a rolling temporal split: for each target semester t, training uses all prior semesters {t₁,…,tₙ} and testing uses tₙ₊₁, with no information from the test period used in any feature computation. Three phases execute per target semester: Phase 1 — Adaptive Clustering (Algorithm 1): K-Means sweeps k from 2 to ⌊n/τ⌋. After each run, clusters smaller than τ are iteratively merged with their nearest centroid (Algorithm 2). The sweep halts when increasing k yields a previously observed cluster structure. The top-3 configurations by silhouette score are retained. τ is set via the "one-in-ten" rule: τ ≥ 10×D where D is the post-encoding feature count (D=59, yielding τ=590; empirical optimum found at τ=300 with < 0.005 RMSE difference). Phase 2 — Per-Cluster Model Selection: For each of the three candidate cluster configurations, each cluster j selects its best model from nine candidates: Ridge, Lasso, SVR-RBF, Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, Bagging, MLP (128–64–32 neurons, ReLU, Adam), kNN (k=5, distance-weighted), and item-based collaborative filtering (Euclidean distance similarity). Validation uses the most recent historical semester matching the target season (Fall→previous Fall; Spring→previous Spring). Cross-season fallbacks (80/20 random splits) handle early semesters with insufficient same-season history. A data-density fallback guard additionally triggers 80/20 splits when strict chronological sequencing yields empty cluster training matrices. Model selection minimizes HEM = 2×RMSE×MAE / (RMSE+MAE). The configuration with lowest aggregate cluster validation error wins; its cluster models are retrained on all matching-season historical data. Phase 3 — Soft-Ensemble Inference: Each test instance x is assigned weight wⱼ = 1/(dⱼ+ε) to cluster j, where dⱼ is Euclidean distance to centroid j. Normalized weights are used to combine cluster model predictions. All aggregate features (mean course grade, mean subject GPA) are recomputed from scratch on the training partition at every temporal split — data leakage prevention is explicitly formalized (Eq. 4). Feature sets: student-based partitioning uses cumulative GPA, completed credits, and one-hot department code; course-based uses mean grade, grade standard deviation, and per-letter-grade percentages for that course in training data. Regression features include course level, curriculum year, student GPA, completed credits, subject-average GPA, course-average grade, and letter-grade percentage distributions (59 features total after one-hot encoding). All models use scikit-learn with hyperparameters from grid search (reported in Table 2). Random state fixed at 42 throughout. Key Results All evaluation uses strict rolling temporal splits on a DELL R720 server (Python 3.6.9, scikit-learn). SEHIR dataset (Istanbul Sehir University, 2010–2015, 49,347 instances from 2,759 students on a 0–4.1 GPA scale after filtering 11% pass/fail and incomplete records): Course-Based Adaptive Ensemble achieves RMSE 0.57, MAE 0.41, HEM 0.48. Global best (k=1, GBM): RMSE 0.60, MAE 0.42. Best prior method Cakmak 2017 CB: RMSE 1.02. Mimis 2019 GNB: RMSE 2.27. NN baseline (Mimis): RMSE 4.92. Statistical significance vs. global baseline: p ≈ 7.87×10⁻⁴⁰ (MAE), p ≈ 9.02×10⁻¹⁵⁵ (RMSE). Bootstrap HEM test: 100% of 1,000 bootstrap samples favor the clustered framework (p < 0.001). Bagging selected as optimal model for 31/32 course clusters (96.9%). Course-based vs. student-based clustering on SEHIR: RMSE 0.57 vs. 0.74 (p < 0.001); average silhouette 0.20 vs. 0.12. ANHUI dataset (Anhui Xinhua University Software Engineering, 382 students, 50 courses, 7 semesters, 19,100 instances, 1–5 normalized grade scale): Framework achieves W-RMSE 0.84, W-MAE 0.69, W-HEM 0.76 across 16,044 test instances. Cakmak 2017: W-RMSE 0.93. Zhang 2024 (knowledge graph + CF): W-RMSE 1.02. Statistical significance vs. Zhang: p ≈ 4.08×10⁻²⁸ (MAE), p ≈ 1.38×10⁻²⁰⁷ (RMSE). Data-density fallback guard activated in 22.36% of cluster windows; MLP wins 48.45% of windows (vs. 0% on SEHIR). τ sweep on SEHIR (200–2000): empirical optimum at τ=300; heuristic τ=590 within 0.005 RMSE. Counter-intuitive finding: global models degrade as training data grows in later semesters (RMSE increases from 0.56 to the 0.59–0.62 range), contrary to the usual expectation that more data improves accuracy — attributed to increasing distributional heterogeneity as the course catalog expands. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The method contribution is the structured combination of silhouette-guided adaptive K-Means, minimum-cluster-size merging, season-matched temporal validation for per-cluster model selection, a data-density fallback guard, and soft-ensemble inference — applied to pre-course letter-grade regression. Each individual component is well-established; the integration is the contribution. The genuinely novel empirical findings are: (1) course-based clustering dominates student-based clustering for grade prediction by a large margin quantified in both silhouette and RMSE; (2) the optimal model family is dataset-structure-driven (Bagging for dense traditional data; MLP for sparse block-cohort data), which the framework automatically discovers without manual configuration. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations present: student-based vs. course-based clustering, τ sweep (200–2000), global vs. clustered (seven semesters each), 9-candidate model evaluation per cluster. Statistical testing is non-parametric (Mann-Whitney U) and appropriate given the right-skewed error distributions; bootstrap testing used for the composite HEM metric.
