Effect sizes are reported alongside p-values. Code is released at github.com/itu-bioinformatics-database-lab/adaptive-multi-level-course-grade-prediction-framework. The ANHUI dataset is publicly available (MOOCCube). The primary SEHIR dataset is proprietary and non-reproducible, which is the single largest reproducibility limitation. Methodological concern: τ=300 is reported as the benchmark value because it is the empirical optimum on SEHIR — this is a form of in-dataset tuning, though the paper demonstrates the heuristic τ=590 performs within 0.005 RMSE, partially mitigating the concern. The extreme p-values (p~10⁻¹⁵⁵) are partly an artifact of large N (46,827), and the 0.03 absolute RMSE improvement over the best global model warrants independent per-student utility analysis not provided. The paper's abstract page reads "Preprint submitted to Elsevier" while the cover page credits Scientific Reports — a pre-press editing artifact that may affect citation. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL — the paper runs and evaluates a real system on institutional data. - Practical: Runs entirely on scikit-learn on a standard 24-core server with 80 GB RAM; no GPU or specialized hardware required. Institutional deployment requires only historical grade records in tabular form. - Efficient: The k-sweep plus 9-model validation per cluster per semester imposes meaningfully higher compute than a single global model. No wall-clock times are reported, so the overhead cannot be quantified. The paper asserts the retraining is "easily parallelizable" without benchmarking this claim. - Effective: RMSE 0.57 on a 0–4.1 scale corresponds to roughly 0.57 grade points average error — approximately half a letter grade. The paper does not decompose accuracy by student seniority, course difficulty tier, or department, so it is unknown whether predictions are uniformly useful across advising scenarios or whether errors concentrate in high-stakes cases. - Scalable: Tested on up to ~12,000 instances per semester (SEHIR final semesters) and 382 students (ANHUI). No experiment at 10× or 100× scale. The paper acknowledges computational overhead but does not test or claim performance at large research university scale (e.g., 50,000+ students). The work delivers a reusable software artifact (GitHub repo, scikit-learn compatible). Strengths - The temporal evaluation discipline is rigorous: all aggregate features are recomputed from training partitions only, season-matching avoids cross-season contamination, and the rolling temporal split is correctly enforced even for edge cases (single historical semester, two historical semesters). - The course-based vs. student-based clustering comparison is a concrete, quantified empirical finding (RMSE 0.57 vs. 0.74, silhouette 0.20 vs. 0.12) with a principled explanation grounded in the structure of higher education — courses are more homogeneous entities than students across their enrollment portfolios. - External validation on a structurally contrasting dataset (ANHUI: block-cohort dynamics, single-semester course lifespans) exercises the fallback mechanism under genuine stress and demonstrates automatic adaptation to a completely different model-selection regime (MLP-dominant vs. Bagging-dominant). - Data-minimal design — requiring only enrollment and grade history, no surveys or behavioral logs — is the primary practical advantage over knowledge-graph or LMS-based approaches. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly identify four limitations: (1) cold-start failure for entirely new courses with no grade history; (2) feature asymmetry when institutional records are fragmentary (MOOC anonymization); (3) computational overhead from the k-sweep and multi-model sweep; (4) sensitivity to institution-wide grading policy shocks (e.g., mass pass/fail transitions) that decouple historical validation from the new target distribution. These are confronted directly and honestly. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The SEHIR dataset is proprietary and not independently reproducible; all primary quantitative claims rest on a dataset that external researchers cannot access or audit. - The abstract's headline "18.6% RMSE improvement over global baselines" refers specifically to improvement over the global collaborative filtering baseline (CF: RMSE 1.13), not over the global best model (GBM: RMSE 0.60). The actual improvement over the competitive global baseline is 5% (0.60→0.57), which is the honest comparison. This framing is misleading. - No comparison to any post-2022 deep learning or transformer-based EDM method. The Mimis et al. (2019) ANN baseline uses a shallow single-hidden-layer architecture that is not representative of modern neural approaches. - The extreme statistical significance (p~10⁻¹⁵⁵) is partially an artifact of N=46,827 — a null hypothesis test at this scale will reject for any nonzero effect. The paper does not report effect sizes (Cohen's d or equivalent) or prediction utility at the advising decision level (e.g., what fraction of at-risk predictions are actionable). - No per-student prediction uncertainty is reported; for advising use cases, interval estimates matter as much as point predictions, and a student told "you will get a B" needs error quantification to act on that information. - The finding that Bagging dominates 96.9% of SEHIR clusters raises a question: if Bagging-only (without the adaptive selection) performs nearly identically to the full adaptive ensemble, the model-agnostic selection mechanism may be adding complexity without proportional value on standard institutional data. Bottom Line A practitioner building a pre-enrollment grade prediction pipeline on institutional records will find this paper directly actionable — the temporal evaluation discipline, course-clustering insight, and open-source implementation are practical contributions — but the 0.03 RMSE gain over a well-tuned global Bagging model on a proprietary dataset, with no comparison to post-2022 methods and no per-student uncertainty quantification, limits its significance to the research community.
Search Results
#75 — Innovative (65)

Role-Agent: Bootstrapping LLM Agents via Dual-Role Evolution (30 ▲ )

Xucong Wang, Ziyu Ma, Shidong Yang, Tongwen Huang, Pengkun Wang, Yong Wang, Xiangxiang Chu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 10 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Role-Agent, a reinforcement learning training framework in which a single LLM simultaneously serves as agent and environment, enabling bootstrapped co-evolution through two modules: World-In-Agent (WIA), which uses future-state prediction as a process reward, and Agent-In-World (AIW), which reshapes training data distribution by retrieving tasks that match the agent's past failure modes. - Why it's important: Existing agentic RL training relies on static environments that provide sparse, non-targeted feedback and cannot adapt to expose an agent's specific weaknesses, limiting generalization on multi-step interactive tasks. - How it does it: WIA prompts the LLM to predict the next H environment states after each action, scores prediction accuracy via Longest Matching Subsequence (LMS), and uses this score to multiplicatively modulate the task reward; AIW prompts the same LLM to classify failed trajectories by error type, stores them in a failure library, and up-weights training tasks sharing the same failure mode. - The good: Role-Agent achieves 90.9% on ALFWorld and 71.9% success on WebShop with Qwen2.5-1.5B, outperforming GiGPO (86.7% / 65.0%) and GRPO (72.8% / 56.8%) at only 5.2% extra compute overhead, with standard deviations reported over three runs. - The missing: The method is evaluated exclusively on text-based environments with small backbone models (up to 7B), and the AIW failure library remains tiny (11 unique modes on ALFWorld), raising questions about whether the approach scales to richer, more diverse environments. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on agentic RL training or curriculum learning for LLMs; practitioners seeking production-ready methods or theoretical grounding should wait for evaluation on broader and harder environments.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (72) is the strongest dimension — std devs over three seeds, WIA/AIW ablations, hyperparameter-sensitivity tables, an efficiency breakdown, and released code, which is unusually well-supported for an agentic-RL paper; it beats its strongest current baseline (GiGPO) rather than a proxy. Novelty (60) is moderate: the dual-role co-evolution framing is original but recombines world-model imagination, process rewards, and error-taxonomy replay. The composite stays in the Innovative band because evaluation is confined to text-only simulators with ≤7B backbones and a tiny 11-mode failure library, leaving scale and generalization (Completeness 68) undemonstrated.
empirical Novelty 60 Rigor 72 Non-Obv 60 Completeness 68 Resilience 62 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multi-step LLM agent training via reinforcement learning is bottlenecked by the quality of environmental feedback. Static environments (ALFWorld, WebShop) provide sparse binary rewards that do not reveal which specific reasoning steps failed or what classes of mistakes the agent repeatedly makes. Synthetic environments that adapt to the agent's deficiencies require separate task-generator models and scheduling mechanisms, increasing complexity. The gap is: how to provide fine-grained, failure-targeted feedback for agent RL training without introducing extra models or manual annotation? Key Idea: The single core insight is that the same LLM being trained can also act as an approximate world model and curriculum designer: it predicts the consequences of its own actions (generating a process reward signal correlated with task success at r=0.41) and, when it fails, diagnoses its own error pattern and retrieves analogous training tasks. Both roles require no additional model, achieving adaptive environment behavior at minimal marginal cost. Methodology: Role-Agent builds on Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with two additions. In WIA, after each action at step t, the LLM is prompted to predict the next H environment states; Longest Matching Subsequence between predicted and actual states yields a predictive reward matrix r̃ ∈ R^{T×H}. The combined per-step reward is R_t = R_task(at) · (1 + R_pre(at)), a multiplicative formulation that prevents failed trajectories from being rewarded for plausible predictions. State-level advantages (from GiGPO) are combined with trajectory-level advantages via mixing coefficient α: A(a_t) = A^S(a_t) + α·A^E(τ_i). In AIW, each failed trajectory is analyzed by the LLM to produce a structured failure record (DOMINANT_TYPE, CRITICAL_STEP, CORE_LESSON, RETRIEVAL_QUERY). A growing failure library is queried at each training iteration to reweight the task sampling distribution toward historically problematic patterns. Three critical design decisions: (1) multiplicative rather than additive reward modulation—well-justified to prevent rewarding failed trajectories; (2) state grouping by hash-based LMS similarity threshold 0.9—inherited from GiGPO without task-specific justification; (3) failure retrieval by error pattern rather than task text—reasonable, enabling transfer across surface-different tasks sharing procedural weaknesses. Key Results: With Qwen2.5-1.5B-Instruct on ALFWorld, Role-Agent reaches 90.9% (±0.8) average success vs. GiGPO 86.7% (±0.6), GRPO 72.8% (±1.5), PPO 54.4%. On WebShop success rate: Role-Agent 71.9% (±0.9) vs. GiGPO 65.0% (±1.1), GRPO 56.8% (±0.7). With Qwen2.5-7B: Role-Agent 93.8% / 77.1% vs. GiGPO 90.8% / 72.8%. On search-augmented QA (Qwen2.5-3B), Role-Agent achieves 45.8% average across 7 datasets vs. GiGPO 42.1%, with larger gains on multi-hop tasks (+8.2% on 2Wiki, +5.2% on MuSiQue) than single-hop. Ablation (Table 3) shows both WIA and AIW contribute: removing AIW costs 5.0% on WebShop, removing WIA costs 3.7%. Hyperparameter sensitivity shows performance degrades sharply when prediction horizon H > 5%·T_max, and advantage mixing α = 1.0 optimal. Baselines are current (GiGPO, GRPO, Search-R1, ZeroSearch); the main comparator GiGPO is the immediate predecessor from 2025 and a strong baseline. Scale tested only up to 7B parameters and standard benchmarks; no stress-scale evaluation. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD. The dual-role co-evolution framing is original: the multiplicative reward modulation via self-predicted state accuracy (WIA) and the failure-mode-indexed curriculum replay (AIW) are new components. However, each individually recombines known ideas: WIA combines world-model-inspired imagination with process reward models; AIW is a form of experience replay filtered by LLM-classified error taxonomy. The genuine novelty is eliminating the need for a separate environment model by reusing the policy LLM in both roles. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations isolate WIA and AIW contributions (Table 3). Standard deviations over three runs are reported (Table 7). Hyperparameter sensitivity is studied for α and H (Table 4). A correlation analysis between predictive reward and outcome reward is reported (r=0.41, p<0.01, n=200 rollouts). Code is available at https://github.com/AMAP-ML/roleagent. Prompts are reproduced in the appendix. Methodological concerns: (1) ALFWorld is a narrow household-task environment; 11 unique failure modes across the entire training set suggests limited diversity and may overstate generalizability. (2) The LMS metric for state comparison is a surface string-overlap heuristic—it may reward plausible-looking but semantically incorrect state predictions. (3) The predictive reward correlation of 0.41 is moderate and measured on only 200 rollouts from one benchmark with one backbone. This is a preprint; results are not peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check): EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: Trains on 8×NVIDIA H20 GPUs with backbones up to 7B; within reach of academic labs but not lightweight. - Efficient: Role-Agent adds only ~5.2% wall-clock overhead relative to GiGPO on ALFWorld; AIW retrieval from a small failure library (11 unique modes) is negligible, though overhead may grow with larger and more diverse task pools. - Effective: Evidence shows consistent improvement over GRPO and GiGPO on all three benchmark types with statistical variance reported; the method demonstrably outperforms its strongest baseline rather than a proxy. - Scalable: No experiments beyond 7B backbone or beyond the three benchmarks tested; the claim that the approach extends to multi-modal or embodied settings is stated as future work, not supported by evidence. The paper delivers a software artifact (code released on GitHub) and a training algorithm, not just paper form. Strengths: - The dual-role framing eliminates the need for a separate environment model, making adaptive curriculum learning tractable without added deployment complexity—a genuine practical contribution. - The multiplicative reward modulation (rather than additive) is a principled design choice that prevents reward hacking from plausible-but-wrong state predictions; the justification is explicit and the ablation confirms it matters. - Standard deviations over three seeds, hyperparameter sensitivity tables, and an efficiency breakdown are all present, making the empirical claims unusually well-supported for an agentic RL paper. - Gains are largest on compositionally hard tasks (Look, Pick2 in ALFWorld; multi-hop QA), suggesting the failure-targeted curriculum genuinely helps structured reasoning rather than just improving easy cases. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly state three limitations: (1) using a stronger frozen environment LLM would improve AIW but compromise fair comparison with same-backbone baselines; (2) the state grouping similarity threshold is borrowed from GiGPO and may not generalize across tasks; (3) evaluation is confined to text-based environments, with multi-modal and embodied extensions requiring additional machinery (vision-language state descriptions, latent-state matching). These are real and the authors confront them honestly. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The failure mode taxonomy on ALFWorld is only 11 types across all training, suggesting the benchmark's task space is too constrained to stress-test the AIW module; it is unclear whether the approach remains coherent when the failure library must distinguish among hundreds of distinct error patterns at scale. - LMS is a character/token overlap metric borrowed from GiGPO for state comparison; no ablation compares LMS against semantic similarity or embedding-based metrics for state prediction quality, leaving open whether the specific metric drives performance or is interchangeable. - The AIW failure analysis relies on the same LLM being trained: early in training, when the model is weakest, the quality of failure analysis is also lowest, creating a bootstrapping problem the paper does not analyze (e.g., does analysis quality improve over training? Are early failure diagnoses accurate?). - WebShop and ALFWorld have deterministic reward functions; it is not stated whether the approach degenerates in stochastic environments where the same action does not reliably produce the same state. - No comparison with methods that use a separately trained process reward model (PRM), which is the natural baseline for the WIA component. Bottom Line: Role-Agent delivers a clean, practically efficient method for adaptive curriculum RL training of LLM agents that measurably improves over GiGPO on standard text-based benchmarks, but its generalizability beyond narrow household and shopping simulators remains undemonstrated, and researchers working on richer agentic settings should treat the gains as preliminary until tested on more diverse environments.
Recommended 2606.10917 PDF
#76 — Innovative (65)

DeNovoSWE: Scaling Long-Horizon Environments for Generating Entire Repositories from Scratch

J Zhao, G Chen, F Meng, WX Zhao, R Song, JR Wen… · arxiv.org · automated generated environments for agent testing - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces DeNovoSWE, a dataset of 4,818 instances for supervised fine-tuning of code agents on whole-repository generation from documentation, together with a difficulty-aware trajectory filtering strategy that calibrates quality thresholds to per-instance complexity. - Why it's important: Existing SWE training datasets overwhelmingly target single-issue bug fixes; agents trained on them fail catastrophically on repository-scale generation tasks (e.g., Qwen3-30B-A3B scores 5.8% on BeyondSWE-Doc2Repo without domain-specific training data), creating a data bottleneck that blocks progress on the more demanding end of software engineering automation. - How it does it: A divide-and-conquer pipeline decomposes each GitHub repository into capability-level chunks, synthesizes test-aligned documentation via iterative Draft-Critic-Repair agent loops inside sandboxed Docker environments, then filters generated trajectories using instance-difficulty-scaled pass-rate thresholds derived from fusing structural, LLM-annotator, and empirical rollout signals. - The good: Fine-tuning Qwen3-30B-A3B on DeNovoSWE raises its BeyondSWE-Doc2Repo score from 5.8% to 47.2%, surpassing the larger vanilla Qwen3.5-35B-A3B (43.8%) baseline; gains are consistent across two Qwen backbones and two benchmarks. - The missing: No ablation isolates the contribution of the critic-repair documentation pipeline itself (draft-only vs. iterated), and the evaluation benchmarks are tiny (50 and 104 instances respectively), making it hard to interpret 1–2 percentage-point differences in the ablation tables with confidence. - Read it or not: Researchers and engineers building long-horizon code agents or SWE training pipelines should read this; the dataset construction methodology and difficulty-aware filtering are directly actionable, though the proprietary construction backbone (GPT-5.4/5.5) limits exact replication.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (75) is high — NEW DATASET + NEW METHOD with a genuinely new test-trace-grounded documentation synthesis pipeline at 46× prior scale, plus data-driven difficulty-calibrated trajectory filtering. Rigor (58) is the main drag: tiny 50/104-instance eval sets where 1–2 pp ablation differences are within noise, no error bars, no critic-repair or signal-component ablations, and a proprietary GPT-5.4/5.5 + DeepSeek-V4 construction backbone that blocks exact replication (preprint). Non-Obviousness (70) and Impact (75, released dataset, 5.8%→47% gain) are strong; Completeness (55) suffers from no limitations section, no data-scaling curve, and no intrinsic documentation-quality metric; Resilience (62) holds on the headline gain but not the ablation margins. Composite ≈ 0.20·75 + 0.25·58 + 0.15·70 + 0.15·55 + 0.15·62 + 0.10·75 = 65. This resembles the user's positive set (rigorous LLM-training methodology) but is capped by small benchmarks and a closed pipeline.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 58 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 55 Resilience 62 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Frontier LLMs are approaching saturation on SWE-bench-Verified (predominantly single-issue bug-fix tasks in existing codebases), and the benchmark no longer discriminates well among strong agents. Meanwhile, new long-horizon benchmarks (BeyondSWE-Doc2Repo, NL2RepoBench) reveal that even top agents cannot reliably generate entire repositories from high-level specifications — a task requiring architect-level planning, cross-file dependency management, and multi-component implementation coherence. The bottleneck is training data: prior repository-level SWE datasets contain at most 104 labeled generation instances, far too few for supervised fine-tuning. Constructing such data at scale is non-trivial because raw GitHub repositories have incomplete or misaligned documentation, verifiable test-based evaluation must prevent oracle leakage, and long-horizon tasks produce partially-correct trajectories that are hard to filter with uniform quality thresholds. Key Idea The central insight is that generating test-aligned documentation — specification rich enough for an agent to reimplement a repository and pass its unit tests — can be decomposed by functionality: profile which functions and classes are exercised by unit tests (direct vs. core-indirect vs. non-core-indirect), assign them to capability chapters, and iteratively refine each chapter through a critic that enforces coverage of evaluation-critical APIs. This transforms the intractable problem of "write documentation for an entire repo" into a set of parallelizable, bounded documentation tasks, each checkable against known test coverage. Methodology The pipeline has five major phases. (1) Repository selection: Candidate GitHub repos are filtered to those with ≥90% original unit-test pass rate and ≥50% source code coverage when built in Docker, yielding 4,818 qualifying instances. (2) Divide phase: An overview-writer agent summarizes the repository; a capability-writer agent partitions it into functional chapters. Concurrently, a repository profiler runs the unit-test suite and captures runtime traces to classify implementation units into direct (test-invoked), core-indirect (execution-reachable and behavior-affecting), and non-core-indirect (internal, excluded from documentation) components. An LLM-as-judge maps each component to its capability chapter. (3) Conquer phase: For each capability, three sandboxed agents iterate: a draft agent generates initial documentation from code access; a critic agent checks API coverage against a rule-based missing-component detector and flags under-specified contracts; a repair agent revises until the iteration budget is exhausted. Merged capability docs form the final task document. (4) Leakage prevention: Docker environments undergo git history sanitization (full `.git` reinitialisation), pip cache and site-package purging, and runtime command blocking (no `pip install`, `git clone`, or `curl` to the target package). An LLM-as-judge audits agent traces for circumvention attempts. (5) Difficulty-aware trajectory filtering: Three signals are fused into a per-instance difficulty score: log-normalised executable Python line count (structural), and 5-level difficulty judgements from two independent LLM annotators. Weights are optimized by maximizing absolute Pearson correlation between predicted difficulty and empirical mean rollout pass rates on a calibration subset. Five difficulty buckets receive monotonically relaxed pass-rate thresholds (0.90, 0.85, 0.80, 0.70, 0.60 from easiest to hardest), yielding ~11k training trajectories from DeepSeek-V4-Pro rollouts. SFT uses lr=1e-5, batch=128, cosine schedule, 131,072 context length. All construction agents use GPT-5.4 and GPT-5.5. Key Results On BeyondSWE-Doc2Repo (50 instances): Qwen3-30B-A3B-Instruct baseline 5.8% → Scale-SWE-Agent (issue-level data) 29.2% → DeNovoSWE-Agent-30A3B 47.2%. DeNovoSWE-Agent-35A3B achieves 50.0%, within 2.0 pp of Gemini3-Pro (52.0%) and 2.4 pp of Qwen3.5-Plus (52.4%), and 11.7 pp below GPT-5.4(CodeX) (61.7%). On NL2RepoBench (104 instances): baseline 4.3% → 18.3% (Scale-SWE) → 23.0% (DeNovoSWE-30A3B) → 27.1% (DeNovoSWE-35A3B). All results are means over 3 independent trials. Ablation (Table 4): difficulty-aware filtering at (0.90/0.85/0.80/0.70/0.60) achieves 50.0%/27.1% vs. best fixed threshold (0.60/0.60/.../0.60) at 48.8%/26.4% — a 1.2 pp / 0.7 pp gain. The surprise finding is that a fixed threshold of 0.60 (maximally permissive) outperforms both 0.80 and 0.95, confirming that including partial-success trajectories from hard instances is strictly better than requiring high pass rates uniformly. Novelty Classification NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW METHOD. What is genuinely novel: the scale (4,818 instances, 46× NL2Repo), the test-trace-grounded documentation synthesis pipeline, and the difficulty-calibrated trajectory filtering with data-driven weight optimization. What is a combination of existing ideas applied in a new setting: the critic-repair loop is a standard agentic refinement pattern; sandboxed evaluation follows SWE-Gym/SWE-bench conventions; SFT on agentic trajectories is standard practice. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations cover the filtering strategy (Table 4) across 6 conditions with 3-trial averaging — adequate for the specific claim. However, there is no ablation of the critic-repair iteration count, no ablation of documentation-only vs. documentation+profiling, and no ablation of the difficulty signal components individually (structural vs. LLM1 vs. LLM2). Error bars are not reported for any experiment; with 50-instance evaluation sets, 1–2 pp differences in the ablation table are likely within noise. The dataset and GitHub repo are mentioned as publicly released, but this is a preprint (arXiv:2606.10728v1, June 2026) and release status is unverified. Data construction relies on GPT-5.4 and GPT-5.5 (proprietary, not generally accessible), and trajectory generation on DeepSeek-V4-Pro High, making exact replication of the pipeline non-trivial for most researchers. No code for the construction pipeline is separately described beyond prompt templates in the appendix. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS (data construction pipeline evaluated through downstream SFT experiments). - Practical: Evaluation requires Docker with locked dependencies; construction requires API access to GPT-5.4/5.5 and DeepSeek-V4-Pro, which are commercially available but expensive at this scale. The fine-tuned models themselves (Qwen3.5-35B-A3B, ~35B active parameters) are deployable on high-end consumer or cloud GPU instances. - Efficient: Construction cost is not quantified — the paper reports no token counts, API call budgets, or wall-clock time for generating 4,818 documentation instances with up to three Draft-Critic-Repair iterations each. This is a significant opacity for practitioners estimating replication cost. - Effective: The fine-tuned models substantially solve the stated task on held-out benchmarks (47–50% on Doc2Repo vs. 5.8% baseline), though the 11.7 pp gap to GPT-5.4(CodeX) shows the problem is far from solved. Gains transfer across two benchmark distributions. - Scalable: The dataset is bounded by qualifying GitHub repositories and the paper tests no scaling curve — it is unknown whether doubling to 10,000 instances would further improve performance or whether the method plateaus. The paper delivers the dataset but no software artifact beyond prompt templates. Strengths - The dataset fills a concrete and previously unaddressed gap: prior repository-generation training corpora were 50–104 instances; DeNovoSWE provides 4,818 with high test coverage (mean 85.5%, median 89.6%), directly enabling SFT at this task scope for the first time at meaningful scale. - The repository profiler's distinction between direct, core-indirect, and non-core-indirect components is a principled mechanism for controlling documentation information content — ensuring the task is neither under-specified (missing required APIs) nor trivially leaked (implementation copy-paste). - Difficulty-aware trajectory filtering with Pearson-optimized multi-signal weights is a principled, data-driven improvement over ad-hoc threshold selection, and the ablation validates the individual contribution of the adaptive component. - Sandboxed leakage prevention is unusually thorough (git sanitization, pip cache purging, LLM-as-judge trace auditing), addressing a real attack surface in agentic evaluation that most SWE datasets ignore. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The paper does not include an explicit limitations section. The authors implicitly acknowledge that open-weight models still trail proprietary frontiers (50.0% vs. 61.7% for GPT-5.4-CodeX) and frame this as motivation for continued data scaling. They do not directly confront the small evaluation set sizes, the proprietary construction pipeline, or the absence of documentation quality metrics independent of downstream performance. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Small evaluation sets: BeyondSWE-Doc2Repo (50 instances) and NL2RepoBench (104 instances) are far too small to reliably distinguish 1–2 pp differences; without confidence intervals, the ablation comparisons in Table 4 are difficult to interpret statistically. - No critic-repair ablation: The paper does not show what performance would result from draft-only documentation, leaving the contribution of the iterative refinement loop unquantified. - Proprietary pipeline bottleneck: GPT-5.4/5.5 are used for all construction agents but are not publicly available; this makes the pipeline non-reproducible in its exact form and raises the cost barrier for community adoption. - No data scaling curve: Without varying dataset size (e.g., 500/1000/2000/4818 instances), it is unknown whether performance gains are driven primarily by data volume or data quality, and whether the method saturates before 4,818 instances. - Documentation quality not intrinsically measured: The quality of generated documentation is assessed only through downstream SFT performance — no human evaluation, information coverage metric, or comparison against manually written documentation is provided. - Potential benchmark/dataset overlap: The paper does not provide an explicit analysis confirming that repositories used in DeNovoSWE construction are disjoint from the evaluation benchmark repositories at the function or module level. Bottom Line Practitioners building long-horizon code agents have a direct, actionable resource here — the dataset, filtering strategy, and construction methodology are all contributions worth adopting — but the proprietary construction backbone and small evaluation benchmarks mean the reported numbers should be treated as strong directional evidence rather than precise measurements.
Search Results 2606.10728 PDF
#77 — Innovative (65)

Computer-Use Agents as Judges for Generative User Interface

H Collaboration, CCUA Collaboration · openreview.net · automated generated environments for agent testing - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces AUI-Gym, a benchmark of 52 web applications with 1,560 programmatically verified tasks, and a Coder–CUA collaboration framework where a coding LLM generates and iteratively revises single-page HTML interfaces while a Computer-Use Agent judges them by navigation success. - Why it's important: CUAs are forced to adapt to human-centric GUIs optimized for aesthetics rather than machine navigability; this work inverts the usual paradigm by adapting the environment to the agent rather than adapting the agent to the environment. - How it does it: The CUA navigates generated UIs and accumulates two feedback signals—task solvability failures (missing functionality) and navigation failure trajectories—which are compressed via a CUA Dashboard into a single image and fed to the Coder for iterative HTML revision. - The good: Cross-CUA generalization holds (Operator-based revision improves UI-TARS success from 19.4% to 32.0%), and grounding error rates drop substantially (GPT-5: 34.42% → 19.89%), suggesting interface simplification addresses genuine agent-navigation deficits rather than overfitting to a specific CUA. - The missing: Absolute CUA success rates remain low even after revision (GPT-5 best: 26.0%; Gemini-3-Pro best: 47.0%), only 2 revision rounds are tested, and no human-designed-website baseline exists to bound the headroom. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on CUA evaluation or agent-native UI design; the benchmark infrastructure is the primary contribution, and practitioners expecting production-ready UI optimization numbers will be underwhelmed by the absolute success rates.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (78) is the strength — the problem inversion (optimize the environment for the agent rather than the agent for the environment) is a genuinely new formulation distinct from all prior CUA benchmarks, paired with the empirical finding that VLM-as-Judge is near-random for trajectory verification. Rigor (55) is pulled down by single-run results with no error bars despite stochastic CUA navigation, only 6.4% human validation, and an unanalyzed circularity (GPT-5 both constructs apps/verifiers and is the primary evaluated Coder). Non-Obviousness (78) is high; Completeness (62) and Resilience (58) are limited by low absolute success rates (best 47%) that the paper rarely confronts and the absence of any human-designed-website baseline. Impact (60) reflects a released benchmark + framework but no trained weights and proof-of-concept-level gains. Composite ≈ 0.20·78 + 0.25·55 + 0.15·78 + 0.15·62 + 0.15·58 + 0.10·60 = 65.
empirical Novelty 78 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 78 Completeness 62 Resilience 58 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Computer-Use Agents are trained to navigate GUIs designed for human aesthetics—animations, colorful layouts, multi-step visual hierarchies—properties that are irrelevant or actively harmful to automated task completion. Prior work universally treats the GUI as fixed and adapts the agent via large-scale human trajectory imitation or reinforcement learning. No framework exists to evaluate or optimize the reverse direction: generating and iteratively redesigning digital environments that maximize agent task success. Separately, coding LLMs already generate functional single-page HTML apps from natural language, but these outputs are judged by human reviewers rather than by the agents that will use them. The gap is a testbed and method for agent-driven UI evaluation and redesign. Key Idea The core insight is that CUA navigation failure trajectories—previously treated as training signal for the agent—can instead serve as design feedback for the interface. Rather than asking what action the agent should take next in a fixed environment, the paper asks what layout, affordance structure, or interaction design would have made the task solvable without changing the agent at all. The CUA Dashboard operationalizes this: it crops multi-step screenshot trajectories to only the interactive regions touched by the CUA, tiles them into a single fixed-resolution image, and passes this compressed visual summary to the Coder as interpretable redesign guidance. Methodology The pipeline has four components. (1) AUI-Gym benchmark: 52 single-page HTML apps across 6 domains (App, Landing, Game, Interactive, Tool, Utility) seeded from OpenAI's GPT-5 coding examples. GPT-5 proposes 30 candidate tasks per app; human annotators filter for quality (~10–15 min/app, totaling ~10 hours; 91% agreement with human judgments on a 100-task random sample). A GPT-5-powered Verifier reads each candidate website's HTML at test time and generates a task-specific deterministic JavaScript checker function; tasks for which no checker can be constructed are discarded. Actual evaluation is rule-based JavaScript execution, not LLM judgment. Metrics are Function Completeness (FC = fraction of tasks with a valid checker) and CUA Success Rate (SR = fraction of solvable tasks completed by the CUA). (2) Coder as Designer: an LLM Coder (tested: GPT-5, GPT-4o, Qwen3-Coder-30B, Gemini-3-Pro) generates a complete single-file HTML/CSS/JS app and revises it based on language caption feedback. (3) CUA as Judge: two feedback signals—Task Solvability (Verifier identifies unsolvable tasks, summarized and returned to Coder) and CUA Judge (for solvable tasks, the CUA navigates and records success/failure trajectories). CUAs tested: UI-TARS-1.5-7B and Operator. (4) CUA Dashboard: multi-step trajectories (e.g., 6 × 1280×720 frames + action logs) compressed into a 1920×1080 image by cropping and tiling only interactive regions, with region size scaled by step order; this reduces visual content by 76.2% for 6-step trajectories. The Dashboard is passed to a Commenter model (GPT-5 for GPT-5 Coder experiments; Qwen2.5-VL-72B for Qwen Coder experiments) that outputs a structured JSON of actionable UI changes. The framework is formalized as an MDP with UI state, Coder revision actions, and CUA performance reward. Key design decisions: (a) deterministic JavaScript verifiers instead of VLM-as-Judge—justified by Table 3, which shows VLM evaluation achieves balanced accuracy ~0.55 and F1 ~0.24 on trajectory outcomes; (b) CUA Dashboard over raw screenshots—reduces token load while preserving failure localization cues; (c) two complementary feedback signals rather than one—the paper shows neither alone is sufficient for stable improvement across all domains. Key Results Main results across 4 Coders (Table 2): GPT-5 Coder baseline FC = 67.9%, SR = 24.5%; with full revision (+Task & CUA feedback): FC = 81.5% (+13.6pp), SR = 26.0% (+1.5pp). Qwen3-Coder-30B: FC 42.1% → 60.1%, SR 7.3% → 19.0% (+11.7pp). GPT-4o: FC 36.3% → 43.1%, SR 8.8% → 16.1% (+7.3pp). Gemini-3-Pro: FC 71.7% → 72.5%, SR 35.8% → 47.0% (+11.2pp). The average SR improvement across Coders is 7.9pp; improvement is larger for weaker Coders. Cross-CUA generalization (Table 4): Operator-based revision improves UI-TARS success across all 3 Coders, overall 19.4% → 32.0%, suggesting interface changes reflect fundamental usability improvements rather than agent-specific artifacts. Grounding error analysis (Table 5): GPT-5 34.42% → 19.89%, GPT-4o 54.22% → 36.08%, Qwen 32.15% → 29.64%. Dashboard ablation (Fig. 6c-d): Dashboard outperforms text-only and screenshot-only for GPT-5 Commenter (FC: 70.8% vs 62.1% vs 41.7%), but text-only matches or exceeds Dashboard for Qwen Commenter, indicating Dashboard benefit depends on VLM visual perception quality. VLM-as-verifier is shown unreliable: balanced accuracy 0.55, F1 (Pass) 0.24, Cohen's κ 0.15 against rule-based oracle. All experiments run at standard scale (52 apps, 1,560 tasks, ≤2 revision rounds). No stress-scale testing. Novelty Classification NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK (AUI-Gym: 52 apps, 1,560 tasks, rule-based verifiers), NEW SYSTEM/TOOL (Coder–CUA collaboration framework + CUA Dashboard), NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING (agent-centric UI simplification via feedback improves CUA navigation; VLM-as-Judge is near-random for trajectory verification). The problem formulation—treating the GUI as a tunable variable to optimize for CUA success—is genuinely novel and distinct from all prior CUA benchmarks, which fix the environment. The individual components (LLM code generation, iterative refinement loops, agent feedback) are standard; their composition for agent-native GUI optimization has not been done before. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations cover CUA policy choice, commenter mechanism, and number of revision rounds—meaningful but narrow. No statistical significance tests or error bars are reported for any metric; CUA navigation is stochastic and single-run results are presented as definitive. The benchmark was constructed using GPT-5 to propose tasks and generate verifiers for apps that GPT-5 also generates—creating a circularity where GPT-5 is both constructor and primary evaluated Coder; this is partially mitigated by including Qwen, GPT-4o, and Gemini, but the confound is not analyzed. Human agreement validation covers 100 of 1,560 tasks (6.4%), and the selection method for those 100 tasks is not described. Code, benchmark, and framework are committed to public release per the Reproducibility Statement, though no URL is provided in this preprint. Deployability (EMPIRICAL profile) - Practical: UI-TARS-1.5-7B (7B parameters) is runnable on commercially available hardware; GPT-5, Gemini-3-Pro, and Operator require proprietary API access, limiting fully open reproducibility. - Efficient: Each revision round requires navigating all 30 tasks per application with a CUA (30 rollouts × 52 apps), plus Coder API calls; the CUA Dashboard reduces visual token cost by 76.2% per trajectory but does not eliminate the navigation cost. Per-application compute is non-trivial at scale. - Effective: For weaker Coders (Qwen, GPT-4o), SR improvements are 7–12pp absolute; for GPT-5, the SR gain is only 1.5pp (24.5% → 26.0%), suggesting marginal returns for already-strong Coders. Functional completeness gains are more consistent. - Scalable: Tested only on 52 single-page, single-file HTML apps at ≤2 revision rounds; the paper explicitly excludes deep DOM trees, long scrollable pages, and multi-page workflows. No testing beyond this scope is claimed. The work delivers a benchmark dataset and a code framework; no trained models or weights are released. Strengths - The inversion of the adaptation target—optimizing the environment rather than the agent—is a conceptually distinct and underexplored contribution that complements the dominant CUA training literature. - The rule-based JavaScript verifier design is well-motivated: Table 3 empirically demonstrates that VLM-as-Judge on CUA trajectories is unreliable (F1 ≈ 0.24, κ ≈ 0.15), and the separation of GPT-5-generated checker code from its deterministic execution avoids model-in-the-loop bias at evaluation time. - The cross-CUA generalization experiment (Table 4) is the most credible evidence in the paper: Operator-trained revisions improve UI-TARS success, confirming that improvements are not agent-specific overfitting. - The grounding error analysis (Table 5) connects UI redesign choices directly to a mechanistic proxy (coordinate-level click accuracy), providing a causal interpretation beyond overall success rate. Author-Acknowledged Limitations Authors directly state: (1) AUI-Gym is limited to apps improvable via layout/styling/interaction simplification; complex DOM trees, long scrollable pages, and multi-page workflows are out of scope; (2) conclusions depend on the CUA policies tested—open-source CUAs introduce grounding/navigation errors, while closed-source CUAs exhibit different preferences; (3) the verifier pipeline may miss edge cases and does not capture human usability or accessibility. The Ethics Statement additionally acknowledges that agent-centric redesigns may reduce human accessibility and could affect UI/UX employment. These are honest, direct, and credibility-positive disclosures. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Absolute success rates remain low even under best conditions (GPT-5+Task&CUA: SR = 26.0%; best across all Coders: Gemini-3-Pro 47.0%). The paper reports relative gains but rarely confronts the absolute ceiling, which limits claims about practical utility. - Only 2 revision rounds are evaluated; Figure 7 shows SR drops for GPT-5 after round 2, suggesting instability or feedback overfitting with no principled stopping criterion. - Tasks and verifiers are generated by GPT-5 for apps also generated by GPT-5. GPT-5 may generate apps that are inherently easier for GPT-5-designed verifiers to validate, inflating its apparent advantage over other Coders. No analysis isolates this confound. - No human-designed website baseline is included. Without knowing how a professionally designed comparable app performs under CUA evaluation, the headroom and the magnitude of the environment-design problem are unquantified. - The Dashboard ablation (Fig. 6c-d) shows Dashboard underperforms text-only for the Qwen Commenter but the paper attributes this entirely to weaker visual perception. It does not test whether the specific Dashboard layout (cropped interactive regions, adaptive tiling) causes confusion independently of raw visual capability. - Single-run results throughout; no confidence intervals despite CUA navigation being stochastic. Bottom Line The benchmark (AUI-Gym) and the problem formulation are the primary contributions worth engaging with; the framework results demonstrate proof-of-concept rather than a deployable solution, and researchers working on GUI agent evaluation or agent-native interface design will find this useful, while those seeking production-grade automated UI optimization should note the low absolute success rates and limited evaluation scope.
Search Results
#78 — Innovative (65)

BiNSGPS: Geometry Problem Solving via Bidirectional Neuro- Symbolic Interaction

Q Wang, P Wang, F Yin, CL Liu · arxiv.org · neural symbolic - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces BiNSGPS, a closed-loop neuro-symbolic framework for geometry problem solving that establishes bidirectional runtime feedback between an MLLM Adviser and a Symbolic Solver, enabling iterative recovery from formalization errors and knowledge-augmented reasoning when the solver deadlocks. - Why it's important: Existing neuro-symbolic GPS pipelines pass neural outputs into symbolic solvers unidirectionally, meaning a single formalization error or a missing theorem in the predefined rule set causes the entire reasoning chain to collapse with no recovery path. - How it does it: The Symbolic Solver detects two failure modes—logical contradictions and deductive deadlocks—generates structured diagnostic feedback, and triggers the MLLM Adviser to either patch the formal representation (modify/delete predicates) or inject auxiliary hypotheses drawn from its pretrained geometric knowledge, all validated through a rule-based filter before re-entering the solver. - The good: The bidirectional adviser alone accounts for 17.2% accuracy gain on Geometry3K and 18.0% on PGPS9K in completion mode, and step-wise logical coherence reaches 96.0% versus 78.0% for the raw Qwen3-VL-Plus backbone, indicating substantially sounder proof traces. - The missing: Evaluation is restricted to two standard benchmarks (Geometry3K, PGPS9K) that the authors themselves acknowledge impose minimal auxiliary-construction requirements, leaving the hardest class of geometric reasoning—construction-requiring Olympiad problems—entirely unevaluated. - Read it or not: Researchers in neuro-symbolic reasoning, mathematical AI, or agentic LLM systems should read this; the bidirectional feedback architecture is a principled and well-ablated departure from the field's unidirectional status quo, though absent code release replication requires effort.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (75) is the high point — the bidirectional runtime feedback loop targets the root cause of unidirectional neuro-symbolic brittleness and is well-ablated (rectify-only, hypothesis-only, no-adviser each interpretable). Rigor (62) and Impact (50) pull the composite down: no code/weights released, the 100-question coherence study lacks inter-annotator agreement, GPT-5.2 baseline is uncitable, and the method is explicitly untested on the construction-requiring problems that are the hardest GPS class. Honest limitation acknowledgment helps Completeness (68). Weighted: 0.20*70+0.25*62+0.15*75+0.15*68+0.15*62+0.10*50 = 65.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 68 Resilience 62 Impact 50
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Geometry problem solving requires joint interpretation of diagrams and natural-language text followed by logically sound numerical or relational deduction. Symbolic GPS systems (InterGPS, E-GPS) offer high precision but suffer from a formalization bottleneck: they are brittle to initial parsing errors and cannot handle geometric configurations outside their hard-coded theorem sets. Neural and multimodal LLM approaches (G-LLaVA, GPT-4o) handle diverse inputs flexibly but produce hallucinated deduction steps that undermine mathematical validity. Recent neuro-symbolic hybrids (AutoGPS, GeoDRL) attempt to combine both by feeding MLLM-parsed formal representations into a symbolic solver, but the information flow remains strictly forward: the solver is a passive recipient with no mechanism to signal errors back to the neural component. A minor perceptual error in parsing—mislabeling a tangent as a chord, misidentifying an intersection point as a vertex—propagates through the entire derivation without correction opportunity, and when the solver exhausts its theorem library, the system fails silently rather than calling back to the MLLM's broader geometric knowledge. Key Idea The core insight is to treat the Symbolic Solver as an active diagnostic partner rather than a passive executor: when the solver detects a predicate conflict (e.g., the same angle assigned two different measures) or reaches deductive deadlock (no applicable theorem in library R), it packages the exact contradicting predicates or the current hypergraph state into structured diagnostic feedback and sends it to the MLLM Adviser. The MLLM then either surgically repairs the formal representation—constrained to modify or delete existing predicates, not freely hallucinate new ones—or proposes auxiliary hypotheses (geometric properties implied by visual context but absent from the theorem library) that resume the deduction. This transforms MLLM pretraining into a dynamic, on-demand extension of the symbolic rule set. Methodology BiNSGPS comprises three components in an iterative closed loop. Multimodal Representations Alignment operates in three stages. Standardized annotation invokes a small specialist model when diagrams lack labeled points. Dual-source extraction runs PGDPNet—a specialist diagram parser achieving >99% F1 on points, lines, circles, and spatial relations—to extract structural primitives (B, P) as anchored ground truth, while the MLLM parses textual assertions via few-shot prompting with visual grounding. Information integration has the MLLM synthesize both sources into the initial predicate set L, explicitly instructed to prioritize PGDPNet's high-fidelity B and P over the more error-prone textual extraction T'. A rule-based filter validates L against the formal predicate schema before any solver invocation. Symbolic Solver models reasoning as hypergraph expansion: geometric facts are nodes, theorems are hyperedges. It applies theorems in complexity-ascending order (by antecedent count). Each derived node stores directed pointers to its premises and the rule applied, enabling minimal solution graph extraction by recursive back-trace. Two failure modes trigger the bidirectional feedback: (a) logical contradiction—two predicates conflict—halts derivation and flags the conflicting predicates; (b) deductive deadlock—no applicable theorem remains. MLLM Adviser / Bidirectional Feedback operates in two modes. In rectification mode (triggered by contradiction), the MLLM receives D', T, the conflicting L subset, and the error trace; it modifies or deletes inconsistent predicates, using B and P as anchored ground truth to avoid inventing new hallucinated facts. In hypothesis mode (triggered by deadlock), the MLLM generates new predicates H via a rationale-first chain-of-thought—geometric properties implied by the diagram that the predefined rule set R cannot derive. If H introduces a new conflict, the diagnostic cycle repeats. Maximum iteration limit T=3; beyond that, a fallback invokes the MLLM directly in analyze-answer-proof format. Successful symbolic derivations extract the minimal solution subgraph, which the MLLM then converts into a human-readable step-by-step proof. Three critical design decisions: (1) anchoring rectification to PGDPNet's B and P prevents the MLLM from hallucinating new structural facts under the pretense of correction—well-justified by PGDPNet's near-perfect accuracy; (2) rationale-before-hypothesis ordering forces explicit geometric reasoning before formal predicate generation—a plausible hallucination mitigation, though not ablated in isolation; (3) the rule-based filter as a schema gateway prevents MLLM outputs from injecting malformed or non-existent entity references into the solver—well-justified as a deterministic safety layer. Key Results On Geometry3K (3,001 problems, 4-choice): 95.2% Choice / 90.5% Completion with Qwen3-VL-Plus backbone. Next best is AutoGPS (GPT-4o) at 81.6% / 75.4%—a gap of +13.6% / +15.1%. GPT-5.2 reaches 84.5% / 77.9% in pure neural mode, trailing by 10.7% / 12.6%. On PGPS9K (9,022 problems): 92.7% / 90.1%, versus AutoGPS best of 81.8% / 75.7%—a gap of +10.9% / +14.4%. On MathVista GPS subset: 92.9%, second only to DreamPRM (95.7%) which uses a Process Reward Model. Step-wise Logical Coherence (100-question human evaluation on Geometry3K): BiNSGPS 96.0% vs Qwen3-VL-Plus 78.0%, GPT-4o 70.0%—a 18-point coherence advantage over the strongest baseline MLLM. Ablation (completion mode): removing the MLLM Adviser entirely reduces accuracy to 73.3% on Geometry3K (−17.2%) and 72.1% on PGPS9K (−18.0%); rectify-only gives 80.4% / 75.6%; hypothesis-only gives 77.6% / 79.2%—confirming both modes are independently necessary. Alignment accuracy on 100-sample subset: BiNSGPS 80.0% vs GPT-4o 60.0%, Qwen3-VL-Plus 58.0%. Computational cost: 2,634 tokens/question average, 3.54 min average latency per problem. Fallback mode triggered on 6.56% of problems (105 questions); fallback accuracy 33.3% (vs 25% random baseline for 4-choice). Of cases requiring MLLM Adviser intervention: 170 resolved in 1 iteration, 77 in 2, 35 in 3 (fallback). Baselines include current frontier MLLMs including GPT-5.2, making comparisons strong, though GPT-5.2 lacks a published citation. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The genuinely novel contribution is the bidirectional runtime feedback architecture—structuring GPS as a closed loop where the Symbolic Solver actively generates typed diagnostic signals (contradiction traces, deadlock states) that the MLLM Adviser responds to with constraint-typed repairs or knowledge injections. The individual components—PGDPNet, hypergraph symbolic solvers, MLLM-based formalization—are adapted from prior work; the architectural pattern of runtime neuro-to-symbolic and symbolic-to-neuro bidirectional signaling is new in the GPS context and distinguishes it from AutoGPS's one-time pre-reasoning validation. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and informative, isolating the adviser's two modes (rectify, hypothesis) and the alignment module (with vs. without PGDPNet). However, the step-wise logical coherence evaluation—the paper's main qualitative evidence for proof soundness—is conducted on only 100 questions with no inter-annotator agreement reported; a study of this size does not support confident population-level claims. Error bars and significance tests are absent throughout. No code repository, pretrained weights, or formal representation datasets are released, making independent replication substantially harder despite the detailed system description. Comparison to GPT-5.2 lacks a citable reference and relies on self-run evaluations on a non-public model. The alignment accuracy study uses a 100-sample random subset of Geometry3K without stratification by diagram complexity. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2606.04648v1, June 2026) and has not undergone peer review. Deployability (EMPIRICAL) - Practical: The system runs on commodity API access to a frontier MLLM plus a locally deployable PGDPNet; no specialized hardware requirement is stated beyond what MLLM inference demands. - Efficient: At 3.54 min/question and ~2,634 tokens/question with up to 4 MLLM invocations per problem (alignment + up to 3 feedback cycles + proof generation), BiNSGPS is substantially more expensive than single-pass MLLM inference; no wall-clock comparison against baselines is provided. - Effective: The evidence from two standard GPS benchmarks and the human coherence study consistently supports the claim that BiNSGPS produces more accurate and logically sounder solutions than both pure-neural and unidirectional neuro-symbolic baselines. - Scalable: Tested on up to 9,022 standard GPS problems; no evaluation on Olympiad-level problems, problems with complex auxiliary constructions, or larger theorem libraries. Authors acknowledge the datasets' minimal auxiliary-construction requirements limit generalizability to harder settings. No code artifact is delivered; the paper stops at empirical reporting. Strengths - The bidirectional runtime feedback mechanism cleanly targets the root cause of unidirectional neuro-symbolic brittleness—error irrecoverability—rather than optimizing perception or theorem selection in isolation. - The ablation design is unusually informative: rectify-only, hypothesis-only, and no-adviser conditions are all evaluated, and the additive contributions are large enough (each adds 4–7%) to be interpretable rather than noise. - Anchoring MLLM rectification to the deletion/modification of existing predicates (constrained by PGDPNet ground truth) is a principled and operational hallucination-reduction strategy, distinct from generic prompt engineering. - The step-wise coherence metric captures a real failure mode of LLM-based GPS—logically invalid intermediate steps masked by correct final answers—that completion accuracy misses entirely; the 18-point gap between BiNSGPS and Qwen3-VL-Plus on this metric is the paper's most compelling quality claim. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly state two limitations: (1) Geometry3K and PGPS9K impose minimal auxiliary-construction requirements, so the system explicitly avoids prompting the MLLM to generate auxiliary points or lines; this means the framework has not been tested on the hardest class of GPS problems where such constructions are necessary. (2) Prompt-based MLLM output control is imperfect—the MLLM may enter runaway loops repeatedly questioning its prior steps, producing excessively long outputs. The authors treat these as future work rather than minimizing them, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The step-wise coherence human evaluation (100 questions) lacks inter-annotator agreement; this is the primary evidence for the claim that BiNSGPS proofs are logically sound, and the sample size is too small to sustain population-level confidence. - No code, pretrained models, or evaluation scripts are released, making independent replication contingent on reimplementing a multi-component system from text descriptions. - GPT-5.2 appears as a baseline without a published paper or API citation date; if this model's benchmark behavior changes between evaluation and submission, comparisons become unreliable. - The fallback mode (triggered 6.56% of the time) achieves only 33.3% accuracy—barely above the 25% random baseline—yet no analysis characterizes what types of problems cause fallback, whether they are systematic failures of the formal representation scheme, or what improvement strategies exist. - All accuracy figures use Qwen3-VL-Plus as the primary backbone; the single Qwen3-VL-32B comparison (90.3% vs 95.2% Choice) shows a 4.9-point gap attributable to backbone strength, but no analysis disentangles how much of BiNSGPS's margin over baselines is architectural versus backbone-strength confounded. - Alignment accuracy (80%) is measured on a 100-sample random subset without stratification, and the impact of the ~20% alignment error rate on final solving accuracy is not modeled or bounded. - Both evaluation benchmarks are well-studied and may have been used iteratively during system development; no held-out evaluation on a distribution-shifted test set is provided. Bottom Line BiNSGPS delivers a clear architectural advance over unidirectional neuro-symbolic GPS—the bidirectional feedback loop is well-motivated, well-ablated, and produces large accuracy gains on standard benchmarks—making it a worthwhile read for researchers in neuro-symbolic AI and mathematical reasoning, despite the absence of code release and the method's untested generalization to construction-requiring problems.
Search Results 2606.04648 PDF
#79 — Innovative (65)

MemDreamer: Decoupling Perception and Reasoning for Long Video Understanding via Hierarchical Graph Memory and Agentic Retrieval Mechanism (35 ▲ )

Cong Chen, Guo Gan, Kaixiang Ji, ChaoYang Zhang, Zhen Yang, Guangming Yao, Hao Chen, Jingdong Chen, Yi Yuan, Chunhua Shen · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 10 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces MEMDREAMER, a plug-and-play framework that decouples long-video understanding into an offline perception phase that builds a hierarchical causal graph memory and an online reasoning phase that answers queries by navigating this compact pure-text structure via a tool-augmented Observation-Reason-Action loop. - Why it's important: Hours-long videos generate 1.62M+ tokens at 1 FPS sampling, causing token explosion and attention dilution that degrades reasoning ability in every current VLM, including models with 1M-token context windows like Gemini-2.5-Pro (72.0 on LVBench). - How it does it: A perception model processes each 10-minute video chunk to build a three-tier graph (Video Root → Super Events → Macro Events) with leaf subgraphs where micro-events are first-class nodes connected by spatial, subject-object, and temporal-causal edges; at inference, a separate reasoning model navigates this graph using 7 tools, keeping each reasoning turn to ~6K tokens. - The good: Ablations isolate every design choice with large, consistent incremental gains, and a correlation analysis shows that end-to-end VLM accuracy is insensitive to backbone reasoning capacity (Pearson R=0.70, p=0.052) while MemDreamer accuracy scales strongly with it (R=0.897, p<0.01), providing a mechanistic explanation rather than just benchmark numbers. - The missing: Memory construction compute cost is entirely absent from the paper — how long and how expensive it is to process a 2-hour video with Gemini-3.1-Pro is never stated, which is critical for deployment decisions. - Read it or not: Essential reading for researchers working on long-video understanding, agentic VLMs, or RAG-based multimodal systems; less urgent for practitioners who cannot afford multi-frontier-model pipelines.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (70) and Novelty (68) are strong: micro-events as first-class graph nodes (enabling multi-hop causal traversal that SPO triplets cannot) plus the mechanistic correlation finding (MemDreamer accuracy scales with reasoner capacity R=0.897 vs end-to-end R=0.70) reframes the problem from 'bigger context' to 'better reasoner + structured memory', and the ablations are unusually careful with large consistent gains. Held below 75 by Completeness (60) and Rigor (64): preprint, no code, memory-construction cost (the most operationally critical number) entirely absent, all four benchmarks are MCQ, frame-sampling for closed-source baselines is uncontrolled, and the R=0.897 claim rests on 8 points with the perception model overlapping the reasoner family. Weighted: 0.20*68+0.25*64+0.15*70+0.15*60+0.15*62+0.10*58 = 65.
systems Novelty 68 Rigor 64 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 60 Resilience 62 Impact 58
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Hours-long video understanding fails for current VLMs due to two coupled bottlenecks: (1) token explosion — a 2-hour 720p video sampled at 1 FPS with a standard patch tokenizer generates 1.62M visual tokens, far exceeding practical context limits; and (2) attention dilution — redundant tokens induce the "lost in the middle" phenomenon that degrades long-range reasoning even when context fits. Scaling context windows (Gemini-2.5-Pro at 784K tokens) does not solve this: the model still relies on aggressive frame subsampling, achieving only 72.0 on LVBench. The fundamental gap is that existing systems tightly couple perception and reasoning in a single forward pass, so the model must simultaneously encode all visual tokens and reason over them — neither task is done well when the other is so expensive. Key Idea Convert a long video into a purely textual hierarchical causal graph, built once offline, so that a reasoning model can answer any query by navigating a compact structure rather than ingesting the raw visual sequence. The critical technical move that distinguishes this from prior graph-based memory systems is treating micro-events as first-class graph nodes (not just edge labels in standard subject-predicate-object triplets), which enables multi-hop traversal of causal chains (A causes B causes C) that flat or SPO-based representations cannot support. Methodology The pipeline has two cleanly separated stages. In the memory construction stage, a streaming adaptive segmentation mechanism divides the video into semantically complete Macro Events using a τ=10-minute sliding window, avoiding the arbitrary truncation of fixed-interval chunking. For each Macro Event, downward subgraph extraction builds a heterogeneous graph gi=(Vi, Ei) where the vertex set Vi = VEi ∪ VMi incorporates both entity nodes (person, object, location, group) and micro-event nodes (atomic actions with second-level timestamps). Three edge categories encode distinct relations: spatial-attribute edges (VE↔VE) for layout and coreference; subject-object edges (VE↔VM) for role binding; and directed temporal-causal edges (VM→VM) for chronological and causal dependencies. Upward hierarchical aggregation then clusters Macro Events bottom-up into Super Events and a single Video Root, with cross-tier SUBEVENT_OF edges and inter-episode causal progression edges. In the agentic retrieval stage, the reasoning model R operates a max-12-step Observation-Reason-Action loop using 7 tools: GetSummary, GetSuperEvent, GetMacroEvent, GetSubgraph (hierarchical navigation); SearchNodes, SearchByTime (semantic and temporal search over embeddings); and GetRelationGraph (multi-hop graph traversal). A critical design decision is observation distillation — instead of concatenating raw tool outputs, the model compresses each observation to relevant clues before appending to history, bounding the per-round context to ~6K tokens regardless of how many rounds have elapsed. Three design decisions are well-justified: (1) pure textual memory avoids expensive raw-video revisiting during retrieval and is shown to be robust (perception backbone swaps cost only 0.4–1.4%); (2) micro-events as nodes directly addresses the failure of static SPO triplets to capture temporal evolution; (3) observation distillation is validated by showing that passing the full memory as context (78.9) underperforms selective tool retrieval (90.7). Key Results MEMDREAMER achieves 90.7 on LVBench (103 videos, 30min–2hr, 1,549 QA pairs) with Gemini-3.1-Pro as reasoning engine, versus 78.2 for the same model end-to-end — a 12.5-point absolute gain — narrowing the human expert gap to 3.7 points (human: 94.4). On LongVideoBench (188 videos, 15–60 min) the framework achieves 92.9 vs. 78.6 end-to-end (+14.3 pts); on Video-MME long split 92.1 vs. 80.3 (+11.8 pts); on EgoSchema 88.2 vs. 76.4 (+11.4 pts). With Qwen3-VL-235B as reasoner, LVBench improves from 63.6 to 84.8 (+21.2 pts). The reasoning context is bounded to 5.9K–6.3K tokens, 41–124× smaller than end-to-end ingestion (240K–784K). Ablations on LVBench are large and systematic: Flat-Chunk 77.4 → Flat-Graph 84.8 (+7.4 from graph edges) → Hierarchical-Chunk 86.3 (+8.9 from hierarchy) → Full Hierarchical-Graph 90.7 (+13.3 combined); retrieval: Vanilla Embedding 70.5 → Full Context 78.9 → Agentic Search Only 80.2 → Full Tools 90.7 (+20.2 from tool-augmented retrieval vs. embedding lookup). Progressive tool ablation shows Graph Traversal contributes the largest single jump (+6.6) and Hierarchical Navigation adds +3.9. The strongest prior agentic system, VideoARM (OpenAI-o3, 79.7), is beaten by 11 points using a different reasoning backbone. Baselines are current and strong. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The three-tier hierarchical graph with micro-events as distinct nodes is genuinely novel for video understanding — standard LLM-based graph approaches (GraphRAG-style) use SPO triplets that cannot represent temporal evolution within an event. The empirical finding that decoupling unlocks a strong scaling relationship between agentic reasoning capacity and long-video accuracy (R=0.897 vs. R=0.70 end-to-end) is a substantive new observation, though it rests on 8 models. The ORA loop over graph memory is adapted from ReAct but the specific graph structure and tool taxonomy are original. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are unusually thorough: four memory architecture variants, four retrieval strategies, four round-budget settings, three top-k settings, and a progressive tool-category ablation all clearly isolate contributions. No error bars or confidence intervals appear in any table — all results appear to be single-run evaluations, which is common for these benchmarks but limits statistical claims. Code is not released; a project page is listed (aim-uofa.github.io/MemDreamer) but no repository URL is provided. This is an arXiv preprint (v1, June 5, 2026), not peer reviewed. Closed-source baseline comparisons use inconsistent frame sampling: Gemini models at 1FPS, Claude-Opus-4.6 at 128 frames uniformly, GPT at 50 frames — this non-uniform treatment could systematically disadvantage end-to-end baselines at different rates. The correlation analysis (R=0.897) uses 8 data points; while p<0.01 is reported, small-N correlations are unstable to individual outliers. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS — the paper builds and evaluates a complete pipeline on real benchmark videos. - Practical: Memory construction requires a frontier VLM capable of processing 10-minute video clips (Gemini-3.1-Pro used here) plus a text embedding model (Qwen3-Embedding); reasoning requires another frontier LLM; this is practical only for users with API access to closed-source proprietary models, not for on-device or cost-constrained settings. - Efficient: Reasoning uses 5.9K–6.3K tokens per tool-call round with at most 12 rounds (~75K tokens per query), far cheaper than a single 784K-token end-to-end forward pass; however, memory construction cost (time, tokens, cost per video-hour) is entirely absent from the paper — a material gap for practitioners. - Effective: Evidence shows it substantially solves the stated problem: 12.5–21.2 point absolute gains over same-backbone end-to-end baselines across four diverse benchmarks, with only 3.7 points separating the best configuration from human experts on LVBench. - Scalable: The 10-minute segmentation window bounds per-chunk perception context pressure by design, enabling scaling to arbitrary video lengths; behavior beyond 2-hour videos and on specialized domains (medical, scientific lectures, surveillance) is not evaluated and remains an open empirical question. The paper delivers a research pipeline, not a released library or trained model weights. Strengths - The correlation analysis between AIME2025 agentic reasoning scores and LVBench performance is a compelling mechanistic argument: it shows that the failure of end-to-end VLMs is not a reasoning capacity limitation but a perceptual bottleneck, and that MemDreamer removes it, allowing reasoning ability to transfer directly — this reframes the scaling direction from "bigger context window" to "better reasoner + structured memory." - The ablations cleanly decompose the contribution of each component: graph topology alone (+7.4 pts), hierarchy alone (+8.9 pts), their combination (+13.3 pts), and each tool category progressively (+1.3, +6.6, +3.9 pts), all on the same benchmark with the same backbone. - The video-centric graph ontology with micro-events as nodes addresses a genuine limitation of GraphRAG-style SPO triplets for dynamic content — the qualitative case studies show that causal chains encoded as explicit edges enable correct answers that end-to-end models miss even when they see the relevant frames. - The plug-and-play design is empirically validated: swapping the perception backbone costs only 0.4–1.4 points, confirming that the hierarchical graph effectively insulates the reasoning stage from perceptual quality variation. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The paper has no explicit limitations section. Implicitly, the authors acknowledge only that the memory is purely textual (and thus incapable of direct visual grounding for pixel-level queries) and that the system requires two separate frontier-model deployments. Neither limitation is confronted directly or quantified. The lack of a failure analysis and the omission of memory construction costs are not acknowledged. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Memory construction compute cost (latency, API cost, GPU-hours) is entirely absent; without this, practitioners cannot make informed deployment decisions — a 2-hour video requires roughly 12 × 10-minute Gemini-3.1-Pro calls for segmentation plus one aggregation pass, which may dominate total cost. - All four evaluation benchmarks use multiple-choice QA (A/B/C/D); open-ended generation, temporal grounding, or dense video captioning tasks are not tested, leaving it unclear whether the causal graph structure supports more demanding inference patterns. - Closed-source baseline frame-sampling rates are not controlled (Gemini 1FPS, Claude 128 frames, GPT 50 frames), potentially disadvantaging baselines at inconsistent rates; a controlled re-evaluation with matched sampling would strengthen the main table claims. - The correlation analysis uses 8 models as data points, two of which (Gemini-2.5-Pro and Gemini-3.1-Pro) also serve as the perception model that builds the memory — this creates a potential alignment advantage between the memory's language style and these reasoning models, which is uncontrolled. - No failure case analysis: what fraction of queries fail due to memory gaps (events not captured in subgraphs), hallucinated graph edges from the perception model, or exhausted tool budgets (agent hits Tmax=12 without answering)? Bottom Line A practitioner in long-video understanding gains something concrete here: the decoupling paradigm is empirically validated with large gains across four benchmarks, the ablations are unusually careful, and the correlation analysis provides a compelling new direction (scale reasoning capacity, not context window) — but the paper should be read with awareness that it is an unreviewed preprint using proprietary frontier models throughout and that the memory construction cost, the most operationally critical number, is never reported.
Recommended 2606.07512 PDF
#80 — Innovative (64)

Joint Structural Pruning and Mixed-Precision Quantization for LLM Compression

HL La, TT Le, A Taherkordi, PH Ha · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces TOGA (train-once-get-all), the first end-to-end framework that jointly optimizes structured pruning and mixed-precision post-training quantization (PTQ) of LLMs via a hypernetwork trained on the global language modeling loss. - Why it's important: Deploying frontier LLMs on resource-constrained hardware requires aggressive compression, but sequential or layer-wise approaches leave significant performance on the table because they ignore cross-layer error propagation and fail to co-optimize the accuracy-efficiency Pareto frontier. - How it does it: A hypernetwork learns binary masks for both structural pruning decisions and per-layer bit-width assignments simultaneously by minimizing cross-entropy loss plus a budget regularization term, using Gumbel-Softmax and the Straight-Through Estimator to allow gradient flow through discrete mask selections. - The good: At ultra-low precisions, TOGA-q reduces WikiText-2 perplexity by 5–21% vs. ResQ (strongest weight-activation baseline) and 9–59% vs. PTQ-1.61† (a LoRA-preprocessing-dependent weight-only baseline), while requiring no fine-tuning on external data; and the full TOGA framework achieves up to 2× prefill speedup and 6.5× peak memory reduction vs. FP16 using custom CUDA kernels. - The missing: Hypernetwork training uses WikiText-2 as both calibration and primary evaluation benchmark, creating a Goodhart's Law concern; performance benchmarks (latency/memory) are only demonstrated on Llama-2-7B, leaving generalizability across architectures unevaluated. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners and researchers working on LLM deployment compression who need hardware-realizable speedups beyond what unstructured sparsity delivers, though the WikiText-2 calibration/evaluation overlap and absence of code release warrant caution before adopting the perplexity numbers at face value.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (60) and Resilience (58) pull the composite down: the hypernetwork uses WikiText-2 as both calibration and primary perplexity benchmark — a direct Goodhart's Law concern — with no error bars, no released code, the hypernetwork architecture only in an absent appendix, and latency/memory measured on a single model (Llama-2-7B). The contribution is real — a unified hypernetwork search over pruning masks and per-layer bit-widths driven by global loss, plus custom CUDA kernels (Novelty 70) — and model coverage and baselines are broad. But the circular evaluation and incomplete reproducibility cap confidence. Weighted: 0.20*70+0.25*60+0.15*65+0.15*65+0.15*58+0.10*65 = 64.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 65 Resilience 58 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Deploying LLMs on edge or resource-constrained hardware requires reducing memory footprint and inference latency without catastrophic quality loss. Post-training quantization and structural pruning are the dominant techniques, but existing PTQ methods optimize a per-layer reconstruction error independently, ignoring how quantization error accumulates and propagates through the full network.
