This layer-wise greedy approach also applies fixed, uniform saliency thresholds for identifying sensitive weight channels, despite empirical evidence that inter-layer sensitivity varies substantially. Standard pipelines further compound this suboptimality by applying pruning and quantization in isolation or in a fixed sequential order. Unstructured and semi-structured joint methods (SparseGPT+GPTQ, OBR) address ordering but remain tied to sparsity patterns that offer limited wall-clock speedup on standard GPU hardware beyond NVIDIA's supported 2:4 format. Key Idea: Reformulate both structured pruning decisions and bit-width allocation as a unified binary mask search problem over a single supernet, and train a hypernetwork end-to-end on the global language modeling loss to discover which channels to prune and which to retain at high precision. Because the hypernetwork sees the full-model loss signal, it can assign more salient-weight budget to earlier and later transformer layers (which are empirically more sensitive) and fewer to middle layers, rather than applying a uniform threshold as Atom and ResQ do. The Gumbel-Softmax trick with the Straight-Through Estimator makes discrete mask selection differentiable, enabling end-to-end gradient propagation. Methodology: TOGA builds on DISP-LLM's hypernetwork-based binary mask framework for structured pruning and extends it to cover quantization masks simultaneously. The pipeline proceeds as follows: (1) A masked compressible supernet is constructed by applying pruning operators (input/output channel masks for MHA; input/intermediate/output masks for MLP) and quantization operators (high-precision Qh(·) for salient channels, low-precision Ql(·) for non-salient) to every linear layer, with pruning applied before quantization per Equation 10. (2) Activation channels are reordered by mean absolute magnitude over 128 WikiText-2 calibration samples to cluster outlier channels, preventing extreme values from inflating quantization scales. (3) GPTQ is applied to further reduce residual quantization error. (4) The hypernetwork is trained with a loss combining cross-entropy LCE and a logarithmic budget regularization term R(b, B(S)) that drives the effective budget B(S) toward the target b. (5) Gumbel-Softmax + Straight-Through Estimator converts continuous hypernetwork outputs into discrete binary vectors. Critical design decisions: (a) prune-then-quantize ordering is adopted based on prior literature showing it consistently yields lower perplexity than the reverse; this is justified by citation rather than an in-paper ablation across orderings; (b) using only two bit-widths per layer (rather than three as in SliM-LLM) avoids extra kernel-launch overhead during inference; (c) the hypernetwork architecture is inherited from DISP-LLM and described only in a referenced appendix, not in the main paper. Custom CUDA kernels (CUTLASS-based) implement mixed-precision INT4/INT8 GEMM and fuse RMSNorm with reordering/quantization stages to reduce compute overhead at inference. Key Results: (Preprint — arXiv:2606.07819v1, June 2026; not yet peer reviewed.) Models tested: Llama-2-7B, Llama-2-13B, Llama-3-8B, Llama-3.1-8B, Llama-3.2-1B, Llama-3.2-3B, Mistral-7B-v0.3, Qwen-3-8B. Evaluation: WikiText-2 and C4 perplexity; zero-shot accuracy on ARC Easy/Challenge, BoolQ, Winogrande, Hellaswag, MMLU. Quantization-only variant TOGA-q vs. ResQ at W(3.2)A(3.2): perplexity reduction of 5–21% on WikiText-2, zero-shot improvement of 1.6–4.5% averaged across six benchmarks. TOGA-q vs. PTQ-1.61† at W(1.6)A16: 9–59% lower perplexity on WikiText-2, 16–85% lower perplexity on C4, 2.3–5.4% higher zero-shot accuracy — notably, PTQ-1.61† requires 10,000 LoRA fine-tuning iterations on RedPajama while TOGA-q does not. SpinQuant (uniform W3A3) collapses entirely on Llama-2-7B (perplexity 438), confirming the necessity of mixed precision at sub-4-bit. Full TOGA (joint) vs. DISP-LLM + Atom sequential: TOGA dominates across the full compression budget range on WikiText-2 Pareto curves. OBR is excluded from ultra-low-precision comparisons due to catastrophic failure (perplexity > 1500 for Llama-2-7B at W3A3). Inference benchmarks (Llama-2-7B on NVIDIA L40, context length 2048, 100 runs): TOGA achieves 2× prefill speedup and 6.5× peak memory reduction vs. FP16; 1.3× prefill speedup and ~10% memory savings vs. OBR. Training: single A100 80GB, 2,000 steps for quantization-only, 10,000 steps for joint pruning+quantization. Experiments are averaged over 5 runs. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The genuine novelty is the unified hypernetwork search space that simultaneously optimizes structural pruning masks and per-layer quantization bit-width assignments via a global end-to-end loss, rather than applying these as independent sequential operations. The custom CUDA kernels for mixed-precision (W4A4+W8A8) GEMM are a systems contribution. The underlying building blocks — DISP-LLM hypernetwork, GPTQ, Gumbel-Softmax/STE, activation reordering — are pre-existing components applied in a new joint formulation. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations in Table 3 isolate the contribution of each quantization component (INT8 salient channels, channel reordering, GPTQ, INT4 KV cache) on Llama-2-7B — this is helpful but limited to one model. Figure 4 provides an interpretability result showing TOGA-q's non-uniform salient channel allocation aligns with known layer sensitivity patterns. Results are averaged over 5 runs with no error bars reported; no significance testing is provided. A critical methodological concern: the hypernetwork calibration dataset is WikiText-2 and the primary perplexity evaluation benchmark is also WikiText-2 — this introduces a Goodhart's Law risk and means the perplexity improvements on that dataset cannot be taken at face value without independent benchmarks; C4 and zero-shot reasoning results partially mitigate this but the concern remains. The hypernetwork architecture is described only in an appendix (Section 5) not present in the reviewed PDF, limiting reproducibility. Code and model weights are not mentioned as publicly released. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS (the paper both measures accuracy and benchmarks a built inference kernel). - Practical: Training requires a single A100 80GB GPU and is limited to models up to 32B parameters; inference benchmarks run on an L40 — both are commercially available. - Efficient: Hypernetwork training requires holding the full model in GPU memory throughout (O(model size) overhead), which the authors acknowledge is a major limitation for very large models; inference with custom kernels achieves substantial gains over FP16 and OBR. - Effective: Perplexity and zero-shot reasoning improvements are demonstrated across eight model families and multiple compression regimes; the latency/memory claims are measured rather than extrapolated, though only on one model (Llama-2-7B). - Scalable: Not tested beyond 13B parameters; 70B is explicitly stated to exceed current memory capacity; the claimed path forward (distributed training/offloading) is future work, not implemented. The paper delivers custom CUDA kernels and an inference pipeline but does not release code or pretrained compressed models. Strengths: - The global-loss-driven hypernetwork approach is a principled solution to the well-known failure mode of per-layer PTQ optimization, and the connection to the empirical layer-sensitivity literature (Figure 4) lends credibility. - The comparison set is broad and current: Atom, ResQ, SpinQuant, PTQ-1.61, BiLLM, SliM-LLM, SparseGPT+GPTQ, OBR — covering both weight-only and weight-activation PTQ, plus joint pruning baselines. - Custom CUDA kernels that translate accuracy gains into measured wall-clock speedups are a concrete systems contribution that most purely algorithmic PTQ papers skip. - Training without external fine-tuning data or LoRA preprocessing, while matching or outperforming methods that require such steps, is a practical advantage. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly state that the requirement to load the entire LLM on GPU during hypernetwork training limits applicability to models up to 32B on an 80GB GPU. They identify distributed training and offloading as the remedy and frame it as future work. This is a genuine and consequential limitation, stated directly. Analyst-Identified Gaps: (1) WikiText-2 is both calibration and evaluation set — the paper does not discuss this circularity or demonstrate that the gains are not partially artifacts of this overlap; the C4 and reasoning results are more trustworthy. (2) Inference performance benchmarks (latency, memory) are restricted to Llama-2-7B on an L40 GPU; whether the speedup figures generalize to newer architectures (e.g., Llama-3, Mistral) or other hardware is untested. (3) OBR is excluded from ultra-low-precision comparisons because it fails — valid, but this means the strongest claimed joint competitor is effectively absent from the most aggressive compression regime comparisons. (4) The hypernetwork architecture (Appendix Section 5) is absent from the reviewed PDF, making independent reproduction difficult. (5) Training cost for TOGA (10,000 steps, full model in memory) is not compared to the calibration cost of baselines like SparseGPT or GPTQ; if TOGA's training time is an order of magnitude longer, this is a significant practical tradeoff not discussed. (6) Evaluation is entirely on general-purpose reasoning benchmarks; performance on domain-specific or instruction-following tasks is unknown. Bottom Line: TOGA makes a credible case for joint structured pruning and mixed-precision PTQ as a step beyond sequential pipelines, with measured inference speedups and broad model coverage — practitioners targeting edge deployment of 7B–13B LLMs on standard GPUs will find the results relevant and the systems contribution (custom CUDA kernels) immediately actionable, but should independently verify perplexity claims given the WikiText-2 calibration/evaluation overlap before committing to this approach.
Search Results 2606.07819 PDF
#81 — Innovative (64)

VASO: Formally Verifiable Self-Evolving Skills for Physical AI Agents

Y Yang, NP Bhatt, K Wang, S Tetteh, Z Wang, U Topcu · arxiv.org · automated generated environments for agent testing - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces VASO, a closed-loop framework that converts formal model-checker counterexamples into natural-language textual gradients to iteratively refine reusable LLM-generated robot skill contracts — each represented as a coupled LTL specification, proposition-aligned labeling function, and plan template — without updating model weights. - Why it's important: LLM-generated robot skills can pass every sampled execution test while still violating temporal safety specifications on untested conditions; for physical robots, even rare safety violations (e.g., a drone briefly exceeding its altitude envelope) are unacceptable, and trace-level feedback cannot close this gap. - How it does it: Each skill is compiled into a NuSMV transition system via a proposition-aligned labeling function that bridges symbolic model checking and LLM planning; when verification fails, the counterexample is rendered as a textual gradient that updates the skill contract via LLM-AutoDiff, leaving foundation model weights frozen. - The good: On real Clearpath Jackal and PX4 quadcopter hardware, VASO achieves 97.2% specification compliance across 11 LTL specs in fewer than 100 optimization samples and under 20 minutes per skill, beating the strongest learning-based baseline RLVF (94.3% safety score) at one-fourth the training time. - The missing: The proposition-aligned labeling function is itself LLM-generated and unverified, so the formal guarantee is conditional on AI-generated code being correct — a foundational assumption the title's "formally verifiable" framing substantially overstates, and the paper's mitigation evidence (< 1% degradation in one narrow setting) is insufficient. - Read it or not: Worth reading for robotics and safe-AI researchers working at the intersection of LLM planning and formal verification; the architectural insight is real and the real-robot demonstrations are credible, but the narrow evaluation scope (4 task types, proprietary models, no code release) limits immediate replication.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile (per Deployability tag). Novelty (75, weight 0.20) and Non-Obviousness (75) carry the score: the dual-interface semantic contract and the move to treat model-checker counterexamples as textual gradients targeting the reusable skill artifact (rather than discarding the plan) is a genuinely non-obvious architectural insight. Rigor (52) is held back by single-run convergence plots, no variance with stochastic GPT models and 40-plan samples, mixing real-robot SS with simulated TC, proprietary models, and no code — a preprint. Completeness (70) benefits from a directly confronted limitation with a numerical bound, but Resilience (58) is dented by the overstated 'formally verifiable' framing resting on unverified AI-generated labeling functions and only 4 narrow task types. Impact (55) reflects credible real-robot demos but no released artifact. Weighted ≈ 64.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 52 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 70 Resilience 58 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Physical robot agents that use LLMs for task planning face a trust gap that execution-based feedback cannot close. Systems like Voyager, Eureka, and AutoSkill refine robot skills via successful rollouts, unit tests, or LLM self-critique, but these signals are trace-level: they confirm the skill worked on observed executions, not that it will satisfy temporal safety constraints under all possible conditions. In practice, even the strongest execution-feedback baseline tested in this work leaves more than 1-in-20 generated plans violating formal specifications. Formal verification has been applied to LLM outputs before, but only as a post-hoc pass/fail filter on individual plans — counterexamples are rejection certificates, not improvement signals. The missing piece is a skill representation that allows model-checker feedback to propagate back to the reusable artifact that produced a failed plan, rather than discarding the plan and starting over. Key Idea The core insight is a dual-interface skill representation called a semantic contract: each skill simultaneously exposes a formal interface (an LTL local rule plus a proposition-aligned labeling function L_sk that maps robot states, sensor readings, and control commands to logical propositions) and a planner-facing interface (a text-based plan template). The labeling function is the bridge — it lets the same skill artifact be consumed by a NuSMV model checker (which reasons over propositions) and by an LLM planner (which reasons over natural language). This makes it possible to treat verification failures not as dead ends but as structured optimization signals targeting the reusable skill contract itself. Methodology VASO operates as a two-level verification and refinement loop. At the skill level, the framework checks logical feasibility: given a combined specification Φ_sk = (∧φ∈G φ) ∧ ψ_sk, it constructs a universal transition system M = (2^AP, 2^AP, 2^AP × 2^AP, id) and checks M ⊨ ¬Φ_sk via NuSMV — if true, no satisfying trace exists and the LLM is re-queried to fix the local rule. At the plan level, the planner generates π = F_plan(C_sk, τ); Algorithm 1 compiles this into a transition system A_π by traversing the ordered API call / waypoint sequence and labeling each state via L_sk; NuSMV then checks A_π ⊗ M ⊨ G ∧ ψ_sk. Failure returns a counterexample trace, which is rendered as a textual gradient g (e.g., "violate specification: G(person_observed → X stop)") and fed to F_skill to update the contract: C_sk^(k+1) = F_skill(C_sk^(k), g, τ). This instantiates LLM-AutoDiff's textual gradient descent applied to the skill contract rather than a flat prompt or model parameters. GPT-5-nano serves as F_skill and GPT-4o-mini as F_plan. Three critical design decisions: (1) optimizing the contract artifact rather than model weights — well-justified by the ablation showing structured rep outperforms flat prompt optimization; (2) two-level verification ordering — well-justified to avoid plan-sampling compute waste on contradictory specs; (3) automatic proposition alignment via F_skill rather than hand-engineering — justified empirically by near-parity with handcrafted mappings, though the correctness assumption is load-bearing. Key Results On 11 LTL specifications and 400 generated plans across Clearpath Jackal and PX4 quadcopter platforms: VASO with handcrafted proposition alignment achieves 97.2% safety score (SS) and 85.3% task completion (TC); VASO with automatic proposition alignment achieves 96.8% SS and 86.5% TC. Against zero-shot baselines: LLM-Planner reaches 90.3% SS / 89.3% TC; ReAct reaches 73.3% SS; LLM+P reaches 79.5% SS. Against learning-based baselines: RLVF (fine-tuning with formal feedback) reaches 94.3% SS / 82.5% TC; DSPy reaches 86.8% SS. Convergence: VASO exceeds 90% safety within 7 optimization steps; LAD-VF (flat prompt optimization with verification feedback) plateaus near 85% after 10 steps. Held-out skill reuse: 92–100% SS and 80–96% TC on unseen task prompts within the same skill family. Skill-level feasibility: 89% of initial LLM-generated skills pass feasibility; 97% after one iteration. Training time: approximately one-fourth of RLVF. Evaluation scale is narrow — 4 skill types on 2 real platforms with up to 40 plans per skill. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The genuinely novel contribution is the dual-interface skill representation (LTL contract coupled with a proposition-aligned labeling function and plan template as a single reusable artifact) and the closed loop architecture where model-checker counterexamples become textual gradients targeting that artifact specifically. NuSMV model checking, LTL specifications for robotics, and LLM-AutoDiff textual gradient descent are all prior work; VASO's contribution is the integration architecture that makes them mutually compatible. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablation study is present and isolates four design choices: structured skill representation, automatic proposition alignment, iterative optimization, and skill-mediated planning. No error bars, confidence intervals, or multi-seed runs are reported; the convergence plots represent single runs. Task completion for the baseline comparison (Table 1) is evaluated "in simulation" while real-robot safety scores are measured on hardware — the gap between simulation TC and real-robot TC is not assessed. No code, trained models, or skill libraries are released; no repository URL is provided. This is a preprint (arXiv:2606.05395v1, June 2026), not peer-reviewed. GPT-5-nano and GPT-4o-mini are proprietary and unavailable for independent replication. The LTL specifications used are simple (single implication formulas, no nested temporal operators), and it is unknown how the approach scales to richer temporal properties. Deployability (EMPIRICAL profile) - Practical: Runs on commercially available ground robots (Clearpath Jackal) and drones (PX4) with standard velocity and detection APIs, and uses NuSMV, an open-source model checker; no custom hardware required. - Efficient: Under 20 minutes per skill and fewer than 100 optimization samples — approximately one-fourth the training time of RLVF fine-tuning; NuSMV model checking is fast for the finite plan automata generated here. - Effective: 97.2% specification compliance on 11 LTL specs across two platforms is credible evidence that the approach resolves real safety violations the paper demonstrates concretely, but effectiveness is shown only for 4 narrow task types and simple sequential plans. - Scalable: Untested beyond 4 skill types and 11 simple specifications; the authors explicitly acknowledge the framework does not handle concurrent or interleaved skill executions, and proposition alignment correctness may degrade with richer APIs. The work stops at paper form with real-robot demonstrations; no software artifact (library, CLI, dataset) is released. Strengths - The dual-interface skill representation is a genuine architectural contribution: coupling LTL specs, proposition-aligned labeling, and plan templates into a single artifact that is simultaneously machine-checkable and LLM-plannable is a non-trivial design advance. - Demonstrations on real physical robots (not just simulation) under realistic conditions, including sensor noise (YOLO detection) and physical dynamics, give the results more credibility than simulation-only baselines. - The two-level verification ordering (feasibility check before plan sampling) is an efficient and principled design choice that prevents the optimizer from refining contradictory specifications. - Automatic proposition alignment achieving near-parity with handcrafted functions (96.8% vs. 97.2% SS) substantially lowers the engineering burden and makes the approach usable without formal methods expertise. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly identify two limitations: (1) the proposition-aligned labeling function is AI-generated and unverified, so errors in proposition mapping can invalidate formal guarantees — demonstrated with a concrete drone altitude example where 2D vs. 3D norm confusion produces a spurious pass; and (2) verification assumes sequential, non-concurrent skill execution, so interleaved or overlapping skill executions are outside scope. The authors propose probabilistic grounding validation and multi-agent extensions as future directions, and they confront the first limitation directly with a numerical bound (<1% safety score degradation from auto vs. handcrafted alignment), which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The title's "formally verifiable" framing is misleading: formal soundness is conditional on AI-generated code correctly implementing propositions, which is precisely the assumption formal verification is meant to avoid. The paper's <1% degradation claim is measured in the same narrow task setting where the alignment was optimized, not on adversarial or out-of-distribution propositions. - The evaluation covers only 4 task types, none drawn from standard LLM-planning benchmarks (VirtualHome, AI2-THOR, ALFRED, BEHAVIOR-1K), making comparison against the broader literature impossible. - Table 1 mixes real-robot SS with simulated TC for baseline comparison without explaining whether simulation TC is a reliable proxy for real-robot TC, particularly for the learning-based baselines. - The held-out reuse experiment uses prompts from "the same task family" with unseen parameterizations — not true cross-task generalization; a skill optimized for bypass-object is never tested on find-object. - No variance or confidence intervals across runs; with GPT models' stochastic outputs and small sample sizes (40 plans per skill), the reported differences between methods could be within noise bounds. - All experiments use proprietary commercial models (GPT-5-nano, GPT-4o-mini); no open-weight model evaluation limits reproducibility. - The 11 LTL specifications are uniformly simple (G(p → Xq) form); behavior under complex specifications with nested operators, fairness conditions, or conflicting global/local rules is untested. Bottom Line VASO delivers a genuine and implementable architectural insight — using the model checker's counterexample to update the skill contract rather than to discard the plan — and backs it with real-robot results, but the narrow evaluation scope, proprietary model dependency, absence of code release, and the foundational tension between the "formally verifiable" framing and the unverified AI-generated labeling functions mean practitioners should treat this as a proof-of-concept requiring significant validation before deployment.
Search Results 2606.05395 PDF
#82 — Innovative (64)

FlowSteer: Prompt-Only Workflow Steering Exposes Planning-Time Vulnerabilities in Multi-Agent LLM Systems

F Li, J Wu, T Fu, N Jaques, W Zhou, MY Kan · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces FLOWSTEER, a prompt-only attack that steers the workflow formation process of planner-executor multi-agent LLM systems by exploiting social influence priors to place a malicious signal at high-influence subtasks and guide dependency construction toward propagation-favorable paths. - Why it's important: Planner-executor MAS are deployed in high-stakes domains (medical triage, financial analysis, policy simulation), and existing defenses inspect the generated workflow post-hoc, leaving the planning boundary — the moment a user prompt is converted into subtasks, roles, and routing paths — entirely unprotected. - How it does it: Offline white-box profiling on a clean workflow identifies the highest-influence subtask (via single-node perturbation scoring) and most propagation-favorable dependency; these priors are encoded into a single crafted prompt combining a task-aware sycophantic argument and natural-language dependency-steering guidance submitted through the ordinary user interface. - The good: FLOWSTEER raises malicious attack success rate (MASR) to 65.74% on MisinfoTask with GPT-4o-mini (G1) versus 29.63% for naive malicious prompting, transfers across four model families and three planner-executor capability configurations using priors profiled only once, and remains effective (45.37% MASR) even with black-box-inferred workflow priors — a 15-point lift over naive injection. - The missing: The offline profiling phase assumes white-box access to a clean-task execution trace, which is not always realistic; the black-box pilot study (Appendix F.1) is limited to one benchmark and one model family, so the degradation from white-box to true black-box is not systematically quantified across all settings. - Read it or not: Security researchers designing or auditing production planner-executor MAS should read this; it identifies a concrete, under-defended attack surface and proposes a testable defense, but ML practitioners not deploying dynamic multi-agent pipelines can skip it.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (75) is the lead — framing workflow formation as an independently attackable boundary in planner-executor MAS is a genuine NEW PROBLEM, and the prompt-only attack exploiting structural sensitivity plus sycophantic framing is a new method. Non-Obviousness (70) is high via the practically important, non-obvious finding that post-hoc graph-level defenses (ARGUS, G-Safeguard) can increase attack success. Rigor (58) is the main limiter: every TASR/MASR number is a single-run point estimate at temperature 0.7 on 100–108-sample benchmarks with no variance, ASB-Bench is author-LLM-constructed and judged by the same model family (circularity), and the MAS shares infrastructure with a defense baseline. Resilience (58) and Completeness (62) are further reduced by no adaptive-attacker evaluation and no code release. 0.20*75+0.25*58+0.15*70+0.15*62+0.15*58+0.10*55 = 64.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 58 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 62 Resilience 58 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Planner-executor multi-agent systems (MAS) decompose user tasks into subtasks, assign executor roles, and dynamically construct communication dependency graphs before collaborative execution begins. This makes the workflow itself a safety-critical artifact: a malicious signal that enters at a high-influence subtask can propagate through downstream agent exchanges and surface in the final aggregated output. Prior MAS security work assumes the adversary intervenes after workflow formation — hijacking executor agents, poisoning memory, or corrupting inter-agent messages — and therefore targets a different and more intrusive access model. No prior work asks whether an ordinary user-facing prompt, with zero access to internals, can bias the planner into generating a workflow that amplifies a malicious signal. This gap matters because encapsulated planner-executor services (coding assistants, financial analysis tools) expose only the prompt interface to end users, yet the planning process shapes everything downstream. Key Idea The core insight is that planner-executor MAS have two separable vulnerabilities that compound multiplicatively: (1) structural sensitivity — the same malicious signal produces sharply different downstream effects depending on which subtask it enters, because influence scores vary across the workflow graph; and (2) framing vulnerability — sycophantic cues (authoritative language, pre-emptive debunking of reference facts, fake consensus citations) make downstream agents more likely to adopt and relay an injected claim. FLOWSTEER converts offline estimates of both vulnerabilities into a single crafted prompt that does not modify any internal component. Methodology The pipeline has two phases. In offline vulnerability profiling, a clean workflow Gt = P(t) is obtained and each subtask node vi is perturbed in turn with a standardized hijack instruction; the social influence score SI(vi) = (Smax − Sref(O(i), Rt)) + Smal(O(i), Mt) combines deviation from the reference solution with movement toward the malicious target. The highest-influence subtask v is identified. Separately, each active dependency edge exy is scored by its average propagation score PS(exy) across rounds; the most and least propagation-favorable edges (e+, e−) are identified. In online attack construction, FLOWSTEER encodes three elements into one user prompt: (a) a task-aware sycophantic argument a_SF_H aligned with v's reasoning role, using pseudo-authoritative fake frameworks, pre-emptive debunking of reference-solution facts, and compliance-oriented cues; (b) natural-language dependency-guided steering s* that instructs the planner to preserve paths similar to e+ and suppress paths similar to e−; (c) the original task. No objective function is optimized; the attack is template-based, using LLM calls to generate the task-aware argument and framing from offline profiling outputs. The defense, FLOWGUARD, acts at the input boundary before planning: an intent triage module decomposes the prompt into task intent, methodological intent, and argument-framing intent, then a decontamination module rewrites the prompt by softening structural mandates and downgrading assertive axioms to hypotheses while preserving task objectives. Evaluation uses two metrics: TASR (fraction of runs where MAS output deviates from the reference solution, Sref ≤ 5) and MASR (fraction where output aligns with the malicious target, Smal ≥ 6), scored by GPT-4o as LLM-as-a-Judge, validated against three human annotators (accuracy 0.95/0.96, Fleiss' κ = 0.86/0.83). The MAS itself uses the ARGUS framework with R=3 communication rounds and a temperature of 0.7. Key Results On MisinfoTask (108 tasks) under G1 (same LLM for planner and executors), FLOWSTEER achieves MASR of 65.74% (GPT-4o-mini), 63.89% (Gemini-2.5-flash), 51.85% (Qwen-3.5-flash), and 56.48% (DeepSeek-V3), compared to naive malicious prompting (NM) MASR of 29.63%, 14.81%, 12.04%, and 12.96% respectively. On ASB-Bench (100 tasks), FLOWSTEER achieves up to 63% MASR vs 13% for NM. Strengthening the planner (G2) does not reliably reduce vulnerability: Gemini-2.5 G2 MASR rises to 75.00% under FLOWSTEER. Strengthening executors (G3) reduces attack success more consistently but does not eliminate it (39.81%–49.07% MASR for GPT-4o-mini G3). Against defenses: ARGUS and G-Safeguard sometimes increase MASR (G-Safeguard on Gemini-2.5: 73.15% vs 63.89% undefended), while FLOWGUARD consistently reduces MASR to 22.22%–38.89% on MisinfoTask. A surprising finding: extended multi-round interaction does not self-correct — Gemini-2.5 MASR increases from 63.89% at R=3 to 78.70% at R=4 on MisinfoTask, suggesting that additional rounds amplify rather than correct planning-time contamination. Direct Goal Injection (DGI) achieves comparable raw MASR on ASB-Bench but is detected as explicitly malicious 68.52% of the time vs 7.41% for FLOWSTEER on MisinfoTask, demonstrating that FLOWSTEER evades input-level guardrails more effectively. Novelty Classification NEW PROBLEM + NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The framing of workflow formation as an independently attackable boundary is genuinely novel relative to prior MAS security work, which assumes post-formation access. The social influence diagnostic applied to MAS workflow structure, and the combined task-aware sycophantic + dependency-guided steering attack, are new methods. The empirical finding that post-hoc graph-level defenses can increase MASR under planning-time contamination is a non-obvious and practically important result. Using LLM sycophancy literature as a design principle for adversarial framing is a creative but not surprising combination. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations in Table 16 isolate the contribution of each FLOWSTEER component (structural priors alone, naive argument, generic sycophancy, task-aware sycophancy, full attack), confirming that neither component alone drives results and that the combination is essential. The probing study (§4) separates fixed-topology from auto-topology conditions, isolating structural sensitivity from replanning effects. Human validation of the LLM-as-a-Judge rubric (κ = 0.86/0.83) is a credibility-positive practice. Error bars and multiple seeds are not reported; given the stochastic nature of LLM outputs at temperature 0.7, point estimates without variance across random seeds are a methodological weakness. ASB-Bench is constructed by the authors using GPT-generated malicious targets and arguments, and evaluated on the same distribution, creating a potential circularity: the benchmark construction and the attack both use LLM-generated content, and the judge (GPT-4o) evaluates outputs from models in the same family. Code and data release are not mentioned in the main text; the paper is a preprint. The MAS is built on top of the ARGUS framework, meaning vulnerability profiling and attack construction share infrastructure with one of the defense baselines, which may create subtle confounds. Deployability (4-property check) EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: All experiments run via commercial LLM APIs (GPT-4o-mini, Gemini-2.5-flash, Qwen-3.5-flash, DeepSeek-V3) at cost of approximately $0.10 per 10 instances; no specialized hardware required. - Efficient: FLOWSTEER adds one LLM API call for task-aware argument generation and sycophantic framing on top of standard MAS execution; total overhead is minimal (~$0.02 per instance above vanilla MAS cost). FLOWGUARD adds two preprocessing calls (intent triage + decontamination) at ~$0.05 overhead per instance. - Effective: The attack reliably shifts MAS outputs toward malicious targets across four model families and three planner-executor configurations; the defense reduces but does not eliminate attack success (residual MASR 19%–46% after FLOWGUARD). Effectiveness against adaptive attackers who vary phrasing or split intent across multiple prompt components is not evaluated. - Scalable: Experiments are limited to MisinfoTask (108 samples) and ASB-Bench (100 samples); the largest MAS uses GPT-4o as planner and GPT-4o-mini as executors. Performance at enterprise scale (hundreds of agents, longer workflows, real tool calls) is claimed but not tested. The paper delivers prompt templates and evaluation protocols; no code or trained models are released. Strengths - The core threat model — a prompt-only attacker who exploits the planning boundary without touching agents, memory, or messages — is precisely specified and practically grounded (most planner-executor deployments expose only the prompt interface). - The two-phase separation of offline profiling (white-box diagnostic) from online execution (prompt-only) is methodologically clean and maps onto a realistic adversary model where framework traces are available for development or red-teaming. - The finding that post-hoc defenses (ARGUS, G-Safeguard) can increase MASR is non-obvious and well-supported by results across four model families; it directly falsifies the implicit assumption that post-formation inspection is sufficient. - FLOWGUARD's selectivity — reducing malicious success without significantly degrading benign task-enhancement prompts — is demonstrated empirically and distinguishes it from blunt input filtering approaches. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly acknowledge: (1) offline profiling requires white-box workflow access, which may not always be available, though partial observability via tracing tools and black-box interaction is argued to be realistic; (2) generalization beyond planner-executor workflows (debate-style systems, swarm coordination, memory-augmented agents) is not evaluated; (3) FLOWGUARD is a first step and should not be treated as a complete safeguard; high-stakes systems should combine it with planner-side verification, provenance tracking, and human oversight; (4) defenses should be evaluated against adaptive attackers who may vary phrasing or hide intent across multiple prompt components. These limitations are confronted directly, which is a credibility-positive signal. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The most important unacknowledged gap is the lack of variance reporting. All TASR/MASR figures are point estimates from single runs. With LLM temperature = 0.7 and relatively small benchmarks (100–108 samples), the sampling variance could be substantial, and the difference between, say, FLOWSTEER (51.85%) and G-Safeguard (48.15%) on DeepSeek TASR may not be statistically significant. - ASB-Bench is constructed by the authors using LLM-generated malicious targets and reference solutions, then evaluated using LLM-as-a-Judge from the same provider family. This creates a potential evaluation circularity that the authors do not acknowledge. - The social influence scoring (Eq. 2) uses Smal computed by LLM-as-a-Judge during profiling, meaning the oracle model's biases influence which subtask is deemed high-influence, and therefore which attack is constructed. The sensitivity of attack performance to the choice of judge model during profiling is not studied. - DGI outperforms FLOWSTEER on several ASB-Bench G2/G3 configurations, suggesting that for certain task types, the sophisticated steering machinery provides no advantage over simple goal injection; the boundary conditions are not systematically characterized. - The multi-round robustness experiment (Table 18) shows MASR increasing with more rounds for some models, but the mechanism is only described qualitatively in a case study; whether FLOWGUARD also degrades or holds at R=4/5 is not reported. Bottom Line Practitioners building or auditing planner-executor MAS for high-stakes applications should read this paper: it identifies a concrete, previously unnamed attack surface (planning-time workflow steering), demonstrates severity across four major model families with a prompt-only attack that evades standard intent detectors, and provides a testable input-side defense — though the absence of variance estimates and the narrow benchmark scope mean the exact numbers should be treated as directional rather than precise.
Recommended 2605.11514 PDF
#83 — Innovative (64)

Imagine Before You Predict: Interleaved Latent Visual Reasoning for Video Event Prediction

T Jiang, L Wu, S Xia, S Li, Z Yan, H Yang, Y Qiao… · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces FUTURE-L1, a framework that equips a multimodal LLM with interleaved latent visual reasoning for video event prediction, alternating between text tokens and continuous latent visual spans during autoregressive decoding, trained via visual-gain data curation, supervised fine-tuning aligned to future-frame embeddings, and a novel latent-aware RL objective (LA-DAPO). - Why it's important: Video event prediction requires reasoning about unobserved future dynamics — motion, geometry, object interactions — that are systematically lost when forced into text tokens, making existing text-CoT MLLMs prone to plausible but visually ungrounded predictions. - How it does it: The core mechanism anchors intermediate hidden states (latent spans) to visual embeddings of actual future frames during SFT via an MSE loss, then refines sampled latent trajectories during RL with an outcome-contrastive reward that pulls correct-rollout latent paths together and a temporal-diversity reward that pushes successive latent spans apart. - The good: FUTURE-L1-RL raises Qwen3-VL-8B from 61.0 to 85.4 on FutureBench, exceeding the prior best text-reasoning method (Video-CoE, 75.0) by 10.4 points, with the largest gains (+29.3) on the hardest non-consecutive-event split (Interp.), while also reducing inference tokens from 398.5 (Video-R1) to 195.3 at 0.91 seconds per sample. - The missing: The method is tested only at 8B scale; no ablation or result covers larger backbone sizes, leaving the question of whether latent reasoning remains the dominant factor or is subsumed by scale unresolved. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on video understanding, multimodal reasoning, or latent-space CoT — the visual-gain curation protocol and LA-DAPO reward design are concrete and replicable contributions, though practitioners should note both benchmarks are close to the TwiFF training distribution.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. The composite is held below 75 by Rigor (60) and Resilience (60): single-run results with no error bars on a high-variance RL pipeline, an LLM-judge circularity (Qwen-family judge + Qwen backbone), and benchmarks close to the TwiFF training distribution. Novelty (70) and Non-Obviousness (72) are real — interleaved latent reasoning anchored to future-frame embeddings plus the annotation-free LA-DAPO rewards is a fresh combination, and the text-only control isolating an 8.2pt latent-modality gain is convincing. Weighted: 0.20*70+0.25*60+0.15*72+0.15*64+0.15*60+0.10*58 = 64.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 64 Resilience 60 Impact 58
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Video event prediction (VEP) asks models to infer unobserved future states from a partially observed video prefix. Unlike retrospective video understanding, where answers are grounded in visible frames, VEP requires constructing internal hypotheses about object trajectories, spatial relationships, and event chains that have not yet occurred. Existing MLLMs address this through text-based chain-of-thought: the model verbalizes intermediate hypotheses before producing an answer. The fundamental problem is that verbalization is a lossy compression of dynamic visual semantics — fine-grained motion direction, relative position, and geometric relationships are poorly encoded by natural language. This creates a systematic gap: models generate plausible-sounding but visually ungrounded predictions, especially when the correct answer depends on subtle temporal dynamics. Prior latent visual reasoning methods (Mirage, LVR, Monet, SwimBird) use static helper images as latent anchors, but VEP requires a temporally organized process that updates imagined visual states across multiple reasoning steps — a setting none of these methods were designed for, as confirmed by their poor transfer (Monet: 47.9, LVR: 21.0 on FutureBench vs. random chance ~50% for 4-way MCQ). Key Idea The key insight is that the intermediate reasoning modality should match the structure of what is being reasoned about: dynamic future visual states are better preserved in continuous visual embedding space than in discrete token sequences. Rather than projecting intermediate hypotheses to the vocabulary (text) or to pixel space (video generation), FUTURE-L1 feeds the model's own hidden states at designated latent positions back as input embeddings, and anchors those states to future-frame visual embeddings during training. This creates a channel in which the model can maintain and update a compact visual hypothesis without generating a full pixel-space simulation, and without losing spatial-geometric information to the text bottleneck. Methodology FUTURE-L1 augments Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct with three special tokens: `<|latent_start|>`, `<|latent|>`, and `<|latent_end|>`. During generation, positions marked by `<|latent|>` produce hidden states that are fed directly back as the next input embedding rather than projected to vocabulary logits; these states accumulate in the KV cache and condition subsequent text generation. Span length is dynamically determined by when the model emits `<|latent_end|>`, capped at Lmax tokens. Training proceeds in three stages. First, FUTURE-L1-50K is curated from TwiFF-2.7M by a visual-gain probe: each candidate example is evaluated under two conditions (text-only input, hinted with intermediate future frames), each with 8 rollouts judged by Qwen3.5-397B-A17B. Samples where text-only is not saturated (pt ≤ 6) and where the visual hint provides measurable lift (pv − pt ≥ 2) are retained; the top 50K by descending gain are selected. Second, SFT optimizes a joint objective: standard cross-entropy over text positions plus an MSE loss aligning each latent hidden state ht to the visual embedding of the corresponding future reasoning frame extracted by the Qwen3-VL encoder (weight λ=0.1). This cold-starts latent spans on the future-frame manifold. Third, LA-DAPO extends DAPO with two trajectory-level rewards: Rctr is a hardest-positive InfoNCE reward computed over normalized latent trajectories, pulling correct-rollout latent paths together and using incorrect-rollout paths as negatives; Rdiv penalizes cosine similarity between adjacent span representatives (mean-pooled within each span) to discourage the model from repeating the same visual thought. The final reward is R = 0.9·Racc + 0.1·Rfmt + λc·Rctr + λd·Rdiv with λc=0.2, λd=0.1. Critical design decisions: (1) the MSE anchoring during SFT to real future frames rather than synthesized sketches is well-justified; (2) the choice of λ=0.1 is supported by a sweep showing both too-weak (λ=0.01, −4.1 points) and too-strong (λ=1.0, −3.7 points) alignment hurt; (3) Lmax=4 is validated against spans up to 64, with longer spans degrading performance monotonically. Key Results On FutureBench (multiple-choice VEP, 4 reasoning-depth splits), FUTURE-L1-SFT reaches 73.2 overall accuracy vs. 61.0 for the backbone zero-shot baseline; FUTURE-L1-RL reaches 85.4, surpassing Video-CoE (75.0, the prior best) by 10.4 points and GPT-5 (57.9) by 27.5 points. The text-only SFT control trained on identical FUTURE-L1-50K data reaches only 65.0, isolating an 8.2-point gain attributable to the latent modality rather than data curation. Gains on harder splits are disproportionately large: +29.3 on Interp. (55.8 → 85.1), +20.7 on 2-Hop, +20.4 on 3-Hop vs. +19.0 on 1-Hop, suggesting latent reasoning helps more when longer future chains must be extrapolated. On TwiFF-Bench (open-ended, LLM-judged 0–5 scale), FUTURE-L1-RL reaches average 3.04 vs. TwiFF-2.7M (2.79), a model trained on 2.7M in-distribution examples. RL data scaling on TwiFF-Bench shows monotonic improvement: 5K→10K→20K retained samples yields 2.78→2.89→3.04, with no saturation visible. Inference on FutureBench: FUTURE-L1-RL uses 195.3 tokens at 0.91s per sample, achieving 93.8 accuracy/second vs. 19.3 for Video-R1 and 2.7 for Video-o3. Ablations confirm both Rctr (+0.7 over DAPO) and Rdiv (+1.0 over DAPO) contribute independently, and their combination (+1.6) is additive. Visual-gain filtering accounts for 4.8 points vs. random 50K selection (73.2 vs. 68.4 SFT accuracy). This is a preprint; results have not undergone peer review. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK (curated corpus). The genuine novelty is the combination of: (1) interleaved latent visual reasoning grounded to future-frame embeddings during SFT (as opposed to static helper images), and (2) LA-DAPO's two trajectory-level latent rewards that operate without intermediate-frame annotations at RL time. The broader SFT-then-RL pipeline and the use of DAPO as the base RL algorithm are standard machinery; the visual-gain filtering protocol for data curation is a meaningful but incremental extension of influence-function-style data selection ideas applied to a new setting. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are thorough: λ and Lmax sweeps for SFT, full RL objective component ablation (GRPO, DePO, DAPO, +Rctr, +Rdiv, both), and reward coefficient sweeps for λc and λd. Error bars and multiple seeds are not reported for any table — all results appear to be single-run, which is a meaningful gap given RL variance. The FutureBench RL training set is only 2K samples; the degree to which results are sensitive to this small set is not evaluated. Code and model weights are promised via https://github.com/OpenGVLab/Future-L1 (mentioned on the cover page). TwiFF-Bench uses LLM-as-judge scoring (Qwen3.6-27B), introducing model-family bias: the RL accuracy reward uses the same judge family (Qwen3.6-27B), and the backbone is Qwen3-VL-8B, so there is a risk that the system is optimized to score well under Qwen-family judges. FutureBench is multiple-choice and thus judge-free, which makes those results more trustworthy. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL — an end-to-end trained system evaluated on two benchmarks. - Practical: Training requires 8× H200 GPUs; the SFT data curation pipeline requires 16 rollouts per example (8 text-only + 8 hinted) judged by a 397B model, making curation non-trivial but feasible with institutional compute access. Inference runs at 0.91s per sample on comparable hardware. - Efficient: FUTURE-L1-RL uses fewer output tokens (195.3) than text-reasoning baselines like Video-R1 (398.5) while achieving higher accuracy, because latent computation replaces verbose textual chain-of-thought; the marginal cost of latent positions is hidden-state forwarding with no vocabulary projection. - Effective: On FutureBench (multiple-choice, objective evaluation), the method substantially outperforms all listed baselines including proprietary models; on TwiFF-Bench (LLM-judged), improvement over TwiFF-2.7M is meaningful but the judge-family concern slightly reduces confidence. - Scalable: Tested only at 8B parameters and 32 frames; no experiments at larger scales or with more frames, and RL data scaling on TwiFF-Bench (up to 20K samples) does not saturate but also does not confirm scaling beyond this regime. The work delivers a trained model checkpoint (promised via GitHub) and a curated 50K dataset, in addition to paper form. Strengths - The visual-gain filtering protocol is a principled way to select training examples where the visual modality is necessary rather than redundant, backed by an explicit measurability criterion (pv − pt ≥ 2) and an ablation confirming its 4.8-point value over random selection. - The LA-DAPO reward design elegantly handles the annotation-free RL challenge: by using rollout outcomes and latent state comparisons rather than intermediate-frame annotations, the contrastive and diversity rewards provide dense latent supervision without requiring per-step ground truth at RL time. - The adaptive latent span count increasing with task difficulty (1.79 spans for 1-Hop vs. 2.52 for 3-Hop) is an emergent property that was not explicitly trained and provides meaningful behavioral evidence that the model uses latent computation purposefully. - The ablation table cleanly separates data selection effects, latent modality effects, and RL objective effects, making the component-wise contribution attributable with reasonable confidence. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly show one failure case (Figure 18) where the model identifies the high-level context but its latent trajectory drifts toward a generic continuation, failing to preserve fine-grained event-specific visual identity (carpet, refrigerator, dugout sequence). They acknowledge that invoking latent spans is necessary but not sufficient — the sampled trajectory must also capture fine-grained event details, and this motivates LA-DAPO. They do not explicitly discuss scale limitations, the judge-family bias, or the single-seed evaluation concern. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Single-scale evaluation: all results are at 8B parameters; it is unknown whether the latent reasoning advantage persists at larger scales where the backbone's own text-reasoning capability is stronger. - LLM-as-judge circularity: FutureBench RL trains with Qwen3.6-27B as judge; TwiFF-Bench final scoring also uses an LLM judge; and the backbone is Qwen3-VL-8B. Results may partially reflect optimization toward Qwen-family evaluation preferences rather than generalizable future-event reasoning. - No error bars or significance tests on any table: with RL variance being notoriously high, single-run results — especially where margins between ablation conditions are 0.3–0.9 points — may not be reproducible. - The visual-gain curation runs 16 rollouts per candidate from a 2.7M pool; the compute cost of this step is not reported, making replication budget unclear. - FUTURE-L1-SFT's CoT score on TwiFF-Bench decreases from the zero-shot baseline (2.75 → 2.62), with only the answer score rising (2.14 → 2.42); this suggests the interleaved format hurts reasoning legibility even as it helps prediction, a tradeoff that goes undiscussed. - Generalization beyond VEP to other video reasoning tasks is not evaluated; both benchmarks are closely related to TwiFF-format data, which was the curation source. Bottom Line FUTURE-L1 makes a credible empirical case that preserving dynamic future visual structure in latent space is substantially more effective than text-chain-of-thought for video event prediction, with large gains on hard benchmarks and an efficient inference profile, making it required reading for researchers in video reasoning or latent-space MLLM post-training — but the single-scale evaluation and LLM-judge concerns mean the results should be treated as promising preprint evidence rather than definitive findings.
Citations 2606.05769 PDF
#84 — Innovative (64)

Image Triaging for Budget-Aware Universal Attacks on Vision-Language Models

WY Tan, D Lu, WT Ooi · openreview.net · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces an image-triaging framework that predicts which inputs are most vulnerable to a fixed universal adversarial perturbation (UAP) on Vision-Language Models, enabling a budget-constrained attacker to concentrate limited perturbation attempts on the most susceptible inputs without querying the victim model. - Why it's important: Real-world adversarial deployment is budget-constrained — physical access limits, detection risk, or limited pipeline control restrict how many inputs can be perturbed — yet the standard UAP evaluation protocol assumes unconstrained access and reports aggregate ASR, obscuring this practical threat dimension. - How it does it: A lightweight XGBoost regressor is trained offline on surrogate-attack outcomes using clean image-level features (low-level statistics, YOLO-based semantic cues, ResNet-50 embeddings), then ranks deployment candidates by predicted vulnerability without any victim model queries at inference time. - The good: At B=0.01 (1% budget) under XTransfer, the triager raises ASR_B from a random baseline of 24–58% to 73–97% across six diverse VLMs, demonstrating dramatic efficiency gains from input selection alone. - The missing: The entire study is confined to a single task (road accident false-positive induction) using two UAP methods that both rely on CLIP-style surrogate encoders — generalization to other tasks, target directions, or non-CLIP attack families is undemonstrated. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on adversarial robustness of VLMs in safety-critical settings or on input-screening defenses; less relevant if your focus is UAP construction rather than deployment strategy.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (72) is the high point — NEW PROBLEM (budgeted UAP deployment as a victim-free ranking task) plus the non-obvious finding that transfer vulnerability is input-intrinsic and predictable from cheap low-level statistics; the oracle decomposition ASR_B=(p/B)·R_B is a rigorous diagnostic. Resilience (62) and Impact (50) pull it down: the entire study is one binary task (road-accident false-positive) on dashcam video with two CLIP-based attack families, the only baseline is random selection (no simple-heuristic comparison), and no artifact is released. Weighted: 0.20*72+0.25*65+0.15*70+0.15*65+0.15*62+0.10*50 = 64.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 65 Resilience 62 Impact 50
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Universal adversarial perturbations (UAPs) for VLMs are evaluated under an implicit assumption of unconstrained access to the input stream: every candidate input is perturbed and aggregate attack success rate (ASR) is reported. In practice, attackers face physical access limits, detection risk, or limited control over input pipelines, restricting how many inputs they can perturb. Because UAPs are input-agnostic by construction, their effectiveness varies substantially across inputs — indiscriminate deployment wastes limited budget on resistant inputs. Levy et al. (2024) studied transferability ranking for instance-level classifier attacks but tied ranking to a specific perturbation and required surrogate inference per candidate, neither of which extends to universal attacks where success variation arises from input content alone. No prior work has formalized budgeted deployment of UAPs against VLMs, nor studied how to rank candidate inputs without victim feedback. Key Idea Transfer vulnerability of a fixed UAP is largely an intrinsic property of the clean input rather than of the perturbation or victim model, and this property is predictable from inexpensive low-level image statistics (edge density, brightness, blur, contrast) using a lightweight regressor trained on surrogate attack outcomes — enabling victim-free, perturbation-agnostic ranking of deployment candidates. The non-obvious corollary is that low-level statistics alone nearly match a full-feature model, and regressors trained on one UAP method transfer effectively to another. Methodology The framework has two phases. In offline training, a surrogate VLM f_s evaluates a fixed UAP δ on a surrogate dataset D. Because per-frame binary attack success labels are too sparse for regression supervision, continuous vulnerability scores are constructed by temporal aggregation: for each anchor frame, the mean surrogate success rate over M consecutive neighboring frames produces a soft label ȳ ∈ [0,1], under the assumption that vulnerability is locally consistent across adjacent video frames. Each input is represented by an 81-dimensional feature vector φ(x) concatenating (i) low-level image statistics — brightness, contrast, saturation, hue, edge density, blur variance; (ii) YOLOv8l object-detection semantics — vehicle counts by class, density, confidence; and (iii) ResNet-50 mean/variance deep embeddings. Group-level statistics (min, max, mean, std) over 15-frame temporal groups are included. Recursive feature elimination reduces the 81 features to 24, and XGBoost is trained to minimize binary cross-entropy against ȳ. At deployment, candidate inputs in victim pool S are scored by g(φ(x)), ranked in descending vulnerability order, and the top K=⌊Bn⌋ are perturbed with δ. Three critical design decisions: (1) temporal aggregation for soft labels is well-motivated for video but assumes temporal consistency — no ablation varies window size M; (2) decoupling perturbation generation from deployment selection is the core architectural insight and is well-argued; (3) using recursive feature elimination rather than end-to-end learned features is pragmatic but limits expressiveness in selection-limited regimes. Key Results Evaluation covers six open-source VLMs (Ovis2-8B, LLaVA-OneVision-7B, Qwen2.5-VL-7B, InternVL3-14B, Kimi-VL-A3B-Instruct, DeepSeek-VL2-small) on a road accident false-positive monitoring task using BDD100K (2,000 videos, surrogate) and D²-City (3,000 videos, deployment). Under XTransfer at B=0.01, the triager achieves ASR_B of 73–97% versus a random baseline of 24–58%. Under AnyAttack the absolute rates are lower due to weaker transfer (random baseline 2–10%), but relative gains remain large at B=0.01 for five of six victims. In ranking quality evaluation, same-attack ROC-AUC ranges from 0.817 (LLaVA) to 0.928 (Ovis); cross-attack evaluation (XTransfer regressors vs. AnyAttack labels) shows a mean ROC-AUC drop of only 0.028. PR-AUC varies widely (0.017 for Qwen to 0.464 for DeepSeek), driven by vulnerability prevalence differences. Feature ablation shows low-level statistics alone nearly match the full-feature model; YOLO-only and deep-only features lag. In defense experiments, regressor-guided screening at B=0.25 reduces ASR by 0.22–0.25 absolute versus 0.11–0.16 for random (forward direction), and achieves a 2.9× improvement over random in the cross-attack reverse direction. The baseline is random selection throughout — no comparison against other selection heuristics or adapted prior methods is provided. Novelty Classification NEW PROBLEM + NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The formalization of budgeted UAP deployment as a ranking problem for VLMs is new. The image-triaging methodology (temporal soft-label aggregation + lightweight feature-based regressor trained on surrogate outcomes) is a new method for this problem. The empirical finding that transfer vulnerability is largely input-intrinsic and capturable by low-level statistics without any surrogate inference at deployment time is genuinely non-obvious. The distinction from Levy et al. (2024) — no per-candidate surrogate inference, applicable to universal attacks, generalizable across attack methods — is substantive rather than incremental. Rigor & Reproducibility Feature family ablations (low-level vs. YOLO vs. ResNet vs. all) are present and informative. The oracle analysis (Section 4.3) that decomposes ASR_B into scarcity (p/B) and selection quality (R_B) components is a rigorous diagnostic; oracle curves are plotted against triage curves providing clear upper bounds. Standard deviations across surrogate models are reported for AUC metrics. Cross-attack evaluation strengthens the generalization claim. However: no code or data are stated to be publicly released; the window size M for temporal aggregation is not ablated; no significance tests are reported; both UAP methods use CLIP-style surrogates, so cross-attack generalization does not span fundamentally different attack architectures; and the evaluation is on a single binary task type. This is a preprint under ICML review. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: All components (XGBoost, YOLOv8, ResNet-50) run on commodity CPU hardware; no GPU is required at deployment time beyond what the VLM itself demands. - Efficient: Feature extraction and XGBoost inference are negligible overhead compared to VLM inference, making the triager effectively free to deploy as a pre-filter in any VLM pipeline. - Effective: Yes for the tested setting — 73–97% ASR_B at 1% budget under XTransfer across six VLMs represents strong evidence of effectiveness; cross-attack and cross-dataset results further support robustness of the signal. - Scalable: Tested on 3,000 deployment videos (~45,000 temporal groups), a moderate scale; behavior at 100× scale (continuous video feeds, live surveillance) is not evaluated, and significant domain shift beyond dashcam driving is undemonstrated. The work delivers a methodology described at paper level with no released software artifact, dataset, or trained model weights. Strengths - The oracle analysis decomposing ASR_B into scarcity-limited vs. selection-limited regimes (Section 4.3) is the strongest intellectual contribution: the formal identity ASR_B = (p/B)·R_B separates prevalence from ranking quality and enables principled diagnosis of why gains vary across victim models — this is more useful than raw ASRB numbers alone. - The cross-attack generalization finding (mean ROC-AUC drop of 0.028 from same-attack to cross-attack evaluation) is non-trivial and supports the claim of input-intrinsic vulnerability rather than perturbation-specific artifacts. - The dual use as both an attack efficiency tool and a defensive screening mechanism (Appendix D, 2.9× improvement over random at B=0.25 in the cross-attack direction) substantially broadens the contribution's relevance. - The finding that low-level image statistics (edge density, brightness) nearly match the full 81-feature model is a practically important result: it implies that an adversary or defender needs no learned perception beyond standard image processing, lowering the deployment barrier considerably. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state: (1) restriction to road accident monitoring as the sole task domain; (2) evaluation uses only two UAP methods operating in a digital threat model, and extension to physical-world perturbations, VQA/captioning tasks, and alternative aggregation schemes remains future work; (3) the triager uses hand-crafted features and does not explore learned ranking models or streaming/online selection policies; (4) it is unclear whether cross-attack generalization holds for attacks built on fundamentally different (non-CLIP) surrogate architectures. These limitations are confronted directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The only baseline is uniform random selection. No comparison is made against simpler non-learned heuristics (e.g., rank by edge density alone, rank by blur score) or against Levy et al.'s approach adapted for UAPs. Without these, it is impossible to quantify how much of the gain is attributable to the trained regressor versus simple threshold-based filtering. - All experiments target false-positive induction (predicting accident when none exists). The symmetric attack direction — suppressing true accident detection — is arguably the higher-stakes threat and is not evaluated; the vulnerability signal may not transfer across target directions. - The BDD100K→D²-City cross-dataset shift is real but modest: both are dashcam driving datasets from similar visual domains. Generalization to substantially different visual distributions (indoor, aerial, satellite, medical) is assumed but unvalidated, and the low-level-statistics finding may be domain-specific. - Temporal aggregation window size M is never ablated, and the consistency assumption is asserted without validation — scenes with rapid cuts or sudden object appearances could violate it. - The false-precision cost (attacker wastes a budget slot on a high-scored input that turns out resistant) is not quantified, making it unclear how performance degrades when the triager overestimates vulnerability for a specific subset. Bottom Line This is a focused, well-executed empirical paper on a practically important and understudied problem — budgeted deployment of universal adversarial attacks — that delivers a genuine finding (low-level image statistics predict UAP vulnerability across victim models and attack methods) and a rigorous analytical framework (scarcity vs. selection regime decomposition); practitioners in VLM security and safety-critical monitoring should read it, with the significant caveat that all results come from one binary task on dashcam video using two CLIP-based attack families.
Search Results
#85 — Innovative (64)

AN EXPLAINABLE CALIBRATION AND SHAP AUDIT OF HELP-SEEKING FEATURES IN CLASSICAL KNOWLEDGE TRACING

M Hameed, M Karim, M Nauman, MA Fida, N Khan · thesesjournal.com · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper demonstrates that adding help-seeking behavioral features (hint counts, bottom-hint usage, first-action rate, attempts-per-problem) to a well-tuned LightGBM knowledge-tracing model on ASSISTments 2009-10 statistically degrades both AUC (0.838 → 0.832) and calibration (ECE 0.0099 → 0.0140), and uses TreeSHAP to explain why. - Why it's important: Instructional designers and tutor engineers routinely instrument hint logging on the assumption that richer behavioral signals improve mastery prediction; this paper provides direct evidence that assumption fails for tree-ensemble models on this widely-used benchmark. - How it does it: It fits PFA, LR-KT, LightGBM, and XGBoost across three nested feature blocks (outcome-only, +help-seeking, +response-time) on a user-grouped 80/20 split, reporting four metrics with bootstrap 95% CIs, then applies TreeSHAP with per-skill cohort decomposition to attribute the result. - The good: The AUC degradation from Block A to Block B in LightGBM (0.838 vs. 0.832) is confirmed by non-overlapping bootstrap CIs, and the per-skill ECE table identifies exactly which three skills (Add/Sub Integers, Mul/Div Integers, Angles) drive the calibration damage — a mechanistically specific, reproducible finding. - The missing: The paper evaluates a single corpus (ASSISTments 2009-10), omits BKT due to an installation failure, uses unweighted ECE that is dominated by high-volume skills, and does not test recency-weighted variants of help-seeking features that might carry more signal. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners who build or evaluate ITS mastery models and need a calibration-aware audit protocol; the negative result is clean and the per-skill SHAP decomposition is a transferable methodology, but the single-corpus scope limits how far the conclusion generalizes.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. A clean, mechanistically specific negative result that aligns well with the user's taste for controlled, hypothesis-driven studies. Completeness (75) is the top dimension — six directly confronted limitations (scaffolding filtering, cold-start, top-12-skill restriction, BKT omission, collinearity, attempt-weighted ECE) make the paper unusually credible. Rigor (72) is strong on protocol (user-grouped split, bootstrap CIs on all metrics, SHAP rank stability across 10 seeds) but pulled down by single-corpus scope, an unverifiable code-release claim with no URL, fixed (non-cross-validated) hyperparameters, and a non-mainstream venue. Novelty (55) reflects a real but narrow empirical finding (help-seeking features hurt a tuned GBM on ASSISTments 2009-10). Non-Obviousness (62) credits the expressivity/feature-richness substitution insight; Resilience (62) is bounded by the single-corpus generalization risk; Impact (50) is a reusable audit protocol that stops short of a packaged tool. Weighted: 0.20*55 + 0.25*72 + 0.15*62 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*62 + 0.10*50 ≈ 64.
empirical Novelty 55 Rigor 72 Non-Obv 62 Completeness 75 Resilience 62 Impact 50
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Knowledge tracing (KT) models deployed in intelligent tutoring systems produce per-attempt correctness probabilities that drive mastery decisions, pacing, and remediation. Two blind spots pervade the published literature: (1) behavioral signals — particularly help-seeking behaviors like hint requests and bottom-hint abuse — are well-motivated in the metacognitive tutoring literature but rarely folded into classical KT feature sets; (2) evaluation almost universally reports only AUC, ignoring calibration (ECE, Brier), which determines whether predicted probabilities are actionable. The gap is that no prior work jointly varies both model class and feature richness while reporting calibration alongside discrimination metrics with proper uncertainty quantification on this benchmark corpus. Key Idea The core insight is that model expressivity and feature richness are substitutes, not complements, in this setting: a tree ensemble with sufficient depth already extracts all available signal from outcome histories, so adding help-seeking features introduces noise (confounded with skill identity) rather than independent signal. The linear model lacks that expressivity and therefore does benefit marginally from behavioral features. TreeSHAP makes the substitution quantitative: Block-A outcome features carry mean |SHAP| ≈ 2.4 versus ≈ 0.30 for all five help-seeking features combined — roughly a 5:1 ratio — confirming that the GBM uses help-seeking only as a fine adjustment once outcome features have already placed a prediction on the response surface. Methodology The study uses the public ASSISTments 2009-10 Skill Builder release (433,161 attempts, 4,163 students, 123 skills). Preprocessing retains only original=1 attempts, drops ≈0.1% rows with missing IDs or outcomes, and builds causal rolling features exclusively from rows strictly preceding the current attempt in (user, skill, order) ordering, eliminating leakage. Three nested feature blocks are defined formally: Block A (6 outcome features + skill ID: per-student and per-(student,skill) rolling accuracy and attempt counts); Block B (Block A + 5 help-seeking features: rolling hint count, bottom-hint count, hints-per-attempt, first-action rate, attempts-per-problem); Block C (Block B + 2 log-transformed response-time features). Four models are fit: PFA (logistic regression on per-skill success/failure counts with per-skill intercepts); LR-KT (L2-regularized logistic regression, C=1); LightGBM (400 trees, lr=0.05, 31 leaves, min_child_samples=200, skill_id as native categorical); XGBoost (400 trees, lr=0.05, max-depth 6, min_child_weight=10, one-hot skill). Hyperparameters are held constant across blocks. The train/test split is user-grouped 80/20 (GroupShuffleSplit, seed 42), yielding 92,582 held-out attempts from 833 students. Metrics reported with bootstrap 95% CIs (B=500): AUC, F1 (threshold 0.5), ECE (15 equal-width bins), Brier. Per-skill ECE is computed for the top-12 skills by held-out volume (threshold ≈200 rows for stable bin estimates). TreeSHAP is run on LightGBM Block C with global mean(|SHAP|) ranking, dependence scatter plots, per-skill mean(|SHAP|), and rank stability across 10 random GroupShuffleSplit seeds. Critical design decisions: (1) user-grouped split prevents student-level leakage and is stricter than k-fold — well-justified; (2) fixed hyperparameters across blocks ensures performance differences reflect data, not tuning — well-justified; (3) restricting per-skill ECE to top-12 skills avoids unstable calibration estimates in the long tail — reasonable but acknowledged as a limitation. Key Results PFA baseline: AUC 0.696 [0.692, 0.700], ECE 0.0235, Brier 0.1898 — worst across all metrics. LR-KT Block A: AUC 0.776 [0.773, 0.779], ECE 0.0159; adding help-seeking (Block B) leaves AUC unchanged but worsens ECE to 0.0202; adding response time (Block C) recovers ECE to 0.0159 and marginally nudges AUC to 0.779 [0.776, 0.782] (overlapping CI with Block A — not reliably significant). LightGBM Block A is the best overall configuration: AUC 0.838 [0.836, 0.841], ECE 0.0099 [0.0081, 0.0123], F1 0.861, Brier 0.1419. Adding help-seeking (Block B) drops AUC to 0.832 [0.829, 0.834] and raises ECE to 0.0140 [0.0118, 0.0165]; Block-A and Block-B CIs do not overlap on either metric — degradation is statistically real. Block C recovers ECE only partially to 0.0128, with AUC unchanged at 0.832. XGBoost tracks LightGBM in AUC direction but is poorly calibrated (ECE 0.023–0.027), trailing even LR-KT on calibration. Per-skill ECE in Table T-PSE: LightGBM Block A holds ECE < 0.05 on 9 of 12 top skills; the three exceptions (Add/Sub Integers 0.106, Mul/Div Integers 0.206, Angles 0.148) are precisely where Block B makes calibration dramatically worse (Add/Sub Integers ECE rises to 0.222 under Block B). Global SHAP ranking for LightGBM Block C: top 7 features are all outcome/skill-ID features (skill_prior_acc mean |SHAP| 0.72, skill_id 0.48, skill_prior_correct 0.42, prior_acc 0.39, skill_prior_incorrect 0.18, skill_prior_attempts 0.11, prior_attempts 0.10); first non-outcome feature is skill_prior_mean_log_first_resp at rank 8 (0.085); leading help-seeking feature (skill_prior_bottom_hint) ranks 9th (0.079). Rank stability across 10 seeds: top-7 features stable with SD ≤ 0.5; help-seeking features shuffle within ranks 8–12 (SD 1.4–2.1). The LightGBM Block A AUC of 0.838 sits inside the DKT-leakage-corrected range of 0.81–0.82 reported by Xiong et al. (2016), consistent with Khajah et al. (2016)'s argument that strong classical models close most of the deep-net gap on this corpus. Novelty Classification NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The specific contribution — that help-seeking features hurt a well-tuned GBM on ASSISTments 2009-10 while only marginally benefiting a linear model — is not established in the prior literature. The methodology (nested block comparison + calibration metrics + TreeSHAP audit) is a competent assembly of existing tools applied to an open question; no new algorithm, model, or formalism is introduced. Rigor & Reproducibility Strengths: bootstrap CIs are reported for all metrics, enabling proper comparison; user-grouped splitting prevents the most common leakage in KT; formal feature definitions with explicit causal ordering are given; SHAP rank stability is evaluated across 10 seeds; pipeline, fitted models, per-skill reliability diagrams, and bibliography are stated to be released (no URL or repo link is provided in the paper — this is a reproducibility concern). Weaknesses: no ablation over hyperparameters (fixed tuning choice could systematically favor or disfavor one block); F1 is reported at a fixed 0.5 threshold despite acknowledging class imbalance (69.3% positive rate) makes it a poor discriminating metric on this corpus; BKT is omitted due to a Python version incompatibility rather than a principled decision; the skill_prior_correct/skill_prior_attempts collinearity (r ≈ 0.96) is identified but not addressed via regularized LR-KT; per-skill ECE uses attempt-weighted bins, not skill-weighted, which the authors themselves flag as biasing toward high-volume skills. The journal (Spectrum of Engineering Sciences, thesesjournal.com) and the Zenodo DOI suggest this is not published in a mainstream peer-reviewed venue; results should be weighted accordingly. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL — methods and experiments that the paper actually runs. - Practical: LightGBM with 400 trees over rolling tabular features runs on commodity hardware; the pipeline trains and evaluates on 433K rows in negligible time. - Efficient: The Block-A model uses 6 numeric features plus a categorical, making inference per-attempt effectively instantaneous; the authors note it is simultaneously the simplest, fastest, and most accurate configuration. - Effective: The evidence shows LightGBM Block A solves the stated next-attempt-correctness prediction task at AUC 0.838 and ECE 0.0099 on the held-out user fold, which is competitive with deep KT models on the same corpus after leakage correction; the per-skill ECE table identifies three skills requiring further recalibration. - Scalable: Tested at one corpus scale (433K attempts, 4K students); no experiment at larger scale; the authors note the audit protocol generalizes to any KT corpus with hint and attempt counts, but this is a claim, not a measurement. The work delivers a released pipeline and fitted models (location not specified in the paper), stopping short of a library or packaged tool.
