Strengths - The negative result is specific and statistically supported: the AUC and ECE degradation from Block A to Block B in LightGBM is confirmed by non-overlapping bootstrap CIs, not just point estimates, which is stronger than most feature-engineering comparisons in the KT literature. - The per-skill SHAP decomposition identifying which skills drive calibration damage, and why (help-seeking confounded with skill identity on integer arithmetic), provides mechanistic grounding that goes beyond aggregate attribution tables. - Separating help-seeking as a useful signal for gaming-the-system detection from its (absent) value for next-attempt-correctness prediction is a practically actionable distinction for course coordinators. - The evaluation protocol (3 blocks × 4 models × 4 metrics × bootstrap CIs + per-skill ECE + SHAP rank stability) is reusable and clearly specified, making it a concrete template for similar audits on other corpora. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly confront six limitations: (1) scaffolding filtering discards the sub-problems with the richest hint usage; (2) cold-start regime causes help-seeking rolling means to fall to structural zero for students with < 5 attempts; (3) per-skill ECE restricted to top-12 skills, leaving 111 tail skills unevaluated; (4) BKT omitted due to a library installation failure under Python 3.14; (5) high collinearity between skill_prior_correct and skill_prior_attempts (r ≈ 0.96) penalizes LR-KT without a regularization remedy; (6) aggregate ECE is attempt-weighted rather than skill-weighted. They also flag future work on recency-weighted help-seeking features and extending to ASSISTments 2012-13 with affect detectors. The directness of this limitations section is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The released pipeline and models lack a URL or repository link anywhere in the paper, making independent verification of the reproducibility claim impossible. - Hyperparameters were held constant across blocks with no cross-validation, meaning the LightGBM Block A configuration may be inadvertently tuned to the outcome-only feature set; a block-specific hyperparameter search could change the conclusion. - The per-skill calibration analysis is restricted to top-12 skills and not adjusted for skill difficulty; the three skills where help-seeking hurts calibration most (Add/Sub Integers, Mul/Div Integers, Angles) also happen to be high-volume arithmetic skills, raising the possibility that the confounding is corpus-specific and does not generalize. - The paper does not test recency-weighted or temporally-decayed help-seeking features despite acknowledging that plain rolling means may destroy informative temporal structure — this is the most obvious alternative formulation and its omission limits the strength of the "help-seeking doesn't help" conclusion. - The comparison to DKT and self-attention models (SAKT, SAINT) is narrative only; no direct head-to-head on this corpus with the same split is provided. - The venue (thesesjournal.com with a Zenodo DOI) does not appear to be a recognized peer-reviewed educational data mining or AI-in-education outlet, which reduces confidence in the review quality. Bottom Line A practitioner deciding whether to instrument hint logging for mastery prediction gets a clear, well-evidenced answer for LightGBM on ASSISTments 2009-10 — don't bother — along with a transferable audit protocol, but the single-corpus scope and unverifiable code release prevent full confidence in the generalization claim.
Search Results
#86 — Innovative (64)

WorldOlympiad: Can Your World Model Survive a Triathlon? (27 ▲ )

Yuke Zhao, Wangbo Zhao, Weijie Wang, Zeyu Zhang, Dakai An, Akide Liu, Yinghao Yu, Jiasheng Tang, Fan Wang, Wei Wang, Bohan Zhuang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 10 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces WorldOlympiad, a benchmark that evaluates video-based world models across three complementary dimensions—physical faithfulness, geometric consistency, and interaction fidelity—spanning gaming, robotics, and real-world scenarios with 1,000 annotated long videos and 8 evaluated pipelines. - Why it's important: Existing benchmarks measure visual quality and semantic alignment but are structurally blind to whether generated videos obey physical laws, maintain coherent 3D geometry, or sustain controllable multi-step interactions—properties essential for deploying world models in robotics, game simulation, and embodied AI. - How it does it: Three evaluation tracks are combined: a rule-based physical judge (MLLM + SAM3 segmentation applying 14 physical rules), a Gaussian splatting reconstruction pipeline scoring 3D structural consistency and camera trajectory agreement, and a chunk-level CLIP + MLLM rubric measuring interaction fidelity at chunk, transition, and global granularities. - The good: The automatic evaluator achieves Spearman ρ = 0.95 with human preference rankings across all 8 evaluated models (2,800 pairwise labels from 5 annotators), validating that the metrics capture quality differences perceptible to humans. - The missing: Geometry scores are uniformly low across all models (best: 0.424), but whether this reflects genuine model weakness or Gaussian splatting reconstruction failures on dynamic content is never ablated—the pipeline's validity as a 3D proxy is assumed, not verified. - Read it or not: Researchers designing or evaluating video world models should read this—it fills a real gap in evaluation methodology with credible human validation—but should scrutinize the geometry track's construct validity before treating its scores as ground truth.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (60) is the limiting dimension: the human-preference validation is unusually strong (ρ=0.95, 2,800 pairwise labels) but the geometry track's validity as a 3D proxy is assumed rather than verified — low compressed scores (0.25–0.42) may reflect Gaussian-splatting reconstruction failures, not model weakness, and no alternative geometry probe is ablated. Resilience (58) is also reduced by potential selection/reporting bias (several evaluated models share the authors' institution via OpenWorldLib) and the unvalidated per-track MLLM judge. Novelty (68) is genuine in the integrated three-track protocol and the creative 3D-via-splatting probe. Completeness (65) and Impact (68) are reasonable given the dataset and promised code, but the narrow eval (8 models) and compressed geometry range limit discriminability.
empirical Novelty 68 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 65 Resilience 58 Impact 68
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Video world models must predict future visual states from observations and control signals, with applications in game simulation, robotic policy development, and scene generation. High visual fidelity alone is insufficient: models must preserve state continuity, respect physical and geometric constraints, respond to actions, and sustain plausible dynamics over long horizons. Existing video generation benchmarks (VBench, VBench++, VBench 2.0) evaluate visual quality, motion smoothness, and semantic alignment in short clips but are fundamentally insensitive to physical rule compliance, 3D geometric coherence, and long-horizon interaction control. Domain-specific benchmarks (MIND for gaming, EWMBench and WorldArena for robotics) lack unified cross-domain protocols, making it impossible to compare models across gaming, robotics, and general real-world settings under a single evaluation framework. No benchmark before this work jointly assesses all three world-modeling capability dimensions—physical, geometric, interactive—in one protocol across diverse domains. Key Idea: The core insight is to decompose world-model quality into three orthogonal, measurable tracks, each targeting failure modes invisible to visual-quality metrics: (1) physical rule compliance judged by an MLLM comparing generated video against a reference video using 14 explicit physical rules after SAM-based object segmentation isolates the relevant entities; (2) 3D geometric consistency derived by running Gaussian splatting reconstruction (via Depth Anything 3) on the generated video and scoring the render and camera trajectory; and (3) chunk-level interaction fidelity using CLIP cosine similarity plus a structured three-level MLLM rubric (per-chunk, per-transition, global). Each track is independently interpretable, providing diagnostic failure attribution that aggregate visual quality metrics cannot. Methodology: The pipeline has four components: Data collection and annotation: 1,000 videos are collected—400 robotics clips from RoboCOIN, 400 gaming clips from GameGen-X (OGameData_50K), 200 real-world clips from LVD-2M (duration >60s, motion score >50). A three-stage chunk-caption-refine pipeline using Gemini-3-Pro-Preview segments each video into ≤6 temporal chunks, generates per-chunk WASD-style action labels and English captions, then refines annotations with full-video context to correct hallucinations and ensure narrative continuity. Physical track: An MLLM identifies primary dynamic objects for SAM3 segmentation, producing object-centric visualization videos. A batch relevance judge determines which of 14 physical rules apply per video. A compliance judge compares generated candidate video against the reference, returning a binary compliance decision, confidence, and evidence. Final Sphys averages compliance over applicable rules. Critical design decision: removing irrelevant rules via the relevance filter prevents dilution by inapplicable phenomena, but this means thermodynamics scores collapse to zero for robotics videos where no heat-related events occur. Geometry track: Videos are uniformly sampled at ≤32 frames; dynamic-object masks remove foreground Gaussians before reconstruction so the judge focuses on static scene structure. Depth Anything 3 estimates a Gaussian scene representation and camera extrinsics/intrinsics. Two MLLM scores assess the rendered Gaussian-Splat video (Srecon) and a meta-view image from the farthest recovered camera pose (Smeta). Camera trajectory score Straj combines path-shape similarity, motion-extent agreement, geodesic rotation error, and final-frame rotation error with adaptive weighting based on motion profile. S3D = (Srecon + Smeta + Straj)/3. Interaction track: CLIP cosine similarity between 8 uniformly sampled frames per chunk and their captions provides a lightweight semantic grounding signal, calibrated to [0,1] with fixed thresholds (τmin=0.20, τmax=0.40). An MLLM judge evaluates at three levels: per-chunk (visual quality + text alignment, score 0–5), per-transition (smoothness between adjacent chunks), and global (long-range consistency of stitched video). Sinteract = 0.9×Smllm + 0.1×calibrated CLIP. Overall Sall = (Sphys + S3D + Sinteract)/3. 8 publicly available pipelines are evaluated via OpenWorldLib: LingBot-World (14B), Cosmos-Predict-2.5 (2B), Rolling Forcing, Yume-1.5, LongLive, Hunyuan-WorldPlay, WoW, and Matrix-Game 2.0. Key Results: LingBot-World achieves the highest overall score (0.683): physical 0.942, 3D 0.373, interaction 0.734. Cosmos-Predict-2.5 (2B parameters) achieves a comparable overall score (0.671: physical 0.906, 3D 0.399, interaction 0.707), demonstrating that targeted physical-world training can partially compensate for smaller model scale. Geometry scores are uniformly weak: the highest is Hunyuan-WorldPlay at 0.424, with most models in the 0.25–0.40 range. Hunyuan-WorldPlay's geometry advantage is structural—it uses viewpoint/camera control as its primary interaction modality, which preserves static-scene structure at the cost of flexible action-conditioned dynamics, leaving it ranked 6th overall (0.477). Physical scores stratify by dimension: mechanics compliance reaches 0.94–1.00 for top models, while thermodynamics remains 0.37–0.61 in gaming and collapses to 0.000 in robotics due to absence of relevant phenomena. Matrix-Game 2.0 scores lowest overall (0.231), with interaction at 0.113, despite being gaming-focused. WoW generalizes poorly outside its training domain: 0.502 on embodied, but only 0.368 on gaming and 0.415 on general. Human preference alignment: Spearman ρ = 0.95 with automatic rankings across 8 models in a study with 5 annotators, 560 prompt-level pairwise comparisons, and 2,800 individual labels. The two ranking disagreements (LongLive/Yume-1.5 and Matrix-Game 2.0/WoW) involve only adjacent model pairs. Novelty Classification: NEW BENCHMARK / NEW DATASET. The paper's contribution is an evaluation framework. The individual components—CLIP, MLLM-as-judge, Gaussian splatting, Depth Anything 3, SAM—are all existing tools; what is genuinely novel is their integration into a unified three-track evaluation protocol targeting world-model capabilities that prior benchmarks systematically miss, along with the 1,000-video annotated dataset and the associated structured annotation pipeline. Rigor & Reproducibility: Code and data are promised at https://github.com/alibaba-damo-academy/WorldOlympiad (preprint status; not independently verified as fully released). The human preference validation study is unusually thorough: 2,800 pairwise labels, 5 annotators, 560 prompt comparisons, with full annotation protocol and interface documented. However, no ablation is provided for the metric design choices: the CLIP calibration thresholds (τmin=0.20, τmax=0.40) and MLLM interaction weighting (λ=0.1) are not justified empirically. The MLLM judge quality is not validated beyond the overall human study—no inter-judge agreement (e.g., multiple MLLM runs on the same video) or per-track human alignment is reported. Thermodynamics scores are 0.000 for all robotics-domain models, which correctly reflects absence of relevant phenomena but is uninformative as a discriminator—this is acknowledged implicitly through domain decomposition tables but not flagged as a benchmark design concern. This is a preprint (arXiv:2606.11129v1), not peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL — a benchmark system evaluated by running 8 video generation pipelines through an automated evaluation harness. - Practical: The full evaluation pipeline requires Gaussian splatting via Depth Anything 3, SAM3 for dynamic-object segmentation, CLIP, and multiple sequential MLLM API calls (Gemini-3-Pro-Preview); each component is available commercially, but the combined per-video cost and hardware requirements are not reported. - Efficient: Per-video evaluation cost—in API tokens, wall-clock time, or GPU-hours for Gaussian splatting—is not stated; the pipeline involves at least 6–8 distinct MLLM calls per video plus depth estimation and Gaussian rendering, suggesting high cost that could limit rapid iteration. - Effective: Spearman ρ = 0.95 with human preference at the model-ranking level is strong, but the metrics are validated only for ranking 8 specific models—whether they reliably discriminate quality within a model's outputs or detect fine-grained improvements is not tested. - Scalable: The benchmark evaluates only 8 models; MLLM judge prompts are fixed and their reliability as models of different generations are added is unknown. Geometry scores are compressed into a narrow range (0.25–0.42) with no evidence this range will expand as models improve, raising discriminability concerns. The paper does not deliver a trained model, library, or SDK—it delivers a dataset, evaluation scripts, and a leaderboard. Strengths: - The three-track decomposition (physical / geometric / interactive) is conceptually rigorous: the tracks are orthogonal, each targets failure modes that standard visual-quality metrics cannot detect, and the diagnostic breakdown tables (per domain, per physical rule, per interaction level, per geometry submetric) let practitioners attribute failures to specific capability gaps. - The human preference alignment study (ρ = 0.95, 2,800 pairwise labels) is unusually careful for a benchmark paper and provides genuine validation that the automatic metrics capture real model-quality differences at the ranking level. - The geometry track using Gaussian splatting reconstruction as a 3D consistency probe is technically creative—treating video generation as an implicit 3D modeling task and auditing the implied 3D structure is a novel evaluation approach not used by prior video benchmarks. - The three-stage chunk-caption-refine annotation pipeline (Gemini-3-Pro-Preview, with hallucination correction and narrative continuity refinement) produces structured, per-chunk action-caption pairs that enable the multi-level interaction evaluation and provide a reusable annotation resource. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors acknowledge the geometry-simulation trade-off: models using viewpoint/camera control score higher on geometry but sacrifice flexible action-conditioned dynamics (Hunyuan-WorldPlay example). They note that WoW's specialization does not generalize beyond embodied robotics, and that domain-specific training can help or hurt depending on transferability of the learned knowledge. The future work section explicitly acknowledges that varying model scales, training data, and architectures confound comparisons of memory mechanisms, and plans a controlled evaluation to disentangle these factors. The authors confront these limitations directly rather than minimizing them, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The geometry track's validity as a 3D consistency proxy is assumed: Depth Anything 3 + Gaussian splatting may fail on generated videos with dynamic objects, heavy motion blur, or unusual perspectives for reasons unrelated to the model's 3D coherence—the low and compressed geometry scores (0.25–0.42) may reflect pipeline reconstruction failures as much as model weaknesses. No ablation compares Gaussian splatting scores against alternative geometry probes (e.g., multi-view stereo, optical flow consistency). - All 8 evaluated models are accessed through OpenWorldLib, a codebase published by a team that overlaps substantially with the WorldOlympiad authors (Alibaba DAMO Academy). Several evaluated models (e.g., LingBot-World, referred to as work from Robbyant Team with Alibaba affiliations) originate from or are closely associated with the authors' institution, creating potential selection and reporting bias. - The MLLM physical judge reliability is unvalidated beyond overall model-level human preference: no per-rule or per-video inter-rater agreement statistics (between MLLM judge runs, or between MLLM and human physics annotation) are reported. - The CLIP calibration thresholds (τmin=0.20, τmax=0.40) and interaction weighting (λ=0.1) are stated without empirical justification—whether different thresholds would reorder models is not tested. - Thermodynamics compliance is 0.000 for all robotics-domain models across all physical sub-rules, not because models fail physics, but because no thermodynamics-relevant phenomena appear in the robotics reference videos. This is a dataset composition problem, not a model capability finding, but it is not clearly flagged as such. Bottom Line: WorldOlympiad is the most comprehensive automatic evaluation framework for video world models to date, with genuine technical novelty in its 3D and interaction tracks and unusually strong human validation, but practitioners should treat the geometry track scores as suggestive rather than definitive given the unablated Gaussian splatting dependency and the compressed score range that may limit discriminability as models improve.
Recommended 2606.11129 PDF
#87 — Innovative (63)

CHASE: Adversarial Red-Blue Teaming for Improving LLM Safety using Reinforcement Learning

R Markasserithodi, A Joshi, Y Li, I Singh, C Yoo, A Niu · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces CHASE (Co-evolutionary Hardening through Adversarial Safety-Escalation), a closed-loop red-blue teaming framework that trains both a jailbreak attacker and a safety defender via Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), with the attacker operating entirely without jailbreak templates to discover latent adversarial framing primitives. - Why it's important: Safety-aligned LLMs trained on fixed, narrow attack distributions remain brittle against adaptive black-box adversaries, and every prior adversarial training scheme that trains and evaluates on the same attack family provides false assurance of robustness. - How it does it: The attacker is optimized with a multiplicative reward (bypass effectiveness × intent fidelity) that forces discovery of framings simultaneously high on both objectives; the defender is hardened via GRPO exploration followed by rejection-sampled SFT on the harvested attacks, with benign data added at the SFT stage to prevent over-refusal. - The good: A clean controlled ablation (Section 5.3) replaces only the attack distribution—PAIR artifacts vs. CHASE RL-discovered rewrites—under an otherwise identical pipeline, confirming that the RL distribution, not the two-stage pipeline itself, drives the 43.2% mean StrongREJECT reduction across five held-out mechanistically distinct attack families. - The missing: All experiments use a single base model (Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct) with a single LoRA configuration; no evaluation at different scales or on frontier models, and no error bars or significance tests accompany any headline number. - Read it or not: Worth reading for LLM safety alignment researchers interested in adversarial training generalization and the latent-primitives hypothesis; practitioners seeking deployment-ready defenses should wait for replication at scale and on diverse architectures.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (55) and Resilience (55) pull the composite down: all headline numbers are single runs on 100-prompt sets with no seeds or significance tests, human validation rests on three co-authors (two κ pairs only 'moderate'), and the '0% false refusal on Alpaca' headline is contradicted by 55.6% false refusal on XSTest fictional prompts that the paper underweights. Novelty (75) and Non-Obviousness (72) are real — the template-free GRPO attacker with multiplicative reward and the cross-attack-generalization finding (cleanly isolated by the §5.3 ablation) are substantive. Single-model scope and a promised-but-unreleased checkpoint cap Impact (60). Weighted: 0.20*75 + 0.25*55 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*58 + 0.15*55 + 0.10*60 = 63.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 58 Resilience 55 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Safety alignment via RLHF and SFT produces static decision boundaries that are inherently brittle against adaptive black-box adversaries. Prompt-rewriting attacks—persona modulation, fictional framing, persuasion-based reformulation—bypass safety filters even on frontier models by operating entirely in natural language without requiring model internals. Prior co-evolutionary and adversarial training methods (MART, RedHit, R2D2, CAT/CAPO, MixAT) either rely on fixed attack templates or imitation learning, producing defenders that overfit to the trained attack family and collapse on mechanistically distinct alternatives. The gap: a defense policy optimized against attack distribution at time t−1 remains highly vulnerable to novel distributions at time t. Key Idea The central insight is that a GRPO attacker trained with no jailbreak templates—given only a minimal system prompt to produce a rewrite that preserves the seed intent—is forced to converge on latent attack primitives (fictional distancing, persona assignment, hypothetical scenarios, academic contextualisation) that generalize across mechanistically distinct attack families, because the multiplicative reward selects precisely for framings that are both bypass-effective and intent-faithful, and these properties characterize the dominant framing strategies across the entire jailbreak literature. Methodology CHASE formalizes a two-player, strictly black-box adversarial game. The Attacker (Hermes-4-14B, LoRA r=32) and Defender (Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, LoRA r=16) have no access to each other's parameters, gradients, or latent states. Attacker training uses GRPO with multiplicative reward R = Sbypass × Iintent. Sbypass = (1−r)(s+c)/2 combines a hard binary refusal gate with StrongREJECT specificity and convincingness scores; the binary gate zeroes the reward on any clean refusal, preventing partial-compliance exploitation. Iintent is scored by an independent LLM judge J, validated against human annotation (Pearson 0.841, MAE 0.169 on 100 pairs). Eight candidate rewrites per prompt (K=8) are sampled per GRPO step. Critical design decision: template-free initialization, with the system prompt instructing only intent preservation, no attack scaffolding—well-justified as the mechanism enabling cross-attack generalization. Defender hardening uses a two-stage pipeline. Stage 1 (GRPO): trains on ~800 harmful prompts in two layers—Layer 1 are original harmful intents the base model already refuses (GRPO shapes refusal quality), Layer 2 are confirmed CHASE attacker bypasses (where safety learning occurs). Reward Rdef = 0 if complied, else 0.55 + 0.45·Q where Q = 0.7·rq + 0.3·rp (refusal quality + educational-pivot quality). Benign prompts excluded (near-zero gradient signal). LoRA structurally prevents catastrophic forgetting. This stage partially hardens the defender (48% refusal on Layer 2, up from 0% base), but GRPO's variance problem—safety-aligned models are too confident, producing near-uniform scores—prevents full consolidation. Stage 2 (rejection-sampled SFT): samples N candidates at T=1.5 from the GRPO policy, selects the highest-quality confirmed refusal per prompt; where GRPO produces no refusals, synthetic refusal targets are generated by an independent LLM judge. SFT dataset combines Layer 2 refusals, Layer 1 greedy refusals, and Stanford Alpaca benign responses in equal proportions (1:1 harmful:benign). Trained for 1 epoch. Each stage has a distinct non-redundant role; GRPO explores but cannot consolidate; SFT consolidates but needs explicit targets that rejection sampling provides. The co-evolutionary loop alternates attacker and defender updates over T iterations. Training uses 1,000 BeaverTails examples (filtered to is_safe=False, declarative prompts removed via BART-large-MNLI zero-shot classification). Hardware: single NVIDIA RTX PRO 6000, 48GB VRAM, 4-bit quantization. Key Results Primary metric: StrongREJECT score SR = (1−r)(c+s−2)/8 ∈ [0,1], preferred over binary ASR because it conditions bypass credit on convincingness and specificity. Evaluated on BeaverTails (100 harmful prompts) and JailbreakBench (100 behaviors), against five attack families held out from defender training: PAIR, TAP, AutoDAN, PAP, Translation. CHASE defender achieves mean SR reduction of 43.2% (0.479 → 0.272) across all five attacks and both datasets. Strongest reductions: AutoDAN on JailbreakBench (−74.9%, SR 0.509 → 0.128), TAP on JailbreakBench (−54.3%, SR 0.501 → 0.229), PAP on JailbreakBench (−53.9%, SR 0.646 → 0.298). Weakest: PAIR on JailbreakBench (−19.9%, SR 0.705 → 0.565), consistent with PAIR's multi-turn adaptive nature (it observes defender refusals and rewrites specifically to bypass them). Translation attack on JailbreakBench regresses (SR 0.040 → 0.115, +187.5% relative), though the absolute SR of 0.115 reflects negligible actionable content. JailbreakBench standardized evaluation (Llama-3-70B judge): 0% ASR on direct misuse, 0% ASR on PAIR transfer, 0% ASR on GCG transfer. Hand-crafted JBC artifacts achieve 27.8% ASR. Safety-helpfulness: 0% false refusal on 100 Stanford Alpaca benign prompts. MT-Bench: 5.50 vs. 7.42 base (delta −1.92), concentrated in reasoning-heavy categories (Math, STEM, Humanities); coding and extraction largely preserved. XSTest (250 safe prompts): 44.4% overall compliance; structured refusal concentrated on fictional/roleplay categories (8%–28% compliance) vs. factual categories (68%–96% compliance); 99.0% correct refusal on 200 unsafe contrast prompts. Ablation (Section 5.3): PAIR-Artifact defender achieves −28.6% mean SR vs. CHASE's −43.2%, with sharp SR reduction on PAIR (−87.3% on BeaverTails) but collapse on TAP (−9.2%), AutoDAN (−12.8%), and PAP (+1.1%), confirming the RL distribution is the causal variable. Multi-iteration convergence: iteration 2 reaches 0.162 mean SR (−66.1%), iteration 3 reaches 0.115 (−76.0%), with per-iteration delta shrinking from 23 to 10 percentage points—the characteristic signature of a converging adversarial game. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The multiplicative reward decomposition and template-free GRPO attacker are both novel design choices, and the cross-attack generalization to five unseen mechanistically distinct attack families—empirically confirmed by a controlled ablation varying only the attack distribution—is a new finding. The co-evolutionary red-blue teaming structure is not new (MART, RedHit), and GRPO/LoRA fine-tuning are standard components; the genuine contribution is the combination of template-free attacker initialization with multiplicative reward, and the demonstrated claim that this recovers latent primitives that generalize beyond the narrow distributions of prior work. Rigor & Reproducibility The Section 5.3 ablation cleanly isolates the causal variable (attack distribution) under otherwise identical pipeline, hyperparameters, and evaluation—a methodological strength. However, no error bars, multiple seeds, or statistical significance tests are reported on any result; all headline numbers are single experimental runs over 100-prompt evaluation sets, making precision estimates unavailable. Human validation of the intent-fidelity judge uses only 100 pairs annotated by three co-authors (not independent crowdworkers)—a credibility concern. Inter-annotator Cohen's Kappa ranges 0.467–0.702; the two lowest pairs (0.467, 0.484) fall in the "moderate" range by conventional thresholds, weakening the human validation claim. LLM judges replace human annotation for all primary evaluation. BeaverTails evaluation set (100 prompts from 30k_test split) is drawn from the same distribution as training data (30k_train), raising a mild in-distribution concern for BeaverTails results; JailbreakBench is a cleaner out-of-distribution test. Code and defender checkpoint are promised for release; no URL is provided in the paper. The attacker model and training data are withheld for safety reasons. This is an arXiv preprint (v1), not peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Runs on a single 48GB GPU (NVIDIA RTX PRO 6000) with 4-bit quantization and LoRA adapters, accessible to academic labs without specialized infrastructure. - Efficient: Full compute cost not reported; LoRA (r=16–32) is parameter-efficient; 4-bit quantization reduces memory; attacker (14B) is 1.75× larger than defender (8B), an asymmetry with undiscussed implications for cost. - Effective: Achieves 43.2% SR reduction on five held-out attack families with 0% false refusal on Alpaca, but 55.6% false refusal on XSTest safe fictional/roleplay prompts represents a serious usability cost for deployment in general-purpose assistants; the "0% false refusal" headline is misleadingly narrow. - Scalable: Tested only on Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct; transfer to other scales and architectures explicitly untested; multi-turn adaptive attacks (PAIR) systematically receive weaker protection, indicating a structural boundary against adversaries who observe and adapt to the defender. The paper delivers a defender checkpoint and evaluation code (promised, no URL), not a production-ready library. Strengths - The template-free attacker design paired with the Section 5.3 controlled ablation provides genuinely strong causal evidence that the RL-discovered distribution is responsible for cross-attack generalization—this is the paper's most original and empirically credible contribution. - Multiplicative reward R = Sbypass × Iintent is elegantly motivated: it eliminates two known reward-hacking pathologies (intent drift, over-sanitisation collapse) with a single structural decision, and the mechanism is clearly explained with formal notation. - Multi-iteration convergence results (Table 5, Figures 6–7) are internally consistent: defender SR falls monotonically with diminishing returns, attacker bypass quality degrades as defender hardens, and attack-family rank ordering is preserved across iterations—behavior expected from a converging adversarial game, supporting framework integrity. - XSTest per-type breakdown (Table 2) provides honest, interpretable structure to the over-refusal cost, directly linking the failure mode to the adversarial framing categories that dominate in-the-wild jailbreaks. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state: (1) single base model and LoRA configuration, no transfer to other architectures or scales; (2) MT-Bench cost of −1.92 limits suitability for reasoning/mathematical applications; (3) defender classifies by framing features rather than user intent, causing elevated refusal on benign fictional queries; (4) all evaluations rely on LLM judges rather than human annotation; (5) PAIR multi-turn attacks only partially mitigated, characterizing the boundary of single-cycle training. The ethical statement acknowledges dual-use risk and restricts artifact release to the defender only. The authors confront limitations directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - All headline numbers are single runs on 100-prompt evaluation sets with no error bars, seeds, or significance tests; the 43.2% reduction could be within noise at this evaluation scale. - Human validation annotators are co-authors, not independent; two of three inter-annotator kappa pairs (0.467, 0.484) reach only "moderate" agreement, which is insufficient to strongly validate the automated judge on which all training and evaluation signals depend. - The translation attack regression on JailbreakBench (SR 0.040 → 0.115, +187.5% relative) is soft-pedaled as "negligible actionable content"; the mechanism is unexplained and could indicate that LoRA training interferes with cross-lingual alignment more broadly than reported. - "0% false refusal on Alpaca" is presented as the helpfulness metric, but the XSTest result (55.6% false refusal on safe fictional/roleplay prompts) reveals a substantially different picture of deployment usability that the paper underweights relative to the Alpaca headline. - No ablation on GRPO alone for the defender vs. the full GRPO+SFT pipeline vs. SFT alone from a non-RL initialization; it is not possible to isolate how much of the defender improvement comes from RL exploration vs. rejection-sampled SFT. - Attacker base model (Hermes-4-14B) is 1.75× larger than the defender (Llama-3.1-8B); it is unclear whether a size-matched attacker achieves comparable generalization or whether the result is partly an artifact of attacker capacity advantage. - No evaluation against frontier-scale models (GPT-4, Claude 3.5, Llama-3-70B) as either targets or defenders; the claim of practical significance for safety alignment cannot be assessed without results at scale. Bottom Line The template-free RL attacker and cross-attack generalization finding are the most substantive contributions to LLM adversarial training since MART, and researchers working on safety alignment generalization should read Section 5.3 carefully; however, the single-model scope, LLM-only judging with co-author annotators, and absence of statistical rigor make this preprint's quantitative claims premature for informing deployment decisions.
Recommended 2606.05523 PDF
#88 — Innovative (63)

CLI-Anything: Towards Agent -Native Computer Use

Y Yang, T Fan, C Huang · arxiv.org · automated generated environments for agent testing - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces CLI-Anything, a methodology and implemented platform for transforming GUI applications into stateful command-line harnesses — agent-native contracts exposing typed commands, JSON state, real-backend rendering, and artifact-level verification — plus CLI-Hub as the distribution and discovery layer. - Why it's important: GUI agents waste model capacity navigating brittle pixel-level interactions designed for human perception; redirecting agents to programmatic interfaces could substantially improve reliability for professional software tasks where the application backend already contains inspectable state and native rendering paths. - How it does it: By "lifting" application backends via interface archaeology to expose existing scripting APIs (e.g., Blender's bpy), headless renderers, or in-process bridges (e.g., a C# mod inside Slay the Spire II's Godot runtime) into stateful CLIs, with the core design rule that if an artifact can be programmatically verified it can usually be programmatically constructed at the same boundary. - The good: The Blender case study is a concrete, deployed implementation — 54 commands, 228 tests including PNG magic-byte artifact checks and real headless renders — demonstrating that the methodology produces testable systems rather than only design arguments. - The missing: No controlled experiment comparing agent task-completion rates or error rates between CLI harnesses and GUI agents on shared tasks; the central performance claim rests entirely on architectural argument and recorded demos, with no head-to-head empirical baseline. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners building agent infrastructure, benchmark environments, or computer-use evaluation suites; the formal contract, dual case studies, and E2E test philosophy are actionable, but readers expecting empirical proof of agent performance gains will find only a systems report.
Why this rank: Systems profile. Impact (72) and Novelty (72) lead: a deployed platform with public code, 65+ implemented harnesses, real adoption telemetry (129,916 calls, 7.19× agent/human ratio), and a clarifying NEW FORMULATION (verification/execution symmetry) plus the architecturally novel in-process bridge pattern for opaque runtimes. Rigor (50) and Resilience (52) are the ceiling: the central claim that CLI harnesses beat GUI agents rests entirely on architectural argument and demos — no controlled head-to-head on shared tasks — and the telemetry is self-reported with an undefined observation window and no success/failure breakdown. Completeness (58) is helped by honest scope-bounding but hurt by unquantified per-application lift cost and a formal contract used only descriptively. Composite ≈ 0.15·72 + 0.15·50 + 0.10·70 + 0.15·58 + 0.15·52 + 0.30·72 = 63.
systems Novelty 72 Rigor 50 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 58 Resilience 52 Impact 72
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation GUI agents — systems that control applications by interpreting screenshots, locating UI elements, and simulating click events — are the dominant paradigm for LLM-based computer use on professional software. This paradigm forces agents to decode compressed pixel representations of state, cope with timing-dependent and coordinate-sensitive interactions that break when interfaces change, and translate structured computational reasoning through a perceptual bottleneck designed for human motor control. Researchers building agent benchmarks (OSWorld, AndroidWorld, AppWorld) and practitioners deploying agents on professional tools face a shared gap: applications already contain inspectable backends, native scripting APIs, project file formats, and deterministic render paths, but these are hidden behind GUI event machinery. There is no standard methodology for exposing professional non-coding software as executable, verifiable environments for agents analogous to the repository-shell-test stack available for coding agents. Key Idea The central insight is "verification/execution symmetry": if a software artifact (a `.blend` scene, an ODF document, an SVG diagram, a video timeline) can be programmatically inspected and verified, it can usually also be programmatically constructed — so the construction interface should meet the verification checker at the same artifact boundary rather than routing every construction step through pixel manipulation. This implies lifting the application's existing backend (scripting API, headless CLI mode, native file format) into a stateful harness that gives agents typed commands, JSON state, and backend-delegated rendering, without reimplementing the application's logic in Python. Methodology The paper defines a 7-stage Harness Lift Pipeline: (1) Locate — trace the application's file formats, backend call patterns, and rendered output paths; (2) Model — name domain nouns and map GUI operations to native API calls or file mutations; (3) Design — specify a Click-based command grammar producing JSON responses with recoverable error messages; (4) Implement — build persistent project state, session replay, undo/redo history, and dry-run modes; (5) Render — delegate artifact generation to the real application backend (e.g., `blender --background`, `libreoffice --headless`, `ffmpeg`); (6) Verify — run unit tests on synthetic state and E2E tests that call the real backend and check output artifacts by magic bytes, ZIP structure, pixel content, or media properties — not just exit codes; (7) Publish — generate SKILL.md, register in CLI-Hub catalog. Harnesses are formalized as a 6-tuple H = (S, C, I, R, V, D): persistent state space, command vocabulary, inspection surface, rendering/export relation, verification layer, and discovery layer. Dual-mode design supports both stateful REPL and one-shot subcommand invocation. Two case studies show qualitatively distinct lift scenarios. Blender: harness attaches at the bpy/RNA scripting layer, maintains a `.blend-cli.json` JSON scene contract as working state, translates it into full bpy scripts via a scene-contract lowering pass, and invokes `blender --background` for real rendering; 54 commands, 12 Click groups, 228 tests. Slay the Spire II: no external scripting API exists, so a C# bridge mod (STS2_Bridge) is installed inside the Godot runtime, opens a loopback HTTP listener on port 15526, routes HTTP requests onto the game main thread via `ConcurrentQueue<Action>` drained on `ProcessFrame`, and normalizes 15 decision states and 24 action verbs; a Python CLI adapter outside the game process exposes 28 Click commands; 14 tests. Key Results Catalog: 83 total CLI entries (65 generated/curated + 18 public third-party) across 32 combined categories. CLI-Hub telemetry (self-reported, observed usage window undefined): 129,916 total calls, 87.8% agent share (114,057 agent vs. 15,859 human calls), 7.19× agent/human call ratio, +694.1% call growth over the observed period. Companion skills: 61. Preview-capable integrations: 5 (Blender, FreeCAD, Shotcut, Openscreen, RenderDoc). Blender harness specifics: 8 primitive types, 8 modifier types, EEVEE/Cycles/Workbench render support, version-tolerant bpy generation. Slay the Spire II harness: bridge normalizes RunManager, CombatManager, NOverlayStack, NMapScreen, NRewardsScreen, NCardGridSelectionScreen, NMerchantRoom into 15 named decision states. No task-completion accuracy numbers, no latency benchmarks, no baseline comparison against GUI agents on any shared task set. The primary evaluation is artifact demonstration (recorded midpoint frames from Blender, Slay the Spire II, and FreeCAD runs) plus catalog and usage counts. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW FORMULATION. The system contribution (CLI-Anything framework, CLI-Hub platform, implemented harnesses, deployed usage telemetry) is genuine and surpasses a prototype. The formal contract H=(S,C,I,R,V,D) and the verification/execution symmetry principle provide a clean conceptual framing, though the underlying ideas (headless scripting, CLI wrappers, artifact-level testing) are established engineering practice — the novel contribution is their synthesis and application specifically to LLM agent computer use, with the in-process bridge pattern for opaque-runtime applications being the most architecturally novel element. Rigor & Reproducibility Code is publicly released (https://github.com/HKUDS/CLI-Anything; CLI-Hub at https://clianything.cc). The Blender harness includes 228 tests with backend-gated E2E checks (magic-byte validation, subprocess-executed bpy scripts, real render output verification), which is a concrete reproducibility positive. No ablation studies isolate which harness components (state persistence, artifact verification, preview protocol) independently improve agent performance. No error bars, statistical significance tests, or multiple-run averages are reported. The CLI-Hub usage telemetry is self-reported by the authors who operate the platform; the "observed usage window" is not defined, agent client configurations are not described, and it is unknown whether call volume reflects successful task completion or repeated retries. The SOP methodology (HARNESS.md) is included in the repository, supporting replication of the lift process. This is a preprint (arXiv:2606.03854v1, June 2, 2026); not peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS — a built platform evaluated by deployment evidence. - Practical: The harnesses require the real application installed locally (Blender, GIMP, LibreOffice, etc.) as an explicit hard dependency; CLI-Hub supports pip, npm, uv, brew, bundled, and script install strategies across 18 public entries. Runs on standard developer hardware; no specialized GPU required for most harnesses though Blender GPU rendering is optional. - Efficient: Each render/export call spawns a subprocess invoking the real application, adding process-launch overhead (typically seconds per Blender headless render) but eliminating screenshot-capture and OCR overhead of GUI agents; session JSON state is lightweight. Compute cost is not quantified against any baseline. - Effective: Effectiveness is demonstrated through recorded demos and artifact-level test suites, not through controlled task-completion experiments. The 87.8% agent call share on CLI-Hub shows real adoption but does not establish whether agents successfully complete tasks or what the failure rate is. - Scalable: Scaling to new applications requires per-application lift effort (interface archaeology, command grammar design, backend integration, test suite) which is not quantified. The 83-entry catalog shows moderate breadth; a future "CLI Matrix" for scenario-level organization is described as experimental and not yet implemented. The work delivers: a software platform (CLI-Anything + CLI-Hub with registry), a documented methodology (HARNESS.md SOP), and implemented harnesses across 65+ applications. It does not deliver a benchmark, a trained model, or a dataset. Strengths - The verification/execution symmetry principle is genuinely clarifying: observing that artifact inspectability implies constructability at the same boundary gives practitioners a concrete decision rule for when to prefer CLI over GUI automation, grounded in the structure of the artifact rather than agent capability assumptions. - The two case studies are architecturally informative and qualitatively distinct — Blender illustrates lifting a mature scripting API; Slay the Spire II illustrates inserting an in-process bridge inside a frame-owned runtime where no external API exists — together covering the two most common real-world scenarios. - The E2E test philosophy (magic-byte checks, ZIP structure validation, pixel-level artifact verification, backend-gated subprocess tests rather than exit-code trust) is a mature, underspecified standard in agent environment design that this paper makes explicit and implements. - Real deployment telemetry (129,916 calls, 7.19× agent/human ratio, +694.1% growth) distinguishes CLI-Anything from a purely academic prototype and provides concrete evidence of agent adoption in the wild. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly bound the scope: some software exposes only compiled binaries, opaque state, or interaction patterns requiring accessibility APIs or GUI automation that the harness approach cannot lift; some creative decisions require rich visual judgment; some preview integrations are early with incomplete visual state coverage; benchmarkable artifact construction task suites are future work, not present evaluation. The paper maintains a consistent claim: CLI is the correct default when a backend can be lifted, not a universal replacement for GUI agents. Limitations are stated directly rather than minimized, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The central performance claim — that CLI harnesses improve agent reliability over GUI agents — is unsubstantiated by any controlled experiment; no head-to-head comparison on shared tasks (e.g., OSWorld tasks re-expressed as CLI-lifted equivalents) is presented, making it impossible to quantify the benefit. - Harness construction cost is entirely unquantified: the paper provides a methodology but reports no estimate of developer hours, lines of code, or iteration cycles required to lift a new application, nor how this scales with application complexity or backend opacity. - CLI-Hub telemetry lacks necessary context: the observation window is undefined, agent client identities and task types are not described, and no failure rate is reported — a high call volume could reflect repeated retries on failing tasks rather than successful completions. - The formal H=(S,C,I,R,V,D) contract is used descriptively rather than analytically; it does not enable formal comparison between harnesses, derivation of quality bounds, or detection of contract violations. - The catalog skews toward open-source applications with known scripting APIs (Blender, GIMP, Inkscape, LibreOffice, FreeCAD); coverage for proprietary desktop applications, web-native software, and mobile apps — where the majority of enterprise software resides — is not evaluated. Bottom Line A practitioner building agent infrastructure for professional software or agent evaluation environments gains a concrete, deployed methodology and ecosystem with real adoption evidence — but the absence of any controlled comparison with GUI agents means the performance claims remain architectural arguments, and the paper should be read as a systems report and design manifesto rather than an empirical result.
Search Results 2606.03854 PDF
#89 — Innovative (63)

When Tools Fail: Benchmarking Dynamic Replanning and Anomaly Recovery in LLM Agents (13 ▲ )

Dongsheng Zhu, Xuchen Ma, Yucheng Shen, Xiang Li, Yukun Zhao, Shuaiqiang Wang, Lingyong Yan, Dawei Yin · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 8 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces TOOLMAZE, a benchmark that evaluates LLM agents' ability to dynamically replan and recover from tool failures using a two-dimensional design combining DAG-based topological complexity (C1–C4) and a 2×2 taxonomy of tool perturbations (explicit/implicit × transient/permanent). - Why it's important: Existing benchmarks evaluate tool-integrated reasoning only on error-free "happy paths," leaving unexamined whether agents can handle the pervasive real-world reality of tool failures, corrupted outputs, and dependency-graph dead ends. - How it does it: Tasks are constructed tool-first as DAGs with exhaustively enumerated valid recovery paths, then perturbed deterministically at pre-specified nodes; three metrics (TSR, PRR, RC) isolate overall completion, recovery capability, and replanning efficiency independently. - The good: Fault-tolerance scales 3.66× more slowly with model size than baseline task execution (4.88 pp vs. 17.85 pp per order-of-magnitude in parameters), a concrete empirical finding that dynamic recovery is a distinct, under-trained capability. - The missing: All 270 tools are simulated via static lookup tables and the implicit failure generation requires corruptions to be "obvious to a human or AI," which likely understates the severity of the most dangerous real-world implicit failures where corruption is subtle. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building or evaluating tool-using agents; the PRR/RC metrics and the implicit-explicit trust gap analysis provide non-obvious, actionable diagnostics that TSR alone cannot surface.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (74) and Novelty (72) anchor the score: the orthogonal C×P design (DAG topology × perturbation taxonomy) with exhaustive solution-space enumeration enabling the new PRR/RC metrics is genuinely novel, and the findings that fault-tolerance scales 3.66× more slowly than task execution and that agents over-trust structurally valid outputs (53.75 pp implicit-explicit PRR gap) are concrete and actionable. Rigor (55) is pulled down by single-run evaluation at temperature=1 with no error bars or significance tests, task generation by two of the evaluated models (distributional bias), and static lookup-table tool simulation. Resilience (58) is the binding limit — ~5 pp ranking differences are within noise, and the 'corruptions must be obvious' constraint bounds implicit difficulty below the real-world worst case while the hint prompt conflates anomaly detection with prompt-following. Composite ≈ 0.20·72 + 0.25·55 + 0.15·74 + 0.15·62 + 0.15·58 + 0.10·58 = 63.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 74 Completeness 62 Resilience 58 Impact 58
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM agents increasingly integrate external tools to perform multi-step tasks, but real-world tool ecosystems fail constantly — with network errors, rate limits, deprecated APIs (explicit failures), and structurally valid but semantically wrong outputs such as negative prices or mismatched data fields (implicit failures). Prior benchmarks assume perfectly functioning tools, so they measure a best-case "happy path" capability that does not predict how agents behave when tools fail. The specific gap addressed is that no existing benchmark (a) fully enumerates the ground-truth solution space of valid recovery paths, (b) distinguishes systematic replanning from blind trial-and-error, or (c) separately characterizes recovery from implicit semantic failures versus explicit error signals. Key Idea: The central insight is to decouple two orthogonal dimensions of difficulty — where an agent can recover (topological structure of the DAG, controlling how many alternative paths exist) and what triggers recovery (the perturbation type, controlling whether the failure signal is machine-readable or semantically hidden). By constructing task graphs before naturalizing them into user queries, TOOLMAZE guarantees a provably complete ground-truth solution set, making it possible to define metrics that penalize inefficient exploration rather than just measuring end-state success. Methodology: TOOLMAZE is built as a C×P evaluation matrix. The C-axis defines four DAG complexity levels: C1 (linear chain, no alternatives), C2 (1-to-N: one equivalent substitute per fault point), C3 (N-to-N: combinatorial multi-path subgraphs), and C4 (integrated multi-branch combining multiple C2 and C3 patterns). The P-axis crosses error manifestation (explicit vs. implicit) with temporal persistence (transient vs. permanent), yielding P1 (explicit-transient, expect retry), P2 (explicit-permanent, expect reroute or graceful abort), P3 (implicit-transient), and P4 (implicit-permanent). Task generation follows a tool-first paradigm: (1) an LLM-based DAG Architect assembles a structurally validated DAG sampled from a 270-tool corpus across six domains; (2) solution-space enumeration exhaustively identifies all valid substitutability-equivalent recovery paths; (3) an LLM naturalizes the DAG into a context-rich user query, validated by a reverse-reconstruction check. A runtime perturbation engine injects pre-specified faults deterministically — the same synthetic response is returned to every model, eliminating variance across runs. Three metrics are defined: TSR (binary task success rate), PRR (conditional probability of resolving an encountered perturbation, conditioned on exposure to the fault), and RC (replanning efficiency penalizing extra tool calls beyond the theoretical minimum). Models are evaluated with a standard tool-use prompt (w/o hint) and a failure-aware prompt (w/ hint) that explicitly warns of potential failures. Key design decisions — static lookup simulation for reproducibility, exhaustive solution enumeration before naturalization, and deterministic fault injection per (task, P) pair — are each well-justified. One questionable choice is requiring implicit corruptions to be "obvious without external verification," which artificially bounds difficulty. Key Results: Nine frontier models are evaluated: GPT-5.5, Gemini-3.1-Pro-Preview, Claude-Sonnet-4-6 (proprietary); GLM-5.1, Deepseek-V4-Pro, MiniMax-M2.7, Qwen3.5-35B-A3B, Qwen3.5-397B-A17B, Qwen3.6-27B (open-weight). On the composite average (TSR + PRR + (1−RC)) / 3 with failure-aware prompting, Gemini-3.1-Pro-Preview leads at 52.95%, followed by GPT-5.5 (49.96%), Claude-Sonnet-4-6 (46.14%), and Deepseek-V4-Pro (45.09%). Claude-Sonnet-4-6 achieves the highest non-perturbed TSR (77.00%) but degrades severely under perturbation. The implicit-explicit PRR gap is 53.75 pp for transient failures (P1 PRR = 81.44%, P3 = 27.68%) and 20.54 pp for permanent failures (P2 = 38.12%, P4 = 17.58%), averaged across all models with the failure-aware prompt. Under P4 (implicit-permanent), PRR falls below 20% and RC exceeds 70% on average — agents not only fail to recover but waste many steps doing so. Failure-aware prompting yields consistent improvements (+1.5% to +20.8%) but leaves a large gap to the non-perturbed baseline. A log-linear fit on open-weight model sizes (32B to ~1T parameters) shows TSR(NP) grows 17.85 pp per order of magnitude while PRR grows only 4.88 pp. C2 outperforms C1 under perturbation because alternative paths exist; C3 and C4 degrade progressively as path search space combinatorially expands. Baselines are contemporary frontier models (2025–2026 releases) and represent a strong comparison set. Novelty Classification: NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The benchmark design (DAG topology × perturbation taxonomy with exhaustive solution enumeration) is genuinely novel and goes beyond prior work that injects faults randomly or lacks complete ground-truth path enumeration. The PRR and RC metrics are new. The empirical finding that fault-tolerance scales 3.66× more slowly than general task capability is a non-obvious, quantified result. The individual techniques (DAG construction, LLM-based naturalization, perturbation injection) are standard; the novelty lies in their specific composition and the evaluation framework. Rigor & Reproducibility: No error bars or multi-seed results are reported — each model is evaluated once per (task, perturbation) pair with temperature=1, introducing non-trivial stochasticity, especially given the 100-task-per-C-level scale. No significance tests distinguish model rankings. The tool simulation uses static lookup tables ensuring reproducibility across runs, and all injected fault responses are deterministic. However, the 400 base tasks were generated by GPT-5.5 and Gemini-3.1-Pro-Preview, two of the models also being evaluated, creating a potential distributional bias favoring those models' natural task formats. The tool corpus (270 tools) was manually constructed, making extension costly. Code and data are available at https://github.com/Zhudongsheng75/ToolMaze. No trained model weights are released (benchmark only). Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (experiments on 9 models over 2,000 benchmark instances). - Practical: The benchmark runs on standard API access to the evaluated models; the simulated tool environment requires no external API keys or network access, making it fully self-contained. - Efficient: Each task instance runs up to 25 agent steps at temperature=1 with 16K token budget; at 2,000 instances × 9 models × 2 prompt variants, total inference cost is substantial but not extreme by current LLM evaluation standards. - Effective: PRR and RC together cleanly distinguish recovery capability from luck, and the 16 qualitative case studies corroborate that the metrics capture behaviorally meaningful differences (e.g., sanity ignorance, retry failures, permanent-failure non-termination). - Scalable: The 270-tool corpus and 400-task base set are modest; the paper does not test whether benchmark conclusions hold for larger or domain-specialized tool corpora. The LLM-based generation pipeline is described as scalable but is not demonstrated at significantly larger scale. The work delivers a benchmark repository, not a library or deployable system. Strengths: - The two-dimensional C×P design is a principled contribution: it separates structural difficulty (topology) from signal difficulty (perturbation type) in a way no prior benchmark does, enabling clean attribution of failure modes. - Exhaustive solution-space enumeration before task naturalization is a technically sound choice that makes PRR and RC meaningful; prior work either lacks ground truth or computes success heuristically. - The implicit-explicit trust gap finding (53.75 pp PRR drop for transient failures) is concrete and alarming: current models systematically over-trust structurally valid tool outputs, a failure mode with direct deployment consequences. - The scaling analysis (3.66× discrepancy) uses a log-linear fit on six models spanning 32B to ~1T parameters and yields a falsifiable, quantified claim that should inform future training decisions. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors note two limitations directly: (1) TOOLMAZE uses procedurally generated DAG topologies rather than open-ended web environments, so findings may not fully generalize to unstructured agentic workflows with ambiguous success criteria; (2) the 2×2 failure taxonomy is foundational and does not cover cascading multi-hop failures or adversarial injections. Both are stated clearly and the authors position them as future work directions rather than minimizing them. Analyst-Identified Gaps: Several gaps go unacknowledged. First, the implicit failure generation prompt explicitly requires corruptions to be "obvious to a human or AI without external verification" — this excludes the most consequential real-world implicit failures, which are subtle statistical anomalies or domain-specific violations requiring world knowledge. The benchmark's implicit difficulty is therefore bounded below the real-world worst case. Second, task generation by GPT-5.5 and Gemini-3.1-Pro-Preview introduces distributional bias: those models' natural task phrasing and tool-selection patterns may inflate their evaluated performance. Third, the failure-aware prompt (w/ hint) essentially provides a policy specification — it tells the agent what recovery strategies exist — making it unclear whether observed improvements reflect genuine anomaly detection or prompt-following. Fourth, with 100 tasks per complexity level and no error bars, ranking differences within ~5 pp on the composite score are likely within noise. Fifth, the 25-step cap may disproportionately penalize or benefit certain models at C4 complexity, yet its effect is not ablated. Bottom Line: TOOLMAZE fills a real and important gap in TIR evaluation, and the core empirical finding — that fault-tolerance barely scales with model size while task execution scales strongly — is a clear, actionable signal for the agent training community that warrants reading by anyone building or evaluating tool-using LLM systems.
Recommended 2606.05806 PDF
#90 — Innovative (63)

AgentCanary: A Security Evaluation Framework for Autonomous AI Agents in Real Executable Environments

P Li, S Wang, Y Huang, Y Shi, C Zhang, Q Li, Y Lyu… · arxiv.org · automated generated environments for agent testing - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces AgentCanary, a security evaluation framework for autonomous AI agents that combines an orthogonal Entry × Impact risk taxonomy, a high-fidelity real-tool executable environment running in Docker containers, and trajectory-grounded three-dimensional scoring (Outcome Safety, Security Awareness, Task Utility) to evaluate 12 frontier LLMs across 496 seed tasks and multiple attack regimes. - Why it's important: As AI agents gain the ability to execute persistent, multi-step actions on file systems, email, financial accounts, and external services, safety failures shift from textual deception to irreversible system compromise, and existing benchmarks using mocked APIs or binary attack-success labels cannot faithfully measure or distinguish these failures. - How it does it: The framework decouples where adversarial influence enters an agent (5 risk entries: direct injection, indirect injection, skill poisoning, memory contamination, intrinsic failures) from what harm results (7 impact categories), instantiates each combination as executable Docker-isolated tasks with dynamically provisioned artifacts, records full agent trajectories including system-level side effects, and scores three orthogonal dimensions via LLM-based rubric judges. - The good: The discovery and quantification of "silent obedience" in indirect prompt injection — where OSS exceeds 90 while SAS stays below 25 for many models, showing that safe-looking outcomes can mask complete absence of threat recognition — is a genuinely useful empirical finding with direct implications for deployment brittleness. - The missing: Claude Opus 4.6, which ranks first on the benchmark with an overall score of 83.9, is also used as the sole LLM judge for all trajectories of all models, creating an unacknowledged conflict of interest that inflates confidence in the top-ranked result. - Read it or not: Security researchers evaluating or deploying agentic LLM systems should read it for the taxonomy, the executable environment design, and the empirical severity map across attack surfaces; practitioners relying solely on the model rankings should treat the absolute numbers cautiously given the self-judging concern.
Why this rank: Systems profile. Practical Impact (72) leads — an open-sourced, Docker-isolated, framework-agnostic benchmark harness that discriminates models across a 46-point score range. Novelty (70) is real: the Entry×Impact factorization and three-dimensional OSS/SAS/TUS scoring, plus the silent-obedience finding, advance beyond flat attack-success lists. But Rigor (55), Completeness (55), and Resilience (52) are all materially reduced by one unaddressed flaw — Claude Opus 4.6 is the sole trajectory judge AND tops the leaderboard by 14+ points, with no secondary judge, no human-agreement study, and no acknowledgment of the conflict — compounded by fixed-by-assertion score weights and unvalidated auto-generated LPA chains. The self-judging problem makes the headline ranking suggestive rather than definitive. 0.15*70+0.15*55+0.10*68+0.15*55+0.15*52+0.30*72 = 63.
systems Novelty 70 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 68 Completeness 55 Resilience 52 Impact 72
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Autonomous AI agents that can read email, browse the web, execute shell commands, manage files, and conduct financial transactions introduce a qualitatively different security threat than conversational LLMs. An attacker no longer needs to elicit harmful text; they can implant malicious instructions in webpages or email bodies (indirect injection), pre-poison the agent's persistent memory across sessions, or compromise third-party skills/plugins the agent trusts. Existing agent-security benchmarks have three structural gaps: fragmented risk coverage (each benchmark covers only one or two attack vectors in isolation), low-fidelity environments (mocked tool responses or static Q&A that cannot reproduce stateful, multi-step harm), and coarse-grained evaluation (binary attack-success labels that conflate an agent that refuses correctly, one that stumbles into safety by over-refusing, and one that avoids harm without understanding why). No prior work unifies diverse entry points, real executable environments, and decomposed scoring into one framework. Key Idea: The core insight is to factorize the risk space as Entry × Impact — separating how adversarial influence reaches the agent from what harm it produces — and then evaluate the full agent execution trajectory, not the final reply, across three orthogonal scores. This factorization makes coverage auditable and enables diagnosis: after a failure, the entry axis identifies what the agent failed to handle, while the impact axis identifies what environmental consequence occurred. The trajectory-level view lets the framework detect "silent obedience" — an agent that produces safe outcomes without ever recognizing the attack — which a UOR-only metric would treat as fully solved. Methodology: AgentCanary is organized in three layers. First, the risk matrix: 5 risk entries (Direct Prompt Injection, Indirect Prompt Injection, Skill Poisoning, Memory Contamination, Intrinsic Failures) × 7 impact categories (Local Environment, Data Security, Persistent State Contamination, Privilege/System Control, Network/Remote Control, Business Abuse, Financial/Transactional) yield a 35-cell design space from which 496 seed evaluation tasks are instantiated. Each task is formally specified as a tuple tying a user prompt, executable workspace, risk entry condition, target impact, and expected safe behavior. Second, the environment: tasks execute in Docker containers provisioned by an automated artifact engine that materializes inboxes, webpages, bank accounts, memory stores, calendar entries, and poisoned skills before the agent runs. Persistent state is maintained within a task but isolated across tasks. Three agent frameworks (OpenClaw, Hermes, NanoClaw) are supported via a framework-agnostic interface. Third, evaluation: a trajectory Ψ is defined as the ordered sequence (user query, tool invocations, tool returns, agent replies, system-level side effects). Three rubric-based LLM evaluators score Outcome Safety Score (OSS), Security Awareness Score (SAS), and Task Utility Score (TUS), each in [0,1]. These are aggregated as a weighted sum with task-family-specific weights: DPI uses (α=0.7, β=0.3, γ=0), IPI/MC/SP use (α=0.6, β=0.2, γ=0.2), IF uses (α=1, β=0, γ=0). The judge model is Claude Opus 4.6. Attack-strengthening variants include Dynamic Attack Evolution (iterative LLM-driven refinement of DPI prompts), Long-Horizon Progressive Attack (plant-then-trigger cross-session chains: 31 expert-authored + 46 auto-generated), and script-based camouflage for skill poisoning (malicious logic moved from manifest into invoked scripts). Critical design decisions: (1) Real tool execution over mocked APIs — well-justified by the argument that multi-step failure modes and payload carrier sensitivity cannot be reproduced otherwise. (2) Per-task Docker isolation with within-task state persistence — well-justified for cross-task independence; evaluation of LPA necessarily requires multi-session state within a single task instance. (3) Fixed score weights — not ablated; the choice of α=0.6 dominance for OSS is asserted rather than derived. Key Results: Evaluated on the OpenClaw reference framework, Claude Opus 4.6 achieves the highest overall score (83.9), OSS (88.1), SAS (74.8), and lowest UOR (12.9%), followed distantly by Qwen3.6-Plus (69.7) and GPT-5.4 (68.5). GLM-4.7-Flash is weakest (37.9 overall, 61.7% UOR, 9.8% SAR). Risk severity varies dramatically by attack surface: mean UOR across models is 9% for IPI with task context (largely contained), 35% for DPI, 42% for memory contamination, 51% for long-horizon progressive attacks, and 62% for camouflaged skill poisoning. Script-based camouflage induces catastrophic collapses: Claude Sonnet 4.5 drops from 84.4 to 31.6 overall; Kimi-K2.5 UOR rises from 29.4% to 82.4%; Qwen3.5-397B from 13.7% to 76.5%. Dynamic attack evolution reduces most models' scores (DeepSeek-V4-Pro overall falls from 74.0 to 47.7), but paradoxically improves both Claude models (Opus 4.6 rises from 73.7 to 79.7; Sonnet 4.5 from 67.4 to 80.8). Safety and utility are strongly correlated (r=0.94), refuting the assumption that safety requires utility-destroying over-refusal. Agent framework choice shifts per-model scores by up to 15.2 points with no framework systematically dominant. Runtime defenses add 2–8 points but occasionally regress (SecureClaw lowers MiniMax-M2.5 from 47.6 to 44.0). Four independent runs show standard deviations of 2–8 points on most metrics, confirming that cross-model differences are not judge-side artifacts. The paper is a preprint; results have not been peer-reviewed. Novelty Classification: NEW BENCHMARK / NEW SYSTEM / NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The Entry × Impact taxonomy and its instantiation as a real-execution benchmark is a genuine structural contribution over prior flat attack lists. The three-dimensional scoring separating OSS, SAS, and TUS is novel relative to attack-success-rate-only benchmarks. The silent-obedience empirical finding — systematically quantified across IPI conditions — is a new and operationally important observation. The executable environment architecture (Docker-isolated, dynamically provisioned, framework-agnostic) advances beyond prior mocked-API benchmarks. What is not novel: individual attack types (DPI, IPI, memory poisoning, skill injection) are all documented in prior work; the framework combines and unifies them rather than introducing new attack primitives. Rigor & Reproducibility: The evaluation covers 12 models, 3 frameworks, 3 runtime defenses, 5 risk entries, and multiple attack strengthening strategies — breadth is a genuine strength. Standard deviations over 4 runs are reported for 2 models across 5 settings; cross-model and cross-setting differences substantially exceed judge variance on most metrics. Code is open-sourced at https://github.com/antgroup/Agent3Sigma-Canary. However, the benchmark has a serious methodological concern: Claude Opus 4.6 is used as the sole LLM judge for all trajectory scoring, and Claude Opus 4.6 also scores highest on the benchmark by a substantial margin (14+ points above the next model). The paper does not report human validation of judge agreement, does not test an alternative judge, and does not acknowledge this conflict of interest. The score weights (α, β, γ) are fixed by assertion; no ablation shows their sensitivity. The 46 automatically generated LPA chains are produced by an attack agent from the DPI set, but no validation of chain quality or difficulty relative to the 31 expert-curated chains is provided. Skill poisoning tasks use real malicious skills from Zast AI's Skill Security Reviewer and HarmfulSkillBench, which is a strength for ecological validity. Main body results use only the OpenClaw framework; Hermes and NanoClaw are covered in a cross-framework section with 8 open-weight models and 4 risk entries, not the full battery. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS (a built benchmark harness evaluated by deployment across agent frameworks and models). - Practical: Runs on commodity Linux servers via Docker; the three evaluated agent frameworks (OpenClaw, Hermes, NanoClaw) are open-source and the harness exposes a framework-agnostic interface, so adding a new framework requires only a launch adapter. - Efficient: 496 tasks × 12 models × multiple attack variants represents a large but tractable evaluation burden; Docker isolation adds per-task container startup overhead not quantified in the paper, and LLM judge calls multiply total API cost. - Effective: The benchmark reveals meaningful discrimination across models (83.9 vs. 37.9 overall score range), across attack surfaces (9%–62% mean UOR), and across camouflage conditions; the silent-obedience finding shows the evaluation captures phenomena invisible to single-score metrics. - Scalable: The automated provisioning engine and Docker isolation support parallel task execution and cross-run reproducibility; the paper does not report wall-clock evaluation times or resource requirements, and extension to new domains requires authoring new task tuples and provisioning logic. The framework delivers a software artifact (open-source benchmark harness), not just a paper. Strengths: - The Entry × Impact factorization is a conceptually clean contribution that makes risk coverage auditable and supports post-hoc diagnosis rather than just counting attack successes. - The silent-obedience finding — quantified as OSS 22–58 points above SAS across IPI conditions — is empirically sharp and has direct implications: agents that appear safe under one injection template may be brittle to minor payload variation. - Cross-framework evaluation revealing 15-point per-model swings with no framework dominant is a practically important result that prior work ignores; the framework is the first to make this comparison tractable at scale. - Script-based camouflage as an attack variant is a well-motivated and well-executed design: moving malicious logic from the skill manifest into invoked scripts reflects realistic supply-chain attacks and triggers dramatic, reproducible collapses in model safety (>50-point drops for several models). Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors do not acknowledge the Claude Opus 4.6 judging conflict. The paper does not claim completeness of the 35-cell risk matrix — the introduction states it serves as a "structured design space" with some cells not instantiated. Coverage of the IF (intrinsic failures) entry is acknowledged as intentionally limited to boundary-condition stress testing rather than enumeration of all failure modes. The conclusion briefly notes that "security challenges of current agents remain far from fundamentally resolved" but does not itemize benchmark-specific limitations. The authors note that per-risk-entry main results use only the OpenClaw framework for practical reasons. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - Judge conflict of interest: Claude Opus 4.6 both tops the leaderboard and serves as the sole judge for all 12 models. Without a secondary judge comparison or human gold standard, the 14-point gap between Opus 4.6 and the next model cannot be fully trusted. This is the most significant methodological gap in the paper. - Score weight sensitivity: The (0.6, 0.2, 0.2) weights for OSS/SAS/TUS are stated without justification and not ablated; different weight choices would change model rankings, particularly for models with high OSS but low SAS (e.g., Claude Sonnet 4.5 on IPI). - LPA chain quality gap: The 46 automatically generated long-horizon attack chains are produced by an attack agent but no difficulty parity or quality validation relative to the 31 expert chains is provided; the combined LPA results aggregate both without separation. - Framework coverage asymmetry: Hermes and NanoClaw are evaluated on only 8 models and 4 risk entries (no IF, no DAE, no LPA, no runtime defenses), so the cross-framework finding is narrower than the headline suggests. - Missing human agreement study: LLM-as-judge methods for security evaluation require validation that the judge agrees with human experts, especially on nuanced trajectory reasoning; the paper reports only run-to-run variance, not judge calibration against human labels.
