All three are well-justified, though the UCB exploration coefficient c=1.0 is not ablated. Key Results On ExpSuite-Static (10 benchmarks; ARC-C, CommonsenseQA, GPQA, MMLU, OBQA, GSM8K, GSM-Symbolic, MATH, HumanEval+, MBPP+), ExpGraph with Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct achieves average accuracy 69.57 vs. next-best MemRL 62.00 (+12.2%); with Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct it achieves 78.75 vs. MemRL 75.20 (+4.7%). On ExpSuite-Agentic (ALFWorld Seen/Unseen, AppWorld Test-N/Test-C), with Qwen3-32B as small executor: weighted average score 0.534 vs. best baseline S3 0.440 (+21.4%), average steps 14.4 vs. S3 16.5 (−12.7%); with Gemini-3.1-Flash-Lite as large executor: score 0.623 vs. S3 0.553 (+12.7%), steps 15.2 vs. MemRL 19.5 (−21.6%). Baselines include 11 methods spanning retrieval-centric (ReasoningBank, ExpeL, LightMem, Mem0, AWM, MemRL), LLM-centric (IRCoT, Search-o1, S3), and prompt-based (ReAct, Reflexion) categories, all given the same number of historical experiences for fair comparison. These are reasonably current baselines. A surprising finding: gains are larger on the smaller executor (+21.4% agentic vs. +12.7% for large), suggesting external experience is more valuable when the executor has limited built-in reasoning capacity. Cross-executor transfer experiments show that Graph+Copilot transfer consistently outperforms graph-only or copilot-only transfer, supporting the claim that both components capture complementary information. Ablation on five domains confirms that Flat Experience (no graph structure) produces the largest drop, followed by removing graph diffusion, then utility ranking. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM. The combination of executor-agnostic frozen-LLM design, a self-evolving graph-structured experience memory with online utility tracking, a PPO-trained copilot that outputs diffusion control parameters, and a utility-grounded marginal-gain reward is novel as a unified framework. Individual building blocks—personalized PageRank, UCB exploration, PPO—are well-established; the novelty lies in integrating them to decouple experience learning from executor fine-tuning. The experience-as-graph (rather than flat pool) formulation with adaptive diffusion is a genuine technical contribution beyond applying known retrieval ideas to a new setting. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present for all four key components with consistent evaluation across five domains; this is a strength. However, no error bars, confidence intervals, or significance tests are reported anywhere in the paper, including on test sets as small as 39 instances (GPQA: 39 test, HumanEval+: 39 test), where a single-sample difference equals a 2.56% accuracy swing. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2605.30712v1); results have not undergone peer review. Code is promised at https://github.com/ulab-uiuc/ExpGraph but is not yet released. Infrastructure is documented (4× A6000 48GB, Contriever embeddings, Qwen2.5-3B copilot, 20–40 GPU-hours per training run). The cold-start initialization procedure—running the executor on training tasks without experience to seed the initial graph—is described but not characterized in terms of how many cold-start samples are required or how it affects baseline comparisons. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL - Practical: The copilot trains on 4× NVIDIA A6000 GPUs (48GB each, commercially available but high-end); inference requires only the lightweight copilot + embedding server, with executors accessed via commercial APIs (NVIDIA NIM, Google Gemini), making inference costs largely API-determined and deployable without specialized hardware. - Efficient: The 2,000-node graph cap and cosine-similarity seeding keep retrieval fast at tested scale; copilot inference adds minimal overhead beyond an API call per task. Training (20–40 GPU-hours per task domain) is a one-time cost but non-trivial for practitioners without access to multi-GPU clusters. - Effective: Consistent improvements across 14 benchmarks and 4 executor models, with gains grounded directly in task performance rather than proxy metrics; the marginal-gain reward explicitly measures whether retrieved experiences are helpful rather than merely relevant. - Scalable: Untested beyond 2,000 experience nodes; no experiment at 10× larger experience pools or longer time horizons. The fixed capacity cap with eviction is a hard engineering boundary, not a principled scaling strategy; the authors list longer-horizon applications (web browsing, scientific discovery) explicitly as future work rather than evaluated claims. Delivers: a software framework with a trained copilot (code promised, weights not released), not a dataset or trained executor model. Strengths - The executor-agnostic design—treating the LLM as a frozen, replaceable black box and learning only through input context—directly addresses a real operational pain point: maintaining experience learning across model generations without repeated fine-tuning. - The utility-grounded marginal reward (s_with − s_without) is a principled signal that measures whether retrieved experiences actually help the executor, avoiding the failure mode of retrieving plausible-but-harmful context. - Cross-executor transfer experiments are thorough and directly validate the main claim: Graph+Copilot transfer approaches target-specific ExpGraph across three transfer directions (small-to-large, large-to-small, non-reasoning-to-reasoning), demonstrating that the learned components are not executor-specific artifacts. - The ablation design isolates each component cleanly, and the finding that flat experience memory (no graph structure) produces the largest drop is a concrete, quantified argument for the graph design choice. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge: (1) evaluation is restricted to reasoning benchmarks and two agentic environments; longer-horizon real-world tasks (web browsing, scientific discovery, collaborative multi-agent workflows) are left for future work; (2) fixed hyperparameters for graph construction (K_nn=5, θ=0.3, capacity=2,000, β=0.1) may not be robust across domains with different memory distributions—adaptive strategies are identified as an open problem; (3) prompt-level experience injection may translate inconsistently into executor behavior, and tighter coupling via experience-conditioned fine-tuning or distillation is noted as a future direction. The authors confront these limitations directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Several test sets are too small for reliable comparison without significance testing: GPQA (39 samples), HumanEval+ (39 samples), and MBPP+ (80 samples). The absence of any error bars is a serious omission given that claimed improvements on these sets (e.g., GPQA: 27.00 vs. 28.33) represent fewer than two samples' difference. - The labeling of Qwen3-32B as the "Small LLM" in agentic experiments while Llama-3.1-8B is the "Large LLM" in static experiments is inconsistent by parameter count, making cross-setting comparisons misleading; the paper does not address this. - The 2,000-node graph capacity is treated as a hyperparameter but is never ablated or compared to larger/smaller caps; it is unknown whether performance degrades or improves as the experience pool grows toward or beyond this limit. - The copilot model (Qwen2.5-3B) is fixed without ablation over alternatives; it is unclear whether the results reflect the retrieval policy design or simply that a reasoning-capable 3B copilot outperforms the implicit uniform retrieval in baselines. - The cold-start initialization procedure (seeding the graph by running the executor without experience on a subset of training tasks) is described but not quantified in terms of how many samples are needed or whether baselines receive equivalent warm-up; this could advantage ExpGraph in early training. - The step-efficiency comparisons cite different baselines for small vs. large executors (S3 for small, MemRL for large), making the −12.7% and −21.6% figures not directly comparable and potentially cherry-picked. Bottom Line ExpGraph offers a practically motivated and technically sound contribution to executor-agnostic experience learning, with the utility-grounded reward and graph diffusion being its most defensible novel elements—researchers building LLM agent memory systems should read it, but should not trust the quantitative margins on small test sets until error bars and a public code release are available.
Recommended 2605.30712 PDF
#108 — Innovative (61)

ARM: An AutoRegressive Large Multimodal Model with Unified Discrete Representations (17 ▲ )

Junke Wang, Xiao Wang, Jiacheng Pan, Xuefeng Hu, Feng Li, Jingxiang Sun, Chaorui Deng, Zilong Chen, Yunpeng Chen, Kaibin Tian, Matthew Gwilliam, Hao Chen, Danhui Guan, Kun Xu, Weilin Huang, Zuxuan Wu, Haoqi Fan, Yu-Gang Jiang, Zhenheng Yang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 10 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces ARM, a 7B autoregressive large multimodal model built on a single unified discrete visual tokenizer that handles image understanding, text-to-image generation, and instruction-guided editing within one next-token prediction framework. - Why it's important: Existing unified multimodal systems either use two separate visual encoders (semantic + reconstruction-oriented), incurring dual-representation overhead, or sacrifice understanding quality to support generation — ARM attempts to eliminate this tradeoff with a single shared discrete vocabulary. - How it does it: A Finite Scalar Quantization (FSQ) tokenizer is trained with four complementary losses — captioning, pixel reconstruction, sigmoid contrastive, and feature distillation — to simultaneously preserve language-aligned semantics and pixel-level appearance details, then GRPO reinforcement learning with GPT-o3/GPT-4.1 reward models aligns visual token prediction for generation and editing. - The good: The cross-task RL synergy finding is non-obvious and well-supported: optimizing only text-to-image RL improves editing scores (GEdit G_O from 5.75 to 5.92) and vice versa, while understanding benchmarks remain stable across all RL stages. - The missing: GEdit-Bench-EN evaluation uses GPT-4.1 as the judge while editing RL training uses GPT-4.1 as the reward model — this circular dependency is not acknowledged, making the editing performance gains difficult to interpret independently. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on unified multimodal architectures or GRPO-style preference alignment for visual generation; less essential for those focused on understanding-only VLMs, where specialist models still outperform ARM by a wide margin.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Completeness (50) is the weakest dimension — the paper acknowledges essentially no limitations, omitting inference latency, RL reward cost, the understanding gap versus specialists, and the FLUX decoder dependency. Rigor (58) is reduced by the reward-evaluator circularity (GPT-4.1 is both editing RL reward and GEdit judge), no error bars, internal training data, and proprietary RL infrastructure that cannot be replicated. Non-Obviousness (72) is the highlight: the cross-task RL synergy (T2I RL improving editing and vice versa without degrading understanding) is a genuinely novel and well-ablated finding suggesting shared latent structure. Novelty (68) reflects the four-loss unified tokenizer plus that finding. Resilience (55) and Impact (60) are moderate — code is linked but the 18-point MMMU gap to same-scale specialists and the uncounted ~12B decoder weaken the standing.
empirical Novelty 68 Rigor 58 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 50 Resilience 55 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Unified multimodal models that jointly handle image understanding, generation, and editing face a fundamental representation mismatch. Semantic visual encoders (CLIP, SigLIP) preserve high-level semantics for recognition but discard fine-grained texture and structure needed for pixel-accurate generation and editing. Reconstruction-oriented encoders (VQ-VAE, VAE) do the reverse. Most competitive unified systems resolve this by using two separate visual pathways (Janus-Pro, Bagel, Mogao), which doubles context overhead at inference and creates structural fragmentation. The few systems that attempt true unification (Chameleon, Emu3) sacrifice understanding quality to enable generation. This tradeoff is most acute for instruction-guided image editing, which simultaneously requires semantic grounding to interpret instructions and pixel-level fidelity to preserve non-edited content. Key Idea: A discrete visual tokenizer can simultaneously serve recognition and generation if its quantized code is trained with complementary supervision that explicitly pressures it to encode both language-aligned semantics and pixel-level appearance. Rather than accepting the semantic-fidelity tradeoff as fundamental, the key trick is to compose four distinct loss signals — captioning (semantic), pixel reconstruction (appearance), contrastive alignment (semantic regularization), and feature distillation (structure preservation) — so that the quantized vocabulary is shaped by all four simultaneously rather than optimized for a single objective. Methodology: The system has three main components. First, the unified discrete visual tokenizer: a frozen SigLIP2-SO400M-512 encoder produces semantic features; a 6-layer attention projection maps to a compact latent; Finite Scalar Quantization (FSQ, L_i=2 for 1≤i≤16, yielding a 65K codebook) discretizes; a symmetric projection maps back. Four losses are combined as L_Tok = L_cap + 5·L_pix + 5·L_sig + L_feat, where L_cap is cross-entropy captioning via a frozen 0.5B Qwen2.5 LM, L_pix is a rectified-flow DiT decoder trained in pixel space (avoiding VAE bottleneck compression), L_sig is sigmoid contrastive alignment between quantized embeddings and SigLIP2 text embeddings, and L_feat is cosine-distance distillation from original SigLIP2 visual features. High-quality detokenization is handled separately by a FLUX.1[dev]-initialized latent DiT conditioned on the quantized embeddings. The tokenizer trains on 2.2B internal image-text pairs. Second, the autoregressive LMM: initialized from Qwen2.5-7B with an added linear layer for visual token prediction; trained over four stages (PT: 2.5T tokens on 200 GPUs, 100K steps; Continual Training: 2.5T tokens; SFT: 0.2B tokens; RL). Third, preference RL via GRPO: only visual token prediction is optimized; GPT-o3 provides rewards for T2I (object/attribute/spatial correctness) and GPT-4.1 for editing (instruction following, non-target preservation, visual quality); three sequential RL stages (T2I RL → Edit RL → Joint RL). Critical design decisions: (1) pixel-space rather than VAE-space reconstruction loss, claimed to avoid VAE lossy compression — plausible but not ablated against VAE-space reconstruction; (2) FSQ rather than VQ codebook — well-justified to avoid codebook collapse; (3) proprietary GPT models as both training reward and evaluation judge for editing — creates a circularity concern not addressed in the paper. Key Results: Understanding: ARM achieves 87.3 POPE, 40.2 MMMU, 1463 MMEPerc, 73.1 SeedBench, 80.7 MMB — substantially above prior discrete unified models (Emu3: 85.2 POPE, 31.6 MMMU; VILA-U: 85.8 POPE) but below the best continuous unified models (Bagel: 55.3 MMMU) and well below understanding specialists (Qwen2.5-VL-7B: 58.6 MMMU, InternVL2.5-8B: 56.0 MMMU). Generation (ARM-RL): GenEval 0.86 overall (color attribute 0.90, position 0.89), DPG 86.00 overall, WISE 0.56 — competitive with FLUX.1[dev] (0.66 GenEval, 0.50 WISE) and Bagel (0.82 GenEval, 0.52 WISE). Editing (ARM-RL): GEdit-Bench-EN G_O = 6.68, close to Step1X-Edit (6.70) and above BAGEL (6.52); perceptual quality G_PQ = 7.68 is highest in the comparison table. Tokenizer ablation (Table 7): removing both L_sig and L_feat collapses ImageNet zero-shot accuracy from 80.2 to 0.2 and degrades PSNR from 19.6 to 15.2, confirming semantic regularization is essential. RL synergy (Table 8): T2I RL alone improves GEdit G_O from 5.75→5.92; Edit RL alone improves GenEval from 0.79→0.80; joint RL achieves further gains without understanding degradation (POPE stable at 87.1–87.6 across all RL variants). Scale tested: 7B model, ~5T+ training tokens across pre-training and continued training. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The core novelty is the four-loss tokenizer training objective that simultaneously serves recognition and generation within a single discrete vocabulary, and the empirical discovery of cross-task RL synergy in unified discrete AR models. Individual components (FSQ, GRPO, SigLIP2, FLUX-based decoding) are existing; the novelty is in their specific composition and the observed emergent generalization of policy updates across tasks. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations for the tokenizer (Table 7) and RL recipes (Table 8) are present and well-structured. No error bars, confidence intervals, or multi-seed runs are reported for any benchmark. Code is linked at https://github.com/wdrink/ARM, but the 2.2B training image-text pairs and RL prompt sets are internal and not released, limiting replication. The RL training requires API access to GPT-o3 and GPT-4.1, which are proprietary and variable over time. The editing benchmark (GEdit-Bench) uses GPT-4.1 as the judge, which is also the editing RL reward model — this Goodhart's Law concern (the model optimizes the exact metric used to evaluate it) is not acknowledged or controlled for. WISE and GenEval evaluations are independent of the reward model and thus more credible. This is a preprint, not peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS. - Practical: The 7B AR backbone can run on commercially available GPU hardware, but end-to-end generation requires FLUX.1[dev] as the diffusion decoder — adding roughly 12B additional parameters — making the effective deployed system substantially larger than the reported model size. - Efficient: Detokenization requires 28 diffusion sampling steps, making per-image inference substantially slower than single-pass decoding; the three-stage RL training requires repeated GPT-o3/4.1 API calls, which are costly. The semantic tokenizer enables reducing or eliminating CFG in the AR stage, which partially offsets inference cost. - Effective: Evidence on GenEval (0.86), GEdit G_O (6.68), and understanding benchmarks (87.3 POPE) demonstrates competitive performance across all three task categories; however, editing evaluation suffers from the reward-evaluator circularity and understanding meaningfully lags specialist VLMs. - Scalable: Training used 200 GPUs for pre-training; no scaling experiments beyond 7B are reported, and it is unknown whether the discrete representation advantage holds or degrades at larger model scales. The work delivers a trained model system and links a code repository; internal training data and RL prompt datasets are not released. Strengths: - The four-loss tokenizer ablation is thorough and mechanistically informative: the semantic regularization losses (L_sig, L_feat) are shown to be essential, collapsing ImageNet ZS from 80.2 to ~0.2 when removed, which directly validates the design rationale rather than asserting it. - The cross-task RL synergy finding is the most intellectually novel result: that discrete visual token RL generalizes policy improvements across T2I and editing tasks — without interfering with understanding — suggests shared structure in the latent visual vocabulary that could inform future unified model design. - The CFG reduction analysis is clean and has a concrete practical implication: a semantic tokenizer already encodes strong conditioning signals, so the AR stage can operate without CFG, halving the number of forward passes in the AR component. - Evaluation spans three genuinely distinct task categories with a diverse set of strong baselines, giving a credible picture of capability tradeoffs rather than cherry-picked benchmarks. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors acknowledge almost no limitations explicitly. The conclusion states results "demonstrate the potential" of the approach — hedged framing — but no specific failure modes, scope boundaries, or known weaknesses are enumerated. The paper does not discuss inference latency, the cost of GPT-based RL rewards, training data dependency, or the understanding gap relative to specialists. This absence of explicit limitation acknowledgment is a credibility concern under rigorous review standards. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - Reward-evaluator circularity: GEdit-Bench scoring is performed by GPT-4.1, which is also the editing RL reward model; the paper presents no alternative evaluation (human studies, CLIP-based metrics) that is independent of the training signal, making the editing gains partially uninterpretable. - Understanding gap unexplained: ARM reaches 40.2 MMMU vs. 58.6 for a same-scale non-unified Qwen2.5-VL-7B; the paper does not investigate whether this 18-point gap comes from the discrete tokenizer, the data mixture heavily weighted toward T2I (70% in PT), or both — no ablation on data ratio or tokenizer type for understanding. - Decoder size not counted: The FLUX.1[dev] detokenization decoder (~12B parameters) is omitted from the "7B model" framing used throughout comparisons; competing diffusion-based models' decoder sizes are not reported either, making parameter-count comparisons misleading. - No scaling experiments: All results are at 7B only; whether the discrete approach continues closing the gap with continuous representations at larger scale is unknown. - Proprietary RL infrastructure: Reproducing the RL stage requires API access to GPT-o3 and GPT-4.1, and the internal RL prompt datasets are not released — the RL contribution cannot be independently replicated. Bottom Line: ARM is a competently executed system paper with a genuinely interesting empirical finding (cross-task RL synergy) and a well-ablated tokenizer design, but practitioners should discount the editing benchmark results due to reward-evaluator circularity, treat the "7B" parameter count as incomplete given the FLUX decoder dependency, and recognize that the discrete representation approach still carries a meaningful understanding performance tax relative to specialist VLMs.
Recommended 2606.11188 PDF
#109 — Innovative (61)

OmniGameArena: A Unified UE5 Benchmark for VLM Game Agents with Improvement Dynamics (16 ▲ )

Mingxian Lin, Shengju Qian, Yuqi Liu, Yi-Hua Huang, Yiyu Wang, Wei Huang, Yitang Li, Fan Zhang, Zeyu Hu, Lingting Zhu, Xin Wang, Xiaojuan Qi · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 9 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces OmniGameArena, a real-time benchmark of twelve newly built Unreal Engine 5 games spanning Solo, PvP, and Coop regimes, paired with the Improvement Dynamics Curve (IDC), an agentic-reflection harness that measures how VLM agents improve their own skill prompts over multiple rounds of self-reflection. - Why it's important: Existing VLM game benchmarks report only a single first-attempt score per agent, masking whether agents can improve through experience and whether learned skills transfer to novel variants — properties that matter for deployment. - How it does it: The IDC runs each agent for K=5 episodes per round under a current skill prompt, then a tool-using reflector LLM autonomously inspects trajectories (Explore → Diagnose → Validate → Distill) to rewrite the skill prompt for the next round, iterating for R=10 rounds with best-skill rollback when score drops sharply. - The good: The central empirical finding — that origin-task gain and held-out variant transfer are dissociated, with Opus 4.7 gaining +201% on origin but transferring negatively on every variant while GPT-5.5 gains only +130% but transfers positively on all three — is a concrete, surprising result that single-round leaderboards cannot expose. - The missing: IDC is run on only 2 of 12 games and 4 of 12 agents, limiting the generalizability of the transfer-vs-origin-gain dissociation to a very narrow experimental base. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building or evaluating VLM game agents who care about self-improvement and generalization; practitioners looking for a deployable benchmark will need to wait for broader IDC coverage and a public environment release.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (52) and Resilience (52) limit the score: N=5 episodes per cell with standard deviations often 30–50% of the mean and no significance tests make many leaderboard comparisons statistically uninformative, IDC ablations are entirely absent, and the central transfer-dissociation finding rests on only 2 of 12 games and 4 of 12 agents (1 game for the headline result). Novelty (70) is real — twelve custom UE5 games across Solo/PvP/Coop with contamination controls, plus the IDC harness and the dissociation finding. Non-Obviousness (72) reflects that origin-gain and transfer can be inversely related across model families, a surprising and actionable result. Completeness (60) is helped by clearly-stated limitations, but benchmark/code availability is unconfirmed, capping Impact (62).
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 52 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 60 Resilience 52 Impact 62
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Vision-language model agents are increasingly tested in interactive game environments, but existing game benchmarks (BALROG, LMGame-Bench, ORAK, VideoGameBench) share two structural weaknesses. First, they report only a cold-start single-attempt score, leaving the trajectory of improvement under repeated interaction invisible. Second, they focus almost entirely on single-agent Solo play, under-representing adversarial PvP and cooperative Coop regimes that require opponent modeling, role assignment, and fault recovery. Both gaps matter because deployment-readiness hinges on whether an agent can adapt through experience and whether it can coordinate with or compete against others. An additional concern is pre-training contamination: most existing benchmarks reuse commercial games that are heavily represented in training corpora, making scores hard to interpret as novel capability. Key Idea: The central technical insight is separating two observables that a single-round score conflates — origin-task performance trajectory (the per-round IDC curve) and held-out variant transfer (does the learned skill generalize?) — and showing these are empirically dissociated. The mechanism that enables this is a lightweight agentic-reflection harness in which the reflector LLM has genuine tool-use autonomy (deciding what to read and when to stop) within a small fixed tool surface (read, diagnose, judge, write), rather than executing a fixed-template script. Methodology: The pipeline has two layers. The per-episode loop exposes a Gym-like interface: at each step the harness delivers an RGB frame plus timestamp; the VLM agent emits a chunked keyboard-mouse action chunk; the engine advances; the harness maintains a sliding window of the last L observation-response pairs as bounded visual history. For VLM agents this is a prompt-based interface; for specialized game policies (NITROGEN, OPEN-P2P) the native interface is preserved. The outer IDC loop wraps this per-episode loop with three modules: (1) an experience acquisition module that runs K=5 episodes and computes mean round score Sr; (2) a reflection module with four autonomous stages — Explore (read-only sandboxed tool access to per-episode trajectories), Diagnose (submit explicit failure modes), Validate (propose updated skill, adjudicated by an independent LLM judge that rejects proposals containing map-memorization or diagnosis contradictions, with up to 5 retries), Distill (finalize skill and optionally edit the experience notebook); (3) a persistent module carrying a 2000-token experience notebook, a 1200-token validated skill prompt (mr), a best-skill cache (mr∗), and the score sequence. Best-skill rollback fires when Sr+1 < 0.5 × Sr∗. Design decision 1: single bounded skill prompt rather than a growing skill library — deliberately lightweight, justified by keeping comparisons interpretable. Design decision 2: same model as player and reflector — a stated limitation rather than a principled choice. Design decision 3: independent LLM judge for skill validation — well-motivated as a guard against skill drift and map memorization, though not ablated. Key Results: The cold-start leaderboard covers 12 VLM agents across 12 games with N=5 episodes per cell. No single commercial VLM dominates: GPT-5.5 leads 4 of 7 Solo games, Claude Opus 4.6 leads CueChase (0.840 vs. next 0.580), and Gemini 3.1 Pro leads MonsterShoot (0.710 vs. next 0.464). Newer is not monotonically better: Claude Opus 4.6 outperforms Opus 4.7 on 5 of 7 Solo games. Open-weight VLMs (Qwen3.5 MoE at 397B-A17B and 122B-A10B) score below 0.15 on every game and exactly 0.00 on several; specialized game policies (NITROGEN gamepad, OPEN-P2P) collapse to near zero on all but a handful. In Coop games, even the strongest model (GPT-5.5) reaches only 0.368 on SharedFloor and 0.184 on HandoffRun, leaving substantial headroom. For IDC (2 games, 4 agents, R=10 rounds): all four agents improve over cold-start on both games; best-round gains on LastStand range from +0.54 (GPT-5.5, +130%) to +0.70 (Gemini 3.1 Pro, +305%); peak is typically mid-curve rather than round 10. Transfer dissociation: on LastStand, Opus models gain +437% and +201% on origin but transfer negatively on VAR2 and VAR3 (–72%, –76%, –76%); GPT-5.5 gains only +130% on origin but is the only model with positive transfer on all 3 variants. SharedFloor transfers universally (16/16 positive), with +6% to +56% across all agents and variants. Latency-controlled real-time (LCRT) experiments show that score degradation on throughput tasks (SoloCraft, SharedFloor) is almost entirely an action-budget effect rather than per-action efficiency loss. Novelty Classification: NEW BENCHMARK/DATASET, NEW SYSTEM/TOOL, NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. What is genuinely novel: (1) the twelve custom-built UE5 games spanning all three interaction regimes (no commercial title reuse); (2) the IDC harness as a multi-round agentic-reflection measurement protocol with per-round scoring and variant transfer; (3) the empirical demonstration that origin-task gain and transfer are dissociated across model families. What is combination-of-existing-ideas: the reflection pipeline itself (Explore-Diagnose-Validate-Distill) is a straightforward structured elaboration of Reflexion/ExpeL/Self-Refine applied to game agents; the best-skill rollback is a standard early-stopping safeguard; the Gym-like interface is standard. Rigor & Reproducibility: N=5 episodes per (agent, game) cell is very small and produces high variance — standard deviations often exceed 30–50% of the mean (e.g., Claude Opus 4.6 on ObstacleRun2D: 0.338±0.003 and Gemini 3.1 Pro on MonsterShoot: 0.710±0.138). No significance tests or confidence intervals for between-model comparisons are reported. IDC ablations are absent: there is no comparison to a fixed-template reflector, no test of best-skill rollback benefit, and no ablation of the validate stage. The benchmark environment code and the IDC harness code are not stated to be released; the paper provides a project page URL but does not explicitly confirm public code or model availability. The contamination analysis is limited to visual novelty and rule leakage from screenshots, not from gameplay interaction. The IDC experiments cover only 2 of 12 games and 4 of 12 agents, so the central transfer-dissociation finding rests on a very narrow experimental base. This is a preprint (arXiv:2606.09826v1, June 2026); it has not undergone peer review. Deployability (4-property check): EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS profile — the paper builds a real-time evaluation environment and runs agents inside it. - Practical: The harness runs on commercially available hardware with standard GPU inference for open-weight VLMs; commercial VLMs are accessed via API. The UE5 environment requires a machine capable of running UE5 in real time, which is achievable on consumer-grade gaming hardware. - Efficient: IDC costs R×K agent episodes plus R reflector passes per (agent, game) pair; at R=10, K=5, this is 50 player episodes plus 10 reflection calls per pair, which is substantial for large VLMs at commercial API rates. The paper does not report wall-clock runtime or API cost estimates. - Effective: The benchmark exposes real capability differences (commercial vs. open-weight gap, leadership rotation across games, transfer dissociation) that single-round scores hide; the IDC curves show genuine behavioral improvement supported by skill inspection in Appendix C. - Scalable: IDC is tested at 2 games and 4 agents; scaling to all 12 games and 12 agents would require 12×12×(R×K) episodes, an order of magnitude more compute. This scaling is claimed as future work but not tested. The paper delivers a software system artifact (UE5 environment + harness) but availability is not confirmed. Strengths: - The three-regime design (Solo/PvP/Coop) within a single real-time environment with unified action interfaces is a genuine structural contribution: it allows heterogeneous agent classes (commercial VLMs, open-weight VLMs, specialized game policies) to be compared on the same footing, something no prior benchmark achieves. - The transfer-dissociation finding is a concrete, non-obvious empirical result: that models with large origin-task gains can transfer negatively while models with smaller gains transfer positively, attributable to inspectable differences in skill style (conservative movement-minimizing policies vs. adaptive reassessment loops), is actionable for benchmark design and model development. - The contamination analysis, though narrow, is more principled than the field norm: explicitly testing screenshot recognition and mechanics leakage rates, and reporting 0% recognition for OmniGameArena vs. 66–100% for competitors, provides empirical support for the novelty claim rather than relying on asserted novelty. - The LCRT vs. PDQ protocol decomposition cleanly separates decision-quality from inference-latency effects and provides a principled explanation (action-budget depletion) for performance drops on throughput tasks. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly state: (1) IDC scope is narrow — only 2 environments and 4 agents due to compute constraints; (2) the single-skill format is a deliberate simplification, not a complete treatment of skill accumulation; (3) using the same model for player and reflector is untested relative to asymmetric setups. These limitations are stated clearly and directly, which is a credibility positive, though the IDC scope limitation is substantial given that the central finding rests on it. Analyst-Identified Gaps: N=5 episodes per cell with no significance testing makes many between-model comparisons on the cold-start leaderboard statistically uninformative given the observed variance. The contamination analysis uses a single representative model (Gemini) to assess recognition and mechanics leakage, not the full set of evaluated models; a model that learns game rules from one screenshot interaction might still be contaminated in ways this test misses. PvP evaluation (Figure 4) includes only 5 commercial VLMs, excluding open-weight models and game policies without explanation; it is unclear whether this is a scope limitation or a deliberate choice. The validate stage uses an independent LLM judge but the judge model is not named and no ablation shows its benefit over unconstrained skill updates. The dissociation between origin gain and transfer transfer (the paper's most important finding) is observed across 4 agents on 1 game (LastStand); 3 of 4 variants on SharedFloor transfer positively for all agents, giving essentially no discriminating signal on that game. Bottom Line: OmniGameArena delivers the most structurally complete VLM game benchmark to date — three regimes, unified interfaces, contamination controls — and the IDC transfer-dissociation finding is the kind of concrete, surprising empirical result that should reshape how the field measures VLM self-improvement; the main limitation is that both the benchmark infrastructure availability and the IDC experimental coverage are too narrow to act on without the authors' follow-up.
Recommended 2606.09826 PDF
#110 — Innovative (61)

An algebraic multiscale preconditioner for large sparse SPD matrices

Y Zhou, S Fu, ETS Chung · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a two-level algebraic multiscale preconditioner for large sparse symmetric positive definite systems from elliptic PDEs with highly heterogeneous coefficients, constructed entirely from the system matrix via METIS graph partitioning and local generalized eigenvalue problems. - Why it's important: Standard algebraic multigrid (AMG) iteration counts blow up under high coefficient contrast (e.g., from 78 to 474 at DoF=256³ as contrast rises from 10³ to 10⁵), making it unusable for realistic subsurface flow and composite-material simulations without an alternative coarsening strategy. - How it does it: Non-overlapping subdomains are defined algebraically by graph partitioning; within each subdomain, a modified local matrix with algebraically-imposed Neumann boundary conditions is assembled and its smallest eigenvectors (relative to a diagonal mass matrix) serve as coarse basis functions that capture the low-energy modes responsible for slow convergence. - The good: At DoF=256³ with contrast 10⁵, iteration count stays at 54 versus 474 for PCGAMG, reducing total wall time from 6.2s to 1.6s — a 74% reduction — and iteration count remains essentially flat across all tested contrasts at that problem size. - The missing: Only PCGAMG is compared against; the closely related parallelized multiscale preconditioners from the same author group ([7,8]) are cited but never benchmarked, leaving the incremental contribution over prior work unquantified. - Read it or not: Worth reading for computational scientists solving large-scale heterogeneous elliptic systems on HPC clusters who need a geometry-free, matrix-only preconditioner with rigorous convergence theory and open-source code; those already familiar with [7,8] from the same group should check whether the differences are material.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (64) is the strongest dimension — a formal convergence bound (Theorem 4) tying error to the smallest excluded local eigenvalue, plus a GitHub release — but undercut because the constant C is left uncomputed (non-predictive bound), only one baseline (PCGAMG) is tested, all problems are synthetic with no SPE10 benchmark, and a broken 'Section ??' cross-reference confirms a rough, pre-submission preprint. Novelty (62) and Non-Obviousness (62) are solid: the GHEP→HEP conversion is a clean non-obvious 4–5× setup speedup, but the spectral-coarse-space idea is established and the incremental value over the same group's [7,8] is unbenchmarked. Completeness (60) and Resilience (56) are limited by honest-but-unresolved degradation at 512³/10⁵ and the missing comparison to prior work. Weighted: 0.20*62+0.25*64+0.15*62+0.15*60+0.15*56+0.10*60 = 61.
empirical Novelty 62 Rigor 64 Non-Obv 62 Completeness 60 Resilience 56 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Solving large sparse SPD linear systems arising from FEM/FVM/FD discretizations of elliptic PDEs — particularly Darcy flow, heat conduction, and reservoir simulation — with highly heterogeneous, high-contrast permeability or diffusion coefficients (spanning several orders of magnitude across the domain). Krylov solvers require effective preconditioners because raw condition numbers grow with contrast. Geometric multigrid requires mesh hierarchies unavailable in matrix-only or unstructured settings. Standard AMG coarsens based on algebraic connectivity, which does not align with the physical multiscale structure under high contrast, causing iteration counts to degrade catastrophically: for a DoF=256³ problem, PCGAMG requires 474 iterations at contrast 10⁵ versus 78 at contrast 10³. The gap to fill is a preconditioner that (a) requires no geometric information, (b) is provably robust to coefficient contrast, and (c) is naturally parallelizable at HPC scale. Key Idea: Algebraically impose homogeneous Neumann boundary conditions on each local subdomain problem by modifying the extracted submatrix's diagonal so that all row sums are zero, making the local matrix Â_i symmetric positive semi-definite. This algebraic Neumann treatment allows local generalized eigenvalue problems Â_i ψ = λ S_i ψ (where S_i is the diagonal of Â_i) to capture genuine low-energy modes of the operator without contamination by artificial Dirichlet boundary effects. The smallest eigenvectors of these purely algebraic local problems, zero-extended to the global space, form a coarse basis that provably controls the convergence of the two-level iteration. Methodology: The preconditioner P⁻¹ = R₀ᵀ A₀† R₀ is constructed in four stages. (1) Graph partitioning: METIS decomposes the sparsity graph of A into N = proc × sd non-overlapping subdomains {Ω_i}, one sd-block per MPI process. (2) Local eigenproblems: for each Ω_i, extract A_i = R_i A R_iᵀ, form Â_i by zeroing row sums via diagonal adjustment, define diagonal S_i from diag(Â_i), then solve Â_i ψ_k = λ_k S_i ψ_k using SLEPc. A critical optimization converts this to the standard symmetric EVP S_i^{-1/2} Â_i S_i^{-1/2} z = λz via Cholesky factorization of S_i (diagonal, so trivially inverted), yielding 4–5× speedup (Table 4: 13.0s GHEP vs 3.2s HEP at subdomain size 40³). (3) Coarse space: the first L* eigenvectors per subdomain, zero-extended globally, assemble the prolongation operator R₀ᵀ; the coarse matrix A₀ = R₀ A R₀ᵀ is solved by SUPERLU_DIST. (4) Fine-scale: GMRES with this preconditioner, tolerating relative residual 10⁻⁶. Convergence analysis (Theorem 4) shows ‖E‖²_A ≤ 1 − μ_c/C, where μ_c is the smallest excluded local eigenvalue across all subdomains and C depends on the smoother's largest eigenvalue. Three design decisions: (a) algebraic Neumann BCs — well-justified as they isolate interior low-energy modes; (b) diagonal S_i — enables the GHEP→HEP conversion without changing spectrum, justified empirically by 4–5× speedup; (c) non-overlapping partition — essential for the Lemma 2 orthogonality argument but limits the coarse space's ability to capture cross-subdomain modes. Key Results: All experiments solve a 3D steady-state diffusion problem on [0,1]³ with heterogeneous permeability (long-channel and fracture geometries) using RT₀ mixed FEM, on an HPC cluster (56-core Intel Xeon Gold 6258R nodes, Infiniband). Baseline: PCGAMG (PETSc's AMG). At DoF=128³ (560 MPI processes): proposed method 43–45 iterations across contrasts 10³–10⁵, total time 0.4–0.7s; PCGAMG 63–293 iterations, 0.6–1.4s. At DoF=256³: 51–54 vs 78–474 iterations, 1.3–1.8s vs 1.7–6.2s. At DoF=512³: 31–160 vs 60–470 iterations; at contrast 10³ PCGAMG is faster (10.4s vs 11.5s due to cheaper setup), but at 10⁴ the proposed method wins (15.6s vs 28.3s) and at 10⁵ wins (40.7s vs 61.3s). Strong scaling on DoF=512³: total time drops from 314.8s (27 processes) to 11.9s (512 processes), approximately 26× speedup for 19× core increase. Weak scaling (64³ DoF/process, 125–512 processes): elapsed time grows from 4.7s to 8.9s. Parameter study: sd=2³ with L=3–4 is the optimal tradeoff; more subdomains per process enlarges the coarse problem and makes SUPERLU_DIST the bottleneck (total time 2.3s vs 10.6s at L=5 for sd=2³ vs sd=4³). Notable counter-intuitive finding: at DoF=512³, contrast 10⁵, the proposed method degrades to 160 iterations — the authors acknowledge partial loss of contrast-independence at this scale with fixed parameters. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD. The algebraic translation of GMsFEM-style spectral coarse spaces into a fully matrix-only, MPI-parallelized two-level preconditioner framework is the contribution. The individual ingredients — spectral AMG, GMsFEM, algebraic multiscale solvers (see [11] Wang et al. 2014), and two-level Schwarz preconditioners — are all established. What is new relative to [7,8] from the same author group is the fully matrix-only formulation (no PDE information beyond the assembled matrix) and the GHEP→HEP conversion that materially reduces setup cost. Whether this constitutes a significant advance over [7,8] is unclear without a direct comparison. Rigor & Reproducibility: Theorem 4 gives a rigorous convergence bound with a formal proof via Lemmas 2–3 and the abstract framework of Xu–Zikatanov [12]. The constant C is not explicitly computed, making the bound non-quantitative for predicting iteration counts. No error bars, multiple seeds, or statistical tests are reported; each configuration appears to be run once. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/pentaery/Algebraic-2G. Key methodological concerns: (a) only one baseline (PCGAMG) is tested — related spectral AMG methods and the authors' own prior methods [7,8] are not compared; (b) all experiments use synthetic permeability fields on the unit cube, with no standard benchmark (e.g., SPE10) that would enable community comparison; (c) the paper contains an unresolved internal cross-reference ("Section ??") in the introduction's roadmap, confirming this is a rough preprint not yet in peer-review-ready form. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL/SYSTEMS. - Practical: Runs on standard MPI clusters using publicly available libraries (METIS, SLEPc, SUPERLU_DIST, MFEM); source code is available on GitHub; no specialized hardware required. - Efficient: Setup cost exceeds PCGAMG at low contrast and large scale (6.5s vs 2.9s at DoF=512³, contrast 10³), but the GHEP→HEP conversion reduces local eigensolver cost 4–5×; the coarse direct solve becomes the dominant bottleneck at large sd. - Effective: Iteration counts remain nearly flat under contrast 10³–10⁵ at DoF≤256³ and the method delivers 2–4× total-time reductions over PCGAMG in the high-contrast regime; partial degradation (160 iterations) appears at the 512³ scale with contrast 10⁵. - Scalable: Demonstrated on problems up to ~1.34×10⁸ DoF (512³) with up to 512 MPI processes; strong and weak scaling are good, but the coarse system solved by a direct solver will ultimately limit weak scalability at very large process counts — this regime is not tested. Delivers source code (GitHub repository). Strengths: - Rigorous convergence bound (Theorem 4) ties the error propagation operator directly to the smallest excluded local eigenvalue, giving a mathematically grounded explanation for why more eigenvectors per subdomain improve convergence. - Fully matrix-only construction enables deployment in black-box contexts (unstructured meshes, third-party assembled matrices) where geometric grid hierarchies are unavailable. - The GHEP→HEP conversion via diagonal scaling is a clean, non-obvious engineering decision that yields a concrete 4–5× speedup in the setup's dominant component (Table 4). - The authors honestly report the regime where their method loses (low contrast, large scale) rather than cherry-picking favorable comparisons. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors state that at DoF=512³ with contrast 10⁵, iteration count rises to 160, indicating "some loss of contrast-independence" and suggest "a richer coarse space may be beneficial for the most extreme case." They acknowledge that at low contrast, PCGAMG's cheaper setup makes it faster overall. They identify extending to multilevel variants, adaptive basis selection, and broader PDE classes as future work — meaning the current two-level scheme has no hierarchical coarsening, limiting its theoretical optimality claims. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The only baseline is PCGAMG. The papers [7] (SIAM J. Sci. Comput. 2024) and [8] (J. Comput. Phys. 2025), both by overlapping author sets and both addressing high-contrast Darcy preconditioners, are cited but never benchmarked against — making it impossible to assess the incremental contribution of this work over the group's recent publications. - All test problems are synthetic (long-channel and fracture permeability on the unit cube with Neumann BCs). The SPE10 benchmark, which is standard in this community, is absent; without it, claims of practical robustness cannot be externally validated. - The constant C in the convergence bound (Theorem 4) is left uncharacterized; without an explicit or computable expression, the theorem establishes existence of a bound but gives no guidance on parameter selection or predicted iteration counts. - The coarse system is solved by SUPERLU_DIST, a parallel sparse direct solver. For very large coarse problems (large sd or L*), this will saturate weak scalability. Figure 5 already shows the direct-solver cost becoming dominant at sd=3³–4³, but the scaling of the coarse solve itself is not analyzed separately. - A broken cross-reference ("Section ??") in the paper's introduction roadmap reveals the manuscript is not in submission-ready form; the actual numerical section is Section 5, suggesting the paper was incompletely assembled before posting. Bottom Line: A practitioner solving high-contrast heterogeneous elliptic systems on HPC clusters will find this a useful, open-source alternative to AMG with clear iteration-count advantages above contrast 10⁴ — but should read the same group's [7] and [8] first to understand what is actually new, and should not expect peer-reviewed validation from this preprint in its current state.
Search Results 2606.04864 PDF
#111 — Innovative (60)

Trust the Batch, Online or Offline: Adaptive Policy Optimization for Post-Training

R Fakoor, M Aubry, N Stranges, A Smola · openreview.net · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper proposes applying the P3O objective (Fakoor et al., UAI 2019) to large-language-model post-training, replacing fixed PPO/GRPO clip ranges with the normalized effective sample size (ESS) of per-token importance ratios as a batch-adaptive score-function cap and KL regularizer. - Why it's important: Fixed clip hyperparameters in GRPO/PPO must be retuned per task, model scale, and rollout infrastructure, and they catastrophically fail under practical off-policy mismatches such as BF16 training with FP8 rollout generation — a mismatch that causes GRPO to collapse to near-zero benchmark performance by iteration 30. - How it does it: ESS, computed as (Σρ)²/(|B|·Σρ²) over the batch of token ratios, simultaneously drives the score-function weight cap (min{ρ_t, e_B}) and the KL regularizer coefficient (1−e_B), so updates tighten automatically when data drifts and relax on fresh on-policy data — all from one statistic with no new hyperparameter. - The good: Under BF16 train + FP8 rollout, GRPO collapses to pass@1 ≈ 0.002 on AIME24 and 0.000 on AIME25 by iteration 30, while P3O retains 0.173 and 0.254 respectively on the same held-out benchmarks. - The missing: All experiments use models up to 8B parameters on a single task domain (mathematical reasoning); there is no evidence the method holds at 70B+ scale or for RLHF from human preferences, and DAPO and GSPO are not empirically compared despite being named alternatives. - Read it or not: ML engineers building distributed LLM RLHF pipelines with mixed-precision or asynchronous rollout engines should read this; for standard on-policy BF16 training, the gains over a well-tuned GRPO baseline are modest and not clearly significant.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (65) and Rigor (62) lead: reframing ESS as a single hyperparameter-free statistic that unifies trust-region and off-policy control is clean, and the BF16-train+FP8-rollout result (P3O 0.173/0.254 vs. GRPO 0.002/0.000 on AIME-24/25) is a decisive demonstration of a real failure mode. The composite is capped at the bottom of the Innovative band because the method is an adaptation of P3O (Fakoor 2019) to a new setting, evaluation is single-domain (math) at ≤8B with single-seed P3O runs, no code is released, and the named DAPO/GSPO alternatives are argued-against but never benchmarked — so the standard-setting advantage over a tuned GRPO is not clearly significant.
empirical Novelty 58 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 60 Resilience 60 Impact 50
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation RL post-training of large language models requires specifying a fixed clip range (ε_l, ε_h) in PPO or GRPO before training begins. This range controls both trust-region behavior (how far the policy can move per update) and off-policy correction (how much stale or mismatched data is down-weighted). In practice, the rollout and training engines almost never agree exactly: they may differ in numerical precision (BF16 vs FP8), sampling temperature, or model architecture, and rollouts are routinely reused across multiple optimizer epochs. There is no single clip value that is appropriate across these regimes — a value suited to fresh on-policy data over-clips stale data and vice versa — so practitioners retune the clip for each task, model size, and infrastructure configuration. This retuning cost scales with experiment expense and is especially acute for large models where each run is costly. Key Idea The normalized effective sample size (ESS) of the per-token importance ratios in the current training batch is a single, hyperparameter-free statistic that measures how off-policy the batch is: ESS ≈ 1 when ratios are uniform (on-policy data), and ESS falls toward 1/|B| when a few tokens dominate (stale or mismatched data). Using this statistic to set both the score-function weight cap and the KL regularizer coefficient — rather than fixing these as hyperparameters — allows the objective to behave like a standard score-function update on fresh data and tighten automatically as data drifts, without any retuning. Methodology The method casts autoregressive generation as a token-level MDP and adopts the GRPO group-relative advantage baseline (no value function; per-prompt reward statistics replace a learned critic). The surrogate objective is: L_P3O(θ) = E[Σ_t (−sg(min{ρ_t, e_B}) log π_θ(y_t|c_{<t}) A + (1−e_B) KL(π_θ ∥ π_b))] where ρ_t = π_θ/π_b is the per-token ratio, e_B = sg(ESS(B; θ)) is the stop-gradient ESS, and KL is toward the behavior policy. The ESS is all-reduced across distributed workers in one communication step (negligible overhead). Three design decisions: (1) stop-gradient on ESS treats it as a data property rather than a learnable quantity — well-justified to avoid circular optimization; (2) ESS is computed over tokens rather than sequences, matching the token-level objective — appropriate; (3) the KL regularizer targets the behavior policy π_b rather than the frozen reference policy π_θ0, which is a separate KL regularizer still present in the full objective — this distinction is not spelled out clearly in the paper. The authors also formulate a two-anchor extension (Eq. 14) that adds a proximal snapshot to disentangle within-epoch drift from behavior-policy drift, but this extension collapses during training at elevated rollout temperature and is relegated to the appendix. Key Results All experiments use Qwen3-4B-Thinking-2507 and Qwen2.5-1.5B (clip and temperature ablations) or Qwen3-8B-Base (FP8 ablation) on the DeepScaleR-Preview dataset (40K math problem-answer pairs). Benchmarks are AIME24/25/26, AMO-Bench, and AMC, all held out from training. Hardware: 8 NVIDIA H100 GPUs per run. Runs span 30–50 training steps. Clip sensitivity: GRPO reward varies substantially across ε ∈ {0.2, 0.4, 0.6} (visible spread in reward curves); P3O with no clip range matches or exceeds the best GRPO variant across both model families. Temperature off-policy (T=0.6 and T=1.2): GRPO degrades at both temperatures relative to the T=1.0 baseline; P3O matches or exceeds standard-temperature performance without retuning. BF16 train + FP8 rollout (16K tokens): GRPO pass@1 at iteration 30 — AIME24: 0.002, AIME25: 0.000, AMC: 0.029. P3O at iteration 30 — AIME24: 0.173, AIME25: 0.254, AMC: 0.529. This is the strongest result in the paper. Standard clip variants (4K tokens, Table 7): P3O achieves 0.165/0.183/0.160/0.010/0.493 vs GRPO average 0.176±0.039/0.160±0.123/0.126±0.087/0.012±0.007/0.381±0.195 — P3O is within or slightly above GRPO variance, not a decisive win in the standard setting. The sole baseline throughout is GRPO; DAPO and GSPO are described but not empirically evaluated. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD (adaptation). The P3O objective originates in Fakoor et al. (UAI 2019) for tabular/continuous-control RL; this paper is the first application to large-model post-training. The genuine novel contribution is the reframing of ESS as a unified, parameter-free solution to both trust-region and off-policy concerns in LLM RLHF, plus the empirical demonstration that practical engineering mismatches (FP8 quantization) are off-policy problems that ESS naturally handles. The GRPO group-relative advantage, token-level MDP, and KL regularization are all existing components assembled around the P3O objective. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations cover three off-policy sources (clip range sweep, sampling temperature, FP8 quantization) across two model families — adequate for the claims made. Error bars on P3O itself are absent; only GRPO's clip sweep yields variance estimates. The two-anchor extension is tested and reported as failing — a credibility positive. Training details are fully tabulated (Tables 2–6). No code, trained weights, or data are released (not mentioned). The paper is submitted to NeurIPS 2026 (preprint, not yet peer-reviewed). Benchmark evaluation uses held-out data (not in the training distribution), which is methodologically sound. The comparison against only GRPO — despite DAPO and GSPO being named alternatives — is a gap: the paper argues P3O subsumes these objectives but does not verify this empirically. Deployability (EMPIRICAL) - Practical: Runs on 8 H100s with a standard stack (DeepSpeed ZeRO-3, vLLM, Ray, FlashAttention 2); the ESS all-reduce adds no meaningful overhead over GRPO. - Efficient: Compute cost matches GRPO exactly — same rollout phase, same gradient step, one extra all-reduce per micro-batch for ESS. - Effective: Strong evidence of effectiveness under BF16+FP8 mismatch and temperature perturbations; marginal advantage in the standard on-policy setting where GRPO can be tuned. - Scalable: Largest tested model is 8B parameters; no evidence at 70B+ scale; runs span at most 50 training steps, which is far below full post-training curricula. The work delivers an algorithm (implemented in the authors' FeynRL framework) but no public software artifact is released. Strengths - The insight that ESS unifies trust-region control and off-policy correction into a single, parameter-free statistic is conceptually clean and directly addresses the stated fragility of fixed-clip objectives. - The BF16+FP8 experiment captures a real, widely encountered engineering constraint and shows GRPO's practical failure mode clearly — near-zero benchmark performance by iter 30 is a compelling result. - The authors honestly report that the two-anchor extension collapses under elevated rollout temperature (Appendix D), rather than cherry-picking favorable conditions. - Removing ε_l and ε_h without introducing any replacement hyperparameter is a genuine simplification with practical value for practitioners who run at scale. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The two-anchor extension destabilizes under high rollout temperature (T=1.2), collapsing to near-zero reward by step 24; the authors attribute this to gradient variance amplification when the proximal snapshot drifts far from the behavior policy, and defer fixes to future work. The paper frames itself as a starting point for batch-adaptive post-training methods rather than a complete solution. The authors note that P3O does not make arbitrary off-policy data reliable — meaningful token-level ratios and adequate support in the behavior data are still required. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Evaluation is confined to mathematical reasoning (DeepScaleR-Preview → AIME/AMC benchmarks); it is unknown whether ESS-based adaptation holds for RLHF from human preferences, instruction following, or code generation, where reward signal and completion length distributions differ substantially. - All models are ≤8B parameters; the claim of applicability to large-model post-training is not verified at the scale (70B+) where clip retuning is most expensive. - P3O is run with a single random seed in all figures; GRPO's variance is quantified only across clip values, not across seeds, making it impossible to assess whether differences in the standard setting are significant. - DAPO and GSPO are presented as conceptual alternatives in Table 1 but never benchmarked; the paper's claim that P3O is strictly simpler and at least as effective relative to these methods rests entirely on argument, not experiment. - The KL regularizer in Eq. (12) targets the behavior policy π_b, while the standard RLHF KL targets the reference policy π_θ0; the interaction between these two regularizers in practice is not analyzed. Bottom Line Practitioners running LLM RLHF with mixed-precision or asynchronous rollout pipelines gain a concrete, drop-in fix for a real failure mode (P3O vs GRPO under FP8 rollout is decisive); for standard on-policy BF16 training, the benefit over a tuned GRPO is small and the evidence at production scale is absent.
Search Results
#112 — Innovative (60)

MaxProof: Scaling Mathematical Proof with Generative-Verifier RL and Population-Level Test-Time Scaling (69 ▲ )

Jiacheng Chen, Xinyu Zhang, Shunkai Zhang, Yanmohan Wang, Lin Li, Tiancheng Qin, Qin Wang, Zhengmao Zhu, Tianle Li, Jingyang Li, Zehan Li, Binyang Jiang, Jin Zhu, Han Ding, Fei Yu, Chenyu Du, Zijian Song, Jiayuan Song, Zhi Zhang, Yunan Huang, Weiyu Cheng, Pengyu Zhao, Yu Cheng · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 12 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces MaxProof, a population-level test-time scaling framework that combines evolutionary search, verifier-guided refinement, and pairwise tournament selection to convert a base language model into a competition-level mathematical proof system, achieving 35/42 on IMO 2025 and 36/42 on USAMO 2026 with the MiniMax-M3 model. - Why it's important: Competition-level mathematical proof is among the hardest reasoning tasks for language models because there is no executable oracle, correctness requires a tightly coupled chain of valid steps, and reward hacking under generative verifiers is a previously undercharacterized failure mode that can silently plateau a long RL run. - How it does it: M3 is trained with three specialist capabilities — a Proof Expert via long-horizon GRPO-style RL under a four-layer defense-in-depth generative verifier, a Verifier Expert aligned to error localization rather than score regression, and a Fixer Expert trained by rejection-sampling fine-tune on critique-conditioned repair — then merged; MaxProof runs an evolution-inspired search over 32 candidates with up to 10 PATCH/REWRITE refinement rounds and a pairwise tournament final selection. - The good: The four-layer verifier design (bad-case filter → solution normalization → multi-judge parallel scoring → pessimistic min aggregation) is directly motivated by four documented reward-hacking failure modes from a prior M2 training run, giving the design rationale unusual empirical grounding. - The missing: No component-level ablations are reported: it is unknown how much each verifier layer, the std-threshold group filter, the Verifier Expert, or the Fixer Expert individually contributes; the entire gain over one-shot M3 is attributed jointly to MaxProof without decomposition. - Read it or not: Read it if you are working on inference-time compute scaling, reward hacking in generative RL, or competition-level reasoning; skip it if you need independent peer-reviewed evidence — this is a vendor preprint evaluated on 12 contest problems with self-reported judges.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (52) is the limiting dimension: the contest evaluation rests on only 12 problems with no error bars, no component ablations, self-reported/competitor-vendor judges, and no released code or weights — though the explicit decontamination and full output release in Appendix D are credibility positives. Novelty (70) and Non-Obviousness (70) are real — the defense-in-depth verifier optimizing for false-positive rate rather than benchmark accuracy is a principled reframing grounded in four documented M2 reward-hacking modes. Resilience (55) suffers because the 12-problem sample cannot separate the MaxProof framework's contribution from the base model's capability (no best@32-with-oracle baseline). Weighted with empirical profile gives ~60.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 52 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 60 Resilience 55 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Competition-level mathematical proof — specifically IMO/USAMO-style olympiad problems — requires constructing long, tightly coupled natural-language arguments with no executable ground truth. Standard RL approaches that work for code (unit-test feedback) or short-answer math do not apply because the reward must come from a generative verifier, which introduces noise, false positives, and reward hacking vulnerabilities. The prior M2 training cycle demonstrated that a single-judge rubric verifier leads to a reward-hacking plateau rather than genuine capability gains, motivating a redesign of both the verifier and the training objective. The paper also addresses the test-time challenge of converting a model with reasonable best@K capability into a reliable pass@1 system without committing prematurely to a single candidate under noisy verification. Key Idea: The central insight is that RL-time verifier design must optimize for minimum false-positive rate — not maximum accuracy on a static benchmark — because RL amplifies any high-scoring invalid proof into a training target. This is operationalized through pessimistic min-aggregation over multiple heterogeneous judges after bad-case filtering and surface normalization, making all four documented reward-hacking modes (length bias, format hacking, semantic shortcut, judge-specific preference) substantially harder to exploit simultaneously. At test time, the same conservative verifier serves as a fitness function in an evolutionary search that maintains a diverse archive and uses dual PATCH/REWRITE refinement to balance exploitation and exploration. Methodology: The pipeline has three training stages and a test-time framework. Stage 1 (Proof Expert): for each problem, a rollout policy samples G candidate proofs; each is scored by a four-layer verifier returning a scalar in [0,7]; group-normalized advantages are computed; CISPO (an off-policy REINFORCE objective that clips importance-sampling weights rather than surrogate losses) updates the policy only when group reward variance exceeds threshold τ_std, preventing gradient updates from noisy all-same-score groups. Training data uses competition-style problems with domain (algebra 24.7%, combinatorics 30.5%, geometry 27.9%, number theory 17.0%) and trick-frequency balancing after difficulty filtering via the prior M2.7 model. Stage 2 (Verifier Expert): trained to produce structured assessment/errors/verdict output via composite reward R = 0.7·R_error + 0.3·R_verdict where R_error uses a frontier LLM judge for spatial and semantic error alignment; training data is harvested for free from Stage 1 rollouts. Stage 3 (Fixer Expert): fine-tuned on (problem, flawed_proof, critique) triples also harvested from Stage 1, accepted only when the same pessimistic-min verifier returns no_errors. MaxProof loop: initialize N=32 candidates, score each K_verify=4 times, run R=10 refinement rounds selecting M=4 diverse parents per round (lexical-distance diversity filter), produce PATCH (exploit critique) and REWRITE (explore new route with sibling context) offspring, early-stop when ≥2 candidates reach 7/7, finalize via top-K=4 pairwise tournament with K_ranker=3 votes per match. Critical design decisions: (1) pessimistic min aggregation is well-justified by the M2 hacking case studies; (2) dual PATCH/REWRITE is reasonable but not ablated against single-mode refinement; (3) population-level early stop (requiring 2 perfect candidates, not 1) to reduce false-positive promotion is well-motivated. Key Results: On standalone benchmarks (no MaxProof), M3 achieves 67.40 on IMOProofBench and 81.56 on IMOAnswerBench, compared to GPT-5.5's 90.85/90.60 and Gemini 3.1 Pro's 75.71/90.00; Opus 4.7 scores 65.85/79.90. M3 is second-to-last on IMOProofBench, indicating meaningful gap to frontier closed-source models on proof construction. With MaxProof on contest problems: M3 one-shot scores 27/42 on IMO 2025 and 26/42 on USAMO 2026; M3+MaxProof reaches 35/42 (+8) and 36/42 (+10) respectively, both exceeding the human gold-medal threshold. Per-problem breakdown: M3+MaxProof achieves 7/7 on 9 of 12 problems; IMO P6 scores 0/7 across all rounds (base-model capability ceiling); USAMO P2 has a 6/7 oracle-best candidate that the tournament fails to select (self-pick = 2/7, selection loss = 4 points); USAMO P3 reaches 6/7 but never 7/7 due to single-judge disagreement. The IMO P1/P4/P5 7/7 solutions emerge at rounds 7, 10, and 7 respectively, indicating even routine problems require substantial search. An independent expert review confirms all 7/7 self-picks are complete and correct proofs. Baselines are contemporary frontier models, making comparisons meaningful, though the contest evaluation set is only 12 problems. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM. The defense-in-depth verifier design principle (optimizing for false-positive rate rather than benchmark accuracy in the RL loop) is genuinely novel and well-supported by ablative evidence from the M2 failure case. The MaxProof evolutionary search framework synthesizes population-based search, verifier-guided fitness, and dual exploitation/exploration refinement in a new configuration. The three-expert training pipeline (generate/verify/fix as specialist capabilities merged into one model) is a new compositional training strategy. The core RL algorithm (CISPO) and the std-threshold filter are components from prior MiniMax work. The application domain (competition math proof) is not new but is considerably harder than prior benchmarks. Rigor & Reproducibility: The contest evaluation covers only 12 problems total (6 IMO 2025 + 6 USAMO 2026), which is an extremely small sample for drawing strong statistical conclusions. No error bars, seeds, or significance tests are reported on contest results. The standalone benchmarks cover more problems but use GPT-5.4 and GPT-OSS-120B as judges — vendor model judges evaluating a competitor's model creates potential evaluation bias. No component ablations are provided: the contribution of each verifier layer, the std-threshold filter, the Verifier Expert distillation, and the Fixer Expert are not isolated. The M2 reward-hacking case study (Appendix C) provides unusually strong qualitative evidence — four concrete examples with training-verifier scores of 1.0 and expert-judge scores of 0.05–0.1, plus a cross-verification cohort showing 33% incorrect at perfect training score — but this is evidence that the M2 verifier failed, not that the M3 verifier succeeded. Code, model weights, and training data are not released. The paper explicitly excludes IMO 2025, USAMO 2026, IMOProofBench, and IMOAnswerBench from training data with near-duplicate filtering, which is a credibility positive. The full selected model outputs in Appendix D allow expert inspection of claimed 7/7 solutions; the appendix-provided proofs for P1–P5 and USAMO P1/P4–P6 appear structurally complete though they rely heavily on case analysis. Deployability (EMPIRICAL profile): - Practical: MaxProof with N=32 candidates, K_verify=4, R=10 rounds, and K_ranker=3 is computationally expensive — the paper does not report wall-clock time or token counts per problem, so hardware requirements are unstated. - Efficient: The Verifier Expert is explicitly motivated as a distillation of the expensive external multi-frontier-judge pipeline to reduce per-candidate latency from seconds to hundreds of milliseconds; total MaxProof compute per problem is not quantified. - Effective: On the 12 contest problems evaluated, the system produces expert-verified correct proofs for 9/12 at the self-pick level; the three failures (P6 zero, P2 selection error, P3 judge disagreement) are candidly documented with per-round search dynamics. - Scalable: The paper claims MaxProof is model-agnostic and separable from M3, but testing on only 12 problems does not establish behavior at larger problem sets; the base model's standalone benchmark gap to GPT-5.5 (~23 points on IMOProofBench) suggests the approach depends heavily on the base model's underlying capability ceiling. No software artifact or model weights are released; the system exists in paper form only. Strengths: - The M2 reward-hacking case study is exceptionally valuable: four documented hacking patterns with concrete rollout examples (training score 1.0, expert score 0.05–0.1) constitute rare empirical evidence for the reward gaming failure mode, and the design of the M3 verifier is directly traceable to each failure mode. - The verifier design philosophy — prioritizing false-positive suppression over static benchmark accuracy — is a principled and novel framing that reframes prior verifier evaluation methodology. - Releasing full model outputs for all 12 contest problems (Appendix D) enables independent mathematical scrutiny of the claimed 7/7 proofs, making the evaluation more auditable than typical vendor reports. - The Fixer Expert's training data being a free byproduct of the Proof Expert's RL run is an elegant efficiency that reduces annotation overhead while maintaining verifier-aligned supervision. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly acknowledge: (1) M3's solutions on routine problems are verbose and rely on exhaustive case analysis rather than reaching essential structure, unlike concise GPT-5.5 solutions; (2) the selection loss on USAMO P2 (tournament selects 2/7 over oracle-best 6/7 due to ranker preference diverging from verifier score); (3) three problems never reach 7/7 in the population (P6 as base-model ceiling, P3 as judge disagreement, P2 as selection failure); (4) stronger closed-source systems remain ahead, and M3 is self-described as a "follower chasing the frontier"; (5) future work needs larger top-K tournaments and ranker-prompt variations to reduce selection loss. The authors confront these limitations directly rather than minimizing them. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The paper provides no component ablations: whether the four verifier layers individually matter, whether CISPO outperforms GRPO with the same data, whether the Verifier Expert distillation improves over using the external verifier directly at test time, and whether the Fixer Expert contributes beyond basic REWRITE — none of these are tested. The contest evaluation sample (12 problems) is too small to distinguish the framework from lucky problem selection; a strong baseline of best@32 random sampling with oracle selection is not compared, making it impossible to attribute gains to the evolutionary search vs. simply sampling more. The judge used for IMOProofBench evaluation is GPT-5.4 (another MiniMax competitor), creating potential evaluation conflict of interest that goes unaddressed. The paper measures USAMO 2026 but this contest occurred less than a month before the paper's submission date (arXiv June 11, 2026), raising questions about whether training data decontamination was possible; the authors state they removed USAMO 2026 from training but the timeline is tight. The conservative pessimistic-min verifier may produce a systematic bias toward verbose, exhaustive proofs (documented in §6.3.1) — this trade-off between proof quality and verifier fidelity is acknowledged but not quantified. Bottom Line: A practitioner working on LLM reasoning, inference-time scaling, or reward modeling for open-ended tasks will find genuine value here — particularly the M2 reward-hacking anatomy and the defense-in-depth verifier design — but the 12-problem contest evaluation and absence of component ablations mean the engineering claims cannot yet be separated from the base model's capability, and the system remains closed-source.
Recommended 2606.13473 PDF
#113 — Innovative (60)

AtomWorld: A Benchmark for Evaluating Spatial Reasoning in Large Language Models on Material Structures

T Lv, A Chen, F Xie, C Wu, J Meng, D Zhou, Y Wang… · openreview.net · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces AtomWorld, a benchmark and data-generation framework that evaluates LLMs on their ability to execute ten types of atomic structure modification operations on crystal CIF files, instantiated as AtomMotor-2K (2,500 test cases) with verifiable ground-truth comparison via pymatgen's StructureMatcher. - Why it's important: Constructing and modifying atomic structures is the foundational and least-automated step in materials science research, yet no prior benchmark measured whether LLMs can correctly execute these spatial operations, as opposed to answering knowledge questions about materials. - How it does it: A generator programmatically applies each action (e.g., rotate_around, insert_between, super_cell) to a Materials Project CIF to produce a ground-truth output structure, then evaluates LLM-generated CIF outputs through three hierarchical checks: format validity, composition match, and symmetry-aware structural matching with 0.5 Å site tolerance. - The good: The benchmark cleanly stratifies tasks by difficulty — frontier models achieve >90% on simple identity operations (add, remove, change) but collapse to <12% on rotation even for the best-performing Claude Opus 4.6, and pure coordinate tests confirm this is an intrinsic spatial reasoning failure, not a CIF-parsing artifact. - The missing: The benchmark is restricted to structures with ≤100 atoms (all models fail beyond 200 atoms), uses a single CIF format from a single source, and the StructProp sub-evaluation uses only 10 samples per model per property — far too few for any statistical conclusion. - Read it or not: Worth reading for materials informatics researchers building LLM-assisted structure workflows or designing structure-aware training curricula; it fills a genuine evaluation gap, though the paper is a preprint under ICML review and several components are preliminary.
Why this rank: Empirical (benchmark) profile. Novelty (65) reflects the first benchmark with verifiable programmatic ground truth for LLM execution of atomic-structure modifications, plus the clean empirical finding that rotation fails categorically (Claude Opus 4.6 at 12%) even on stripped coordinates. Rigor (58) is held back by the N=10 StructProp sub-benchmark (below any inference threshold), no significance tests, masked code, and irreproducible model names (GPT-5.4, Gemini 3.1 Pro Preview do not map to documented releases).
