This outcome-level distillation (RISD) is distinguished from trajectory-level distillation (OPD) by the observation that a reward model's deployment requirement is fast, stable, calibrated scoring, not explanation generation. Methodology The pipeline has four stages. (1) Annotation: human annotators score images across four dimensions (Text-Image Alignment, Realism, Aesthetics, Physical Plausibility) on a 9-level half-point scale {1.0, 1.5, …, 5.0}, using a rubric document with 15-20 annotated reference images per score bin. A score-adjustment step allows same-prompt comparisons to shift scores by ±0.5, and quality control gates annotators by accuracy threshold. (2) Teacher training via GDSO: builds on GRPO (group-normalized advantage estimation) and adds three supervised terms. A pointwise reward r^pt = 1 − |μ − ŝ| / (S_max − S_min) and its cross-entropy loss L^pt_CE anchor the decoded expected score to the annotated rubric score. A pairwise reward r^pw and gap loss L^pw supervise the difference between predicted scores for same-prompt image pairs against the annotated score gap — unlike Bradley-Terry, this penalizes margin inflation and enforces magnitude, not just direction. The full GDSO objective is L_GRPO({r}) + α_pt L^pt_CE + α_pw L^pw, trained iteratively with GRPO-style reference policy updates. Crucially, rewards are computed from the expectation of the decoded score-bin distribution (Q-Align-style decoding from score token probabilities), not from parsed text, preserving sub-integer signal. (3) Student distillation via RISD: the frozen teacher generates a reasoning trace and score distribution for each (prompt, image, dimension) triple; the student minimizes KL(q_T ∥ q_φ) where q_T is the teacher's reasoning-conditioned distribution and q_φ is the student's direct distribution conditioned only on the image-prompt pair. The student outputs a single expected score. (4) Downstream T2I optimization: ReFL-style direct reward backpropagation through the diffusion denoising process using aggregated multi-dimensional expected scores as the gradient signal. Critical design decisions: (a) distribution-expectation reward vs. text parsing — well-justified by ablation showing parsing quantizes reward signals; (b) pairwise gap loss vs. Bradley-Terry — well-justified by score-collapse argument; (c) outcome-level vs. trajectory-level distillation — well-justified by OPD ablation; hyperparameter values (α_pt, α_pw, λ_pt, λ_pw) are referenced but not reported. Key Results All reward model evaluation is on the authors' proprietary internally annotated test set. On the 27B model: GDSO achieves PLCC=0.762, SRCC=0.713, HPA=89.56%, Margin-HPA=98.85%, versus GRPO at PLCC=0.720, SRCC=0.683, HPA=86.04%, and SFT at HPA=81.35%. On the 9B model: RISD achieves PLCC=0.739, SRCC=0.688, HPA=88.64%, Margin-HPA=98.01%, versus the OPD baseline at HPA=83.11%/Margin-HPA=96.43% with ~750 output tokens, versus GDSO at 9B at HPA=83.95% with ~750 output tokens. RISD reduces inference cost to 1 output token — approximately 750× fewer decode steps than OPD or GDSO. A notably counter-intuitive finding: 9B RISD outperforms 9B GDSO despite seeing no reasoning text at inference; reasoning quality is captured in the distributional target, not the trajectory. The RewardDance baseline improves 9B HPA (+4pp over SFT) but degrades PLCC and SRCC, confirming that preference-direction improvements and absolute-score calibration are dissociable objectives. For downstream T2I optimization, blind human evaluation on 400 prompts (compositional, attribute-binding, physically challenging scenes) shows 41.3% net GSB improvement over the SFT baseline. No public T2I reward benchmark results (GenEval, T2I-CompBench, HPSv2 eval set) are reported. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The combination of GRPO-augmented distribution-level supervision (GDSO) and outcome-level distributional distillation (RISD) is genuinely novel as a reward modeling framework. The pairwise gap loss as a replacement for Bradley-Terry in an RL setting is a well-motivated contribution. The core building blocks — GRPO, KL distillation, Q-Align score decoding, soft ordinal labels — are standard prior work; the novelty is the specific integration and the deployment-motivated reframing of distillation from trajectory-level to outcome-level. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and informative: distribution expectation vs. text parsing (Figure 4, both GRPO and GDSO), RISD vs. OPD (Table 3), and the full SFT→RewardDance→GRPO→GDSO→RISD progression (Table 2). No error bars, multiple seeds, or significance tests are reported anywhere. Code, data, and model weights are not released — no repository URL is mentioned. The primary evaluation benchmark is internal to the authors and uses images generated by the Z-Image model (the same generator being optimized), raising a potential Goodhart's Law concern: the reward model is evaluated on the same distribution it was designed to optimize. The OPD baseline implementation closely follows published formulations, providing a credible comparison, but no external benchmark result makes cross-paper comparison impossible. Hyperparameters α_pt, α_pw, λ_pt, λ_pw, group size G, and training scale are not stated in the paper. Deployability (4-property check) EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: The 9B RISD student can run on commercially available high-memory GPUs (~18GB in FP16); the 27B teacher is needed only for offline distillation data generation, not deployment. - Efficient: The RISD student outputs 1 score token per query, reducing decode latency by ~750× vs. the reasoning teacher or OPD student at reward inference time; the compute cost of teacher distillation generation is not quantified. - Effective: The 41.3% GSB improvement in human evaluation confirms that Z-Reward's signal translates to human-perceived image quality, not only to proxy-reward improvement, which directly addresses the reward-hacking concern — though the absence of a reward-vs-human-preference divergence curve leaves reward overoptimization behavior uncharacterized. - Scalable: Only tested with a 27B teacher and 9B student; no scaling experiments across model sizes, dataset sizes, or RL training duration. T2I optimization is demonstrated at 10k iterations on internal prompts; production-scale behavior is not evaluated. Claims about extension to video, LLM reward modeling, and other modalities are speculative. The work delivers a method in paper form only; no software artifact, dataset, or trained model is released. Strengths - The pairwise gap supervision (Equations 9–11) is a well-motivated and technically clean improvement over Bradley-Terry for calibrated reward modeling: it enforces both preference direction and annotated score magnitude, preventing unconstrained margin inflation that decouples the reward scale from the rubric. - The RISD outcome-level distillation insight — that reward model deployment requires reproducing teacher judgments, not teacher reasoning — is clearly stated, distinguishable from prior OPD work, and empirically supported by the 5.5pp HPA gap over OPD at 750× lower inference cost. - GDSO's combination of policy-gradient and direct supervised losses addresses the context mismatch problem (annotators consult a long rubric; deployed models see one image at a time) without requiring the model to recover calibrated scoring purely through self-exploration. - The downstream T2I optimization experiment in Section 5 provides end-to-end evidence that reward model accuracy improvements survive the proxy-reward bottleneck and manifest in human-judged output quality. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly flag reasoning-score weak coupling: the direct supervised terms (α_pt L^pt_CE + α_pw L^pw) may cause the teacher's final score to depend on direct score supervision rather than on the generated reasoning trace, potentially making the reasoning chain loosely coupled to the output distribution. They characterize this as a minority case in their experiments but acknowledge it as a design gap requiring future attention (e.g., reasoning-score consistency checks or contrastive supervision). Extension to video, text, and other modalities is left as future work without empirical support. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The entire reward model evaluation is on a proprietary internal benchmark derived from the same annotation pipeline and image generator used in training. No experiment uses GenEval, T2I-CompBench, HPSv2 evaluation sets, or any external held-out distribution, making independent verification impossible and direct comparison with published reward model results unavailable. - The downstream 41.3% GSB improvement has no reward-signal ablation: there is no experiment comparing Z-Reward as the RL signal against other reward models (ImageReward, HPSv2, HPSv3) applied to the same generator with the same RL procedure, so the improvement is attributed to Z-Reward's quality but may partly reflect the RL recipe or the base generator. - No reward hacking or overoptimization curve is measured: the paper cites reward overoptimization as a key risk for prior methods but never plots reward score vs. human preference accuracy as optimization proceeds for Z-Reward itself. - Key hyperparameters (α_pt, α_pw, λ_pt, λ_pw, G) are undefined in the paper, making reproduction impossible even for groups with access to the same backbone models. - The human evaluation for T2I optimization (Section 5.3) uses professional annotators from the same pool as the training annotation pipeline, introducing possible familiarity bias; no inter-annotator agreement or annotation diversity is reported for this evaluation. Bottom Line Z-Reward makes a technically sound and well-motivated contribution to visual reward modeling — the GDSO + RISD design is conceptually tight and the OPD comparison is the strongest piece of evidence — but the complete reliance on a proprietary evaluation benchmark, the absence of externally verifiable numbers, and the lack of any released artifacts make it currently impossible to assess how the gains generalize beyond the authors' pipeline.
Recommended 2606.09076 PDF
#119 — Innovative (60)

Efficient Clustering with Provable Guardrails for LLM Inference at Scale

L Wang, W Keung, P Ghodasara, RZ Wang, A Dashti… · openreview.net · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a two-stage clustering algorithm that combines Mini-batch K-Means with a greedy SET COVER representative-selection step over α-balls in embedding space, provably enforcing user-specified minimum within-cluster similarity and exact categorical attribute matching at n~10^7 scale. - Why it's important: LLM inference over tens of millions of users costs millions of dollars and hundreds of days of wall-clock time; no existing clustering method simultaneously guarantees a similarity floor, attribute equality, and sub-quadratic scalability — the three properties needed to safely run a representative-only LLM pipeline in production. - How it does it: Within each Mini-batch K-Means pre-cluster, it runs the Johnson–Chvátal greedy heuristic for SET COVER over α-balls (similarity ≥ α AND attribute equality), which guarantees every point is assigned to a representative it matches by construction, with a (1 + ln n_k)-approximation on representative count. - The good: Production deployment on 38M customers achieved a 50-fold reduction in downstream LLM compute (from $1.13M/508 days to ~$22.5K/10 days) with only 0.7% product relevance degradation, validated by an A/B test with statistically significant business metric improvement. - The missing: All controlled benchmarks run on 100K-sample subsets; no wall-clock runtime at 38M scale is reported, and the production section omits any comparison against alternative clustering approaches. - Read it or not: Worth reading for ML engineers and applied researchers building LLM-serving pipelines at scale; the combination of exact formal guarantees and documented production impact is rare for a workshop paper.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Practical Impact (70) drives the composite: a deployed 38M-customer production algorithm delivering a documented 50× LLM-compute reduction with a statistically significant A/B test is rare and directly actionable, though no open-source artifact is released. Rigor (50) and Resilience (50) pull it down — proofs are complete, but all controlled benchmarks stop at 100K (no controlled 1M–10M run, no 38M wall-clock), the A/B numbers are confidential and unverifiable, and the relevance validation reuses an LLM Marketing Critic that is itself a pipeline component (auto-correlation). Novelty (60) and Non-Obviousness (65) reflect a clever set-cover-over-α-balls composition of non-novel ingredients plus the non-obvious skewed-distribution tail-trimming insight. Completeness (55) is adequate with quantified, honest limitations. Weighted: 0.15*60 + 0.15*50 + 0.10*65 + 0.15*55 + 0.15*50 + 0.30*70 = 60.
systems Novelty 60 Rigor 50 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 55 Resilience 50 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Large-scale LLM deployments in eCommerce face prohibitive inference costs — the paper's motivating case is a personalized recommendation pipeline over 38M customers where LLM stages alone cost $1.13M and 508 days of compute at allocated throughput. Input-side clustering (running the LLM only on cluster representatives) is a natural cost reduction, but it introduces approximation error: every member of a cluster inherits the representative's LLM output, and in customer-facing applications this error must be explicitly bounded for quality and safety reasons (e.g., preventing age-inappropriate product recommendations). No existing clustering method satisfies all four requirements jointly: a minimum semantic similarity between each sample and its representative, exact categorical attribute matching, ≥10× data reduction, and runtime scalable to n~10^7. Standard methods (K-Means, GMM, BIRCH, Spectral) provide no similarity constraint. Threshold-based methods (Agglomerative Clustering, Community Detection) accept a similarity cutoff but require O(n²) pairwise computation — roughly 90TB for n=5M — ruling them out at production scale. Lexical near-deduplication (MinHash) cannot handle semantic paraphrases. Key Idea: The key insight is to reframe within-cluster representative selection as greedy SET COVER over α-balls in embedding space: a candidate representative r covers point i if cosine_sim(E_r, E_i) ≥ α AND their categorical attributes match. Applying the classical Johnson–Chvátal greedy heuristic independently within each Mini-batch K-Means pre-cluster restricts the O(n²) pairwise computation to within-cluster pairs of size n/K, reducing overall complexity to O(n²d/K) while preserving exact coverage by construction. The match relation is reflexive and symmetric but not transitive — which is what makes the problem non-trivial and makes set cover the right abstraction. Methodology: Stage 1 runs Mini-batch K-Means on the embedding vectors {E_i} to produce K initial clusters {C_k}. Stage 2 operates independently within each C_k: compute pairwise cosine similarity matrix S_ij and binary match matrix M_ij = 1{S_ij ≥ α ∧ A_i = A_j}; iteratively select r = argmax_i |{j ∈ U : M_ij = 1}|, assign all matching unmatched points to r, remove them from the unmatched set U; repeat until U is empty. An optional post-hoc reassignment step (Algorithm 2) reassigns each point to its most-similar feasible representative, provably increasing average similarity without changing |R| or violating the guardrail. Overall complexity is O(nd + n²d/K) time and O(nd + n²/K²) memory — linear in n for K = Θ(n). Three critical design decisions: (1) Mini-batch K-Means for Stage 1 is justified as cheap and approximate, with Stage 2 correcting partition-level errors — sound in theory and validated empirically in Table 3; (2) greedy rather than optimal set cover is justified by NP-hardness of MIN-SET-COVER, with the classical (1 + ln n_k) approximation bound — well-justified; (3) pairwise computation restricted within each initial cluster is the core scalability move, with K as an explicit scalability knob — this is well-analyzed in Appendix E. Key Results: Benchmarks on 100K-sample subsets of three datasets (internal shopping personas, AG News, Cosmopedia) against seven baselines (Mini-batch K-Means, K-Means, Agglomerative, BIRCH, Spectral, Gaussian Mixture), with baseline cluster count fixed to match the proposed method's output: - 0.0% of samples violate the similarity guardrail under the proposed method; every baseline violates it for 0.8%–21.2% of samples. - Runtime: 2.7 sec (Shopping Personas), 4.2 sec (AG News), 6.8 sec (Cosmopedia) vs. 28–6281 sec for baselines — 10×–1000× faster. - Tail trimming: top 4% of clusters cover 90% of customers (vs. ~30% for balanced-cluster baselines at the same 4% threshold), enabling an additional 25× data reduction. - Similarity–relevance correlation: Pearson r=0.781, Spearman ρ=0.797 (p<0.001, n=7,000 member–representative pairs across 7 similarity buckets), validating cosine similarity as a relevance proxy. - Production (38M customers, α=0.77, 5 matched categorical attributes): 50× data reduction; LLM query-generation from $114K/22.8 days to $2.1K/0.4 days; Marketing Critic from $1.02M/485 days to $20.4K/9.7 days; 0.7% lower product-to-member relevance rate vs. product-to-representative rate; A/B test showed statistically significant positive business metric impact (exact numbers withheld for confidentiality). Baselines are standard scikit-learn implementations — current and appropriate for the comparison task. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The formulation of constrained clustering as greedy set cover over α-balls in embedding space — simultaneously handling a similarity floor, attribute equality, and scalability — is the genuine novel contribution; neither ingredient (Mini-batch K-Means, greedy set cover) is new, but their composition to achieve provable input-side LLM deduplication at scale is. The observation that greedy set-cover representative selection inherently produces a right-skewed cluster-size distribution suitable for aggressive tail trimming is a non-obvious secondary finding. Rigor & Reproducibility: The formal proofs (Theorems 1, 4, and the approximation bound in Appendix D) are complete and correct. Robustness ablations across Mini-batch K-Means hyperparameters (Table 3: batch size, init size, K from 10 to 250) are thorough and show the min-similarity guarantee holds exactly across all settings. No code or data is released; the internal shopping persona dataset is proprietary; no URL or repository is mentioned. The 100K benchmark size is too small to verify the 38M production deployment directly — controlled benchmarks at 1M or 5M for the methods that do scale are absent. The A/B test business metric results are omitted for confidentiality, making the production impact claim unverifiable to readers. No error bars or multiple-seed results are reported for the similarity/runtime benchmarks. The production relevance evaluation uses an LLM-based Marketing Critic, which is the same type of component as in the downstream pipeline — this creates potential auto-correlation between the relevance signal and the LLM generating recommendations. This is a workshop paper under anonymous review, not a peer-reviewed main-conference publication. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS — the algorithm is implemented and deployed in a production recommendation pipeline. - Practical: Runs on a single r7i.12xlarge CPU-only instance; embedding computation uses all-MiniLM-L6-v2 (no GPU required); Stage 2 pairwise computation is parallelizable across initial clusters by design. - Efficient: O(nd + n²d/K) time and O(nd + n²/K²) memory; at 100K with K=40 (0.04% of n), total Stage 1 + Stage 2 runtime is under 7 seconds; production-scale runtime at 38M is not explicitly reported, which is the main unresolved efficiency question. - Effective: Provably satisfies similarity and attribute guardrails by construction; 0.7% relevance gap in production and statistically significant A/B test results support effectiveness, though the A/B metric numbers are confidential. - Scalable: Deployed at 38M in production; K is the scalability knob that reduces per-cluster quadratic cost; Table 3 validates robustness to K, but no wall-clock or memory benchmark at n > 100K under controlled conditions is provided. The work delivers a deployable production algorithm but no open-source artifact. Strengths: - The set-cover framing yields provable exact guardrails by construction, not probabilistically or empirically — this is a qualitative improvement over all seven baselines, which violate the constraint on 0.8%–21.2% of samples. - Production validation at 38M customers with documented business impact (50-fold compute reduction, A/B test success) is compelling and rare in algorithmic papers; most clustering papers stop at synthetic benchmarks. - The skewed cluster-size distribution as an intended structural consequence of greedy set cover is a non-obvious insight: 4% of clusters covering 90% of customers gives a further 25× reduction beyond the baseline 50× — potentially more valuable than the primary algorithm itself. - Robustness to the K hyperparameter (Table 3) is practically important — the exact similarity guarantee is independent of K; only data-reduction efficiency varies modestly. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly acknowledge: (1) the trade-off between the base greedy algorithm and the reassignment variant — reassignment improves average similarity but flattens the cluster-size distribution, reducing tail-trimming efficiency; (2) the partition-constrained optimum may differ from the global optimum when similar items land in different initial clusters, with the gap controlled by initial clustering quality; (3) adding attribute-matching constraints increases cluster count relative to similarity-only clustering (Table 2); (4) future work directions (richer similarity metrics, learned representatives) implicitly acknowledge that cosine similarity is an imperfect proxy. Authors confront these limitations directly and with quantification, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - Benchmark scale gap: All controlled comparisons run at 100K because competing methods fail beyond that. The paper never benchmarks the proposed method alone at 1M–10M, leaving the scaling claim between 100K and 38M empirically unvalidated in a controlled setting; runtime at 38M is not reported. - No alternative at production scale: The production section compares only against "no clustering" (full LLM cost), not against Mini-batch K-Means or BIRCH (which do scale) even if they violate the guardrail — a cost-quality trade-off comparison would strengthen the motivation. - Relevance circularity: The similarity-relevance validation uses an LLM-based Marketing Critic to rate (member, product) pairs; the same LLM-based Marketing Critic is a component of the production pipeline that generated the products. This creates structural auto-correlation that could inflate the measured similarity-relevance relationship. - Confidential A/B test: Business metric improvement is stated but entirely omitted; the production impact claim rests on unverifiable self-reported numbers. - Embedding-to-LLM-output gap: The algorithm's correctness guarantee is over cosine similarity, but the downstream quality guarantee depends on cosine similarity correlating with LLM output agreement — not just relevance ratings. This connection is not directly measured. Bottom Line: This is a well-executed systems paper with clean formal foundations and compelling production evidence; ML engineers building LLM-serving pipelines over large user bases will find the combination of exact guardrails, sub-10-second benchmarks, and a documented 50× compute reduction directly actionable, and there is no obvious stronger alternative that simultaneously provides provable similarity constraints with this scalability profile.
Search Results
#120 — Moderately Innovative (59)

Artificial Intelligence for Mathematical Reasoning: An Integrated Survey of Language Models, Neuro- symbolic Systems, and Verified Discovery

SR Raiyan, M Kabir, H Mahmud, MK Hasan · arxiv.org · neural symbolic - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a unified survey of AI for mathematical reasoning covering the full arc from 1960s rule-based MWP solvers through mid-2026 reasoning models, Lean-based formal provers, and verified-discovery workflows, organized around four axes (informal, multimodal, formal, discovery) and a novel supervision-ladder framework. - Why it's important: Mathematical reasoning benchmarks (GSM8K, MATH, AIME, IMO) have become the primary yardstick for AI capability, yet no prior survey integrates the classical MWP lineage, multimodal geometry, Lean-based formal proving, and open-problem discovery into a single coherent account through the reasoning-model era inaugurated by OpenAI o1. - How it does it: A structured SLR over 1,847 candidate records (yielding 238 included) is organized around a supervision-ladder metaphor in which each era of progress is characterized by its dominant external verification constraint — from equation templates through Python execution, process reward models, and Lean kernel checks. - The good: The survey is the only entry in its own comparative positioning table (Table III) to achieve full coverage across all eight axes (MWP, geometry/MM, LLM, reasoning-model era, multi-agent, formal, discovery, multilingual), and the supervision-ladder framework offers a unifying explanatory lens with genuine predictive power about where bottlenecks will emerge next. - The missing: Many frontier-model numbers are vendor-supplied technical-report figures rather than independently replicated results; the paper flags this explicitly throughout, but Tables XI and XIX mix rigorous and provisional results at the same display salience, making it easy to overweight the 2025–2026 entries. - Read it or not: Worth reading for any researcher working in AI reasoning, NLP, or formal methods who needs a single comprehensive reference through mid-2026; specialists already deeply embedded in one sub-area (e.g., only Lean theorem proving) may find more technical depth in the dedicated sub-surveys cited in Table III.
Why this rank: Theoretical (survey) profile. Novelty (65) and Non-Obviousness (60) come from the supervision-ladder framework and the comprehension–generation–verification triad, which are original organizing contributions with some predictive content beyond prior fragmented surveys. Rigor (55) is limited by missing inter-rater reliability for 238 coded records, reliance on vendor-supplied frontier numbers, and preprint status, though the GitHub repo and explicit § flagging help. Completeness (65) is a positive — Section X confronts reward hacking, contamination, and the Erdős incident directly. As a synthesis with no new experiments and resembling the review-style work the user has rated down, the composite lands at the upper-incremental band. Weighted: 0.25*65+0.20*55+0.25*60+0.10*65+0.15*58+0.05*60 ≈ 60, adjusted to 59 for review-genre calibration.
theoretical Novelty 65 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 60 Completeness 65 Resilience 58 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Mathematical reasoning research had fragmented into largely disconnected sub-literatures — MWP solving, geometry, formal theorem proving, and mathematical discovery — each with separate datasets, evaluation protocols, and methodological traditions. Prior surveys either covered only pre-LLM MWP work, or began covering LLMs but stopped before the reasoning-model era (OpenAI o1, DeepSeek-R1, 2024–2026), or were narrowly scoped to a single axis. The rapid emergence of RLVR-trained reasoning models, Lean-based large-scale provers (AlphaProof, DeepSeek-Prover-V2), Olympiad-geometry systems (AlphaGeometry2), and AI-assisted attacks on Erdős open problems created an urgent need for an integrated account connecting these developments to the sixty-year MWP lineage and to each other. Key Idea The core intellectual contribution is the supervision-ladder framework: progress in AI mathematical reasoning is driven not by architectural novelty alone, but by access to successively stronger external verification constraints. Each era is defined by the constraint that made it tractable (hand-coded schemata, equation templates, Python execution, process reward models, symbolic geometry engines, Lean kernel checks) and by the bottleneck that constraint could not resolve, which motivated the next transition. This framework is instantiated in Table XXII across seven generations, retrospectively explains every major paradigm shift, and generates actionable design guidance — e.g., it predicts that multi-agent debate is insufficient without an external judge independent of the generator's training distribution, and that formal verification advances fastest relative to informal methods because the Lean kernel satisfies all three conditions for a useful verifier (accurate, adversarially resistant, rich in partial-progress signal). Methodology The survey follows a structured SLR methodology adapted from Kitchenham & Charters guidelines. Search covered Scopus, Web of Science, ACL Anthology, IEEE Xplore, and arXiv (cs.CL, cs.AI, cs.LG, cs.SC) between January 2023 and April 2026, supplemented by backward/forward snowballing from 12 prior surveys and arXiv monitoring for late-breaking systems. From 1,847 candidate records, 312 duplicates were removed; 1,089 excluded at title/abstract stage (outside scope, pure code generation, education technology, scientific QA without explicit mathematical reasoning); 208 excluded at full-text review (scope mismatch, insufficient technical detail, superseded versions, no quantitative evaluation), yielding 238 included records. Each record was coded by primary contribution, task family (MWP, geometry, formal, discovery), method family, modality, supervision signal, benchmark, and reported performance. Taxonomy axes are: informal text-only (105), multimodal/geometry (35), formal reasoning (30), discovery (8), cross-cutting benchmarks/multilingual/adjacent surveys (60). No statistical meta-analysis is performed, as evaluation protocols are too heterogeneous; instead the paper constructs a systematic map. Five guiding research questions structure the narrative: evolution from rule-based MWP to RLVR (RQ1), multimodal evidence use (RQ2), formal proving progress (RQ3), definition of genuine AI-assisted discovery (RQ4), and benchmark design principles (RQ5). Critical design decisions include: (1) including arXiv preprints and technical reports for frontier systems to avoid a systematic recency bias, acknowledged as reducing reproducibility guarantees; (2) not double-counting multi-role papers in the Sankey taxonomy; (3) explicitly flagging vendor-supplied vs. independently replicated numbers throughout. Key Results The survey documents a sharp performance discontinuity at the onset of the reasoning-model era (lavender band in Fig. 14). GSM8K saturated from ~55% (seq2tree, 2017) to >97% (Kimi K2, 2026); MATH-500 from 6.9% (GPT-3) to 99.2% (LongCat-Flash-Thinking); AIME 2024 from 12% (GPT-4o) to 91.6% (o3); FrontierMath from <2% (GPT-4o) to ~25% (o3 tiers 1–3) / 48% Tier-4 (AI co-mathematician). Formal proving moved fastest in relative terms: MiniF2F-test rose from ~25% (2022) to 93% (early 2026); PutnamBench from near-zero to 86/658 (Goedel-Prover-V2 32B). At IMO 2025, Gemini Deep Think achieved gold-medal performance (35/42) in natural language within the 4.5-hour time limit. Compound AlphaGeometry2 solved 42/50 IMO 2000–2024 geometry problems, exceeding the average gold medalist. A counter-intuitive finding from MATHARENA: most models score 10–20 percentage points higher on AIME 2024 than AIME 2025, with one model (QwQ-Preview-32B) inflated by nearly 60%, providing strong empirical evidence of training-data contamination. On USAMO 2025, even the best model (Gemini 2.5 Pro, 10.1/42) scores far below the human median of 15/42, exposing a persistent answer-generation vs. proof-writing gap. GSM-SYMBOLIC shows accuracy drops of up to 65% under irrelevant clause insertion, even for strongest reasoning models. Baselines across the MWP section are strong and historically representative; frontier-model numbers in Tables XI and XIX are partially vendor-supplied (flagged with § symbols) and should be treated as provisional. Novelty Classification NEW FORMULATION + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING (synthesized). The supervision-ladder (Table XXII) and comprehension–generation–verification triad (Fig. 9) are original taxonomic contributions not present in prior surveys. The integrated four-axis taxonomy (Fig. 4) and the benchmark functional taxonomy organized by lifecycle position (Table XV) are also original to this work. No new experiments, models, or datasets are contributed. What is genuinely novel is the unifying explanatory framework applied to 238 records through mid-2026; what is a competent synthesis of existing ideas is the historical MWP survey sections, which extend well-covered prior surveys. Rigor & Reproducibility No original experiments; this is a survey. SLR methodology is described at reasonable depth (screening numbers, eligibility criteria in Table IV, search strings specified). Coding dimensions are listed but no inter-rater reliability statistics are reported — a standard SLR quality indicator that is absent. The paper explicitly flags vendor-supplied frontier-model numbers (§ symbol in tables) and recommends treating live leaderboard snapshots as provisional. The companion GitHub repository (https://github.com/Starscream11813/awesome-AI4Math) provides the curated reference list. No decontamination audit of the included records against each other is reported. The paper is an arXiv preprint (arXiv:2606.08728v1, 7 June 2026); not yet peer-reviewed, so all claims carry preprint-level confidence. Deployability Profile: THEORETICAL (survey/synthesis — no empirical system is proposed or evaluated). - Sound: The supervision-ladder is a descriptive/explanatory framework, not a formal theorem; its claims are well-supported by the cited evidence across seven generations of MWP systems. The CGV triad has concrete predictive content (e.g., why outcome-only RLVR produces fragile systems) that is consistent with cited empirical results. - General: The framework applies across all four axes reviewed and generalizes to Isabelle and Coq as well as Lean, as the authors note. It is scoped to mathematical reasoning and does not claim to extend to other verifiable-output domains without further argument. - Tight: No formal bounds; the framework is a structured narrative argument. Evidence quality is heterogeneous — some findings rest on rigorous controlled studies (SVAMP, GSM-SYMBOLIC, PRM800K ablations), others on provisional vendor reports. - Implementable: Yes — the framework directly informs practical system design choices (which verification mechanism to deploy, when multi-agent collaboration is cost-effective vs. when single-agent sampling with a verifier suffices, Section IV.H). The survey delivers no software artifact, dataset, or trained model. It delivers a structured literature map, a novel taxonomy, benchmark data compilation, and design-principle synthesis. Strengths - Genuine integrative breadth: The first survey to unify the MWP lineage (Sections III–IV), multimodal geometry (Section V), Lean-based formal proving (Section VI), and mathematical discovery (Section VII) in a single account through mid-2026; the positioning table (Table III) demonstrates this is a real gap, not a manufactured one. - Supervision-ladder framework: Table XXII pairs each era with its dominant external constraint and the bottleneck it resolved; this is concretely predictive (e.g., it explains why outcome-only RLVR produces benchmark-strong but perturbation-fragile systems) and is not post-hoc rationalization, as it makes testable predictions about where future bottlenecks will appear. - Benchmark ecosystem synthesis: Tables XV–XIX provide the most comprehensive structured catalog of mathematical reasoning datasets organized by function, including critical contamination analysis for AIME 2024 and the pass@1 / majority@k / pass@k distinction that is routinely conflated in the literature — a genuine methodological service to the community. - Honest treatment of failure modes: Section X directly addresses reward hacking, benchmark contamination, the October 2025 Erdős incident (where a vendor claim of ten solved open problems turned out to be literature lookups), multimodal shortcutting, and the energy/access cost of reasoning-scale inference, without softening the critique. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state: (1) frontier results are often first reported via technical reports rather than peer-reviewed venues, making complete model details, inference budgets, and tool-access conditions unavailable; (2) performance tables aggregate numbers under heterogeneous protocols and should be read as a structured map rather than a controlled leaderboard; (3) open-problem and discovery claims range from literature retrieval to fully formalized proofs, and only the latter provides a strong correctness certificate; (4) Lean 4 is emphasized because of current centrality, but the structural analysis applies mutatis mutandis to Isabelle/Coq. These are confronted directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No inter-rater reliability for coding: The SLR describes eligibility criteria and coding dimensions but reports no inter-rater agreement statistics. With 238 records coded across seven dimensions, coding consistency across the four-author team is unverified — a standard quality check for systematic reviews. - Vendor-number conflation in Tables XI/XIX: Despite explicit § flags, the compressed heatmap format in Table XIX places provisional live-leaderboard snapshots (e.g., AIME 2025 = 100.0% for Gemini 3 Pro and GPT-5.2 Pro) alongside rigorously replicated results at equal visual weight. A reader scanning the table will likely draw stronger conclusions than the text warrants. - Discovery section is structurally thin: Only 8 included records cover discovery. FUNSEARCH, ALPHAEVOLVE, and the Erdős workflows receive sound narrative coverage, but the section lacks a formal characterization of what makes an AI contribution to a mathematical problem "genuine" — the October 2025 incident shows this distinction is operationally non-trivial and the paper's proposed criteria (autonomous discovery, independent verification, Lean formalization) are stated but not operationalized into a scoring rubric. - Energy/access costs are quantified by a single data point: Section X.K raises compute cost and access inequality as serious concerns but provides only the $30,000/task ARC estimate for o3-high as quantitative evidence; a systematic table of FLOPs or inference-cost estimates across the reviewed frontier systems would have made this critique more actionable. - The convergence hypothesis lacks a testable operationalization: Section IX.E argues for and against the convergence of the four research axes but does not propose any experimental design that would settle the question — understandable for a survey, but the framing as an open research question would benefit from more precision about what evidence would constitute confirmation or refutation. Bottom Line A practitioner or researcher in AI reasoning, NLP, or formal methods gains real reference value from this survey — it is the most comprehensive single account of the field through mid-2026 and the supervision-ladder framework is a genuinely useful design lens — but preprint status and reliance on vendor-supplied frontier numbers mean the quantitative claims about the most recent systems should be treated as provisional until independent replication appears.
Search Results 2606.08728 PDF
#121 — Moderately Innovative (59)

BIDENT: Heterogeneous Operator-level Mapping for Efficient Edge Inference

H Kim, A Das, S Ghosh, A Raha, V Raghunathan · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces BIDENT, an operator-level orchestration framework that maps individual neural network operators to the most suitable processing unit (CPU, GPU, or NPU) on heterogeneous edge SoCs by casting the assignment problem as a shortest-path search over a weighted execution graph. - Why it's important: Current edge inference stacks assign entire models monolithically to a single PU, leaving order-of-magnitude latency and energy penalties on the table for emerging architectures (SSMs, KANs, VLA pipelines) whose operators have wildly divergent PU affinities. - How it does it: A profiler measures per-operator latency and energy on each PU offline; Dijkstra's algorithm then finds the optimal operator-to-PU mapping over a DAG whose edge weights encode both execution cost and PU-transition overhead, with separate graph formulations for sequential, intra-model parallel, and multi-model concurrent regimes. - The good: Across 190 multi-model pairs on a real Intel Core Ultra SoC, BIDENT achieves a 3.42× geometric mean speedup over homogeneous serial execution and 48.2% average energy reduction in concurrent settings, with individual pairs reaching 22.4×. - The missing: All experiments run on a single SoC generation (Intel Core Ultra Lunar Lake); no evidence that the profiling-derived schedules or their speedup magnitudes transfer to other SoC families (Qualcomm, Apple M-series, MediaTek), and no code or profiling data is released. - Read it or not: Read it if you build or research edge inference runtimes for heterogeneous SoCs — it provides a clean, implementable formulation with solid empirical grounding; skip it if your target is cloud/server inference or homogeneous hardware.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. The unified Dijkstra formulation subsuming sequential, intra-model parallel, and multi-model concurrent scheduling at operator granularity is intellectually clean (Novelty 60, Non-Obviousness 58), and evaluation breadth across 10 unusual architectures (KAN, Hyena, SNN, VLA) is genuine. Practical Impact (62) reflects a usable, implementable framework with strong concurrent-mode gains, but no code is released and gains are concentrated on emerging architectures. Rigor (52) and Resilience (56) are the limiting dimensions: no variance/error bars despite 200 repetitions, no ablation isolating the contention model, a single Intel Lunar Lake SoC, and a possibly-weak 'homogeneous serial' baseline inflating the 3.42× headline; it is an unreviewed preprint. Weighted: 0.15*60+0.15*52+0.10*58+0.15*58+0.15*56+0.30*62 = 59.
systems Novelty 60 Rigor 52 Non-Obv 58 Completeness 58 Resilience 56 Impact 62
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Edge SoCs integrate CPU, GPU, and NPU in a shared-memory package, yet frameworks such as OpenVINO, TensorRT, and ONNX Runtime compile and execute an entire model on a single chosen PU. This monolithic approach is increasingly costly as deployed architectures diversify: Hyena's FFT-based long convolutions and Mamba's selective-scan recurrences have no efficient NPU path, KAN's spline evaluations are control-heavy and CPU-bound, and VLA pipelines chain stages with radically different compute character. The mismatch causes non-GEMM operators to incur NPU latency penalties of 4.7–8.7× relative to CPU on tested hardware. Simultaneously, operators with no data dependencies between them could execute on separate idle PUs but are serialized instead. Existing heterogeneous engines address subsets of this — AxoNN and HaX-CoNN target specific model families or coarse scheduling granularities, HeteroLLM and Agent.xpu handle LLM-specific phase-level partitioning — leaving no model-agnostic, operator-granularity solution that also handles intra-model parallelism and multi-model concurrency. Key Idea: The central insight is that operator-to-PU assignment, intra-model parallelism across data-independent operators, and multi-model co-scheduling are all instances of the same shortest-path problem over a weighted directed acyclic graph, so a single Dijkstra-based search engine handles all three regimes by varying only the graph construction. PU-transition costs (H2D/D2H overhead, dispatch latency) are measured empirically and encoded as edge weights, avoiding approximation errors from analytical cost models. Methodology: BIDENT operates in three offline stages. (1) Profiler: decomposes each model into fused operators (using OpenVINO's PERF_COUNT), extracts each operator as a standalone sub-model, compiles it independently on each PU, and measures four cost components — H2D transfer, kernel dispatch, kernel execution, and D2H transfer — plus sustained package power. This runs once per model–SoC pair. (2) Execution graph builder: for sequential execution, creates a DAG with NK nodes (N operators × K PUs) and NK² edges; edge weights encode execution cost and PU-transition overhead; energy mode replaces latency weights with the product w×p. For intra-model parallelism, a topological traversal partitions the fused-operator DAG into phases (fork/join points as barriers) and branches (independent computation paths); contention is modeled as an empirically measured slowdown factor SF(P_run, P_interfere) applied per-operator. For multi-model concurrency, an aligned Dijkstra (same-model pairs, lockstep state = (step, d₀, d₁)) or joint (i,j) Dijkstra (mixed-model pairs, state = completed-operator-count per model) searches the product state space. (3) Search engine: Dijkstra runs in O(NK²log(NK)) time; for sequential models this is sub-millisecond. Critical design decisions: profiling at actual operand shapes captures size-dependent PU crossover without analytical assumptions; phase-boundary synchronization barriers trade some parallelism for correctness; co-execution costs for multi-model pairs are profiled directly (barrier-synchronized simultaneous measurement) rather than modeled parametrically. Key Results: All experiments are on a single Intel Core Ultra (Lunar Lake Series 2) with CPU (8 cores, 3.3 GHz), Intel Arc 140V GPU (64 Xe Vector Engines), and Intel AI Boost NPU 4 (6 compute tiles), 16 GB DDR5 unified memory. Ten model families are tested: ResNet-50, ViT-B/16, LLaMA-7B (1 decode layer), BitNet, Mamba-370M, Hyena, KAN, SNN-VGG9, LAVISH, and π0.5 VLA; 19 configurations (FP16 and INT8 where applicable). Baselines are the best-performing single PU per model. Sequential orchestration: 1.01–1.58× speedup across 19 configs, geometric mean 1.09×. Largest gains on emerging architectures: SNN-VGG9 FP16 1.58× (93 operators split across NPU/CPU/GPU), π0.5 1.23× (3,992 of 4,595 operators routed to NPU, 603 to CPU), Hyena FP16 1.16×. Conventional architectures (ResNet, ViT) gain only 1.04–1.10× because their operator mix is already nearly homogeneous. Standard Transformer decode (LLaMA, dominated by large MatMul) gains ≤1.02×. Energy-optimal sequential scheduling reduces energy by 9.2% average (vs. best single-PU baseline) with zero regressions, outperforming the latency-optimal schedule (3.7% average, 5 regressions out of 19). Intra-model parallel orchestration: up to 1.60× speedup (SNN-VGG9 FP16); ViT-B/16 gains +29% over sequential (1.55×) from 51 concurrent attention-head phases; π0.5 gains +16% (1.46×) from 615 concurrent phases; models with homogeneous or fully sequential operator chains (BitNet, KAN) gain nothing. Parallel energy reduction averages 7.6% vs. 9.2% sequential, a 1.6% gap attributed to measured memory-bandwidth contention. Multi-model concurrent orchestration: 3.42× geometric mean speedup across 190 unique pairs vs. homogeneous serial execution of both models; range 0.86–22.4×. Largest gains from complementary-affinity pairs: KAN_INT8 + LAVISH_FP16 = 22.4×; Mamba_FP16 + SNN_INT8 = 21.7×. Same-model pairs also benefit substantially (BitNet×BitNet = 14.1×, Mamba×Mamba = 10.5×). Only 2 of 190 pairs (1.1%) show speedup below 1× due to memory-bandwidth contention. Concurrent energy-optimal scheduling achieves 48.2% average energy reduction. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL, NEW METHOD. The genuine novelty is the unified graph-search formulation that subsumes sequential heterogeneous mapping, intra-model parallel execution, and multi-model concurrent scheduling within a single Dijkstra framework — no prior system combines all three at operator granularity in a model-agnostic way. The use of Dijkstra for operator-to-PU assignment is a clean reduction of a known combinatorial problem; the empirical contention modeling and the joint (i,j) state-space search for mixed-model concurrent scheduling are incremental extensions. The framework's model-agnosticism — handling CNNs, SSMs, KANs, spiking networks, and VLA pipelines with the same algorithm — is a practical contribution beyond prior model-specific approaches. Rigor & Reproducibility: Profiling methodology is detailed (20 warm-up, 200 measurement iterations; VTune for H2D/D2H; ETW/xperf for dispatch overhead; HWInfo64 for power). No error bars or variance statistics are reported for any measured latency or speedup numbers, making it impossible to assess whether 1.04× differs meaningfully from 1.00×. No ablations isolate the contribution of the contention model versus ignoring contention. The evaluation uses only one hardware platform (Intel Core Ultra Lunar Lake) and one inference backend (OpenVINO 2025.3); generalizability to other SoCs or backends is claimed but not demonstrated. No code, profiling tables, or model configurations are publicly released — reproducibility requires access to the same SoC and inference stack. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2606.05271v1, June 2026); results are not peer reviewed. The multi-model concurrent baseline is "homogeneous serial execution" (running both models sequentially on their best individual PU) rather than, e.g., a time-multiplexed scheduler on the GPU alone, which would be a stronger real-world baseline for the 3.42× headline number. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS — BIDENT is a built orchestration framework evaluated by deployment on real hardware. - Practical: BIDENT targets Intel Core Ultra SoCs with OpenVINO 2025.3; it runs on commercially available hardware (Intel Lunar Lake laptops), but the profiling pipeline requires VTune and ETW/xperf tooling available only on Windows and Intel platforms. - Efficient: Graph construction takes O(NK²) time and memory; for typical N=10–500, K=3 this is sub-millisecond; profiling is a one-time offline cost per model–SoC pair, but it scales quadratically in the number of model pairs for concurrent scheduling (all 19×19 = 361 combinations must be profiled for the full multi-model evaluation). - Effective: The framework demonstrably reduces latency and energy on the tested hardware; the evidence is strongest for architectures with heterogeneous operator mixes (SSMs, VLAs, spiking networks) and weaker for homogeneous ones (Transformers, CNNs). - Scalable: No evaluation beyond the Intel Core Ultra platform; behavior on SoCs with different PU counts, dispatch overheads, or memory bandwidths is untested; the static mapping also does not adapt to runtime variability (thermal throttling, competing workloads), an acknowledged limitation. BIDENT delivers a software framework (not released publicly) and a scheduling algorithm that could be integrated into existing inference backends; no trained models or datasets are produced. Strengths: - The unification of sequential, intra-model parallel, and multi-model concurrent scheduling into a single shortest-path formulation is intellectually clean and practically useful — reducing what could be three separate scheduling systems to one. - Empirical motivating observations (Figures 2–4) are carefully constructed: per-operator latency across all three PUs at actual model operand shapes, with crossover as a function of operand size, provide honest grounding for the approach. - Energy-optimal scheduling via edge-weight substitution (w×p instead of w) is a principled and zero-overhead extension of the same framework, and the finding that energy-optimal and latency-optimal schedules diverge substantially (9.2% vs. 3.7% average energy reduction, with the latency objective creating 5 energy regressions) is an actionable insight for system designers. - Evaluation breadth is genuine: 10 diverse model families including unusual architectures (KAN, Hyena, SNN, π0.5 VLA) that are rarely covered together, revealing that BIDENT's gains are concentrated precisely where prior art is weakest. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly acknowledge that the current mapping is static and offline, making BIDENT unable to handle dynamic workloads (agentic LLM invocations, thermal throttling, runtime contention shifts). They note that profiling once per model–SoC pair means re-profiling is needed on new hardware or after SoC generation changes. They acknowledge underutilization bubbles at phase boundaries and the possibility of intra-PU tile-level optimization (NPU has 6 compute tiles; current BIDENT treats each PU as a monolithic resource). These are confronted directly and honestly in the Future Work section. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - Single SoC vendor lock-in: all results are on one Intel Core Ultra Lunar Lake board. The profiling tables and scheduling decisions are hardware-specific; the paper claims model-agnosticism but not hardware-agnosticism. Transferability to Qualcomm Snapdragon X Elite, Apple M-series, or MediaTek Dimensity (which have very different NPU architectures and dispatch overheads) is entirely undemonstrated. - The 3.42× multi-model concurrent headline is measured against "homogeneous serial execution" — running both models sequentially on their best single PU. A fairer baseline would be time-multiplexed GPU-only or NPU-only execution of both models, which is what most current frameworks actually do. The gap between this baseline and "run both on the same single PU simultaneously" is not reported. - No variance or confidence intervals on any measurement. With 200 inference repetitions, variance estimates would be trivial to include; their absence makes it impossible to assess significance of small speedups (e.g., 1.01× for LLaMA INT8). - LLaMA-7B is profiled as a single decode layer, not the full model. A single layer has 13 fused operators; the full 32-layer model has different memory-bandwidth dynamics and different opportunities for inter-layer heterogeneous scheduling. - No ablation that isolates the contention model's contribution: removing the SF correction factor and measuring the resulting prediction error would validate whether the contention model actually improves scheduling decisions or merely adjusts cost estimates. - No public code or profiling tables are released, and the tool depends on Intel VTune + ETW + xperf on Windows 11 — reproducibility is gated by platform and tooling access. Bottom Line: BIDENT makes a useful engineering contribution — a clean, implementable graph-search framework for operator-level heterogeneous scheduling — and the concurrent multi-model results are genuinely compelling for emerging architectures, but the work's impact is currently limited to a single Intel SoC platform with no public code release, modest sequential gains for standard Transformer workloads, and missing variance statistics; practitioners targeting Intel Core Ultra edge deployments with diverse model mixes will find it directly actionable, others should wait for broader hardware validation.
Search Results 2606.05271 PDF
#122 — Moderately Innovative (59)

Mind the Gap: Can Frontier LLMs Pass a Standardized Office Proficiency Exam ?

T Lv, D Zhang, J Ding, Y Jia, Y Zhao, Y Huang, W Wu… · arxiv.org · automated generated environments for agent testing - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces OFFICEEVAL, a benchmark of 200 practical Office-automation tasks derived from China's National Computer Rank Examination (NCRE), scored against 7,118 machine-gradable criteria across Word, Excel, and PowerPoint, and reports evaluations of 7 frontier LLMs under single-turn and agentic settings. - Why it's important: Knowledge workers spend the majority of their time in Office software, yet existing agent benchmarks rely on synthetic tasks, narrow single-application slices, or LLM-as-judge scoring that cannot capture the long-horizon, parameter-precise nature of real professional Office work. - How it does it: Tasks and rubrics are lifted directly from the NCRE certification exam, scored deterministically by an Open XML SDK / COM-based evaluation engine, with models producing Python code rather than interacting with the GUI. - The good: The best autonomous coding agent (Codex/GPT-5.5) reaches 68.8% Score Rate (SR), and criterion-level error taxonomy reveals that after execution failures are repaired, 97.4% of remaining loss is implementation-knowledge errors (wrong OOXML property paths, enumeration constants, color encodings), giving concrete direction for future improvements. - The missing: The single-turn vs. agentic comparison conflates execution feedback, repair budget, scaffolding, and COM tool access simultaneously — no ablation isolates which factor drives the 32pp gain, making it impossible to determine where effort should be concentrated. - Read it or not: Researchers building or evaluating Office automation agents should read this for its fine-grained error taxonomy; practitioners expecting a reproducible benchmark should note that the underlying NCRE materials are copyrighted and not redistributed.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (60) and Completeness (65) are the strengths — anchoring an Office-automation benchmark to a nationally administered, expert-calibrated certification (NCRE) with 7,118 deterministic criterion-level signals is a real measurement contribution, and the error taxonomy isolating implementation-knowledge errors (OOXML paths, enumeration constants) is actionable, with honest limitation handling. Impact (45) and Resilience (58) pull it down: the benchmark is copyrighted and not redistributed (no public reproducibility), it runs only on Windows+Office, no contamination analysis is done despite NCRE plausibly being in pretraining, and the single-turn vs. agentic comparison conflates feedback, repair budget, scaffolding, and tool access. Rigor (62) is helped by 3-run means and significance tests.
empirical Novelty 60 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 55 Completeness 65 Resilience 58 Impact 45
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Organizations deploying LLM agents as "digital workers" need assurance that these systems can handle complex, multi-step document operations in real productivity software. Existing Office benchmarks evaluate either isolated applications (spreadsheet formulas, slide layout) or use workflow-level binary pass/fail rather than criterion-level partial scoring. None are anchored to an externally validated human certification, making it impossible to compare model capability against human proficiency thresholds. The result is an untested capability gap: agents may excel at code synthesis but fail at the fine-grained semantic operations — exact style names, color hex values, animation enumeration constants — that constitute real Office proficiency. Key Idea: Anchoring a benchmark to the NCRE certification exam, which was designed by domain experts, calibrated for difficulty over decades, and scored by deterministic rubrics, provides an externally validated difficulty scale and a human reference point (the 60-point passing threshold and 95.5% community reference score) that synthetic or crowdsourced benchmarks cannot offer. The key insight is that the NCRE rubrics are already machine-readable XML scoring configurations, so evaluation can be fully deterministic rather than requiring LLM or human judges. Methodology: OFFICEEVAL extracts the practical-operation sections (Word, Excel, PowerPoint) of NCRE Level 1 (foundational) and Level 2 (advanced) exams, yielding 200 tasks and 7,118 scoring criteria. Each task consists of an input document, natural-language instructions (often including reference images for visual formatting targets), and a machine-readable XML scoring configuration specifying criterion type, expected value, comparison operator, and point allocation. The evaluation engine parses ISO/IEC 29500 Open XML structures using the Open XML SDK (.NET, Windows) and invokes COM automation for a subset of checks requiring Office rendering. Two agent paradigms are compared: (1) a single-turn baseline that provides task instructions, document screenshots, and input file path in a single API call, expecting executable Python (python-docx/openpyxl/python-pptx) in return; and (2) autonomous coding agents (Claude Code and Codex) that receive only the raw exam RTF and input documents, with no turn limit, one-hour timeout, and unrestricted tool access including win32com COM automation. Results are averaged over 3 independent runs at temperature=1.0. Critical design decisions: using code generation rather than GUI interaction (scalable but misses visual affordances); restricting single-turn agents to three standard Python libraries (introduces a ceiling unrelated to model knowledge); and not separating the multiple confounds in the agentic setting (acknowledged but not resolved). Key Results: On the 200-task extracted practical subset, the best single-turn model (Claude Opus 4.7) achieves 36.6% SR — well below the 60-point reference threshold and the 95.5% community reference score. The top two single-turn models (Claude Opus 4.7: 36.6%, GPT-5.5: 36.2%) are statistically indistinguishable (p=0.82; bootstrap 95% CI for difference: [−3.5, 4.4]pp). The weakest model (Grok-4.1-fast) reaches 2.8% with 2.2% execution success rate. Autonomous coding agents raise performance substantially: Claude Code achieves 53.0% and Codex achieves 68.8%, with code execution success rising from 57–62% (single-turn) to 98–99% (agentic). Despite these gains, neither agent surpasses the community reference. Per-skill analysis shows Tables (59.1% for Claude Opus 4.7), Charts (51.4% for GPT-5.5), and Data & Formulas (48.5% for GPT-5.5) are strongest, while Animation (26.7%) and Graphics & Media (29.1%) are weakest. In the coding-agent error taxonomy, 97.4% of non-crash weighted loss is labeled implementation knowledge, with OOXML property paths (25.8%), enumeration constants (22.4%), and color/theme encodings (14.8%) as the dominant subtypes. Cross-language analysis shows the strongest models (Claude Opus 4.7: −1.9pp; GPT-5.5: +3.8pp) are nearly unaffected by switching from Chinese to English instructions, while mid-tier models (Gemini: +6.1pp, Qwen3.5: +8.8pp) benefit substantially, and all PowerPoint scores improve 10–17pp in English due to alignment of animation and style constant names. Novelty Classification: NEW BENCHMARK/DATASET and NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The genuine novelty is the use of a nationally administered, expert-calibrated human certification as a benchmark anchor, combined with deterministic criterion-level scoring at scale across all three major Office applications. The experimental pipeline (code generation against document files) and the models evaluated are standard; the contribution is the rigorous measurement instrument and the resulting diagnosis of where frontier models fail. Rigor & Reproducibility: Results are means of 3 independent runs with standard deviations reported (0.4–2.1pp on overall SR) and paired significance tests for headline comparisons. The per-skill and per-criterion error taxonomies are detailed and actionable. However, the error taxonomy for Codex relies on automated labeling (scorer errors and final outputs) rather than manual audit, which the authors acknowledge may over-count implementation-knowledge errors and under-count library limitations. Critical reproducibility limitation: the NCRE tasks, input documents, and scoring configurations are copyrighted by the National Education Examinations Authority and are not redistributed; independent replication requires separately obtaining the same materials. The evaluation engine requires Windows with Microsoft Office installed, further limiting accessibility. No trained models or datasets are released. The paper provides prompts, environment specs, and criterion-level aggregate statistics, but the benchmark itself is not publicly available in the conventional sense. No training data contamination analysis is performed despite NCRE materials plausibly appearing in pretraining corpora of frontier models. Deployability (EMPIRICAL): - Practical: The evaluation engine runs only on Windows with Microsoft Office installed, and the coding-agent setting additionally requires win32com; Linux or macOS deployment is not supported. - Efficient: Autonomous agents consume up to 133 turns and one hour per task; at 200 tasks, evaluation of a single agent requires roughly 200 wall-clock hours — a substantial cost even if parallelized. - Effective: The benchmark demonstrates that frontier code-generating LLMs and agents cannot reliably perform professional-grade Office automation, and the criterion-level error taxonomy identifies specific knowledge gaps (OOXML property paths, enumeration constants) that are actionable for improvement. - Scalable: Extension of the benchmark is constrained by copyright; the evaluation pipeline scales in principle but the underlying task supply does not. The paper does not test agent behavior as task complexity scales beyond the NCRE Level 2 ceiling. The work delivers a paper-form methodology and analysis with no public dataset, benchmark release, or tool artifact beyond the prompts and evaluation statistics documented in the appendix. Strengths: - Using a nationally administered human certification as a benchmark anchor provides externally calibrated difficulty and a meaningful human reference point — a structural advantage over synthetic or crowdsourced task collections. - The 7,118 criterion-level deterministic scoring signals, distributed across 8 skill categories, enable fine-grained capability diagnosis rather than a single binary pass/fail or aggregate score. - The criterion-level error taxonomy cleanly separates execution crashes from implementation-knowledge errors, revealing that the bottleneck after agent repair is not comprehension failure but low-level representation mismatch — a concrete and actionable finding. - The cross-language ablation (Section 7) usefully distinguishes language comprehension as a secondary bottleneck for mid-tier models from Office-specific implementation knowledge as the primary bottleneck for strong models. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: - English experiment uses translated NCRE tasks rather than a natively English certification (e.g., MOS), so font-name substitutions and style-constant mappings may introduce confounds. - Only code-generation agents are evaluated; GUI-based agents with visual feedback may perform differently, particularly on layout-heavy and visual-property tasks. - The single-turn vs. coding-agent comparison changes execution feedback, repair budget, scaffolding, and COM access simultaneously; isolating these factors remains future work. - Community reference solutions (95.5%) are not a formal human baseline and may contain errors. - NCRE materials may appear in public training data, with contamination effects of unknown direction and magnitude. - The benchmark is not redistributed, requiring independent acquisition for replication. The authors confront these limitations directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The Grok-4.1-fast result (2.8% SR, 2.2% Exec%) is anomalously low for a frontier model — lower than open-weight models like Kimi-K2.6 (9.4%) — and the paper does not investigate whether this reflects a capability failure, an API configuration issue, or a model that was not fine-tuned for code generation tasks of this format. - The community reference (95.5%) is used as both a sanity check and an implicit human performance ceiling, but it represents informal exam-prep solutions with no time constraint or controlled conditions. A proper human baseline under NCRE exam conditions would strengthen the framing. - No analysis of how performance varies with the number of criteria per task or with specific criterion types within each skill category; per-skill averages obscure whether models fail uniformly or concentrate errors on a small fraction of high-weight criteria. - Iterative regression (coding agents scoring worse than single-turn on 23 tasks / 11.5%) is noted but not characterized: the paper does not reveal which skill categories or task types are most susceptible to this failure mode. - The decision to constrain single-turn agents to three standard Python libraries (python-docx, openpyxl, python-pptx) introduces a ceiling that is an artifact of the evaluation protocol, not model capability — COM access is available to humans but withheld from single-turn models, making the gap between single-turn and agentic results partly an ablation of tool access rather than agent architecture. Bottom Line: A practitioner building Office automation agents will find the criterion-level error taxonomy — identifying OOXML property paths, enumeration constants, and color encodings as the dominant failure modes after execution crashes are fixed — immediately useful for targeting improvements, but the benchmark itself is not publicly reproducible and the single-turn vs. agentic comparison conflates too many variables to guide architectural decisions.
Search Results 2606.10956 PDF
#123 — Moderately Innovative (59)

NetKV: Network-Aware Decode Instance Selection for Disaggregated LLM Inference

MA Ojewale · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces NetKV, an O(|D|) per-request greedy scheduling algorithm and a thin operator-to-scheduler "network cost oracle" interface that incorporates topology tier and dynamic congestion signals into decode-instance selection for disaggregated LLM inference. - Why it's important: In disaggregated inference, KV cache transfers dominate TTFT (a 128K-context Llama-3-70B request generates ~40 GB of KV data, taking ~3.2 s per shard pair on 25 Gbps RDMA), yet every production scheduler (Mooncake, Dynamo, llm-d, DistServe) ignores network topology and congestion entirely when routing these transfers. - How it does it: A lightweight oracle periodically sends the scheduler four maps (static tier topology, tier bandwidth, tier latency, and dynamic congestion per tier); the scheduler uses them to score each candidate decode instance by the composite cost Ttransfer + Tqueue + Tdecode, then selects the minimum. - The good: Ablation shows the static tier map alone captures >90% of the gain with zero new dynamic telemetry infrastructure, making a minimal deployment immediately viable. - The missing: All experiments run on an author-built discrete-event simulator; packet-level and flow-level models already diverge by 4–6 percentage points on the headline metric, and real-cluster validation is explicitly deferred to future work. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners building or extending disaggregated LLM inference schedulers (vLLM, Dynamo, llm-d); the conceptual contribution and formal analysis are solid, but the simulation-only evaluation limits confidence in the absolute numbers.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Novelty (70) and Non-Obviousness (72) are the strengths: network-topology- and congestion-aware per-request KV routing in disaggregated inference is a genuinely new problem framing, and the formal results (suboptimality grows linearly in context length; explicit staleness-tolerance bound) plus the counter-intuitive ablation finding that static topology alone captures >90% of the gain are non-obvious. Rigor (50) and Impact (50) hold the composite down: the entire evaluation rests on an author-built simulator with no real-cluster validation, the flow-vs-packet model diverges 4-6 pp on the headline metric, no code is released, and it is a single-author preprint — and the contribution is delivered as a paper spec only, with no software artifact despite a credible plugin path. Resilience (58) and Completeness (65) are aided by an unusually thorough, honest limitations section. Composite: 0.15*70 + 0.15*50 + 0.10*72 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*58 + 0.30*50 = 59.
systems Novelty 70 Rigor 50 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 65 Resilience 58 Impact 50
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Disaggregated LLM inference splits prefill and decode onto separate GPU pools, requiring the KV cache to traverse the datacenter network before decoding can begin. For long-context requests this transfer dominates TTFT: a 128K-context Llama-3-70B request under TP=4 sharding produces ~10 GB per GPU pair, requiring ~3.2 s on a 25 Gbps RDMA link. Production schedulers route KV transfers using only compute load and prefix-cache locality; none incorporates network topology or dynamic congestion. This creates a fundamental information asymmetry: the inference scheduler has no visibility into physical topology or link utilization, while the cluster operator has detailed network telemetry but no knowledge of upcoming KV transfers or their latency sensitivity. The consequence, proved formally in the paper, is that cache-aware-only scheduling is arbitrarily suboptimal as context length grows—because the KV cache size scales linearly with input length while queuing terms remain bounded. Key Idea The single core insight is that the three-way tradeoff among cache locality, compute load, and network transfer cost can be exposed to the scheduler through a minimally invasive abstraction—a per-tier bandwidth and congestion oracle—without requiring any changes to the transport layer, inference engine, or network hardware. Once a candidate decode instance's tier is known, computing the expected transfer time is two multiplications and one division; the prefix-cache lookup is already performed by existing cache-aware schedulers. The oracle separates external congestion (from operator telemetry) from self-contention (tracked by the scheduler's own in-flight counters) to avoid double-counting and to remain useful even when the operator cannot isolate KV flows from background traffic. Methodology The system model targets a four-tier fat-tree cluster (Tier 0: NVLink 100–600 GB/s; Tier 1: ToR RoCE 25–100 Gbps; Tier 2: pod-level 10–25 Gbps; Tier 3: cross-pod 5–12 Gbps). KV cache size is deterministic from model parameters: 320 KB/token aggregate for Llama-3-70B FP16, or 80 KB/token per TP=4 shard. Effective bandwidth is modeled as Beff(p,d) = Bτ(1 − cτ) / (1 + n_inflight^τ(p)), composing a residual-bandwidth approximation for external congestion with a max-min fair-share model for the scheduler's own concurrent flows. Algorithm 1 filters to memory-feasible instances, computes composite cost for each candidate, and selects argmin; the in-flight counter is incremented on dispatch and decremented via the engine's existing transfer-complete callback (KVConnectorBase_V1.get_finished in vLLM). Two propositions are proved: Proposition 1 gives a closed-form condition under which a cache-aware scheduler doubles transfer time relative to a network-aware one, with the gap widening linearly in context length; Proposition 2 bounds the staleness tolerance for tier-order preservation as ε < (Bτ(1−cτ) − Bτ'(1−cτ')) / (Bτ + Bτ'), yielding 42 percentage points for a 4:1 oversubscribed fabric under moderate congestion. Evaluation uses a discrete-event, flow-level simulator modeling 64 H100-class GPUs (4 prefill + 12 decode instances at TP=4) driven by Mooncake production traces (23K requests). Inference timing is fitted as piecewise-linear functions from DistServe, vLLM v0.6 benchmarks, and MLPerf v5.0 (within 25% of published numbers, biased conservatively). Five baselines: round-robin, load-aware, cache-aware, tuned cache+load-aware (CLA, grid-searched weights on a held-out trace window), and NetKV-Static (adds static tier map and self-contention to CLA but withholds live congestion). Three critical design decisions: (1) per-tier congestion aggregation rather than per-link, justified as exact for Tier 0/1 and approximate for Tier 2/3 under ECMP; (2) serial transfer-then-decode cost model as a conservative upper bound; (3) separate tracking of external congestion and self-contention to avoid double-counting when operator telemetry cannot isolate KV flows. Key Results Experiment 1 (load sweep, RAG profile): NetKV-Full reduces mean TTFT by 18.9% at 100% load and 21.2% at 200% load over round-robin, and 11.8% / 14.3% over tuned CLA. SLO attainment lifts 2.1–3.7 pp. TBT overhead is +0.23 ms at 100% load rising to +0.45 ms at 250%, well below interactive SLO thresholds. Experiment 2 (context length, RAG): Peak TTFT reduction of 20.2% over RR and 17.6% over CLA at 16K tokens, with +20.1 pp SLO attainment (0.791 → 0.992); gains collapse for all schedulers uniformly above 16K as the workload exceeds simulated capacity. Experiment 3 (topology stress): At 8:1 oversubscription and 40% background traffic, NetKV-Full cuts mean TTFT by 15.9% over CLA on RAG; at 1:1 / 0%, only 3.7%; NetKV-Full wins in all 60 topology × workload cells. Experiment 4 (oracle staleness): All three metrics are invariant from 100 ms to 60 s refresh intervals, confirming Proposition 2. Experiment 6 (ablation): Static tier map alone contributes 10.2% (RAG) and 11.2% (long-context) TTFT reduction; self-contention adds 1.9–2.8%; dynamic congestion adds 0.2–0.3%. Experiment 7 (scalability): Packet-level simulation (ground truth) at 64 and 128 GPUs yields 7.0–7.4% TTFT reduction; flow-level model reports 11.0–13.6% for the same configurations, overestimating by 4–6 pp. Transfer time under CLA rises from 559 ms at 64 GPUs to 851 ms at 128+ GPUs as cross-pod routing fraction grows; NetKV-Full holds transfer time at ~451 ms (packet) / ~603 ms (flow) across all scales. The flow-level gap plateaus at −13.6% from 128 GPUs upward because the per-tier abstraction saturates. Scheduler decision latency stays below 1.5 ms even at 1024 GPUs. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The network cost oracle interface and the NetKV scheduling algorithm are the primary contributions; the formal analysis of staleness tolerance and scheduling suboptimality is secondary. Genuinely novel is the application of network topology and dynamic congestion awareness to per-request KV cache routing in disaggregated LLM inference—no prior system addresses this combination. The individual components (tier-based bandwidth modeling, max-min fair sharing, greedy scheduling, residual-bandwidth congestion approximation) are standard building blocks applied to a new problem formulation. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and well-structured (four-step additive ladder isolating static topology, self-contention, and dynamic congestion). Five seeds per experiment; standard deviations reported throughout. Statistical significance is tested (p < 10^-4, two-sample t-test, 20-seed chatbot run).
