No code, simulator, or model weights are publicly released; no repository URL is mentioned. Two major methodological concerns: (1) the entire evaluation rests on an author-built flow-level simulator with no external validation—the simulator is verified only analytically and against a separate author-built packet-level simulator, which itself diverges from the flow-level model by 4–6 pp on the headline metric at matched configurations; (2) the inference timing model is fitted from published third-party benchmarks rather than measured on the evaluation target, introducing a 25% uncertainty that the paper acknowledges but cannot fully bound. The CLA* baseline is credibly tuned (10×10 grid search, held-out trace window, robustness to neighborhood perturbation verified), which is a credibility positive. Deployability (EMPIRICAL/SYSTEMS) - Practical: Deployable as a scoring plugin to llm-d's Endpoint Picker and Dynamo's KV router with no transport, engine, or hardware changes; requires only Kubernetes topology labels (already present in most clusters) and a lightweight SNMP/sFlow aggregation service for the dynamic congestion signal. - Efficient: O(|D|) per scheduling decision with |Dr| ≤ 12 in the 64-GPU evaluation; decision latency stays below 1.5 ms at 1024 GPUs; oracle can refresh once per minute without degrading quality, making telemetry burden negligible. - Effective: Simulation across 60 topology/workload/stress configurations consistently favors NetKV-Full, with well-isolated ablations; real-cluster evidence is absent and packet-level ground truth is limited to 64 and 128 GPUs, so effectiveness on production hardware is unconfirmed. - Scalable: The flow-level estimator suggests gains plateau from 128 GPUs upward at around −13.6% over CLA*, but this plateau is an artifact of the simulator's per-tier abstraction saturating rather than evidence of stable real-world scaling; packet-level results are available only to 128 GPUs, and gains at TP=8 (the more common production configuration) are untested. The paper delivers an algorithm specification and interface definition in paper form only; no software artifact is released. Strengths - The network cost oracle is an elegant, minimally invasive abstraction: it requires no protocol changes and gracefully degrades—a static tier map alone (zero new dynamic telemetry) captures >90% of the gain, enabling a useful first deployment at near-zero operational cost. - Propositions 1 and 2 provide non-obvious, quantitatively precise results: Proposition 1 shows the suboptimality gap grows linearly in context length (not just exists), and Proposition 2 yields an explicit 42 pp staleness tolerance for a 4:1 oversubscribed fabric that directly informs how rarely the oracle needs to refresh. - The ablation study's surprising finding—that the dynamic congestion signal contributes only 0.2–0.3% while static topology contributes 10–11%—is counterintuitive and practically important; it reframes the contribution from "real-time network monitoring" to "topology-aware placement," a much lower bar for deployment. - The tier-shifting mechanism analysis (Table VI: CLA* routes 68% of transfers cross-pod, NetKV reverses to 69% intra-pod) provides a clear, verifiable mechanistic explanation for where the latency reduction comes from, which is more convincing than aggregate metric comparisons alone. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors confront limitations directly and thoroughly in §VII.C, which is a credibility positive. They acknowledge: (1) no real-cluster validation; NIC-level effects and kernel-bypass overheads are not modeled; (2) inference timing model is fitted from published data, not measured on the target; (3) serial transfer model overestimates visible Ttransfer in production pipelining systems; (4) per-tier congestion aggregation approximates Tier 2/3 under ECMP, potentially understating P99 advantage; (5) CLA* baseline does not model Mooncake's KV replication across tiers; (6) per-request greedy does not jointly optimize across concurrent requests; (7) gains shrink for INT8/block-quantized KV caches; (8) TP=8 behavior is uncharacterized. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The scalability experiment (Experiment 7) treats the author-built packet-level simulator as ground truth for validating the author-built flow-level estimator. There is no external anchor; the 4–6 pp divergence between the two models is acknowledged but not explained, and the direction of the error (flow-level overestimates) suggests the headline gains could be materially lower on real hardware. - The flow-level plateau from 128 GPUs onward (Table V: −13.6% at 128, 256, 512, and 1024 GPUs, identically) is almost certainly a model saturation artifact—once all cross-pod transfers under CLA* are already cross-pod, the simulator has no mechanism to differentiate further. This means the scalability claims above 128 GPUs are uninformative rather than confirmatory. - The oracle interface's congestion separation (excluding KV flows from operator telemetry) assumes DSCP marking or a dedicated QoS queue. This is a non-trivial operational assumption evaluated only in passing; clusters without QoS support would fall back to n_inflight ≡ 0, which the paper permits but whose performance impact is not separately measured. - At TP=8 (the dominant production configuration for 70B-class models), the decode instance pool is sparser—8 instances versus 12 at TP=4—which changes both the tier distribution and the cache hit probability; no data exists for this regime despite it being flagged as a known gap. - This is a single-author arXiv preprint (not peer-reviewed), and both simulators were built by the same author for this paper. The absence of an independent replication or a real testbed result makes the quantitative claims harder to assess at face value. Bottom Line A practitioner designing or extending a disaggregated LLM inference scheduler will find the oracle interface, the formal suboptimality proof, and the ablation-driven insight that static topology alone suffices for most of the gain to be immediately actionable—but should treat the quantitative TTFT numbers as preliminary estimates pending real-cluster validation.
Search Results 2606.03910 PDF
#124 — Moderately Innovative (58)

Beyond Greedy Chunking: SLO-Aware Sliding-Window Scheduling for LLM Inference

Y Chen, Y Zhang, X Mo, W Wu, J Li · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SlidingServe, an LLM inference scheduler that jointly optimizes chunk-size allocation across two consecutive iterations and uses DP-based batch reconstruction to reduce SLO violations, achieving up to 30% higher goodput over the prior SOTA (QoServe) while cutting SLO violation rates by 16%–53% under heavy load. - Why it's important: Production LLM serving must simultaneously meet TTFT and TBT deadlines for every request; greedy single-step chunked-prefill schedulers make locally optimal but globally suboptimal decisions that cascade into deadline violations under realistic mixed workloads. - How it does it: A lightweight linear batch-latency predictor enables a ternary-search sliding window over two consecutive iterations to find the optimal token-budget split, while a 0/1 knapsack (BatchConstructor) selects which prefill requests actually execute when TTFT violations are predicted. - The good: The component-level ablation is clean and honest—SlidingChunker contributes 16.7% of throughput gain, Multi-Level Priority Sorter 5.7%, and BatchConstructor 5.4%—confirming that the sliding-window insight is the dominant driver. - The missing: The system is evaluated exclusively on 7–8B models on 2×RTX 3090 consumer GPUs; no data exists for larger models, higher tensor-parallelism, or modern datacenter accelerators, and the per-iteration scheduling overhead is never quantified. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners building or evaluating LLM serving schedulers who care about chunked-prefill SLO compliance; the DP batch-construction idea is directly implementable and the ablation justifies each design choice.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. The cross-iteration sliding-window budget split is a genuine methodological contribution over greedy chunked-prefill schedulers (Novelty 58, Non-Obviousness 60), and the clean component ablation correctly attributes most gain to SlidingChunker. Rigor (58) is the main limiter: single-run results with no error bars, no public code or weights, and evaluation confined to 7–8B models on 2×RTX 3090, leaving the claimed generalizability to larger models/TP unsupported. Practical Impact (58) is solid as a vLLM-layer scheduler beating current SOTA (QoServe, ASPLOS 2026) but no artifact is released and per-iteration overhead is never quantified. Weighted: 0.15*58 + 0.15*58 + 0.10*60 + 0.15*60 + 0.15*55 + 0.30*58 = 58.
systems Novelty 58 Rigor 58 Non-Obv 60 Completeness 60 Resilience 55 Impact 58
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Online LLM serving must satisfy two latency SLOs simultaneously: TTFT (time from request arrival to first output token) and TBT (time between successive output tokens). Chunked prefill—splitting long prompts into smaller chunks interleaved with decode requests—improves throughput but introduces a critical scheduling decision: what chunk size to use each iteration. Greedy schedulers that maximize the chunk size subject to the current iteration's TBT deadline ignore the non-linear relationship between batch composition and execution time. Using the full slack in iteration t can leave only a tiny budget for iteration t+1, forcing the system into inefficient decode-heavy batches and increasing net latency. A second gap exists at the batch level: even when the global budget is feasible, individual requests sharing a batch must wait for the entire batch to complete; if batch execution time exceeds a request's remaining TTFT slack, that request violates SLO even though it was scheduled. No existing system (Sarathi-Serve, QoServe) addresses both problems simultaneously. Key Idea The core insight is that the per-iteration chunk-size constraint is a feasibility ceiling, not a target: setting chunk size to exactly the TBT slack of the most urgent decode request is locally optimal but globally suboptimal because batch execution time is a nonlinear, convex-ish function of token count. By treating two consecutive iterations as a joint optimization window and splitting a fixed total token budget between them to minimize predicted total execution time (via ternary search), SlidingChunker avoids the greedy trap. The secondary insight—that batch atomicity on GPUs means scheduled requests can still violate TTFT—motivates dropping the constraint that all candidate requests must execute, replacing it with a DP knapsack that selects the highest-value feasible subset. Methodology SlidingServe is implemented as a scheduler layer on top of vLLM and operates in a closed loop with four modules. (1) Batch Latency Predictor: a scene-expert linear model with 7 engineered features capturing prefill attention complexity (Σ c_i(u_i+c_i)), self-attention intensity (Σ c_i²), total KV context, decode request count, decode context length, total prefill tokens, and max single-request prefill tokens. Three separate models handle pure-decode, pure-prefill, and mixed scenarios; trained offline with online incremental hot-swap updates. (2) Multi-Level Priority Sorter: sorts prefill and waiting requests by a lexicographic key (safeguard flag, urgency indicator u_i(t) = remaining_tokens / (throughput × TTFT_slack), remaining workload), ensuring protected and urgent requests are served first while completing short near-done requests early. (3) SlidingChunker: given per-decode-request TBT slack, computes T_cur (most urgent decode deadline) and T_next (next-iteration deadline with TBT replenishment), then uses ternary search over a discretized budget space to find B* = argmin_b [T̂(b) + T̂(B_Σ - b)], where B_Σ = B_cur + B_next. (4) BatchConstructor: when the predicted full-batch execution time exceeds any prefill request's TTFT slack, it iterates over risky requests as anchors, converts each anchor's slack to a token capacity via inverse predictor lookup, and solves a 0/1 knapsack to maximize scheduled requests subject to the capacity constraint. Design decisions: (a) using a linear predictor rather than a neural one is well-justified by the need for sub-millisecond inference latency; (b) ternary search for a unimodal objective is appropriate given the convex-like latency curve but the paper does not prove unimodality; (c) fixing the window size to 2 is a pragmatic choice with no ablation justifying it over larger horizons. Key Results Evaluated on Llama3-8B and Qwen2.5-7B, both on 2×RTX 3090 (TP2). Workloads: ShareGPT (mean 357 prompt tokens), arXiv-v1 (mean 3,253 tokens), arXiv-v2 (mean 6,267 tokens), and two mixed variants at 3:1 and 5:1 ShareGPT:arXiv ratios. Baselines are Sarathi-EDF and QoServe (ASPLOS 2026, the current SOTA). Goodput (requests/second at p99 SLO compliance with ≤1% violation budget): SlidingServe achieves 9.7%–30% higher goodput than QoServe and 25%–111% higher than Sarathi-EDF across all dataset/model combinations (Figure 4). Gains are largest on long-prompt datasets (Arxiv-v2: 30% over QoServe on Qwen2.5-7B) and smallest on short-prompt workloads (ShareGPT: ~9.4%). Under overload, SLO violation rate is reduced by up to 53% vs. QoServe. In a 20-minute transient-overload experiment (1.0–2.5 QPS alternating every 2 minutes), cumulative violations were 27.56% for SlidingServe vs. 51.30% for QoServe and 57.78% for Sarathi-EDF. Ablation (Table 4): starting from Sarathi-EDF at 1.5 QPS, SC alone reaches 1.75 QPS (+16.7%), SC+MLPS reaches 1.85 QPS (+5.7%), SC+MLPS+BC reaches 1.95 QPS (+5.4%). Under high load (QPS=3), violation reduction: SC→18.5%, SC+MLPS→22.9%, SC+MLPS+BC→8.1% incremental. Predictor accuracy: MAE 2.52–2.72 ms, RMSE 4.12–4.33 ms, R² > 0.99 across RTX 3090, A6000, and A100 hardware. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The cross-iteration sliding-window budget allocation is a genuine methodological contribution—prior SLO-aware schedulers (QoServe, Sarathi-EDF) treat each iteration greedily. The BatchConstructor's reduction of batch construction to a capacity-constrained knapsack is an engineering contribution that operationalizes the observation that batch atomicity makes in-batch request inclusion insufficient. The linear latency predictor with engineered features is a competent engineering choice but not conceptually novel; the multi-level priority sorter recombines known scheduling heuristics (EDF, SJF, fairness flags) rather than introducing a fundamentally new principle. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablation studies are present (Table 4) and well-structured, isolating each of the three main components. However, no error bars or variance estimates are reported across runs, and results appear to come from single-run experiments. The predictor is validated with MAE/RMSE/R² across three GPU configurations, which is appropriate for a regression task. The baseline QoServe is the current SOTA (ASPLOS 2026), making comparisons current and meaningful. No code, trained model weights, or dataset splits are publicly released (not mentioned in the paper). The ternary search for unimodality in objective (16) is assumed but not proved; if the latency-vs-budget curve has multiple local minima under certain batch compositions, the search may return suboptimal solutions. Evaluation is conducted exclusively on 7–8B parameter models on consumer/prosumer 24GB GPUs (RTX 3090) with TP2; generalizability to larger models or higher parallelism is asserted but not measured. This is a preprint (arXiv:2606.05933v1). Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS. - Practical: The system runs on RTX 3090 GPUs (consumer hardware, 24 GB VRAM) and the scheduling logic (linear predictor + ternary search + DP knapsack) adds negligible per-iteration compute. - Efficient: Scheduling overhead is never quantified; the DP knapsack is O(n × C) where C is token capacity—potentially slow for large batches with many risky requests, but in practice n is bounded by batch size and the paper claims "lightweight." - Effective: Goodput and SLO violation metrics on realistic workloads (ShareGPT, arXiv-Summarization) demonstrate actual improvement over the prior SOTA, not a synthetic proxy. - Scalable: Not evaluated beyond 7–8B models on 2×RTX 3090; behavior on 70B+ models with TP8+, where batch latency curves and scheduling frequencies differ substantially, is claimed but untested. The paper delivers a software implementation on top of vLLM; no public artifact is released. Strengths - The cross-iteration budget-splitting insight is both simple and well-motivated: the motivating example (Figure 1) concretely shows that greedy maximization in iteration t can reduce total throughput across t and t+1 due to the nonlinear latency curve. - The BatchConstructor correctly identifies that scheduling a request into a batch is not the same as satisfying its SLO—a subtle but practically important distinction that prior systems ignore. - The ablation table cleanly decomposes the contribution of each module and correctly attributes most of the gain to SlidingChunker rather than to the priority sorter or batch construction. - The latency predictor achieves R² > 0.99 with a 7-feature linear model across three different GPU architectures, validating the feature engineering approach and providing confidence in the underlying scheduling decisions. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors acknowledge that SlidingServe may exhibit higher tail latency in some scenarios because it deprioritizes requests that have already violated their SLOs—a deliberate trade-off to protect requests still within deadline. They note this increases latency for a small subset of overdue requests but improves overall SLO attainment. No other limitations are explicitly stated; the authors do not discuss the assumption of window size 2, the lack of multi-node or larger-model evaluation, or the scheduling overhead of the DP step. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Window size is fixed at 2 with no ablation; it is not clear whether a window of 3 or 4 would improve results or whether the ternary-search optimum is stable across different batch compositions. - The scheduling overhead (time from iteration end to next GPU launch) is never measured; at high QPS with many risky requests, the DP knapsack could become a bottleneck that partially offsets throughput gains. - All serving experiments run on 2×RTX 3090 with 7–8B models; the scheduling problem—and the relative benefit of look-ahead—changes at larger scales (longer iterations, higher TP), so the claimed generalizability is unsupported. - The ternary search assumes a unimodal latency-vs-budget objective; this is plausible but unproven, and edge cases with highly heterogeneous batch compositions may violate it. - No comparison against DistServe (disaggregated prefill/decode) or systems that physically separate prefill and decode phases, which represent an architecturally different but directly competing approach to the TTFT/TBT trade-off. - No public code release, making independent reproduction impossible. Bottom Line SlidingServe delivers a practically implementable, well-ablated scheduling improvement over the current SOTA for chunked-prefill LLM serving, with the cross-iteration budget-splitting insight as a genuine contribution worth adopting; practitioners running vLLM-based services at scale on workloads with long prompts should read this, but should treat the results as preliminary until validated on production-scale hardware and larger models.
Search Results 2606.05933 PDF
#125 — Moderately Innovative (58)

LLM Compression with Jointly Optimizing Architectural and Quantization choices

HL La, TT Le, A Taherkordi, PH Ha · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a differentiable Neural Architecture Search (NAS) framework that jointly optimizes structural pruning (width and depth) and layer-wise mixed-precision quantization for LLM compression in a single supernet training run. - Why it's important: Sequential prune-then-quantize pipelines leave accuracy and latency on the table because architectural and quantization decisions interact — a layer width optimal at FP16 may not be optimal at INT4 — and this is the first NAS framework that treats them as a unified problem. - How it does it: Weight-entanglement supernet with Dirichlet-parameterized architectural variables, per-precision LoRA adapter pairs for each quantization bit-width, importance-aware non-consecutive depth pruning via ReinMax gradient estimation, and a vectorized binary-mask implementation that replaces PyTorch's serial slicing loops. - The good: At equivalent 40% average accuracy on seven reasoning tasks, jointly compressed models achieve up to 1.4× faster inference than sequential subnet-selection + post-hoc quantization; at fixed 30ms latency, they reach ~41% average accuracy versus ~35% for the best sequential baseline. - The missing: All experiments are on Llama-3.1-8B only; there is no ablation isolating how much of the gain comes from joint optimization versus the improved supernet design (importance-aware depth, vectorized width), making it impossible to attribute credit. - Read it or not: Practitioners actively building edge deployment pipelines for 7B-class LLMs should read this; researchers seeking theoretical grounding or broad empirical coverage across model families will find the scope too narrow.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (52) is the decisive weakness: there is no ablation isolating the joint-optimization claim from the improved supernet design (importance-aware depth, vectorized masks), so the paper's central contribution is confounded and unverifiable; additionally no error bars/seeds, no released code, and all experiments are on a single model (Llama-3.1-8B). Completeness (55) and Resilience (52) are similarly capped — the headline 'joint beats sequential' claim cannot be attributed to its stated cause. The joint NAS+quantization framing and the 4.3× vectorized-supernet engineering are genuine (Novelty 68), but the missing isolating ablation and single-model scope leave the mechanism and generality unestablished. Weighted: 0.20*68+0.25*52+0.15*65+0.15*55+0.15*52+0.10*58 = 58.
empirical Novelty 68 Rigor 52 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 55 Resilience 52 Impact 58
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Deploying LLMs on resource-constrained devices (laptops, smartphones) is bottlenecked by memory and compute. Training compact models from scratch is prohibitively expensive (TinyLlama: 90 GPU-days on 16 A100s). Post-training compression via structural pruning and quantization is the practical alternative, but existing NAS approaches for LLMs treat these as sequential steps: first find a compressed architecture, then apply quantization. This decoupling forces each step to optimize without knowledge of the other, yielding suboptimal accuracy-latency trade-offs. No prior work provides a unified search that simultaneously determines which neurons, heads, and blocks to retain alongside which bit-widths to assign per layer. Key Idea The core insight is that architecture and quantization precision are jointly determined rather than separable: the optimal number of attention heads at 4-bit weight quantization differs from the optimum at 8-bit, because quantization noise interacts with width-induced parameter reduction. Encoding both choices as learnable Dirichlet-distributed mixing weights in a single supernet — with a separate LoRA adapter pair per precision level — allows gradient-based optimization to discover configurations unreachable by any sequential pipeline. Methodology The pipeline proceeds in four phases. First, transformer neurons, attention heads, and blocks are reordered by importance (cosine similarity of block input/output for depth; gradient-based magnitude for width) using the permutation equivariance of transformers. Second, a weight-entanglement supernet is built where each linear layer's weight is a Dirichlet-weighted sum of sub-matrix slices (width dimension) and the depth dimension is handled by sampling the number of retained blocks from a categorical distribution Cat(β), keeping only the top-β most important blocks. The critical engineering contribution is vectorizing the mixed-weight computation: instead of iterating over all (i,j) input/output size pairs with non-uniform zero-padding (serial in PyTorch), binary mask matrices G_{i,j} are precomputed at initialization and reused, reducing the operation to a single element-wise multiply and sum that PyTorch parallelizes natively. Third, quantization is integrated by assigning a distinct LoRA adapter pair (A_p, B_p) per precision p ∈ {2,4,8} bits; the quantized mixed-op weight becomes Σ_p α_p^weight * Q_p(W_mixed + B_p A_p,mixed), with Straight-Through Estimator enabling gradient flow. Activation quantization follows the same pattern. Fourth, the optimization is a bi-level constrained problem: architecture parameters ζ minimize validation loss plus a latency term (from a pre-built lookup table) plus ReLU-pair constraint penalties on parameter count; weight parameters w minimize training cross-entropy. When architectural entropy H falls below ε, redundant branches are pruned and the discovered sub-network is fine-tuned via knowledge distillation with the full model as teacher. Design decisions that are well-justified: importance-aware depth pruning (Figure 2 shows substantially lower validation loss than tail-dropping), vectorized masks (4.3× measured speedup), and per-precision LoRA adapters (cited instability with shared adapters across precision levels). Key Results Without quantization, on Llama-3.1-8B compressed into four parameter ranges (2-3B, 3-4B, 4-5B, 5-6B), the proposed method outperforms subnet-selection [24, NeurIPS 2024] and LoNAS [19] on average accuracy across BoolQ, PIQA, HellaSwag, WinoGrande, ARC-E, ARC-C, and MMLU. Representative numbers: at 4-5B parameters, ours achieves 45.93% average accuracy at 53.93ms latency versus 37.22% at 57.45ms (subnet-selection) and 36.91% at 73.72ms (LoNAS). At 5-6B, gains are smaller (60.03% vs 56.56% for subnet-selection) as search space saturation limits differentiation. At <2B, all methods converge to near-random accuracy (~35%). With joint quantization (QLlama3Space), Pareto fronts in Figure 5 show our-quant dominates all fixed-precision variants (W4A4/W8A8/W8A16/W4A16) of both subnet-selection and our-no-quant: 1.4× faster at equivalent accuracy, or ~6% higher accuracy at equivalent latency. Baselines are current (2024 publications). The vectorized supernet training achieves 4.3× speedup for the Llama3Space search space at a cost of 3.2GB additional memory on an 80GB A100. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION. The joint NAS-quantization optimization for LLMs is the paper's primary novel claim; prior work treats these as sequential. The vectorized mixed-op computation is a meaningful engineering contribution that enables practical training. The individual components — Dirichlet NAS (DrNAS), weight entanglement (TangleNAS), importance-based pruning, LoRA, ReinMax — are all drawn from prior work; novelty lies entirely in their integration and the joint optimization formulation. Rigor & Reproducibility No ablation study separates the contribution of joint optimization from the improved supernet design — it is impossible to determine whether gains come from optimizing architecture and quantization together or simply from the better importance-aware depth pruning and vectorized supernet. The depth pruning ablation in Figure 2 reports validation loss only, not downstream task accuracy, and compares against the original TangleNAS tail-dropping, not the subnet-selection baseline used elsewhere. Error bars and multiple random seeds are not reported for any result. Code is not released and no repository is mentioned. Evaluation is confined to a single model family (Llama-3.1-8B). The QLlama3Space search space has an astronomically large candidate count (proportional to 3^32 × 4^32 × 32 for depth and quantization alone), and the paper does not analyze convergence quality for this regime. The LoNAS* baseline incorporates modifications (feature reordering, sandwich training) not present in the published LoNAS — this strengthens the comparison but diverges from the published artifact. Deployability (4-property check) EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: Training requires one NVIDIA A100 80GB GPU (single GPU explicitly stated); compressed models target consumer edge hardware, but the training infrastructure is datacenter-class and the 3.2GB mask overhead must fit within the GPU budget. - Efficient: The vectorized supernet reduces training time 4.3× versus the TangleNAS baseline; inference latency is measured with production-grade kernels (Marlin for W4A16/W8A16, ABQ-LLM for other configs), making latency numbers credible. - Effective: Pareto front dominance on seven reasoning benchmarks versus two contemporary baselines with consistent quantitative margins constitutes adequate evidence for the joint optimization claim within the tested regime. - Scalable: Tested exclusively at the 8B scale; no experiments on 13B, 70B, or non-Llama architectures; scalability claims are absent — the paper makes no extrapolation, but the silence leaves the question open. No software artifact, trained model, or code repository is released. Strengths - The joint architecture-quantization framing is principled: Pareto front dominance over multiple sequential pipeline variants (not just one) across a range of latency targets provides convincing evidence that the problem decomposition itself is the bottleneck the paper claims. - Importance-aware non-consecutive depth pruning is a practically important fix over TangleNAS's restriction to tail-block dropping, backed by an explicit validation loss curve comparing the two strategies. - The vectorized mask implementation (4.3× training throughput) is what makes the approach feasible on a single GPU — this is a real and reproducible contribution, not an incidental speedup. - Experimental setup matches the strongest baseline [24] exactly (same calibration data, same LoRA rank, same Alpaca fine-tuning set, same evaluation shots), making the comparison fair. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly defer full QAT integration (combining the joint NAS search with quantization-aware fine-tuning rather than post-training initialization) to future work. They note QA-LoRA's incompatibility with the proposed quantization mixing design due to shape mismatch in the aggregation equation. The memory trade-off of the binary mask matrices is acknowledged in Section 3.3. These are stated directly and credibly — the scope boundaries are clear. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The central empirical claim (joint optimization beats sequential) is confounded with the supernet design changes (importance-aware depth, improved training efficiency). A controlled ablation using the proposed supernet architecture but with sequential quantization applied post-search would isolate the joint optimization contribution. - All experiments use Llama-3.1-8B; the method's behavior on Mistral, Phi, Qwen, or MoE architectures is entirely untested. The permutation-equivariance preprocessing step may not apply uniformly across all transformer variants. - No total wall-clock cost is reported for the full pipeline (supernet training + search + fine-tuning). The 4.3× throughput improvement is meaningful only in relation to an absolute baseline training time, which is not stated. - The 2-3B parameter compressed models achieve 35-40% average accuracy across seven tasks, which is near random chance on several benchmarks — the practical utility of such highly compressed models is not discussed. - At the 5-6B range, gains over subnet-selection narrow substantially, and both methods converge to the same configurations due to limited candidates in that region; this saturation is not analyzed as a potential limitation of the approach. Bottom Line Practitioners targeting edge deployment of Llama-class 7B models gain a concrete, single-A100 method that demonstrably outperforms the standard prune-then-quantize pipeline on accuracy-latency Pareto fronts, but the absence of ablations and single-model scope mean the mechanism and generality of the improvement remain unverified — it is a preprint (arXiv:2606.04063v1) worth reading for compression practitioners, less so for those seeking transferable insights.
Search Results 2606.04063 PDF
#126 — Moderately Innovative (58)

Data-driven discovery of governing differential equations across physical systems

S Lou, H Xu, W Wang, L Lu, H Sun, Y Liu, L Zhang… · arxiv.org · neural symbolic - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a two-dimensional phase diagram of equation discoverability and the Representation–Evaluation–Optimization (REO) framework as a problem-oriented organizing perspective for the field of data-driven differential equation discovery. - Why it's important: The field has expanded rapidly across dozens of algorithms and application domains without a unifying conceptual framework, making it increasingly difficult to compare methods, understand their failure modes, or judge when a discovered equation genuinely advances knowledge. - How it does it: By decomposing discovery problems along two independent complexity axes — structural complexity (closed-form library to open-form symbolic) and coefficient complexity (constant to equation-inexpressible) — and then decomposing all algorithms into their representation, evaluation, and optimization components, the paper provides a stable conceptual language that transcends individual algorithm taxonomies. - The good: The phase diagram (Figure 2) places ~50 published methods in a 3×3 inference-regime grid and identifies the upper-right corner (open-form structure + equation-inexpressible coefficients) as a virtually unexplored frontier — a more actionable map than prior method-family or domain-centered reviews. - The missing: The phase diagram placement is qualitative and author-curated with no formal procedure; the paper proposes "solvability" as a new evaluation dimension but never operationalizes it with a metric or demonstrates it on a benchmark system. - Read it or not: Essential reading for anyone designing, selecting, or benchmarking data-driven equation discovery methods; the phase diagram gives a durable triage map of a field that can otherwise feel like an undifferentiated pile of special cases.
Why this rank: Theoretical (framework/review) profile. Novelty (65) and Non-Obviousness (65) rest on the 2D discoverability phase diagram and REO decomposition, with the sharp observation about the ambiguous boundary among structural terms, coefficients, and noise. Rigor (55) and Resilience (50) are the constraints: phase-diagram placement is qualitative and author-curated with no documented procedure or inter-rater check, the proposed 'solvability' dimension is named but never operationalized, and the framework lacks formal grounding (acknowledged by the authors). Useful as a triage map but, like the negatively-rated review-style submissions, it contributes no algorithm, benchmark, or experiment. Weighted ~58.
theoretical Novelty 65 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 60 Resilience 50 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Scientists need to infer governing differential equations directly from experimental or simulated data when underlying physics is unclear or derivation from first principles is intractable (e.g., climate dynamics, turbulent flows, multiscale biological systems). Classical first-principles modelling fails at high dimensionality and strong nonlinearity; black-box ML models fit complex dynamics but produce opaque input–output approximators with no mechanistic content. The field of data-driven differential equation discovery has addressed this gap since SINDy (2016), but has grown so rapidly — across sparse regression, symbolic regression, reinforcement learning, physics-informed neural networks, and LLMs — that existing reviews organized by algorithm family or application domain obscure the deeper question: which equations can actually be discovered from available data, and under what conditions? Key Idea Discoverability is determined by two coupled inferential tasks: inferring the functional structure of the equation and determining its coefficients. Organizing these tasks on independent axes — structural complexity (fixed library vs. open-form compositional) and coefficient complexity (constant vs. spatiotemporally varying vs. stochastic random fields) — reveals qualitatively distinct inference regimes that explain why specific method families succeed or fail. Complementarily, all discovery algorithms can be abstracted into three interdependent components — Representation (how equations are encoded for computation), Evaluation (by what criteria candidates are judged), and Optimization (how the search is conducted) — and improvements in isolation cannot substitute for understanding their interplay. Methodology This is a review paper with two original conceptual contributions and an applications survey spanning ~164 references (2007–2026). Phase diagram: A qualitative 3×3 grid places ~50 methods by structural complexity (closed-form library → expandable library → open-form symbolic) and coefficient complexity (constant → equation-expressible → equation-inexpressible). The horizontal axis tracks whether the hypothesis space is a finite predefined dictionary (SINDy, PDE-FIND), an expandable evolutionary library (DLGA, EPDE, PDE-Net 2.0), or an unbounded compositional tree (PySR, DISCOVER, EqGPT, LLM-SR). The vertical axis tracks whether coefficients are global constants (most of the literature), deterministic functions of space/time (grouped sparse regression, SEQL/HEQL, GraphED), or stochastic fields (HIN-PDE, Karhunen–Loève-based methods). The paper argues the upper-right cell is the unexplored frontier. REO framework: Representation options are (1) structured matrix/library, (2) symbolic tree or graph encoding, (3) continuous embedding (latent space), and (4) sequence-based (LLM tokens). Evaluation dimensions are data-fitting accuracy (MAE, R², term recall), mathematical conciseness (term count, AIC/BIC), physical consistency (dimensional homogeneity, symmetry, AI-Hilbert-style polynomial constraints), and solvability (proposed as a new, currently unimplemented dimension: whether discovered equations admit analytical or numerical solutions). Optimization strategies are sparse regression (LASSO, STRidge, elastic net, ℓ₀-MIP), genetic algorithms, reinforcement learning (risk-seeking policy gradients in DSR/DISCOVER), gradient descent (suited to differentiable embeddings), and prompt-based optimization (LLM context steering). Applications survey: Reviews four recurring paradigms across disciplines — recovering equations from noisy/incomplete measurements (turbulence, geosciences, electromagnetics, stochastic systems), bridging microscopic rules to macroscopic laws (active matter, granular flow, plasma physics, single-cell biology), discovering interpretable closure models (RANS turbulence, ocean mesoscale eddies, cloud cover), and discovering previously unreported governing relations (rogue waves, viscous gravity currents, bacterial chemotaxis). Critical design decisions: (1) Framing discoverability as a problem property (structure and coefficient complexity) rather than a method property is well-justified and more informative than prior taxonomies. (2) The ambiguous boundary among structural terms, coefficients, and noise is identified as a conceptually underappreciated issue — a coefficient fixed in one regime may reveal law-like structure in a broader one (Reynolds-number-dependent closures as a concrete example). This is a sharp and correct observation. (3) Proposing solvability as a fourth evaluation dimension is concrete but remains unoperationalized within the paper itself. Key Results This is a survey paper with no primary experimental results. Descriptive findings of note: (1) Figure 4 shows a 20-method × 24-PDE validation coverage matrix — most methods are evaluated on a narrow canonical set (Burgers', KdV, Kuramoto–Sivashinsky), with sparse coverage of 2D systems, coupled equations, or real-world data. (2) The phase diagram reveals that virtually all published methods cluster in the left column (closed or expandable library) combined with constant or equation-expressible coefficients; the open-form + equation-inexpressible regime has essentially one specialized method (HIN-PDE). (3) No standardized PDE discovery benchmark comparable to LLM-SRBench for ODEs currently exists; performance differences reported in the literature may be driven by experimental design rather than algorithmic capability. (4) The paper identifies that evaluation currently stops at data-fitting accuracy in most work, with physical consistency and solvability largely unassessed. Novelty Classification NEW FORMULATION (phase diagram of discoverability), NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING (structural mapping of the coverage and gaps across ~50 methods). The paper introduces no new algorithm, dataset, or empirical experiment. The conceptual contribution — treating discoverability as a function of structural and coefficient complexity, and identifying the upper-right regime as the unexplored frontier — is genuinely novel relative to prior method-centered reviews (North et al. 2023, Brunton & Kutz 2024). The REO decomposition parallels standard ML pipeline descriptions but its application specifically to equation discovery, with the inclusion of solvability as a new evaluation dimension, provides incremental original content. Rigor & Reproducibility As a review paper, standard empirical rigor criteria (ablations, error bars, seeds) do not apply. The paper is honest about its limitations: Figure 4 explicitly disclaims direct performance comparison and bases coverage claims on authors' self-reporting, not independent replication. Phase diagram placement is qualitative and author-curated without a formal assignment procedure; ambiguous cases (hybrid methods, methods spanning multiple cells) are not discussed. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2606.09638v1, June 8, 2026); peer-review status is not stated. No code or supplementary data for the review itself is released. The authors correctly identify lack of standardized protocols, non-public test sets, and open code as field-wide problems requiring remediation — this self-awareness is a credibility positive. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: THEORETICAL — the paper introduces a conceptual framework and organizational perspective; it runs no experiments. - Sound: The phase diagram is descriptively consistent and internally coherent; the REO decomposition correctly spans the space of existing approaches. However, neither is formally grounded — no theorem characterizes search complexity as a function of R/E/O choices, and the authors explicitly acknowledge this gap. - General: Broad by design — the framework covers ODEs and PDEs, sparse regression through LLMs, and applications across 8+ scientific domains. Generality is the framework's explicit goal and its strongest property. - Tight: Not applicable — the paper makes no formal bounds claims. The phase diagram is a qualitative organizer, not a quantitative model of difficulty. - Implementable: The phase diagram informs method selection for a given discovery problem (e.g., if coefficients are spatiotemporally varying random fields, most published methods are inapplicable). The REO framework informs algorithm design. The solvability dimension is defined but not operationalized — no metric, scoring procedure, or example benchmark is provided. The paper stops at paper form with no software artifact. Strengths - The 2D phase diagram is a genuinely novel organizing tool: placing structural complexity on one axis and coefficient complexity on the other reveals which regimes are saturated versus unexplored in a way that no prior review achieves, and immediately tells practitioners which methods are and are not applicable to their problem. - The identification of the ambiguous boundary among structural terms, coefficients, and noise as a deep conceptual issue is sharp and correct — the observation that a Reynolds-number-dependent closure can appear as a fixed parameter in restricted settings but reveal law-like structure in a broader regime is precisely the kind of insight that distinguishes a synthetic review from a catalog. - Figure 4's coverage matrix is an underutilized format for exposing benchmarking gaps: by showing which methods have been validated on which PDEs (rather than claiming performance), it reveals that most methods have been tested on ≤5 canonical equations and almost none on 2D or coupled real-world systems. - The proposal to add solvability as an evaluation dimension is practically motivated and not present in prior reviews; even if underspecified, it corrects a real blind spot (a perfectly data-fitting but numerically ill-posed equation is scientifically useless). Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly confront several limitations: (1) the field lacks standardized benchmarks, non-public test sets, and shared evaluation protocols, so reported performance differences may reflect experimental design choices rather than algorithmic capability; (2) most validation relies on synthetic data from canonical PDEs, limiting generalizability to real empirical systems; (3) the upper-right phase diagram regime is underexplored and represents an open challenge; (4) LLM-based methods hallucinate equations that appear plausible but are physically meaningless, requiring external validation; (5) the more fundamental question — how a discovered equation becomes scientifically meaningful rather than merely data-fitting — is explicitly framed as unsolved and beyond the paper's scope. This direct confrontation of limits strengthens the paper's credibility. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The phase diagram placement procedure is not documented: for a method like SINDy-AE (which combines a neural encoder with a library) or ODEFormer (which uses a pre-trained transformer), the cell assignment requires judgment calls that different reviewers might make differently. No inter-rater agreement or sensitivity analysis is provided. - The REO framework lacks formal grounding: the paper explicitly acknowledges that "a more rigorous mathematical foundation for this interplay is still lacking" (no characterization of how search complexity scales with representation expressivity or evaluation stringency), but does not estimate how far the field is from such a theory. - The solvability dimension is proposed but never instantiated: no example shows how to score a candidate equation on solvability, no benchmark PDE is flagged as posing a solvability challenge, and no method in the reviewed literature is evaluated on this criterion — the dimension exists only as a name. - The "concept formation" outlook (revising existing concepts, mechanistic distillation, closed-loop discovery) is aspirational; the paper presents no evidence that any existing method reliably transitions from equation recovery to conceptual revision, and no criteria are offered for judging when that transition has occurred. - Figure 4 reports self-reported validation coverage based on what papers claim to have demonstrated, not independent replication; a method that claims to recover Burgers' equation under 1% noise under its own experimental protocol is not directly comparable to another claiming the same under a different discretization. Bottom Line A researcher designing or selecting a data-driven equation discovery method will find the phase diagram and REO framework durably useful for navigating an overcrowded field — this review is worth reading specifically for those two tools, even in the absence of new algorithms or benchmark results.
Search Results 2606.09638 PDF
#127 — Moderately Innovative (58)

Beyond Prompt-Based Planning: MCP-Native Graph Planning-based Biomedical Agent System

Z Chen, F Widjaja, W Dai, X Zhang, Y Shen, J Zhou · arxiv.org · automated generated environments for agent testing - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces BioManus, an MCP-native biomedical agent system that replaces flat prompt-based tool retrieval with graph-scaffolded planning over a typed capability graph spanning 910 MCP servers and 3,500 bioinformatics tools auto-generated from heterogeneous software. - Why it's important: Prompt-based tool retrieval in biomedical agents scales catastrophically — context consumption grows 4.2× geometrically when tool inventories expand from 0 to 2,000 tools, causing planning instability and accuracy degradation that makes scaling biomedical AI agents with increasing tool coverage practically infeasible. - How it does it: BioManus converts heterogeneous bioinformatics tools into standardized MCP servers via an LLM-powered BioinfoMCP Compiler, organizes them as a typed heterogeneous graph, and at inference time retrieves a compact task-specific subgraph (~70–80 nodes) rather than injecting raw tool descriptions into the prompt, binding abstract operation-level plans to executable MCP tools only after planning. - The good: On BioAgentBench, BioManus achieves 46.84% mean LLM-judge score while consuming only 0.85M geometric mean prompt tokens, compared to Biomni-2k which consumes 4.28M tokens for a lower score (46.16%), directly validating the core efficiency-performance tradeoff claim. - The missing: BioAgentBench uses only 10 tasks evaluated by an LLM judge (itself DeepSeek-V4-Pro, same model family as the agent backbone), making the headline performance comparison statistically indefensible without error bars or significance tests. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building large-scale tool-use agents or biomedical AI infrastructure; the complexity analysis and BioinfoMCP Compiler are the strongest contributions, but practitioners should not rely on the 10-task benchmark numbers for deployment decisions.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Novelty (75) is high — operation-level graph-scaffolded planning over a typed capability graph (4,490 nodes, 69,697 edges) auto-generated from 3,500 tools is a genuinely novel composition with a formal O(h(ℓ_o+m̄ℓ_s)) complexity argument. Rigor (50) is the limiting dimension: the BioinfoMCP Compiler eval on 3,041 pairs is credible, but the headline BioAgentBench comparison rests on 10 LLM-judged tasks with no error bars and a DeepSeek-V4-Pro-judging-DeepSeek-V4 circularity, graph annotation quality is never measured directly, and nothing is publicly released. Impact (55) is capped by the absence of any release URL for the 910 servers/compiler and unreported wall-clock/dollar cost. Completeness (55) and Resilience (50) suffer from the statistically indefensible 10-task headline and the undiagnosed DbQA gap. Weighted: 0.15*75 + 0.15*50 + 0.10*70 + 0.15*55 + 0.15*50 + 0.30*55 = 58.
systems Novelty 75 Rigor 50 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 55 Resilience 50 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Bioinformatics research requires orchestrating hundreds of heterogeneous software tools spanning command-line programs, Python packages, R/Bioconductor libraries, workflow engines, and database APIs. Current LLM-based biomedical agents adopt prompt-based tool retrieval, injecting candidate tool descriptions directly into the model context before planning. As bioinformatics software ecosystems grow continuously, this approach faces two compounding problems: (1) context size grows linearly with retrieved tool count — measured empirically as a 4.2× geometric mean increase in prompt tokens from 0 to 2,000 MCP tools, with individual tasks exceeding 10^7 tokens — creating cost, latency, and planning instability at scale; and (2) tools are heterogeneous in execution environment, runtime dependencies, and interface conventions, forcing agents to simultaneously manage high-level reasoning and low-level dependency resolution without explicit execution structure. Prior biomedical systems (GeneAgent, CellAgent, Biomni) demonstrate that adding more tools to prompt-retrieval architectures degrades rather than improves task performance. Key Idea The core insight is that biomedical tool retrieval should operate at the operation level rather than the raw tool level: instead of injecting tool descriptions into prompts, BioManus retrieves a compact subgraph of typed biological operations (abstract transformations such as quality_control → alignment → quantification) and binds each operation to specific executable MCP tools only after the operation-level plan is established. This changes planning context complexity from O(kℓ_t) — proportional to retrieved tool count times description length — to O(h(ℓ_o + m̄ℓ_s)), which depends only on workflow horizon h and average candidate tools per operation m̄ ≪ N, making planning scale with task complexity rather than ecosystem size. Methodology BioManus consists of four pipeline stages. First, the BioinfoMCP Compiler ingests tool documentation (manuals, help flags, PDFs) and uses an LLM backbone in a three-stage pipeline (preparation → execution → delivery) to synthesize Python MCP server code with @mcp.tool decorators, subprocess execution, parameter validation, and Docker packaging. Failed generations are iteratively refined until MCP interface compliance is achieved. Second, a Typed Capability Graph G=(V,E) is constructed automatically from the MCP ecosystem, containing six node types (tool, operation, datatype, capability, stage, server) and ten edge types (typed_flow 19,590; follows 9,225; produces 9,046; consumes 8,332; accepts 6,915; etc.) totaling 4,490 nodes and 69,697 typed edges. An LLM annotator infers input/output datatypes, semantic operation categories, and workflow stage membership for each server. Third, Graph-Scaffolded Planning converts a natural-language query into structured task semantics and performs GraphRAG-style retrieval over the global graph to extract a compact subgraph (~70–80 nodes, ~120–200 edges per task from case studies), from which an LLM planner synthesizes an operation-level workflow scaffold P=(o1,...,oh). Critical design decisions: operation-level abstraction before tool binding is well-justified by the complexity proof; typed datatype compatibility edges prevent type-incompatible tool chains; and iterative refinement of generated MCP code is justified by the 77.3% baseline parse success rate of direct prompting. Fourth, Dynamic Execution registers only selected MCP servers at runtime, keeping execution context compact. Key Results On BioAgentBench (10 tasks, LLM-judge scoring 0–1 with DeepSeek-V4-Pro): BioManus achieves 46.84% mean score and 4/10 passes, outperforming Biomni-2k (46.16%, 3/10), Biomni-100 (43.45%, 4/10), Biomni (39.67%, 4/10), and ReAct-Code (39.25%, 4/10). Crucially, Biomni performance is non-monotonic with tool inventory: Biomni-100 (43.45%) > Biomni (39.67%) but Biomni-500 (36.75%) degrades below baseline, directly validating the prompt-scaling failure diagnosis. On LAB-Bench (315 test examples per subset): BioManus achieves SeqQA 90.48% (above human expert 78.80%) and CloningScenarios 81.82% (above human expert 60.00%), but DbQA 67.29% trails Biomni-100 (75.35%). Context efficiency: BioManus geometric mean 0.85M tokens on BioAgentBench vs. Biomni-2k 4.28M; on CloningScenarios, 1.29M vs. Biomni 1.92M and Biomni-2k 2.62M. Ablation (Figure 5): MCP infrastructure alone adds +3.0% on BioAgentBench; graph-scaffolded planning adds +4.2% additional. BioinfoMCP Compiler on 3,041 paired evaluations: structured return 77.3% → 99.4%; multi-tool rate 10.4% → 63.7%; parse success generalizes to Gemini 3.1 (99.5%), GPT-4.1-mini (100%), Kimi 2.6 (100%). Evaluation uses the LLM-judge BioAgentBench (itself a 2026 preprint). No significance tests or error bars are reported for any result. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. The integration of LLM-driven MCP standardization (BioinfoMCP Compiler), typed heterogeneous graph over a large bioinformatics ecosystem, and GraphRAG-style subgraph retrieval for operation-level planning constitutes a genuinely novel architecture for biomedical agents. Individual components — MCP protocol, heterogeneous graphs, RAG over graphs — are existing techniques, but their composition into a scalable, executable biomedical planning framework with formal complexity guarantees and empirical evaluation at 3,500-tool scale is a substantive contribution. The most distinctive element is the operation-node abstraction that mediates between natural-language intent and tool execution. Rigor & Reproducibility The headline BioAgentBench performance comparison rests on 10 tasks with no error bars, seeds, or significance tests — insufficient to establish statistical superiority for differences as small as 46.84% vs. 46.16%. The LLM judge (DeepSeek-V4-Pro) is from the same model family as the agent backbone (DeepSeek-V4), creating a potential circularity not discussed by the authors. The BioinfoMCP Compiler evaluation on 3,041 pairs is the most credible empirical result, with a large enough sample to support its conclusions. Graph node annotations (datatypes, operation categories, stage membership) are generated by an LLM and evaluated only by downstream performance, not by direct annotation quality metrics — wrong annotations would silently degrade retrieval without diagnosis. No code, model weights, or MCP server catalog is publicly released (no URL mentioned). Both BioAgentBench (Fa et al., 2026) and this paper are preprints; neither is peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS — BioManus is a built end-to-end agent system evaluated by deployment on benchmark tasks. - Practical: Deployable on standard CPU hardware (evaluated on 2× Intel Xeon Gold 6430, 128 cores, ~1 TiB RAM); Docker packaging provides portability, but running 910+ MCP servers with their dependency stacks at scale requires substantial infrastructure not quantified in the paper. - Efficient: Prompt token consumption is reduced approximately 4–5× over Biomni-2k baselines, but wall-clock latency, API cost, and memory footprint of the graph infrastructure and dynamic MCP registration are not reported, so whether token reduction translates to end-to-end efficiency is unknown. - Effective: The system achieves best overall results on SeqQA and CloningScenarios and matches or exceeds Biomni variants on BioAgentBench, but the DbQA gap (67.29% vs. 75.35% for Biomni-100) reveals a structural limitation for database-centric workflows that is not fully diagnosed. - Scalable: The O(h(ℓ_o + m̄ℓ_s)) context complexity is theoretically N-independent, but empirical testing stops at 3,500 tools; graph construction quality, annotation accuracy, and retrieval precision at 10× or 100× scale have not been tested. The system delivers a software infrastructure artifact (BioinfoMCP Compiler, typed capability graph, 910 MCP servers), but no public repository or release URL is mentioned in the paper. Strengths - The formal complexity analysis — deriving and comparing O(kℓ_t) for prompt retrieval against O(h(ℓ_o + m̄ℓ_s)) for graph-scaffolded planning — is the clearest and most actionable theoretical framing of the context-scaling problem in biomedical agents published to date. - The BioinfoMCP Compiler achieves 99.4% structured return and 63.7% multi-tool rate across 3,041 paired evaluations, with generalization to three different LLM backbones, demonstrating a robust and practically useful tool-wrapping infrastructure independently of the agent planning claims. - The empirical diagnosis (Figure 2) that Biomni performance degrades non-monotonically with tool inventory — 43.45% at 100 tools, 36.75% at 500, partially recovering at 2k — is a concrete, reproducible measurement that validates the central failure mode being addressed. - The ablation (Figure 5) correctly isolates MCP infrastructure and graph planning as separate, additive contributions rather than conflating them. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge: (1) compiler reliability depends on documentation quality; richer documentation and execution-based validation would improve it; (2) the capability graph uses lightweight semantic annotations and could be extended with finer-grained biological ontologies; (3) effectiveness still depends on graph retrieval quality; (4) BioManus is an assistive system requiring expert oversight for scientific interpretation. These are candid and specific. Notably absent is any acknowledgment of the statistical weakness of the 10-task benchmark evaluation. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Insufficient test set for performance claims. Ten BioAgentBench tasks with LLM-judge scoring and no error bars or significance tests cannot support the conclusion that BioManus is superior to Biomni-2k (46.84% vs. 46.16%). Per-task token variance (Table 5 shows individual tasks spanning 0.15M to 4.49M tokens across methods) suggests high noise relative to the performance differences being claimed. - LLM-judge circularity. Using DeepSeek-V4-Pro to evaluate agents running on DeepSeek-V4 raises bias questions that are not acknowledged or tested with an independent judge. - Graph annotation quality is a black box. The LLM-generated datatype, operation, and stage annotations that form the graph's semantic scaffold are never evaluated directly; error rates in annotation would corrupt retrieval without visible failure signals. - Missing runtime cost comparison. Graph construction, LLM annotation of 3,500 tools, subgraph retrieval, and dynamic MCP registration all add latency not present in prompt-retrieval baselines; the paper reports only token counts, not wall-clock times or dollar costs. - DbQA underperformance undiagnosed. The 8-percentage-point gap versus Biomni-100 on DbQA (67.29% vs. 75.35%) is attributed to database-centricity without ablations or error analysis explaining which specific failure modes cause it. - No public release. The 910 MCP servers and BioinfoMCP Compiler would be the primary artifacts of value to the research community; without a release URL, the scale claims and compiler robustness results are not independently verifiable. Bottom Line BioManus makes a genuine and well-motivated contribution by showing that operation-level graph retrieval can decouple biomedical planning from tool inventory size, and the BioinfoMCP Compiler is independently valuable infrastructure — but the core performance claims rest on a 10-task benchmark too small to support statistical conclusions, and the system is not publicly released for verification.
Search Results 2606.04494 PDF
#128 — Moderately Innovative (58)

VESTA: A Fully Automated Scenario Generation and Safety Evaluation Framework for LLM Agents

L Jia, H Tong, F Zhao, J Li, D Liang, P Wu, Q Zhang… · arxiv.org · automated generated environments for agent testing - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces VESTA, a fully automated pipeline that generates 1,072 executable, multi-turn safety evaluation scenarios for LLM agents across five risk dimensions and evaluates 12 agents using an adaptive attacker, simulated tool environment, and LLM-as-judge scoring. - Why it's important: As LLM agents execute real-world actions through tools and multi-turn interactions, unsafe behavior manifests in intermediate steps rather than final outputs, making static single-turn benchmarks blind to the most dangerous failure modes. - How it does it: Human experts write seven seed scenarios per risk subcategory; GPT-5.4 expands each to 100 candidates with schema-guided generation; quality filtering retains 67 per subcategory; at evaluation time an adaptive attacker LLM applies turn-by-turn pressure while a simulated tool environment records both programmatic tool-call violations (UTR) and trajectory-level LLM judge verdicts (ASR). - The good: Across 25,728 evaluated episodes, the average Attack Success Rate reaches 47.1% with Llama-3.3-70B exceeding 80%, while Claude-Sonnet-4.6 and Claude-Haiku-4.5 fall below 10% — a 8× gap that is consistent across four independent LLM judges (pairwise agreement >0.85). - The missing: GPT-5.4 simultaneously authors the scenarios, generates attacker turns, and serves as the primary judge, creating a systemic circularity risk; no ablation quantifies how much scenario quality or judge accuracy depends on this single model. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone building or deploying LLM agents who wants an empirical baseline on behavioral safety failures; less essential for researchers already familiar with ToolEmu or Agent-SafetyBench, as the conceptual novelty is in integration rather than any new mechanism.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile (built evaluation framework deployed against 12 models). Rigor (55) and Resilience (55) pull the composite down: GPT-5.4 simultaneously authors scenarios, generates attacker turns, and serves as primary judge — a systemic circularity the paper does not justify or ablate, which makes the reported ASR numbers indicative rather than definitive and vulnerable under adversarial review; there are no ablations on scenario quality, expansion model, or thresholds, no sampling-variance error bars, and no confirmed code/scenario release. Novelty (62) is moderate — the contribution is integration of known components (LLM red-teaming, LLM-as-judge, simulated tools) plus a useful empirical finding that process-oriented risks exceed boundary-explicit ones. Multi-judge agreement >0.85 and the dual UTR/ASR design provide convergent validity, and limitations are confronted honestly (Completeness 62), but Impact (58) is capped by the absence of a confirmed public release. Weighted: 0.15*62 + 0.15*55 + 0.10*58 + 0.15*62 + 0.15*55 + 0.30*58 = 58.
systems Novelty 62 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 58 Completeness 62 Resilience 55 Impact 58
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation As LLM systems evolve from chat interfaces into agents that call APIs, browse the web, manage files, and take multi-step actions, unsafe behavior shifts from harmful text to harmful actions — unauthorized operations, dangerous tool calls, goal drift, and compliance under adversarial pressure. Existing safety benchmarks (SafetyBench, HarmBench) test response-level safety on static prompts and cannot observe how failures compound across turns. Scenario-based agent evaluations (ToolEmu, Agent-SafetyBench, AgentHarm) move in the right direction but rely on manually authored cases or predefined static prompts, limiting coverage across diverse risk mechanisms, domains, and tool environments. The specific gap is the absence of a scalable, executable evaluation that measures process-level safety — whether agents maintain authority boundaries, resist adversarial pressure, and avoid unsafe tool calls throughout a full interaction episode, not just in their final response. Key Idea The core insight is that agent safety failures are best detected by recording and judging the full execution trace — tool calls, environment state changes, and accumulated attacker pressure — rather than inspecting the final response alone. VESTA operationalizes this by separating scenario construction (human seeds + LLM expansion + quality filter) from execution (adaptive attacker + simulated tool environment that flags unsafe tool calls programmatically) from judgment (LLM judge reading the complete episode trace). The adaptive attacker is the central mechanism: unlike a static adversarial prompt, it conditions each turn on what the target agent has already done, escalating pressure specifically at the observed failure boundary. Methodology VESTA operates in three phases. In phase one, a risk taxonomy organizes 16 subcategories under five dimensions: Interaction Understanding Failure (intent misinterpretation, sycophantic compliance), Goal–Norm Misalignment (reward hacking, negative side effects, objective drift), Robustness and Generalization Failure (inducement vulnerability, misleading context vulnerability, long-horizon instability), Supervision and Control Failure (insufficient oversight, interruption failure, correction resistance, audit attribution difficulty), and Autonomy and Authority Failure (unauthorized decision-making, privilege escalation, dangerous action execution, single-agent execution cascade). For each subcategory, human experts write a scenario family specification (failure mechanism, authority boundary, safe/risky behavior paths, tool pattern, attacker pressure pattern, judge criteria) and seven complete seed scenarios. GPT-5.4 at temperature 0.7 uses schema-guided generation to produce 100 candidates per subcategory. Coverage-constrained top-k filtering with a similarity threshold of 0.55 and a domain cap selects 60 expanded scenarios per subcategory (67 total with seeds), yielding 1,072 scenarios across 16 subcategories. In phase two, each scenario is instantiated as an executable episode x = (s, e, T, a, j), where s is the target-agent specification, e is environment state, T is the simulated tool module, a is the adaptive attacker configuration, and j is the judge specification. At each turn, the adaptive attacker (GPT-5.4, temperature 0.7) generates a pressure message conditioned on the current trajectory and runtime state; the target agent responds with either a tool call or a final answer; the simulated tool environment (all execution is synthetic — no real credentials, no external network calls) returns a structured observation and updates runtime state including forbidden_tool_called and unauthorized_decision_made flags. Maximum episode length is four turns. Each of the 12 target agents is evaluated on 2,144 episodes (1,072 scenarios × 2 authority contexts: Trust Context and Warning Context). In phase three, two complementary metrics are computed. Unsafe Tool-call Rate (UTR) is programmatic: it flags episodes where predefined unsafe tools were invoked, providing a stable behavioral detection signal. Attack Success Rate (ASR) is semantic: GPT-5.4 at temperature 0.0, given the complete episode execution trace and judge specification, assigns a binary safe/unsafe label. Multi-judge validation reruns ASR with DeepSeek-V3.2, GPT-4o-2024-11-20, and Llama-4-Maverick, all at temperature 0.0. Final reported ASR is averaged across the four judges. Critical design decisions: (1) Simulated tool execution is well-justified for safety and reproducibility but explicitly sacrifices fidelity to real system complexity. (2) The adaptive attacker is a meaningful improvement over static prompts but is constrained to four turns, which limits the depth of long-horizon scenarios. (3) Using GPT-5.4 for scenario generation, attacker generation, and primary judging is a practical choice but creates circular dependency — this decision is not justified in the paper. Key Results Across 25,728 target-agent episodes, average ASR is 47.1%. Claude-Sonnet-4.6 and Claude-Haiku-4.5 achieve ASR below 10%; GLM-5.1 is the next-lowest. A middle tier (Kimi-K2-0905, DeepSeek-V3.2, DeepSeek-V4-Pro, Qwen3.6-Plus, Gemini-3-Flash-Preview, GPT-4o-Mini) clusters near 50%. Llama-3.3-70B exceeds 80% ASR; Doubao-Seed-1.6 and Gemini-2.5-Flash exceed 70%. Open vs. closed weight does not predict safety performance: Llama-3.3-70B (open) is the highest-risk model, but GLM-5.1 (open) is among the safest. Model version updates correlate with lower ASR within Gemini (3-Flash-Preview substantially lower than 2.5-Flash) and DeepSeek families. At the subcategory level, Misleading Context Vulnerability (77.9%), Long-Horizon Instability (71.5%), Objective Drift (67.7%), Inducement Vulnerability (64.7%), and Negative Side Effects (61.9%) have the highest ASR — all process-oriented risks. Subcategories with clearer boundaries (Interruption Failure 12.3%, Sycophantic Compliance 18.5%, Unauthorized Decision-Making 15.7%) have lower ASR, suggesting current models have partial defenses against boundary-explicit violations. Warning Context (explicit permission and safety reminders) reduces ASR for most models, with the largest reductions in Autonomy, Goal-Norm, and Interaction dimensions; Robustness shows limited improvement, indicating context-manipulation risks are not mitigated by system-prompt-level reminders. Multi-judge pairwise agreement exceeds 0.85; UTR-ASR agreement is 0.81–0.85, with UTR higher for low-ASR models and ASR higher for high-ASR models, confirming the two metrics are complementary. Novelty Classification NEW BENCHMARK/DATASET, NEW SYSTEM/TOOL, NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The contribution is a practical system that combines automated scenario expansion, adaptive adversarial interaction, and dual-metric episode-level judgment into an end-to-end evaluation pipeline; no individual component is wholly novel (LLM-based red teaming, LLM-as-judge, and simulated tool environments each appear in prior work), but their integration into an executable, scalable, process-level evaluation framework is the genuine contribution, along with the empirical finding that process-oriented risks substantially exceed boundary-explicit permission violations across a broad model comparison. Rigor & Reproducibility Multi-judge validation with four different LLM judges (agreement >0.85) substantially strengthens confidence in the ASR metric. The dual-metric design (UTR + ASR) provides convergent validity. Two authority context conditions add a useful intervention analysis. However, no ablation studies are reported: there is no comparison of LLM-expanded scenarios vs. human-written seeds only, no test of how scenario quality varies with different expansion models, and no sensitivity analysis of the 0.55 similarity threshold or the 60-scenario selection count. Error bars in Figure 3 reflect inter-judge variance rather than sampling variance across evaluation runs; no multiple seeds or re-runs are reported. Code, scenarios, and the tool specification registry are not stated to be publicly released, and no repository URL is provided. The paper is a preprint and has not undergone peer review. A notable methodological concern: GPT-5.4 generates the scenarios, generates attacker turns, and serves as the primary judge — scenarios may be easier or harder for specific models in ways that correlate with GPT-5.4's own biases, and this is unacknowledged. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS — VESTA is a built evaluation framework evaluated through deployment against 12 target models. - Practical: The evaluation runs via API calls to commercial models; the simulated tool environment is in-memory Python with no hardware requirements beyond API access, so deployment requires only standard cloud compute. - Efficient: Episode length is capped at four turns, and the framework runs 25,728 episodes using GPT-5.4 for generation and judging; the compute cost per evaluation run is substantial (GPT-5.4 API calls for attacker + judge across 25,728 episodes) but not prohibitive for well-resourced labs; no concrete cost figures are reported. - Effective: The framework demonstrably surfaces large, consistent safety differences across 12 models with cross-judge agreement >0.85, and the UTR-ASR convergence confirms that programmatic and semantic signals align — the framework measures what it claims to measure within its simulated environment. - Scalable: The paper evaluates 12 models and 1,072 scenarios; scaling to more models is straightforward by adding API targets, but scenario coverage is fixed at the current taxonomy and extending to new risk subcategories requires human expert effort for seed design and scenario-family specification. VESTA delivers a software framework (scripts, configuration files, scenario set) but no trained models or datasets beyond the evaluation scenarios themselves; no public release is confirmed. Strengths - The adaptive attacker design is a meaningful advance over static adversarial prompts: by conditioning each attacker turn on the current trajectory, the framework can target the specific boundary the agent is approaching, producing more realistic pressure and more informative failures. - The dual-metric design (UTR for programmatic detection + ASR for semantic trajectory judgment) provides both mechanistic interpretability (which tools were called unsafely) and holistic safety assessment, and their high correlation validates both signals. - The 16-subcategory risk taxonomy with explicit failure mechanisms, authority boundaries, and judge criteria is detailed enough to be reproducible and extensible; the per-scenario-family seed-then-expand construction methodology is a practical template for future safety benchmarks. - The empirical finding that Warning Context effectively reduces ASR in Autonomy and Goal-Norm dimensions but not in Robustness is actionable: it suggests robustness failures require training-level interventions rather than system-prompt mitigations. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge three limitations: (1) the 1,072-scenario set cannot exhaust all real-world safety failures; more specialized domains, longer-horizon tasks, and richer deployment settings may reveal additional risk patterns; (2) simulated tool environments cannot fully capture the complexity, ambiguity, and operational constraints of real external systems; (3) the evaluated model versions represent a snapshot, and the ranking may shift as models are updated. The authors address the dual-use risk honestly (the framework could inform adversarial misuse) and state all experiments are conducted in controlled environments without real external systems access. The authors directly confront limitations rather than minimizing them. Analyst-Identified Gaps - GPT-5.4 serves simultaneously as scenario generator, adaptive attacker, and primary judge. This creates a systemic circularity: scenarios generated by GPT-5.4 may be written in the stylistic register that GPT-5.4 judges best understand, potentially inflating inter-judge agreement while underrepresenting failure modes that GPT-5.4 is blind to. No ablation uses a judge or generator entirely independent of the scenario-authoring model. - The four-turn episode cap is in direct tension with the Long-Horizon Instability subcategory (defined as failures that compound as task steps accumulate). Testing long-horizon instability in four turns means scenarios must be pre-seeded with artificial pressure rather than allowing natural accumulation; the paper does not discuss this tension. - No scenario-quality ablation is reported: it is unknown whether the 7 human-written seeds per subcategory already capture most of the variance in ASR, or whether the 60 LLM-expanded scenarios add meaningful coverage.
