This matters for assessing whether the automated generation actually contributes beyond a curated seed set. - The paper does not report whether or how the scenarios were validated against real agent deployments; all scenarios are constructed top-down from the taxonomy, and the taxonomy itself has no external validation (no citation to a human study confirming these five dimensions cover the relevant risk space). - The finding that Claude models achieve <10% ASR is striking but unexplained — the paper does not analyze whether this reflects genuine safety alignment, conservative refusal behavior that would harm task utility, or scenarios that happen to be easier for Claude due to the GPT-5.4-generated framing. - No scenario or code release is confirmed, limiting reproducibility and community adoption. Bottom Line VESTA is a well-engineered evaluation framework that fills a real gap — process-level, executable, multi-turn agent safety assessment at scale — and its empirical finding that most current LLM agents fail half their safety tests, with dramatic variance across models, is immediately relevant to anyone deploying LLM agents; the GPT-5.4 circularity and missing ablations mean the reported numbers should be treated as indicative rather than definitive, but the methodology is sound enough to be worth reading and extending.
Search Results 2606.08531 PDF
#129 — Moderately Innovative (58)

Reason, Then Re-reason: Cross-view Revisiting Improves Spatial Reasoning (28 ▲ )

Chaofan Ma, Zhenjie Mao, Yuhuan Yang, Fanqin Zeng, Yue Shi, Yingjie Zhou, Xiaofeng Cao, Jiangchao Yao · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 11 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces ReRe (Reason, then Re-reason), a training-free, inference-time framework that improves egocentric video spatial reasoning by prompting a frozen MLLM to form a provisional hypothesis on the original video, then verify or revise it against a synthesized allocentric novel-view video. - Why it's important: Single-turn spatial reasoning from egocentric video is structurally fragile because the camera trajectory constrains what is observable, forcing models to rely on semantic priors rather than verifiable geometric evidence, which produces systematic hallucinations in object counting, distance estimation, and navigation tasks. - How it does it: A VGGT-based Geometry-to-Video pipeline recovers a 3D point cloud from the egocentric video, plans an Oblique Sweep camera trajectory (diagonal, 45° elevated), and rasterizes it into standard video frames that a frozen MLLM can consume as a focused verification session against its prior thinking trace. - The good: On VSI-Bench, ReRe lifts Qwen3-VL-4B from 30.7 to 36.5 average (+5.8%), enabling it to rival proprietary GPT-4o (34.0), and a sample-level flip analysis shows corrections outnumber regressions at a 2.52:1 ratio, confirming the net information value of the synthesized view. - The missing: Performance remains far below human level (~36 vs. 79.2 on VSI-Bench tiny), and the re-reason prompt explicitly primes the model that "the answer may be incorrect," a bias that could inflate revision rates independent of geometric evidence quality. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on inference-time enhancement of MLLMs or egocentric spatial understanding; less valuable for those seeking fundamentally new algorithms, as the contribution is a well-executed compositional framework rather than a novel method.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (54) and Non-Obviousness (57) are the limiting dimensions: the contribution is a well-executed compositional inference framework (CoT + VGGT + rasterization + multi-turn prompting), not a new algorithm, and the authors' own framing concedes this. Peer review (ICML 2026) supports Rigor (60), but no significance tests, single-seed, and a re-reason prompt that explicitly primes revision (an uncontrolled confound) weaken Resilience (58). Gains are modest in absolute terms (~36 vs 79 human) and near-zero/negative on temporal subtasks. Weighted: 0.20*54+0.25*60+0.15*57+0.15*62+0.15*58+0.10*60 = 58.
empirical Novelty 54 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 57 Completeness 62 Resilience 58 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) tasked with spatial queries over egocentric videos—counting objects, estimating distances, planning routes—must infer 3D scene geometry from footage whose viewpoint is constrained by the recording camera's trajectory. Occluded regions, ambiguous depth, and weak spatiotemporal correspondence leave the 3D layout underdetermined. Existing methods, whether training-based (Video-R1, SpaceR) or training-free (See&Trek), commit to a single-turn answer under these constraints, resolving geometric uncertainty via learned semantic priors. Prior work that injects VGGT geometric features into MLLMs does so implicitly through latent alignment, which still produces single-turn answers and requires architectural modification or fine-tuning. The gap is the absence of a mechanism for a frozen MLLM to explicitly revisit a spatial hypothesis when complementary viewpoint evidence is available. Key Idea: The core insight is that spatial reasoning should be decomposed into hypothesis formation and cross-view verification: the model first articulates its spatial inferences explicitly as a thinking trace tied to a provisional answer, then receives a synthesized allocentric video that exposes scene structure from a complementary vantage point and is instructed to treat the trace as a checklist to accept or reject. The critical move is making geometry consumable as standard video frames (not point clouds or latent features) so a frozen MLLM can apply its native video reasoning capabilities without any fine-tuning. Methodology: ReRe operates in two sequential phases. In Phase 1 (Reason), the frozen MLLM receives 8 uniformly sampled frames from the egocentric video Vego plus a structured chain-of-thought prompt decomposed into three objectives—Observe, Infer, Conclude—and produces a hypothesis H consisting of a thinking trace T enclosed in `<think>` tags and a provisional answer Ã in `<answer>` tags. In Phase 2 (Re-reason), a Geometry-to-Video pipeline synthesizes an allocentric video Vexo: VGGT processes the full 1-fps egocentric video to predict a 3D point cloud; Trajectory Planning computes the scene center c and horizontal radius r (95th-percentile of ground-plane point distances), then defines an Oblique Sweep camera path p(t) = c + r(1−2t)·d where d = normalize([1, √2, 1]ᵀ), traversing from c+rd to c−rd at a fixed 45° elevation with constant viewing direction; View Rendering rasterizes using z-buffer depth ordering, confidence filtering (threshold 0.5), and per-frame median filtering to suppress artifacts. The resulting 8-frame allocentric video is fed to the frozen MLLM alongside H and Q, with a re-reason prompt instructing Compare → Reflect → Confirm. No parameters are updated at any stage. Three critical design decisions: (1) sequential rather than joint reasoning—ablations show concatenated or interleaved multi-view inputs underperform ReRe, because joint inputs are out-of-distribution for frozen MLLMs and produce cognitive overload without a verification objective; (2) Oblique Sweep over Bird's-eye Orbit or Mid-level Traverse—the elevated oblique angle reduces occlusion while preserving enough canonical visual features for semantic grounding; (3) retaining Vego as the semantic anchor rather than relying solely on Vexo—the allocentric view lacks fine-grained texture necessary for reliable object identification. Key Results: On VSI-Bench (5,000+ QA pairs from 288 real-world egocentric videos), ReRe yields average improvements of +1.0 to +8.5 points across eight model variants in four MLLM families (Qwen2.5-VL, Qwen3-VL, InternVL2.5, InternVL3). The strongest gains occur in object size estimation (+20.7 for Qwen3-VL-2B), absolute distance (+8.7), and object counting (+6.8). Route planning and appearance order see smaller or occasionally negative deltas. Qwen3-VL-4B reaches 36.5 vs. proprietary GPT-4o at 34.0. On the Static Understanding subset of STI-Bench (Dimensional Measurement, Spatial Relation, 3D Video Grounding), ReRe lifts Qwen3-VL-2B by 8.0 average (18.5 on Spatial Relation alone) and InternVL2.5-8B to 34.8, surpassing GPT-4o (31.0). Ablation (Table 3, Qwen2.5-VL-7B): Baseline 24.8 → Concat 25.4 → Interleaved 25.6 → ReRe 29.5, confirming the sequential revisiting protocol rather than mere information availability drives gains. Component ablation (Table 4): re-reasoning on Vego alone (without new view) degrades to 23.5, below the 24.8 baseline; Vexo alone without prior hypothesis reaches 27.3; the full Vego+Vexo pipeline achieves 29.5. Trajectory comparison (Table 5): Mid-level Traverse 25.6, Bird's-eye Orbit 27.4, Oblique Sweep 29.5. Sample-level flip analysis on Qwen3-VL-8B: 15.65% of samples change; of those, 71.6% are positive (wrong→correct) vs. 28.4% negative (2.52:1). ReRe also improves already-specialized spatial models: SpaceR-3B 34.69→35.96, SpatialLadder-3B 44.84→45.58. Latency on A100: ~11s/sample (VGGT dominates at ~9s); reducing VGGT input to 20 frames cuts to ~4s with +2.8 gain retained. Human level on VSI-Bench tiny is 79.2; best ReRe result is ~36, leaving a large gap. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD. The genuine novelty is the revisitable inference paradigm: decomposing spatial reasoning into hypothesis formation plus explicit cross-view verification using geometry-synthesized video rendered in a native MLLM-compatible format without any training. The individual components—chain-of-thought prompting, VGGT for 3D reconstruction, point-cloud rasterization, multi-turn MLLM interaction—are all prior work; the contribution is their integration into a coherent inference protocol with a specifically designed trajectory and a structured dialectical prompt format. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and informative: Table 3 tests three alternative reasoning protocols, Table 4 disentangles view source from reasoning depth, Table 5 compares three trajectory designs, and Table 6 provides sample-level flip statistics. However, no error bars or statistical significance tests are reported for any comparison. Experiments use a single low-temperature setting (T=0.1, top-p=0.001), and it is unclear whether results are averaged across multiple seeds. Code is not stated to be released (a project page at zhenjiemao.github.io/ReRe is listed but does not imply code availability). Evaluation is conducted on standard public benchmarks (VSI-Bench, STI-Bench) with baselines including both open-source and proprietary models, though proprietary model results are taken from prior work rather than re-run under identical conditions. The methodology is reproducible in principle given publicly available VGGT and the described pipeline parameters. The paper is peer-reviewed (ICML 2026). Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (methods and systems run in experiments). - Practical: Runs on a single A100 GPU using publicly available VGGT and frozen open-source MLLMs; no custom hardware required. - Efficient: End-to-end latency is ~11s per sample vs. ~1s baseline, an 11× overhead dominated by VGGT (9s); reducible to ~4s at 20-frame VGGT input with partial performance retention, and further reducible via FastVGGT/LiteVGGT drop-ins. - Effective: The 2.52:1 positive-to-negative flip ratio and consistent gains across 9 model variants on two benchmarks support the claim that the synthesized view carries useful signal; however, performance remains well below human level (best ~37 vs. 79.2) and gains on appearance-order and some multi-choice subtasks are near zero or negative. - Scalable: Only tested on indoor room-scale egocentric videos (VSI-Bench, STI-Bench); behavior in larger environments, outdoor scenes, or longer video sequences is not evaluated. The work delivers a reusable inference-time framework (no trained artifact) applicable to any frozen MLLM that accepts video input. Strengths: - The revisitable-reasoning paradigm is a principled reframing of the single-turn limitation, and the component ablation (Table 4) cleanly demonstrates that both the new view and the structured hypothesis are independently necessary—neither suffices alone. - The Oblique Sweep trajectory design is well-motivated (occlusion reduction + canonical viewpoint preservation) and empirically validated against two alternatives with identical rendering cost. - The framework is genuinely training-free and architecture-agnostic, as demonstrated by consistent gains across four diverse MLLM families and two specialized spatial models (SpaceR, SpatialLadder). - The flip analysis (Table 6) provides mechanistic evidence that coarse geometry information value outweighs reconstruction noise, which is a non-obvious empirical finding. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors acknowledge two limitations directly. First, monocular 3D reconstruction via VGGT is inherently ill-posed and introduces noise; they mitigate this through confidence filtering and by keeping Vego as the semantic anchor but do not claim the issue is fully resolved. Second, the two-phase pipeline adds ~10s per sample on an A100, with the VGGT forward pass as the bottleneck; they note this is modular and will improve as faster geometry priors emerge. The authors confront both limitations constructively rather than minimizing them, and propose concrete mitigation paths (FastVGGT, caching per scene). Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The re-reason prompt explicitly states "The answer may be incorrect due to limited viewpoints," which primes the model toward revision independently of the new view's content; no control condition tests a re-reason prompt without the novel view to isolate this priming effect from geometric information gain. - No statistical significance tests or confidence intervals are reported; given that some gains are in the 1–2 point range and some subtasks regress, it is unclear which individual results are reliable. - Evaluation is restricted to indoor room-scale scenes; the method's generalization to outdoor, dynamic, or large-scale environments is unstated and uncertain given VGGT's training distribution. - The proprietary model baselines (Gemini, GPT-4o) are not wrapped with ReRe, so it is unknown whether the framework would also improve proprietary models or whether the gains are specific to open-source models' higher hallucination rates. - Gains on temporally-grounded tasks (appearance order) are consistently near zero or negative, suggesting the allocentric view is uninformative for dynamic/temporal queries; the authors acknowledge this implicitly in the prompt ("If the question relates to temporal order, primarily maintain your answer from the first round") but do not analyze when cross-view verification fails. - Absolute performance on VSI-Bench (~36) remains less than half of human level (79.2), which limits the claim that the framework "rivals proprietary state-of-the-art performance" to a comparison against underperforming proprietary baselines, not against the task ceiling. Bottom Line: Practitioners working on inference-time enhancement for egocentric spatial reasoning will find the ReRe framework directly applicable and the ablations credible, but should be aware that the gains are modest in absolute terms, statistically unvalidated, and untested outside indoor room-scale domains.
Recommended 2606.11683 PDF
#130 — Moderately Innovative (58)

TabSODA: Tabular Diffusion based Imputation with Skip Pattern Detection and Ordinal Awareness

Y Chen, T Kim, H Shu, Y Feng · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces TabSODA, an EM-based diffusion imputer for mixed-type survey data that explicitly propagates a three-state mask (observed / true-missing / structural-skip) through the EDM denoising loss and reverse-time sampler, and encodes ordinal variables as cumulative-probit scalar latents with learned cutpoints. - Why it's important: Large-scale health surveys such as PATH and NSDUH contain blank cells induced by questionnaire routing logic (structural skips) that, if treated as ordinary missingness, produce invalid imputations and corrupt downstream prevalence and regression estimates used in health-policy research. - How it does it: Structural-skip cells are assigned zero reconstruction weight in the EDM loss, clamped at every reverse Heun step, and excluded from evaluation; when a codebook skip mask is unavailable, a CART-based miner estimates it from raw missingness and questionnaire order using Wilson UCB screening, Fisher's exact test, and Benjamini-Hochberg FDR control. - The good: TabSODA reduces ordinal MACE by 23.7% on PATH relative to the strongest baseline across MCAR, MAR, and MNAR conditions, evaluated over 50 paired replications with standard errors, against a current and comprehensive set of baselines including DiffPuter, TabDiff, and TabSyn. - The missing: The ordinal cumulative-probit encoding benefit is inconsistent: the analog-bit fallback variant (TabSODA-N) outperforms TabSODA on ordinal accuracy for PATH under several masking conditions, and the evaluation is restricted to two U.S. health surveys curated by the authors with no runtime cost reporting. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners working on imputation in structured surveys with ordinal responses and questionnaire skip logic; less relevant if the tabular data lacks these specific structural features.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Completeness (56) and Resilience (52) pull the composite down: the ordinal cumulative-probit encoding — a headline contribution — is inconsistently beneficial (the analog-bit fallback beats it on PATH under several masking conditions) and this is reported but not explained, the NSDUH+mined-skip degradation is undiagnosed, and the PATH benchmark was author-augmented in a way that may favor the method. Novelty (65) is real (three-state mask propagation inside the EDM loss and Heun sampler). Rigor (62) helped by the 50-replication paired protocol but capped by narrow two-survey scope, anonymous-only code, and no runtime data.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 62 Completeness 56 Resilience 52 Impact 52
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Large-scale health and social-science surveys — specifically the Population Assessment of Tobacco and Health (PATH) study and the National Survey on Drug Use and Health (NSDUH) — routinely generate two qualitatively different types of missing cells: structural skips (blank cells rendered inapplicable by questionnaire routing, e.g., tobacco follow-up items for never-smokers) and item nonresponse (applicable cells left unanswered). Existing tabular diffusion imputers conflate these under a binary observed/missing mask, causing the model to attempt imputation of structurally inapplicable cells, producing out-of-domain values, distorting item denominators, and violating skip logic. Separately, survey instruments make extensive use of ordinal variables (Likert scales, frequency bands, severity ratings) where category order carries information, but generic tabular pipelines encode them as nominal labels via one-hot or analog-bit encodings, discarding adjacency information. The survey methodology literature has long recognized both problems, but no generative imputer has incorporated both corrections within a single joint model. Key Idea The core insight is to make the diffusion process structurally aware of non-imputable cells by extending the three-state mask directly into the EDM loss and reverse sampler: skip entries are zeroed from the reconstruction objective, clamped to a fixed inapplicable state at every reverse Heun step, and excluded from conditional sampling — so the denoiser never learns spurious associations with or generates values for structurally inapplicable cells. Simultaneously, ordinal variables are represented as continuous scalar latents in a cumulative-probit space where geometrically adjacent values correspond to adjacent ordinal categories, giving the diffusion model an implicit ordinal geometry without requiring categorical argmax at intermediate steps. Methodology TabSODA inherits the EM backbone of DiffPuter, alternating between an E-step (conditional reverse diffusion sampling of missing cells given observed cells and the denoiser from the previous M-step) and an M-step (EDM denoiser training on the current completed table). Four components distinguish it from DiffPuter: 1. CART-based skip miner: For each target variable processed in questionnaire order, CART is trained to predict raw missingness using only earlier items (raw responses, missingness indicators, and propagated learned skip states). Candidate rules are screened by Wilson 95% upper confidence bounds on false-positive rate (FPR) and false-negative rate (FNR), with position-adjusted caps to accommodate sparse late-follow-up items. Rules passing the Wilson screen are tested for association with target missingness via Fisher's exact test, and Benjamini-Hochberg FDR control is applied at q=0.01. Only raw-blank cells covered by accepted rules are promoted to structural skips. This is a high-precision rule generator, not a classifier — observed cells are never overwritten. 2. Ordinal-aware mixed-type encoding: Ordinal columns with dominant category proportion d_j < 0.70 are encoded as scalar cumulative-probit latents, where observed category k maps to interval (b_{j,k-1}, b_{j,k}]. Cutpoints are parameterized via softplus reparameterization to guarantee strict ordering, initialized from a smoothed empirical CDF, and warm-started across EM iterations. Columns with d_j ≥ 0.70 fall back to analog-bit encoding (unstable cutpoints). The stochastic truncated-normal encoder at r ≥ 1 is the diffusion-time analog of Albert-Chib data augmentation, propagating within-category uncertainty into denoiser and cutpoint updates. 3. Skip-aware EDM loss: The M-step loss (Eq. 10) multiplies reconstruction weights by a skip-exclusion mask w = 1 - s, so structural-skip entries contribute zero gradient. The denoiser receives the skip mask as an additional conditioning channel (p extra input dimensions). The full objective (Eq. 12) combines the diffusion loss with auxiliary cumulative-link NLL and Ranked Probability Score terms for ordinal columns and prototype cross-entropy for nominal columns, weighted by (λ_cont, λ_nom, λ_ord) = (0.25, 1.0, 3.0) selected by one-pass grid search. 4. Per-column temperature calibration: A held-out 20% validation pool per ordinal column is used to select a temperature τ*_j from {0.5, 0.7, 1.0, 1.4, 2.0} by lexicographic minimization of (-ACC, MACE, NLL, |τ-1|). Unlike softmax temperature, cumulative-probit temperature can change the MAP prediction because per-class probabilities are CDF differences rather than monotone logit transforms. The three-phase blockwise M-step (pre-calibrate cutpoints → update denoiser with cutpoints frozen → post-calibrate cutpoints) and the position-adjusted rule-screening are the two most critical design decisions; both are justified but incompletely ablated. Key Results On PATH (N=32,320, d=57: 1 continuous, 32 nominal, 24 ordinal) under 30% MAR, TabSODA achieves Ord. MACE=0.455±0.087 vs. the best baseline MissForest at 0.596±0.083 (23.7% reduction), Cat. Acc.=0.900±0.018 vs. MissForest's 0.811±0.017, and Nom. Acc.=0.983±0.006. On NSDUH (N=58,034, d=29) under 30% MAR, TabSODA achieves Ord. MACE=0.362±0.041 vs. TabSyn's 0.399±0.054 (9.8% reduction) and Cat. Acc.=0.739±0.020. Numerical RMSE is mixed: TabDiff wins on PATH RMSE (13.016 vs. 13.211), while TabSODA wins on NSDUH RMSE (53.114 vs. 56.313 for MissForest). The skip miner achieves precision 0.999/F1 0.995 on PATH-50 and precision 0.943/F1 0.859 on NSDUH-100. All imputation results are averaged over L=50 paired replications. Results under MCAR and MNAR (Appendix I) qualitatively confirm the MAR pattern. A surprising finding: TabSODA-N (analog-bit fallback for all categoricals) outperforms TabSODA on ordinal accuracy for PATH under MAR (0.717 vs. 0.703) and MNAR (0.734 vs. 0.706), suggesting the cumulative-probit encoding is not uniformly beneficial. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The three-state mask propagation inside the EDM loss and Heun sampler is genuinely new. The CART-based skip miner with position-adjusted Wilson screening is a novel engineering contribution for a recognized practical problem. The cumulative-probit scalar encoding within a diffusion imputer is a novel combination, though cumulative-probit models and diffusion imputers are individually established. The integration of all three components into a single EM-diffusion framework is the paper's primary contribution — this is not a straightforward combination; it required rethinking the conditioning structure and loss masking of the EDM denoiser. Rigor & Reproducibility The 50-replication paired evaluation with standard errors is stronger than typical tabular imputation work. Ablations (Tables 3, 9, 10) isolate skip masking and ordinal encoding contributions across all three masking mechanisms. The skip miner is evaluated independently from imputation accuracy (Table 4) on separate variable subsets. The main methodological concern is the auxiliary loss weights (λ_cont, λ_nom, λ_ord) selected by one-pass grid search rather than nested cross-validation — the paper acknowledges this but does not quantify the sensitivity. The PATH benchmark was augmented with 9 author-selected ordinal variables to "densify the ordinal subset," which may bias the evaluation toward conditions favorable to ordinal encoding. The τ_route = 0.70 threshold and skip miner hyperparameters (η_FPR = 0.007, etc.) are fixed before imputation evaluation but were presumably developed on the same datasets. Code is promised at an anonymous URL, consistent with preprint status; peer review has not occurred (submitted to STAI-X 2026). No runtime/compute cost comparisons are reported. Deployability (EMPIRICAL profile) - Practical: Runs on standard academic GPU hardware; the MLP denoiser (H=1024 for PATH, H=512 for NSDUH) with batch size 4096 is tractable for the tested dataset scales. - Efficient: The dominant cost per EM iteration is E-step sampling (50 Heun steps × 20 Monte Carlo imputations × N respondents); no wall-clock numbers are reported, making it impossible to assess overhead relative to baselines such as MissForest or DiffPuter. - Effective: Consistently improves ordinal and categorical metrics over all six baselines on both datasets; numerical RMSE improvements are mixed and TabDiff beats TabSODA on PATH RMSE in multiple conditions, indicating the method is not uniformly superior. - Scalable: Tested on N up to 58,034 respondents and d up to 200 variables (skip miner evaluation only). No experiments at larger scale; the CART miner processes variables sequentially in questionnaire order, making it O(d) tree fits with inherently forward-chained dependencies. The paper promises a software artifact (code at anonymous URL) but no trained model weights or dataset splits are released in this preprint. Strengths - Propagating the three-state skip mask directly inside the EDM loss and reverse sampler (rather than as a preprocessing step) is architecturally principled: it prevents the denoiser from learning correlations with skip cells and avoids the need for post-hoc correction of imputed values. - The CART-based skip miner's combination of forward-only causal construction, Wilson UCB screening, and BH FDR control achieves near-perfect precision (0.999 on PATH) without accessing the codebook, solving a real operational problem in survey data pipelines where codebooks are incomplete or expensive to encode. - The 50-replication paired evaluation protocol with standard errors, shared seeds across methods and masking mechanisms, and rank-based MAR/MNAR generators is methodologically rigorous and enables credible statistical comparison. - The cumulative-probit temperature calibration (Appendix C) is a non-obvious contribution: unlike softmax temperature, temperature scaling in the cumulative-probit setting can change the MAP prediction because per-class probabilities are CDF differences, and the paper provides both analysis and a data-driven selection procedure. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors note that TabSODA does not handle longitudinal surveys with wave-specific skip logic, time-varying ordinal responses, or respondent-level temporal dependence, and flag this as future work. They acknowledge that the τ_route = 0.70 routing threshold is fixed and that the one-pass hyperparameter grid search is a simplification. They explicitly recommend using codebook masks when available, implicitly conceding the mined mask is second-best. These are honest scope statements rather than minimizations. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The ordinal encoding benefit is inconsistent and inadequately explained: TabSODA-N outperforms TabSODA on ordinal accuracy for PATH under MAR (0.717 vs. 0.703), MCAR (0.710 vs. 0.703), and MNAR (0.734 vs. 0.706) in the ablation tables. The paper reports these numbers but offers only a brief qualitative note that "analog-bit fallback remains a strong choice when ordinal columns have lower cardinality" — this leaves the conditions under which cumulative-probit encoding helps uncharacterized. - The NSDUH+SKIP degradation is not diagnosed: TabSODA+SKIP achieves Ord. MACE 0.499 vs. TabSODA's 0.362 under MAR on NSDUH (Table 3) — a 38% degradation from the codebook variant — suggesting mined false-positive skip assignments are corrupting valid imputation targets. The paper notes the small gap on PATH but does not analyze why the gap is large on NSDUH. - Evaluation is restricted to two U.S. national health surveys from the same data-collection ecosystem (SAMHSA/NIH), both curated by the authors. Generalization to census, educational, clinical, or political surveys with different skip complexities and ordinal distributions is undemonstrated. - No runtime comparisons: with CART mining, 5 EM iterations each with 20 Monte Carlo E-step imputations, blockwise M-step calibration, and per-column temperature search, TabSODA's wall-clock cost relative to MICE or MissForest is unknown and likely substantial for practitioners with thousands of variables. - The PATH benchmark augmentation (9 author-selected ordinal variables added to "densify the ordinal subset") constitutes a curation choice that may favor TabSODA's ordinal encoding contribution; a pre-specified or publicly standardized benchmark split would strengthen the evaluation. Bottom Line Practitioners imputing missing data in large-scale health or social surveys with questionnaire-induced structural skips and ordinal variables will find genuine, well-validated improvements in TabSODA, but the inconsistent ordinal encoding benefit, the absence of runtime cost data, and the narrow two-survey evaluation scope leave open whether the method's advantages persist across broader survey contexts.
Citations 2606.05361 PDF
#131 — Moderately Innovative (58)

Generative Molecular Morphing for Flexible-Size Design via Unbalanced Optimal Transport

M Franke, SP Schmid, Z Ivkovic, K Jorner, A Krause · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Morph, a discrete-continuous flow-based generative model for 3D molecular design that dynamically changes atom count during generation by performing simultaneous node insertions, deletions, and substitutions on geometric graphs. - Why it's important: Every current diffusion/flow-based 3D molecular generator fixes the number of atoms at initialization, preventing the model from learning the joint distribution of molecular properties and size and making property steering to size-dependent targets (logP, QED, drug-likeness) and scaffold decoration structurally impossible. - How it does it: Training pairs (prior graph, target molecule) are aligned via Unbalanced Optimal Transport solved as an augmented bipartite assignment problem (Hungarian + Kabsch), which partitions atoms into matched, inserted, and deleted sets; a bounded-hazard CTMC with separate interpolation paths per partition is then trained under the Conditional Generator Matching framework. - The good: Morph generates valid molecules with up to 40% more atoms than the largest training example on QM9, succeeding at OOD size conditioning where fixed-size models fail entirely by construction. - The missing: On GEOM-Drugs—the dataset most relevant to real drug discovery—molecular stability drops to 85.66% versus SemlaFlow's 97.3%, a ~12-point gap the paper does not substantively address; no ablation tests whether UOT matching is actually responsible for the gains over simpler alternatives. - Read it or not: Researchers working on generative models for molecules or flexible-size graph generation should read it; practitioners prioritizing GEOM-Drugs-quality generation should wait for a follow-up that closes the stability gap.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (72) is the strength — a genuine NEW METHOD + NEW FORMULATION composing CGM, Edit Flows, and UOT into a trans-dimensional generator that solves OOD size conditioning and scaffold decoration where fixed-size models fail by construction. But Rigor (50) and Resilience (50) pull the composite down: the GEOM-Drugs molecular-stability gap (85.66% vs. SemlaFlow's 97.3%) is left essentially unexamined, the core UOT-matching contribution is never ablated against simpler couplings, scaffold uniqueness collapses to 50.34%, and no code is released. Composite: 0.20*72 + 0.25*50 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*60 + 0.15*50 + 0.10*40 = 58.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 50 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 60 Resilience 50 Impact 40
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Practitioners in computational drug discovery and materials science use 3D generative models to propose candidate molecules with target physical or pharmacological properties. A persistent limitation is that virtually all diffusion- and flow-based 3D generators (EDM, MiDi, EQGAT-diff, SemlaFlow, FlowMol) fix the atom count n at the start of each generation trajectory, sampling it from an empirical training distribution and holding it constant. This decoupling means the model cannot learn the true joint distribution p(molecule, size) and cannot steer toward properties that require a different molecular size than those seen in training. It also makes scaffold decoration—appending atoms to a fixed substructure—structurally incompatible with the generative framework. Prior flexible-size approaches in 3D were restricted to insertions only (AR and jump-diffusion models), preventing them from using larger prior graphs that require shrinking, which limits the use of informative structural priors. Key Idea: The core technical insight is that, once inter-graph alignment is established by UOT, training a flexible-size 3D generator reduces to a supervised regression problem over conditional paths—one path per edit-set partition—making the trans-dimensional problem tractable without modifying the underlying CGM framework. The UOT alignment recasts the exponentially hard graph-edit-distance problem as a polynomial bipartite assignment (Hungarian algorithm, O(n³)) by zeroing all edge-edit costs, which is justified because 3D molecular bond topology is inferrable from geometry and valency. A bounded-hazard rate construction (λ = Δ · κ̇_t/(1−κ_t)) guarantees that all pending discrete operations terminate by t=1 without requiring explicit boundary conditions. Methodology: Morph models the generation trajectory as a jump-flow process over the trans-dimensional graph space G (disjoint union of SE(3)×S_n quotient spaces for each atom count). The generator decomposes into a continuous flow component (position ODE) and a jump component (CTMC with rate density u_t). Training uses Conditional Generator Matching: the marginal generator is a conditional expectation over endpoint-coupled paths, so fitting a network to regress conditional targets recovers the correct dynamics at the optimum. The training pipeline has three stages. (1) UOT matching: Construct an (n0+n1)×(n0+n1) augmented cost matrix C with match costs wmove·‖x0,i−x1,j‖₂ + wtype·𝟙(a0,i≠a1,j), fixed deletion costs wdel, and fixed insertion costs wins. Solve with Hungarian algorithm; apply Kabsch rotation for SE(3) alignment. (2) Interpolation: Matched nodes follow a Gaussian path on positions and a discrete κ_t^sub schedule on types; insertion nodes appear at a sampled event time tins, with position drawn from a shrinking Gaussian centered on target; deletion nodes stay fixed at prior values until their sampled tdel, then are removed. (3) Losses: Poisson NLL on per-node insertion count Δ^ins; BCE on deletion flag Δ^del; BCE + cross-entropy on atom/edge substitution; MSE on positions of surviving nodes; auxiliary cross-entropy on formal charge. Three critical design decisions: (i) zeroing edge costs in UOT (tractability, justified by geometry→topology inference); (ii) bounded hazard rate (guarantees termination, allows network to learn only Δ rather than the full rate schedule); (iii) Poisson insertion count head (reconciles batch training with infinitesimal-step CTMC theory). The backbone is SemlaFlow's SE(3)-equivariant latent node attention model (~22M parameters); a GMM head with equivariant means predicts joint position/atom-type distributions for inserted atoms. Smoothstep schedules use α=0.8 for insertions/deletions and α=1.5 for substitutions, ensuring structural edits resolve before final refinement. Key Results: On QM9 de-novo generation (Table 1, 10k samples, 3 seeds), Morph achieves atom stability 100.0%, molecule stability 99.37%, validity 99.41%, at 100 NFE—matching SemlaFlow (99.7% mol stab, 99.4% validity) and outperforming EQGAT-diff (98.7% / 99.0%) and MiDi (97.5% / 97.9%) which require 500 NFE. Uniqueness is 94.17% versus SemlaFlow's 95.4%. On GEOM-Drugs (Table 5), Morph shows atom stability 99.98% but molecular stability 85.66% ± 0.40 and validity 85.16% ± 0.49, well below SemlaFlow (97.3% / 93.9%) and EQGAT-diff (93.4% / 94.6%). PoseBusters on GEOM-Drugs (Table 4) shows internal steric clash at 95.5% and no-radicals at 96.8%, indicating real but limited physical quality concerns. OOD size conditioning on QM9 (training max ≈ 29 atoms): validity at ncond=30 is 79.9%, drops to 74.0% at ncond=32, 34.1% at ncond=34, and below 20% beyond ncond=38; fixed-size baselines cannot perform this task at all. Scaffold decoration on QM9 achieves 92.54% validity and 99.85% atom stability but only 50.34% uniqueness. Multi-property conditioning (QED + logP) succeeds for in-distribution combinations but fails entirely for far-OOD tuples (e.g., logP=−2.0, QED=0.7 produced no valid molecule). Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW FORMULATION. The genuine novelty is composing three existing frameworks—Conditional Generator Matching (Holderrieth et al. 2025), Edit Flows (Havasi et al. 2025), and Unbalanced Optimal Transport (Séjourné et al. 2023)—into a coherent trans-dimensional generative model for SE(3)-equivariant discrete-continuous geometric graphs. The UOT-as-augmented-assignment reformulation and its integration with the bounded-hazard CTMC for 3D geometry are original contributions; the individual components are prior work. Rigor & Reproducibility: Error bars over 3 seeds are reported throughout. PoseBusters provides a rigorous physical plausibility check beyond standard stability/validity metrics. However, no ablations test the core UOT matching against simpler alternatives (random matching, nearest-neighbor, or fixed-size coupling), leaving the contribution of that specific component unmeasured. Loss weight tuning is explicitly deferred; the authors note 2.5× more training epochs are needed versus SemlaFlow due to untuned loss balancing, which means the QM9 comparison may understate Morph's potential but also raises concerns about training stability. No code, model weights, or data splits beyond citing PropMolFlow's corrected QM9 are released, making reproduction non-trivial given the multiple hyperparameters (wdel, wins, wmove, wtype, schedule α values). Backbone ablation is explicitly deferred. Deployability (4-property check): EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: QM9 models train on 4 RTX 4090 GPUs in under 32 hours; GEOM-Drugs requires 20 GH200 GPUs for 24 hours. QM9-scale work is feasible on academic hardware; drug-scale work requires HPC. - Efficient: At 22M parameters and 100 NFE, the model is parameter-competitive with SemlaFlow, but needs 2.5× more training epochs and the CTMC nature structurally precludes consistency distillation or few-step sampling approaches that have accelerated fixed-size competitors. - Effective: On QM9, the method demonstrably solves the stated problem (OOD size generation, scaffold decoration). On GEOM-Drugs, the 85.66% molecular stability versus 97.3% for SemlaFlow is a significant gap; for drug discovery applications where structural quality is paramount, this performance level is materially below SemlaFlow. - Scalable: Evaluated on molecules up to 72 atoms (GEOM-Drugs); Hungarian algorithm scales O(n³) per training sample, which becomes a potential bottleneck for larger systems. No experiments on larger molecular systems or materials. OOD generation degrades rapidly beyond ~33 atoms on QM9 (trained to max ~29). No software artifact is released; the work stops at paper form. Strengths: - The trans-dimensional formulation over G as a disjoint union of quotient spaces, combined with bounded hazard rates, is mathematically clean and guarantees correct termination with no ad-hoc termination criterion—a genuine design improvement over padding-based approaches. - The augmented assignment reduction of UOT to a bipartite matching problem is a practical contribution that makes the otherwise intractable inter-graph alignment problem efficiently solvable without approximation (for moderate molecule sizes). - Scaffold decoration emerges naturally from the framework with high validity (92.54%) without any task-specific design, demonstrating that the architectural choice has direct practical payoff for lead optimization workflows. - Demonstrating OOD size generation where prior models fail by construction is a concrete, falsifiable empirical contribution rather than a marginal benchmark improvement. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors acknowledge: (1) 2.5× more training epochs required due to loss complexity; loss weight tuning is deferred. (2) Evaluation is primarily on QM9; GEOM-Drugs results are described as preliminary and needing further evaluation. (3) CTMC nature makes few-step generation hard; the steerability-vs-efficiency trade-off is deferred. (4) Detailed OOD behavior analysis is deferred. (5) Only the SemlaFlow backbone is tested; backbone ablation is left to future work. The authors confront these limitations directly rather than minimizing them, which is credible. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The GEOM-Drugs molecular stability gap (85.66% vs. 97.3% SemlaFlow) is the most significant result in the paper for practitioners and receives only a single sentence acknowledgment; the source of this degradation (loss balancing, insufficient training, architectural mismatch with larger graphs) is not investigated. No ablation of UOT versus simpler inter-graph couplings is provided, despite this being the primary technical contribution claimed. Uniqueness on scaffold decoration is only 50.34%—evidence of mode collapse under scaffold conditioning—that is not discussed. The OOD generation claim of "up to 40% more atoms" requires ncond=35+ where validity is 11.9–34.1% and exact-match rate is 0%; the practically useful OOD window is narrow and not clearly delineated. The corrected QM9 dataset from PropMolFlow [24] is non-standard; comparisons to baselines trained on the original QM9 may not be fully apples-to-apples. Bottom Line: A practitioner working on 3D molecular generation should read this paper for the UOT-alignment technique and the scaffold decoration capability, but should not adopt Morph as a drop-in replacement for SemlaFlow on GEOM-Drugs until the stability gap is closed.
Citations 2606.07239 PDF
#132 — Moderately Innovative (58)

The Two Pillars

RL Yáñez · papers.rlabs.cl · ai agents regulated industries - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This monograph introduces a two-pillar structural theory arguing that generative AI forces software practitioners into a new cognitive mode ("mixer mode"—continuous multi-axis judgment rather than sequential discrete roles) and forces organizations to build automated "meta-software" to supervise machine-pace agentic production. - Why it's important: Organizations adopting agentic AI without redesigning their cognitive practices and supervisory infrastructure face predictable failure; Gartner projects 30%+ of GenAI projects abandoned post-PoC and 40%+ of agentic AI projects canceled by 2027, and the thesis argues these failures share a common structural cause. - How it does it: Derives the two pillars from a deliberately narrow "deflationary" observation (code production is ceasing to be the binding constraint, without naming a successor bottleneck) via analytic autoethnography from a dual founder/corporate-manager position, anchored in Dreyfus/Polanyi/Hutchins cognitive science and Christensen/O'Reilly-Tushman innovation theory. - The good: Exceptional intellectual honesty—explicit conflict-of-interest disclosure, stated falsifiability conditions for every major claim, acknowledged unexecuted triangulation—combined with a genuinely original cognitive construct (the mixer mode) grounded in established theory. - The missing: The multi-case triangulation protocol (12–15 informants, specified in full in Chapter 8) is not yet executed, so all 13 experiential patterns remain hypotheses from a single dual N=1 case; no new quantitative measurements are produced by the author. - Read it or not: Worth reading for technology leaders and organizational researchers navigating the AI transition, but premature as a basis for organizational strategy commitments—this is theory-elaboration awaiting empirical validation, not completed empirical science.
Why this rank: Scored using Theoretical profile. Non-Obviousness (65) and Novelty (65) drive the score upward: the 'mixer mode' is a genuinely original named construct for post-AI expert cognition, grounded in Dreyfus/Polanyi/Hutchins, and the deflationary framing is a deliberate, non-obvious methodological move. Rigor (45) is capped by a dual N=1 autoethnographic base with an explicitly unexecuted triangulation protocol and preprint (non-peer-reviewed) status. Completeness (55) and Resilience (60) are unusually strong for the genre — per-component falsifiability conditions, stated abandonment criteria, and open COI disclosure are real credibility positives that the calibration set (rewards rigorous, honest, mechanism-seeking work) favors. Impact (40) reflects propositional-only instruments and tools not released as reusable artifacts. Weighted: 0.25*65 + 0.20*45 + 0.25*65 + 0.10*55 + 0.15*60 + 0.05*40 ≈ 58.
theoretical Novelty 65 Rigor 45 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 55 Resilience 60 Impact 40
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Software-producing organizations face a structural discontinuity: generative AI is absorbing the cognitive work that has defined the profession for 70 years—writing syntactically correct, semantically plausible code. The problem is not programmer displacement per se, but that every organizational structure (roles, career ladders, hiring criteria, team topologies, governance frameworks, performance metrics) was calibrated to code production as the scarce resource. When that scarcity dissolves—documented by adoption surveys (84% developer AI usage; 78% enterprise adoption), task-capability growth (METR's ~7-month task-horizon doubling time), and labour-market shifts (entry-level tech hiring down ~25% year-over-year; Revelio Labs 2024)—every downstream structure requires renegotiation. Existing work covers productivity impacts (Copilot RCTs), technical architectures (CSD, SE 3.0 frameworks), or adoption factors (HACAF), but none integrates a model of post-AI expert cognition with the organizational capability that cognition requires—the gap the thesis occupies. Key Idea: The central move is the "deflationary framing": refuse to name a successor bottleneck (avoiding vulnerability to single-constraint refutation), and instead identify two structural consequences that follow from the dissolution of the old constraint with near-logical necessity. Pillar 2 is derived from Pillar 1 plus a volume premise—the two form a closed causal structure. The most original single construct is the audio-mixer metaphor for post-AI expert cognition: multiple axes of judgment (product, architecture, implementation, validation—crossed with temporal horizons) held simultaneously at modulated attention levels, replacing the discrete "hats" mode that execution cost historically forced. The key insight is that the hats mode was a degenerate case of the continuous mode—one channel at maximum, others pinned near zero—not a natural cognitive category. Methodology: The work follows analytic autoethnography (Anderson 2006) grounded in abductive reasoning (Peirce). The primary empirical base is a dual N=1 foundational case: the author simultaneously occupies founder-operator of RLABS (a small AI product laboratory) and Software Development Manager at a large (~80,000-employee) Latin American retail conglomerate. Thirteen experiential patterns (P1–P13) are abductively derived across five observational planes: individual (P1–P4 from RLABS—production-comprehension disconnect, cognitive collapse under generation velocity, emergence of meta-systems as structural exit, tool agnosticism), organizational (P5–P7 from the corporate contrast—individual-organization adaptive asymmetry, heterogeneity of transition capacity, structural-before-adaptive), cognitive (P8—the mixer mode), dual-coexistence (P9–P12—incumbent dynamics including structural sequestration as predictable response), and temporal (P13—recruitment-side contraction of the seniority pipeline). A multi-case triangulation protocol is fully specified—12–15 informants, purposive maximum-variation sampling across position/industry/geographic axes, three-pass thematic coding, Cohen's kappa intercoder reliability, member checking—but explicitly not yet executed. Theoretical anchoring draws from cognitive science (Polanyi, Hutchins, Dreyfus, Sweller), sociotechnical theory (Trist & Bamforth, Geels, Henderson & Clark, Christensen, Burgelman, O'Reilly & Tushman), contemporary software engineering (Skelton & Pais, Forsgren et al., Beyer et al.), and AI capabilities literature (METR, SWE-Bench Pro, McKinsey). The manufacturing transition (1910–1980: Taylor, Shewhart, Deming, Ohno) is formalized as a structural analogue; four disanalogies limiting the transfer are explicitly enumerated. Key Results: The work delivers no new quantitative measurements by the author. The sole author-generated quantitative anchor is ~150 artifacts produced over ~12 months by one person in the RLABS context, motivating pattern P2 (cognitive collapse). External empirical anchors cited: METR's ~7-month task-horizon doubling time (falling to <3 months on SWE-Bench Verified); SWE-Bench Pro top model scores of ~23% on enterprise tasks vs. >70% on simpler benchmarks; METR's RCT showing experienced developers ~19% slower with AI tools (interpreted as unredesigned workflows rather than AI limitation); McKinsey's finding that only 6% of AI-adopting organizations capture material EBIT impact, with high performers 3.6× more likely to redesign workflows; Brynjolfsson et al. 2025 (Stanford Digital Economy Lab, large-scale ADP payroll data) showing ~13% relative employment decline for workers aged 22–25 in AI-exposed occupations and ~20% specifically for software developers. The falsifiable longitudinal prediction—Type-A (institutionalizing) organizations outperform Type-C (sequestering) organizations on competitive metrics over 5–10 years—is presented as a future empirical research programme, not a finding. A diagnostic maturity model (4 dimensions × 6 levels; Appendix C), a Mixer Index individual instrument (Appendix E), and a six-mechanism institutionalization pipeline (Chapter 13) are proposed as practical outputs. Novelty Classification: NEW FORMULATION + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING (autoethnographic). The "mixer mode" is a genuinely novel named construct for post-AI expert cognition—derived from but not duplicating Dreyfus/Polanyi—with an operationalized measurement instrument. "Meta-software" as an integrated four-subcategory structural category (functional observability, structural validation, contextual continuity, automated governance) is new as an integrated organizational concept even though each precursor exists. The deflationary framing is a deliberate methodological contribution. The 13 experiential patterns, institutional trajectory typology, and diagnostic instruments are novel. The manufacturing analogy is standard but explicitly formalized with disanalogies. Rigor & Reproducibility: No ablations by design—this is qualitative theory-elaboration. The foundational case is dual N=1 (one person, two organizational contexts); the author acknowledges this throughout. The triangulation protocol is fully specified but unexecuted—all 13 patterns remain hypotheses. Critically, the author explicitly states falsifiability conditions for each component (Chapter 15) and declares what evidence would cause personal abandonment of each pillar—unusual and credibility-positive for this genre. Conflict of interest is openly disclosed: three RLABS open-source products (Atalaya, Akopia, AgentGuard) are cited as meta-software instantiations; Appendix D catalogues multiple alternative instantiations to decouple the structural argument from the specific tools. No new datasets released. Preprint: Labbé Yáñez 2026, Zenodo doi:10.5281/zenodo.20371166. Not peer-reviewed—independent research monograph. Deployability (THEORETICAL profile): - Sound: Assumptions are explicit and the two-pillar derivation is internally consistent and transparently reasoned. Gaps are acknowledged: unexecuted triangulation, dual N=1, Latin American provenance, 2024–2026 temporal specificity. - General: Explicitly scoped to software-producing organizations under moderate regulatory regimes. Partial extension to legal, finance, consulting, journalism, and translation is argued. Out-of-scope categories (physical trades, caregiving, performing arts, high-touch relational sales) are named. Highly regulated industries with intrinsic legacy-core constraints are treated as requiring qualification. - Tight: No formal bounds are derived. "Logical near-necessity" of the two pillars is argued, not proven. Alternative explanations for the autoethnographic patterns are acknowledged; causal attribution of the seniority contraction to AI (vs. macroeconomic confounds) is explicitly presented as unresolved. - Implementable: Several practical instruments are proposed—the diagnostic maturity model, Mixer Index, institutionalization mechanisms, meta-software sub-categories with operational definitions and behavioral indicators—but all are explicitly labeled propositional/pre-validated. The work stops at paper+instrument form; the RLABS tools are open-source but not released as a reusable proof technique independent of the thesis. Strengths: - The mixer-mode construct is conceptually original, grounded in multiple established cognitive science traditions (Dreyfus competent-to-expert transition, Polanyi tacit knowledge, Hutchins distributed cognition), and operationalized—the most distinctive contribution of the work. - Intellectual honesty is exceptional for this genre: explicit conflict-of-interest disclosure, per-component falsifiability conditions, acknowledgment that the triangulation is unexecuted, and stated abandonment criteria for each pillar. - The dual-position autoethnographic design is genuinely unusual—same individual observing the same transition from near-opposite organizational contexts simultaneously, providing natural-experiment leverage unavailable to external observers. - The deflationary framing is a principled methodological choice explicitly engineered to survive partial refutation of any single replacement-constraint claim. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The author directly confronts: dual N=1 foundational case with unexecuted triangulation; Latin American provenance; 2024–2026 temporal specificity; disciplinary bias (computer engineer, not social scientist); overwhelmingly Anglo-American literature base; organizational-size gap (extremes covered, mid-sized organizations underrepresented); RLABS conflict of interest; propositional status of all diagnostic instruments; energy/environmental costs not quantified; and the recursive sustainability problem—whether mixer-mode cognition can form without the bounded-scope practice roles the transition automates away. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The "logical near-necessity" of Pillar 1 rests on the claim that freed cognitive load must be reallocated to judgment rather than leisure or brute-scale production—the empirical evidence cited (McKinsey high-performer workflow redesign) is consistent with but does not uniquely support the mixer-mode interpretation; a manager who simply delegates more without cognitive reorganization would be a potential refutation not cleanly distinguished. - The 150-artifact / 12-month figure is the sole author-generated number and is explicitly not externally verified; its generalizability to other practitioners or tools is unestablished. - Pattern P13 (seniority pipeline collapse) has strong external empirical support (Brynjolfsson et al. 2025) for the contraction signal, but the causal attribution to AI work-allocation rather than macroeconomic confounds (post-pandemic over-hiring correction, interest-rate effects) is acknowledged as unresolved while load-bearing for the policy implications—a tension the thesis names but does not resolve. - The security chapter's practical claims (that the three-phase cycle measurably reduces breach incidence) are explicitly unvalidated; the chapter is structurally coherent but its effectiveness relative to alternatives is unknown. - The boundary between "orchestration as role" and "mixer mode as cognition" (Section 10.5) is argued at some length but remains conceptually difficult to operationalize in practice—the contraposition matrix (Appendix E) proposes a measurement approach, but behavioral distinguishability of the two configurations from observable org-chart or workflow data is not demonstrated. Bottom Line: A conceptually ambitious and unusually honest theory-elaboration monograph whose central construct—the mixer mode—is worth taking seriously by organizational researchers and technology leaders, but whose empirical base is a single dual-position autoethnographic case with an unexecuted validation protocol; readers who need evidence for organizational decisions should treat this as a well-structured research agenda rather than a validated framework.
Search Results
#133 — Moderately Innovative (57)

From Failed Trajectories to Reliable LLM Agents : Diagnosing and Repairing Harness Flaws

M Chen, J Wang, Z Liu, Y Wang, Q Wang · arxiv.org · automated generated environments for agent testing - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces HARNESSFIX, a four-agent framework that compiles failed LLM agent execution traces and harness source code into a structured Harness-aware Trace Intermediate Representation (HTIR), attributes failures to specific harness layers using step-level provenance and control-flow evidence, and generates regression-validated harness patches constrained by scoped repair operators. - Why it's important: LLM agent failures often stem from harness infrastructure flaws—bad tool schemas, lifecycle guards that accept completion before verifying side effects, missing observability—rather than from the base model, yet all major self-improvement methods only optimize prompts or final outcomes without localizing which harness mechanism caused the failure. - How it does it: HTIR decomposes each execution step into three linked evidence types (input provenance showing how requests were assembled from prior steps and harness code, control-flow links showing why the harness executed each step, and artifact/state effect annotations), enabling step-level failure attribution to ETCLOVG harness layers that then drives selection of scoped repair operators and regression-bounded patch validation. - The good: HARNESSFIX improves held-out test performance over the initial harness by 15.2%–50.0% across four diverse benchmarks (SWE-Bench Verified, Terminal-Bench 2.0 Verified, GAIA, AppWorld) and exceeds both human-designed harnesses and state-of-the-art self-evolution baselines (GEPA, SCOPE, ReCreate) on all four. - The missing: All experiments use a single base model (openai/gpt-5-mini) with no repeated runs, no error bars, and no significance tests; on Terminal-Bench the absolute improvement is 6→9 tasks on a 34-task test set, a difference easily within noise for non-deterministic agent systems. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on agent reliability, harness engineering, or automated program repair for LLM systems; the HTIR design and repair operator catalog (Table II) offer immediately actionable vocabulary, but practitioners needing to reproduce or deploy the work will find the absence of released code and cost analysis a significant barrier.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. The HTIR provenance/control-flow decomposition and scoped repair-operator catalog are genuinely non-obvious (Non-Obviousness 75, Novelty 72), and the four-way ablation across four benchmarks is internally consistent. But Rigor (50) and Resilience (50) are undermined by a single model, single run, no significance tests, and margins as small as 3 tasks on a 34-task set; Completeness (45) is a real negative given a one-sentence limitations section that ignores the single-model setup, missing cost analysis, and unreleased artifacts. Weighted composite ≈ 57.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 50 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 45 Resilience 50 Impact 50
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation LLM agents executing long-horizon tasks—repository-level software repair, terminal command-line workflows, open-ended research QA, stateful application automation—depend on a surrounding harness: runtime infrastructure governing tool invocation, context assembly, execution control flow, observability, verification, and governance. When these agents fail, the root cause often lies in harness mechanisms rather than the base model's reasoning, because the harness determines what the model sees, what actions it can take, and what conditions must be met before completion is accepted. Existing remediation splits into two inadequate approaches: runtime workarounds (suppressing errors or adding external patches without fixing the underlying flaw) and outcome-driven optimization (tuning prompts or workflows based on final pass/fail scores without first localizing which step in the trajectory caused the failure or which harness layer is responsible). The result is broad, indirect, or poorly scoped harness changes. The gap being closed is trace-grounded, causally precise failure attribution that connects specific runtime evidence to the modifiable harness artifact responsible for it. Key Idea The single core insight is that heterogeneous agent execution traces and harness source code can be compiled into a unified step-level intermediate representation (HTIR) that makes three complementary causal dimensions explicit per step—how the request was assembled from earlier trace content and harness logic (input provenance), why the harness triggered this step under its control flow (control-flow links), and what observable artifact or state effect resulted—and that this localized, harness-grounded evidence is sufficient to attribute failures to specific ETCLOVG harness layers and constrain repair to scoped operators for exactly those layers, preventing the regression-causing side effects of unconstrained harness edits. Methodology HARNESSFIX is a sequential four-agent pipeline. Stage 1 — Trace Abstraction: The trace abstraction agent ingests raw logs and harness source code and produces HTIR. Each TraceStep node stores: unique sequential ID, full request and response messages, role annotation (information acquisition, tool invocation, artifact editing, validation, orchestration decision, final submission), execution status (success/failure/timeout/blocked), and artifact/state effect (none, read-only, artifact change, state change, mixed, unknown). Three link types connect steps: temporal (preserving execution order), input provenance (identifying how a later request was assembled from earlier request or response spans—both explicit copying and semantic reuse—with file-path and line-number references into the harness code responsible for request construction), and control-flow (identifying the triggering execution logic—continue, retry, delegate, validate, finalize, terminate—with corresponding harness code references). HTIR further attaches a harness-layer responsibility facet to each step by comparing its node-local diagnostic evidence against the ETCLOVG responsibility definitions in Table I. Stage 2 — Harness Flaw Diagnosis: The diagnosis agent executes four substeps: symptom localization (reading external evaluation result and the last step's evidence), evidence backtracking (producing ranked candidate responsible steps via all link types), candidate adjudication (per-step inspection of node-local evidence, request/response messages, and harness code references to select final responsible steps), and layer mapping (using ETCLOVG facets of selected steps). Each failed trace produces a structured diagnosis record containing failure signal, attribution result with root-cause explanation, and harness diagnosis with implicated ETCLOVG layer(s). Diagnosis records are then consolidated by grouping on implicated layer and merging records sharing the same root cause, producing flaw records that represent recurring harness-flaw patterns. Stage 3 — Scoped Repair: The repair agent maps each flaw record to repair operators from Table II (distilled from version-change histories of real agent repositories), selects a primary operator addressing the root cause and auxiliary operators from adjacent layers, and instantiates a flaw-specific repair specification containing: (a) target and scope binding to the flaw record and representative diagnosis records; (b) edit constraints naming allowed and forbidden harness artifacts (benchmark evaluator oracles, task definitions, and held-out test sets are always forbidden); (c) required behavior describing what the patch must implement; and (d) validation and risk criteria specifying the target-flaw reduction threshold δ_min and regression bound r_max. The repair agent then generates candidate harness patches under this specification. Stage 4 — Validation: The validation agent first runs pre-validation checks (diff conformance to repair specification, no forbidden artifact edits, syntax and static correctness), then evaluates the candidate on a held-out validation split requiring ΔD_target ≥ δ_min (reduction in target flaw occurrence rate) AND R_new ≤ r_max (newly introduced regressions). Both thresholds must be satisfied for acceptance. Accepted and rejected outcomes are recorded in harness repair memory with rationale. Critical design decisions: (a) HTIR's separation of input-provenance and control-flow links—well-justified because a step can receive a correctly assembled request yet fail to produce the expected effect (a control-flow error), or vice versa; (b) scoped repair operators rather than free-form editing—empirically justified by the ablation showing unconstrained edits cause regressions that offset targeted gains; (c) two-criterion validation (flaw reduction plus regression bound)—empirically justified by the ablation showing regression-unaware acceptance leaves 4 held-out tasks on the table on AppWorld alone. Key Results All results from the held-out test split (never used during repair or validation). Base model: openai/gpt-5-mini throughout. - SWE-Bench Verified (100-task test set): H0 (mini-swe-agent) = 45 resolved; HARNESSFIX = 57 (+26.7% relative). Best human-designed baseline Trae-Agent = 48; best self-evolution baseline ReCreate = 52. - Terminal-Bench 2.0 Verified (34-task test set): H0 (Harbor Terminus-2) = 6; HARNESSFIX = 9 (+50.0% relative). Best human-designed OpenCode = 7; best self-evolution ReCreate = 7. - GAIA (60-task test set): H0 (open-deep-research) = 26; HARNESSFIX = 37 (+42.3% relative). Best human-designed MiroFlow = 35; best self-evolution ReCreate = 32. - AppWorld (90-task test set): H0 (simplified ReAct) = 33; HARNESSFIX = 38 (+15.2% relative). Best human-designed CUGA = 37; best self-evolution ReCreate = 36. Ablation (held-out test): prompt-only repair = 48/6/30/34 (SWE/TB2/GAIA/AW); w/o trace-grounded diagnosis = 50/7/31/34; w/o scoped repair operators = 49/6/30/34; w/o regression-aware acceptance = 53/8/33/35; full HARNESSFIX = 57/9/37/38. All components contribute, with scoped repair operators and trace-grounded diagnosis providing the largest individual ablation gaps. The absolute margins on Terminal-Bench (3 tasks) and AppWorld (1–2 tasks over CUGA/ReCreate) are small enough to be within non-deterministic agent variance in the absence of significance testing. The strongest human-designed baselines are drawn from different starting harnesses, making the comparison partially controlled but not fully apples-to-apples. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The genuinely novel contributions are: (1) the HTIR design with its three-link type decomposition (temporal, input provenance with harness-code grounding, control flow with harness-code grounding) and per-step ETCLOVG facet—this is more structured and causally precise than existing trace representations like VCC or SWETRACE; (2) the typed repair operator catalog (Table II) grounding repair scope in ETCLOVG layer semantics; (3) the two-criterion validation gate (flaw reduction + regression bound). The use of LLM agents for meta-level tasks (trace abstraction, diagnosis, repair, validation) is not novel, and the ETCLOVG taxonomy is cited from prior work [9]. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and meaningful: all four design choices are ablated independently across all four benchmarks, and the results are internally consistent. However, the paper reports no error bars, no multiple seeds, and no significance tests despite evaluating non-deterministic agent systems—this is a material omission. The small test set sizes (34 tasks for TB2) mean that 2–3 task differences reported as improvements could be noise. No code, traces, or harness artifacts are released or linked. The repair operator catalog (Table II) is described in natural language but not formalized as a machine-readable artifact. Repair specification thresholds δ_min and r_max are referenced but their specific values are not reported, making the acceptance gate non-reproducible. Auxiliary experiments varying model strength are mentioned but not reported in the main paper. The HTIR construction relies on LLM agents whose prompts are not provided. Deployability (EMPIRICAL) - Practical: Requires API access to a capable LLM and full access to the agent harness source code and execution environment; no exotic hardware required, but the framework cannot run without the complete agent development stack. - Efficient: Not analyzed in the paper. HARNESSFIX runs four distinct LLM-agent passes per training trajectory (trace abstraction, diagnosis, repair, validation) plus validation-set re-execution of candidate patches; for 100 training tasks on SWE-Bench, this entails hundreds of LLM calls and full re-executions on the validation split. No timing or token cost figures appear anywhere in the paper. - Effective: Demonstrates consistent improvement on held-out test tasks across four benchmarks over five baselines, which is compelling. However, the very small absolute margins on TB2 and AppWorld, combined with the absence of statistical testing, prevent high-confidence claims on those two benchmarks. - Scalable: Not tested. The paper states future work will examine whether flaw patterns generalize to more domains. No analysis of behavior with larger harness codebases, more complex or interacting flaw records, or iterative multi-round repair is provided. The paper delivers a framework description and experimental results; no software artifact, released dataset, or trained model accompanies it. Strengths - The HTIR input-provenance/control-flow link decomposition is technically non-obvious and important: it enables precise attribution distinguishing whether a failure arose from how a request was assembled versus why the harness chose to execute that step, which prior trace analysis work (VCC, SWETRACE, HORIZON) does not make explicit. - The scoped repair operator catalog grounded in real agent repository version-change histories provides principled constraint of the repair search space, and the ablation confirms this constraint is empirically necessary—not just a design preference. - The cross-benchmark generalization is the strongest empirical argument: consistent improvements across repository-level software repair, CLI workflows, research QA, and app automation suggest the approach captures domain-general harness failure patterns. - The domain-specific flaw pattern analysis (Table VI, Section VI.B) provides independently useful engineering knowledge—e.g., GAIA failures concentrate in Tool Interface and Observability (missing file format support, unpreserved evidence), while Terminal-Bench failures concentrate in Execution, Lifecycle, and Verification—which is actionable for harness engineers independently of the HARNESSFIX pipeline. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors acknowledge a single limitation in the conclusion: plans to study "more agent domains and deployment settings to examine whether the observed harness-flaw patterns generalize beyond the benchmarks used in this paper." No other limitations are disclosed. The paper does not acknowledge the single-model setup, the absence of statistical testing, the small absolute margins on two of four benchmarks, the lack of cost reporting, or the sensitivity to validation threshold choices. This is a credibility-negative minimal limitation section that does not reflect the work's actual gaps. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Single model, single run, no statistics: All experiments use openai/gpt-5-mini with no repeated runs and no significance tests. A difference of 3 tasks on a 34-task test set (TB2) for a non-deterministic system is not reliably distinguishable from noise. The auxiliary model-variation experiments are referenced but not reported. - Cost and latency entirely unreported: HARNESSFIX runs four LLM-agent passes per failed training trajectory plus full validation-set re-execution per candidate patch. For 100 training tasks with multiple failure candidates, the computational overhead could exceed the cost of the original training runs by an order of magnitude. The absence of any cost figure makes the claimed practical value impossible to assess. - Confounded meta-agent and base model: The same model (openai/gpt-5-mini) acts as both the repaired agent and all four HARNESSFIX meta-agents. Results will change substantially if the meta-agent is stronger or weaker than the target agent, but the paper does not isolate this dimension. - Human-designed baselines not fairly controlled: Human-designed harnesses (OpenHands, Trae-Agent, etc.) use different starting implementations, not H0 evolved by human engineers with the same failed trajectories as input. A controlled comparison—giving a human engineer the same failed trajectories and H0 as starting point—is not tested. - No released artifacts: No code, prompt templates, HTIR schemas, operator catalog formats, or trace data are released. The threshold values δ_min and r_max for the validation gate are never specified, making the acceptance criterion non-reproducible. - Validation split sizing and threshold sensitivity: The validation split is as small as 17 tasks (TB2). The paper does not report how sensitive outcomes are to the choice of δ_min and r_max, which are the primary levers controlling the regression/improvement tradeoff. Bottom Line HARNESSFIX makes a genuine conceptual advance—moving agent self-improvement from outcome-driven prompt optimization to step-level, harness-layer-aware failure attribution and scoped repair—and the multi-benchmark ablation results are internally consistent and compelling, but the absence of statistical rigor, cost reporting, and released artifacts means practitioners cannot reproduce or confidently apply the work without substantial independent re-implementation.
Search Results 2606.06324 PDF
#134 — Moderately Innovative (57)

Agentic Neuro- Symbolic Planning and Commissioning for Human-in-the-Loop Industrial Robotics with Digital Twins

Z Liu, VN Fernandez-Ayala, T Wang, Q Qin, XV Wang… · arxiv.org · neural symbolic - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a neuro-symbolic multi-agent framework (Specifier-Designer-Inspector, SDI) that translates natural-language assembly commands into physically verified robot trajectories via a Unity3D digital twin, achieving 100% task success across 70 benchmark commands on a dual-arm ABB robotic station. - Why it's important: LLM-only planners cannot reliably satisfy the hard geometric and kinematic constraints (gravity, collision, reachability, gripping feasibility) required in safety-critical industrial manufacturing, leaving a gap between linguistic fluency and physical executability. - How it does it: LLMs are confined strictly to language understanding (Specifier) and structure generation (Designer), while all constraint verification, assembly sequencing, and execution use deterministic symbolic modules; LangGraph-based routing is reserved only for failure-recovery branching where context-awareness adds value. - The good: An ablation replacing the symbolic Inspector with an equivalently-prompted LLM critic collapses success from 98.1% to 3.8% with a 4× generation-time blowup — a stark, quantitative demonstration that LLM-as-evaluator is insufficient for physical feasibility checking. - The missing: The 70-command benchmark is entirely self-constructed and self-evaluated by the same team with no independent third-party benchmark, and the physical experiment covers only 12 commands without multi-run variance — so the 100% headline should be read as a proof-of-concept, not a deployment-ready claim. - Read it or not: Worth reading for robotics researchers and engineers building LLM-integrated industrial assembly systems who need a principled, reproducible architecture for constraint-verified planning with human oversight; can be skipped by those seeking advances in motion planning algorithms or general-purpose LLM reasoning.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile (built pipeline on physical ABB hardware). Practical Impact (60, weight 0.30) is moderate: a real deployed ROS2+Unity3D dual-arm pipeline, but no public code release caps it.
