Rigor (45) is pulled down hard by a fully self-authored and self-evaluated 70-command benchmark (Goodhart risk), single-run results with no variance, only 12 physical commands, and a single LLM (GPT-4o-mini) — a preprint. Novelty (62) and Non-Obviousness (65) are lifted by the genuinely informative PGE-evaluator-substitution insight, decisively backed by the 98.1%→3.8% LLM-critic ablation. Completeness (60) and Resilience (50) suffer because the headline 100% claim does not survive the missing third-party benchmark and absent statistics. Weighted: 0.15*62+0.15*45+0.10*65+0.15*60+0.15*50+0.30*60 ≈ 57.
systems Novelty 62 Rigor 45 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 60 Resilience 50 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Industrial robot automation increasingly demands that operators specify tasks in natural language, but no existing system closes the full pipeline from free-form language to physical execution with human oversight and closed-loop failure recovery. Classical task-and-motion planning (TAMP) provides formal guarantees but requires hand-coded domain descriptions and cannot accept natural-language input. LLM-based planners such as SayCan and Code as Policies lack constraint verification and recovery. Multi-agent LLM frameworks (AutoGen, CrewAI) operate purely in language space with no connection to physical systems or digital twins. LangGraph-based orchestration that delegates all control-flow decisions to the LLM yields unpredictable behavior and difficult debugging in practice. The core gap is that no prior system unifies NL intent, symbolic physical feasibility, human-in-the-loop inspection via a digital twin, and closed-loop execution-level failure recovery in a single deployable pipeline. Key Idea The key technical insight is that the Evaluator role in software engineering's Planner-Generator-Evaluator (PGE) harness pattern cannot be filled by an LLM in physical robotics — geometric and kinematic feasibility require exact numerical checking against hard thresholds, not probabilistic language model judgment — and so it must be replaced by a deterministic symbolic verifier (the Inspector). This single substitution transforms an unreliable LLM-driven loop into a formally verifiable generate–verify–repair cycle, while the LLM is retained where it genuinely adds value: interpreting ambiguous natural language (Specifier) and generating creative spatial layouts (Designer). Methodology The pipeline has four modules orchestrated by a LangGraph state machine over phases {PLAN, DESIGN, INSPECT, HUMANREVIEW, PREEXECUTE, EXECUTE, REPLAN}. Module 1 (Structure Design Generation): The Specifier skill expands a raw NL command into a structured specification; the LLM Designer generates a candidate 3D layout (per-object ID, position p∈ℝ³, orientation q∈SO(3), size s∈ℝ³); the symbolic Inspector verifies against six constraints: gravity (objects placed bottom-up), reachability (cuboid workspace check), collision (AABB disjointness), contact (non-ground objects must have structural support), gripping feasibility (at least one unblocked lateral approach direction), and assembly sequence precedence. Two design strategies are instantiated: an adaptive constraint-guided loop (fixed control flow, one LLM call per retry, only violated constraints fed back as targeted prompt augmentation) and an agentic skill-based loop (LLM dynamically selects composite or granular verification skills via function calling). A final safety gate always re-verifies before the layout is passed downstream. Module 2 (Human-in-the-Loop): The verified layout is rendered in a Unity3D digital twin; the operator can inspect, translate/rotate/insert/remove objects by direct manipulation; any modification triggers automatic re-verification by the Inspector before proceeding. Module 3 (Symbolic Sequencing): A DAG is built from object support relations (Constraint 1 antecedent); topological sort by iterative sink-node removal yields a disassembly order that is reversed to produce the assembly sequence σ*; gripping feasibility (Constraint 5) is enforced at each sink selection step. Module 4 (Motion Planning and Monitoring): MoveIt with RRTConnect generates collision-free joint trajectories; the digital twin streams both planned (Q^plan) and executed (Q^exec) trajectories; deviation metric δ = max_{r,t} ‖q^plan_r(t) − q^exec_r(t)‖₂ triggers pause, human escalation, or replanning when a threshold is exceeded. Two-tier failure recovery: structure-level failures trigger LangGraph-routed context-aware replanning (Designer ξmodify or geometric recovery skills ξflatten/ξshift that bypass the Designer and feed corrected layouts directly to the Inspector); execution-level geometric failures invoke deterministic recovery skills. All retry and escalation decisions remain with the Orchestrator. Three critical design decisions: (1) LLMs restricted to language/generation — justified by the 98.1% → 3.8% LLM-critic ablation; (2) LangGraph routing applied only to failure-recovery branching, not nominal control flow — reduces hallucinated routing; (3) targeted constraint feedback (only violated constraints, not full K) — reduces prompt complexity and repair iterations. Key Results Evaluated on 70 self-constructed natural-language assembly commands in five difficulty tiers (A: 3–15 steps, explicit; B: 5–15 steps, vague/semantic; C: 7–16 steps, enclosed/dense; D: 6–20 steps, open-ended + 3D packing; E: 13–25 steps, stress-scale) against 10 baselines spanning single-pass LLM, adaptive feedback loops, single-agent tool-use, and multi-agent orchestration variants. Hardware: dual-arm ABB IRB120 + IRB1600, GPT-4o-mini (temp 0.3), Ubuntu 22.04, ROS 2, MoveIt 2, Unity3D. Ours-Hybrid achieves 100% overall success (58/58 Groups A–D; 12/12 Group E), the only method to do so. Best non-agentic baseline is LLM-AdaptLoop and SAMS, both at 93.1% overall, with Group D dropping to 83.3% and 88.9% respectively; weakest baseline (LLM-FullPrompt) reaches only 74.1% overall with 55.6% on Group D. Ablation: removing the Specifier drops Group D from 100% to 95.5%; removing LangGraph routing drops Group C from 100% to 83.3%; replacing symbolic Inspector with LLM critic collapses success from 98.1% to 3.8% with generation time rising from 85.3s to 319.0s. Failure recovery: Hybrid recovers 9/9 human-modification violations and 9/10 injected deterministic execution failures (LGOrch achieves 10/10, surpassing Hybrid on the narrow-corridor scenario where Specifier over-constrains the search space). Physical deployment: 12/12 commands produce valid IK solutions and collision-free trajectories; dual-arm commands achieve up to 1.7× speedup. Average generation time for Hybrid is 85.3s versus 14.9s for LLM-Direct, reflecting multi-agent LLM API overhead. Baselines are moderately strong — LLM-AdaptLoop with targeted feedback and SAMS with 8 granular tools are genuine comparators — but no baseline represents a fully optimized neuro-symbolic prior system (e.g., PDDLStream + LLM grounding), which would be the most relevant comparison. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. The primary novelty is the adaptation of the PGE software-engineering harness to physical robotics by substituting the LLM Evaluator with a deterministic symbolic Inspector (SDI), combined with selective LangGraph routing and two-tier failure recovery. The individual components — LLMs, LangGraph, MoveIt, AABB constraint checking, Unity3D digital twins — are all existing technologies; novelty lies in their principled composition and the empirical demonstration that LLM-as-evaluator fails categorically for physical feasibility tasks. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablation study covers all key architectural axes (Specifier, LangGraph, Inspector, recovery skills) and is the strongest part of the paper — the LLM-critic ablation in particular is a necessary and decisive experiment. No error bars, multiple seeds, or statistical significance tests are reported; each of the 70 commands is apparently run once. No code, dataset, or trained model release is mentioned. The 70-command benchmark is entirely self-authored and self-evaluated, raising Goodhart's Law concerns — a system tuned on this task class, evaluated on the same task class, with no held-out or third-party benchmark. Physical experiments cover 12 commands from a single lab with two specific ABB robots. The paper is a preprint submitted to IEEE, not yet peer-reviewed. GPT-4o-mini is the only LLM evaluated; sensitivity to model choice is untested. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS — a built and deployed pipeline evaluated on physical hardware. - Practical: Runs on commercially available hardware (ABB IRB120/1600, OnRobot grippers, Ubuntu 22.04, ROS 2, Unity3D, GPT-4o-mini API) with no specialized compute requirements. - Efficient: 85.3s average per command due to multi-agent LLM API latency; authors acknowledge this and propose lighter models or cached Specifier outputs as mitigations, but no numbers are provided. - Effective: Evidence from 70 benchmark commands and 12 physical trials supports feasibility for block-stacking assembly with known object positions; the benchmark is not independent, so the 100% success rate should be treated cautiously. - Scalable: Tested on structures up to 25 assembly steps with axis-aligned bounding boxes and known supply-area positions; scalability to heterogeneous parts, visual perception, larger workspaces, or more complex geometry is claimed as future work but not demonstrated. The system delivers a deployed software pipeline (ROS 2 + Unity3D + Python) but no public code release is mentioned. Strengths - The LLM-critic ablation (98.1% → 3.8% success, 4× time blowup) is a genuinely compelling empirical argument for the neuro-symbolic split — not a claim, a measured result that would change a practitioner's design decisions. - The two-tier failure recovery architecture (structure-level LangGraph routing vs. execution-level deterministic geometric recovery skills) is a principled distinction not present in prior LLM robotics systems, and Tables V–VI provide per-scenario detail that exposes where each mechanism succeeds and fails. - The formal specification of the pipeline (Algorithms 1–4, operator chain notation) gives enough detail to replicate the approach without ambiguity. - Real dual-arm physical experiments, while limited (12 commands), validate that the planned trajectories are kinematically feasible on actual hardware — a step beyond simulation-only evaluation that most comparable papers omit. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge: (1) known supply-area positions are assumed and visual perception for object localization was not integrated (stated as out of scope); (2) 85.3s per-command average is slow for latency-sensitive deployments, with lighter models or cached Specifier outputs proposed as mitigations. The authors confront these limitations directly in the Discussion section, which is a credibility positive, though they do not acknowledge the self-constructed benchmark or the absence of statistical testing. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The 70-command benchmark is entirely authored and evaluated by the same team with no third-party benchmark — this is the most significant methodological gap; systems papers of this type typically include at least one independent benchmark (e.g., Assembly-PlanBench, RoboSuite, or standard TAMP domains). - Physical experiments run only 12 commands with no repeated trials — success/failure counts at this scale carry high variance and do not establish deployment reliability. - No sensitivity analysis on LLM choice; GPT-4o-mini may not be representative of weaker or stronger models, and the generalization of the SDI harness to other LLMs is untested. - The Specifier-relaxation recovery mechanism (reverting to raw command after repeated failures) is described but not ablated separately from the geometric recovery skills, making it impossible to attribute recovery credit precisely. - Axis-aligned bounding box collision checking is a significant geometric simplification — real industrial objects with irregular shapes or tight tolerances would require a different verifier, and the system's performance in such cases is unknown. - LGOrch (no Specifier) outperforms Hybrid on the narrow-corridor recovery scenario (10/10 vs. 9/10), suggesting the Specifier's ambiguity-grounding benefit systematically conflicts with recovery flexibility in constrained spaces — a deeper architectural tension that the paper identifies but does not resolve. Bottom Line The SDI ablation result — LLM-critic substitution collapsing success by 94 percentage points — is a genuinely useful finding for any practitioner building LLM-integrated physical robot systems, but the self-authored benchmark and absence of multi-run statistics mean this should be treated as a well-argued design blueprint rather than a validated production system.
Search Results 2606.08214 PDF
#135 — Moderately Innovative (57)

AttackPathGNN: Cross-function vulnerability detection in smart contracts using state interference graphs and conjunction pooling

G Dobrita, SV Oprea, A Bara · arxiv.org · neural symbolic - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces AttackPathGNN, a heterogeneous GNN that detects Solidity smart-contract vulnerabilities by reasoning over explicit cross-function attack paths via a State Interference Graph and a differentiable conjunction pooling operator that enforces AND-logic over named exploit preconditions. - Why it's important: Most real-world smart contract exploits (The DAO, Cream Finance) arise from interactions between functions sharing mutable storage, which single-function detectors systematically miss, leaving billions in DeFi assets exposed to attack classes that no existing automated tool models structurally. - How it does it: It builds a heterogeneous graph with typed storage-interference edges and directed reentrancy-path edges between functions, then applies conjunction pooling — a sum of log-sigmoid terms over eight named preconditions — whose log-product form collapses the exploit score to −∞ whenever any single mitigation (reentrancy guard, access control, SafeMath) is active. - The good: A +74 percentage-point gap between the rule-only ablation (16.8%) and the full model (90.8%) on the same symbolic substrate cleanly isolates the learned GNN components' contribution, providing the strongest internal evidence for the architectural choices. - The missing: The primary benchmark (SmartBugs Curated, 143 contracts) contains only vulnerable contracts, so no false positive rate is measured; and despite six subsections of related work, no competing GNN detector (MANDO-GURU, Peculiar, DR-GCN) is numerically compared. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on program analysis with GNNs or neuro-symbolic security tools, specifically for the conjunction pooling formulation; practitioners should treat detection numbers with caution until false-positive rates and GNN-baseline comparisons are established.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Completeness (50) and Resilience (50) are the dimensions that pull this down: the primary benchmark (SmartBugs Curated, 143 contracts) contains only vulnerable contracts so false-positive rate is entirely unmeasured, and despite six related-work subsections no GNN competitor (MANDO-GURU, Peculiar, DR-GCN) is numerically compared — the two largest gaps for a detection paper. Rigor (55) is mixed: the five-seed evaluation with CIs is a genuine advance for this subfield and the rule-only ablation cleanly isolates a +74pp GNN contribution, but Wild training labels are nine-tool consensus pseudo-ground-truth (circular with the 8%-effective baselines) and no ablation separates the interference graph from conjunction pooling. Novelty (65) and Non-Obviousness (65) are the strengths — conjunction pooling as a differentiable AND-aggregator with a clean probabilistic derivation is a principled, non-obvious contribution. Without an FP rate or GNN baseline the 90.8% figure can't be contextualized. 0.20*65+0.25*55+0.15*65+0.15*50+0.15*50+0.10*55 = 57.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 50 Resilience 50 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Existing smart contract vulnerability detectors — symbolic engines (Mythril, Securify), regex linters (SmartCheck), and the dominant family of GNN classifiers — treat detection as single-function pattern matching. They ask whether an individual function contains suspicious patterns and declare it safe if it looks clean in isolation. This is structurally blind to cross-function exploits: The DAO (2016) and Cream Finance attacks lived not in any single function but in the relationship between functions sharing mutable storage and in the precise simultaneous satisfaction of multiple exploit conditions. Cross-function reentrancy, stale-balance accounting, and shared-state races are invisible to per-function detectors. A comprehensive assessment of 127 real-world DeFi attacks found that existing automated tools could have prevented only 8% of incidents, directly quantifying the gap. The unsolved problems are: which function pairs expose an attack surface through shared state, and which conditions for exploitation hold simultaneously across those functions. Key Idea: Exploit feasibility requires every precondition in an attack chain to hold simultaneously — a single active mitigation (nonReentrant modifier, access control, SafeMath) renders the entire chain infeasible regardless of how many other risk factors are present. The key insight is that this AND-logic is structurally incompatible with standard OR-like pooling (sum, mean, max), and must be implemented as a differentiable conjunction: a sum of log-sigmoid terms whose log-product form drives the aggregate score to −∞ when any single gate closes. This is not a heuristic but a probabilistic identity: under conditional independence of the eight preconditions, the sum of log-sigmoids is exactly log P(∧ₖ cᵢ,ₖ), the log-probability that the full exploit chain holds. Methodology: The system is a seven-stage pipeline over raw Solidity source. Stage 1 parses the contract using Slither AST (primary) or a regex fallback, extracting per-function storage read/write sets Rᵢ, Wᵢ, external call sets Xᵢ, a CEI-violation flag δᵢ, and boolean flags for visibility, reentrancy guard, and access control. Stage 2 computes a 35-dimensional semantic feature vector from 13 syntactic pattern counts and 17 structural metrics. Stage 3 constructs typed interference edges (write–read, read–write, write–write) between every pair of functions sharing mutable storage, plus directed reentrancy-path edges firing only when an explicit five-condition predicate holds: Reenter(fᵢ→fⱼ) ⟺ |Xᵢ|>0 ∧ pub(fᵢ) ∧ ¬guard(fᵢ) ∧ pub(fⱼ) ∧ (Wᵢ ∩ (Rⱼ ∪ Wⱼ)) ≠ ∅. Stage 4 evaluates eight named precondition scores per function (externally callable, has external call, state after call, no reentrancy guard, shares mutable state, no access control, handles value, unchecked arithmetic), with binary or graded [0,1] values. Stage 5 assembles a heterogeneous graph with four node types (line, pattern, function, precondition — 8F precondition nodes total) and seven edge relations. Stage 6 runs the GNN forward pass: two GATv2 layers over line↔pattern edges, one GCN over sequential line edges, one GATv2 over belongs_to aggregating lines into function embeddings, then two cross-function GATv2 layers over the typed interferes edges (4-dim edge features) and the directed can_reenter edges. Conjunction pooling (eq. 5) then aggregates the eight precondition gate probabilities per function via a shared two-layer MLP gate g. Global pooling concatenates mean- and max-pooled line embeddings with the 35-dim semantic vector and max-pooled fused function embeddings to form a 803-dim contract vector, which a two-layer MLP classifies. Loss is binary cross-entropy; optimizer AdamW (lr=10⁻³, weight decay=10⁻⁴) with cosine annealing and gradient clipping. Three critical design decisions: (1) conjunction pooling's log-product form — well-justified by probabilistic derivation; (2) typed, directed reentrancy-path predicate capturing attack directionality with asymmetric edge semantics — well-justified and enables a clean one-line remediation narrative; (3) Slither-first with regex fallback — practically necessary for diverse Solidity versions, though acknowledged to increase false-edge rate. Key Results: On the 143-contract SmartBugs Curated benchmark (recall-only; all contracts vulnerable; 10 DASP10 categories), AttackPathGNN achieves 90.8±2.5% weighted detection rate across five independent seeds (best seed: 94.4%, worst: 88.1%). Six of ten categories are recovered at 100% at every seed (Arithmetic 15/15, Denial of Service 6/6, Front-Running 4/4, Time Manipulation 5/5, Short Address 1/1, Other 3/3). Reentrancy: 98.7±1.8%. Bad Randomness: 95.0±6.9%. Access Control: 80.0±5.0%. Unchecked Return: 83.1±4.6%. Baselines re-executed by the authors on the same benchmark: Slither v0.11.5 at 45.5% overall, Mythril v0.24.8 at 57.3% overall — AttackPathGNN does not lose to either on any category at any seed. The rule-only ablation (same preconditions, no GNN) reaches 16.8%. On the SmartBugs Wild held-out test partition (747 contracts), the abstract reports 92.3±0.2% F1, though per-category breakdown is not provided for this set. The model runs on a single RTX 4060 (8GB VRAM); one training run takes ~27 minutes. Confidence is well-calibrated: mean P(vuln) on detected contracts 0.915±0.040, on misses 0.179±0.026 (separation 0.736±0.053). Baselines are weak: only two static analyzers compared; no GNN baselines included despite extensive related work coverage. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. Genuinely novel: conjunction pooling as a differentiable AND-aggregator over named exploit preconditions is, to this reviewer's knowledge, new in smart contract vulnerability detection; the probabilistic derivation (eq. 6) is clean and the attack-path framing is a meaningful reframing of the detection problem. The named precondition nodes as first-class graph elements giving interpretability without post-hoc explanation is a real contribution. Standard: the GNN building blocks (GATv2, GCN, heterogeneous graphs) are well-established; heterogeneous graph representations for Solidity were introduced by MANDO/MANDO-GURU; the basic pipeline structure of parse→extract→graph→classify is not new. Rigor & Reproducibility: Five-seed evaluation with mean±std and 95% CI reported — explicitly noted as absent from prior GNN smart-contract detectors, and it is a genuine methodological advance for this subfield. The rule-only ablation is informative and cleanly isolates the GNN's contribution. Code is released at https://github.com/gabrieladobritaene/ATTACKPATHGNN. Hyperparameters selected by 54-configuration grid search on validation F1 with no further tuning after test scoring. Key methodological concern: SmartBugs Wild training labels are derived from consensus of nine automated tools, not human annotation — this means the model may learn to replicate the aggregate behavior of those tools rather than ground-truth exploitability, and the cited finding that those same tools prevent only 8% of real-world attacks makes this label-noise risk non-trivial. The primary evaluation benchmark (SmartBugs Curated, 143 contracts) contains only vulnerable contracts, so precision and false positive rate are not measured; the F1 on SmartBugs Wild test partition is stated but not broken down per category. No GNN baseline (DR-GCN, MANDO-GURU, Peculiar) appears in Table 7, despite being the most directly relevant comparators; this is the largest rigor gap. This appears to be a preprint; venue is not stated in the paper. Deployability (4-property check): EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS profile. - Practical: Runs on a single consumer GPU (RTX 4060, 8GB VRAM, Intel Core i7, 16GB RAM); no specialized infrastructure required. - Efficient: ~27 minutes per training run; inference on 143 contracts under 2 minutes; graph complexity is O(L+F) nodes and O(LP+LF+F²) edges, manageable because F<50 for virtually all Solidity contracts; smart truncation (60-function cap) handles large contracts at the cost of missing some vulnerabilities. - Effective: 90.8% detection on a recall-only benchmark of 143 vulnerable contracts; false positive rate is unmeasured on any held-out benign set, so end-to-end effectiveness for a deployment operator cannot be assessed from the reported numbers. - Scalable: Tested on SmartBugs Wild (~3,732 training contracts) and SmartBugs Curated (143 test contracts); no evaluation at Ethereum-mainnet scale (millions of contracts); the O(F²|S|) interference-graph construction is negligible at current scales but not stress-tested beyond the corpus. The work delivers a software artifact (code at GitHub) and a trained model; no library or CLI packaging is described. Strengths: - Conjunction pooling has a rigorous probabilistic interpretation (eq. 6 as log P(∧ₖ cᵢ,ₖ)) that makes the AND-logic principled rather than heuristic, and the empirical precondition gate visualization (Figure 2) clearly illustrates the collapse property on synthetic examples. - Multi-seed evaluation (5 seeds, μ±σ, 95% CI, per-seed values released) is methodologically honest and sets a new standard for this subfield, explicitly filling a gap the authors identify and document. - Failure analysis distinguishing stable misses (8 contracts, architectural ceiling) from borderline misses (10 contracts, training-variance noise floor) provides concrete, actionable extension targets — the delegatecall storage-layout and unintended-constructor preconditions are specific enough to implement. - Structured interpretability output that names the entry function, shared storage variable, and the specific broken precondition closing the attack path goes substantially beyond attention heatmaps or line-level localization, producing auditor-actionable findings rather than opaque risk scores. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly acknowledge the stable misses: Parity wallet bugs require a precondition for "function intended as constructor but not declared as one," which the eight current preconditions do not encode; FibonacciBalance exposes a delegatecall vulnerability whose feasibility depends on attacker-controlled storage layout, below the State Interference Graph's granularity; five deployed-address Unchecked Return contracts exceed the 1,500-line truncation budget. They attribute Access Control (80%) and Unchecked Return (83%) shortfalls to structural limitations of the precondition set rather than training instability — a credibility-positive acknowledgment. They are explicit that the stable-miss ceiling will not be moved by further training under the current precondition set. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - No GNN baseline comparison: DR-GCN, DA-GNN, HGAT, MANDO, MANDO-GURU, Peculiar, and MCR-VD are all discussed in related work but none appear in Table 7. The paper justifies the training subsample size by matching DR-GCN/TMP scale but provides no head-to-head numbers on the shared benchmark. This is the most significant omission for positioning the contribution. - False positive rate is entirely unmeasured: the primary benchmark has no benign contracts, and the F1 on SmartBugs Wild test is stated globally (92.3%) without a per-category breakdown or false-alarm analysis. A high-recall detector that flags most benign contracts is not useful in practice. - Training labels are tool-generated pseudo-ground-truth: SmartBugs Wild labels come from nine automated tools whose combined real-world effectiveness is 8% by [9]'s finding; this circular dependency between training signal and baseline is not addressed. - Category sizes confound 100% claims: Short Address (n=1), Other (n=3), Front-Running (n=4), and Time Manipulation (n=5) yield 100% detection on trivially small samples; these numbers carry no statistical weight. - No ablation separating the State Interference Graph from conjunction pooling: the rule-only ablation removes both the GNN and conjunction pooling simultaneously; an experiment with GNN-without-CP or CP-without-SIG would isolate which of the two primary architectural claims drives the +74pp margin. - No evaluation on post-2023 real-world DeFi exploits or contracts not in SmartBugs, leaving generalization to unseen attack patterns unvalidated. Bottom Line: A practitioner in smart contract security gains a principled architectural idea (conjunction pooling as differentiable AND-logic over named preconditions) and the first multi-seed GNN evaluation in this subfield, but cannot yet assess deployment value because false-positive rates are unmeasured and no GNN competitor is compared — making the 90.8% detection figure difficult to contextualize.
Search Results 2606.05986 PDF
#136 — Moderately Innovative (57)

InternVideo3: Agentify Foundation Models with Multimodal Contextual Reasoning (18 ▲ )

Ziang Yan, Sheng Xia, Jiashuo Yu, Yue Wu, Tianxiang Jiang, Songze Li, Kanghui Tian, Yicheng Xu, Yinan He, Kai Chen, Limin Wang, Yu Qiao, Yi Wang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 11 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces InternVideo3, a framework that combines a closed-loop multimodal reasoning formulation (MCR), a KV-cache-compressing attention reparameterization (M²LA), and a four-stage post-training recipe to produce a 7/8B open-weight video model that achieves leading open-source results on long-video benchmarks and can be instantiated as a tool-using video agent. - Why it's important: Real-world video tasks — long-form surveillance, egocentric activity, instructional video — cannot be reliably solved by single-pass QA; they require iterative evidence gathering, memory, and self-correction, capabilities that the open-source ecosystem has not matched relative to text-dominant agentic work. - How it does it: M²LA replaces standard GQA attention blocks with a low-rank latent KV factorization that caches compact latent vectors and reconstructs head-specific keys/values on the fly, halving KV-cache memory without dropping tokens, while the MCR loop appends observations, reasoning traces, tool outputs, and memory into one growing autoregressive context. - The good: M²LA achieves a 5× decode throughput speedup at 384K prefill tokens and permits operation at 768K prefill where the Qwen3-VL baseline runs out of memory, directly enabling the long-horizon rollouts the paper motivates. - The missing: The video-agent component is evaluated only qualitatively through four curated examples; no quantitative agentic benchmark numbers are reported, making it impossible to assess how much MCR at inference time actually contributes beyond the base model's single-pass performance. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on efficient long-context attention reparameterization or multimodal training recipes; practitioners seeking a drop-in long-video MLLM should note the paper is a preprint and the agentic claims are not quantitatively validated.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. The composite is held down by Completeness (54) and Resilience (55): the paper is titled around 'agentifying' models yet the MCR agent has zero quantitative support (four curated qualitative examples; the one numeric result 73.1->75.8 does not generalize), RL/distillation stages are not ablated separately, M²LA is not compared against other KV-cache compressors, and the model regresses on LVBench vs its own base. The real, hardware-measurable contribution is M²LA efficiency (5x decode at 384K, ~50% KV reduction, 768K prefill) supporting Impact (62), but it is integration/specialization rather than new primitives (Novelty 58). Candor about limitations is a credibility plus. Weighted: 0.20*58+0.25*58+0.15*55+0.15*54+0.15*55+0.10*62 = 57.
empirical Novelty 58 Rigor 58 Non-Obv 55 Completeness 54 Resilience 55 Impact 62
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Open-source multimodal large language models have improved rapidly on short-video QA benchmarks, but they still fail on tasks that require sustained temporal reasoning, cross-scene evidence integration, memory of prior observations, and iterative interaction with tools. Practically, domains such as long-form video analysis, instructional understanding, surveillance review, and egocentric perception demand exactly these capabilities. Scientifically, long-horizon video reasoning provides a concrete testbed for capabilities central to multimodal agency — latent state tracking, temporal abstraction, uncertainty-aware evidence selection — that are underexplored in the open-source ecosystem where agentic progress has concentrated on text-only settings (coding, search, browser control). A secondary bottleneck is hardware: preserving full multimodal context over multi-step rollouts produces KV-cache memory pressure that existing approaches address by dropping tokens (via subsampling, retrieval, or summarization), risking loss of evidence needed for later verification. Key Idea The central insight is that KV-cache compression and context-token preservation are decoupled concerns: instead of deciding which tokens to drop, one can shrink the per-token cached state through low-rank latent factorization inside the attention mechanism, keeping every multimodal token in the context while dramatically reducing memory. Applied to a pretrained GQA backbone, this yields M²LA — a post-training attention reparameterization that caches one compact latent vector per token and reconstructs full head-specific keys and values on the fly, making long multimodal rollouts feasible on commodity hardware without information loss. Methodology The framework has three interlocking components. (1) Multimodal Contextual Reasoning (MCR): formalizes long-horizon video understanding as a closed-loop rollout Cᵢ₊₁ = Cᵢ ⊕ (Rᵢ, Aᵢ₊₁, Fᵢ₊₁), where at each step the model observes visual evidence Vᵢ, produces reasoning Rᵢ, selects an action Aᵢ₊₁ (perceptive, tool, memory, verification, or termination), receives feedback Fᵢ₊₁, and updates the shared context. The rollout terminates when Rₙ satisfies the query. (2) M²LA: converts a pretrained GQA backbone by separating RoPE-carrying positional channels (aggregated via MQA-like shared linear projection calibrated to preserve RoPE geometry) from non-positional content channels (factorized through a learned low-rank bottleneck; cached latent projected up to head-specific KV on demand). Modality-aware adapters handle the distribution mismatch between text and vision tokens. Dynamic latent budgeting reduces rank more aggressively in deeper layers. A norm-concentration argument (empirically validated on calibration data) justifies replacing head-wise QK-RMSNorm with a Global-Norm-Linear approximation, making Qwen3-style backbones compatible. (3) Staged training recipe: (i) Continued pretraining (CPT) on 16M samples / 13.5B tokens to recover language and multimodal alignment after M²LA conversion; (ii) short-to-long SFT on ~7.2M samples with a two-stage curriculum (2 fps / 512 frames / 32k tokens → 4 fps / 2048 frames / 256k tokens) anchored by a curated 379K long-video corpus (mean 15.8 min, ~100K hours) with hierarchical narrative annotation and >1M synthesized QA pairs; (iii) rule-based GSPO on 5K temporal grounding examples (IoU ∈ [0.1, 0.7]) and 10K MC-QA examples with non-degenerate reward structure; (iv) on-policy distillation from Qwen3-235B using a reverse-KL objective on student-generated trajectories where teacher is correct and student is not. Key Results All results are from a single H200 GPU setup; the paper is a preprint. On long-video benchmarks (open-weight comparison, ~8B scale): Video-MME 73.8 (best open-weight, +8.7 vs InternVideo2.5-7B 65.1, +2.4 vs Qwen3-VL-8B 71.4); MLVU 77.3 (best open-weight, +4.5 vs InternVideo2.5-7B 72.8, +19.7 vs MiniCPM-V-4.5 60.6); VRBench 69.4 (best open-weight, +10.0 vs InternVL3.5-8B 64.1); EgoSchema 76.6 (best open-weight, exceeds reported human score of 76.0, +12.7 vs InternVideo2.5-7B 63.9). Weaker on LVBench: 55.7 vs base Qwen3-VL-8B 58.0 — a notable regression the authors acknowledge. On short-video: Short QA Avg 69.0 (best open-weight vs Eagle2.5-8B 67.0); PerceptionTest 81.4 (best open-weight). On temporal grounding: QVHighlights 59.9, Charades-STA 50.4, ANet 47.9 — all best open-weight but clearly below Gemini 2.5 Pro (70.4 / 52.8 / 58.1). On spatial: VSIBench 68.1 (best listed, +9.0 vs Qwen3-VL-8B 59.1). M²LA efficiency: 1.84× decode speedup at 32K prefill, 5.01× at 384K; reduces end-to-end latency 4× at 256K–384K; ~50% KV-cache reduction; baseline OOMs at 512K while M²LA runs to 768K. Agentic inference: Video-MME 73.1 → 75.8 (+2.7) with MCR at test time, but not consistently better across other benchmarks. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. The genuine novelty lies in (a) the M²LA post-training GQA-to-MLA conversion with RoPE-aware positional handling and modality-aware adapters, specifically designed for pretrained multimodal backbones including the QK-Norm compatibility trick; and (b) the combination of this efficient architecture with a staged long-video training recipe in a single coherent framework. The MCR formulation itself is, as the authors honestly acknowledge, a restatement of widely known closed-loop agent ideas applied to the video domain; the training recipe components (GSPO, on-policy distillation) are likewise individually known. The contribution is integration and specialization rather than invention of new primitives. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations (Table 5) cover three ingredients — CPT, long-context training, long-video data — and show complementary contributions across four benchmarks; however, rule-based RL and on-policy distillation are not ablated independently, so their individual contributions are unknown. No error bars, multiple seeds, or significance tests are reported. The GitHub URL is provided but the paper does not explicitly confirm that model weights or training code are released at submission time. The agentic evaluation consists of four curated qualitative examples with no quantitative metrics, making the MCR agent component unreproducible and unverifiable. A potential concern: the long-video SFT corpus includes V-MME-style tasks (77K videos), which overlaps in distribution with Video-MME evaluation benchmarks; the paper does not discuss whether evaluation contamination was checked. The model regresses on LVBench relative to its own Qwen3-VL-8B base (55.7 vs 58.0), indicating the training recipe does not universally improve over the base model. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Runs entirely on a single H200 GPU at 7–8B parameters with standard bf16 precision; no frontier-scale infrastructure required. - Efficient: M²LA reduces KV-cache by ~50% and achieves 1.84×–5.01× decode throughput gains over the GQA baseline, with the advantage scaling with context length; prefill cost is not substantially reduced and remains the dominant cost at 256K+ tokens. - Effective: Achieves best or near-best open-weight results on 7 of 13 reported video benchmarks, with the largest gains on long-horizon tasks directly motivating the work; regresses on LVBench and is not uniformly dominant, suggesting some benchmark-specific specialization in the training data. - Scalable: Supports up to 768K prefill tokens at inference (vs 512K OOM for baseline), and 256K training context; behavior at longer contexts or with more than one video stream is not evaluated, and the claim of enabling general long-horizon agents remains conceptual rather than experimentally demonstrated. The paper delivers a trained model as a software artifact (model checkpoint, pending release confirmation) rather than a reusable library; code availability is referenced via GitHub but not confirmed at submission. Strengths - M²LA is a technically careful post-training attention conversion that cleanly handles two distinct challenges (RoPE-incompatible positional channels and modality-distribution mismatch) with empirical validation of the QK-Norm approximation — the KV-cache halving and context extension results are concrete and hardware-measurable. - The long-video SFT corpus (379K videos, mean 15.8 min, ~100K hours, hierarchical narrative annotation with scene-level merging) is a meaningful data contribution that enables genuine long-horizon supervision. - The paper is unusually candid about its own limitations: acknowledging that newer models already surpass it, that MCR is conceptually not novel, that LVBench regresses, and that the agentic evaluation is proof-of-concept only — this transparency is a credibility asset. - The ablation study (Table 5) clearly demonstrates that CPT, long-context training, and curated long-video data each contribute differently, validating the four-stage recipe logic and ruling out a single dominant component. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly identify: (1) the open-weight ecosystem evolved during the project and newer models (Qwen3.5, GLM, Kimi, StepFun, DeepSeek-V4) already surpass InternVideo3 on several benchmarks; (2) M²LA is a post-training GQA-to-latent conversion that is less directly applicable to models with native MLA or linear attention; (3) MCR's conceptual ingredients (closed-loop reasoning, tool use, memory, self-correction) are widely shared across agent literature and the contribution is formulation/specialization rather than invention; (4) tool errors propagate into the shared context and tool interfaces are not jointly optimized; (5) evaluation is limited to video domains and does not cover GUI, browser, mobile, embodied, or multi-session settings. These limitations are confronted directly rather than minimized — a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The agentic evaluation is the paper's central motivation yet has zero quantitative support: the MCR video-agent is demonstrated only on four hand-selected qualitative examples, and the one quantitative agentic result (Video-MME 73.1 → 75.8 with MCR) is characterized as a preliminary proof-of-concept that does not consistently generalize to other benchmarks. A paper claiming to "agentify foundation models" that cannot report a single agentic task success rate is making a claim that outruns its evidence. - Rule-based RL and on-policy distillation are not ablated separately (Table 5 omits both), so their individual contributions to the final numbers are entirely unknown. - M²LA is not compared against other KV-cache compression methods (StreamingLLM, H2O, SnapKV, LESS, or the original DeepSeek MLA) on the same backbone; efficiency and quality trade-offs relative to alternatives cannot be assessed. - The long-video SFT corpus includes 77K videos sourced from V-MME-style tasks, raising a Goodhart's Law concern for Video-MME evaluation; no contamination analysis is provided. - RL training uses only 5K temporal grounding and 10K MC-QA examples — a very small post-training set — with no analysis of whether performance saturates or how sensitive results are to this data volume. - The model regresses on LVBench (55.7 vs base Qwen3-VL-8B 58.0) and the explanation — "base model retains dataset-specific advantages" — is not supported by any experiment isolating why. Bottom Line Practitioners building long-video reasoning pipelines will find the M²LA efficiency results and the staged training recipe genuinely useful; the benchmark gains on MLVU, VRBench, and EgoSchema are substantial, but the paper's central claim of producing a multimodal agent is not quantitatively validated, making it a strong video-MLLM improvement paper packaged as an agent paper.
Recommended 2606.12195 PDF
#137 — Moderately Innovative (57)

SpatialWorld: Benchmarking Interactive Spatial Reasoning of Multimodal Agents in Real-World Tasks (37 ▲ )

Hongcheng Gao, Hailong Qu, Jingyi Tang, Jiahao Wang, Zihao Huang, Hengkang Qiao, Shihong Huang, Junming Yang, Yi Li, Hongyixuan Yuan, Wenjie Li, Bohan Zeng, Wenbo Li, Bo Wang, Jianhui Liu, Olive Huang, Haoyang Huang, Wentao Zhang, Guoqing Huang, Nan Duan, Yinpeng Dong · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 9 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SPATIALWORLD, a unified benchmark of 760 human-annotated tasks across 8 heterogeneous 3D simulation backends for evaluating interactive spatial reasoning of multimodal agents under vision-only partial observability. - Why it's important: Static VQA and simulator-specific embodied benchmarks cannot reveal whether MLLMs can actively explore, update spatial beliefs, and complete closed-loop tasks in the diverse 3D environments that real-world deployment requires. - How it does it: A simulator-agnostic abstraction layer maps 8 disparate backends to a single egocentric RGB observation space and unified high-level text action interface, with success measured by terminal-state verifiers rather than trajectory matching. - The good: Even the strongest tested model (GPT-5) achieves only 17.4% average task success rate, and model rankings shift substantially across domains — a result only possible with cross-platform evaluation — exposing separable bottlenecks that single-simulator benchmarks miss. - The missing: The paper provides no inter-annotator agreement statistics for the success condition annotations that are the sole evaluation signal, reports no error bars or significance tests, and does not explain why VirtualHome TSRs (GPT-5: 44.7%) are 3× higher than AI2THOR (16.1%), which may undermine the difficulty-homogeneity claim. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building or evaluating spatial agents — it provides a well-engineered multi-environment scaffold and concrete evidence of capability gaps, but treat the numbers as single-run estimates pending statistical validation.
Why this rank: Systems profile (a built evaluation framework). The simulator-agnostic abstraction over 8 backends is a genuine engineering contribution and the cross-platform ranking shifts plus the Interaction-vs-Hybrid gap (50.2% vs 4.2%) are diagnostic findings unavailable from single-simulator benchmarks, supporting Impact (62). But Rigor (55), Resilience (55), and Completeness (58) are all limited by the same gaps: no inter-annotator agreement on the verifier scripts that are the sole evaluation signal, no error bars on 3-17% TSRs where binomial SE is non-trivial, an unexplained 3x VirtualHome/AI2THOR anomaly that undercuts the difficulty-homogeneity claim, and unconfirmed release status. Weighted (systems): 0.15*60+0.15*55+0.10*55+0.15*58+0.15*55+0.30*62 = 57.
systems Novelty 60 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 55 Completeness 58 Resilience 55 Impact 62
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation MLLMs must actively navigate partially observable 3D environments, gather egocentric visual evidence, and execute multi-step physical tasks — capabilities that static VQA and video benchmarks do not assess. Existing interactive benchmarks (ALFRED, EmbodiedBench, VLABench) couple evaluation to a single simulator's sensor assumptions, action interface, or execution pipeline, making it impossible to determine whether task success reflects general spatial reasoning or adaptation to that specific platform. The field needs an evaluation protocol that can test off-the-shelf MLLMs without simulator-specific fine-tuning, under realistic perceptual constraints, across diverse task domains simultaneously. Key Idea The core technical insight is to interpose a strict simulator-agnostic I/O bottleneck between 8 heterogeneous 3D backends and the MLLM agent: all environments expose an identical egocentric RGB screenshot + high-level text action interface, while an Action-to-Code mapping layer silently translates unified primitives into simulator-specific calls. This decoupling ensures that measured TSR differences reflect spatial reasoning ability rather than interface familiarity. Incorporating abstract 3D games (Maze, Snake, Block3D, Rubik's Cube) alongside photorealistic simulators further separates visual-semantic grounding from pure geometric and topological reasoning. Methodology Tasks are formalized as POMDPs ⟨S, O, A, T, Ω, R⟩ with vision-only observations: at each step the agent receives one egocentric RGB screenshot and no auxiliary sensor data (no depth, no semantic maps, no global occupancy). The MLLM policy πθ predicts a high-level action from the observation-action history. The unified action space A has four categories: Navigation (Move with direction and granularity), Viewpoint & Posture (Rotate, Tilt, ChangePosture), Interaction (Pick/Place, ChangeState, Manipulate), and Task-Control (EndTask, Communicate). The Action Interface maps these to simulator-specific calls (e.g., Move(forward, medium) → AI2THOR's MoveAhead(moveMagnitude=0.5) or CARLA's FollowLane(distance=10)). Step budget per task = 2g + 10, where g is the human-annotated reference step count. Evaluation uses temperature τ = 1.0 and a sliding context window of w = 30 turns. Primary metric is Task Success Rate (TSR), measured by a task-specific terminal-state verifier checking final environment state. Secondary metric is Step Efficiency (SE = n*_i / n_i, averaged over successful tasks only), distinguishing efficient agents from trial-and-error solvers. Data construction follows three stages: (i) task design — annotators write natural-language instructions and configure initial states; (ii) human execution — annotators independently solve each task to record ground-truth terminal states and reference trajectories; (iii) cross-verification — separate reviewers check feasibility, instruction clarity, and verifier script correctness. Three complexity levels are assigned per task: Navigation (movement/viewpoint only), Interaction (object-state changes without extensive exploration), and Hybrid (both combined). The 760 tasks span 6 scenario categories distributed across 8 backends: AI2THOR (311), ProcTHOR (127), VirtualHome (38), CARLA (80), EmbodiedCity (53), Multi-AI2THOR (29), Multi-ProcTHOR (17), and 3D Games (105). All three design decisions — vision-only observation, high-level text actions, and terminal-state verification — are well-justified by the goal of evaluating off-the-shelf MLLMs without simulator-specific adaptation. Key Results Fifteen current-generation MLLMs were evaluated via official APIs or open-weight checkpoints without task-specific fine-tuning. GPT-5 leads overall at 17.4% average TSR (14.4% physical, 36.4% digital); Qwen-3.5-397B-A17B is the top open-source model at 14.1% (12.2% physical, 26.0% digital); Gemini-3.1-Pro leads digital games at 39.0% TSR. Smaller models (Kimi-VL-A3B: 0.9% physical, GLM-4.6V: 2.7% physical) are near-zero on physical tasks. Complexity breakdown reveals a compound bottleneck: Interaction-only tasks achieve 50.2% mean TSR while Hybrid (Navigation + Interaction) tasks achieve only 4.2%, with GPT-5 dominating Interaction (69.4%) and Gemini dominating Navigation (8.6%). Environment-level breakdown shows GPT-5 and Qwen-3.5 lead indoors (AI2THOR: 16.1% and 16.7%), while Gemini-3-Flash and GPT-5 lead outdoors (CARLA+EmbodiedCity: 9.0% and 8.3%). VirtualHome TSRs are anomalously high (Gemini-3.1-Pro: 50.0%, GPT-5: 44.7%) relative to all other physical environments. For digital games, GPT-5 achieves 91.2% on Snake but 0.0% on Block3D; Qwen3-VL-235B-Thinking leads Maze (70.0%); Gemini-3-Flash leads Rubik's Cube (50.0%). Models that succeed on reactive/navigation tasks systematically fail on multi-step geometric transformation tasks. SE analysis shows Doubao-2.0-Lite achieves the highest efficiency (SE=0.704) despite low TSR, while GPT-5 achieves TSR via persistence (median 7 success steps, 22 failure steps) and GPT-5.4 fails primarily via premature termination (32.4% premature DONE, 48.5% explicit FAIL with median 3.5 success steps). Perceptual ablations show resolution has negligible effect on TSR; higher FOV modestly improves performance but plateaus. Temperature sensitivity is marginal at τ=1.0. Novelty Classification NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The simulator-agnostic unified evaluation framework spanning 8 heterogeneous backends simultaneously is genuinely new; prior work (ALFRED, EmbodiedBench, OSWorld) evaluated within a single platform or environment family. The individual components — POMDP formulation, terminal-state verification (borrowed from OSWorld/Spider2-V), high-level action abstraction — are drawn from prior art, but their systematic integration for cross-platform MLLM evaluation is the contribution. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are narrow: inference hyperparameters (temperature, window size, action parameterization) are studied on a subset of models, but no ablation tests which benchmark design choices (e.g., vision-only vs. adding depth) drive observed TSR gaps. No error bars, confidence intervals, or significance tests are reported anywhere; with TSRs in the 3–17% range across 760 tasks, single-run variance could be substantial. No inter-annotator agreement metric (Kappa or similar) is provided for success condition correctness despite this being the sole evaluation signal. The paper mentions a project page (https://spatial-world.github.io) but does not explicitly state code, data, or verifier script release status at preprint submission. The qualitative failure analysis covers only 100 manually inspected trajectories — a small fraction of the total failed runs. The unexplained 3× performance gap between VirtualHome and AI2THOR is a reproducibility concern: if VirtualHome tasks are structurally simpler, the benchmark's difficulty calibration claim is weakened. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS — a built evaluation framework deployed by running 15 MLLM agents on 760 tasks. - Practical: Proprietary models run via cloud APIs; open-source models require 8× NVIDIA H200 GPUs, a significant barrier for most research groups. - Efficient: Full evaluation consumed ~5,000 GPU hours on H200 hardware; per-task cost scales as (2g+10 steps) × number of models, making exhaustive evaluation of new architectures non-trivial. - Effective: The framework demonstrably separates model capabilities across domains, complexity levels, and task types — model rankings differ across environments in ways that expose distinct algorithmic strengths, validating the diagnostic claim. - Scalable: 760 tasks is small by benchmark standards; the paper acknowledges that human annotation is the bottleneck and that rapid scaling is a future challenge. The modular architecture can accommodate additional backends, but task creation remains manual. The work delivers a benchmark framework (tasks, verifiers, evaluation harness) rather than a software library or trained model; code/data release status is not confirmed at preprint stage. Strengths - The simulator-agnostic abstraction layer is a genuine engineering contribution: it enables, for the first time, a controlled comparison of off-the-shelf MLLMs across 8 backends without simulator-specific adaptation, and the finding that model rankings shift substantially across domains would be invisible with single-simulator evaluation. - Including abstract 3D games (Block3D, Snake, Rubik, Maze) alongside photorealistic simulators cleanly disentangles visual-semantic grounding from pure geometric reasoning; the gap between Interaction-only (50.2% TSR) and Hybrid (4.2% TSR) is a clear, actionable finding about long-horizon planning deficits. - The GPT-5 vs. GPT-5.4 case study (Appendix G) is well-executed: it diagnoses the counter-intuitive result (GPT-5 outperforming the newer GPT-5.4) as a difference in termination policy rather than raw capability, using step-count distributions and failure-mode categorization to support the claim quantitatively. - Evaluating 15 current-generation models including GPT-5, Gemini-3.1-Pro, Gemini-3-Flash, Qwen-3.5-397B-A17B, and Kimi-K2.5 provides strong face validity and a comprehensive leaderboard snapshot at the time of writing. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state two limitations: (1) the benchmark operates in simulated environments rather than physical robotic platforms, and real-world transfer remains future work; (2) the 760-task scale is modest compared to automatically generated datasets due to the human annotation requirement. Both are stated directly and credibly without minimization. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No inter-annotator agreement statistics: the verifier scripts are the sole evaluation signal, and their correctness is claimed via cross-validation but never quantified (no Kappa, no disagreement rate). A systematic bias in success condition definition would silently invalidate all TSR numbers. - The VirtualHome anomaly is not investigated: GPT-5 achieves 44.7% TSR on VirtualHome but only 16.1% on AI2THOR; if VirtualHome tasks have smaller scene graphs, simpler affordance structures, or scripted routines that match training distributions better, the benchmark's homogeneity across environments is compromised. - Single-run evaluation with no confidence bounds: at 3–17% TSR over 760 tasks, binomial standard errors are on the order of 0.7–1.5 percentage points, which is non-negligible relative to gaps between adjacent models on the leaderboard. - The step budget formula (2g+10) is not ablated; it is unclear whether tighter or looser budgets would change model rankings, particularly for models with efficient-but-incomplete exploration strategies. - The resolution-insensitivity finding is asserted ("spatial perception relies on physical, projective, and ray relationships unaffected by lower resolutions") without mechanistic evidence; this is a surprising empirical result that warrants more investigation rather than a post-hoc explanation. Bottom Line SPATIALWORLD makes a credible and useful contribution by demonstrating that no current MLLM reliably solves interactive 3D spatial tasks under realistic perceptual constraints, and its cross-platform architecture produces diagnostic findings unavailable from any single-simulator benchmark; however, the 760-task scale, absence of statistical validation, and the unexplained VirtualHome performance anomaly mean practitioners should treat the specific TSR numbers as directional estimates rather than definitive measurements.
Recommended 2606.09669 PDF
#138 — Moderately Innovative (57)

MMAE: A Massive Multitask Audio Editing Benchmark (39 ▲ )

Ziyang Ma, Ruiqi Yan, Ruiyang Xu, Jie Fang, Zhikang Niu, Yi-Wen Chao, Wenming Tu, Tianrui Wang, Auden, Qi Chen, Wenxi Chen, Jiaying Chi, Yanru Huo, Zixuan Jiang, Xiquan Li, Yalin Li, Junxi Liu, Minghao Liu, Binghao Qiang, Yijia Shan, Zheshu Song, Tian Tan, Zixiang Wang, Zeyu Xie, Zhifei Xie, Xiaoyu Xing, Qixiang Xu, Chen Yang, Guanrou Yang, Shan Yang, Yifan Yang, Steve Yves, Haotian Zhang, Haina Zhu, Kai Yu, Liefeng Bo, Eng-Siong Chng, Xie Chen · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 8 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces MMAE, a benchmark of 2,000 audio editing samples paired with 17,741 atomic rubric-based evaluation criteria spanning 7 audio modalities, 6 complexity levels, and 8 operation types, designed to evaluate instruction-based audio editing systems. - Why it's important: No comprehensive cross-modal evaluation standard exists for the rapidly emerging class of instruction-guided audio editing models, making it impossible to systematically identify failure modes or compare systems across the full range of real-world editing tasks. - How it does it: Each sample is paired with atomic multiple-choice rubrics that decompose the editing task into independently verifiable criteria across instruction-following and content-consistency dimensions, scored by an external MLLM judge (Qwen3-Omni) via majority vote over 3 independent queries with shuffled option positions. - The good: The Exact Match Rate metric's near-zero scores (<5% across all models, 0% EMR for multiple models on mixed-modality tasks) provide a surprisingly clean diagnostic signal that exposes a large gap between apparent partial competency (moderate IFR/CR) and actual reliable editing. - The missing: No validation of the Qwen3-Omni judge against human ground truth is presented, leaving the benchmark's core measurement instrument unverified; if the judge has systematic perceptual biases, all reported scores are unreliable. - Read it or not: Essential reading for researchers building or evaluating audio editing models; the EMR finding and the mean-seeking vs. mode-seeking behavioral dichotomy are immediately actionable insights.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (52) and Resilience (52) cap the score: the entire benchmark's validity rests on Qwen3-Omni as audio judge with no human-vs-judge agreement experiment reported, no inter-annotator statistics for rubric annotation, rubrics derived from captions of the input (not the edited output), and no diffusion baselines despite their prominence in related work. Non-Obviousness (62) is helped by the genuinely interesting EMR diagnostic and the mean-seeking vs. mode-seeking behavioral dichotomy. Novelty (62) reflects the first cross-modal multi-complexity audio-editing benchmark, though the rubric-decomposition paradigm is adapted from analogous text/image work. Completeness (55) suffers from no formal limitations section. Public release supports Impact (65), but the unvalidated core instrument is a serious unresolved flaw.
empirical Novelty 62 Rigor 52 Non-Obv 62 Completeness 55 Resilience 52 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Instruction-based audio editing — where users modify speech, music, or sound effects via natural language commands — has matured into a distinct research area with multiple competing systems, but evaluation infrastructure has not kept pace. Existing test sets (RealEdit, Ming-Freeform-Audio-Edit, Step-Audio-Edit-Benchmark, StoryGen-Eval, MMEdit's internal evaluation) are each restricted to a single modality or narrow operation set, and rely on signal-level metrics (FAD, LSD, CLAP similarity, WER, MOS) that cannot explicitly assess whether the requested edit was executed correctly or whether unrelated audio content was preserved. System builders have no unified testbed to measure multi-modal competence, benchmark complex compositional tasks (multi-hop reasoning, iterative multi-round edits), or identify specific capability bottlenecks across the instruction-following / context-preservation tradeoff. Key Idea The core insight is rubric decomposition: rather than applying a single aggregated metric to an open-ended editing output, each sample's expected behavior is encoded as a set of atomic, independently verifiable multiple-choice questions. Each rubric probes exactly one perceptual property (e.g., "does the output contain dog barking?", "is the background music unchanged?") and is answered by an audio language model judge. This sidesteps the ambiguity of composite signal metrics while remaining automatable at scale and interpretable per-dimension. Methodology Benchmark construction follows five stages: (1) expert brainstorming sessions to enumerate creative and practical editing scenarios across modalities and complexity levels; (2) taxonomy design establishing three orthogonal axes — Modality (7 categories: sound, music, speech, and all pairwise/triple combinations), Complexity (6 levels: Single, Multi-part, Multi-instruction, Multi-audio, Multi-round, Multi-hop), and Operation (8 types organized into Local additions/removals/replacements/extractions/alterations and Global background-change/foreground-change/alteration); (3) instruction-centric data collection where annotators manually retrieve and trim real-world audio from online video sources with dynamic balancing across taxonomy cells; (4) human-agent collaborative rubric annotation using OmniDetective for detailed audio captioning, LLM-generated initial rubric drafts from captions + instructions + metadata, human annotator refinement (add/remove/edit rubrics), and LLM-based post-processing for linguistic normalization; (5) blind cross-validation quality inspection with iterative revision and discard of irreparable cases. Critical design decisions: rubrics must satisfy Completeness, Atomicity, Orthogonality, and Objectivity principles; the 5.29 Consistency vs. 3.58 Instruction-Following rubrics per sample reflects the authors' judgment that context preservation has more dimensions to verify. Evaluation uses Qwen3-Omni as external judge with 3-query majority vote and random option shuffling per query, yielding IFR, CR, and EMR. Five recent models are evaluated: Step-Audio-EditX, Ming-UniAudio, MMEdit, Audio-Omni, and SmartDJ (with and without an external Gemini 2.0 Flash planner for task decomposition). Key Results All five models achieve EMR below 5% overall; several hit 0% EMR on mixed-modality settings (Sound-Music: Step-Audio-EditX and Ming-UniAudio both 0.00%; Sound-Music-Speech similarly near-zero). Best overall IFR: Audio-Omni at 50.73% on the ≤10s subset; Step-Audio-EditX at 44.86% on the full set. Best CR: Step-Audio-EditX at 58.88% (full set). Performance universally degrades from Single to Multiple complexity: Audio-Omni's IFR drops 58.43% → 41.70%, CR drops 64.57% → 47.94%. Speech-only editing yields highest CR (Step-Audio-EditX: 77.27%), while Sound-Music-Speech is hardest. Adding the external planner to SmartDJ raises IFR (38.20% → 42.26%) but drops CR (55.41% → 48.33%) with no EMR gain — cascaded generation steps accumulate artifacts. Counter-intuitively, Step-Audio-EditX outperforms Ming-UniAudio on both IFR (44.86% vs. 29.82%) and CR (58.88% vs. 52.71%) yet achieves lower EMR (3.05% vs. 3.20%), which the authors interpret as a mean-seeking (broad partial success) vs. mode-seeking (sparse perfect success) behavioral split. The Identity baseline (returning input unchanged) achieves 94.13% CR but only 27.37% IFR, confirming the IFR/CR tradeoff is fundamental rather than a metric artifact. Note that MMEdit, Audio-Omni, and SmartDJ are evaluated on only 801/2,000 samples (≤10s inputs) due to model constraints, limiting direct cross-model comparisons. Novelty Classification NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK; NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. MMAE is the first cross-modal, multi-complexity benchmark for instruction-based audio editing. The rubric-decomposition evaluation paradigm is adapted from analogous work in text RL reward (Gunjal et al. 2025), audio reasoning (IS2026 challenge), and image editing (Edit-Compass) — the application to audio editing is new to the domain, but the individual components are standard adaptations of established evaluation ideas applied to a new problem space. Rigor & Reproducibility The dataset and rubrics are publicly released on GitHub (https://github.com/ddlBoJack/MMAE) and Hugging Face (https://huggingface.co/datasets/BoJack/MMAE). Evaluation stability is addressed via 3-query majority voting with shuffled option positions. However, no inter-annotator agreement statistics are reported for the human annotation stage, which is a significant methodological gap. No validation of the Qwen3-Omni judger against human ground truth is reported; the entire benchmark's validity rests on this unverified assumption. No ablations are presented on rubric design choices (e.g., how rubric count or granularity affects model rankings). No error bars, confidence intervals, or significance tests are reported for model comparisons. This is a preprint (arXiv:2606.07229v1, June 2026), not peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL — the paper's primary claims rest on benchmark evaluation experiments. - Practical: The benchmark data and rubrics are publicly available; evaluation requires access to Qwen3-Omni (a commercially available MLLM) and the target audio editing models, all of which run on standard GPU hardware. - Efficient: Full evaluation requires approximately 3 × 17,741 = 53,223 MLLM judge inference passes; wall-clock evaluation time is not reported, but this is non-trivial compute for a 2,000-sample benchmark. - Effective: The benchmark successfully differentiates model capabilities across complexity and modality axes, and its EMR metric delivers clearer diagnostic signal than individual IFR/CR averages; the mean-seeking vs. mode-seeking finding is a direct consequence of the multi-rubric structure. - Scalable: The taxonomy is compositional and the curation pipeline is documented, suggesting extensibility; however, no experiments demonstrate that adding more samples or modalities changes model rankings or reveals new failure modes. The dataset stops at a paper artifact — no versioning, update, or extension plan is described. Strengths - The three-axis orthogonal taxonomy (Modality × Complexity × Operation) covers substantially broader scope than any prior audio editing benchmark, particularly the inclusion of multi-hop reasoning and multi-round iterative editing tasks that reflect realistic user workflows absent from prior test sets. - The IFR/CR/EMR decomposition correctly captures the fundamental tradeoff in audio editing (change what was requested, preserve everything else), and the reference baselines (Identity achieves 94% CR; Noise achieves ~32% IFR) empirically justify reporting these as separate metrics rather than as a composite. - The mean-seeking vs. mode-seeking behavioral dichotomy (Step-Audio-EditX vs. Ming-UniAudio inversely ranked by average metrics vs. EMR) is a genuine empirical insight that directly challenges standard optimization targets and mirrors a well-known tension in generative modeling. - Human-agent collaborative rubric generation using OmniDetective for audio captioning reduces LLM hallucination in rubric construction by grounding generation in observable audio descriptions rather than model imagination. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors do not include a formal limitations section. They implicitly acknowledge that three of five models are constrained to ≤10s inputs, preventing a clean full-set comparison. They note that agentic planners show no consistent improvement and interpret this as indicating base model capability is the bottleneck — a reasonable inference, but not further substantiated. The paper does not explicitly acknowledge judger reliability, inter-annotator agreement gaps, or the dependency of rubric quality on the OmniDetective captioning model's accuracy. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Judger validity is unverified: Qwen3-Omni is used as the sole judge for all rubrics, but no human-vs.-judge agreement experiment is reported. If the judger systematically misperceives certain audio properties (e.g., subtle timbre changes, pitch shifts in mixed-modality audio), all downstream scores are biased in unknown directions. - No inter-annotator agreement statistics are reported for the human rubric annotation stage; annotation consistency is asserted through quality control process description but not quantified. - Rubrics are derived from OmniDetective captions of input audio, not from human listening to the edited output — the "correct" answer in each rubric is constructed before any edit is produced, which may encode caption errors as ground truth. - The 2:1 Consistency-to-IF rubric ratio (5.29 vs. 3.58 per sample) may systematically disadvantage models that attempt aggressive global edits; this ratio is not justified empirically. - Three models are evaluated on only the ≤10s subset (801/2,000 samples), introducing selection bias — shorter clips may be structurally simpler or drawn from a different difficulty distribution than the full set, making direct comparisons across evaluation subsets unreliable. - No diffusion-based baselines (AUDIT, AudioEditor, AudioMorphix) are included in the evaluation despite extensive coverage in the related work section, leaving a gap between what is benchmarked and what the field has produced. Bottom Line MMAE is the field's most comprehensive audio editing evaluation resource and delivers a genuinely important finding — that all current instruction-based audio editing models fail to reliably execute even moderately complex edits — but its credibility depends entirely on the unvalidated reliability of Qwen3-Omni as an audio judge, and establishing that reliability should be the immediate follow-up priority before the benchmark is adopted as a standard.
Recommended 2606.07229 PDF
#139 — Moderately Innovative (57)

Pretraining Language Models on Historical Text

X Luo, Z Shinnick, N Griesshaber, Y Wang, J Yu, F Shi… · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces TYPEWRITERLM, a 7.24B-parameter language model trained exclusively on pre-1913 English text, together with TYPEWRITERCORPUS (54B tokens), two instruction-tuning datasets (HISTORY-LIMA, HISTORY-SELFINSTRUCT), and HISTORY-EVENT, a 2,344-event benchmark for verifying temporal knowledge cutoffs. - Why it's important: Modern LLMs have memorized historical events and their outcomes, making them methodologically unsuitable for historical analysis, diachronic language research, or temporal prediction tasks where lookahead bias invalidates any apparent foresight. - How it does it: By enforcing leakage control at every pipeline stage — rule-based corpus cleaning during pretraining, lexically grounded instruction tuning (responses drawn verbatim from pre-1913 source documents and constrained to source-document vocabulary), and a temporally calibrated evaluation benchmark. - The good: The BPB surprisingness metric cleanly shows that TYPEWRITERLM becomes substantially more surprised by post-1913 events (BPBpost/BPBpre ratio 1.35) compared to Llama-3.1-8B's flat profile, providing empirical evidence that the knowledge cutoff is encoded in the model's learned distribution. - The missing: The pretraining corpus is 97.7% Institutional Books from Harvard Library — a single institutional collection — yet no ablation examines whether this extreme domain concentration degrades model quality or temporal calibration relative to a more balanced corpus. - Read it or not: Worth reading for NLP researchers studying temporally bounded language models, computational historians, or anyone building lookahead-bias-free models for finance or science — but not a substitute for the larger Talkie-1930 if raw factual recall is the primary requirement.
Why this rank: Empirical/systems profile, scored on empirical weights. Impact (65) and Novelty (62) are the strengths — full open release of a 7.24B history-bounded model, 54B-token corpus, two instruction datasets, and a temporal benchmark constitutes a complete research infrastructure, and end-to-end leakage control across pretraining, instruction tuning, and evaluation is a genuine subfield advance. Rigor (50) and Resilience (50) are the limiters: no ablation on the 97.7%-single-source corpus concentration, the BPB cutoff comparison against Llama-3.1-8B is fatally asymmetric (38 vs. 1,163 post-cutoff events), Gemini serves as both gold-answer generator and evaluator (circularity), and no held-out historical perplexity is reported. Low pre-cutoff factual recall (6.2% strict) further bounds practical utility.
empirical Novelty 62 Rigor 50 Non-Obv 58 Completeness 62 Resilience 50 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Modern LLMs trained on web-scale contemporary data suffer from lookahead bias: when queried about historical events, they draw on post-hoc knowledge absorbed during pretraining rather than reasoning from period-consistent information. This invalidates their use in (1) historically grounded social science research, (2) diachronic language-change studies where a model must reflect a specific era's vocabulary and values, and (3) temporal prediction benchmarks where training data contamination renders accuracy measurements meaningless. Prior History LMs (Talkie-1930, Ranke-4B, TimeCapsuleLLM) have addressed pretraining cutoffs but documented residual leakage, remain small in scale, or reintroduce anachronisms through modern-LLM-generated instruction-tuning data. No previous work controls leakage simultaneously at the pretraining, post-training, and evaluation stages. Key Idea Temporal leakage is not solely a pretraining data problem — it re-enters the model at instruction tuning if responses are generated by frontier LLMs, even when prompts are filtered for historical content, because modern language distributions persist at the lexical and stylistic level. The core insight is to invert the standard instruction-tuning pipeline: fix responses as direct extractions from pre-1913 source documents (guaranteeing temporal purity by construction), and generate or fine-tune only the instruction side. A verifiable lexical constraint — every response token must appear in the source passage vocabulary V(p) ∪ A (where A is a small function-word allowlist) — enforces this property mechanically rather than by heuristic filtering. Methodology Pretraining corpus (TYPEWRITERCORPUS): Aggregates 54B tokens from 10 sources spanning 1700–1913, dominated by Institutional Books (Harvard Library, 52.7B tokens, 97.7%). Additional sources provide genre diversity: BL Books (British Library), Hansard (parliamentary), Royal Society Corpus (scientific), Old Bailey (legal/spoken), CLMET (literary), and four smaller corpora. OCR cleaning includes conservative split-word joining, punctuation normalization, and quality filtering (OCR score >92 for Institutional Books, >80 for BL Books); paragraphs under 100 characters are discarded. Rule-based leakage filtering removes institutional provenance metadata, HTML artifacts, and editorial imprint lines. Model architecture: 7.24B-parameter decoder-only Transformer following Llama 3 (32 layers, hidden size 4096, SwiGLU FFN inner dimension 14336, GQA with 32 query / 8 KV heads, head dimension 128, 8192-token context, RMSNorm, RoPE). A custom 32K BPE tokenizer is trained from scratch on TYPEWRITERCORPUS to avoid modern subword contamination. Trained with AdamW (β1=0.9, β2=0.95, weight decay 0.1, cosine LR peak 3×10⁻⁴) on ~140B tokens (~2.59 epochs over the corpus). HISTORY-LIMA: 1,030 single-turn instruction-response pairs (plus 30 multi-turn dialogues). Responses extracted from source documents and verified by exact lexical membership in V(p) ∪ A, with controlled recovery operations for OCR artifacts (morphological normalization, dehyphenation, page-break reconstruction). Instructions generated by Claude Opus, then human-reviewed for historical coherence and leakage. Top-scoring pairs retained via LLM judge. HISTORY-SELFINSTRUCT: Inverts SelfInstruct — HISTORY-LIMA serves as seed; the base model is LoRA-fine-tuned (r=64) on the 1K seed to produce a historically consistent instruction generator; this generator produces candidate instructions over a large corpus of lexically grounded responses; LLM-judge filtering retains 287,860 pairs. Responses remain lexically grounded throughout. Instruction fine-tuning: LoRA (r=64, α=128, dropout=0.05) targeting all attention and MLP projection matrices plus token embeddings and LM head. HISTORY-LIMA variant: 15 epochs, batch 16, 1×GH200. SELFINSTRUCT variant: 1 epoch, batch 64, 4×GH200. Evaluation: General benchmarks (ARC-Easy, ARC-Challenge, HellaSwag, filtered AlpacaEval 2.0, IFEval); HELLASWAG-1800 (topic-filtered and historically rewritten to isolate style vs. topic effects); HISTORY-EVENT (2,344 Wikipedia century-timeline events, BPB surprisingness scoring, strict/relaxed factual recall judged by Gemini-3.1-Flash-Lite). Critical design decisions: (1) Custom tokenizer — well-justified to prevent modern subword patterns from entering the vocabulary.
