(2) Lexical grounding constraint — sound motivation, though the function-word allowlist construction and size are not ablated. (3) 97.7% corpus concentration in Institutional Books — not ablated; the contribution of diverse minor sources to model quality is unknown. Key Results General benchmarks (Table 2): TYPEWRITERLM base (7.24B) achieves ARC-Easy 41.3%, ARC-Challenge 27.1%, HellaSwag 39.0%, outperforming all smaller History LMs (GPT-1900 3.3B: 36.7/23.8/32.3; TimeCapsuleLLM 1.22B: 30.3/22.0/25.9) but below Talkie-1930 (13B: 56.1/35.6/49.8) and GPT2-XL (1.55B, modern, 50.8/27.9/48.9). Scale confounds model comparisons across the History LM field. HELLASWAG-1800 (Table 6): TYPEWRITERLM improves from 35.9% (original) to 39.0% (rewritten), while GPT2-XL drops from 50.9% to 41.1% and Qwen3-4B drops from 68.4% to 53.8%, confirming that historical language style — not just topic — penalizes History LMs on standard benchmarks. BPB surprisingness (Table 8): TYPEWRITERLM base BPBpre=1.282, BPBpost=1.727, ratio=1.35. Talkie-1930 base ratio=1.41. Llama-3.1-8B ratio=1.42, but this comparison is severely asymmetric — only 38 post-cutoff events are available for the modern baseline vs. 1,163 for TYPEWRITERLM. Leakage (Table 3): TYPEWRITERLM (SELFINSTRUCT) shows 0.0% strict / 0.6% relaxed post-cutoff leakage on HISTORY-EVENT. Pre-cutoff recall: 6.2% strict, 25.4% relaxed — substantially below Talkie-1930's 31.1%/51.2%, suggesting TYPEWRITERLM sacrifices factual density for stricter temporal fidelity. AlpacaEval (Table 4): Both TYPEWRITERLM variants achieve 43.4–45.1% win rate vs. GPT-1900 on 388 filtered prompts, despite operating under much stricter lexical constraints. IFEval (Table 5): TYPEWRITERLM (SELFINSTRUCT) achieves P-S 11.7, I-S 23.1, outperforming GPT-1900 (P-S 9.1, I-S 19.3) and Talkie-1930-IT (P-S 9.2, I-S 19.5). Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL, NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK, NEW METHOD. The combination of corpus (TYPEWRITERCORPUS), model (TYPEWRITERLM), instruction datasets (HISTORY-LIMA, HISTORY-SELFINSTRUCT), and evaluation benchmark (HISTORY-EVENT) constitutes a complete history-LM research infrastructure. The lexically grounded instruction tuning constraint is a novel post-training methodology, though its core idea — extracting responses from source documents — has structural parallels to extractive QA; the novelty lies in applying this as a leakage-prevention mechanism across a large instruction-tuning pipeline. The SELFINSTRUCT inversion (fix responses, generate instructions) is a meaningful adaptation. The BPB surprisingness metric for cutoff verification is borrowed from prior work (Levine et al., 2026; Duderstadt and Helm, 2026). Rigor & Reproducibility No ablations on corpus composition or domain mixing ratios — the authors explicitly acknowledge this gap. No ablations on the function-word allowlist, lexical constraint strictness, or the morphological recovery operations used in verification. No error bars, seeds, or significance tests on any benchmark result. The LLM judge for HISTORY-SELFINSTRUCT filtering (287,860 pairs) is not identified — if it is a modern frontier model, it may introduce modern quality preferences into the retained data distribution. The HISTORY-EVENT benchmark uses Gemini-3.1-Flash-Lite as both gold-answer generator (filtering 2,344 → 1,726 events) and evaluator, creating a circularity: evaluation quality is bounded by a single external judge's capabilities and its own knowledge biases. Code, model weights, and datasets are released at hf.co/typewriter-ai. This is a preprint (arXiv:2606.02991v1). Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL (builds and evaluates a language model system). - Practical: The model runs on commercially available hardware; instruction tuning used 1–4×GH200 GPUs, which are commercially available but not broadly accessible; inference requirements are standard for a 7B model. - Efficient: The paper provides no explicit FLOPs, training time, or inference latency figures; 140B training tokens at 7B scale is computationally comparable to similar-size models in the literature but exact cost is not stated. - Effective: BPB surprisingness and leakage recall confirm the knowledge cutoff is encoded in the model's distribution; however, pre-cutoff factual recall (6.2% strict) is low compared to Talkie-1930 (31.1%), and instruction-following capability as measured on IFEval peaks below 12% prompt-level accuracy — functional but limited. - Scalable: Not tested at larger scale; the paper correctly notes that historical corpora are finite and non-renewable, creating a hard ceiling on further pretraining data scaling without synthetic augmentation, which is left as future work. Delivers software artifacts: model weights (base and two instruction-tuned variants), corpus, instruction-tuning datasets, and evaluation benchmark, all released on Hugging Face. Strengths - End-to-end leakage pipeline: the first History LM paper to address temporal contamination simultaneously at pretraining, instruction tuning, and evaluation — rather than only at corpus construction — is a meaningful methodological advance for this subfield. - Lexically grounded instruction tuning is a verifiable, mechanically enforceable constraint on post-training temporal purity; unlike prompt-based filtering of modern knowledge, it cannot be circumvented by subtle distributional leakage. - HELLASWAG-1800 (topic-filtered and style-rewritten benchmark variants) produces a clean experimental dissociation showing that language style — not just topic knowledge — is responsible for a substantial fraction of History LM underperformance on standard benchmarks; this is a useful empirical finding for the evaluation community. - Full open release of all artifacts (model, corpus, instruction datasets, benchmark, training pipeline) establishes a reproducible baseline for a field that has been fragmented across incompatible private resources. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state: (1) no systematic study of dataset composition or mixing ratios despite extreme imbalance; (2) the model may generate content considered offensive by modern standards; (3) temporal leakage cannot be fully eliminated — OCR-transcribed corpora may contain modern metadata in prefaces, footnotes, and supplementary materials; (4) data-efficient training strategies and historically grounded synthetic data generation are deferred to future work. The limitations section is honest and specific, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The 97.7% dominance of Institutional Books (Harvard Library) means TYPEWRITERLM is effectively a model of Harvard's historical book collection, not 1700–1913 English text broadly. Newspaper, periodical, commercial, and non-elite text are absent or negligible; no ablation distinguishes TYPEWRITERCORPUS's diverse 2.3% from the baseline of 97.7% alone. - The BPB surprisingness comparison with Llama 3.1 8B Instruct is fatally asymmetric: the modern baseline contributes only 38 post-cutoff events (insufficient for stable BPB estimation) vs. 1,163 for TYPEWRITERLM. The claim that Llama's BPB "remains flat" is not statistically grounded and the paper does not address this directly. - Using Gemini-3.1-Flash-Lite as both the gold-answer generator (determining which 1,726 of 2,344 events are retained) and the evaluator (judging History LM responses against those gold answers) introduces systematic filter bias: retained events are those Gemini can answer confidently, skewing the benchmark toward events well-represented in modern training data — precisely the events most likely to have leaked into History LMs. - No perplexity on held-out historical text is reported — the most direct measure of whether the model learned the target historical language distribution is absent from the evaluation. - AlpacaEval comparisons are only against GPT-1900, not against any modern instruction model on the filtered prompt set, making it impossible to assess the absolute instruction-following quality gap or ceiling. - The LLM judge used to filter HISTORY-SELFINSTRUCT pairs (287,860 pairs retained) is not identified, leaving open whether its quality preferences inject modern distributional biases into the instruction-tuning data. Bottom Line This is a solid infrastructure contribution — the corpus, benchmark, and lexically grounded instruction-tuning method will be useful to the History LM community — but the absence of ablations on the 97.7% single-source corpus concentration and the asymmetric BPB evaluation leave its central empirical claims less tightly supported than they appear, and practitioners needing strong factual recall from a History LM should note that Talkie-1930 at 13B still outperforms on that dimension by a wide margin.
Recommended 2606.02991 PDF
#140 — Moderately Innovative (57)

LLM-Assisted Reranking to Operationalize Nuanced Objectives in Recommender Systems

A Ghasemian, H Hosseinmardi, U Dutta, DJ Watts · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper empirically demonstrates that zero-shot LLM-assisted reranking of YouTube political news recommendations amplifies exposure to ideologically extreme and conspiratorial content, and shows that lightweight prompt-level regularization reduces this amplification at modest relevance cost. - Why it's important: LLM rerankers are being adopted by platforms without empirical characterization of their societal harms; in politically sensitive domains, unchecked personalization can systematically surface conspiracy and extremist material to users who have consumed it before. - How it does it: The paper layers GPT-4o-mini as a zero-shot reranker over YouTube sidebar candidates, comparing a naive personalization-only prompt (bLLM+YT) against a constraint-injected variant (rLLM+YT) that instructs the model to diversify ideology and demote conspiratorial content, evaluated on 9,848 real political news sessions from 97 U.S. users. - The good: Naive LLM reranking raises rank-weighted problematic content at top-5 from 0.18 (YouTube baseline) to 0.31 (Cohen's d=0.48, p≈0), while the regularized prompt cuts it to 0.09 (d=−0.44), with all comparisons backed by paired t-tests across multiple top-k thresholds. - The missing: The problematic-content classifier driving the key outcome measure has only 65% precision and 63% recall on 300 manually annotated videos, introducing substantial noise into the paper's central safety claim; additionally, only one prompt design per condition is tested, leaving sensitivity to prompt wording unevaluated. - Read it or not: RecSys researchers and AI platform-safety practitioners should read this — it is the first empirically grounded study of partisan and problematic-content amplification by LLM reranking on real YouTube data and produces directly actionable prompt-design conclusions; ML generalists can safely skip it.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (62) and Non-Obviousness (60) drive the score — the first empirically grounded study of LLM-reranking amplifying partisan/conspiratorial content on real replayed YouTube sessions, with the counter-intuitive finding that embedding-based reranking amplifies more than the LLM, is a genuine contribution with large, replicated effect sizes. Rigor (52) and Resilience (52) are the limiters: the problematic-content classifier driving the headline outcome has only 65% precision / 63% recall and that noise is not uncertainty-quantified, the proxy-click metric reuses the same embedding model as emb+YT (circularity), and only one prompt per condition is tested, leaving wording-sensitivity open.
empirical Novelty 62 Rigor 52 Non-Obv 60 Completeness 62 Resilience 52 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Recommender systems on platforms like YouTube are optimized for engagement signals (clicks, watch time), with essentially no operational mechanism for suppressing content that is ideologically extreme, conspiratorial, or socially harmful. The emergence of LLM-based reranking layers introduces a powerful new personalization capability that, because it operates on language, can exploit the well-documented correlation between writing style and political ideology — potentially amplifying exposure to problematic political content beyond what engagement-driven baselines already produce. Prior work documents structural vulnerabilities in YouTube's recommendation pipeline and calls for socially aware ranking, but these calls remain largely conceptual; no study had empirically characterized whether LLM reranking in a real news-consumption setting increases or decreases exposure to ideologically extreme or conspiratorial material, or whether prompt-level safeguards can mitigate such effects. Key Idea The core insight is that LLMs personalize by matching statistical regularities in language — framing, vocabulary, rhetorical style — between user history summaries and candidate content summaries, and since political ideology is deeply encoded in language, naive personalization latently amplifies ideological alignment including alignment with conspiratorial and extremist material. The flip side is constructive: because the reranker operates on natural language instructions, a single sentence added to the prompt instructing it to diversify ideology and demote problematic content reshapes the same statistical matching behavior toward safer outcomes, without model retraining. Methodology Data comes from Nielsen's desktop panel: 97 U.S. adults' YouTube viewing histories (Oct 2021–Dec 2022) replayed by automated counterfactual bots that record the top-30 sidebar recommendations after each viewed video. Trajectories are segmented into 9,848 overlapping 10-video sessions (stride 1), retaining only those with ≥6 political/society videos in history and ≥5 in candidates, yielding a benchmark with 165,675 raw sessions filtered to 9,848. The paper compares four conditions: (1) YouTube baseline (YT), (2) recency-weighted embedding cosine similarity reranking using OpenAI text-embedding-3-large (emb+YT), (3) zero-shot GPT-4o-mini reranking for personalization (bLLM+YT), and (4) same with added constraints for ideological diversity and problematic content demotion (rLLM+YT). All videos are represented as ≤150-word transcript summaries. Metrics are: session-level AUC for proxy-click prediction (nearest-embedding subsequent watch), topical relevance (recency-weighted dominant-topic overlap using exponential decay d=0.9), ideological alignment (LLM-scored 0–100 scale validated at r=0.92 to human annotations), rank-weighted problematic content exposure (exponential decay over rank), and ideological diversity entropy. Statistical tests are paired t-tests with Cohen's d and 90% CIs. Two synthetic experiments use constructed sessions with controlled partisan/topic compositions to distinguish whether LLMs rerank by topic or partisan signal. The problematic content outcome is annotated by 3 research assistants on 300 videos (65% precision, 63% recall) and then LLM-classified at scale. Three critical design decisions: (1) Proxy-click via nearest embedding is unavoidable given the dataset but is circular with emb+YT's scoring model, likely inflating emb+YT's AUC. (2) A single baseline and single regularized prompt are compared — sensitivity to wording is not tested. (3) Focus on right-leaning sessions is justified by statistical power (4,811 right vs. 483 left-left), but it introduces an asymmetry in scope. Key Results Personalization AUC: emb+YT = 0.75±0.27 (median 0.86), bLLM+YT = 0.59±0.31 (median 0.65), YT = 0.49±0.30, rLLM+YT = 0.51±0.30. The embedding method substantially outperforms all LLM variants. Naive LLM reranking raises problematic content AUC from 0.48 (YT) to 0.59 (bLLM+YT), and the regularized variant drops it to 0.42 — below the platform baseline. Rank-weighted problematic content at top-5: YT = 0.18, bLLM+YT = 0.31 (d=0.48), rLLM+YT = 0.09 (d=−0.44). The amplification effect is consistent across all top-k thresholds (k=5 through k=30). Correlation between history problematic weight and recommendation problematic weight: YT r=0.31, bLLM+YT r=0.34 (both p≈0), rLLM+YT r=0.30. Ideological diversity entropy at k=10: rLLM+YT achieves 2.23 bits (right sessions) vs. YT 2.22, bLLM+YT 2.20, emb+YT 2.18, demonstrating modest diversification. Topical relevance cost from regularization is small. Synthetic experiments show that bLLM+YT prioritizes topic over partisanship for focused topics (abortion, immigration) but shifts to partisan alignment for the more diffuse topic of elections, indicating the behavior is topic-dependent and driven by statistical co-occurrence rather than semantic ideology. Validated on 1,447 right-leaning MIND sessions (AUC = 0.58±0.30) and 533 right-leaning sessions via RecPrompt, both qualitatively consistent with main findings. A notable counter-intuitive finding: embedding-based reranking (emb+YT) produces more problematic content amplification (AUC = 0.64) than LLM reranking (bLLM+YT AUC = 0.59), suggesting ideological amplification is not an LLM-specific failure mode but a consequence of content-similarity matching in any representation space. Novelty Classification NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK. The empirical characterization of how LLM reranking amplifies partisan and problematic-content exposure in real political news sessions, and that prompt-level regularization can counteract this, is a genuine contribution. The benchmark dataset of 9,848 political news sessions with ideology and problematic-content labels is a reusable resource. The zero-shot reranking pipeline itself is a direct application of known techniques (LLMs as zero-shot rankers, instruction-following reranking); the novelty lies in the measurement and its domain, not the mechanism. Rigor & Reproducibility Paired t-tests with Cohen's d are reported throughout, and effect sizes are consistent across multiple top-k thresholds, providing credible robustness. Ideological scoring is validated at r=0.92 to human annotations. However, the problematic content classifier — the primary outcome variable — achieves only 65% precision and 63% recall on 300 manually annotated videos, meaning roughly 1 in 3 positive labels are false positives; this noise propagates into AUC and rank-weighted metrics and is not uncertainty-quantified in the results. The proxy-click construction using the same OpenAI embedding model used in emb+YT creates circularity that artificially advantages emb+YT's click-prediction AUC. No ablation of prompt wording is presented — a key open question is whether the specific text of the regularized prompt drives the result or whether the effect is robust to paraphrasing. The paper states the benchmark dataset is released; explicit code or model-weight release is not mentioned. Data dates from 2021–2022, before major YouTube algorithm updates and the public deployment of commercial LLM reranking, limiting temporal generalizability. This is a preprint (arXiv:2606.02883v1); peer-reviewed results warrant higher confidence. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL - Practical: The approach requires only API access to an LLM and ≤150-word transcript summaries per video; it can run on commercially available infrastructure, though the paper does not report per-session API cost or latency. - Efficient: One GPT-4o-mini call per session to rerank 30 candidates — computationally lightweight compared to fine-tuning alternatives, but API cost scales linearly with traffic volume in a way the paper does not characterize; emb+YT is cheaper and achieves higher click AUC. - Effective: The regularized variant reduces top-5 rank-weighted problematic content from 0.18 to 0.09 and keeps click-prediction AUC on par with the YouTube baseline (0.51 vs. 0.49), supporting the claim that safety and personalization are not in fundamental tension at this scale. - Scalable: Tested on 9,848 sessions from 97 users, which is a modest academic-scale evaluation; no experiment approaches platform-scale volumes, and whether prompt regularization generalizes to a wider range of political contexts, languages, or non-YouTube platforms is untested. The paper delivers a benchmark dataset; no software library or deployed tool is described. Strengths - Real-world ecological validity: using actual Nielsen panel data replayed by counterfactual bots grounds the exposure estimates in real user behavior rather than synthetic models, making the findings more credible than purely simulated studies. - Clean multi-condition experimental design: comparing four conditions (YT, emb+YT, bLLM+YT, rLLM+YT) on the same session pool isolates the contribution of LLM instruction-following over content-similarity matching, identifying that problematic-content amplification is a property of any content-aware reranker, not just LLMs. - Synthetic experiment design: constructing sessions with controlled partisan/topic compositions allows the authors to make mechanistic claims (statistical pattern matching vs. semantic understanding of ideology) that go beyond observational correlation. - Effect sizes are large and consistent: Cohen's d values of 0.47–0.50 for amplification and −0.38 to −0.44 for mitigation, replicated across all top-k thresholds and in two independent dataset validations (MIND, RecPrompt), establish the robustness of the core result. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly confront several limitations: (1) inability to confirm that each session video was actually selected from the sidebar (replay fidelity is approximate); (2) time delay between user behavior and recommendation collection; (3) left and left-left sessions are underrepresented, making those stratum estimates unreliable; (4) LLMs rely on statistical regularities rather than genuine ideological understanding, with mixed behavior under explicit partisan prompting; (5) prolonged engagement and mental-health effects are out of scope. The authors address these credibly and honestly. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The proxy-click metric is constructed using OpenAI text-embedding-3-large — the same model used for emb+YT ranking — creating a circularity that likely inflates emb+YT's reported click-prediction AUC (0.75 vs. 0.59 for bLLM+YT); the paper does not acknowledge this confound. - Only one prompt design per condition is tested; the paper makes no claim about how robust the regularization benefit is to rephrasing, temperature variation, or alternative safety-instruction wording, leaving a significant reproducibility question open. - No rule-based content-filtering baseline is included: rLLM+YT is not compared against simply demoting the 65%-precision-classified problematic items directly without LLM reasoning, which would establish whether the LLM's semantic capability adds value over a simpler filter. - The 97-user sample is not described demographically beyond ideological strata — age, media literacy, and prior exposure distributions are unknown, limiting the external validity of the exposure effects. - The paper does not test whether rLLM+YT's gains are stable over a longer recommendation horizon (multiple sessions), where the demotion of problematic content might shift user behavior and history composition in ways that either amplify or attenuate the benefit. Bottom Line This is a timely, empirically grounded study whose core finding — that naive LLM reranking amplifies problematic political content exposure and that prompt-level regularization mitigates it — is directly actionable for platform designers, though its narrow user base, a noisy outcome classifier, and a single-prompt design leave meaningful uncertainty about the magnitude and generalizability of the effect.
Recommended 2606.02883 PDF
#141 — Moderately Innovative (56)

STAGE-Claw: Automated State-based Agent Benchmarking for Realistic Scenarios

S Liang, B Yu, P Wang, S Guo, W Hu, P Cao, J Zhao… · arxiv.org · automated generated environments for agent testing - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces STAGE-Claw, a four-stage automated framework that constructs and evaluates personal-agent benchmarks by verifying persistent system-state changes (calendar events, reminders, notes, email, files) rather than textual outputs, and applies it to build a 40-task benchmark evaluated across 11 frontier LLMs. - Why it's important: Existing agent benchmarks replace real application state with sandboxed artifacts and score only final text outputs, masking entire classes of tool-execution failures and systematically overstating agent capability. - How it does it: A benchmark-authoring LLM generates task instances from keyword hints, an independent validation agent checks reproducibility and difficulty, evaluated agents execute in real macOS environments via the OpenClaw framework, and task-specific executable verifiers score persistent tool-state snapshots against hidden ground truth. - The good: A controlled state-based vs. virtual-evaluation comparison shows that output-only scoring inflates agent scores by 4.83–6.93 points for two representative models, with the gap traced to specific failure categories (execution failures and real-state gaps) that text-only checkers cannot detect. - The missing: Only 40 tasks, each evaluated once per model with no significance tests and a limited repeated-run diagnostic deferred to an appendix, making it impossible to judge whether single-run scores are reliable; additionally, the benchmark-authoring agent (Claude-Sonnet-4.6) is also one of the evaluated models, a conflict the paper does not acknowledge. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers designing agent evaluation frameworks and practitioners selecting LLMs for agentic workflows — the per-model cost/performance/failure breakdown is immediately actionable, though the small scale means results are indicative rather than definitive.
Why this rank: Systems profile (built state-based benchmarking framework on real macOS). The state-vs-virtual comparison is the strongest element — Non-Obviousness (60) and the empirical finding are solid — but Rigor (45) is pulled down hard by single-run scoring, no significance tests, no framework ablations, and an unacknowledged confound (the authoring model Claude-Sonnet-4.6 is itself evaluated). Practical Impact (55) is capped by macOS-only lock-in, illegible code link, and the explicit 'pilot, not leaderboard' scale of 40 tasks. Weighted composite: 0.15*65+0.15*45+0.10*60+0.15*60+0.15*50+0.30*55 ≈ 56.
systems Novelty 65 Rigor 45 Non-Obv 60 Completeness 60 Resilience 50 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Personal LLM agents that coordinate email, calendars, file systems, and browsers are increasingly deployed, but evaluation lags in three ways. First, most benchmarks replace real application state with synthetic inboxes stored as text files or .ics artifacts, so evaluation measures artifact generation rather than the agent's ability to correctly interact with live applications; permission errors, tool-access failures, and OS-level side effects are invisible. Second, tasks are manually curated, which is expensive and limits coverage to a fixed scenario distribution that cannot adapt to diverse user preferences. Third, existing checkers score only final outputs, preventing localization of errors within multi-step workflows — a miscreated calendar event could stem from timezone conversion failure, entity-alignment error, or source-reconciliation failure, all of which look identical to a final-state checker. These gaps motivate a state-based, automatically generated, diagnostically rich evaluation paradigm. Key Idea The core insight is that personal-agent correctness is a property of persistent environment state, not text output. Each benchmark instance is formalized as a state-transformation tuple B = (q, E₀, G, R, V): the agent sees only the task prompt q and initial environment E₀; the target state G, scoring rubric R, and executable verifier V are hidden. Success is defined as the agent's final environment state sH ∈ SG (the acceptable target-state set), evaluated by running V against environment snapshots. This reframes evaluation from "did the agent write the right text?" to "did the agent produce the required durable changes across real tools?" Methodology STAGE-Claw is a four-stage pipeline. Stage 1 (Benchmark Authoring): given a task-hint keyword, a benchmark-authoring agent (Claude-Sonnet-4.6) explores realistic user scenarios, selects one with sufficient complexity, and outputs a task prompt, environment-construction guide, hidden ground truth, and executable verifier. Tasks must satisfy six criteria: multi-step (≥3 distinct operations), cross-tool (≥2 tools or information sources), reproducible, blind to oracle information, objectively gradable without human judgment, and time-independent. Stage 2 (Validation): an independent validation agent assesses four properties — Structure, Reproducibility (environment rebuilt twice from clean state, snapshots compared across file, calendar, reminder, note, and email state), Verifiability, and Difficulty calibration (checking for cross-source conflicts, hidden dependencies, noisy data, entity alignment, tool-state updates, cross-tool consistency) — producing a 100-point weighted checker score; instances below 80 are returned for repair and resubmitted (average 2.67 iterations). Stage 3 (Agent Execution): each model runs in an isolated macOS OS environment under OpenClaw with reasoning disabled and temperature 0; executions are serial to avoid inter-task tool-state conflicts; execution metadata and final environment snapshots are recorded. Stage 4 (State-Based Evaluation): task-specific verifier programs check file outputs (directory traversal + content hashing), calendar events (icalbuddy with 30-day window), reminders and notes (AppleScript queries), and email state against hidden ground truth; LLM-assisted adjudication is a fallback only when the verifier fails to execute or times out due to evaluator-side tool instability. Three critical design decisions: using real macOS application APIs rather than simulated state — well-justified by the paper's thesis and validated by the state-vs-virtual experiment; hiding G, R, V from the agent — standard and correct; using a weighted threshold admission system — only partially justified, as the weighting scheme for the four validation properties is described at a high level without derivation. Key Results Across 40 tasks and 11 frontier models (all 2026 releases): Claude-Opus-4.7 leads with 77.1 average score and 80.0% first-pass rate (proportion of tasks scoring >60 on first run), at $6.55 per task. Claude-Sonnet-4.6 scores 69.43 at $3.98. GPT-5.5 scores 69.19 at $1.33, offering strong cost-performance. GPT-5.4 achieves 65.37 with the lowest latency (192.5s), lowest token consumption (0.236M), and fewest tool calls (21.8) at $0.69. MiniMax-M2.7 is the weakest performer at 47.53 and 35.0% first-pass. API unit price correlates positively with task score (r=0.80, R²=0.65), but not absolutely: GLM-5 achieves 67.40 at a low price, while GPT-5.5 underperforms relative to cost (possibly due to reasoning modules being disabled). Tool-call frequency does not predict performance: Qwen3.5-Plus makes 59.3 tool calls and scores 59.74 vs. GPT-5.4's 21.8 calls and 65.37 score. Failure analysis over non-passing runs (multi-label): Tool Failure 95.4%, Invalid Format 75.0%, Trap and Reconciliation Failure 69.7%, Verification Failure 59.2%, Information Coverage Error 52.6%, Incomplete Execution 49.3%, Spurious Output 44.1%. State-based vs. virtual-state evaluation on two models: virtual scoring inflates DeepSeek-V4-Pro by +6.93 and Qwen3.5-Plus by +4.83, with the largest gaps driven by execution failures (7 tasks, avg +76.57) and real-state gaps (13 tasks, avg +14.05). Memory perturbation over 4 tasks with Qwen3.5-Plus: all three perturbation types reduce performance; Misleading and Conflict perturbations cause the largest drops (e.g., AI Conference Tracker drops from 77.0 to 23.0 under any perturbation). All models run once per task; limited 3-run diagnostic covers only 3 models in an appendix. Novelty Classification NEW BENCHMARK/DATASET + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The formal state-transformation framing, the automated four-stage construction-validation pipeline, and the real-tool snapshot evaluator are a genuine integration not seen in prior work. The state-based vs. virtual evaluation comparison is a new empirical finding that quantifies benchmark validity. Individual components — LLM-generated benchmarks, environment snapshots, multi-tool evaluation — are known; the novelty is their integration into a self-validating, reproducible pipeline with executable verifiers over real application APIs. Rigor & Reproducibility No ablations over framework components are presented (e.g., whether the validation stage materially improves task quality, how results change with vs. without the repair loop). No error bars, confidence intervals, or significance tests are reported for the main leaderboard (Table 2); each model-task pair is run once. The repeated-run diagnostic (3 models × 3 runs) is deferred to Appendix A.6 without summary statistics in the main text. Human annotation (3 annotators) validated task quality with 87.0% retention (40/46 candidates); inter-annotator agreement is not reported. Code is stated as available via a hyperlink in the abstract, but no URL is legible in the paper. A methodological concern: the benchmark-authoring agent is Claude-Sonnet-4.6, which is also one of the evaluated models; if authoring implicitly shapes task structure toward Claude-style tool-use patterns, Claude-Sonnet-4.6's score may benefit — the paper does not acknowledge this confound. LLM-assisted adjudication fallback frequency and accuracy are not reported. Deployability (EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS) Profile: SYSTEMS — a built framework instantiated on real macOS infrastructure and evaluated by deployment. - Practical: Requires macOS with Apple Calendar, Reminders, Notes, and Mail accessible via AppleScript and icalbuddy; not portable to Linux or Windows without substantial re-engineering of both the environment setup and the snapshot tools. - Efficient: Task construction costs $35–$40 per accepted task and 1–2 hours (automated, excluding model evaluation); model evaluation costs $0.17–$6.55 per model-task pair and 3–15 minutes — high but disclosed and benchmarked. - Effective: The state-based vs. virtual comparison on 2 models across 40 tasks shows that the framework exposes failures concealed by output-only evaluation; three qualitative case studies (Appendix B) confirm the diagnostic value. Whether the 40-task sample is representative of the personal-agent task space is not established. - Scalable: The paper explicitly positions this as a high-quality pilot benchmark, not exhaustive coverage; per-task cost ($35–40) and macOS platform lock-in are significant barriers to scaling to thousands of tasks. Automated construction in principle supports expansion, but the practical ceiling is cost-driven rather than technically demonstrated. The work delivers a framework (code referenced) and a 40-task benchmark. No trained evaluator weights, datasets in a standard format, or detailed per-task ground truth are released in the paper. Strengths - The state-based vs. virtual-evaluation experiment is the paper's most rigorous contribution: it provides a controlled, quantified demonstration that output-only evaluation overestimates agent capability by tracing score inflation to specific failure categories, not just asserting the problem. - The four-property validation stage with automated LLM-driven repair loops (avg 2.67 iterations, 87.0% retention) is a principled quality-control mechanism that goes beyond standard manual curation and is itself an operationalizable contribution. - The failure taxonomy (7 multi-label categories, decomposed across 11 models and 3 tool-category subcategories) offers finer diagnostic granularity than any existing personal-agent benchmark, and the finding that tool-call frequency anticorrelates with quality (Qwen3.5-Plus vs. GPT-5.4) is a non-obvious empirical result. - The memory perturbation study opens a direction — evaluating agent resilience to stale or conflicting persistent memory — that existing benchmarks do not address at all. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly identify three limitations: (1) 40 tasks with first-valid-run evaluation yield a "controlled snapshot rather than a statistically exhaustive leaderboard"; (2) high per-task construction cost limits scalability; (3) real-environment execution confounds model capability with OpenClaw framework behavior, OS configuration, permissions, and runtime stability, potentially introducing platform-dependent failures. These are stated clearly and honestly, and the authors do not overstate the generalizability of the benchmark-wide conclusions. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The benchmark-authoring agent is Claude-Sonnet-4.6, the same model evaluated in Table 2. If the authoring agent's implicit task-design choices (e.g., favoring certain tool sequences, output formats, or reasoning styles) favor Claude-family models, the evaluation is not fully blind — this confound is unacknowledged. - The 40 task-hint keywords were manually selected by the authors; the coverage, diversity, and representativeness of the hint set relative to actual personal-agent use cases are not characterized or independently validated. - The LLM-assisted adjudication fallback frequency is unreported; if it fires in more than a handful of tasks, it reintroduces subjective scoring and partially invalidates the "executable verification" claim. - All evaluations run through OpenClaw; no ablation over agent frameworks is provided, so it is unclear how much performance variance is attributable to the underlying LLM vs. the agent harness. - The memory perturbation study covers 4 tasks and 1 model (Qwen3.5-Plus), far too small to generalize the finding that memory quality affects downstream state updates — it is suggestive, not evidential. - The repeated-run variability data (3 models, 3 runs) is not summarized numerically in the main text, making it impossible to assess whether single-run leaderboard positions are stable or noise-driven at the observed score differences (e.g., Sonnet-4.6 at 69.43 vs. GPT-5.5 at 69.19). Bottom Line Practitioners evaluating or building personal-agent systems gain real, concrete value from this paper — the state-vs-virtual comparison makes a quantified case that existing benchmarks overstate agent capability, and the per-model cost/performance/failure breakdown is immediately usable for model selection — but the 40-task scale, single-run protocol, macOS-only platform dependency, and unacknowledged authoring-model conflict mean the results should be treated as a well-motivated pilot rather than a definitive leaderboard.
Search Results 2606.10394 PDF
#142 — Moderately Innovative (56)

KForge: LLM -Driven Cross-Platform Kernel Generation for AI Accelerators

T Sereda, B Bartan, A Nayak, TS John, N Serrino… · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces KForge, a two-agent LLM framework that autonomously generates and optimizes compute kernels across 4 hardware vendors and 6 programming models via iterative functional-correctness and performance-optimization passes. - Why it's important: Heterogeneous AI inference deployments require high-performance kernels on each accelerator in the pipeline, and writing or porting these kernels manually is time-intensive, demands scarce low-level expertise, and does not scale as accelerator diversity grows. - How it does it: A generation agent refines candidates using compilation and correctness feedback while a separate performance-analysis agent distills profiling data—including GUI profiler screenshots—into a single focused optimization recommendation that seeds the next synthesis iteration, keeping LLM prompt size short enough to avoid long-context retrieval degradation. - The good: On Intel Arc B580—a platform with no hand-tuned baselines—KForge achieves 5.13× geometric mean speedup over the faster of torch.compile and eager-mode PyTorch across 37 KernelBench Level 2 GEMM+tail-ops problems, primarily via operator fusion and FP16 mixed-precision. - The missing: On NVIDIA B200 only 3 kernels are optimized (selected by profiling from a top-10 list), the autotuner must be disabled to obtain reproducible baselines, and no ablations compare the two-agent design against a single-agent alternative or quantify the contribution of profiling feedback. - Read it or not: Worth reading for ML systems engineers targeting emerging non-NVIDIA accelerators; for NVIDIA-focused practitioners there are likely stronger alternatives (CUDA-LLM, KernelBlaster) with more thorough evaluation.
Why this rank: Systems profile. Impact (55) and Novelty (65) anchor a moderate score: the dual-agent decomposition with a dedicated profiling-interpretation agent is a real structural contribution, and the cross-vendor Intel Arc bring-up (5.13× geomean, no hand-tuned baselines) plus end-to-end TensorRT-LLM validation fill genuine gaps. Rigor (50) is mixed — the B200 evaluation is disciplined (7 runs, CV<0.1%, clock pinning) but there are no ablations of the two-agent design or profiling feedback, no error bars on Intel results, the autotuner-disabled baseline departs from production config, and it is a workshop paper with closed code. Resilience (52) is weakened by the narrow 3-kernel B200 selection and unevaluated AMD/Apple support; generation cost is entirely unreported. Composite ≈ 0.15·65 + 0.15·50 + 0.10·62 + 0.15·58 + 0.15·52 + 0.30·55 = 56.
systems Novelty 65 Rigor 50 Non-Obv 62 Completeness 58 Resilience 52 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Production AI inference increasingly runs across heterogeneous accelerators—NVIDIA, AMD, Intel, Apple—with each pipeline stage assigned to the hardware best suited to its compute and memory profile. Each accelerator exposes a distinct programming model (CUDA, HIP, Triton, SYCL, Metal) and a distinct memory hierarchy, so porting a kernel is never a syntactic translation: the algorithmically optimal implementation differs by platform. Writing and tuning these kernels by hand requires deep specialist expertise that is scarce and expensive. Existing LLM-based kernel generation work focuses almost exclusively on NVIDIA/CUDA where training data is abundant, and evaluates per-kernel speedups rather than end-to-end impact inside production runtimes where graph fusion, autotuning, and surrounding kernel selection interact with the kernel under study. Key Idea The central insight is to separate the generation problem into two specialized agents with short, focused prompts rather than one agent handling both correctness and performance. A dedicated performance-analysis agent compresses raw profiling data (text metrics and GUI profiler screenshots) into a single optimization recommendation before it reaches the generation agent. This decomposition is explicitly motivated by evidence that LLM retrieval accuracy on relevant information drops to 50% at 32K-token inputs versus near-perfect at under 1K tokens—keeping each agent's effective prompt short enough for reliable reasoning. Methodology KForge alternates between two pass types. In functional passes, the generation agent (F: p → k) receives a Jinja2-templated prompt containing a task description, a one-shot example pairing a PyTorch reference with a target-platform implementation, and feedback from the previous iteration. The agent outputs a complete PyTorch-compatible NewModel class wrapping the kernel. Execution is classified into five states (generation failure, compilation failure, runtime error, numerical/shape mismatch, correct), and the relevant error feeds the next iteration. Once a correct kernel is obtained, optimization passes begin: the performance-analysis agent (G: (o, k, {v₀,...,vₙ}) → r) ingests both programmatic profiler output (e.g., nsys CUDA kernel summary) and visual profiler screenshots, emits a single concrete recommendation r_t, and that recommendation feeds the next synthesis call (F: (p, k_{t-1}, r_{t-1}) → k_t). Three synthesis strategies compose: iterative refinement, cross-platform translation (using a working implementation on one backend as reference for another), and profiling feedback. Critical design decisions: (1) two-agent separation is well-justified by long-context degradation evidence; (2) candidate guardrails (banned code patterns such as datatype downcasting and fast-math flags) prevent unsafe optimizations—reasonable but not ablated; (3) the underlying LLM is Claude Opus 4.6 in high-effort mode—not compared against alternatives. Key Results NVIDIA B200 (three kernels selected from top-10 nsys profile of gpt-oss-20b MoE decode): Fused Add+RMSNorm achieves 1.10–1.13× speedup across batch sizes 1–128; MoE finalize achieves 1.06–1.43× with the largest gains at batch size 128; Bias+RoPE+KV update is essentially flat (0.99–1.05×). End-to-end over TensorRT-LLM v1.3.0rc9 (7 independent runs, CV < 0.1%): throughput improves by 2.12% (2601.55 → 2656.61 tok/s), wall-clock time decreases by 2.07%. Reproducibility required disabling TensorRT-LLM's autotuner and pinning GPU clock to 1500 MHz. Intel Arc B580 (37 GEMM+tail-ops problems from KernelBench Level 2, 5 generate-refine iterations per problem): 5.13× geometric mean speedup over the faster of torch.compile and eager PyTorch; four representative examples show 4.1–6.5× speedups. The Intel results come from operator fusion, FP16 matmul with FP32 accumulation on XMX units, single-pass streaming reductions, and shape-tuned partitioning. AMD and Apple hardware are mentioned as supported but receive no evaluation. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The dual-agent architecture with a dedicated profiling-interpretation agent is a genuine structural contribution not present in prior LLM kernel generation work; the cross-vendor evaluation beyond NVIDIA (Intel Arc with no hand-tuned baselines) and end-to-end validation inside TensorRT-LLM fill explicit gaps in prior benchmarks. Individual components—iterative LLM refinement, compilation feedback loops, profiling-guided optimization—appear in CUDA-LLM and KernelBlaster; the novelty lies in their composition into a cross-platform system and in the Intel Arc bring-up use case. Rigor & Reproducibility The B200 evaluation is methodologically careful: 7 independent runs, CV < 0.1%, explicit clock pinning and autotuner disabling to eliminate nondeterminism. The Intel Arc results report best kernel across 5 iterations—it is not stated how often iterations fail to improve or regress vs. baseline, nor is a failure rate reported. No ablations are present: the two-agent design is never compared to a single-agent baseline, profiling feedback is not ablated against without-feedback, and the number of refinement iterations is not swept. No error bars are reported for Intel results. The framework is from Gimlet Labs Inc. and no code or model weights are publicly released. This is a workshop paper (MLArchSys at ISCA 2026), not a full peer-reviewed venue—results should be weighted accordingly. The autotuner-disabled B200 baseline is a methodological concern: production TensorRT-LLM deployments use the autotuner, so the 2.12% gain may not hold in standard deployment configuration. Deployability (SYSTEMS profile) - Practical: Targets commercially available hardware (NVIDIA B200, Intel Arc B580) and runs standard programming models; practical for any organization with access to these GPUs. - Efficient: The cost of the KForge optimization loop—number of LLM calls, wall-clock time for the generation process, and Claude Opus 4.6 API cost—is never reported, making it impossible to assess amortization vs. hand-tuning. - Effective: Demonstrates 2.12% end-to-end gain on B200 against TensorRT-LLM (though under a modified deployment configuration) and 5.13× geometric mean on B580; effectiveness on B580 is strong, on B200 limited to three non-proprietary kernels. - Scalable: AMD and Apple hardware receive no evaluation despite being listed as supported; joint optimization across multiple interacting kernels is acknowledged as future work; behavior at larger model scales (e.g., 70B+ parameter models) is untested. No software artifact is publicly released. Strengths - Cross-vendor reach: the only evaluated system in this paper's reference set that generates and benchmarks kernels on Intel Arc where no hand-tuned baselines exist, filling a practical bring-up gap. - End-to-end TensorRT-LLM evaluation rather than isolated kernel benchmarks—directly addresses the gap the authors identify in related work (KernelBench measures per-kernel speedup, not full-model impact). - Two-agent decomposition with an explicit, empirically grounded justification (long-context LLM retrieval degradation) rather than ad-hoc architecture choices. - Reproducibility discipline on B200: clock pinning, autotuner stabilization, 7-run statistics, and CV reporting make the B200 numbers trustworthy within the stated experimental setup. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly acknowledge four limitations: (1) the framework requires a source-level reference implementation to seed the loop; many production kernels (fused attention cubins, vendor BLAS) ship without source; (2) PyTorch is the only source framework (JAX not supported); (3) isolated kernel speedups do not always translate to end-to-end gains because faster kernels can shift register/shared-memory pressure or perturb autotuner selections for neighboring kernels—they plan joint block-level optimization as future work; (4) more rigorous verification approaches (differential testing, formal equivalence checking) would increase confidence in generated kernels. These are confronted directly, which adds credibility. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The three B200 kernels were selected because they had available source code, not because they represent the best optimization opportunities; the seven proprietary cubin kernels (attention, GEMM-with-activation fusions) that likely dominate runtime are inaccessible, making the 2.12% gain a lower bound on what's achievable but also a ceiling on what this approach can currently address. - The autotuner-disabled baseline changes the deployment scenario in ways that may favor KForge unfairly: disabling autotuning freezes the surrounding kernel selection, eliminating dynamic adaptation that could partially compensate for suboptimal kernels in the baseline. - AMD (HIP/Triton) and Apple (Metal) are listed as supported but receive zero evaluation—readers cannot assess whether cross-platform claims beyond NVIDIA/Intel hold. - KForge generation cost (LLM calls, wall-clock optimization time) is entirely absent, making TCO comparison against hand-tuning impossible. - The Intel Arc B580 comparison uses "the best generated kernel across 5 iterations"—there is no reporting of success rate, failure modes, or distribution of outcomes across the 37 problems. - No comparison with Autocomp [8], the closest prior work targeting non-NVIDIA tensor accelerators, despite being cited in related work. Bottom Line KForge makes a credible case that dual-agent LLM-driven synthesis can produce meaningful end-to-end gains against a vendor-optimized runtime and serve as a practical bring-up tool for emerging hardware, but the narrow kernel selection on B200, the closed codebase, the modified deployment configuration, and the absence of ablations leave the work in the category of promising early evidence rather than a definitive systems contribution.
Search Results 2606.02963 PDF
#143 — Moderately Innovative (56)

EurekAgent: Agent Environment Engineering is All You Need For Autonomous Scientific Discovery (18 ▲ )

Amy Xin, Jiening Siow, Junjie Wang, Zijun Yao, Fanjin Zhang, Jian Song, Lei Hou, Juanzi Li · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 12 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces EUREKAGENT, an autonomous scientific discovery system that achieves new state-of-the-art results on mathematics, kernel engineering, and machine learning tasks by engineering the execution environment of off-the-shelf CLI agents rather than prescribing agent workflows. - Why it's important: LLM agents capable of autonomous research already exist, but they routinely fail through reward hacking, evaluation contamination, and artifact manipulation — reliability problems that detailed workflow engineering cannot prevent and that block real scientific adoption. - How it does it: EUREKAGENT wraps general-purpose CLI agents (Claude Code + GLM-5.1) in a Docker-sandboxed Prepare→Propose→Implement loop with four engineered layers: permission boundaries (hidden evaluator, same-round isolation, GPU locking), Git-backed artifact memory, budget controls (wall-clock + API cost limits with resume), and a human oversight UI. - The good: EUREKAGENT achieves SOTA on all five evaluated tasks while remaining training-free and using an open-source LLM, outperforming prior test-time-training systems — the TriMul kernel result (2005 µs vs. 2247 µs for TTT-Discover, a 10.8% improvement) is the most substantial margin. - The missing: No ablation isolates the contribution of any individual environment engineering dimension, so the causal claim that environment design drives performance is undemonstrated; the MLE-Bench evaluation covers only 7 self-selected tasks from the 22-task Lite split. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building autonomous research agent infrastructure, but treat the performance claims cautiously — the math improvements are at the 5th–6th decimal place in single runs with no significance testing.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Impact (70) carries the score — a full open-source system achieving SOTA across five tasks training-free on an open-source LLM, with a substantive and methodologically careful TriMul kernel result (10.8% over TTT-Discover under a controlled re-grade). But Rigor (45) is weak: no ablation isolates any of the four environment dimensions, so the central causal claim that environment engineering drives performance is asserted not measured; the math improvements (5th–6th decimal, single runs, no significance testing) are within numerical noise, and the 7-of-22 self-selected MLE-Bench tasks compared single-run against published multi-run upper bounds is a systematic asymmetry. Completeness (40) and Resilience (45) are further pulled down by a limitations section that reads as future-work agenda rather than candid accounting. Novelty (65) reflects the coherent environment-over-workflow framing. Weighted: 0.15*65 + 0.15*45 + 0.10*60 + 0.15*40 + 0.15*45 + 0.30*70 = 56.
systems Novelty 65 Rigor 45 Non-Obv 60 Completeness 40 Resilience 45 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation LLM-based agents for metric-driven scientific discovery (mathematical optimization, GPU kernel engineering, ML competition engineering) have reached performance competitive with human experts, but suffer from reliability failures that make their results hard to trust: agents contaminate evaluations by inspecting or modifying grader code, manipulate result files, copy peer solutions within a parallel exploration round, or exploit weak procedural constraints. These failure modes are documented in real agentic research systems. Existing systems respond by prescribing detailed workflows — evolutionary selection, solution trees, structured self-review — but as general-purpose coding agents become more capable, there is growing evidence that such workflow constraints are unnecessary: Claude Code and Codex, used as standalone agents, already outperform research-specific systems on ResearchClawBench. The gap is the absence of a principled, task-agnostic environment layer that preserves agent autonomy while suppressing integrity violations and enabling human oversight. Key Idea The core insight is an analogy from Gibson's affordance theory: an environment shapes what actions are possible, and the bottleneck for reliable autonomous scientific discovery is therefore shifting from prescribing agent behavior to designing the environment in which agents act. Giving a capable general-purpose CLI agent the right affordances (Docker isolation, a hidden evaluator exposed only as a scoring API, Git-backed cross-session memory, budget controls) while removing harmful ones (direct evaluator access, same-round peer visibility, uncontrolled GPU acquisition) lets the agent discover SOTA solutions without any task-specific workflow engineering. The environment replaces the workflow prescription. Methodology EUREKAGENT orchestrates a fixed three-stage loop: (1) Prepare — a one-time agent session that reads the problem, tests the hidden evaluator, installs dependencies, and writes a preparation summary; (2) Propose — per round, one agent session reads prior ranked solutions and web sources, then writes up to P hypothesis descriptions; (3) Implement — per round, P parallel agent sessions each independently code, evaluate, and refine a hypothesis, submitting to a hidden grading service. This loop runs for up to R rounds with configurable parallelism. Four environment engineering dimensions govern the loop. Permissions engineering: runs execute inside Docker containers; the evaluator and test data are outside agent-visible workspace and accessed only through a grading service that returns scores but not source; result files are controller-owned with hooks blocking agent writes; same-round parallel sessions cannot inspect each other; GPUs require explicit API lock acquisition. Artifact engineering: the filesystem and Git history serve as shared cross-session memory storing preparation summaries, proposal manifests, solution code, evaluator feedback, scored submissions, and a ranked solution log; web-search results are cached. Budget engineering: wall-clock limits are set separately for propose and implement stages; agents can query remaining time via a helper API; deadline warnings are injected passively; API cost is tracked and triggers run abort on limit; runs are resumable from the latest filesystem state. Human-in-the-loop engineering: a terminal TUI exposes live session outputs and accepts chat input; a web monitor visualizes score evolution, per-round and global best approaches, and budget usage. All experiments use Claude Code as the CLI agent and GLM-5.1 as the base LLM with Web Search Prime MCP and Playwright MCP for internet access. No model fine-tuning or test-time training is performed. Hyperparameters: math tasks R=5–8, P=3, 20 min propose / 120 min implement; TriMul R=13, P=3, 160 min implement; MLE-Bench R=12, P=3, 100 min implement. Key Results Mathematics (three optimization problems): Circle packing (26 circles, maximize sum of radii) — 2.635999 vs. prior AI best 2.635986 (OpenEvolve/AlphaEvolve-style system using R1-Distill-Qwen3-8B) vs. human ~2.634. Erdős' minimum overlap — 0.380870 vs. prior AI 0.380876 (gpt-oss-120b, test-time training). First autocorrelation inequality — 1.502861 vs. prior AI 1.502863 (gpt-oss-120b). All three improvements are at the 5th–6th decimal place; single-run results with no significance testing; the circle packing run cost under $11. Kernel engineering (TriMul, A100 GPU): Best EUREKAGENT kernel (CUDA Graph) achieves 2005.03 µs median vs. top regraded human leaderboard solution at 2096.04 µs (4.3% improvement) and TTT-Discover at 2247.78 µs (10.8% improvement). Four EUREKAGENT solutions all rank above the prior human leaderboard top. Note: the official leaderboard is closed; comparison is via local re-grading on the same A100 with 3 warmup + 10 measured rounds in shuffled order. Machine learning engineering (7-task MLE-Bench Lite subset): 85.71% any-medal rate vs. 71.43% for all five listed baselines (AIBuildAI, Famou-Agent, Famou-Agent 2.0, LoongFlow, CAIR MARS+). 71.43% gold-medal rate, tied with AIBuildAI and CAIR MARS+. 100% above-median rate. Baselines use Claude-Opus-4.6, Gemini-2.5-Pro, or Gemini-3 variants; EUREKAGENT uses open-source GLM-5.1. Baselines are cited from their published papers with upper-end of multi-run ranges reported. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The individual components — Docker sandboxing, hidden evaluators, Git artifact tracking, budget limits, human oversight UIs — are well-established engineering techniques. The genuine novelty is their systematic co-design as an environment engineering layer for autonomous research agents, framed as a principled alternative to workflow prescription, and the empirical demonstration that this approach matches or exceeds workflow-prescribing systems including test-time-training methods on their own benchmarks while remaining training-free. Rigor & Reproducibility No ablation study is presented that isolates the contribution of any individual environment engineering dimension or compares against a Claude Code baseline with no environment engineering. The central causal claim — that environment engineering drives performance — is asserted but not measured. Math improvements are single-run results at the 5th–6th decimal place with no statistical testing; it is impossible to determine whether these represent genuine improvements or random variation in numerical optimization runs. The TriMul evaluation uses a careful local re-grading protocol (warmup rounds, shuffled order, same hardware) but cannot be compared to the official closed leaderboard. The MLE-Bench evaluation covers 7 self-selected tasks from 22 (selection criterion: tractability proxy from prior agent medal rates), and compares a single EUREKAGENT run against published upper-bound multi-run baseline results — a methodological asymmetry favorable to EUREKAGENT. Code is open-sourced at https://github.com/THU-Team-Eureka/EurekAgent. This is an arXiv preprint, not peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS — a built tool evaluated by deployment on research tasks. - Practical: Runs on commercially available hardware (A100 for kernel tasks, standard compute for math/ML); requires Docker, Claude Code CLI, and a GLM-5.1 API endpoint; accessible to research groups with moderate infrastructure. - Efficient: Math tasks cost under $17 total API cost per run; ML tasks consume one GPU per competition for up to 24 hours; kernel tasks run 13 rounds × 3 parallel sessions × 160-minute implement windows, totaling roughly 100+ A100-hours for a single task run — high but not prohibitive for academic labs. - Effective: Achieves SOTA on all five task types evaluated; TriMul improvement is substantial and credible under the re-grading protocol; math improvements are directionally positive but too small to confidently attribute to the system vs. optimization stochasticity. - Scalable: R and P are user-configurable; the architecture supports parallel sessions and run resumability, but the paper does not evaluate behavior at larger R, P, or on harder or longer-horizon open-ended tasks. Delivers a software artifact (open-source system at the linked GitHub repository). Strengths - The environment engineering framing is intellectually coherent: the four dimensions (permissions, artifacts, budget, human oversight) map directly onto documented failure modes in agentic research systems, and the paper grounds the framework in prior incident reports rather than hypothetical concerns. - The most striking result — outperforming test-time-training systems while remaining training-free and using an open-source LLM — challenges the assumption that stronger models or fine-tuning are the primary lever for scientific discovery performance. - The TriMul kernel engineering result (10.8% improvement over TTT-Discover) is substantive and methodologically careful, with a documented re-grading protocol that controls for hardware and ordering effects. - Full open-source release with hyperparameter details in appendices enables the community to reproduce and extend the work. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The conclusion section briefly acknowledges that current experiments are restricted to metric-driven tasks with executable evaluators, and states that environment engineering becomes "even more important" as research moves toward broader, more open-ended settings. The authors commit to extending to richer domains. They do not acknowledge the absence of ablations, the marginal math improvements, the MLE-Bench evaluation design asymmetries, or the impossibility of official TriMul comparison. The limitations section reads more as a future-work agenda than a candid accounting of the current work's boundaries — a credibility negative. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The central causal claim is unsubstantiated: without a baseline running Claude Code in an unengineered environment on the same tasks, the paper cannot demonstrate that environment engineering — rather than Claude Code's raw capability or GLM-5.1's quality — accounts for the results. - MLE-Bench evaluation design is biased: 7 self-selected tasks, EUREKAGENT run once, baselines cited from published upper-bound multi-run scores — a comparison unfavorable to baselines in a systematic way; the full 22-task Lite evaluation with a standardized single-run protocol would be needed for a fair comparison. - The propose stage's utility is untested: a comparison against a simpler multi-restart implement-only loop (no structured hypothesis generation round) is absent, so the value of the propose stage is unknown. - Math improvements are within numerical noise: margins of 0.000013 (circle packing), 0.000006 (Erdős' overlap), and 0.000002 (autocorrelation) in single runs are not distinguishable from stochasticity without repeated experiments. - Same-round isolation is presented as a design benefit for diversity, but no experiment measures whether it actually increases solution diversity or improves final scores compared to a shared-workspace setting. Bottom Line A practitioner building autonomous research agent infrastructure will find the environment engineering framework and open-source system worth studying as a practical architecture for reliable agentic research, but should not accept the paper's performance claims at face value given the absence of ablations, marginal math improvements, and self-selected evaluation design.
Recommended 2606.13662 PDF
#144 — Moderately Innovative (56)

Synthetic APTs: the Collapse of TTP-Based Attribution

F Balassone, V Mayoral-Vilches, M Sanz-Gómez… · arxiv.org · generating environments for agent testing in enterprises - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper demonstrates, through 20 live adversary emulation experiments on professional-grade cyber ranges, that AI agents configured with distinct APT group profiles converge on identical initial-phase attack techniques regardless of assigned persona, undermining TTP-based attribution while achieving 55–80% MITRE ATT&CK precision at later kill chain phases. - Why it's important: Cyber threat intelligence attribution — which underpins sanctions, indictments, and diplomatic responses to nation-state cyber operations — depends on threat actors leaving distinguishable operational fingerprints; if AI agents can plausibly impersonate any documented APT group, false-flag operations become accessible to any actor with model access. - How it does it: Five AI attacker agents (Claude Opus 4.6), each configured with a distinct MITRE ATT&CK group profile prompt, were deployed against AI defender agents across two professional cyber range scenarios, with outcomes measured on operational compromise, ATT&CK technique adherence, and detection stealth. - The good: The emergent, profile-independent Velociraptor C2 weaponization — observed in 8 of 10 Enterprise experiments across all five APT personas — is a genuinely surprising and operationally significant finding that no existing TTP catalog documents. - The missing: All attacker experiments use the same single model (Claude Opus 4.6), so the observed technique convergence could be a property of that specific model's training distribution rather than a universal property of AI-driven operations, a confounder the paper acknowledges but cannot rule out. - Read it or not: Worth reading for CTI analysts and red team practitioners tracking how AI agents change the threat landscape; the specific convergence data and emergent behaviors are novel, but the sample size (20 experiments, 2 scenarios, 1 attacker model) warrants skepticism about the generality of the conclusions.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. This is the cluster's strongest paper on idea: Novelty (70) and Non-Obviousness (75) are high because the profile-independent convergence on early-phase techniques and especially the emergent Velociraptor C2 weaponization (8 of 10 experiments, all five personas) is a surprising, undocumented finding, and the execution-based ATT&CK fidelity metric is a real methodological contribution. Rigor (40) is the main drag — a single attacker model, single proprietary CSI scaffold, only 2 self-selected scenarios, no released code/data, inflated success from pre-seeded credentials, and measurement circularity (the same model classifies its own actions). Completeness (60) benefits from candid limitation acknowledgment, but the buried low recall (4–34% of techniques) tempers it; Resilience (50) reflects that the attribution-collapse thesis rests on thin N=20/one-model ground; Impact (45) is conceptual influence for CTI rather than a deployable artifact. Weighted: 0.20*70 + 0.25*40 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*60 + 0.15*50 + 0.10*45 = 56. A genuinely interesting hypothesis-generating study.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 40 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 60 Resilience 50 Impact 45
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Cyber threat intelligence attribution links intrusions to specific state-sponsored actors by identifying their distinctive TTPs as cataloged in MITRE ATT&CK. Attribution then drives geopolitical responses including sanctions and indictments. This fingerprinting model assumes adversaries maintain consistent, distinguishable toolkits across operations. The proliferation of shared offensive frameworks (Cobalt Strike, Impacket, Metasploit) has already eroded some distinctiveness. The arrival of LLM-driven attack agents that can be prompted with any group's operational doctrine — while drawing from the same underlying toolset — raises the question of whether profile-level differentiation survives at all. No prior work had measured this convergence empirically with profile-specific agents on production-grade ranges against active AI defenders. Key Idea: The core insight is that TTP-based attribution faces a two-sided collapse under AI-driven operations: at early kill chain phases, all AI agents converge on the same reconnaissance and initial access techniques (T1595, T1046, T1110) driven by a shared toolset and cold-start constraint, producing indistinguishable signatures; at later phases, profile-specific behaviors emerge (PtH for APT28, DCSync for APT29, PAM interception for APT41) but only when the network architecture permits sufficient kill chain progression. The MITRE ATT&CK profile thus shifts from being an actor-identifying fingerprint to a behavioral floor that any sufficiently capable AI agent can reproduce. Methodology: The experimental design deploys AI agents from the authors' Cybersecurity SuperIntelligence (CSI) framework across 20 experiments: five APT profiles (APT28, APT29, APT41, APT44, Lazarus) × two cyber range scenarios × two defender model configurations (Claude Opus 4.6 vs. Opus 4.6; Claude Opus 4.6 attacker vs. alias2-mini ~30B-parameter defender). The attacker model is fixed at Claude Opus 4.6 throughout. Each agent receives a base system prompt (~2,500 tokens) defining a TRACE operational loop (Think→Plan→Act→Observe→Decide) plus an APT-specific profile prompt (~3,000–4,000 tokens) encoding the group's MITRE ATT&CK doctrine, TTPs, and example commands; operator messages are identical across all profiles within a scenario. Defenders receive a 30-minute head start for hardening before attacker activation. Scenario A (Enterprise, ~20 hosts, flat topology with Active Directory, Webmin firewalls, Velociraptor/Wazuh SIEM) and Scenario B (Military, ~15 hosts, dual-organization with VLAN isolation and VyOS gateways) are the same ranges used in prior dynamic cyber range work, enabling baseline comparison. Three measurement dimensions are collected: operational outcome (hosts compromised to root/SYSTEM), MITRE ATT&CK adherence (qualitative comparison of observed vs. documented techniques), and stealth (time-to-detection and detection trigger). Post-experiment reports are generated by LLM analysis of raw logs, then human-reviewed. ATT&CK adherence is assessed qualitatively (None/Low/Partial/High/Very High), with quantitative precision ratios computed over range-relevant technique subsets. Critical design decisions: identical operator messages across profiles (so behavioral differences are attributable to profile prompts only); active AI defender in every experiment (enabling stealth measurement); and post-compromise ranges that include pre-existing artifacts. These choices are well-motivated. However, adherence measurement relies partly on the same LLM that ran the experiment to categorize techniques from logs, introducing a potential circularity. Key Results: A binary outcome pattern holds without exception across all 20 experiments: all 10 Scenario A (Enterprise) experiments resulted in attacker compromise (2–12 hosts), while all 10 Scenario B (Military) experiments were defended or stalemates, regardless of APT profile or defender model. On Scenario A, the same-model configuration (Opus vs. Opus) yielded 2–8 hosts compromised per experiment (mean 4.6); the cross-model configuration (Opus attacker vs. alias2-mini defender) yielded 6–12 hosts compromised (mean 9.8). MITRE ATT&CK precision across the 10 Scenario A experiments ranged from 55% (Lazarus: 21/38) to 80% (APT29: 33/41), with 16–41 distinct technique sub-IDs observed per experiment. Scenario B experiments exhibited only 3–24 techniques (4–8% of range-relevant profiles). The single most consistent finding across all 20 experiments was convergent Velociraptor weaponization: in 8 of 10 Scenario A experiments, all five APT profiles independently hijacked the defender's endpoint management platform as a C2 channel via pre-configured credentials. Key emulation gaps absent across all profiles: spearphishing-based initial access, custom malware deployment, encrypted C2 channels, and anti-forensics. The most striking individual result: the Lazarus cross-model experiment achieved total domain compromise (10 hosts) within 84 minutes while the defender never detected the breach. Novelty Classification: NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW METHOD (execution-based ATT&CK fidelity measurement). The empirical demonstration of technique convergence across profile-differentiated agents on production-grade ranges against live defenders is genuinely new — prior work either lacked explicit APT profiles, multi-host professional ranges, or real-time AI defenders. The execution-based fidelity metric (precision/recall against official MITRE group profiles from live campaigns, not text extraction) is a methodological contribution. The specific Velociraptor weaponization finding is a novel undocumented emergent behavior. The broader argument that AI agents erode TTP-based attribution has been theorized but not measured experimentally with this specificity. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are limited: there is no systematic variation of the attacker model, agent scaffold, or profile prompt depth. Error bars, significance tests, and multiple seeds are absent — the binary outcome pattern is compelling given N=20 but 2 scenarios × 1 attacker model constrain inference. The ATT&CK adherence analysis is partially qualitative (ordinal levels assigned without inter-rater reliability) and partially quantitative (precision ratios), with the former dominating the narrative. Reports used for technique cataloging are generated by the same model family as the attacker, creating a measurement circularity not fully resolved by human review. Cross-harness validation is preliminary: only 2 of 5 profiles (APT28, APT29) were tested on an independent scaffold (Decepticon), and full replication is deferred to future work. No code, data, or model weights are released; the CSI framework is a proprietary commercial product. The pre-compromised range environments and deliberate vulnerability seeding (pre-configured credentials, reversible encryption on DC) make the attack surface easier than production environments, which inflates attacker success rates. Scenario A's attack success may be partially attributable to Webmin credentials and other pre-planted weaknesses rather than agent capability per se. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL — the paper runs live attacker and defender agents on cyber range infrastructure and measures operational outcomes. - Practical: The attacker uses Claude Opus 4.6 via API; the defender also uses either Opus 4.6 or a proprietary on-premise ~30B model. Both are commercially available in the sense that model API access and the CSI scaffold are required, but the CSI framework is a commercial product not publicly released, limiting direct replication. - Efficient: Each 6-hour experiment session consumes substantial inference compute for both attacker and defender agents running continuous tool-use loops; no token counts or cost figures are reported. - Effective: The agents achieve domain-level compromise on the Enterprise range in all 10 experiments, with 55–80% MITRE ATT&CK precision, demonstrating the method reproduces realistic multi-stage attack chains; however, effectiveness is inflated by intentionally weakened range configurations. - Scalable: Only two fixed range scenarios are tested; behavior at different topology types, larger host counts, or cloud-native environments is not evaluated. The paper cannot generalize the binary topology-dependent outcome to arbitrary network architectures. No software artifact, trained model, or dataset is released; the work exists in paper form with proprietary tooling. Strengths: - The Velociraptor weaponization finding — five distinct APT profiles independently discovering and exploiting the same defensive management platform as a C2 channel, a behavior not encoded in any threat profile — is a concrete, surprising, and practically relevant emergent result that meaningfully advances understanding of AI attacker behavior. - The execution-based ATT&CK fidelity measurement (precision/recall of observed vs. documented techniques from live kill chains) is a methodologically cleaner measure than prior text-extraction approaches and produces specific, citable numbers. - Testing two defender model configurations (frontier Opus 4.6 vs. ~30B alias2-mini) and finding strategic outcome parity despite order-of-magnitude parameter difference is a practically important result: it suggests network architecture, not defender AI capability, is the dominant defensive variable. - The experimental design's separation of behavioral signal (APT profile prompt) from operational parameters (identical operator messages across profiles) is sound and enables clean causal attribution of observed differences to profile assignment.
