Matrix blocking further tiles each thread's work into blocks of size α×β that fit into the 768 KB Array Memory, loaded in batches for SIMD accumulation (Algorithm 3). The theoretical runtime model accounts for partially overlapped communication, yielding a per-iteration complexity of O(nnz/p) when overlap is complete. Critical design decisions: the ELL/CSR split is justified by vectorization alignment requirements but the threshold α is hardware-fixed (DSP SIMD width), not tuned per matrix—the paper offers no sensitivity analysis. Fusing the two inner products into one MPI call is well-justified. Loop fusion of WAXPBY with SpMV merging is justified by memory traffic reduction but not independently ablated from the DSP offload. Key Results All experiments use 10 symmetric positive definite matrices from the SuiteSparse Collection (rows ranging from 66 K to 1.59 M, nnz from 597 K to 17.6 M). Primary platform: MT-3000 CPU–DSP heterogeneous node (16 CPUs + 4 DSP clusters of 384 accelerator cores each), 4 MPI processes per node. Single-node: optimized CG achieves average 1.44× speedup over classical CG (max 2.12×) and average 1.60× over pipelined CG (max 2.07×). GFLOPS improvement over classical CG averages 80.18%; CPU efficiency improvement averages 38.99%. SpMV on DSP (SpMV_DSP) achieves 3.15× over CPU-only SpMV_CPU and 3.22× over CPU-based ELL (SpMV_ELL). Ablation: communication optimization alone yields 1.20× speedup; computation optimization (DSP offload + loop fusion) yields 1.49× average—the two together produce the full gain. Multi-node scaling (strong scaling): at 1–8 nodes (4–32 processes), optimized CG averages 2.29× speedup vs. 1.94× classical and 2.03× pipelined. At 768–1024 nodes, average parallel efficiency is 75.08% (peak 88.69%). At 2048 nodes, efficiency drops to 22.63% average (peak 30.94%), compared to 15.05% for classical and 15.21% for pipelined CG—the optimized method scales better but still degrades substantially. GPU portability: on A100, V100, and 8×P100, the method achieves 1.06×, 1.04×, and 1.24× over HeteroCG (cuSparse-based); SpMV_GPU achieves 1.44×, 1.18×, 1.22× over HeteroSpMV. GPU memory overhead is modest (≤3.17% increase on A100/V100, modest reduction on P100). Convergence: iteration counts for the optimized CG are within 1–2% of classical CG for most matrices, slightly higher (e.g., 3136 vs. 3068 for tmt_sym at κ=7.87×10⁸). Numerical stability: pipelined and optimized CG accumulate larger residual drift than classical CG on ill-conditioned matrices, as expected from their modified recurrences. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD / ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION. The contribution is a specific combination of: (a) communication-computation overlap via pipelined CG (existing algorithm from Ghysels & Vanroose 2014) applied to a CPU–DSP target; (b) an architecture-aware SpMV kernel for MT-3000 DSPs using ELL/CSR split, SIMD-aligned matrix blocking, and thread partitioning. The genuinely novel element is the integration of these optimizations for the MT-3000 DSP architecture, particularly the matrix preprocessing/partitioning/blocking strategy for DSP SIMD execution; the pipelined CG and data segmentation strategy are established ideas applied to a new platform. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablation is present (Fig. 8 separates communication vs. computation optimization), though it conflates DSP offload with loop fusion rather than isolating each. No error bars, confidence intervals, or multiple-run variance are reported—single-run timings throughout. Convergence and numerical stability analyses (Tables 4 and Fig. 14) are a strength. No code, data, or models are released; no repository URL is mentioned. The MT-3000 is a specialized Chinese HPC processor with limited public availability, which substantially limits reproducibility. The GPU comparison baseline (HeteroCG based on cuSparse) is weak—no comparison against AMGX, PETSc with GPU backend, or other optimized GPU CG libraries. The ELL zero-padding ratio varies enormously across test matrices (0.08% to 85.48%), yet performance is reported as averages without weighting—matrices like finan512 (85.48% padding) drag down DSP efficiency significantly and skew averages. All 10 matrices are SPD; results do not generalize to non-SPD systems. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS (an implemented, evaluated framework on real hardware). - Practical: Runs on an MT-3000 node (a specialized Chinese HPC processor) and commodity CPU–GPU platforms (A100, V100, P100); the MT-3000 path requires hardware not generally commercially available outside China's HPC ecosystem. - Efficient: Memory overhead is modest (≤3.17% additional GPU memory vs. HeteroCG); no preprocessing time is reported, which matters since matrix preprocessing/partitioning/blocking are one-time setup costs that could be non-trivial for large matrices. - Effective: For SPD matrices on MT-3000, the method demonstrably reduces iteration wall-time; for ill-conditioned systems without preconditioning, it will require more iterations or fail to converge, making it ineffective for a wide class of real problems. - Scalable: Tested up to 2048 nodes (8192 processes); parallel efficiency degrades to 22.63% at 2048 nodes—acceptable for dense-communication-regime problems but problematic for sparse, low-nnz-per-row matrices at extreme scale. No guidance on when to prefer this method vs. preconditioned alternatives. The work delivers a software implementation but no public release; it stops at paper form. Strengths - The ELL/CSR hybrid SpMV targeting DSP SIMD architecture is architecturally well-motivated and yields a 3.15–3.22× SpMV speedup, which is the dominant kernel benefit. - The large-scale scalability evaluation (768–2048 nodes) is unusually thorough for this paper type and reveals honest degradation rather than cherry-picked results. - The per-matrix analysis linking zero-padding ratio to DSP efficiency (e.g., parabolic_fem at 0.17% padding achieving best DSP performance, finan512 at 85.48% achieving worst) provides actionable insight for practitioners. - The convergence and numerical stability analysis (Table 4, Fig. 14) explicitly quantifies residual drift, which many HPC optimization papers omit. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state that the method lacks preconditioning, which limits convergence efficiency and numerical stability on ill-conditioned matrices (large condition numbers may cause slowdowns or oscillatory behavior). They propose future work to add problem-specific preconditioners and parallelize the preconditioning step. This is a candid acknowledgment of a significant scope restriction. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The GPU baseline (HeteroCG using cuSparse) is not a strong comparison: no evaluation against PETSc, AmgX, Trilinos, or vendor-optimized CG solvers means the claimed GPU speedups (1.04–1.24×) are not independently verifiable as meaningful. - Preprocessing overhead (matrix partitioning, ELL conversion) is never measured—for applications with many right-hand sides (same matrix, many b vectors), setup cost amortizes; for single-solve use cases, it may dominate. - Performance is averaged across 10 matrices with wildly varying ELL padding ratios without acknowledgment that the ELL format is a poor choice for matrices with high row-length variance (e.g., finan512 at 85.48% wastes memory and bandwidth); a hybrid format selection criterion is not provided. - Strong scaling only: no weak scaling experiments, which would better characterize whether the method maintains efficiency when problem size grows with processor count. - The 2.12× single-node speedup over classical CG—while real—is modest given the significant engineering investment; the gap to the theoretical maximum is not analyzed. - The paper claims generality to CPU–GPU and CPU–DSP, but the GPU experiments show only marginal gains over an already-not-best-in-class baseline, making the "generality" claim weaker than presented. Bottom Line A competent HPC systems paper that demonstrates measurable gains on a specialized CPU–DSP platform through disciplined application of pipelined CG and SIMD-aware SpMV, but its practical reach is limited by the absence of preconditioning, the restricted SPD test suite, and a weak GPU baseline—practitioners targeting mainstream GPU clusters will find stronger alternatives in existing libraries.
Search Results
#168 — Moderately Innovative (50)

Cross-Vendor Sola ISPM Benchmark: Evaluating Agentic AI for Federated Identity Security Reasoning

E Yavin, G Engelberg, K Koutsyi, L Goldberg, G Baron · arxiv.org · generating environments for agent testing in enterprises - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a 50-task benchmark and multi-metric evaluation framework for assessing agentic AI systems on cross-vendor Identity Security Posture Management (ISPM) tasks that require multi-hop entity resolution across eight live enterprise platforms. - Why it's important: Enterprises face federated identity risks — terminated employees retaining access, MFA bypass paths, privilege escalation chains — that span systems never designed to interoperate, yet no standardized benchmark exists to measure whether AI agents can detect them. - How it does it: Constructs production-grounded tasks over a live environment (AWS, Okta, Azure AD, Google Workspace, HiBob, GCP, GitHub, MongoDB), then systematically ablates five levels of injected relational context — from no schema to full schema plus security graph plus few-shot examples — across four frontier LLMs. - The good: The consistent finding across all four models that injecting cross-vendor graph topology is the single largest driver of join fidelity improvement, and that LLMs already reason correctly about security posture but fail at evidence reconstruction, is a clean, actionable insight. - The missing: Every experiment evaluates only Sola's own proprietary agent on Sola's own production environment using a benchmark Sola co-generated — no external agent baseline, no public data release, and no independent verification of any number in the paper. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners building ISPM agents who want to understand which context modalities matter most for cross-vendor reasoning, but all quantitative results should be treated as unverified vendor self-reports.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (38) and Resilience (40) are the decisive drags: every number comes from Sola evaluating Sola's own proprietary agent on Sola's own production environment using a benchmark Sola co-generated, with no external agent baseline, no public release, no significance tests, a judge-subject conflict (Claude Sonnet 4.6 both judges and is evaluated), and a self-contradictory 'three vs four LLMs' claim — so the headline figures collapse under adversarial review as unverifiable vendor self-reports. Novelty (65) is real — first cross-vendor ISPM benchmark requiring foreign-key-free entity resolution across 8 live platforms — and the graph-topology-dominates-join-fidelity finding is genuinely actionable (Non-Obviousness 62), but Practical Impact (45) suffers from no open artifact. This paper's vendor-grounded, self-evaluated framing resembles the user's negatively-rated set. Composite: 0.20*65 + 0.25*38 + 0.15*62 + 0.15*50 + 0.15*40 + 0.10*45 ≈ 50.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 38 Non-Obv 62 Completeness 50 Resilience 40 Impact 45
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Modern enterprise identity infrastructure spans incompatible systems: HR systems (HiBob), multiple identity providers (Okta, Azure AD), cloud platforms (AWS, GCP), productivity suites (Google Workspace), developer tools (GitHub), and databases (MongoDB). Critical security risks — terminated employees with lingering cloud access, dormant accounts propagating across tenants, privilege escalation paths that originate in an IdP and terminate in a CSP — increasingly cross these vendor boundaries. Detecting them requires what the authors call solving the "Correlation Gap": inferring that j.doe@okta.com is the same principal as arn:aws:iam::123:role/JDoe-Dev when no shared foreign key exists. Existing AI security benchmarks (ExCyTIn-Bench, CTIBench, OrgAccess) address either threat detection on single platforms or RBAC policy interpretation over synthetic data; none evaluate whether an agent can execute verifiable, evidence-backed queries across a real heterogeneous identity stack. Key Idea Frontier LLMs already possess strong abstract security reasoning capability — they can formulate coherent step-by-step investigation plans even with zero schema context (ReasoningUtility scores of 0.94–0.99 baseline). The bottleneck is not reasoning but evidence reconstruction: without an explicit cross-vendor relational map (a "security graph" that encodes which field in Okta links to which field in AWS IAM), LLMs hallucinate table names and join paths, producing confident but grounded-in-nothing answers. Injecting that graph converts trial-and-error exploration into targeted, correct relational execution. Methodology The benchmark construction uses a four-stage iterative multi-agent pipeline: (1) a schema-aware generative agent with web search proposes candidate NL questions and ground-truth SQL queries against a live 8-platform environment; (2) an automated execution filter discards candidates whose SQL fails to execute or yields non-deterministic answers; (3) a Critic-Composer refinement loop enforces technical correctness, cross-vendor relevance, PII compliance, and semantic uniqueness; (4) a human cybersecurity expert performs final validation. This produces 50 tasks, each with a NL question, executable SQL, and ground-truth answer. The ablation study runs Sola's proprietary agent (full-path Tree-of-Thought exploration mode) across five context configurations: No Context, Schema Only, Schema + Graph, Schema + Examples, and Full Context (all three). Four LLMs are evaluated: Claude 4.6 Sonnet, Claude 4.8 Opus, GPT 5.5, Gemini 3.1 Pro. Each configuration × model combination runs T=5 independent execution traces. Evaluation uses a dual-LLM judge panel (Claude Sonnet 4.6 + GPT-4.1) scoring 9 metrics across Retrieval & Context Use, Reasoning Quality, SQL Quality, and Answer Quality categories, plus 6 deterministic structural metrics (table and join precision/recall/F1 vs. ground truth). Final scores use majority vote (mode) over 10 judge evaluations (2 judges × 5 traces). Three aggregate metrics are derived from the AnswerCorrectnessVsGT distribution: SuccessRate (score=1), SuccessRate≥0.5, and FailureRate (score=0). Key Results Under Full Context, Claude 4.8 Opus achieves the best performance: AnswerCorrectnessVsGT = 0.78, SuccessRate = 60%, FailureRate = 4%. At No Context, Opus baseline is 0.58, a 34% relative improvement. GPT 5.5 improves from 0.54 (No Context) to 0.69 (Full Context), a 28% relative gain. Claude 4.6 Sonnet goes from 0.53 to 0.76; Gemini 3.1 Pro from 0.58 to 0.74. The ~70% reduction in average SQL exploration queries is consistent across all models when moving from No Context to Full Context. Graph topology injection (Schema + Graph) drives the largest discrete jump in join F1 scores: Gemini Joins F1 rises from 0.52 (Schema Only) to 0.61 (Schema + Graph); Sonnet from 0.53 to 0.61. The most striking finding is the gap between AnswerVerdictCorrectnessVsGT (high-level binary verdict: is the configuration risky or not?) and AnswerCorrectnessVsGT (full semantic correctness with supporting evidence). Under Full Context, Opus achieves A.Ver = 0.94 but A.Corr = 0.78; Sonnet achieves A.Ver = 0.98 but A.Corr = 0.76. Models correctly diagnose the security posture but fail to reconstruct the complete evidentiary chain. No external agent baselines are included; all comparisons are between context configurations of the same proprietary agent. Novelty Classification NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK and NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The cross-vendor ISPM benchmark is genuinely novel: it is the first to ground ISPM evaluation tasks in a live multi-vendor production environment requiring foreign-key-free entity resolution across 8 platforms. The empirical finding that graph topology is the dominant contributor to structural join fidelity (exceeding the contribution of few-shot examples or schema alone) is a non-obvious and actionable result. The evaluation framework itself is a competent combination of established components (G-Eval rubric-guided LLM judging, RAGAS retrieval assessment, deterministic precision/recall metrics) rather than a novel evaluation methodology. Rigor & Reproducibility Five independent execution traces per question with majority-vote scoring provides some variance stabilization. No error bars or statistical significance tests are reported. The benchmark is not publicly released (grounded in Sola's production environment containing PII), and neither the Sola AI Agent nor the security graph is open-sourced. No code repository is mentioned. Three methodological concerns warrant flagging. First, this is a vendor self-report: all experiments evaluate Sola's proprietary agent on Sola's own production environment using tasks co-generated by Sola's own pipeline; there is no independent replication path and no external agent baseline. Second, the benchmark tasks were generated with full visibility into the security graph — the key contextual abstraction driving performance gains — creating a risk that tasks are inadvertently easier when the graph is provided. Third, one of the two LLM judges (Claude Sonnet 4.6) is also one of the evaluated agents, introducing a judge-subject conflict. The abstract states the study evaluates "three frontier LLMs" but the paper actually evaluates four, an internal inconsistency that undermines credibility. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL (the paper runs the agent against live databases and reports measured outcomes) and SYSTEMS (the Sola AI Agent is a deployed production system). - Practical: The agent runs against SQL-accessible enterprise databases on standard infrastructure; no specialized hardware required. - Efficient: Full-context reduces average SQL exploration queries by approximately 70% compared to the no-context baseline; absolute query counts and wall-clock latency are not reported, and the cost of context injection (security graph construction and maintenance) is not quantified. - Effective: The agent achieves 60% strict SuccessRate and 78% AnswerCorrectnessVsGT under optimal conditions, but these numbers are measured on tasks designed and validated by the same team that built the system, reducing confidence that they reflect performance on unseen real-world ISPM questions. - Scalable: Tested on one company's production environment with 8 platforms and a 50-task benchmark; no analysis of performance degradation as the number of vendor platforms, identity volume, or hop depth grows beyond the tested range (maximum 3 hops). The work delivers the benchmark question set (not publicly released), the evaluation rubric framework (described in the paper), and an ablation characterization of the Sola AI Agent — but no open software artifact. Strengths - Production-grounded evaluation: tasks require answers from live enterprise data rather than synthetic schemas, making the benchmark structurally more realistic than prior IAM evals (OrgAccess uses synthetic hierarchies; ExCyTIn-Bench uses simulated environments). - The verdict-correctness vs. full-correctness gap — LLMs diagnose security posture correctly but fail at evidentiary reconstruction — is a precise and operationally meaningful characterization of where frontier LLM agents break down in security workflows. - The combination of LLM-as-judge semantic metrics with deterministic table/join precision-recall-F1 metrics guards against LLM evaluation noise on structural questions that have exact answers. - The clean five-level context ablation design lets readers decompose the contribution of each context modality (schema vs. graph topology vs. few-shot examples) rather than treating the full pipeline as a black box. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors note that 74% of tasks classify as "Easy" under Spider 2.0's token-length metric, and that the maximum relational depth tested is 3 hops. They commit to extending the benchmark to 4+ hops and broader ISPM coverage (behavioral analytics, identity risk scoring, mitigation planning) in future work. They explicitly note that fast-path execution was not characterized in the ablation and that Gemini exhibits context-saturation behavior. These limitations are acknowledged directly and proportionately. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The absence of any external agent baseline is the most serious omission. The paper measures how the Sola agent improves as Sola's proprietary context is injected; it does not establish whether a vanilla GPT-5.5 or Gemini agent with a different context strategy would perform comparably or better. Without this comparison, the performance gap attributed to Sola's architecture cannot be separated from the performance gap attributable to context richness alone. - The benchmark question generation pipeline had full schema visibility into the security graph. If the Critic-Composer loop implicitly favored questions answerable with graph-assisted joins, the 34% relative improvement under graph injection may be inflated relative to a truly held-out question set. - One LLM judge (Claude Sonnet 4.6) is also an evaluated agent. If the judge model and evaluated model share training or fine-tuning, the judge may score that model's outputs more favorably — a bias the dual-judge design does not fully eliminate. - 50 tasks at T=5 traces and 20 configurations gives very small per-cell sample sizes (50 questions per configuration). Performance differences of 2–4 percentage points in SuccessRate correspond to 1–2 questions; without significance tests, many reported differences may be noise. - No latency, cost, or context-window consumption data is reported. For enterprise security operations where time-to-detection matters, this omission prevents practical deployment planning. - The abstract's claim of "three frontier LLMs" contradicts the four-model experimental section — a minor but credibility-undermining factual error in the submitted preprint. Bottom Line The cross-vendor ISPM benchmark fills a real gap and the graph-topology ablation finding is credible and useful, but because every number in the paper comes from Sola evaluating Sola's own agent on Sola's own data with a benchmark Sola designed, no result can be treated as independently verified — a practitioner needing to make procurement or architecture decisions cannot rely on these figures without external replication.
Search Results 2606.02674 PDF
#169 — Moderately Innovative (49)

Time series Foundation Models based on Physics-Informed Synthetic Histories for Cold-Start Photovoltaic Forecasting

L Longarini, A Rongoni, S Silenzi, E Frontoni, R Rosati · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a zero-shot pipeline that pairs physics-based synthetic PV production histories with time-series foundation models (TSFMs) to enable day-0 photovoltaic yield forecasting before any site-specific observations are available. - Why it's important: PV operators need production forecasts at commissioning time for grid integration and battery sizing, but no historical site data exists yet, making standard supervised approaches inapplicable and prior cold-start methods dependent on neighboring-site data or proprietary sources. - How it does it: A deterministic physics simulator (the new open-source OPAQUE, or PVGIS as an alternative) generates synthetic daily yield histories from plant metadata and ERA5 meteorological covariates; these replace the missing target history at TSFM inference time without any parameter updates. - The good: Covariate-aware foundation models (TabPFN-TS, Chronos-2, TimesFM 2.5) achieve approximately 1.7–2× lower error than Prophet under Real Feedback across 440 sites, and TSFM rankings are invariant to whether OPAQUE or PVGIS generates the context (< 0.5 pp WAPE difference), operationally validating generator substitutability. - The missing: All evaluations use perfect-foresight ERA5 horizon covariates known at forecast time, which is unrealistic in deployment; no experiment uses actual NWP forecast inputs, so all reported numbers are optimistically inflated. - Read it or not: Worth reading for PV system integrators who need a practical day-0 forecasting pipeline and for TSFM researchers studying how covariate architecture determines robustness under distribution shift — but verify the ERA5 foresight assumption before drawing operational conclusions.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (42) and Resilience (42) pull the composite down: single-seed evaluation, no per-component ablations, no code URL despite emphasizing OPAQUE's openness, and — most seriously — the perfect-foresight ERA5 assumption inflates every number relative to operational deployment, a critique the central claims do not survive cleanly. The source-independence finding (Novelty 58) is genuinely actionable, but under the true day-0 regime TSFMs barely beat Prophet. Workshop-depth contribution.
empirical Novelty 58 Rigor 42 Non-Obv 58 Completeness 52 Resilience 42 Impact 45
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation PV operators must produce production forecasts at commissioning time for grid integration, self-consumption optimization, battery sizing, and asset valuation. No historical observations from the target system are available at that moment, so standard supervised models requiring past target observations cannot be applied. Prior cold-start solutions depend on either transfer learning from neighboring or data-rich sites (requiring site-adjacent observations), hybrid physics-ML models with domain-adversarial training, or data-driven generators (GANs, diffusion) that themselves need pre-existing real training data. SolNet (Depoortere et al., 2024), the closest prior work, fine-tunes an LSTM on a PVGIS-derived synthetic history but remains tied to one surrogate and one architecture. Whether physics-based synthetic histories can serve as a reliable inference-time context for zero-shot TSFMs — without fine-tuning or any real target data — is the unanswered question this paper addresses. Key Idea The key insight is that TSFMs conditioned on temporal context do not require an accurate reconstruction of the target plant's history — they only require a plausible surrogate that captures seasonal envelope, capacity-normalized scale, and weather-driven variability. Any physics simulator that achieves this is interchangeable, because the TSFM's pretrained representations supply the residual modeling capacity. This decouples the synthetic-history source from downstream forecasting performance and makes the pipeline generator-agnostic. Methodology Two-stage pipeline. Stage 1 (synthetic history generation): either PVGIS or OPAQUE maps plant metadata and ERA5 hourly reanalysis covariates (retrieved via Open-Meteo) to a daily specific-yield sequence through three deterministic stages — (1) plane-of-array irradiance transposition via the Hay-Davies anisotropic model, (2) cell temperature modeling via NOCT with linear thermal derating coefficient, (3) DC-AC daily integration following PVWatts with per-cohort loss priors, inverter efficiency, and 0.5% annual degradation. Stage 2 (forecasting): five zero-shot TSFMs (Chronos-2, Moirai 2.0, TimesFM 2.5, TiRex, TabPFN-TS) plus naive persistence, seasonal-naive, and Prophet baselines are evaluated under three context strategies — Cold-Start Baseline (CSB: synthetic history only, single 365-day forward pass), Real Feedback (RF: 15-day rolling windows that append measured post-commissioning production to context), and Self-Forecast Feedback (SFF: 15-day rolling windows that append the model's own prior predictions). No model weights are updated; adaptation is inference-only. Models are taxonomized by covariate consumption mode: M1 native multivariate (Chronos-2, TabPFN-TS), M2 univariate backbone with additive regressor (TimesFM 2.5), M3 univariate (Moirai 2.0, TiRex). Critical design decisions: capacity-normalized specific yield (kWh kWp⁻¹ d⁻¹) for cross-site aggregation is well-justified; perfect-foresight ERA5 horizon covariates simplify comparison but reduce realism; single-seed evaluation is a weakness acknowledged by the authors. Key Results Evaluation spans 440 sites across four public datasets: Ausgrid (300 sites, Sydney), UK-PV (100 sites, temperate UK), PVDAQ (30 sites, multiple US climate zones), and DKASC (10 sites, Alice Springs desert). Under Real Feedback, TabPFN-TS achieves the lowest error (MAE 0.514, RMSE 0.721 kWh kWp⁻¹ d⁻¹ with PVGIS context; 0.512/0.717 with OPAQUE), Chronos-2 second (0.535/0.737), and TimesFM 2.5 third (0.587/0.793); all are approximately 1.7–2× below Prophet (0.930/1.138). Under Self-Forecast Feedback, Chronos-2 leads with WAPE 28.24% (PVGIS), demonstrating superior robustness to recursive self-conditioning. Under the strict Cold-Start Baseline, all TSFMs cluster within 0.05 MAE of one another (~0.90–1.02) and barely surpass Prophet (0.930), indicating a performance ceiling when no observational signal is available. The source-independence finding is the most surprising result: OPAQUE vs. PVGIS yields < 0.5 pp WAPE difference under RF and SFF, with preserved model rankings across both contexts. OPAQUE has substantially worse fidelity against ground truth on UK-PV (21.7% vs. 12.0% WAPE) due to PVGIS using SARAH3 satellite irradiance data for Europe, yet this fidelity gap does not translate to downstream forecasting degradation. Arid DKASC cohort sees sub-10% WAPE for the top-3 TSFMs under RF; cloudy UK-PV degrades to 15–17% WAPE. Moirai 2.0 and TiRex remain ~2× worse than the top-3 under RF. Baselines are current (Chronos-2 2025, Moirai 2.0 2025, TimesFM 2.5 2024, TiRex 2025, TabPFN-TS 2025) and strong within the zero-shot regime. Novelty Classification NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The empirically established generator-invariance of TSFM cold-start performance is genuinely novel and operationally actionable. OPAQUE is a new open physics-based PV simulator. The three-strategy benchmark (CSB/RF/SFF) across 440 sites with a covariate-consumption taxonomy is a novel evaluation framework. The underlying components (pvlib, PVGIS, existing TSFMs) are established; the contribution lies in systematic integration, evaluation design, and the source-independence finding — not in architectural innovation. Rigor & Reproducibility No per-component ablations are reported; the two-source comparison (OPAQUE vs. PVGIS) functions as a sensitivity analysis for generator quality but does not isolate individual OPAQUE modeling stages. Single-seed evaluation is explicitly acknowledged; no error bars, confidence intervals, or significance tests appear. The four datasets are publicly available at cited URLs. TSFMs are evaluated on published checkpoints with no retraining, making those results reproducible in principle. OPAQUE is described as open and satellite-free, but no code repository URL is given in the paper. The paper is a workshop contribution (2nd ICML Workshop on Foundation Models for Structured Data, 2026) and has not undergone full conference peer review. The primary methodological concern is the perfect-foresight ERA5 assumption: horizon meteorological covariates are treated as known at evaluation time, inflating all reported numbers relative to operational deployment where only NWP forecast outputs are available. Deployability (4-property check) EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: OPAQUE requires only ERA5 reanalysis (freely available via Open-Meteo) and standard plant commissioning metadata; TSFM inference runs on standard ML GPU hardware without site-specific training. - Efficient: OPAQUE generation is a lightweight deterministic pipeline (pvlib + NumPy); TSFM inference cost varies by model architecture (TabPFN-TS's tabular in-context learner may be slower than transformer models at long context lengths), but no inference latency or memory benchmarks are reported. - Effective: Under RF (post-commissioning regime), evidence of 1.7–2× improvement over Prophet across 440 sites is credible, but under CSB (true day-0 regime), covariate-aware TSFMs do not meaningfully outperform Prophet (~28–32% WAPE vs. 29–31% WAPE), so the operational value at commissioning day itself is limited. - Scalable: Tested at 440 sites; the generator and TSFMs are both stateless per-site, implying linear scaling — but this is a claim, not an experiment at 10× scale. The work stops at paper form; no software artifact or model checkpoint is released. Strengths - The source-independence finding is the paper's most actionable result: practitioners can substitute any plausible physics simulator without degrading TSFM forecasting accuracy, which removes a key engineering barrier to deployment. - The three-strategy evaluation (CSB/RF/SFF) is a principled decomposition that cleanly separates strict day-0 performance from post-commissioning feedback effects and reveals qualitatively different failure modes (long-horizon extrapolation drift vs. recursive autoregressive drift). - The covariate-consumption taxonomy (M1/M2/M3) explains observed performance differences mechanistically — models without horizon covariates (TiRex, Moirai 2.0 under SFF) degrade predictably, while Moirai 2.0's decoder-only design accounts for its unusually small autoregressive penalty (ΔSff = 6.80 pp vs. 14–17 pp for the top-3). - Dataset breadth (three continents, four climate regimes, 440 sites) is solid for a workshop paper and supports generalization claims across diverse irradiance conditions. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly flag daily temporal resolution (intraday forecasting not addressed), single-seed evaluation (variance uncharacterized), and perfect-foresight ERA5 inputs as leading to optimistic performance estimates. They identify synthetic-data-driven fine-tuning and the zero-shot vs. fine-tuning trade-off as open future directions. These are stated directly in the conclusions section, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The ERA5 perfect-foresight assumption is the most serious unaddressed concern: operational cold-start forecasting requires weather forecasts (NWP outputs with 20–40% additional uncertainty), not reanalysis. No experiment tests the pipeline with forecast-quality meteorological inputs, so the reported numbers cannot be used directly to predict operational accuracy. - Under CSB — the actual day-0 regime the paper is framed around — the advantage of TSFMs over Prophet is marginal or absent; the 1.7–2× improvement claim applies only to the RF regime, which requires real post-commissioning data to accumulate. The abstract and conclusions do not adequately distinguish this. - Ausgrid dominates the dataset (300/440 = 68% of sites), potentially biasing aggregate metrics toward the Australian residential profile; per-cohort tables exist but aggregate headline numbers are Australia-weighted. - No comparison against SolNet (Depoortere et al., 2024), the closest prior work that also uses PVGIS-derived synthetic histories for cold-start forecasting — the gap between zero-shot TSFMs and a fine-tuned LSTM is never quantified. - No code repository URL is provided for OPAQUE despite the paper emphasizing its open, reproducible design. Bottom Line A PV system integrator evaluating day-0 forecasting solutions or a TSFM researcher studying covariate-architecture robustness will find the source-independence finding and three-strategy evaluation framework genuinely useful — but must account for the perfect-foresight ERA5 assumption inflating all numbers before drawing operational conclusions.
Citations 2606.07457 PDF
#170 — Moderately Innovative (49)

DrugAgent: Reliable Multi-Agent Aggregation under Conflicting Biomedical Evidence

Y Inoue, T Song, X Wang, A Luna, T Fu · openreview.net · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces DrugAgent, a multi-agent aggregation framework that integrates ML-based affinity prediction, knowledge graph path reasoning, and PubMed RAG into a structured, rule-guided pipeline for drug-target interaction (DTI) strength classification. - Why it's important: Biomedical AI systems routinely receive conflicting signals from heterogeneous evidence sources, and existing multi-agent approaches that rely on implicit consensus or sycophancy-prone debate fail to handle this conflict in a principled, interpretable way. - How it does it: Each specialized agent converts its output into a shared JSON intermediate representation; a cross-source summarization step flags agreement and conflict; then a deterministic majority-vote rule (with KG/pKd/RAG tie-breaking and a constrained LLM fallback) produces the final label, keeping reasoning and decision-making strictly separated. - The good: The paper's most credible contribution is its own self-critique: it empirically demonstrates that removing RAG increases LLM-as-a-judge plausibility scores (from 76.3% to 76.7% "good" on kinase) while degrading evidence grounding, exposing plausibility scores as a measure of perceived coherence rather than evidence alignment. - The missing: DrugAgent's classification accuracy (0.391 kinase, 0.590 Tox21) is strictly outperformed by the naive ML+KG combination (0.408, 0.632), and no competing multi-agent DTI system is evaluated on the same benchmark, leaving the practical advantage of the structured aggregation machinery unestablished. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers studying evaluation methodology pitfalls in LLM-judge frameworks; practitioners expecting a DTI prediction advance will find the system inferior on objective metrics to simple pairwise source combination.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile (released GitHub artifact assembling DeepPurpose, BFS-KG, and RAG). The composite is pulled down primarily by Resilience (45) and Impact (50): DrugAgent's classification accuracy (0.391/0.590) is strictly dominated by the naive ML+KG combination (0.408/0.632), and no competing multi-agent DTI system is benchmarked, so the structured-aggregation machinery shows no objective advantage. Rigor (50) is limited by a circular LLM-judge faithfulness metric, no CIs on the 900-pair results, and unshown KG sensitivity analysis. Its redeeming, non-obvious contribution (50) — the honest demonstration that LLM-judge plausibility anti-correlates with evidence grounding — is more valuable than its prediction performance. Composite: 0.15*45 + 0.15*50 + 0.10*50 + 0.15*50 + 0.15*45 + 0.30*50 = 49.
systems Novelty 45 Rigor 50 Non-Obv 50 Completeness 50 Resilience 45 Impact 50
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Drug-target interaction prediction in biomedicine requires synthesizing three epistemically distinct evidence types: quantitative ML affinity estimates (noisy but scalable), curated biomedical knowledge graphs (interpretable but incomplete), and published experimental literature (authoritative but sparse in co-mentions). These sources frequently conflict — a KG may record only indirect multi-hop associations while a literature assay reports a direct IC50. Existing single-modality predictors discard two of the three evidence channels entirely. Multi-agent debate and voting approaches (Du et al. 2023; Ki et al. 2025) aggregate implicitly and are vulnerable to sycophancy: agents converge prematurely on the majority position, suppressing legitimate disagreement. The gap this work targets is the absence of a framework that makes conflict explicit, resolves it by rule rather than by unconstrained LLM reasoning, and produces traceable rationales for each decision. Key Idea The key idea is strict separation of evidence reasoning from decision-making: rather than asking an LLM to simultaneously weigh and decide, DrugAgent forces each agent to emit a structured JSON object (label + rationale + confidence), combines these into a conflict-aware summary representation, and then applies a deterministic priority rule (majority → source-specific tie-breaking → constrained LLM fallback) to produce the final label. This makes conflict resolution auditable and reproducible independent of LLM stochasticity. Methodology The pipeline runs in three stages. (1) Coordination: ML, KG, and RAG modules execute independently in fast mode (or optionally via a Coordinator Agent) with outputs constrained to a shared JSON schema. (2) Evidence generation: the ML Agent runs DeepPurpose (pretrained on BindingDB) producing continuous pKd values, discretized as Strong (pKd ≥ 7.0), Moderate (6.0–7.0), Weak (<6.0). The KG Agent constructs a heterogeneous drug-gene graph from DrugBank, CTD, and BindingDB, then performs BFS up to depth H=5, ranking candidate paths by S(P) = (1/|P|) · λ^H(P) with λ=0.6, selecting top-3 paths; an LLM interprets selected paths to produce a label and rationale. The RAG Agent issues three query types (drug–target pair, drug-only, target-only) against PubMed Central Open Access, retrieving up to 10 documents per query and re-ranking by embedding similarity (text-embedding-3-large) plus heuristic structural signals. (3) Aggregation: the Reasoning Agent converts all outputs into a structured JSON with per-source evidence_analysis entries and a cross-source summary_reasoning field. The decision module applies: majority vote first; if tied, tie-breaking by KG structure, pKd threshold, and RAG direct evidence in that order; constrained LLM fallback as last resort. All agents run on OpenAI GPT-5.2 via Azure (temperature=0.0, seed=42 except reasoning module); framework implemented in Microsoft AutoGen. Critical design decisions: (i) Deterministic decoding throughout is well-justified and confirmed by stability analysis; (ii) Multi-channel RAG retrieval is well-motivated by sparsity (only 178/900 kinase pairs have ≥1 direct co-mention article); (iii) Hub-node penalization (degree > 300) in KG path scoring is a reasonable heuristic but is not ablated independently. Key Results Two datasets: Kinase benchmark (900 balanced pairs, 300 per class, 178 kinases × 42 inhibitors) and Tox21 androgen receptor dataset (500 balanced pairs, single target). Primary evaluation uses LLM-as-a-judge on two dimensions: faithfulness (is the explanation grounded in the provided evidence?) and biological plausibility (is the DTI association mechanistically coherent?). Faithfulness: 98.8% of DrugAgent outputs rated Agree or Strongly Agree (N=889/900). Plausibility: DrugAgent achieves 76.3% "Not contradicted & Plausible"; removing RAG counterintuitively increases this to 76.7%, a result the paper itself attributes to the LLM encountering fewer constraints, not better reasoning. Classification accuracy (Kinase/Tox21): standalone ML 0.372/0.612, KG 0.380/0.582, RAG 0.341/0.526; naive ML+KG 0.408/0.632 (best overall); DrugAgent 0.391/0.590, underperforming ML+KG on both benchmarks. Stability on 100-sample subset (k=3 runs): mean label agreement 0.983, perfect agreement rate 0.95. Conflict dynamics: full-conflict cases (n=172) resolve to Moderate 68% of the time; overall bias P(Strong)−P(Weak) ≈ 0.02, balanced at the population level but with structured pattern-level directional biases. The Sunitinib-LRRK2 (correct) and Nilotinib-EPHA2 (incorrect, due to EPHA1 overgeneralization) case studies illustrate the mechanism. No baseline multi-agent DTI system is compared. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The framework assembles existing components (DeepPurpose, BFS KG reasoning, RAG) without algorithmic novelty in any individual module; what is genuinely new is the structured intermediate representation and explicit rule-governed conflict resolution architecture. The empirical finding that LLMaJ plausibility anti-correlates with evidence grounding (removing RAG increases plausibility) is an honest and independently useful observation that extends prior work on faithfulness vs. plausibility (Agarwal et al. 2024). Rigor & Reproducibility Ablation study is present: each of the three modules (ML, KG, RAG) is removed individually and the system is re-run from scratch with no information leakage from the ablated source. Stability is tested on a 100-sample subset with 3 repeated runs. No error bars, confidence intervals, or significance tests are reported on the 900-pair classification results. Code is released at https://github.com/sciluna/DrugAgent. Three methodological concerns: (1) The primary evaluation metric (faithfulness) is circular — an LLM judge assesses whether an LLM's explanations are grounded in the evidence the LLM itself consumed and summarized; high scores are structurally expected, as the paper acknowledges. (2) The classification accuracy comparison is self-labeled a "sanity check," but it is the only objective evaluation, and DrugAgent loses to the naive ML+KG baseline on both benchmarks. (3) KG hyperparameters (λ=0.6, H=5, k=3) are claimed to be validated by sensitivity analysis, but those results are not shown. This is a workshop paper (ICML 2026 Life Sciences Workshop), not peer-reviewed as a main conference contribution. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS (and EMPIRICAL — the system is evaluated via experiments, not deployment metrics). - Practical: Runs on Azure OpenAI API (GPT-5.2) with standard DeepPurpose inference; no specialized hardware required beyond cloud API access. - Efficient: Not stated. Processing 900 drug-target pairs with multiple GPT-5.2 calls per pair (KG interpretation, reasoning, faithfulness judging, plausibility judging) incurs non-trivial API cost; no runtime or cost analysis is reported. - Effective: On the objective classification metric, DrugAgent (0.391 kinase accuracy) does not match the naive ML+KG combination (0.408); the interpretability advantage is real but evaluated via a circular LLM-judge protocol. - Scalable: Tested on at most 900 pairs against 178 targets and 42 inhibitors. The Tox21 dataset has a single target (androgen receptor), which is not a meaningful test of generalizability to diverse chemical–biological spaces. No scaling analysis is provided. Delivers a software artifact (GitHub repository); ML and KG sources are third-party dependencies. Strengths - The paper's most valuable intellectual contribution is its self-critical demonstration that LLMaJ plausibility is anti-correlated with evidence grounding: removing a grounding source (RAG) increases plausibility scores, which is a concrete, actionable warning for the evaluation community. - Explicit mapping of full-conflict cases to Moderate with near-zero average bias (0.02) is a principled uncertainty-aware design choice that outperforms sycophancy-prone debate methods. - Multi-channel RAG retrieval (pair + drug-only + target-only queries) is a well-engineered solution to the co-mention sparsity problem; it achieves full gene coverage (178/178) and near-complete drug coverage (39/42) for the kinase benchmark. - The stability analysis — 0.983 mean agreement rate, 0.95 perfect agreement rate across 3 runs — provides a concrete, quantified reproducibility guarantee for a system with LLM components. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state in the Impact Statement that "outputs may appear plausible despite imperfect evidence alignment" and recommend the system be used only with human expert validation. They directly confront the plausibility–grounding gap in the Results section, attributing inflated No-RAG plausibility to reduced constraint rather than better reasoning. They acknowledge RAG-induced errors via overgeneralization (the EPHA1/EPHA2 case). They frame classification as a "sanity check" rather than a primary claim — a reasonable scope limitation given the framework's stated goal of interpretable aggregation, though it leaves the question of practical utility against objective ground truth unanswered. Analyst-Identified Gaps - DrugAgent is strictly dominated on both classification benchmarks by the naive ML+KG heuristic combination, and the paper provides no analysis of which specific aggregation decisions contribute to this gap or how the rule-based fusion could be improved. - No competing multi-agent DTI system (e.g., MedAgents, or LLM-only pipelines) is evaluated on the same benchmark, making the architecture's advantage over prior multi-agent work entirely conceptual. - The Tox21 "generalizability" experiment uses a single target (androgen receptor) — this is not evidence of generalization across diverse biological targets or chemical classes. - Sensitivity analysis for KG hyperparameters (λ, H, k) is claimed but not shown; the scoring function S(P) = (1/|P|) · λ^H(P) is heuristic with no theoretical justification. - Frequency of RAG-induced overgeneralization errors (like the EPHA1/EPHA2 case) is unknown beyond two qualitative examples; this failure mode could be systematically more common when targets share name prefixes. - API runtime and cost per query are not reported, making operational deployment planning impossible. Bottom Line DrugAgent is an honest, well-engineered workshop contribution whose principal finding — that LLM-judge plausibility is a misleading proxy for evidence grounding — is more valuable than its DTI prediction performance, which is outperformed by simply averaging ML and KG outputs without any LLM reasoning layer.
Search Results
#171 — Moderately Innovative (49)

Making Sense of Continuous Process Dynamics

S Dodangeh, M Kabierski, H van der Aa · hanvanderaa.com · business process mining or process mining - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a three-step algorithmic approach that applies classical time series decomposition (additive, multiplicative, STL) and zero-lag Pearson correlation analysis to event-log-derived process characteristic time series in order to separate systematic from random process fluctuations and quantify inter-characteristic dependencies. - Why it's important: Process mining analysts currently rely on purely visual inspection of raw time series to interpret temporal process dynamics, a method that cannot distinguish trend from seasonality from noise nor quantify how one process dimension relates to another, leading to ambiguous or misleading conclusions. - How it does it: Event logs are segmented into tumbling daily windows; scalar characteristics (case counts, throughput time, resource counts) are extracted per window; the decomposition method that minimizes the residual variance fraction is auto-selected; component variance fractions and Ljung-Box residual tests characterize each series; Pearson correlation matrices characterize cross-characteristic dependencies. - The good: On time series where seasonal variance fraction exceeds 0.30, decomposition-based ARIMA/SARIMA prediction outperforms raw-series baselines in 6 of 7 cases, with the (Tc+Rc, Sc) configuration yielding an average RMSE improvement of 24.03% — including reducing throughput-time RMSE in BPIC18 from 7707.44 to 5789.62. - The missing: The predictive baselines are limited to ARIMA and SARIMA; no modern forecasting methods (Prophet, LSTM, N-BEATS) are included, and no significance tests are applied to RMSE differences, some of which are marginal. - Read it or not: Worth reading for BPM/process mining researchers wanting a quantitative analytical layer on top of process dynamics visualization; practitioners expecting a user-validated tool or definitive predictive gains can wait for a more mature follow-up.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (45) is held back by the absence of significance tests on RMSE deltas (several within noise), shallow baselines (ARIMA/SARIMA only, no Prophet/LSTM/N-BEATS), fixed window/seasonal parameters with no sensitivity analysis, and zero-lag Pearson correlation applied to non-detrended near-unit-root series — but public code and standard 4TU benchmarks lift it above L001. Novelty (45) is a genuine NEW METHOD via operationalization of STL with an automated min-residual-variance selector plus the vf(Sc)>0.30 empirical rule, though all components are off-the-shelf. The composite is moderate because no single dimension is strong: Non-Obviousness (50) from the counter-intuitive correlation findings and Completeness (60) from direct, honest limitation acknowledgment are the bright spots. Weighted: 0.20*45 + 0.25*45 + 0.15*50 + 0.15*60 + 0.15*45 + 0.10*50 = 49. A solid reproducible workshop exploration rather than a settled result.
empirical Novelty 45 Rigor 45 Non-Obv 50 Completeness 60 Resilience 45 Impact 50
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Business process analysts need to understand how processes change continuously over time — seasonal workload peaks, long-term operational shifts, and irregular disruptions all co-occur in event logs. Current process mining tools (DyLoPro, VDD, PEA) address this entirely through visualization: plots of case counts or cycle times are shown to analysts who interpret patterns manually. This creates two concrete problems: visual inspection cannot disentangle trend, seasonality, and noise — leading to ambiguous or outright false readings of what drives observed changes — and it cannot quantify how fluctuations in one process dimension (e.g., resource availability) co-vary with fluctuations in another (e.g., throughput time). The gap is the absence of a data-driven, quantitative analytical layer that sits between raw event logs and the decisions analysts make about process management. Key Idea The core insight is that mature time series decomposition techniques developed in econometrics and signal processing (STL, additive, multiplicative decomposition) can be transferred directly to process characteristic time series derived from windowed event logs, and that doing so yields both interpretable structural diagnostics (via component variance fractions and Ljung-Box tests) and cleaner signals for downstream prediction (by feeding structured components to ARIMA/SARIMA separately). The novelty is not any individual technique but their systematic operationalization in the process mining context with an automated method-selection rule. Methodology The pipeline has three stages. (1) Time series derivation: the event log is segmented into non-overlapping tumbling windows of fixed duration l (evaluated at daily granularity). Four scalar characteristics are computed per window: new case count, active case count, average throughput time, and active resource count. Warm-up and cool-down windows are removed using a 25th-percentile threshold on active case counts, following prior work. (2) Individual time series analysis: classical additive (yc,t = Tc,t + Sc,t + Rc,t), classical multiplicative (yc,t = Tc,t × Sc,t × Rc,t), and STL decomposition are each applied to every characteristic time series. The method with the smallest residual variance fraction vf(Rc) = Var(Rc)/(Var(Tc)+Var(Sc)+Var(Rc)) is selected automatically. The Ljung-Box test (α=0.05, h=10 lags) then checks whether residuals are white noise. (3) Interrelation analysis: zero-lag Pearson correlation coefficients are computed for all pairs of characteristic time series. The design choice to use zero-lag only (rather than cross-correlation at multiple lags) is explicitly acknowledged as a limitation. Critical decisions: minimum residual variance fraction as selector is well-motivated but not independently validated; window size and seasonal period (fixed at 7 days in experiments) are user-specified with no automated guidance; correlation is applied to non-detrended, potentially non-stationary series without stationarity checks. Key Results Backward-looking: CVs range from 0.07 (Hospital Billing active cases) to 3.45 (BPIC18 new cases) across four logs. Hospital Billing assigns vf(Tc)=1.00 to all four characteristics, indicating near-pure trend structure. BPIC18 active resource counts show seasonal dominance (vf(Sc)=0.59). BPIC15-1 average throughput time is dominated by the residual (vf(Rc)=0.71), with residuals confirmed as white noise, indicating the process behaves largely unpredictably. Correlation analysis reveals counter-intuitive findings: the BPIC18 new-case-to-active-case correlation is −0.17 despite the intuitive expectation of co-movement, while Hospital Billing shows new-case-to-resource correlation of 0.67 versus active-case-to-resource correlation of only 0.18. Forward-looking: across 16 time series (4 logs × 4 characteristics), decomposition outperforms both ARIMA and SARIMA baselines in 6 cases, improves over at least one baseline in 9. When decomposition wins, the (Tc+Rc, Sc) configuration achieves an average RMSE improvement of 24.03%; when it loses, degradation averages only 3.48%. Among series with vf(Sc)>0.30, decomposition improves at least one baseline in 6 of 7 cases. Baselines (ARIMA, SARIMA) are classical and exclude all modern forecasting methods. Evaluation scale is limited to 4 public logs from the 4TU repository. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD, NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The genuine contribution is the operationalization of STL decomposition with automated method selection (via residual variance fraction minimization) as a structured analytical layer for process mining, plus the empirical identification of vf(Sc)>0.30 as a condition predicting when decomposition benefits downstream forecasting. Individual components (STL, ARIMA/SARIMA, Pearson correlation, event-log windowing) are all established techniques applied to a new domain. Rigor & Reproducibility Code is publicly available at https://git01lab.cs.univie.ac.at/sanad95/cbf; the four event logs are standard public benchmarks from the 4TU repository, enabling reproduction. No error bars or statistical significance tests are reported for RMSE differences, some of which are small enough to be sampling noise (e.g., BPIC15-1 new case counts: ARIMA=0.84, SARIMA=0.83, (T+R,S)=0.85). No sensitivity analysis is performed on window size (fixed daily) or seasonal period (fixed 7 days), which are major structural parameters. The decomposition method selector (minimum residual variance fraction) is heuristic with no theoretical justification or comparison to alternatives. No user study is conducted, despite the stated aim of supporting business analysts. The paper is a workshop submission (BPM Forum 2026), not a peer-reviewed journal. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL — the approach is applied to real event logs and evaluated against forecasting baselines. - Practical: Yes — implemented in Python using PM4Py and statsmodels, both widely available; no specialized hardware is required. - Efficient: Trivially low compute — STL and ARIMA/SARIMA on daily time series of hundreds to low thousands of points runs in seconds on any modern workstation. - Effective: Partially — decomposition reliably improves prediction when seasonal variance fraction exceeds 0.30, but provides no benefit or slight degradation in the majority (10/16) of tested series; backward-looking insights are qualitatively plausible but lack user validation. - Scalable: Not tested beyond four logs or four characteristics; the all-pairs correlation matrix grows quadratically in the number of characteristics and may become unwieldy, but this is not addressed. The paper delivers a Python implementation in an open repository; no trained models or datasets are contributed beyond the existing public logs. Strengths - The component variance fraction metric gives analysts a concrete, interpretable measure of how much of a process's temporal variability is structural (trend or seasonality) versus random — more actionable than visual inspection and more nuanced than binary trend/seasonality strength tests. - The empirical finding that vf(Sc)>0.30 is a reliable condition for decomposition-based prediction gains provides a practical decision rule grounded in data. - The automatic decomposition method selector (minimum residual variance fraction) is a clean, principled criterion that requires no analyst judgment. - Counter-intuitive correlation findings (e.g., negative new-case-to-active-case correlation in BPIC18) demonstrate the approach surfaces non-obvious insights that visualization cannot. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state: (1) evaluation covers only four event logs, limiting generalizability; (2) results depend on design choices including window size, seasonal period, and selected characteristics; (3) the forward-looking comparison is limited to ARIMA and SARIMA — SARIMAX, VAR, VARIMA may yield different outcomes; (4) no user study has been conducted; (5) lag-aware cross-correlation is left to future work. The authors confront these directly in the conclusion, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Zero-lag Pearson correlation is applied to raw (non-detrended) time series that in many cases have near-unit-root trend components (vf(Tc)=1.00). Correlation between trending series produces spuriously inflated coefficients driven by the shared trend, not by genuine co-movement of fluctuations. The paper provides no stationarity checks or detrending prior to correlation computation. - All modern forecasting baselines are absent: Prophet, N-BEATS, LSTM, Temporal Fusion Transformer, and PatchTST are standard in applied time series forecasting. Claimed improvements over ARIMA/SARIMA may vanish against these methods, or alternatively, decomposition-assisted modern models may improve even further — either finding would be more informative. - RMSE differences in Table 3 are reported without significance tests. Multiple entries differ by less than 3% (e.g., BPIC15-1 new case counts: 0.84 vs 0.85 vs 0.83), making it impossible to determine whether observed differences are meaningful or sampling noise across 16 series. - Window size (1 day) and seasonal period (7 days) are fixed across all four logs without sensitivity analysis. These choices fundamentally determine what structure the decomposition identifies; a monthly window with a 4-week period may expose entirely different variance fraction profiles. - The minimum-residual-variance-fraction criterion for decomposition method selection is asserted as optimal but never compared against alternatives (e.g., AIC-based model selection, cross-validation RMSE on held-out windows). Bottom Line A process mining researcher will find a clean, reproducible operationalization of time series decomposition for process dynamics analysis and a useful empirical characterization of when it helps prediction — but the shallow baselines, absence of significance testing, four-log evaluation, and unaddressed stationarity issues in the correlation analysis mean this is best read as a promising workshop exploration rather than a settled contribution.
Search Results
#172 — Moderately Innovative (48)

AdMem: Advanced Memory for Task-solving Agents

R Wang, H Lu, S Liu, L Dong, J Zhu · arxiv.org · automated generated environments for agent testing - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces AdMem, a unified three-agent memory framework for LLM task-solving agents that integrates semantic, episodic, and procedural memory in a bi-level (short-term + long-term) architecture with a bandit-style effectiveness scorer for procedural memory retrieval. - Why it's important: Existing procedural memory systems replay only successful trajectories and lack principled mechanisms for learning from failures or for online memory quality management, causing them to actively harm performance in many domains. - How it does it: A critic agent annotates each completed action with a binary reward (comparing actual vs. expected outcome) and a reflective note; an EM algorithm then updates per-entry effectiveness scores v_m, and retrieval combines v_m with cosine context similarity so that demonstrably useful memories surface preferentially. - The good: On PDDL (60 tasks), AdMem achieves 76.7% task completion vs. 33.3% for ReAct and 43.3% for AWM, and on Jericho across three streaming epochs completion rises from 45% → 50% → 60%, showing genuine continual learning. - The missing: The evaluation uses only two baselines (ReAct and AWM), excludes directly comparable recent systems (Agent KB, Mem^P, HiAgent), tests only one LLM backbone (Claude Haiku 4.5), and reports no error bars or significance tests across a benchmark where domain sizes range from 20 to 251 tasks. - Read it or not: Researchers designing long-horizon multi-task agents should read it for the critic-annotated procedural memory and bandit retrieval patterns, but practitioners should wait for a stronger empirical comparison before committing to the architecture.
Why this rank: Systems profile (multi-agent memory framework run in simulation). Rigor (38) is the dominant drag: only two baselines with directly comparable systems (Agent KB, Mem^P, HiAgent) excluded, a single backbone (Haiku 4.5), ablation confined to 20-task Jericho, and no error bars on domains where one task equals 5 points. Completeness (45) and Resilience (42) suffer because the thin baseline set and unexplained WebShop/ScienceWorld regressions go unaddressed, and no library is released (Impact 45). The bandit-EM procedural scorer is a genuine idea (Novelty 62), but the evidence is too narrow to support the quantitative claims. Weighted composite ≈ 48.
systems Novelty 62 Rigor 38 Non-Obv 60 Completeness 45 Resilience 42 Impact 45
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation LLM-based agents operating in persistent, multi-task environments — embodied AI, text games, web navigation, tool-use pipelines — cannot reliably accumulate and reuse decision-making knowledge across sessions. Three specific failure modes motivate this work: (1) prior procedural memory systems (e.g., AWM) extract guidance only from successful completions, missing the failure cases where help is most needed; (2) sparse, task-level binary feedback is insufficient to assign credit to individual steps in a long interaction chain; (3) existing systems are designed for offline train-then-deploy settings and lack mechanisms for online memory evaluation, pruning, or adaptive reranking. The paper targets developers of life-long agentic systems where the same agent repeatedly encounters similar tasks without environment reset between them. Key Idea The central insight is to treat procedural memory retrieval as a contextual bandit problem: each memory entry carries a learned effectiveness parameter v_m ∈ [0,1] updated via EM each time it is retrieved and a downstream reward is observed. Retrieval score = v_m × ψ(c_t)^T ψ(c_m), so entries that demonstrably helped past actions in similar contexts rise in rank, while harmful or irrelevant entries decay and eventually get pruned. This replaces the pure similarity-based retrieval used in prior systems, enabling the memory store to self-correct over time without human intervention. Methodology AdMem uses three parallel agents. The actor agent maintains short-term memory (STM) as a call-stack: the current sub-goal and its plan occupy the top of the stack; completed or compressed turns are summarized below, mimicking a program stack for scope management. At each turn the actor retrieves relevant LTM and selects an action, simultaneously declaring an expected outcome. The critic agent buffers (context, action, expectation) tuples and, once all downstream observations arrive, generates a binary reward r_t ∈ {0,1} and a reflective annotation by comparing expectation to outcome; it then reports the new procedural memory entry and the reward attributions for retrieved entries to the memory agent. The memory agent maintains three stores: dense-retrieval over semantic/episodic memories, and bandit-scored retrieval over procedural memories. Memory management applies a disuse threshold ε (entries retrieved below ε frequency are pruned) and a co-retrieval merge rule (entries consistently co-retrieved with frequency > 1−ε are merged). The EM update for v_m is derived from a binary OR model: reward r_t = l_1 ∨ l_2 ∨ … ∨ l_n ∨ l_base, where each l_i^(t) ~ Bernoulli(v_{m_i} × sim(c_t, c_{m_i})). Initial v_m = 1 (optimistic initialization). Three planning tools force structured sub-goal decomposition: plan_for_task, add_subgoal, conclude_subgoal, enabling the critic to properly scope which observations belong to which action. Critical design decisions: (1) stack-based STM is well-motivated but no ablation isolates it against simple rolling summarization; (2) the OR reward model is a simplification that cannot identify which of multiple co-retrieved memories was actually helpful; (3) critic reward annotation introduces an LLM-generated proxy reward with its own error rate, unquantified in the paper. Key Results Evaluated on AgentBoard (Ma et al., 2024) across 8 domains, single life-long streaming run, using Claude Haiku 4.5. Task completion (C) and average progress (P) for AdMem vs. ReAct vs. AWM: - AlfWorld (134 tasks): 63.4%/0.776 vs. 49.3%/0.659 vs. 47.0%/0.547 - BabyAI (112 tasks): 100.0%/1.000 vs. 97.3%/0.973 vs. 93.7%/0.937 - Jericho (20 tasks): 50.0%/0.533 vs. 40.0%/0.400 vs. 10.0%/0.223 - PDDL (60 tasks): 76.7%/0.907 vs. 33.3%/0.554 vs. 43.3%/0.641 - Tool-query Academic (20): 94.7%/0.967 vs. 70.0%/0.837 vs. 55.0%/0.819 - Tool-query Weather (20): 75.0%/0.904 vs. 60.0%/0.857 vs. 25.0%/0.753 - WebShop (251 tasks): 41.4%/0.765 vs. 42.2%/0.751 vs. 29.2%/0.701 - Science World (90 tasks): 56.7%/0.845 vs. 58.9%/0.826 vs. 31.1%/0.632 AdMem underperforms ReAct on WebShop and Science World by 0.8 and 2.2 percentage points respectively. AWM harms performance vs. ReAct in 5 of 8 domains. Multi-epoch Jericho (Table 2) shows AdMem improving 45% → 50% → 60% completion over 3 streaming epochs while LTP-only reaches only 25% → 30%. Per-step overhead: up to 2× wall-clock time and 3× LLM calls in the worst case; agents run in parallel to limit latency impact. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. The bandit-style EM effectiveness scoring for procedural memory retrieval (combining demonstrated reward with context similarity) and the critic agent's failure-case annotation mechanism are genuine contributions not present in prior work. The tri-type memory taxonomy and POMDP formulation are standard cognitive science taxonomy and existing formalisms applied to a new integrated system. Rigor & Reproducibility The paper is a preprint under review. No ablation is run across all 8 domains — the component decomposition study (Table 2) uses only Jericho (20 tasks), too small to generalize from. No error bars, multiple seeds, or significance tests are reported; on 20-task Jericho, a one-task difference is 5 percentage points. Only one LLM backbone (Claude Haiku 4.5) is tested; it is unknown whether the reward annotation quality holds with weaker or stronger models. No code or trained models are stated to be publicly released. The EM update and its convergence properties are described informally without proof of correctness or convergence analysis. The OR reward model's identifiability assumptions are not analyzed. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS — AdMem is a built multi-agent framework evaluated by running tasks in a simulated environment. - Practical: Runs on any LLM API (demonstrated with Claude Haiku 4.5 from Anthropic); no specialized hardware required. - Efficient: Worst-case 3× LLM calls per step and 2× wall-clock time vs. vanilla ReAct; parallelization of the three agents is claimed to recover most latency, but absolute measured latency figures are not reported. - Effective: Achieves substantial task completion improvements in 6 of 8 domains, with particularly strong gains in PDDL (+43.4 pp vs. ReAct) and Tool-query Academic (+24.7 pp); marginal underperformance on 2 domains suggests the pruning/retrieval tuning is domain-sensitive. - Scalable: Only tested at benchmark scale (20–251 tasks per domain, single streaming epoch for most domains); multi-epoch only on Jericho; no evaluation with memory stores growing beyond a few hundred entries or with diverse cross-domain streaming. The system delivers a running implementation (prompts provided in appendix), but no public library or CLI is released. Strengths - The failure-annotation design (critic agent generating reflections and rewards on actions that did not meet expectations) addresses a concrete gap over AWM-style success-only procedural memory; the Jericho result where AWM achieves only 10% vs. AdMem's 50% quantifies this gap starkly. - Bandit-style EM scoring integrates contextual similarity with demonstrated effectiveness in a lightweight, interpretable model that can be updated online without retraining. - Multi-epoch Jericho evaluation provides direct evidence of continual improvement from 45% to 60% over three passes, distinguishing AdMem from systems that provide one-shot transfer benefits. - Stack-based STM cleanly maps hierarchical sub-goal planning onto context management, a principled design that connects to well-understood program execution models. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors acknowledge the extra prompt, planning steps, and background LLM calls that increase per-step cost (worst case 2× time, 3× LLM calls) and note that parallelization mitigates — but does not eliminate — this overhead. They also acknowledge that memory benefit is domain-dependent: on domains where transferable knowledge is sparse, memory may not help. These are framed as known trade-offs rather than failure modes, which is credible. No acknowledgment of the thin baseline set, single-backbone limitation, or small ablation scope. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Baseline exclusions are under-justified: Agent KB (Tang et al., 2025) and Mem^P (Fang et al., 2025) are explicitly discussed as procedural memory systems for interactive environments (GAIA, SWE-bench, TravelPlanner, ALFWorld), yet neither is evaluated; the stated rationale (QA-focus) does not apply to them. - Ablation scope: Table 2 runs component decomposition only on Jericho (20 tasks). It is unclear whether the short-term planning contribution and long-term memory contribution hold in the same proportions across AlfWorld (134 tasks) or WebShop (251 tasks). - Single LLM backbone: The critic's reward and reflection quality are fundamental to the framework; whether Claude Haiku 4.5 reliably assigns correct binary rewards for complex multi-step actions is not evaluated, and the system's behavior with a weaker or different backbone is unknown. - No analysis of memory store growth: how many procedural entries accumulate per domain, what the retrieval recall looks like as the store grows, and whether merging/pruning actually bounds memory size in practice are not reported. - WebShop and Science World underperformance vs. ReAct is acknowledged implicitly but never analyzed — no investigation of whether it results from retrieval noise, incorrect reward annotation, or architectural overhead. - The OR model for reward attribution cannot distinguish which of n co-retrieved memories actually contributed; the EM updates may therefore mis-score entries in a predictable direction (under-estimating effective memories when retrieved alongside unhelpful ones). Bottom Line AdMem makes a real contribution with its critic-annotated procedural memory and bandit retrieval scorer, and the multi-epoch Jericho results demonstrate genuine online learning; but the paper's thin two-baseline evaluation, single LLM backbone, and Jericho-only ablation leave enough uncertainty that practitioners should treat the quantitative claims as suggestive rather than definitive until a broader experimental comparison appears.
Search Results 2606.06787 PDF
#173 — Moderately Innovative (48)

PRIMA: a bidirectional state-space architecture and training approach for sequence modelling of protein-protein interactions

A Fiorellini-Bernardis, S Boyer, J Quentin, G Jebali… · openreview.net · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces PRIMA, a 7.3M-parameter protein language model for protein-protein interactions built on bidirectional Mamba (BiMamba-S) and trained in two phases—first on 5M UniRef50 single sequences, then on 12M STRING PPI pairs concatenated as single inputs. - Why it's important: Transformer-based PLMs cap practical input length at ~1024 tokens due to O(L²) attention, which forces truncation of large multi-chain complexes and prevents scalable PPI foundation model training. - How it does it: Replacing self-attention with selective state-space model (SSM) layers that scale O(L) with sequence length, plus a bidirectional scan to preserve symmetric inter-chain information, eliminates the quadratic bottleneck while retaining length extrapolation via implicit positional encoding in the SSM recurrent state. - The good: PRIMA achieves 11.5× lower forward-pass latency and 115× lower peak GPU memory than a matched-parameter transformer at L=16,384, while outperforming that transformer on all four binding-prediction benchmarks at both cropped and full-length input. - The missing: PRIMA is compared only against PET, the authors' own ESM-2 8M model trained from scratch on the same subsampled data—never against MINT (ESM-2 650M + cross-attention, the actual SOTA PPI PLM) or any externally pretrained PLM, so the claim of superiority over "transformer-based PLMs" is substantially overstated. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers designing sequence-level PPI models who need long-context efficiency; the efficiency benchmarks are convincing, but the biological-validity evidence (especially binding affinity) is preliminary and the scope of comparison is narrow.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (45) and Resilience (45) cap the score: the central superiority claim rests on PET, a deliberately weak ESM-2 8M baseline the authors trained from scratch on subsampled data, with no comparison to MINT or production ESM-2, no ablations of the two-phase/bidirectional/tied-head choices, no significance tests, and no released code or weights — so the 'outperforms transformer-based PLMs' claim collapses under scrutiny. Novelty (55) is a credible but assembled method, and Practical Impact (35) is low as the paper ships only an architecture and recipe, not an artifact; the honest 'proof-of-concept' framing and leakage-awareness lift Completeness but not enough. Composite: 0.20*55 + 0.25*45 + 0.15*50 + 0.15*55 + 0.15*45 + 0.10*35 = 48.
empirical Novelty 55 Rigor 45 Non-Obv 50 Completeness 55 Resilience 45 Impact 35
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Predicting and representing protein-protein interactions from sequence is critical for drug discovery, immunology, and mechanistic cell biology, but the dominant PLM paradigm—transformer encoders such as ESM-2—hits a hard practical wall at ~1024 tokens due to quadratic attention cost. Multi-chain complexes routinely span thousands of amino acids when concatenated, forcing either truncation (MINT crops to 512 tokens per chain) or separate chain encoding followed by cross-attention (MINT's design), neither of which captures the full inter-chain context. A prior SSM-based model, LC-PLM-G, extended Mamba to multi-protein graphs but never trained on actual concatenated complexes and never evaluated on binding affinity. PRIMA targets this specific gap: a single SSM encoder that ingests the full concatenated PPI pair natively.
