The primal program maximizes Σ_t Σ_{i∈R_t} U_i(x_{i,t}) — a sum of concave utilities (proportional fairness, α-fairness, or weighted linear) — subject to per-step compute capacity C_compute, a cumulative memory budget M_GPU, and box constraints 0 ≤ x_{i,t} ≤ 1. The completion constraint is handled implicitly by removing requests from the active set once cumulative service reaches L^dec_i. Convexity is verified (concave objective, linear constraints, polyhedral feasible set). The Lagrangian dual introduces λ_t (compute price) and μ_t (memory price); strong duality holds under Slater's condition. KKT conditions yield the stationarity equation, and complementary slackness classifies four operating regimes (underloaded, compute-bound, memory-bound, doubly constrained). Greedy discrete rounding converts continuous rates to a feasible schedule by sorting requests by x*_{i,t} at each step. Three solvers are compared: Clarabel and ECOS (interior-point, high accuracy) and SCS (first-order ADMM, scalable but lower accuracy). Critical design decisions: (1) removing the explicit completion constraint in favor of active-set evolution — well-justified by standard NUM practice; (2) not including a tighter relaxation for memory release at completion — a known and acknowledged gap; (3) using greedy rounding rather than randomized or LP-rounding — motivated by simplicity but not proven to preserve an approximation ratio. Key Results: Evaluated on N=50 synthetic requests (prefill lengths uniform in [5,60], decode lengths in [10,100], arrivals in [0,20]) across six constraint scenarios. In compute-bound (S2) and doubly-constrained (S5) regimes, the convex-guided policy achieves 7.26 tokens/step versus 6.81 (FCFS) and 6.85 (SJF), a ~6% throughput gain. In underloaded, moderate-memory, and memory-bound regimes, all three policies converge to identical throughput, consistent with the regime analysis. CLARABEL and ECOS achieve zero duality gap (continuous = discrete) in all non-memory-bound scenarios. In the memory-bound scenario (S6), a 11.5% gap persists: the discrete scheduler achieves 9.09 tokens/step versus 8.15 from the continuous relaxation, because the fluid model charges memory over the full horizon rather than releasing it at request completion. Clarabel fails outright on the doubly-constrained case (S5); SCS marks several cases "optimal inaccurate." At N=200–500, interior-point solvers become impractical; SCS remains usable but loses precision. The ~6% throughput claim is measured only at N=50 on synthetic data with no statistical testing and no error bars. Novelty Classification: NEW FORMULATION + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The adaptation of Network Utility Maximization to LLM scheduling is the genuine contribution — specifically, the temporal coupling in the memory constraint (the memory mortgage Π^mem_t) is absent from static NUM and yields the new KKT structure. The individual pieces (fluid relaxation, NUM, KKT conditions, SRJF intuition) are all known; the novelty is their composition and the analytical recovery of SRJF/fair-sharing from first principles rather than heuristic argument. The paper does not introduce a new dataset, benchmark, or deployed system. Rigor & Reproducibility: No ablations in the conventional sense (the formulation has no hyperparameters to ablate). No error bars or multiple random seeds reported; a single synthetic draw of 50 requests is used for all six scenarios. No code or data released. The paper is a course project (Section 1 lists four students' task assignments), not a preprint or peer-reviewed publication — confidence in claimed results should be calibrated accordingly. The stationarity condition and convexity argument are straightforward and appear correct. The integrality gap argument (M^fluid ≥ M^disc) is clearly stated and the 11.5% figure is plausible. The claim that "continuous = discrete in non-memory-bound regimes" is not proven in general but is supported empirically for these six scenarios. The weaker-than-modern baseline (static FCFS rather than iteration-level FCFS from Orca) is flagged by the authors as future work. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (simulation on synthetic workload). - Practical: The convex program runs on a CPU via CVXPY; it does not require GPU resources for the optimization step itself, but the scheduling target is a GPU serving system. At N=50 and T≈800, solve time ranges from 0.5s (ECOS) to 31s (SCS) per scheduling horizon — far too slow for millisecond-granularity online scheduling. - Efficient: Interior-point solvers fail at N≥200; decision variables scale as O(N·T), making the method impractical as a real-time scheduler for production workloads of thousands of concurrent requests. The authors position it as an offline oracle or guide, not an online policy. - Effective: The simulation demonstrates ~6% throughput improvement in constrained regimes on synthetic data; there is no evaluation on real GPU hardware with actual LLM weights, real request traces, or real KV-cache dynamics. - Scalable: The paper explicitly states the formulation does not scale past N≈200 with current solvers; no experiments at scale are provided, only qualitative discussion of solver degradation. The work delivers no software artifact; it stops at paper form with CVXPY simulation code implied but not released. Strengths: - The KKT stationarity condition U′_i(x_{i,t}) = λ_t + m_block·Σ_{s≥t} μ*_s is a genuinely illuminating result that unifies SRJF and fair-sharing under one equation, providing an optimization-theoretic explanation for heuristics previously justified only empirically or via queueing theory. - The four-regime classification (underloaded / compute-bound / memory-bound / doubly constrained) from complementary slackness is actionable: dual variables serve as runtime diagnostic signals for triggering KV-cache eviction or quantization at the moment they become beneficial. - The formal analysis of why the fluid relaxation fails in the memory-bound regime (M^fluid ≥ M^disc due to memory not being released at fractional completion) is honest and precise, correctly attributing the 11.5% gap to a structural mismatch rather than solver error. - The connection to Ao et al.'s fluid equilibrium model — showing it is a special case of the convex formulation under stationarity — situates the work clearly in the literature and is non-trivial. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly acknowledge: (1) known output lengths are assumed, which does not hold in production; (2) the fluid relaxation introduces an 11.5% optimality gap in memory-bound regimes that requires tighter relaxations or integer-aware rounding to close; (3) the primary baseline (static-batch FCFS) is weaker than iteration-level FCFS from Orca; (4) the convex program does not scale to large N with current solvers. These acknowledgments are credibility positives — the limitations are confronted directly rather than minimized. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The paper's Section 1 identifies this as a course project with divided task assignments — the work has not undergone peer review and should be evaluated accordingly. Beyond author-acknowledged limitations: the ~6% throughput gain is measured on a single random draw of 50 synthetic requests with no statistical significance testing, no confidence intervals, and no sensitivity analysis to the random seed; the result may not be reproducible. The SJF baseline used is not iteration-level SJF but a simpler variant — the claimed advantage over SJF is therefore conservative in some regimes and potentially misleading in others. The paper asserts the convex-guided policy "completes all requests" while baselines do not, but this appears to be a function of the greedy rounding respecting constraints by construction, not an inherent advantage of the optimization. The claim that dual variables serve as "online diagnostic signals" is suggestive but requires solving the full convex program ahead of time, negating the online framing. Finally, the paper benchmarks three solvers on solve time and RAM but does not discuss what scheduling latency budget is acceptable for real inference systems, making the runtime numbers difficult to contextualize. Bottom Line: This course-project paper provides a clean optimization-theoretic derivation of why SRJF and fair-sharing emerge as optimal LLM scheduling policies in different resource regimes — a conceptual contribution worth understanding — but the toy-scale synthetic experiments, absent real GPU validation, weak baselines, and no code release mean it does not yet establish practical deployment value.
Search Results
#180 — Moderately Innovative (46)

How Small Can You Go? LoRA Fine-Tuning 270M-8B Models for Merchant Information Extraction in Financial Transactions

D Huang, T Drietomsky, B Barrett, Z Wang · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper conducts a deployment-oriented empirical study of 24 LoRA-fine-tuned model variants (270M–8B parameters) across four model families for extracting structured merchant information from noisy bank transaction strings, with production validation on Databricks Model Serving endpoints. - Why it's important: The existing production system (LLaMA 3.1-8B, 96.95% F1) requires 16+ GB GPU memory in half-precision and limits throughput for a pipeline processing millions of financial transactions daily, making operational costs prohibitive. - How it does it: A systematic sweep over model families (Gemma 3, Qwen 3.5, Aya, LLaMA), two prompting strategies (chain-of-thought Free-Thinking vs. JSON-Only), and two Qwen training templates (Think vs. Nothink), followed by real Databricks endpoint deployment of all 14 sub-8B finalists to measure benchmark-to-production accuracy transfer. - The good: Qwen 3.5 4B with JSON-Only prompting achieves 96.60% F1 at 3.8× lower per-sample inference latency than LLaMA 3.1-8B, and benchmark accuracy transfers to managed production endpoints with a mean ΔF1 of only −0.0081 across 14 deployed variants. - The missing: Checkpoint selection criterion (validation vs. test set) is never explicitly stated, raising the possibility of evaluation leakage; no comparison against any non-LLM baseline (BiLSTM-CRF, RoBERTa) is provided, leaving open whether the near-97% F1 ceiling requires LLM scale at all. - Read it or not: Worth reading for ML engineers and financial NLP practitioners making concrete model-size decisions for production structured extraction; skip if seeking methodological novelty or task-agnostic SLM findings.
Why this rank: Empirical profile (deployment study; authors explicitly position it as 'deployment-focused rather than methodologically novel'). Impact (60) is the strength — real Databricks endpoint validation of 14 variants with code and LoRA weights released, plus the actionable Think/Nothink finding (SFT suppresses extended thinking at zero accuracy cost, up to 2.9× faster). Novelty (35) and Rigor (45) pull the composite down: LoRA + CoT applied to financial NER is a known framing, no error bars or significance tests are reported despite treating <0.004 F1 deltas as conclusions, no non-LLM baseline (BiLSTM-CRF, RoBERTa) establishes that LLM-scale is even necessary, and the unresolved validation-vs-test checkpoint-selection question leaves the absolute accuracy claims credible only as relative comparisons.
empirical Novelty 35 Rigor 45 Non-Obv 45 Completeness 55 Resilience 45 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Bank transaction strings — e.g., "VIATOR IT-1558003355 360 3RD ST, STE 400, SAN FRANCISCO 7027495744" — must be decomposed into ten structured entity fields (merchant name, dynamic descriptor, intermediary, street, city, state, ZIP, phone, website, country) for downstream fraud detection, merchant categorization, and consumer analytics. The input is compressed, abbreviated, and non-standard, lacking the linguistic context that aids general-purpose NER. Mastercard's current production system is a LoRA-fine-tuned LLaMA 3.1-8B running at approximately 97% F1, but 8B-parameter models impose 16+ GB GPU memory requirements in half-precision, constrain inference throughput, and incur substantial energy costs at millions-of-transactions-per-day scale. The research question is whether a sub-8B model can maintain production-grade accuracy (>96% F1) with meaningfully reduced hardware requirements. Key Idea There is no single algorithmic trick; the paper's central empirical bet is that Qwen 3.5's hybrid Mamba-attention architecture — combining linear-time selective state-space sequence modeling with precise positional attention — is particularly suited to substring extraction from noisy, positionally structured text, enabling 0.8B Qwen to match 2–4B standard transformers on this task. A secondary insight is that chain-of-thought instructions during fine-tuning improve internal representations even when reasoning is excluded from output labels (implicit CoT), and that this benefit is largest for smaller models, suggesting CoT framing partially compensates for limited capacity. Methodology The dataset contains 8,015 labeled bank transaction strings (8,014 unique) split 6,125/668/1,213 train/validation/test. Ten entity fields exhibit highly imbalanced presence rates: MERCHANT_NAME appears in 100% of samples, WEBSITE in only 5.4%. All models are fine-tuned using LLaMA-Factory on a single NVIDIA DGX Spark workstation (GB10 Blackwell Superchip, 128 GB unified LPDDR5x memory). LoRA hyperparameters are fixed across all models: rank r=8, α=16 (scaling ratio 2.0), 6 epochs (2,298 steps), LR 1e-4 cosine schedule, effective batch size 16 (4×4 gradient accumulation), with 12 checkpoints saved per run at 200-step intervals. Evaluation reports the best checkpoint across the 12 per-model. The two prompt strategies (Free-Thinking at 3,700 chars with two-step CoT preamble; JSON-Only at 2,870 chars with direct extraction instruction) share identical entity classification rules and output labels — only the instruction framing differs. For Qwen 3.5, two training templates are compared: Think (qwen3_5, includes <think>...</think> tokens in training labels) and Nothink (qwen3_5_nothink, excludes thought tokens). Production validation deploys all 14 family×size×prompt fine-tuned sub-8B variants as Databricks Model Serving endpoints via MLflow, then re-evaluates each endpoint on the 1,213-sample test set. Per-field F1 is computed by exact string match; overall F1 is micro-averaged. Critical design decisions: (1) best-checkpoint reporting rather than final-checkpoint — justified by observed moderate overfitting after step 1,400–2,000; (2) exact-string-match F1 — stringent and standard for extraction but awards no credit for partial matches; (3) separation of experimentation (LLaMA-Factory on DGX Spark outside the production perimeter) from production deployment (Databricks MLflow) — explicitly justified by security policy constraints on which base models may enter the production workspace. Key Results On the 1,213-sample test set: LLaMA 3.1-8B rank-8 FT achieves 96.75% F1 (rank-32 baseline: 96.95%, gap = 0.20 pts). Qwen 3.5 4B JSON-Only achieves 96.60% F1 at 0.51 s/s vs. LLaMA 8B FT at 0.13 s/s — a 3.8× per-sample latency reduction for 0.35 F1 points. Qwen 3.5 0.8B FT Nothink achieves 94.75% F1 at 1.32 s/s, matching the Gemma 3 1B (93.93%) despite being 25% smaller. Gemma 3 scaling is log-linear with diminishing returns: +6.58 F1 pts from 270M→1B, +2.31 from 1B→4B; Gemma 3 270M FT floors at 87.35%. Chain-of-thought fine-tuning (FT over JO) gains 0.18–1.82 F1 pts across most models; Qwen 3.5 4B is the sole exception where JO beats FT by 0.45 pts. Think vs. Nothink F1 differences are <0.004 across all six Qwen 3.5 configurations; Nothink is 1.0–2.9× faster under FT prompts because Think-trained models generate empty <think></think> blocks, adding overhead without contributing reasoning. In production endpoint validation, 12 of 14 variants fall within 1.1 F1 points of their benchmark score (mean ΔF1 = −0.0081); Aya 3.35B is the sole outlier, dropping 5.1 pts (FT) and 2.7 pts (JO) under serving conditions, attributed to Cohere2 architecture immaturity in common serving runtimes. No CRF, BiLSTM-CRF, or encoder-only (BERT, RoBERTa) baselines are evaluated. Novelty Classification NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION. LoRA fine-tuning and chain-of-thought prompting are established techniques; applying them to financial transaction NER is a known problem framing. What is genuinely new: (1) the empirical finding that Qwen 3.5's hybrid Mamba-attention architecture yields disproportionate accuracy at sub-1B scale for substring extraction tasks; (2) the observation that SFT universally suppresses extended thinking in Think-trained Qwen models on structured extraction, with zero accuracy cost; (3) the benchmark-to-production transfer validation across 14 endpoints — rarely reported in this literature — with the Aya serving-stack failure as an actionable architectural finding. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations cover LoRA rank (8 vs. 32 on two models) and prompt strategy systematically across all model sizes, which is adequate for the stated claims. However, no error bars, confidence intervals, or multiple training seeds are reported — a notable omission given that differences of <0.004 F1 (Think vs. Nothink) are treated as substantive conclusions on a 1,213-sample test set where sampling variance alone could produce comparable variation. The checkpoint selection criterion is not explicitly stated: the paper says "we report the best" across 12 checkpoints but does not specify whether "best" is determined by validation-set or test-set F1. If the latter, reported numbers are optimistically inflated by checkpoint search. Code and dataset are released at github.com/inflaton/gaime-slm.git with a reproducibility checklist. The dataset is proprietary Mastercard internal data; the code release alone does not guarantee full reproducibility. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2606.08051v1, June 2026); results are self-reported industry numbers. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS (models trained and deployed as real production endpoints). - Practical: All training and inference run on a single NVIDIA DGX Spark desktop workstation; sub-8B models at half-precision require <8 GB GPU memory, well within commercially available consumer hardware. - Efficient: Qwen 3.5 4B JO achieves 0.51 s/s at batch size 4 on DGX Spark vs. LLaMA 8B at 0.13 s/s (3.8× faster); GPU VRAM requirements per model are not explicitly stated despite being the paper's primary cost motivation, and throughput is only reported at batch size 4 — production-realistic larger batch behavior is absent. - Effective: Endpoint F1 of 96.57% for Qwen 3.5 4B JO on the same 1,213-sample test set confirms the method solves the stated task in the actual serving environment, not just in simulation; exact-string-match F1 may understate practical utility when partial field matches are acceptable. - Scalable: No load testing at production transaction volumes is reported; the claim that sub-8B models enable millions-of-transactions-per-day processing is inferential, not measured. The paper delivers trained LoRA adapters (code and weights via GitHub) loadable via HuggingFace Transformers. Strengths - End-to-end production serving validation: deploying 14 model variants as real Databricks endpoints and re-evaluating on the same test set is rare in SLM benchmarking papers and directly answers the question of whether lab results translate to production — the Aya 5-point degradation finding would be invisible without this step. - Think/Nothink finding: demonstrating that SFT completely suppresses extended thinking (empty <think> blocks on 100% of test predictions) and that Nothink achieves identical accuracy with up to 2.9× faster inference is a non-obvious, practically actionable result that challenges assumptions about reasoning-capable models for deterministic extraction tasks. - Pareto-frontier analysis with quantified trade-offs (0.15 F1 pts for 3.8× latency reduction) is directly usable for deployment decisions, not just a ranking of models. - The hybrid Mamba-attention observation for Qwen 3.5 0.8B — matching 2–4× larger standard transformers — is a concrete cross-architecture finding tied to a plausible mechanistic explanation (linear-time sequence modeling for substring localization). Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly position the paper as "deployment-focused rather than methodologically novel." They acknowledge the single-domain proprietary dataset and do not claim generalizability. They flag Aya's serving-runtime incompatibility as a failure mode. Endpoint validation of the LLaMA 8B variants is deferred to future work. The paper does not claim that their two-stage workflow generalizes beyond Databricks/LLaMA-Factory environments, and notes that security-policy constraints motivate design choices that may not apply universally. These are direct and credible scope statements. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Checkpoint selection ambiguity: the paper never states whether the "best checkpoint" is chosen by validation-set or test-set F1. If the latter, every reported number is inflated by a 12-checkpoint search and the test set has been effectively used for model selection. - No non-LLM baselines: a BiLSTM-CRF or fine-tuned RoBERTa-base model is conspicuously absent. The paper motivates sub-8B LLMs as a replacement for its current 8B system, but never establishes that LLM-scale is necessary — a 400M-parameter encoder might reach 94%+ F1 at far lower cost. - No statistical significance testing: differences of <0.004 F1 on a 1,213-sample test set are below the threshold of statistical reliability without confidence intervals; the Think vs. Nothink "near-identical" framing is qualitatively correct but the quantitative deltas are not verified as noise-level. - Throughput measured only at batch size 4: production serving with request batching at sizes of 32–256 could change relative latency rankings, especially for the hybrid Mamba architecture whose linear-time advantage is most pronounced at longer sequences or larger batches. - GPU memory footprint not reported: despite being the paper's primary cost motivation (16+ GB for 8B), actual VRAM consumption for each model under inference is never stated, making the hardware-tier recommendations incomplete. - Single organization, single task domain: 8,015 samples from Mastercard transaction strings; how much of the performance is specific to this data distribution (consistent formatting conventions, limited vocabulary of merchants) versus generalizable is unknown. Bottom Line A production ML engineer choosing among sub-8B models for financial structured extraction will find the Databricks endpoint validation and Pareto analysis directly actionable; the Think/Nothink finding is an under-appreciated result that generalizes to any structured extraction task — but the absence of encoder-only baselines and the unresolved checkpoint-selection question leave the absolute accuracy claims less credible than the relative comparisons.
Search Results 2606.08051 PDF
#181 — Moderately Innovative (45)

Multi-Dimensional Assessment of Supply Chain Finance Risks in Fashion Textile and Leather: ESG Integration and Application of AHP-FAHP Method

X Zhang, Z Wu, Q Li · tlr-journal.com · digital twins for enterprises - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper constructs a four-dimension, twelve-indicator supply chain finance risk assessment framework for the fashion textile and leather industry using an AHP-FAHP hybrid method applied to surveys from 18 interdisciplinary experts, producing quantified risk weights and three practitioner decision tools. - Why it's important: Supply chain finance products in fast fashion are priced using generic credit-risk frameworks that fail to capture the industry's ultra-short product lifecycles, social-media-driven demand volatility, and rising ESG compliance burdens — mismatches that can lead to systematic mispricing and liquidity failures. - How it does it: AHP derives hierarchical importance weights from expert pairwise comparisons (Saaty 1–9 scale), while FAHP converts five-level Likert risk ratings into triangular fuzzy numbers and defuzzifies them into composite scores; the two are combined via a multiplicative adjustment formula to produce hybrid weights. - The good: The paper surfaces a credible and counter-intuitive finding: supply chain agility risk (41.54% weight) dominates over corporate credit risk (12.09%), consistent with the order-based financing model and ultra-short sales windows that characterize fast fashion. - The missing: No results are validated against actual default rates, financing losses, or any external financial outcome data; the entire empirical base is 18 expert opinions, making it impossible to assess whether the weights predict anything about real-world SCF risk. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners in fashion SCF who want a structured taxonomy of fashion-specific risk indicators and a ready-made weighting rationale; skip if you need empirically validated predictors of SCF default or loss-given-default.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. The composite is pulled up to moderate by a genuinely interesting and counter-intuitive empirical finding (Non-Obviousness 55) — that supply-chain agility risk dominates corporate credit risk (41.54% vs 12.09%) in fast-fashion SCF — combined with peer review and documented expert-elicitation consistency tests (Kendall W=0.82, all CR<0.1) that lift Rigor to about 40. But Rigor and Resilience (both ~40) cap the score: there is zero external validation against actual default or loss data, the expert-weighting formula is undisclosed (a reproducibility hole), and a likely misattributed/fabricated citation undermines the secondary statistics. Novelty (~50) is a re-scoping of well-established AHP-FAHP to a new industry, not a methodological advance, and Impact (~35) stops at paper-form decision tools. Honest, at-length limitation acknowledgment supports Completeness (~50). Weighted: 0.20*50 + 0.25*40 + 0.15*55 + 0.15*50 + 0.15*40 + 0.10*35 ≈ 45.
empirical Novelty 50 Rigor 40 Non-Obv 55 Completeness 50 Resilience 40 Impact 35
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Financial institutions extending supply chain finance (invoice discounting, purchase-order financing, inventory-backed lending) to fashion textile and leather companies use frameworks calibrated on traditional manufacturing, which centers on corporate creditworthiness. Fashion supply chains differ in fundamental ways: product lifecycles collapse to weeks or single seasons; social media platforms such as Xiaohongshu drive demand spikes and collapses within days; ESG compliance mandates (EU CSRD, Bluesign, GOTS) impose rapidly escalating certification costs that disproportionately burden small and medium suppliers; and emerging digital collateral such as NFT-linked fashion assets introduces valuation volatility that existing pricing models cannot handle. The gap is the absence of a quantified, fashion-specific risk hierarchy that simultaneously covers operational agility, market volatility, credit exposure, and novel financial instrument risks under a unified assessment framework. Key Idea: The central insight is that in fashion SCF, operational agility risk (can the supplier deliver a time-sensitive order within the sales window?) structurally dominates credit risk (will the supplier default on a loan?) because fast-fashion financing is primarily order-based rather than balance-sheet-based. Once a sales window closes, inventory depreciates sharply, eliminating collateral value regardless of borrower creditworthiness. Capturing this requires incorporating soft, hard-to-quantify factors (social media trend decay, celebrity scandal impact) that standard AHP cannot represent without information loss — motivating the FAHP overlay via triangular fuzzy numbers. Methodology: The study employs a three-level AHP hierarchy (objective → four criteria → twelve sub-risks) with data collected via a three-round Delphi process from 18 stratified experts (5 fashion executives, 5 SCF specialists, 4 e-commerce platform risk controllers, 4 academic researchers). Round 1 is semi-structured interviews; Round 2 feeds back group statistics for re-evaluation; Round 3 finalizes scores. Kendall's W = 0.82 in Round 3 indicates high consensus. AHP pairwise comparison matrices (geometric mean aggregation across 18 experts) yield criterion-layer weights; consistency ratios are all CR < 0.1 (criterion layer CR = 0.0196). FAHP adds a five-level Likert fuzzy evaluation: each expert assigns each of the twelve sub-risks to {very high, high, medium, low, very low} risk; membership degrees are frequency proportions; defuzzification uses a weighted-average dot product with score vector [5,4,3,2,1]. Hybrid weights are computed as W_hybrid,i = (W_AHP,i × S_i) / Σ(W_AHP,i × S_i), where S_i is the FAHP defuzzified risk score. Critical design decisions: (1) expert-type differential weighting — experts are differentially weighted by experience and sector, but the specific formula for assigning expert weights is not disclosed; (2) the 12-indicator selection via Delphi pre-screening, with 83.33% expert content validity; (3) Saaty scale anchors customized to fashion contexts (e.g., C2 is "48-hour delivery compliance rate," anchored from 100% to <50%). A triple sensitivity analysis (±10%, ±20%, ±30% weight perturbations; 20% expert dropout; fuzzy score-vector changes) finds top-5 factor rankings invariant. Key Results: The four-dimension weight ranking is: Supply Chain Agility Risk (B1) = 41.54% > Market Volatility Risk (B3) = 32.03% > Financial Instrument Risk (B4) = 14.34% > Corporate Credit Risk (B2) = 12.09%. At the sub-risk level, the top five by final weight are: (1) Seasonal trendy fabric price volatility (C1) = 19.08%; (2) Environmental material certification cost increase (C8) = 12.59%; (3) 48-hour delivery compliance rate (C2) = 12.59%; (4) Celebrity scandal marginal impact (C9) = 11.37%; (5) Unsold inventory weekly depreciation rate (C3) = 9.87%. By FAHP risk score (out of 5.0): C8 (environmental certification costs) = 4.61 (highest); C7 (Xiaohongshu viral topic decay) = 4.28; C5 (cross-border e-commerce anomalous payment cycles) = 4.17; C10 (NFT collateral valuation volatility) = 3.78. The overall target-level composite score is 3.56/5.0, falling in the defined "high-risk" interval (3.5–4.5). Financial Instrument Risk (B4) scores only 2.75, classified as low-risk, driven by low scores on exchange-rate hedging gaps (C11 = 2.17) and ABS subordinate tranche default probability (C12 = 2.33). A notable weight-score asymmetry exists: C8 has the highest risk score (4.61) but only ranks 2nd in final weight (12.59%); C1 has high weight (19.08%) and moderate score (3.61). No baselines from alternative frameworks are compared on the same data. No validation against actual SCF default rates is provided. Novelty Classification: NEW FORMULATION + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The fashion-specific four-dimension risk taxonomy with ESG and digital-asset indicators is a genuine re-scoping of an existing methodology (AHP-FAHP is well-established) to a new industry context; the formula for combining AHP weights with FAHP defuzzified scores is a straightforward product normalization, not a methodological innovation. The empirical finding that agility risk outweighs credit risk (41.54% vs. 12.09%) in fashion SCF is substantively interesting and not obvious from prior literature. The inclusion of NFT collateral and Xiaohongshu trend decay as formal risk indicators is novel in this domain, though their practical significance for mainstream SCF is acknowledged to be limited (C10 applies to <5% of transactions). Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are absent in the traditional sense; the sensitivity analysis substitutes but only tests weight perturbations, not indicator inclusion/exclusion. Error bars and confidence intervals on risk scores are not reported; the cross-validation (splitting 18 experts into two groups, r = 0.89) is encouraging but with groups of 9, statistical power is negligible. The Kendall W = 0.82 indicates good consensus but not correctness. The χ² test cited for structural representativeness (p = 0.32) is not explained — the null hypothesis and test design are unstated, making this unverifiable. Expert weights are said to be assigned "based on expertise and industry experience" but the formula is never disclosed, creating a reproducibility gap. Several statistics in the Discussion are drawn from secondary sources or industry reports (WEF, UNEP FI, McKinsey) rather than primary data; at least one citation appears mismatched: claim that Xiaohongshu drives 30–40% of fast fashion demand cites reference [11], which is a 2016 Transportation Research paper about social media and bounded rationality that predates Xiaohongshu's prominence. No code, dataset, or survey instrument is publicly released. The paper is peer-reviewed (Textile & Leather Review, published 27 April 2026). Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (expert survey yielding quantitative weights and risk scores). - Practical: The method requires only expert time and standard matrix software (MATLAB R2024a used); no specialized hardware needed, and the framework is self-contained. - Efficient: Data collection requires ~45–60 minute interviews per expert across three Delphi rounds; scaling to many organizations would require re-running the expert process, which is costly but feasible. - Effective: The framework produces plausible risk rankings with high internal consistency (all CR < 0.1, Kendall W = 0.82) but is not validated against any external criterion — it is unknown whether entities scoring higher on this framework actually experience more SCF defaults or losses. - Scalable: The framework is conceptually extensible to other fast-moving consumer goods industries (cosmetics, toys — as authors note), but each new context would require a fresh expert panel and Delphi process; there is no evidence it generalizes beyond the 18 China-centric experts surveyed. The study delivers paper-form decision tools (a tiered risk matrix, agility investment framework, ESG pricing formula) rather than software or deployed systems. Strengths: - The agility-over-credit finding is empirically grounded and theoretically coherent, providing a specific quantified claim (3.4× agility weight vs. credit) that challenges prevailing SCF frameworks. - The three-round Delphi process with documented consistency tests (Kendall W, per-matrix CR) represents methodologically sound expert elicitation for a domain where transaction-level data is commercially sensitive. - The AHP-FAHP comparison (Table 3) concretely demonstrates that FAHP raises weights on soft digital risks (Xiaohongshu +17.1%, NFT +17.5%) relative to AHP-only, providing a legitimate rationale for the methodological hybrid rather than mere claim. - The paper honestly acknowledges China-geographic bias, static weight limitations, and absence of large-sample empirical validation — limitations are confronted directly and at length rather than minimized. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: - Temporal constraints: the 12-indicator framework reflects 2024–2025 conditions; AI-driven design and new digital risks may require indicator updates rapidly. - Regional bias: 50% of experts are from the Yangtze River Delta; ESG compliance risk weights likely underrepresent European and American regulatory contexts and ignore Bangladesh/Vietnam manufacturing centers. - Data access barriers: no actual default rates, financing costs, or inventory turnover data were obtained; the entire empirical base is expert opinion. - Static methodology: AHP-FAHP weights cannot adapt to sudden shocks (e.g., a celebrity scandal that temporarily makes C9 the dominant risk) — the authors propose dynamic Bayesian networks and LSTM-based adaptive FAHP as future directions. The authors confront these credibly and propose specific remediation strategies. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The expert weight assignment formula is never disclosed, making the weight integration step (Equations 7–9) unreproducible; this is a material methodological opacity, not a minor omission. - The citation for the Xiaohongshu 30–40% demand-driver statistic (reference [11]) is a 2016 paper that cannot plausibly contain this claim — the sourcing is either misattributed or fabricated, which undermines confidence in other secondary statistics cited in the Discussion. - No predictive validity test is attempted: are high-scoring suppliers in this framework actually more likely to default, delay, or create financing losses? Without this, the output is structured expert opinion, not a validated risk model. - The ESG pricing model (Tool 3: Interest Rate = Base Rate − ESG Score × Adjustment Factor) is presented with no calibration data for the adjustment factor and no evidence the formula produces economically meaningful rate differentials — it is a formula skeleton, not a deployable pricing model. - The Discussion section contains strikingly ornate prose ("epistemic rift incarnates orthodox finance's risk aversion versus modern retail's growth preeminence") inconsistent with the paper's earlier sections, suggesting sections may have been drafted with AI writing assistance — this raises concerns about internal coherence and editorial review quality. - The comparison table (Table 1) positions this study against only three prior works, all of which conveniently lack fashion-specific features; stronger recent AHP/FAHP supply chain risk papers in apparel and textile SCF are not engaged. Bottom Line: A practitioner in fashion supply chain finance who needs a structured, industry-specific risk taxonomy and a defensible rationale for why agility risks belong in the pricing model will find the indicator framework and dimension weights useful starting points — but anyone needing a predictively validated tool or a framework tested against real financial outcomes will need to look elsewhere or validate these weights on their own transaction data.
Search Results
#182 — Moderately Innovative (45)

ABot-Earth 0.5: Generative 3D Earth Model (194 ▲ )

Ming Qian, Tianjian Ouyang, Mingchao Sun, Zijian Wang, Jincheng Xiong, Jiarong Han, Yongchang Zhang, Jiawei Zhang, Xu Wang, Yu Liu, Luyang Tang, Fei Yu, Zengye Ge, Mengmeng Du, Yuan Liu, Nianfei Fan, Song Wang, Yingliang Peng, Chunxue Jia, Yang Liu, Shiying Zeng, Haozhe Shi, Junnan Lai, Hongyu Pan, Zheng Wu, Ning Guo, Mu Xu, Hang Zhang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 10 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces ABot-Earth 0.5, a generative framework that synthesizes interactive, streamable, hierarchical-LOD 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) environments at planetary scale from single satellite images, deployed across 300+ cities in 190+ countries. - Why it's important: Traditional photogrammetry-based 3D reconstruction requires expensive multi-view aerial acquisition, limiting high-fidelity coverage to a small fraction of global urban area and requiring months-to-years to update, leaving robotics simulation, digital twins, and unmapped regions without viable 3D data. - How it does it: Trains a native 3DGS latent generative model on real-world city-scale reconstructions (not synthetic assets), conditioned on simulated satellite-view renderings, with a seamless sliding-window inference strategy and a VLM-based domain adaptation harness at inference time. - The good: FID of 16.1 vs. the previous published best of 69.5 (EarthCrafter), and demonstrated coverage of 88.4% of Asia vs. Google Earth's 8.5%, showing the accessibility argument is real and not just claimed. - The missing: The FID comparison is explicitly invalidated by the paper itself — Table 2 states that baselines use different ground-truth distributions and different evaluation viewpoints, with results labeled "for reference only" — leaving the paper with no valid apples-to-apples quantitative evaluation of generative quality. - Read it or not: Worth reading for engineers designing large-scale geospatial simulation or UAV training infrastructure; researchers requiring rigorous scientific evaluation of generative quality should treat this as a system report with partial technical disclosure rather than a methodologically complete paper.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Practical Impact (55) is the dominant weighted term and is the paper's real strength — a genuinely deployed planetary-scale product (3.2T primitives, 300+ cities, EarthScape serving pipeline), though no open model/dataset/code caps it below the level a released artifact would earn. Novelty (65) is moderate-to-strong: the integration of real-world 3DGS training data, satellite conditioning, and inherent multi-LOD generation at this geographic scale has no precedent, but every component is an existing primitive. Rigor (22) is severe — the headline FID comparison is self-declared non-comparable by the authors, there are zero component ablations, the human study reports no methodology, no artifacts are released, training data is partly proprietary, and it is an unreviewed preprint. Resilience (30) is low because the central generative-quality claim collapses the moment Table 2's caveats are read, and the stated embodied-AI/sim-to-real motivation has no downstream experiment. Completeness (38) is pulled down by unsupported claims and the 64× inference extrapolation, partly offset by honest acknowledgment of the cross-block seamlessness and fidelity gaps. Composite: 0.15*65 + 0.15*22 + 0.10*50 + 0.15*38 + 0.15*30 + 0.30*55 = 45.
systems Novelty 65 Rigor 22 Non-Obv 50 Completeness 38 Resilience 30 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Large-scale 3D reconstruction of Earth's surface is needed for digital twins, autonomous UAV navigation, smart city planning, and disaster response. Photogrammetry and LiDAR-based pipelines require physical data acquisition infrastructure, restricting high-fidelity 3D coverage to well-funded metropolitan regions (Google Earth covers only ~8.5% of Asian countries in 3D). Update cycles run months to years. Existing outdoor 3D generative models (CityDreamer, GaussianCity, EarthCrafter) rely on synthetic virtual assets or unconstrained hallucination, producing environments that fail the sim-to-real gap test for embodied AI applications. Object-level 3D generators (TRELLIS, HunyuanD, Seed3D) assume mesh-friendly topology and do not scale to unbounded outdoor scenes with foliage, water, and heterogeneous urban geometry. Key Idea Train a generative model directly in native 3DGS latent space using real-world city-scale 3DGS reconstructions as training data — not synthetic proxies — so the model learns authentic photometric and geometric priors from actual urban captures. Combined with satellite imagery as a geospatially ubiquitous conditioning signal, this enables unlimited-coverage 3D generation without requiring new physical acquisition beyond what satellite operators already provide. Methodology The pipeline has four stages. First, multi-source imagery collection: multi-stereo satellite (including DFC 2019), high-resolution oblique aerial (core source), and street/drone urban footage, drawn from proprietary acquisitions plus public datasets (UrbanScene3D, Mill-19, UC-GS, CrossLoc, UrbanBIS, UAVD4L, DenseUAV). Second, ABot-3DGS city-scale reconstruction: hierarchical block-based partitioning across hundreds of km², multi-level appearance variation modeling (disentangles lighting/weather/season), cross-view matching for satellite-to-aerial registration, and generative refinement for under-observed regions. The FromOrbit2Ground module handles satellite-to-3DGS conversion via a two-stage pipeline (Z-Monotonic SDF for geometry + diffusion-based facade texture synthesis). Third, training tile generation: 200m×200m sliding-window tiles with multi-view camera arrays at varying altitude layers and pitch angles, plus simulated satellite-view renderings for conditioning. Fourth, multi-granularity quality filtering: PSNR/SSIM/LPIPS at tile level, VLM perceptual scoring at view level, spatial diversity balancing and semantic deduplication at dataset level. Four core technical components are described but not ablated: (1) compression-generation paradigm in a learned 3DGS latent space; (2) inherent multi-LOD decoder producing a 6-level hierarchy (zoom 14-19) natively during generation rather than post-processing; (3) seamless sliding-window inference blending overlapping tile regions; (4) VLM-based conditioning harness at inference to adapt real satellite imagery characteristics to training distribution. Deployment runs on a 1,000-GPU A100 cluster, with EarthScape post-processing reorganizing 3.2 trillion primitives into OGC 3D Tiles and serving via the Amap Yunjing rendering engine. Key Results FID 16.1 / KID 0.006 vs. CityDreamer 97.3/0.096, GaussianCity 86.9/0.090, EarthCrafter 69.5/0.061 — but Table 2 explicitly states these comparisons use different GT distributions and different evaluation viewpoints and labels results "for reference only," making them non-comparable. Human study (methodology unreported) rates ABot-Earth 0.5 higher than Google Earth on aesthetics (3.91 vs. 3.30) but lower on texture fidelity (3.55 vs. 3.79) and roughly comparable on geometry (3.84 vs. 3.79 for Google Earth). Coverage: 88.4% of Asia, 91.5% of Europe vs. Google Earth's 8.5% and 28.4% respectively. Generation throughput: approximately 10 minutes per km² (single 2.56 km² tile in 25 minutes on A100 GPU configuration); 1,000-GPU cluster completes global ~800,000 km² built-up area in under 10 days. Output scale: 3.2 trillion Gaussian primitives across ~320,000 tiles. Qualitative comparisons against Google Earth for New Zealand, Japan, and Ireland show visual parity in covered regions and plausible synthesis where Google Earth falls back to flat 2D imagery. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The genuine novelty is the integration of real-world 3DGS training data, satellite conditioning, inherent multi-LOD generation, and planetary-scale deployment infrastructure into a single operational system — no prior work has demonstrated generative 3D synthesis at this geographic scale from real-world training data. Individual components (3DGS, latent diffusion, LOD, sliding-window inference) are existing techniques; the contribution is their combination and operationalization at scale, not a fundamentally new algorithmic primitive. Rigor & Reproducibility No ablations are presented for any of the four core technical components; it is unknown whether the VLM conditioning harness, sliding-window blending, inherent LOD decoder, or cross-domain adaptation each contribute independently. The primary quantitative comparison (FID/KID) is self-declared non-comparable by the authors. The human study omits participant count, stimulus selection protocol, blinding procedures, inter-rater reliability, and statistical significance tests. No code, model weights, or dataset are released (official page is a product website, not a research artifact). Training data includes an undisclosed volume of proprietary aerial and satellite imagery, making reproduction impossible. This is a preprint (arXiv:2606.09967v1), not peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS — a built infrastructure evaluated by deployment. - Practical: A100 GPU cluster required; a single 2.56 km² tile takes 25 minutes, placing global production firmly in enterprise-scale infrastructure territory, not accessible on commodity hardware. - Efficient: 10 minutes/km² vs. months-to-years for photogrammetry represents a dramatic throughput gain; however, the 1,000-GPU × 10-day global production run represents enormous infrastructure cost, and the Bhattacharyya-distance CPU-side decimation for lower LOD levels does intelligently exploit heterogeneous compute to reduce end-to-end latency. - Effective: Coverage is demonstrably broader than photogrammetry alternatives and qualitative results show plausible 3D synthesis in previously uncovered regions; geometric and textural fidelity trails Google Earth by the authors' own human study, and no downstream task performance (UAV navigation success rate, digital twin accuracy) is measured despite being the stated application. - Scalable: Demonstrated at true global scale (3.2 trillion primitives, 300+ cities, 190+ countries); cross-block tile seamlessness at continental scale is not yet solved and explicitly flagged as future work; inference operates at 64× the training tile area, an extrapolation regime whose effect on quality is not quantified. Delivers a deployed product (abot-earth.amap.com) and the EarthScape rendering pipeline; no open software artifact, trained model, or dataset is provided. Strengths - Training on real-world 3DGS reconstructions rather than synthetic assets is a principled and well-motivated design choice that directly addresses the limitation that undermines CityDreamer and GaussianCity for sim-to-real embodied AI transfer. - Demonstrated planetary-scale deployment (3.2T primitives, 300+ cities) is an engineering milestone with few academic precedents and credibly validates the scalability claim rather than projecting it. - Inherent multi-LOD generation baked into the decoder (zoom 17-19 native, zoom 14-16 via Bhattacharyya decimation on CPU in parallel with GPU inference) is a practically sound architecture that avoids both post-processing quality loss and redundant storage of multiple discrete scene copies. - Geographic coverage data (continental breakdown showing 88.4% Asia, 91.5% Europe) is concrete and directly relevant evidence for the primary access-equity claim. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly acknowledge that cross-block seamlessness at the 1.6km×1.6km inference scale is unsolved and treated as a key future objective. They concede that Google Earth maintains geometric and textural fidelity advantages, framing the gap as analogous to early generative models vs. professional reconstruction (credible comparison). Street-level detail is explicitly deferred. Scaling laws governing outdoor 3D generation are flagged as requiring systematic future validation — implying they remain unstudied. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The headline quantitative result is methodologically void: Table 2 explicitly states different GT sets and viewpoints across methods, yet the FID improvement (16.1 vs. 69.5) is presented as the primary evidence of generative quality superiority. This is the paper's most significant analytical failure. - Zero component ablations: the paper cannot demonstrate that the VLM harness, sliding-window blending, cross-domain training simulation, or inherent LOD decoder each contribute; any subset could be dropped without measurable loss. - Human study is reported without any methodology: participant count, recruitment, stimulus selection, blinding, and significance testing are all absent, making the aesthetic/texture/geometry scores uninterpretable. - The downstream embodied AI claim (sim-to-real UAV navigation) is entirely unsupported by experiment; only visual landmark compositing is shown, which is not evidence of reduced sim-to-real gap for policy transfer. - Inference runs at 64× the training tile spatial extent; this extreme extrapolation regime is neither analyzed nor ablated, yet spatial coherence at this scale is the central challenge. - Proprietary training data fraction is undisclosed, preventing any reproduction or independent validation. Bottom Line Practitioners building large-scale geospatial simulation infrastructure will find the system architecture, deployment pipeline, and coverage results genuinely useful, but the absence of valid quantitative evaluation, ablations, open artifacts, or downstream task evidence means this functions as a product technical report rather than a scientifically complete research contribution.
Recommended 2606.09967 PDF
#183 — Moderately Innovative (44)

AI-Driven Software Pricing: An Integrated Approach with Prompt Engineering for Market Analysis

GF Muniz, J de Carvalho Tonera, RP Noll… · journals-sol.sbc.org.br · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a reproducible two-phase LLM-mediated analogy pricing protocol that estimates annual market-value ranges for B2B software by feeding official sitemap PDFs to three LLMs under a structured prompt, then validating the estimates against real market quotations across six software products. - Why it's important: Organizations — especially public institutions and SMEs — lack a lightweight, auditable method for initial software price screening before entering formal, time-consuming procurement processes; existing methods (COCOMO, Function Points) target development cost, not market price. - How it does it: A parameterized prompt locks the LLM to "file-only" sitemap-based feature extraction in phase one, then re-enables web browsing for competitive benchmarking in phase two, isolating each run in a fresh chat to prevent context contamination. - The good: ChatGPT 5.1 Thinking covered the real market price within its estimated interval in 5 of 6 cases (83.3%), and all three models converged on the correct order of magnitude for most products, supporting the claim that the protocol produces directionally useful initial estimates. - The missing: The validation set is only 6 software products, each with a single ground-truth price drawn from heterogeneous secondary sources, and the paper never tests whether the protocol outperforms a plain unconstrained LLM query — making it impossible to attribute any accuracy to the protocol itself rather than to the LLMs' pretraining knowledge. - Read it or not: Worth skimming for procurement analysts or IS researchers interested in LLM-assisted market intelligence workflows; insufficient empirical rigor for anyone seeking evidence that the specific protocol design adds accuracy over baseline LLM querying.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. This is the strongest paper in the cluster — the only one with genuine validation, peer review (JBCS), and released artifacts. Rigor (40) is materially lifted by an unusually complete GitHub release (sitemap PDFs with hashed metadata, verbatim prompts, per-session inference logs, output tables), though held down by n=6, no ablations, the temperature confound (0.2 vs 1.0 vs 0.7), and heterogeneous single-source ground-truth prices. Novelty (45) and Non-Obviousness (45) reflect the genuinely new but applied sitemap-as-standardized-functional-proxy idea, assembling known components (LLM prompting, analogy pricing, competitive benchmarking) into a reproducible workflow. Completeness (50) is helped by limitations confronted directly across all sections, but the failure to ablate the core 'file-only' claim (no test of protocol vs. plain LLM query) leaves its marginal contribution unestablished, capping Resilience (40). Impact (50) reflects an immediately deployable, auditable workflow with public artifacts, though no library/CLI/model is shipped. Weighted: 0.20*45 + 0.25*40 + 0.15*45 + 0.15*50 + 0.15*40 + 0.10*50 = 44.
empirical Novelty 45 Rigor 40 Non-Obv 45 Completeness 50 Resilience 40 Impact 50
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Organizations selecting enterprise software face opaque, heterogeneous pricing (per-seat, per-instance, enterprise tiers) and no inexpensive public method for estimating competitive market value before formal procurement. Traditional software cost estimation methods (COCOMO, Function Points) model development effort, not the commercial price a vendor will charge. Manual competitive analysis is time-consuming and non-reproducible. The specific gap is the absence of a lightweight, auditable, publicly replicable protocol for generating initial price bands from freely available artifacts — particularly in contexts such as public-sector institutions that must justify acquisition budgets before soliciting vendor quotes. Key Idea The core trick is a two-phase prompt-engineering strategy within isolated LLM sessions: phase one restricts the model to "file-only" mode, inferring product features solely from the vendor's XML sitemap archived as a PDF, on the theory that sitemaps are standardized, vendor-maintained, and publicly available across all products; phase two re-enables web browsing so the model retrieves competitor prices and synthesizes an analogy-based price range. The architectural insight is using the sitemap — rather than heterogeneous marketing pages or gated documentation — as a reproducible neutral functional proxy. Methodology The study runs a 6 × 3 factorial experiment (6 software programs × 3 LLMs = 18 independent chat sessions, no repeated measures within cells, no control groups). Software: INTEGRA, Salesforce, HYPE Innovation, IdeaScale, Viima/HYPE Boards, Qmarkets. LLMs: ChatGPT 5.1 Thinking (temperature 0.2), Gemini 3 Pro (temperature 1.0), DeepSeek-V3.2 (temperature 0.7). The procedure: official XML sitemaps are archived as PDFs with collection metadata; a single standardized parameterized prompt is sent once per session; phase one extracts a feature list solely from the sitemap text; phase two identifies comparable competitors and retrieves publicly listed prices with explicit URL citations; outputs are a feature list, benchmarking table, comparative feature table, and a price range estimate with justification. Validation compares each LLM range against one real-market annual price per software, sourced from aggregator sites, official pricing pages, or Brazilian public procurement gazettes, using two indicators: binary interval coverage (market price inside/outside estimated range) and quotation ratio (market price ÷ midpoint × 100%). Human verification by two authors checks feature-to-sitemap anchoring and price-to-URL traceability. Critical design decisions: (1) sitemap-as-proxy is justified on reproducibility grounds but acknowledged to underrepresent advanced features and integrations; (2) chat isolation per software/LLM pair prevents context contamination but precludes multi-turn refinement; (3) single validation price per product from heterogeneous secondary sources is the weakest design choice and is not well-justified. Key Results Interval coverage: ChatGPT 5/6 (83.3%), Gemini 4/6 (66.7%), DeepSeek 2/6 (33.3%). Midpoint quotation rates: ChatGPT ranged 26.9%–106.5% across the six products; Gemini 25.0%–115.1%; DeepSeek 9.7%–122.0%, the widest spread. DeepSeek's worst miss: IdeaScale estimated at US$90,000–270,000 vs. market price of US$17,499.50 (quotation ratio 9.7%). Gemini's worst miss: INTEGRA estimated at US$130,000–280,000 vs. US$13,309 (quotation ratio 36.5%). All three models converged on correct ranges for HYPE Innovation (~US$58,560 market price, all models covered it). Informal post-hoc supplier quotes for three products reportedly fell within LLM ranges, but specific figures are not disclosed for confidentiality. No significance tests are reported; n=6 is explicitly described as diagnostic. Baselines used are heterogeneous: one product's price derives from a 2021 public procurement document, another from a biannual TrustRadius subscription price annualized by dividing by two, and Salesforce's reference is the lowest-tier four-user Starter Suite at US$25/user/month — each addressing a different tier of a heavily tiered product. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD (applied). What is genuinely novel: the specific two-phase "file-only → web-enabled" chat protocol using XML sitemaps as a standardized functional proxy for LLM-assisted market pricing — a previously undemonstrated application of prompt engineering to software valuation rather than effort estimation. What is standard combination of known ideas: LLM prompting, competitive benchmarking, and analogy-based pricing are each established; the paper assembles them into a reproducible workflow with validation indicators. Rigor & Reproducibility No ablations are conducted — the paper never tests whether the "file-only" constraint changes outputs versus unconstrained LLM querying, nor whether the structured prompt outperforms a plain request. No statistical significance tests, error bars, or confidence intervals are reported; the authors explicitly acknowledge n=6 is insufficient for generalization. Artifacts are publicly released on GitHub (https://github.com/gregfermun/AI-Driven-Software-Pricing-An-Integrated-Approach-with-Prompt-Engineering-for-Market-Analysis), including sitemap PDFs with collection metadata, full prompt text, session logs with inference parameters, and output tables in CSV/ODS format — unusual care for an applied LLM study. Key methodological concerns: (a) validation prices come from heterogeneous secondary sources at varying tiers of each product, introducing unknown ground-truth measurement error; (b) the "file-only" instruction cannot be verified to prevent training-knowledge use given black-box LLM inference; (c) temperature differs substantially across models (0.2 vs. 1.0 vs. 0.7), confounding cross-model comparisons with generation stochasticity; (d) the human verification step lacks formal inter-rater agreement statistics. The paper is peer-reviewed (JBCS, received September 2025, accepted February 2026). Deployability (EMPIRICAL) Profile: EMPIRICAL — the paper runs chat-based LLM experiments and reports numeric price-accuracy outcomes. - Practical: The protocol runs entirely through public web chat interfaces with no specialized hardware or API keys; any practitioner with access to ChatGPT, Gemini, and DeepSeek can replicate it immediately. - Efficient: Each session takes approximately 1 minute per run; the total 18-run experiment required no computational infrastructure beyond consumer LLM chat access, making it orders of magnitude cheaper than traditional pricing consulting. - Effective: The protocol produces order-of-magnitude price estimates with acceptable coverage for ChatGPT (5/6) but inconsistent midpoint accuracy (quotation ratios 26.9%–106.5%); it solves initial directional screening but not precise pricing, and DeepSeek's 2/6 coverage rate makes it unreliable as a standalone tool. - Scalable: Not tested. Performance at 50 or 500 software titles, in categories beyond innovation management and CRM, or for products without public pricing pages, is entirely unknown. The work delivers a reproducible workflow with publicly released artifacts but no software library, CLI tool, or trained model. Strengths - Reproducibility infrastructure is genuine: the GitHub repository bundles sitemap PDFs with hashed collection metadata, verbatim prompt text, per-session inference parameter logs, and output tables — a higher standard of artifact sharing than typical in applied LLM case studies. - The two-indicator validation framework (binary interval coverage + quotation ratio) is simple but purposeful: it correctly distinguishes "inside the range" from "close to the market price," enabling directional bias diagnosis — DeepSeek is shown to systematically overestimate midpoints, not just miss intervals. - The paper cleanly separates pricing (market value determination) from effort estimation (development cost prediction), a conceptual clarification that corrects a persistent conflation in the applied software engineering literature and correctly positions the work's scope. - Limitations are confronted directly and consistently across all sections rather than confined to a closing paragraph, which strengthens the paper's epistemic honesty. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state: sitemap functional extraction underrepresents advanced features, integrations, and back-office routines; price benchmarking relies solely on public sources, excluding negotiated prices, discounts, and TCO; hallucination risk is mitigated but not eliminated by human verification; sample size n=6 is insufficient for generalization; no formal inter-rater agreement calibration for human verification; some software vendors do not publish prices publicly; LLM internal inference is not fully controllable via web interfaces, introducing residual irreproducibility. These limitations are confronted directly rather than minimized, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No ablation of the protocol's core claim: the paper never compares estimates produced with vs. without the sitemap PDF, making it impossible to determine whether the "file-only" constraint adds value or whether the LLM's price estimates derive entirely from pretraining knowledge regardless of the uploaded file. - Incommensurable ground-truth prices: INTEGRA's reference is a 2021 Brazilian public procurement contract price; IdeaScale's is a biannual TrustRadius subscription annualized by halving; Salesforce's is a four-user Starter Suite entry price — these are not comparable market prices at the same tier or vintage, yet they are treated as equivalent validation benchmarks. - Undisclosed post-hoc validation: the claim that supplier quotes for three products "fell within" LLM ranges is asserted but the figures are withheld for confidentiality, making this an unverifiable rhetorical appeal embedded in the results section. - Temperature confound: ChatGPT ran at temperature 0.2 (near-deterministic) while Gemini ran at 1.0 (high variance); observed differences in interval coverage could reflect generation temperature as much as model capability or training knowledge quality. - No comparison to any established baseline method (including naive unstructured LLM query), so the marginal contribution of the protocol apparatus over simply typing the product name into a chatbot is unestablished. Bottom Line A procurement analyst needing a fast, auditable starting point for enterprise software price screening will find the protocol immediately deployable and the GitHub artifacts genuinely useful; a researcher seeking rigorous evidence that the protocol design adds accuracy beyond baseline LLM querying will find the evaluation — six products, heterogeneous ground-truth prices, no ablations, no statistical tests — too thin to support that conclusion.
Citations
#184 — Moderately Innovative (42)

MetaConfigurator: AI-Assisted RDF Authoring from JSON Data

F Neubauer, M Jafarkhani, K Endo, J Pleiss… · arxiv.org · semantic integration ontologies - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces an RDF Authoring View extension to the open-source MetaConfigurator web application, enabling end-to-end conversion of JSON/YAML/CSV data into RDF via AI-assisted RML mapping generation, in-browser SPARQL querying, ontology-aware IRI completion, and interactive knowledge graph visualization within a single interface. - Why it's important: Researchers working with structured scientific data (e.g., laboratory protocols, simulation outputs) cannot easily achieve semantic interoperability across systems because JSON Schema enforces structure but not meaning, and existing Semantic Web tooling requires switching between multiple disconnected applications. - How it does it: The extension embeds an LLM API call that combines user-supplied natural language hints with a representative subset of the input data to draft RML mappings and SPARQL queries, then executes them client-side using Comunica's in-browser SPARQL engine against the resulting JSON-LD graph. - The good: The tool is open-source, deployed at metaconfigurator.org, and demonstrated on a real chemical dataset (MOF synthesis protocols), showing the full pipeline from JSON input to ontology-linked RDF, SPARQL query, and graph visualization without leaving the browser. - The missing: The paper contains no quantitative evaluation — no user study, no task-completion timing, no accuracy measurement of AI-generated mappings, and no performance benchmark against alternative tools such as Karma, OpenRefine+RDF plugin, or YARRRML/RMLMapper. - Read it or not: Read it if you are building or evaluating tooling for research data management and want a concrete example of integrating LLM-assisted RML/SPARQL authoring into an existing structured-data editor; skip it if you need evidence of effectiveness or scalability beyond a toy demonstration.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Impact (55, weighted 30%) carries the score: a genuinely released, open-source, deployed artifact (metaconfigurator.org, GitHub) integrating RML mapping, in-browser SPARQL, IRI completion, and KG visualization in one tool — the kind of released artifact this dimension rewards. But Rigor (25) is near-floor: the paper has no quantitative evaluation of any kind — no user study, no AI-mapping accuracy measurement, no performance benchmark, only a single self-constructed MOF demo that required manual corrections. Novelty (35) and Non-Obviousness (35) are low — an integration of established components (RML, Comunica, Cytoscape, LLM-prompted drafting). Completeness (45) is helped by honest limitation acknowledgment. The complete absence of evaluation pins this in the Moderately Innovative band despite the real artifact.
systems Novelty 35 Rigor 25 Non-Obv 35 Completeness 45 Resilience 40 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Scientific workflows in domains like computational chemistry and materials science produce large volumes of structured data in JSON or related formats. These formats support machine readability but not semantic interoperability: two systems that consume the same JSON file may interpret field names entirely differently because JSON Schema enforces only shape, not meaning. Transforming such data into RDF with proper ontology links would enable cross-dataset integration, FAIR data principles, and federated querying, but the tooling barrier is high. Defining RML mappings requires expertise in both the source data model and target ontologies; editing RDF triples, running SPARQL queries, and visualizing knowledge graphs have traditionally required separate specialized tools (Protégé, YARRRML editors, Virtuoso, Cytoscape). Domain scientists with strong experimental knowledge but limited Semantic Web experience are effectively excluded from this stack. Key Idea: The core idea is to embed the entire RDF authoring lifecycle — mapping generation, triple editing, SPARQL querying, and graph visualization — as a new view inside an existing JSON Schema editor, and use LLM-generated drafts (for RML mappings and SPARQL queries) seeded with natural language hints and representative data subsets to lower the entry barrier. The human-in-the-loop review step keeps the user in control before any generated artifact is applied. Methodology: The extension adds an RDF Authoring View to MetaConfigurator, a Vue.js web application. The pipeline has four stages. (1) JSON-to-JSON-LD conversion via an RML Mapping Dialog: user optionally provides natural language mapping hints; these are combined with a representative subset of the input data and sent to a configurable external LLM API (e.g., OpenAI GPT) via pre-defined prompt instructions; the LLM returns a candidate RML mapping displayed in an editable code editor; the user reviews and triggers RMLMapper execution to produce JSON-LD. (2) Triple editing via a tabular Triples tab with paging, sorting, filtering, and an edit modal; for Predicate and Object fields, an Ontology Explorer dialog fetches ontology terms from the JSON-LD @context prefixes, caches them in IndexedDB, and offers property search and selection. (3) SPARQL querying via a dialog backed by Comunica's `@comunica/query-sparql` in-browser engine; users can enter queries manually or request AI-generated drafts using the same hint + data-context LLM pattern; live syntax validation uses Sparql.js. (4) Knowledge graph visualization via Cytoscape.js with COSE-Bilkent force-directed layout; nodes and edges are rendered from the in-memory RDF store or from SPARQL CONSTRUCT results; clickable IRI links navigate within the graph or open external ontology definitions. There is no formal objective function; the system is an engineering artifact. Critical design decisions: (a) using JSON-LD as the internal RDF representation is well-justified as it remains valid JSON while adding semantic structure; (b) the human review gate before applying AI output is appropriate given the acknowledged fragility of LLM-generated mappings; (c) browser-side SPARQL execution avoids server infrastructure but limits scalability — this trade-off is acknowledged but not quantified. Key Results: The paper provides no quantitative results. The single application example converts a JSON dataset of metal-organic framework (MOF) synthesis protocols into JSON-LD using an AI-generated RML mapping, executes a SPARQL SELECT to retrieve preparation steps with reagents and quantities across synthesis runs (returning 6 rows for 2 synthesis entries), and renders the graph in Cytoscape.js. The mapping required manual corrections (explicit ordering field added, unit values normalized to QUDT vocabulary). No timing, no accuracy rate for AI-generated mappings, no graph size tested, no comparison against alternative tools. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The contribution is an integrated browser-based environment combining RML mapping, JSON-LD editing, in-browser SPARQL, ontology IRI completion, and KG visualization — all extensions to an existing open-source tool. The individual components (RML, Comunica, Cytoscape.js, LLM-prompted mapping) are established; the novelty is their integration into a single unified authoring workflow inside MetaConfigurator. The LLM integration pattern (hint + data sample → draft → human review) extends a pattern already present in MetaConfigurator for schema generation; its application to RML mapping drafting is the most novel individual feature but is not evaluated. Rigor & Reproducibility: There are no ablations, no error bars, no user study, and no performance measurements of any kind. The code is publicly available at https://github.com/MetaConfigurator/meta-configurator and the application example at the same repo under `documentation_user/examples/rdf`. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2606.07094v1, submitted June 2026) published in ECEASST, a workshop/symposium proceedings journal; it has not been peer-reviewed at a major venue. The single application example was constructed by the authors on their own dataset. The mapping required manual dataset adjustments before conversion succeeded (explicit step ordering, unit normalization) — this dependency is disclosed but treated as minor rather than as a limitation of the AI mapping quality. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS — a built web tool evaluated by a single deployment example. - Practical: Runs entirely in the browser with no special hardware requirements; users need only a web browser and a valid API key for the LLM endpoint. - Efficient: All RDF operations (SPARQL execution, graph rendering) are client-side via Comunica and Cytoscape.js; the paper notes that large graphs trigger a usability warning before rendering, but no graph size threshold or timing is reported. - Effective: The system successfully converts one MOF synthesis JSON record to ontology-linked JSON-LD and retrieves the correct query results; whether it handles the structural variability of real scientific datasets at scale is not tested. - Scalable: The authors explicitly acknowledge that browser-side processing limits scalability for large graphs; no data is given on how many triples or how large a JSON input the tool can handle before performance degrades. The work delivers a software artifact (open-source web application with GitHub release). Strengths: - The integration scope is practical: combining JSON-to-RDF transformation, triple editing, SPARQL querying, and graph visualization in a single tool removes real workflow friction for domain scientists who currently switch between Protégé, YARRRML editors, and separate SPARQL endpoints. - The human-in-the-loop design for AI-generated artifacts is appropriate and explicitly enforced in the UI — the tool positions AI output as a draft requiring review, which is the correct posture given current LLM reliability. - Open-source release with a concrete runnable example lowers the barrier to adoption and independent evaluation. - The ontology caching in IndexedDB for IRI completion is a thoughtful implementation detail that avoids repeated network fetches for large vocabularies. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly state: (1) AI-generated mappings and queries may contain structural or semantic errors requiring human correction; (2) creating high-quality RML mappings still requires domain expert knowledge even with AI assistance; (3) browser-side processing limits scalability for large graphs; (4) selecting appropriate ontologies, classes, predicates, and IRI patterns remains challenging for non-expert users. The authors confront these limitations directly in the conclusion, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The most significant gap the authors do not acknowledge is the complete absence of evaluation. There is no user study measuring task completion time, error rate on AI-generated mappings, or subjective usability compared to alternative tools. There is no quantitative accuracy assessment of LLM-generated RML (what fraction of generated mappings are correct, how many require manual edits, what kinds of errors occur). There is no performance characterization (how many triples before the browser stalls). The comparison to related tools (Karma, OpenRefine RDF extension, YARRRML+RMLMapper CLI, Protégé) is purely qualitative and cited in passing — no head-to-head workflow comparison is conducted. The dataset adjustment required before mapping succeeded (step ordering, unit normalization) suggests AI mapping quality may be lower than implied; this is underreported. The paper derives directly from a master's thesis [Jaf26], and the ECEASST venue is a workshop-level proceedings journal, so the contribution level is commensurate with the scope but should not be mistaken for a fully evaluated systems paper. Bottom Line: This is a useful but unevaluated systems tool paper — a practitioner building research data management infrastructure will find the integration concept and open-source implementation worth examining, but should not rely on any claimed effectiveness without running their own evaluation.
Search Results 2606.07094 PDF
#185 — Moderately Innovative (41)

A Biological Systems-of-Systems Ontology for Multi-Scale Biomanufacturing Process Modeling and Simulation

K Choy, W Xie, J Pei, F Cheng, K Wang, A Nikolov… · par.nsf.gov · semantic integration ontologies - new results
